Representative_Docs: ['JUSTE By Oskars Kalējs Holding a clay bowl in both hands Juste came out of the kitchen. In the courtyard one could still see the paths but darkness was thickening over the horizon, creeping over the bends of the river Palade. In the west a pink cloud- slowly merged with the forest top. There was still frost at night in this unpredictable month of spring. Tonight the warmth was a little more friendly. Swollen round buds were tearing their shells. The month of leaves was advancing and tomorrow, perhaps, would be the first leaf-bringing day. Juste stood a while, hesitating, then walked towards the byre, where in front of the kennel sounding its chain was the house-dog Tecis. Putting the bowl before the dog, she said: "Here, Tecit, supper time! Munch your bone and watch the house!" Tecis\' warm big tongue licked her cheek and then began to lap up the porridge. Juste turned back towards her room. Darkness was now wavering all around like a blanket. Spring sounds penetrated the darkness: a late bird bending its voice, frogs and crickets sounding theirs and across the river in the wood heath-cocks could be heard. Sounds of life passed over her head. She had reached a stage in her life when blood no longer flowed in fast rhythms. Her mind was tied to work, work which waited to be done each spring. Juste stopped at the door of her house and her eyes looked back across the yard. Around the buildings was a more solid, a thicker night than along the horizon. The pink cloud had merged more fully with the blackness of the forest, and above pale stars perched in the dome. The buildings slumbered, as they did each night when the day was done. Juste stepped across the high threshold of her house and bolted the door. Everyone else was already asleep, their healthy breathing echoed strongly in the beams. Juste lit a lamp. There were three beds in her room. All three were covered with similarly patterned blankets. Spread out in the corner was a brown wardrobe. By the window, covered with a dark cloth sprawled a table. On the opposite side were a pair of chairs. Juste dropped down on her bed. The night was for regaining breath and peace. Days were long and claimed all her strength. At nights, having put her head on the pillow, she slept like a log and woke in the morning refreshed and full of strength. While the summer sky stood clear above her head for weeks on end, not one of her clays was bright. Concern and worry about the farm and the household continually grew in size, like mountains in a stormy sky. Concern and worry had claimed her youth. Not fully nineteen years old had she been when Karlis Skalde had brought her across two boroughs to the Grivas farm as a wife. Karlis had had a clever head, but his hands had been too fine for rough work. Those had been restless times. The majority of people had revalued their lives and had found that somewhere it was better and that it had to be fought for, if not with wisdom, then with strength. Karlis had then found a good use for his wise head. He had travelled around attending meetings and had left the farm to her management. It had been fortunate that she had known how to work and how to organize her people. The fields of Grivas had been then as if sown with stones and the meadows were marshy for lack of drainage. She had spent every spare moment gathering stones off the fields and filling with them the ruts in the road. In the spring there had not been enough hay tor the cows, so she had run to the meadows by the river creek to pick butter-cups and marsh marigolds, and so had pulled the cows through until the first green shoots of grass appeared. In the summer there had not been enough flour to fatten the pigs, again she had been saved by the river Palade where among the alders on the bank grew lush dandelions. She had plucked those and steamed them together with bran and had poured this in the troughs before the piglets. Karlis\' fine hands had been of little help with such jobs. Living, as he had been in the world, he had little knowledge whether at times . she had any food to put before the workers. Through those many summers her legs had been raw, as if scalded, her palms cracked, her fingers stiff, her cheeks darkened and her body bent before time, like the old wild apple tree behind the house in the hop field. Thirty years had gone by treading the Grivas paths, but there had been no gain. Juste hid her gnarled fingers in the folds of her skirt, as if protecting them from the gaze of an invisible crowd. Her thoughts continued to pry into separate parts of her past life. Her husband had spent those restless years without as much as a beating, but during the war he had begun to sicken, had grown weaker and weaker for a few years and then died. He had not seen his daughters grow up and he had not seen the realisation of his work, the founding of the state or the independence. Neither had he seen his eldest daughter wander off to a foreign country, where honest hard work was laughed at. However, the younger and the youngest daughters had remained at Grivas. These had been Juste\'s happiest times but no sooner had they recuperated and started to live than her younger daughter took ill and withered like a broken branch. In the first year of the free state she ceased her short life in just such a spring as her father. Juste had then been left with only her youngest daughter Narda, who grew and flourished like a lush plant. But lush plants quickly overgrow and fall, overfull with juices. Narda had turned her back on Grivas and gone to live in Riga when still a very young girl. Juste hadn\'t seen her home to this day. Juste clenched her hands and her deeply sunken eves directed their attention to the log walls of the room. A life had been spent without ease. The fields of Grivas were now well-kept and even, as if they had been swept clean with a brush. A cowherd could not find a stone there to put in his catapult. The marsh had been drained into the river and instead of the grass of parnassus, white clover now grew there. There was plenty of everything at Grivas, but the plenty could not dispel the coldness in her heart. One could not take earth on one\'s lap and hug it like a small child. Everyone who walked the Grivas land was a stranger to her heart. She sighed deeply and stood up. She looked at the time-piece: midnight was near, there were only a few short hours left for sleep. Juste uncovered her bed; it was also the bed of her youth and used to her bones. By the opposite wall stood the two empty beds of her daughters. The blankets were fading and the straw in the mattresses had flattened, but Juste had not the heart to do away with them. Finally she blew out the light and crept under her blankets. Her eyes glued shut as soon as her head touched the pillow. She saw a strange dream. Narda was wading across the river towards this side. The water was bubbling and turning around her. She had nearly readied the bank when a whirlpool seized her and pulled her under. She emerged momentarily, shouting: "Mother, mother!" and then disappeared again. Juste woke up. There was no sign of the life-like vision, but the dog was barking outside. After a while its barking changed to joyful whining and then there was silence. Juste listened. That could not be a stranger out there. Perhaps Jekis was feeding the horses. She calmed down and relaxed. Juste was very seldom troubled by dreams. Sleep, like a cool drink in the heat of midday, was always refreshing. Yet, a vision like that in sleep could not be a sign of anything good. But one could not start interpreting dreams in the middle of the night, one had to sleep. She turned towards the wall and tried to doze off. Her eyes were falling shut again, but didn\'t quite manage, when someone was knocking on the window. Juste jumped up and holding her breath stared at the darkness. The distance was too much, she couldn\'t even see a shadow outside. When she heard the second rattling at the panshe got up and crept towards the window. In the darkness she could make out the shapes of three apple trees, but no human likeness was visible. "Who is knocking there?" she asked, holding her petticoat tightly around her chest. "It\'s me, mother! Let me in!" "You!?" Juste exclaimed and pulled back from the window. It was Narda, noone else. Breathing laboriously Juste whispered: "Go to the door, I will meet you and let you in." "Let me in through the window." "Honest people use the door," Juste said and turned her back to the window. "If I go through the door I\'ll wake everybody in the house," Narda tried to convince her mother. "The window has no hinges. The people are in their first sleep—they won\'t hear," Juste answered rather abruptly. She groped her way as far as the door and drew back the bolt. Hiding in the darkness of the walls came Narda. She quickly stepped over the threshold and hid behind her mother\'s back. Juste closed the door, took her daughter by the hand and pulled her towards her room. "Mother, bolt the door, someone might come in!" "it\'ll be morning soon—the bolt is there only after a fashion." Narda didn\'t reply, only her steps became more wary. In her mother\'s room she was overcome by a strange sensation. The breath of childhood lingered there, among all the old and familiar things. The beds, the wardrobe, the table, the bookshelf and both chairs stood in the same places as they had stood five years ago. "Sit on the bed while I light the lamp," Juste said softly, searching for matches. "No, mother, let\'s sit in the darkness for a bit." Narda made her mother sit on the bed beside her. Juste was looking at her daughter askance. "Child, how did you get here so late? The train from Riga arrives while it\'s still daylight. You could have let me know you were coming, I would have gone to meet you. To cross the river at this time isn\'t safe. The planks are half submerged." "I got across quite easily and I\'ll get back all right too." "Back? Surely not now—this night?" "I only came in for half-an-hour. Before it is light I have to get back to the station." "Just as if you\'d brought the fire!" Juste exclaimed, panting. "It\'s true, mother, I brought fire, that\'s why I have to get back quickly." Narda replied with a strange laugh. Darkness hid her face and her agitated expression but Juste was troubled by her daughter\'s dark laughter and strange words. She moved closer to her and touched her clothes—the edge of Narda\'s skirt was wet and her feet were soaking. She withdrew, exclaiming: Daughter, daughter! What roads have you been travelling? Take off your coat and let me dry your stockings and shoes!" Juste got up and began to grope for her own clothes. "My shoes and stockings will dry in the train. Better give me an old blanket to cover myself." Narda rejected her mother\'s concern. She didn\'t dare to weaken and to accept even the smallest thing. Strong webs of memories had pulled towards the old house, now she sat there as if dazed and stared in the darkness, when she ought to have gone on. Her mother\'s breathing was troubled. It wasn\'t the breathing of a happy person. Was there suspicion in her heart? No, no! Mother couldn\'t possibly know. Narda sat with her head bent. Mother was showing the same concern as before, but that wasn\'t what she had expected. Narda would have felt better for one good word. Juste pulled on her dress and sat down at the table. The joy of seeing her daughter again had shrunk and hidden itself in the corners of the room. Narda had arrived empty-handed and only bad people wandered around in the middle of the night. "Nobody travels a righteous road in the night. You have arrived empty-handed. Tell me where you have been and what you have done?" she asked. Narda was silent for a while. But for the darkness Juste might have seen that her daughter\'s mouth was trembling and that there was a salty haze in her eyes. "On my way here my hands were not empty. I emptied them along the way." Xarda lifted her empty palms towards her mother. "If you hide your deeds, then I shall ask no more. The train will soon be coming, if you want to get to the station in time, then you will have to go. Which blanket do you want. Yours?" "Give me an old one! I only need it for the little way to the station." Juste opened the wardrobe and raked about amongst the clothes. At last she found a soft woolen plaid and placed it around Narda\'s shoulders. "If you think it unworthy, throw it away! It is yours. I shall go with you as far as the river—but I can\'t go any farther." Her mother\'s determination surprised Narda. She wanted to refuse her, but her lips, to spite her, said something quite different: "All right, mother! But do put on something warm. The night is quite cold." "I\'ll just wrap this old plaid around me. Then I\'ll be warm enough," said Juste. They huddled close together and went out into the yard. The night was turning towards morning, but the darkness was still intense, only when their eyes got used to it they could see the path. "Are you going straight across the river?" asked Juste. "Yes." They turned the corner of the byre towards the river. The road, which was green in the summer, was still black and damp. It was rugged and uneven and the occasional holes divided Narda from her mother. Their two shapes were silhouetted against the darkness of the forest. Narda grasped her mother\'s hand. Through all her experiences and adventures her childhood glittered as if it had been lived only yesterday. Beyond the fields bubbled the river. In the spring, as soon as the ice had gone, she had run to the river many times • during the day. And so—all through the summer. Her playmates had been the cowherd or Mirte from Lieknu farm. On the river bank she had found her first primroses and violets, later alders bloomed there and nightingales sang through the nights. On the banks of the Palade they had run wild like foals in spring when the school broke up. What racing there had been, what shouting! Then the whole world had been confined to Grivas and its boundary had been the horizon. At first, when in Riga, she had felt that all that was lovely had been left behind in Grivas. This feeling had lasted for years. Her thoughts were travelling the paths of her early youth and Narda had not noticed that the road had ended and that they were now crossing the meadow. Her foot caught on a mound and the rhythm of her steps and thoughts dispersed. She held on tighter to her mother\'s hand and let her lead the way. Only now she became aware of the roughness of her mother\'s hand. Turning her face towards her, she asked: "When are you going to stop working so hard?" "When you return home." "I shall not return. Grivas doesn\'t mean anything to me, don\'t save them for me!" "You are full of alien knowledge. Bread does not grow without sowing. I don\'t belong anywhere else except here!" Juste\'s reply was bitter. She did not like her daughter\'s words. It became difficult walking hand in hand and their conversation was strained and uneas\\. Narda let go of her mother\'s hand and walked behind her. She glanced around anxiously at the slightest sound and wrapped herself more tightly in her plaid. Noone must know of her visit this night. "Mother, don\'t tell anyone I came to see you tonight," Narda whispered. "1 shall not go around Grivas shouting, if your movements have to be so secret. Jekis is a light sleeper, he will probably have heard," replied Juste, carefully treading the path before her. "If only Jekis knows, then it does not matter so much. I haven\'t taken anybody\'s life." Juste\'s legs became unsteady. She stopped in the middle of the path and stared at her daughter\'s face. "You could have stayed away," she said, sharply. Narda stepped back a pace. No, she should not have come and shown herself to mother, but as she was opposite the Grivas road, it was as if strong hands had pulled her towards the house. The dog had recognized her immediately and brushed against her whining. She couldn\'t have borne leaving without seeing her mother. "We\'ll have to go, the train will soon be in," said Narda. Juste began to walk. Damp coldness was blowing from the tassels of her plaid. In silence Narda walked behind her. In the darkness ahead the river gurgled. The sound quieted her. At the river they would part and go each in their own direction without having found a mutual understanding. The path swiftly inclined downwards. Juste first, then Narda stopped at the water\'s edge. Above the dark water they could see the planks of a foot-bridge swaying. Narda took off her plaid and gave it back to her mother. "Give me the old one, mother. It would be a pity to discard this new one at the road-side." "Keep it, it\'s made of wool from our own sheep, it should be warmer than a bought one," said Juste, pretending not to have heard the bit about throwing it away. Narda wrapped the plaid tightly around her body. The old one would have made her look older and more unrecognizable, but she could not oppose her mother\'s will. "Farewell, mother, and don\'t think of me with bitterness." Narda bent forward and kissed her on the cheek. Juste shivered. Her daughter\'s lips were as cold as ice. But she lifted her roughened hands and caressed N\'arda\'s cheeks. "Go, if you have to go. You are my one and only, how could I forbid you?" Narda tore herself away and stepped on the planks. Having reached the other side she waved and then disappeared behind the alder bushes. Juste stood at the bridgehead like a dead tree stump. Cold shivers ran over her back, like insects. She had seen off all her loved ones. Her husband and her second daughter she had seen off to the cemtery, her oldest daughter to unknown foreign lands, her youngest to the capital. All that was left was Grivas. Grivas had taken away all her strength, but they took the place of a husband and three daughters. Narda had talked nonsense by asking her to stop working. It was like asking her to stop living. Juste was still staring towards the bushes across the river, but only darkness lay there. When she finally heard the train, she wrapped the plaid tighter around her shoulders and turned her back towards the planks. In vain she waited and deluded herself. The girl had other roads to travel. (iroping along the path with her feet she went back. The darkness was becoming less dense. A faint redness in the east proclaimed the morning. As if by magic the birds came to life. They twittered and sang each its own song. Dew hung over the grass and behind her Juste left a long dark trail. II From the region of the forest the sun stepped out and covered the world with a rosy glitter. Juste came into the yard. In the garden and in the trees the birds sang just as vigorously as they had done by the river. Juste sat down in front of the porch. The night was away and the day brought work and release. It was good to know that it was Sunday. The women would help her milk the cows and then they would return to their beds, she would be left alone, she would not need to share her thoughts with anyone. She sat there until the sun rose above the appletrees, then she went to call the maid and the cowherd. While milking, the calmness of the animals dispersed the last of the ill-feeling. Slowly the night visitor departed from her mind. Juste was straining the milk and giving orders to the cowherd and the dog in a loud voice. Jekis, having harnessed the horse, lifted milk cans into the cart and drove off to the dairy. The maid and the cowherd went to have their second sleep and Juste returned to the warm byre alone. The quiet chewing of the animals came from all corners and rose towards the beams and the empty swallow nests. Soon these agile birds would return and occupy their empty nests. The cattle would go out to their green pastures and the door of the byre would remain open until autumn. Juste was stroking the back of the nearest cow, then she turned and went out. The sun was up above the elms, yet she hadn\'t even thought about breakfast. At a trot, she hastened towards the house. Soon the chimney was smoking and the pot bubbling over the fire. When she heard the wheels of the returning cart, she began to lay the table. Having lifted out the cans and unharnessed the horse, Jekis came in and was the first to sit down at the table, waiting for the others. Juste called the others, brought in a big-bellied coffee-jug and sat down at the table at last. Jekis took two hasty sips of the hot drink, then put his hand in his pocket. "Look!" he said, producing a piece of paper from his pocket, "the roads are strewn with these leaflets this morning." He handed the leaflet to Kokis. The lad read it, then folded it up small. "Let me see it!" said Tince, stretching out her hand. "It\'s not for the likes of you!" said Kokis teasing, and hid it under the table. Juste was listening to all this with her eyes downcast, quietly eating her breakfast. A redness started to mount in her cheeks and mist clouded her eyes. How had Narda happened to be here in the night? She had said something mystically about having come with her hands full. Was not she. .. ? Suspicions about her daughter multiplied in her mind. She lifted up her head and quietly demanded: "What sort of leaflet is it? Let me see it!" Kokis gave it to her and betian to eat Juste was turning the leaflet over in her fingers, unable to sit still. She took an empty dish from the table and went to the kitchen. She put down the dish and hastened to her own room, wherein, after putting on her glasses she began to decipher the small print. She couldn\'t understand the meaning of all the words. Repeatedly there was mention of the workers, work and the exploiters, the overlords, the landowners. Juste read all of the printed matter and then threw the leaflet down on the table, exclaiming: "Villains!" Her daughter\'s night-roads stretched before her eyes, black as pitch. Narda, noone else had scattered these slanderous leaflets. What did she know about work, having grown up in great freedom ? She had never held the cowherd\'s switch in her hands, she did not know how sweet is the early morning sleep for a child who has to get up. She had never lifted a strand of meadow grass in the fields of Grivas, she did not know how to work for bread! But she knew how to write and how to talk! Juste squared her shoulders and lifted her head. Such words could only have the value or a mosquito bite. Grabbing the leaflet she crumpled it up and threw it in the flames of the kitchen stove. With her head held high she went to clear the table. Only Jekis had remained sitting, smoking his pipe. "Same sort of joke happened five years ago," said Jekis, "now again we have some mischief-makers tramping in the vicinity." "Are you still blabbering about the same thing? Makes me sick to listen to it!" Juste gave her brother-in-law an angry look. Jekis had read the leaflet on his way home from the dairy, but was that any reason why Juste had to be cross with him ? At the dairy nearly everyone had had a leaflet in his hands. Then he remembered the noise he had heard in the night and asked: "Who disturbed you last night?" Juste\'s hands stumbled against the edge of the table, a cup fell to the floor and broke. While picking up the broken pieces she was trying to think of a reply. She couldn\'t tell the truth — Jekis\' mouth wasn\'t locked with two keys. She threw the pieces away and said: "The dog was barking furiously, so I went out to see what\'s up. Saw a man standing in the yard. When he saw me he came up and asked the way to the station. I showed him and he went." Juste lied. Her eyes were averted and her voice did not carry the necessary conviction. Jekis was looking at her for a long while, shaking his head in disbelief. The dog had barked all right, but it hadn\'t been a furious bark and it had stopped soon. He had wanted to get up and investigate, but Juste had beaten him to it. He had heard no stranger\'s voice in the yard, yet his ears were good. Later he seemed to have heard voices in Juste\'s room, but sleep had soon overcome him. Juste was hiding something, her manner and gait revealed that. "Ach, we should have detained him! How could anyone not knowing the way wander into our house on such a night?" Jekis grumbled and hastily rose to discuss this news with Kokis. Juste\'s limbs relaxed, as if they had become untied. Jekis hadn\'t recognized the night visitor. She would be able to walk among her people with her head held high. No one would be able to point his finger at her. 11l People talked this way and that, but not being able to shed any light on the matter they soon discarded it. No one had seen any strangers walking about on that night. Juste went about with her head held high. Jekis hadn\'t revealed to anyone that she might have been lying. Narda had managed to sneak away unrecognized and unseen. After all, she had a right to visit Grivas in the middle of the night or noon if she pleased, it wasn\'t anybody else\'s business. For days and weeks Juste hadn\'t had much time to devote to thoughts of her daughter. Spring was advancing with great urgency and one job chased another. Having finished all the work in the house, Juste hastened to help in the fields. In the evenings she felt exhausted. She neither felt nor saw how the apple trees came to bloom, she did not hear how the nightingales began to sing on the banks of the river. The sun rose earlier every morning and set later every evening. Sitting at the table with her people she listened to their talk, but their conversation concerned only work and what went on in the near neighborhood. The night\'s events had submerged into forgetfulness. At noon Juste looked up at the sky and noticed the liveliness of spring all around her, but her heart did not warm with joy. During the afternoon her hands hurried over work and hard lines had set in her face. Days and weeks flowed on ahead, like the current of the river. The summer swelled, havings drunk its fill of the sun. One morning, having just returned from the dairy, Jekis stopped at the head of the cabbage furrow, where Juste was pulling up the fat weeds, and asked: "Aren\'t you coming to the house?" Juste\'s eyebrows shot up, but she did not lift her head as she replied: "If you have something to say, you can say it here." Jekis went up to her and placed his hand on her shoulder, but said no more. His strong calm won her over. She threw down the weeds, shook her apron and turned to go to the house. In the big room she sat down on Jekis\' bed and stared in front of her. "Here, read this!" Jekis placed a newspaper on her lap. "Can\'t read it without my glasses." Jekis hurriedly went to the other room and brought her glasses. "Read here!" He put his finger to a long column. Juste read, then stopped. It was an account of how the city people spent their weekends and holidays at the seaside, Forest Park and other places. "What\'s that to do with me? They can do as they like in Riga. I don\'t have holidays or Sundays," she said sulkily and let the paper drop on her lap. "If you don\'t want to read it, I\'m not going to force you. They live in all manners of ways in Riga, but among them is also your daughter, and it is about her r they w rite in this paper," Jekis said, not retreating a step. Juste bit her lips, then began to read again. With a great deal of determination she began to understand what she was reading. The article said that several men and women had been charged with antistate activities. Among them was also ... her daughter. Darkness drew over her eyes, her hands slid down by her sides and the paper fell under the bed. Jekis picked up the paper and left the room on tiptoes. Juste neither saw him nor heard him. Her head felt hot and painful. Narda, her sweet little girl, in prison! At home she had loved and spared every animal, every ladybird. At school she had been more advanced than the rest. But now? No sigh, no groan left Juste\'s lips. She remembered the spring night and hard spite circled her head. Her husband had fought for the freedom and honor of his country, and now his own daughter was opposing him with different ideals. Juste\'s eyes were burning, but no tears appeared. An empty heart was more painful than anything else. The sound of a banged door pulled her up on her feet. There was no one in the room. Sunrays played on the floor and flies buzzed around the white-scrubbed table. The sounds of day pushed her sharp thoughts to one side. Cares of the house sounded in her ears and forbade her to think. Only Grivas was left to her now and because of Grivas all else had to be let go over her head. She went to the kitchen, washed her eyes in cold water, then went out in the yard. In front of the granary Jekis and Kokis were lifting sacks of grain on to the cart. They were getting ready to go to the mill. The last of the flour had already been used up. "Cover up the load, it might rain!" Juste called out loudly. Jekis looked at her and shook his head slowly. How little strength remained in her, only her great will kept her going. The men covered the loads and drove away. Juste walked back and forth across the yard, not knowing what she was looking for. She couldn\'t think of a single job that might occupy her hands and her mind. At noon, when she was milking the cows, tears streamed into the bucket together with milk. She wasn\'t crying because she pitied her daughter, for those that had earned their sentence of judgement were not to be pitied. She was sorry for those who lay in the cemetery. The ways of her daughters might have turned out differently if their father had been there to stand by them with his advice. She had been too busy with work to watch Narda closely, when she should have shown more determination and used stronger words in her upbringing. Now she was empty-handed and would remain empty-handed. Juste finished milking and came out of the byre with reddened, but clearer eyes. She had reached an understanding with herself and would be readier to withstand any attacks made on her by others. Within a couple of days the whole parish was talking and the words buzzed and stung her like wasps. Juste did not feel the pain of these stings. She had never encouraged or supported her daughter in doing wrong. She also couldn\'t set up herself as her daughter\'s first judge. Meeting with Juste\'s calmness the rumors flattened like smoke in heavy weather. The gossipers\' tongues soon found other sources for discourse. The whole summer was spent in endless haste and hurry. Nights were spent in deathlike sleep, which neither dreams nor nightmares could disturb. With the first frost Juste received a letter from Narda. A neighbor, coming from the dairy one morning, brought it and gave it to Juste. "Here\'s a letter for you. Perhaps it\'s from your daughter. When you\'ve read it, tell us how she\'s getting on." He handed over the letter, then quickly turned his horse and drove away. From the granary came Jekis. "A letter, is it?" he asked. Juste\'s fingers were not used to handling paper, they trembled and slithered across the white envelope. At last she managed to get it open. "What\'s she writing?" Jekis couldn\'t contain his curiosity. "What could she write from a place like that?" Juste replied sharply and went to her room. Jekis stamped his feet for a while, then went. It couldn\'t be a good letter if his sister-in-law gave him such an icy answer. Reading the letter, Juste\'s lips narrowed even more. Her daughter wasn\'t troubled by shame or regret. Each word in her letter was as if hewn out of granite. Narda asked for woollen stockings, towels and sheets. Juste hid the letter in her pocket and on meeting Jekis again, she never mentioned the letter or her daughter. In her spare time she looked over the clothes in the wardrobe and tied up a few of them in a bundle. At the end of the week she ordered Jekis to harness the horse, then took the bundle and drove off to the post office. On her return she took off her best clothes and went about her work as usual —unfeeling, like a dead tree. She had responded to her daughter\'s request. The linen had been made of flax grown in their own fields, the woolens made of wool from the backs of their own sheep. And all the clothes had been her own handwork. These things weighed more in value than printed matter or words. She had not said a good word to her daughter when they had met, and she had said none now. Good words could not be picked up like pebbles on the river-bank. Narda herself had helped to demolish her feelings. She had built up Grivas with the help of strangers, she would take her last refreshment from the hands of strangers when her strength had failed. Amidst people Juste walked with her head held high. If anyone asked her about her daughter, she always had an answer ready. There were no round-about answers or lies. Because of her daughter she did not hide behind other people\'s backs. She bent her knees only on entering the church. (Translated by Monika Zariņa) Ēriks Dzenis — "The Pause" — Pastel P. Polna — "The Old Troll" — Bronze K. Langenfelds — "Still Life" — Oil A. Lieven A MAP OF LIVONIA', 'A short story by In his days as a sailor, Rudis had acquired a slightly flowery mode of speech and a big brown walking-stick with the heads of various tropical animals—tigers, lions, elephants, and hyenas—carved on the handle. The best policy, if Rudis happened to be carrying his stick, was to go on admiring it. Then he would slowly get into a mellow mood and begin to talk of his own accord. This is the story he would tell: "It all began at Le Havre. We had arrived there with a cargo of timber. It must have been about fifteen years ago. Until then I never had got an\\ farther than the Baltic sea-ports. The Stick from Le Havre KNUTS LESINS We went ashore in the "evening, the youngest mate and I, just to have a look around and see what there was and where, and what it was like. After a while we dropped anchor in some restaurant. It wasn\'t a had place. In fact, it looked quite decent, what with the tablecloths and menu cards, and there was a green parrot who perched behind the lady at the counter. Our walk through the town had made us hungry, and we began to study the menu. Hut one cannot, just at one go, make out those French dishes. The mate took a chance and ordered a dish with a long name. I thought it safer to drink my beer and wait to see what happened to him. Well, a number of dishes as small as thimbles were put before him, filled with all ssorts of colored little leaves and berries like those which the ladies wear on their summer hats, and each little dish had a different kind of sauce. The mate tasted one, then another, made a grimace and declared: \'The name of this dish happens to have been too long.\' \'Now roast pork or chops with cabbage—that would really be something!\' I remarked. \'It\'s a wonder to me what good ideas you sometimes can get, Rudis,\' he said with a sigh. \'If only we could explain to these devils what we want!" We called the waiter and started to explain. But the waiter, a black man, scarcely knew three words of English or German, and we knew only about three words of French. Then the mate began to grunt like a pig. At last the black fellow understood, but not the way we had meant it. He seemed to think that we were calling him a pig, and he burst into such a torrent of French that we couldn\'t get in a word edgeways. Now the mate became really angry and shouted back at him in Latvian. I may put it on record that they expressed their innermost thoughts with great frankness even though they did not understand each other. They grew so heated that the other customers began to prick up their ears and look in our direction. I saw that things were not going to end well. Therefore I took out a pencil and drew a picture of a pig on the back of the menu card; then I pulled the black man by the hand to attract his attention and stabbed at the picture with my knife. Oh my goodness, he got into a terrible frenzy. He must have thought I was threatening to kill him. All hell broke loose, and there was such a noise that the chef came running from the kitchen. Luckily he spoke a little English, and at last we could explain about what we wanted and what it was all about. The black also cheered up, and his smile expanded like a lemon-blossom. After half an hour we got our roast pork. It wasn\'t served the way we were used to at home, there was no sauerkraut with it, but it tasted all right. The mate had calmed down, and now he started to lecture me. "Everything you did was right, Rudis," he said. "But you made a mistake when you jabbed your knife at that picture of the pig. If you had prodded it with a fork the waiter would have understood you. Straight away it would all have been comme ci, en mine ca, bonjourf You\'ve yet to learn the finer touches, that\'s what it is." I did not say anything though I should have liked to remind him of that dainty dish he had ordered for himself, the one with the long name. But he had reached the brandy-drinking stage, and as he knew all there is to know about French cognac I took care not to annoy him. The trouble is that they serve brandy by the glass in that country. A few glassfuls obviously don\'t get a fellow anywhere, and we sat there with a delicate flavor in our mouths as if we had each swallowed a yellow rose. We asked the waiter to bring us a whole bottle of brandy. The black man blinked a little, but he brought the bottle when the mate said "Grand" and raised his hand from the table to the height of his nose, to show the size he meant. We sat there for quite a while. Having taken in our cargo, as it were, we felt like going full steam ahead. People had started to dance, and we watched them and wondered whether we should also take a turn \'round the floor. Just then, the band struck up really good and loud. But the mate gave a start as if he had sat down on a sharp nail. \'Listen to them, Rudis! They\'re playing our national anthem!" I listened with all my ears. Yes, it sounded a bit like our national anthem, perhaps not exactly the same tune — I don\'t remember how\' it went, but it seemed to be Something in that line. \'Perhaps that\'s in our honor,\' I suggested. \'Honor?\' the mate spluttered with rage. \'Don\'t you see, those devils are dancing to our national anthem!\' He crashed his fist down on the table and shouted at the musicians to stop. But they only smiled and blew their instruments louder than ever. Naturally, we could not put up with such an insult. The mate raised his glass and smashed it on the floor, and then we both stood up and stuck out our chests as though it were Confirmation Day. The mate jumped on a chair, brandishing his fists, and roared as if he were standing on the captain\'s bridge: \'Come on, lads! Aren\'t there  Latvians here to lend a hand?\' We had been sitting near the entrance; the door stood open, and the mate could easily be heard by anyone outside. You\'ll never guess what happened ! There was an answering call from the street, and a voice shouted in pure Latvian: \'Of course there are! How many do you want?\' It is really strange how such things happen. Later we found out that another Latvian ship had docked at Le Havre; it had already been there for several days, and ten of the sailors hap]>ened to be walking past the restaurant when the mate stood on the chair, shouting. At the first moment, he was struck dumb with surprise. But he rallied and let out another roar: "Lads, they\'re making fun of our national anthem!\' And then everything happened at once. In ten minutes we smashed up the whole place. I don\'t remember how we all fared and how it came about that there were so many enemies for each of us to fight. But there are always people skulking in such places to whom an opportunity of coming to blows is just as important as pay day: they wouldn\'t let either go by! I had already pummeled the ribs and bruised the jaws of many a stranger when I caught sight of a swarthy brute of a fellow who was leaning across the table and aiming to give me a blow with a heavy stick. 1 jumped at him in the nick of time, gave him a good push in the chest and caught hold of his stick. He fell to the ground, and the stick remained in my hand—it was the one you see now, with the heads of all those wild beasts carved along the handle. Just then somebody shouted: \'Lads the police will be here at any moment! Let\'s go!\' Afterwards the mate remembered that it was only the green parrot who had been calling for the police all the time, and in such a fiendishly loud voice that some of the lads got scared. In such places the proprietor is not very anxious to call the police, and it was not for nothing that somebody had taught the parrot the right word to shout when there was a fight. We all rushed out of the restaurant. The mate and I had been fighting shoulder to shoulder, and it so happened that we left the place together. I was just going to dart off across the street when he took hold of me by the arm. \'Not like that, Rudis! They\'d catch you at once!\' He straightened his cap and sauntered along in a slow and stately manner as if he were just returning from a visit to his grandmother. \'I\'ve already told you that you\'ve yet to learn the finer touches,\' he said, but when he came to the first corner he flew round it like a jack-knife. We walked briskly along various side streets and finally came to a large boulevard. The harbor quarter lay behind us, and we could breathe more freely. Only then did I realize that blood was i running down my face and that I had a big bruise above my temple. I pressed my handkerchief to it, but the bleeding did not stop. \'Let\'s go back to the ship in a taxi,\' the mate said. \'It\'s a bad thing to loiter in such a well-lit place.\' We looked around. There was no taxi anywhere. But there were two ladies who apparently had just come out of a cafe. They were good-looking girls, and they stopped and seemed to be talking about us. The younger one had lovely eyes, just like a doe, and she looked at me with such compassion that I suddenly felt prodigiously sorry for myself. I smiled at her, and the mate and I continued on our way, but it somehow so happened that we had to walk past the girls. I was about to pass the pretty one when she said something to me. The mate tried to pull me away by the arm, saying that we must not linger and that there would be no shortage of women the next day either, or any day, for that matter. Yet, somehow or other, we began to talk to the girls. They both knewr a little German. The young one was actually a nurse; she was very anxious to help me and asked me to go with her. She lived close-by, just around the corner. I must have made a good impression on her, or else she was a brave girl. At first I hedged a little and said I did not need any bandages and the bleeding was sure to stop by itself, but she looked very grave, and we began to walk in the direction of the place where she lived. The mate no longer objected. He had taken a fancy to the other girl. She was rather plump, her lips were painted a bright red, and she had thick black hair; afterwards he told me she was the very image of his former sweetheart. Her name was Elsa. The younger one was Margot, and I fell in love with her at once. She had a small flat, just a few rooms near the hospital where she was working. I visited her the next day because my bandage had to be changed. After that, I often went to see her. In fact I met her every time I was allowed ashore. Ami that is how we got better acquainted. Such things hapj)en quickly when one is young. It is like a dry juniper bush suddenly catching fire: you are burnt to a cinder straight away! We went for walks in the evenings: sometimes I took her to a cafe or a cinema. After a few days we were simply crazy about each other. Still, I must say, women are artful creatures. Margot was a nice, affectionate girl, of a cheerful disposition, and as pretty as a sweet-pea blossom. I gave her a ring with a red stone, and she wore it every day. 1 was always thinking of running away from my ship and staying with her, or else taking her home with me. But when I told her my thoughts she seemed frightened. She said I must on no account jump ship; on the contrary, I ought to go home in it pretty soon. In the meantime, she said, we were sure to think of something clever, and all our problems would be solved. Elsa, who was distantly related to Margot, owned a small tobacco store. The mate often went there, and he bought all sorts of pipes and tried out a great many kinds of tobacco. But one day he came running back to the ship like a scalded cat. He said there was nothing to that girl at all, the devil take her; but he refused to explain what he meant. I was so busy thinking of Margot that I did not pay much attention to him and his troubles. On the day our ship left, Margot came to the harbor with a bunch of flowers. She stood at the quay side and waved her handkerchief as long as I could catch a glimpse of her. Truly, she was a nice, affectionate girl. When the mate saw how much I was in love he tried to comfort me. I should not give way to grief, he said ; with a woman, it\'s the same as with a doubleedged knife: of you dont get her there\'s tragedy in your heart, and if you get her, there\'s hell in your house. He told me that he himself had luckily escaped from both these misfortunes. One morn he had arrived at Elsa\'s apartment earlier than they had arranged. He wanted to tell her that he was busy and could not keep their appointment; besides, he was anxious to make a date for the following day. Elsa was still in bed, asleep. Her little sister let him in, and the stupid child straight away opened the bedroom door to him. He caught sight of Elsa lying in bed, completely bald; her wonderful hair, it appeared, had been nothing but a wig. When Elsa woke up and saw him standing there she screamed, and then it became clear that she had not a tooth in her mouth; her teeth had been as false as her hair. At the sight of all this, the mate was overcome by such terror that he flung himself out of the house without even saying good-bye, and never stopped running until he came to the nearest bar. I here he fell into a chair and poured every available drink down his throat, first all the French and then all the English ones, in order to recover his equilibrium. \'Well, you\'ve yet to learn those finer touches,\' I said, laughing heartily. I could afford to be amused because everything my Margot had, visible or otherwise, was real enough. Besides, I thought I knew all the secrets of her heart, too. 1 reached home, but I still did not know how I might manage to marry her. Our next voyage was to Murmansk. When I returned to Riga my contract had come to an end, and it took me a long time to find a ship that was bound for Le Havre. 1 regularly wrote to Margot, and I received a few letters from her in which she swore she would be waiting for me. Seven months had passed when I finally got to Le Havre again, but this time the ship was to dock there for several weeks. I had saved some money, and my plan was to marry Margot and then to work hard for another six or seven months in order to earn some more money. After that, I was going to take Margot home with me. I was a mechanic, and it would have been easy enough for me to find work on some local steamer; I might even have found a job on shore. Yes, I had thought it all over and had explored the various possibilities. All night, while the ship was slowly drawing nearer to Le Havre, I kept thinking of Margot and how I was going to caress her pretty little face the next morning. But it so happened that I was not allowed ashore in the morning. Later in the day the captain received a telegram: his wife had given birth to twins both of them boys. Such an important event had to be celebrated, and we all drank large quantities of rum and brandy. It was late in the evening when I at last stepped ashore. I had brought an amber necklace for Margot from Riga, and now I quickly bought a bunch of flowers and rushed off to find her. She was not at home. 1 thought she might be on night duty and went to the hospital. She had a room there; I knew it well because I had often visited her there before. Perhaps all the rum I had drunk and the joy of seeing her again had gone to my head and I was noisier than I ought to have been. Anyway, at the first moment Margot let me fee that she did not like the way I had suddenly rushed into her room. But she relented at once when she saw how sorry I Was. I apologized for having been inconsiderate, and she kissed and caressed me and said I had come at an awkward moment. We were going to have a nice chat later on, she would ask one of the other nun-e; to do night duty instead of her. Just now, though, she was expecting the doctor to call in; he was doing his rounds, and it would be a bad thing if he saw me. In fact, he might arrive at any moment. If I left the room I might even run into him at the very door, or else I might lose my way in all those corridors. She seemed terribly worried, trying to think of where she might hide me. Finally, after listening for footsteps in the passage, she led me to a door. \'It will be a bit dark in there,\' she said. \'But stand close to the door, don\'t move, and keep very quiet. I\'ll let you out when the coast is clear. You\'ll have to wait for about ten minutes.\' She quickly opened the door and pushed me into a dark room. It was so dark in there that I could not even see the buttons on my jacket. Besides, it was rather cold, but the air did not seem very fresh, somehow. Still, one has to do what the circumstances demand, and I stood in the darkness and listened. I heard Margot\'s voice; she was talking to somebody. A man\'s footsteps receded into the distance, and then there was silence. I stood there for quite a while, thinking of nothing in particular. But a sailor isn\'t an inanimate object, like a bottle, which keeps standing where it has been put down, content to know neither what time it is nor what course the ship is taking. I shuffled my feet a little; nothing dreadful happened as a consequence. Then I began to wonder why Margot had especially told me to keep close to the door. I thought 1 might at least smoke a cigarette, and I turned slightly in order to get the pack of cigarettes out of my pocket. But my hand touched something that felt like a sheet, and my fingers brushed across somebody\'s hair and then—a nose! My heart gave a jump, I spread out my fingers and could fell the outline of somebody\'s head, but it was ice cold, like the head of a corpse! My drunkenness evaporated like the morning dew. Was it possible that she had pushed me into the mortuary? Was that why she had told me not to move and keep close to the door? I have never in my life been afraid of any ghosts, black fellows or Chinamen, but at that moment the blood really seemed to freeze in my veins. My only thought was to get out of that place at once, but in my panic I mistook the direction and collided with some wooden post; I stumbled, my hands shot forward for support, and I found myself clutching at yet another corpse. I sufficiently  to my senses to realize that it was no use floundering about in the dark. Squatting down on the floor, 1 lit my cigarette lighter. Of course! Just as I had expected! It was the mortuary all right, dead bodies lying all around me, with bandaged heads, or with an arm or a leg encased in plaster. Margot was working in the surgical ward, and not a corpse here was without some defect. A glance or two were enough for me, and I made for the door, come what may. But when I cautiously opened it 1 caught sight of something equally stupefying: to the left, behind a half-open glass door at the end of the passage, a bespectacled man was holding Margot in a tight embrace and kissing her as fervently as I always did when saying good-bye to her. At first I wanted to rush out of my hiding place and knock that villain dowm, but then I saw that Margot was readily submitting to his caresses and whispering something in his ear, probably telling him it was time to part. I quickly closed my door. So that\'s how things are, I thought. All my fear was gone. I gritted my teeth and stood in the mortuary, prepared to wait until Margot came to let me out. Soon I heard her footsteps. She quietly opened the door and said in a low voice: \'You can come out now. There is nobody there.\' I stepped into the passage and stood before her, trying hard to keep calm but probably looking rather odd. \'Has anything happened to you, my darling?\' she asked anxiously. \'Did you see something in there?\' \'Nothing has happened to me, but you might have taken the trouble to find a place where I could have passed the time more pleasantly.\' \'I told you to stand quietly behind the door,\' she said angrily. \'There was nowhere else to hide you in such a hurry. \'I he doctor might have come at any moment. Besides, you\'re a brave young sailor, and I thought it wouldn\'t do you any harm except to cool off your head a little!\' She tried to smile. \'It hasn\'t done me any harm, and my head is as cool as can be. I stood there perfectly quiet while you were exchanging kisses out there—with the doctor, I presume!" Her lips began to tremble. \'No, it isn\'t true. .. . Anyway, you shouldn\'t have looked. He\'s a relative of mine, we hadn\'t seen each other for a long time....\' \'Is that so?" I snarled. \'Perhaps he\'s the same kind of relative as I am, and he\'s been away exactly as long as I have? Well, may you both be happy!\' I pulled my cap firmly over my eyes and strode away. She called after me, apparently bursting into tears, but I paid no attention. I never tried to find her again, and she made no move to contact me either. But she had been a nice, affectionate girl, and in later years when I had long got over my disappointment, I always thought kindly of her and wished her the best of luck. Translated from the Latvian by RUTH SPEIRS', 'Janis Ezerins I was born at dawn at three o\'clock in the morning. About five o\'clock I left my native place and approached the lobe of a dog\'s ear. The dog was lying on a mat beside the bed of his mistress. On the way to the mat I had my first little dance on the shining floor of inlaid wood. When I was tired of dancing I decided to return to my former isolation. By the time I was ready to leave my quiet hiding-place everything in the room had changed— I saw neither the mat nor the dog; an old woman only was wiping the windows. There could be no doubt as to who this was — it was the caretaker who did not seem to have had much joy in her life. I wanted to cheer the woman, therefore, I crossed the room carefully, jumped on to the window-sill with the intention of showing her one of my loveliest dances. But I was able to take one jump only — the old woman screeched wildly and threw a rag at me. At this moment the door from the adjoining room opened and I recognized the same young lady who had been lying in bed a few hours ago. "What are you screaming for, Annusch?" she inquired. "Dear young lady, I dare not tell you, I saw." "Wel£ what did you see?" "A flea!" This announcement left a shattering impression on the young lady. I inferred this from the fact that she nearly dismissed the old servant, told her to give the dog a bath and only then returned to her place at the breakfast table in the next „ room. But even after breakfast she kept walking about with a disturbed air, complained about the unusual dirt and examined all the corners with suspicion. Much self-control was required on my part, so as not to jump straight into her eyes, for I was a clean little creature, just born. Oh, what conceit! I had more cause to have my doubts about her innocence than for her to have doubts about mine and still she was making a row. All this only showed that all actions towards our black tribe are guided by a feeling of dark hatred which can in no way be justified; it is because of this hatred that during a time of two hours I did not meet a single of my brothers or sisters in any of the rooms. Why is it that our slight small nation calls forth such disdain and such injustice? Is it because we are small and black and the others are large and white? That we can dance and the others cannot even crawl? That under a wet rag we do not even stumble but that others easily die under a heel? Oh, all this may be true. I see that these questions have not even been solved and that there is still no goodwill among living creatures. TOLD BY A FLEA A Short Story In the fight for existence in this world the fools rush about, curse, lose their tempers and even kill themselves. See yourself! The same young lady destroyed a hundred thousand spawn as caviar but trembled lest I should take one milligramme of her blood. Is this noble? Is this clever? No this is neither noble, nor clever. This is simply despising life. Should I therefore have a feeling of respect for a human being? No, no. I followed in the foodsteps of the young lady, so as to be able to prove the contrary. She was still woried about the dirt in her flat. At a moment which seemed the right moment to me — the capricious human being was now sitting on a stool before the piano and had started playing a jolly tune — I said in my own language: "Please, play, young lady, and I will, dance. This music was written for me." You should have seen what happened the moment I appeared on the white keys. The creature started as if stung, tried to catch me with her hands, moved wildly about — my small heart was burning with pleasure. It is clear that excitement is a part of human nature. What virtue, what philosophic virtue! What a wonderful museum of habits the nature of human beings is! At last she called to the old servant for help, for it seemed to her that there were a hundred fleas jumping about, whereas it was only I that was turning somersaults on the shining floor. "Annusch, to-morrow you can look for another mistress! Look at this yourself, black fountains are shooting up into the air." Annusch hung her head, but my pursuer was still burning with anger. Then I took my most refkless jump in front of her, and I must confess I had to pay de"ar for it: the young lady crushed one of my small front legs. I was forced to withdraw into one of the corners and to cure myself for some time. From this moment I decided to take my reverenge on "her. Surely every living being must come to a decision such as this earlier or later. Such is man I I was left alone. Gradually the pains ceased and no longer kept me from leaving the crack in the floor. This crack had probably never imagined that it would have to serve as a resting-place. I walked round the drawing-room; it seemed comfortable and attractive. I paid a visit to the sofa, to the chairs, to the flower pots and stayed for a few moments even among the dried human brains which they call literature. On the shelves I had the honour of meeting several of the famous philosophers whose works were enclosed by cookery books. It appeared that in theory even I was not excluded from the list of those beings that have a right to live, some of the poets I have even inspired to write heroic poems. So ... so ... beginning with this moment my anger increased twofold. I left the bookshelf with the resolution to take revenge on the foolish hypocrisy of my pursuer who so willingly played, the air about a flea but did not wish to meet it face to face. These are human beings they are, however, not to be envied as they cannot get to the bottom of the smallest thought. Hypocrites, who love, caress and spoil only those beings whom they intend to place on a plate later on. Look, how they fondle a calf which they mean to kill for the holidays. They stroke its glossy coat and even touch its moist rosy nozzle with their lips. This is the heavenly love of which they are so proud. This is heavenly love! Thank God, that they do not bestow this love upon me. Thank God that they do not think of me as of something that can give them pleasure for their holidays. I will be able to endure their hatred better in this way. For their love is more dangerous than their hatred. I sat down on the branch of an oleander and watched two winged creatures that were approaching each other behind the window pane. Thank God that you have wings! In the vicinity of man their price is that of life. The day was lovely. My pursuer came into the room again and she had a talk with the servant. "You knew that we are going to have a visitor to-day. He cannot stand insects." Sec what a name! Insects! But as regards the visitor what else would you expect from him. Different gradations of hatred is the highest education of human beings. Hatred of God — that is their religion. They are even capable of hatred towards lifeless things: they dig into mountains with steel drills and call it technique, they destroy the forests and let the timber float down the rivers — this they call commerce.. Truly, I tell you in their hatred they are giants. The ringing of a bell could be heard. The young lady was given a letter. The very first lines seemed to surprise her pleasantly. She sat down and continued to read with still greater enthusiasm. I left the branch of oleander and moved to her shoulder. I risked doing this as we two were now alone. As far as I could guess the "Please, play, young lady, and I will dance f" letter brought her an invitation to go somewhere far far away. Her pulse began to beat quicker. She read the letter several times, then folded it and kissed it. Of course I could only wonder at her way of worshipping paper but they say that with human beings this is not unusual. Then she called happily: "Annusch!" I jumped on to the floor so as to be on the safe sid<\\ Annusch appeared in the door. "The doctor will not be here today. I am leaving for the sea-side at three o-clock. Meanwhile you will once more beat the dust from the carpets and the blankets. Do you understand?" Even I understood. Fancy, this lady was getting ready to go away. Was I going to leave her? No, no. On that very spot I decided to go with her. Surely, she could not prevent me from doing this. Quietly I crept to the leg of the young lady and began to crawl up carefully. What a warm and smooth stocking. I crawled nearly as high as her knees, but then it seemed to me that I had fallen into clouds. It appeared that this was Brussels lace among which I did not feel very much at home. I continued my way upwards. This was a difficult climb. Through folds and hems I took dangerous jumps. And the whole time I was running the risk of touching her rosy body; she would then have thrown off her rags of lace which were perfumed like the woods of Ceylon and I would again be on the floor. I was careful. Not far from her waist I began to mount the very highest snow peak of her chemise and was pleased with the wonderful view that extended itself before me. "See," I thought, "this warm and rosy valley God has given me to live in and to feed on, the same as he gave Canaan to the children of, Israel." But I did not hurry, I waited for my time to come. I only fed my eyes on what I could see and tried to make out the geography of this glowing body, its hills, forests and springs which have with such mastery been described by an Englishman in a poem of his youth "Venus and Adonis." Really, this was the most precious book on the shelves of the young lady, a real traveller\'s guide which makes it possible for a flea even to find its way among the most famous places on this earth; if I am not mistaken the book even indicates which of the places are most often frequented by tourists and which are worth frequenting. For the time being, however, I was not able to make use of these mattei-of-fact indications as I had to think of the shortest cut to a place where I would feel quite safe. At last after some pains I found a narrow path which brought me into the sunny pastures of her breasts. Here I toolc my seat in the lace of her chemise just opposite the peak of her right breast which was the colour of purple flowers. I lay quietly and did not give myself away. I waited... But while all this was taking place the young lady had not been idle. I put out my little snout through one of the many windows of the lace and saw that we were both running across meadows and fields. What a wonderful rush this was! It was a train that was carrying us. We crossed a bridge — they said it was the bridge across the river Lielupe -»-* and then we were soon there. A gentleman was waiting for us. He seemed to be a gentleman in every sense of the word. Slim, gaunt, with white gloves on his hands — he kissed the hand of my escort and then immediately our way began to wind through pines. All this 1 could watch through my window of lace. From his first words I understood that our companion was the doctor who did not like insects. "Certainly," I thought, "everybody must have some kind of an occupation. Perhaps he does not like mustard, too. The preface to the cookery book says there are people who clo not like mustard and that there are such who faint the moment they see someone put his knife into his mouth. But who can prevent them from doing this? Still, people who are as timid as this must have a hard life." Yes, at the moment the behaviour of the young man showed that he was afraid of everything except the nearness of the young lady. He spoke of having worked too much and of his nerves. He also said that for a whole week he had been taking mud baths. Mud baths! Still; it seems to me that it is worth laughing at people from time to time. As for me I will do nothing else all my life but this. Then they spoke of the sun and of the clouds. That, I think, is what is called a lofty theme. But there was nothing of interest for me in this. The only thing that I discovered was that these two were very much in love, recklessly in love and that the man had studied law, for he said; "The clouds the only free state of water... Even the senate of Rome did not pass any laws as regards the clouds." How strange! As high as the clouds human hatred is not capable of rising. But, thank God! I had no objections to this. On the whole I felt pleased with life at the moment. Even the rising and the falling of. the rosy base under me and the beating of the lady\'s heart like the paw of a dog against a carpet did not disturb me in the end. On the contrary. — I enjoyed the swaying. All around me I felt a wave of strong heat rising — but as a strong wind was blowing I did not feel hot. I forgot the injustice I had suffered — one thing only I could not forget: why was there no other flea here whose little snout I could touch and with whom we could spend some pleasant moments together. Would it not have been possible for things to be like this? But they were not. And were not because of the caprice of this woman. A caprice — which she so stubbornly called an aristocratic way of Hying, comfort, decency — a hundred nice but empty words. And again I felt a wave of anger rising; I even remembered that I had not eaten anything all day long. See ... now they walked along silently, they were holding each other\'s hands. They stayed a very short time at the sea-side, then they turned back again to the dunes, walked on for some time till they came to a garden where for money you can get food and drinks — things which God has given the world for nothing. They ordered their meal at last and a bottle or wine. And then all three of us began to have our dinner. I jumped in the direction of my companion\'s shoulder and waited for her to take her first bite, and then I bit into her strong body. I sucked! She shuddered and drew her shoulder up. The doctor looked at her. She seemed abashed and pretended to have been sitting quietly and silently for a hundred years. Then she continued to satisfy her appetite which was no smaller \'than mine. I noticed that she was eating a dish of a large steaming tongue which had for her sake been torn out of the mouth of some animal. This proved once more that I had nothing to bother my conscience with as long as I lived. I looked round immediately for another place to suck and a small mole caught my eye, it showed me that it would be easy to get at the blood here. I stung her very heart. I sucked. She put her hand to her chest but controlled herself and did not wince. I noticed that much se control was required from her for her soft sk grew rosy. I clung to her with twice the strengt She shuddered and threw down her fork. I her go. "What, what is the matter?" wondered t doctor. "Nothing," she answered and blushed still mo "My nerves, too." I bad finished my dinner by now and ha< walked on to her back to give my leg whic by now was again well a try. Yes, my leg w all right. I jumped about to my hear content. My heart was by now well sat fied with food. But the lady drank wine, roll about in her chair, and said that one gulp of wi had for once made her more giddy than a who barrel of wine at other times. The poor doct believed her. "You, too, will have to take mut baths," he said seriously. No, I could not let this happen, at least not f the time being. I stopped dancing. It was tim to take a rest. I moved to my former place and began to examine the world around me. The verandah was lying in the shadow. The day was coming to an end, but my mission was not yet over. No, no my revenge had not even started. What had taken place was only a meal that we had taken together and my dance was the expression of the merriment of a well-fed human being. My revenge took place later. But I do not wish to rush things. After the couple had eaten they got up and resumed their walk. Their walk brought them to the river Lielupe. I heard that they wanted to go in a boat. "Do you know how to row?" asked the doctor. "Yes." "When did you have your bath last ?" he inquired excitedly "That is fine. In my time we did not go in for sport and now it is too late to begin I should like to get further away from people. I keep thinking the whole time You see, I should like to tell you something ..." He was confused now and could not utter a single word more, so that it seemed he had really something on his heart. The best of barristers usually behave in this way. "Yes, what is it, my dearl Tell me immediately." What a fool she was. She had not studied law. An honest lawyer imagines even the church to be the reception room of. God and. therefore, having entered it, sometimes waits a whole hour for his turn. There are things for which you have to wait... there is no help for it. And that what he wanted to say was probably more fit to be said by the light of the moon than by that of the sun. You must wait! But at this moment I too understood that there was no cause to act slowly. Already on the way to the river I decided to leave my hiding-place and to show myself to the doctor. They were again walking side by side silently. I walked through the lane of white breasts and came out on her throat. Here I waited for a chance to look into the eyes of the doctor. This did not seem so easy. There was no other way I began my old dance. I jumped about as if possessed. An onlooker would have thought that I was twisting a black thread round the head of my victim — everything was going so well. I fell into her hair, threw myself from shoulder to shoulder — at last he caught sight of me. I saw how his eyes widened, how he shrugged his shoulders, but for the time being he only bit his lips. "How are you, doctor?" I said in my own language, "Is it true that you do not like insects? Or has this woman whom you love been telling you lies?" I increased my vigour. I raised black storms round her head. For a moment he watched my proceedings— he wanted to say* something, but he only raised his eyebrows. I\'was careful enough not to try and jump on to the doctor. No, no. This was not my intention. Still, cautiously he began to walk further away from his friend. This was my first, though slight victory. But this gave me hope that I would continue on the way to success. And, see, I grew so impudent that I began to leave black spots here and there on her white blouse. I did this with intention. I have no excuse for this, it was the beginning of my revenge, It was a little indecent but I knew what impression this would have on a human being whose idol is a clean rag just taken out of the soap suds. And I was right, when the esteemed doctor raised his eyes again his gaze travelled to the figure of the lady of his choice (and my figure at the same time). This time he was no longer able to keep himself in hand. "When did you have your bath last?" he inquired excitedly. The lady blushed, the question was more than surprising. It was cruel, it was offending. "My dear friend, what is the meaning of this?" she grew impatient. "Was this what you wanted to tell me to-night?" "Evidently." "Impossible," she exclaimed, moved in his direction, wanted to throw her arms round him and — oh Heavens, she even made as if to kiss him. The moment her glowing face came near to that of the doctor I took my last jump straight on to her lips. "Phew," hissed the doctor, drawing back. I do not really know what the mearung of this word is in the language of human beings; in our  it would mean as much as: you obscene flea, you have got what you wanted. Certainly after praise such as this there was nothing for me left to do. I jumped into the coat of a passing poodle and lost the couple out of sight. The poodle walked about all evening along the bank of the river together with his lady friends and I am quite sure that till late in the night not a single boat left the shore. But at the same time I can assure you that life with the dogs is much easier to endure than life with human beings. However, I still intend to return to them one day. There is no revenge that would be cruel enough for them. For the time I praise my poodle; I met another small flea on him for the first time. "What a pleasure!" I exclaimed. "Oh, how I have been waiting for you," she answered. Translated by Lucy Berzins Dusk on the Baltic Sea']

Name: 1_music_festival_song_opera
Representative_Docs: ['Since there are so many choirs, dance groups, and music groups in Latvia, and the size  of any song or dance festival is limited, a system of review concerts (skates) was established to act as a kind of contest for the right to participate in a song or dance festival. The review system has been in place at least since Soviet times and continues to this day in much the same way. The system is easily organized because most music and dance groups in Latvia are affiliated with governmental cultural organizations at the local district, parish, or city level. Earlier, of course, this was a way in which the government could control cultural activities and the people involved in them, as well as add on a nice layer of bureaucracy. Nowadays political control no longer plays a role, Amanda Jātniece although the governmental bureaucracy is still alive and well. But the review system also fulfills a practical need in that it helps festival organizers to know and control exactly how many performers will be participating in any given festival with what kind of repertoire and at what level of quality. It also motivates dance and music groups to develop interesting programs, work at improving themselves, and gain experience in performing. The review system in the choir and folk dance worlds is rigorous. Competition is very fierce, especially in a song festival year. Thankfully the review system for folklore groups is more lax, as in my mind it should be. I don\'t know the precise figures, but I do know that most folklore ensembles receive "passing grades" in review concerts and that often the concerts are more just for the festival organizers to get an idea of what kind of program each group will be bringing to the festival. For several weeks this fall a Before the "Baltic" festival series of review concerts for folklore ensembles has been taking place throughout Latvia. That\'s because Latvia is gearing up for the "Baltica" folklore festival that takes place on a rotating basis in each of the Baltic countries. The festival will take place on July 8-12, 2009, in Riga, Jelgava, and several smaller towns in Zemgale. When planning for a festival like "Baltica" begins, a broad theme for the festival is announced about a year in advance. Kach group that wishes to participate in the festival then has a couple of months to prepare a 15-minute program of their choice relating to the festival theme, which they perform at a regional review concert. Usually there is a series of 2-4 concerts in each region of the country: Latgale, Vidzeme, Zemgale, Kurzeme, and Riga. A jury comprised of five or so folklorists, culture department employees, and festival organizers travels to each concert and judges the ensembles. Points are awarded for 1) conforming to authentic musical traditions, 2) quality of performance, including use of appropriate dialect ana manner of singing, 3) choice of repertoire, interpretation, and originality, and 4) quality of ethnic costumes. The ensembles are then divided into four categories according to the number of points received. In theory, those groups that fall into a certain category or above can participate in the festival. Although a system like this works reasonably well for choirs and folk dance groups, which are stage performers of composed and choreographed material and can be fairly easily judged according to certain defined classic rules of music and performance, it doesn\'t work so well in regards to folklore. In essence, it means judging what is "good" or "bad" folklore. It also brings into question who should have the right to judge folklore. Both are slippery and emotional topics in a field that is about as "by the people and for the people" as it gets. Some groups, for example, are comprised of good friends that have been playing music together for decades, while other groups experience a high turnover of people. Some folk groups may consist of professionally trained musicians and perform at an extremely high technical level, yet include many non traditional elements in their music. Other groups sing purely traditional material, but— out of conviction that FOLKlore should be for all of the folkembrace absolutely everyone with a genuine interest in singing, even those who can hardly hold a tune. Judges try to be fair and neutral, but in the end, their personal tastes still play a role and egos regularly get bruised. It is probably for these reasons that the folklore review system is lax. That, and the fact that the folklore movement in Latvia is relatively small and has always stood a little outside the mainstream, not really fitting in here or there, all the while yearning for some form of legitimacy in the world of Culture. About 2300 participants, or 150 groups, from all corners of (Continued on page 2) Amanda Jātniece Latvia will eventually take part in the "Baltica" festival, as well as a dozen or so guest ensembles from other countries. The review concerts for Riga-based groups took place in November 1 -2: two concerts on Saturday, two on Sunday, with an average of six groups per concert. Folklore performances in Latvia are often fairly spontaneous, but groups generally take time to prepare for review concerts. The concerts are for free and open to the public, so they\'re a wonderful opportunity for aficionados to f;et their heart\'s fill.
