Unfortunately, ike so many other fine cultural events in Latvia, they are very poorly advertised and so attendance is likewise dismal. The theme for next summers "Baltica" festival is "Signs", be they ethnographic, cultural, literary, decorative, ritualistic, mystical, or magical. Most everything can be tilted in such a way to fit under a theme as broad as that, which makes the choice of a program both easy and difficult. The group I sing with— Savieši—has welcomed two new singers into the world this past year. Since we knew that we would be organizing krustabas (a name-giving ceremony) for the youngest one this fall, we decided to make that repertoire perform double duty and use it for our review concert as well. After all, a name-giving ceremony involves many rituals to ensure a good and prosperous life for the new Before the "Baltic" festival child! A few of the other programs I saw at the concerts included protection signs for soldiers, wedding rituals, rituals/signs associated with the spring celebration Ūsiņi, and songs of strength. Several groups focused on songs about tne sun, a very important symbol in Latvian folklore. The Saturday concerts took place at VEF Kultūras pils; the Sunday concerts were held at Mazā ģilde in the old part of Riga. After each concert tne jury met with the directors of the ensembles and expressed their general opinions about each performance and suggestions for improving it, sometimes commenting on specific troublesome aspects, such as the appropriateness of certain harmonies or musical instruments or even costumes to the time period of the melodies sung. But the jury also praised ensembles for things well done, such as a well-crafted program, beautiful and "correct" costumes, or an inclusive spirit. The meeting after our concert eventually became a friendly discussion of what is "authentic" and how far a group can and ought to go when it comes to using traditional materials creatively. We talked about what works and what doesn\'t when a group decides to, for example, compose a melody or ritual of its own. We also discussed how theatrically folklore performances should be staged: standing still in rows like a choir vs. acting out an activity or ritual; including dialogue in the performance vs. just plain singing the songs in as natural a manner as possible. Everyone has an opinion, but no one has definite answers, and in my mind that\'s just fine. Discussions such as these are a sign of health. Strict guidelines and a strict review system would iust put an end to a natural folklore movement. Now that the review concerts are over I can finally breathe a short breath of relief. But we still need to prepare for the actual krustabas in two weeks. Of course, there will be no audience and no judging jury...but this time there\'s food for thirty people to think about.', 'Two distant streams of musical activity are to be traced in the development of Latvian music. The first is the cultivation of the inherited and immensely rich and varied folk music; the second is tfye influence of the Modern Western music, especially its latest schools of the 19th century: romanticism and the main national schools in the other European countries. The close affinities of both these factors is the principal characteristic of modern Latvian music. Therefore we do not notice the conflicts between the sacred and secular music in Latvian music at its historic beginning which were so peculiar in the medieval period in Western Europe; for the same reason Latvian music could develop and grow up in the shortest possible time without much wasted effort or time. Different schools and styles of music in Western Europe gained popularity and became outdated while the Latvian nation, being isolated from the outside world, could do nothing but cultivate the cultural treasure of its own—the admirable folksongs, which were a direct expression of the people\'s common emotions. There is also one reason why the treasure box of the folk songs of the Latvians is so particularly rich both in quality and quantity. There are about 900,000 folksongs, including* numerous variants, collected, compared and systematized by the Institute of Folklore. These folksongs are known as tautas dziesmas or dainas. More than 18,000 melodies have been preserved. Qualitatively they express themselves by a rhythmical richness, delicate style and old tonality, which can be traced ANCIENT LATVIAN FOLK MUSIC by VALENTĪNS BĒRZKALNS The ancient Latvian musical instrument—Kokle. back to the ancient Greek tone system. Moreover, the Latvian folksongs are of great importance as an abundant source of the ancient Latvian religion, traditions and customs. Even the history of the nation is reflected in those little songs. A great many songs perished during the centuries because they were handed down by word of mouth, this being the only wavy to save them and deliver them to the next generations. However, a quantity of them is still alive, and are sung at home and in school, in different festivities as well as in social gatherings, in spite of the strong influence of the modern popular song "hits". Less important and less preserved is the ancient instrumental music. The principal instrument of music of the ancient Latvian was kokle, related somewhat to the medieval dulcimer or hackbrett, essentially being an entirely original and different form known only in Northern Europe. It was used for accompanying the singers and dancers. The origin of it dates back to the centuries even before the Middle Ages. We can find some samples in the museums—instruments one and a half to seven feet long, which consist of broad and long excavated boxes of fir or linden tree, with the one end narrower than the other, and a couple of holes in the top for better acoustics. The kokle was furnished with five to twelve strings, tuned diatonically, and stretched from an iron bar on the one end to wooden plugs on the other end. The player produced the tones by plucking the strings with the forefinger of his right hand,- and muffled the discordant sounds with his left hand. Another ancient instrument was the so-called eglite, about one and a half feet long made of a barked fir tree with little sounding pendants on its branches. The more perfected form was called the trideksnis, furnished with special copper pendants. Both instruments were used for rhythm and can be regarded as the predecessors of the conductor\'s baton. Bag-pipes, drums, horns, wooden trumpets, whistles and fiddles were also Latvian Minstrels in National Costumes. used by the ancient Latvians, but they are not as characteristic of the country as the kokle or eglite mentioned above It is evident that vocal music was most characteristic among the Latvians. One can divide the folksongs into two different groups—the so-called recitative songs and the solo songs. Melodies belong to one or the other group, according to their formal structure, text, and performance. The recitative songs are the oldest ones. Some of them belong to the pre-historic period, thus being in existence long before* the establishment of Christianity in Latvia. A great many of them disappeared after they lost their primary meaning. The recitative melodies were sung in a choir. One voice (teicēja) led the tune, reciting the first two lines of the verse, then the other voices (locitajas) took over the melody and "drew" it, while a third group (vilcējas) chose one of the vowels (usually "c") keeping it in the same pitch until the end of the melody thus giving an impression of organ point of modern harmony. Sometimes another singer, a so-called sijātajā or sikalina sang another melody higher than the main tune. Such singing reminds us of an improvised polyphony and proves that the polyphony is not an invention of the sacred music. The same kind of singing is also evidenced in some other North European countries. The recitative songs did not have a strict unchangeable text, but depended upon the occasion and place where it was sung. The "Ligo Songs", the favorite of the age-old folk songs, were sung on the eve of Midsummer Day, and have survived to the present time. So also did other songs sung on special occasions at different seasons of the year. Many of them are connected with various rites and customs at wedding, child birth, folk festivals and funerals. A great number of these songs praise the work and describe different activities at home and in the field. The rhythmic structure of this first group is simple. Two-four time is predominant. The text of these songs, as mentioned above, is changeable, depending on what the singer is singing about, but there are some strictly unchangeable refrains, like "ligo" for Ligo Songs, "rota" for Spring Songs, etc. These refrains usually consist of one or two bars. A basic form of the melody is made of one musical period expressed in eight bars, sometimes also abbreviated, but more frequently extended to twelve or more bars. The songs are pretty avaricious by nature as concerns the melodies. We can find some tunes consisting of two tones only, but dominating tunes consist of five to six tones, seldom extending to an octave. However, with this scanty material the old singers could produce beautiful and impressive songs. The dominating part of the songs of this group are conceived in the Dorian and Aeolian modes, more seldom in the lonian, Lydian and Mixolydian ones. It is also characteristic that they are entirely devoid of the artificial introductory note, which is evidence that they are older than those of the 17th century. The other group of folksongs, the so-called solo dainas, were usually sung by soloists only. If they appeared in a choir, they were sung in unison. Rhythmically they are more rich than the recitative songs. The same applies to their melodies. Most of them were developed in the last centuries. The text of these songs, in opposition to the other group, is strictly unchangeable. It consists of many four-line verses about some particular events, expressing the emotions of love, joy, about the beauty of nature, satisfaction at work, etc. Many of these songs are exclusively lyric by nature. A careful student can also find fragments of the balladic or epic elements among the songs belonging to this group. The majority of the songs of this group is conceived in the lonian, Aeolian and Mixolydian modes. The Phrygian and Dorian modes were used less frequently. Lydian modes are almost untouched. The range of the melodies of this group is more extended than that of the other group. Predominating tunes consist of an octave, but we can find melodies consisting of ten to fourteen tones. Many of these songs are composed so skillfully and beautifully, that they can compare with the themes of the art songs of any time; therefore they play an important role in the works of the modern Latvian composers. The same can be said about the rhythm of these songs. We find many kinds of measures, most common are those with the regular five-four and seven-four time, and irregular mixed time. The characteristic of all the Latvian folksongs is that they start on the strong beat only. The melodies of the second group of songs are also richer in their forms. Basically made up of eight bars, they vary in their structure, sometimes abbreviated, sometimes extended. The extensions are made by repeating a part of some bars or including interjectory words without any meaning, as "aijāja", "trallalla", "raidairidira" and the like. The first account of the Latvian folksongs dates back to 1632, being found in the printed work of Menius, professor of the University of Tartu (Sintagma de Origine Livonorum). The real renaissance of the folksongs began about 200 years later, when the publications of Johann G. Herder, concerning the influence of folksongs in the culture of the nations, gained wide popularity in different countries. The first attempt to make a systematic collection and studies of the Latvian folk music was shown by Janis Cimze (1814-1881), director of the Latvian teachers\' college at Valmiera. His collection "Dziesmu Rota", published in 1873, gave stimulus to further studies of Latvian folk music on a broader scale. The songs of his collections were harmonized and thus formed the first national repertoire of the Latvian choirs. The most important authority of Latvian folk music was Andrejs Jurjans (1856-1922), the first Latvian composer academically educated. Being a graduate of three different departments of the Conservatory of St. Petersburg, he was a distinguished scientist of the theory of music and an active musician himself. His principal work, "Materiaux pour la musique populaire Latavienne", 1894, consists of 1200 folksongs scientifically collected and interpreted. He was the first scientist who formulated the theoretical principles of Latvian vocal music in analyzing the tonality and harmony of the folksongs, and emphasizing the principles of a diatonic characteristic of these songs. Further studies of the Latvian folksongs were contributed by the composer Emilis Melngailis (b. 1874) and Professor Jēkabs Graubins (b. 1886). However, a complete systematization and publication of the Latvian folk music still must be done. Especially needed are publications and interpretations in foreign languages. The general events of the musical life of Europe were reflected in Latvia since the 13th century. With the introduction of Christianity the church immediately made every effort to strengthen its position among the indigenous people and thus introduced the sacred music to the Latvian audience. However, the sacred music evidently did not affect the Latvian folk music and both forms existed separately until the modern time. From 1682 to 1698 the Duke of Kurzeme, Friedrich Casimir, supported the first Italian opera and court ballet in his capital Jelgava. In 1772, the German opera theater in Riga opened its doors to the audience. From 1837 to 1839 the famous German composer, Richard Wagner, was a conductor of this opera theater and composed his opera Rienzi. Some other German, Russian, French, Finnish musicians spent a shorter or longer time in Latvia. Tied up by their political and economical situation the majority of the Latvians could not play an active role in the art music. Therefore we can assume that the first half of the 19th century was the real beginning of the purely Latvian art music, when the first Latvian choirs made their public appearances under the leadership of the native conductors. The folksong regarded as musical nonsense by their former dictators of musical life in Latvia proved their vitality throughout centuries and did much to promote cultural renovation and new trends in Latvian music. The first General Song Festival held in the summer of 1873 is to be considered the most important event in the natural incline of Latvian music to the musical life of Western Europe. It was the beginning of a series of song festivals which became a fertile ground for the future development of Latvian music and the wide popularization of the musical treasures among the population. In order to illustrate the importance of the song festivals as a growing and attracting musical event among the people we shall mention a few data. The first song festival in 1873 took place with 1,003 singers participating. The number of singers of the last song festival in independent Latvia had increased to 16,000. The audience also increased proportionately, reaching the number of 100,000 in 1938, which means that every twentieth inhabitant of the country took part in this unusual celer bration of Latvian song. Simultaneous with the growth of the festivals, native composition suddenly began to display increased vitality. However, regular and active developments in all branches of music could take place only after 1918, when the independence of Latvia was reestablished. The leading factors of the musical life became the National Opera and the State Conservatory founded in 1919, and the State Broadcasting Corporation, founded in 1925. The first Latvian Opera .was founded as a private enterprise in 1912 ,', 'SVEŠVALODU NODAĻA OTHER LANGUAGES SECTION LATVIAN MUSIC CAMP IN OGRE: "INTERACTION OF MUSIC STYLES OF DIFFERENT PERIODS – A NEW SIT FOR THE CONTEMORARY MUSIC IN EASTERN EUROPE On July 8 – 18, 1998 the 3rd Summer Music Camp took place, in Ogre, Latvia on the site of the Ogre Music School. The camp was entitled "111 Starptautiskā latviešu jauno mūziķu radoša nometne" (\'Third International Music Camp for Young Latvian Musicians") and had the supplementary title "Dažādu laikmetu mūzikas stilu mijiedarbība" ("Interaction of Music Styles of Different Periods). The camp was directed by Ingrida Gūtberga and Dace Aperāns (or Aperāne, to use the Latvian feminine ending for proper names). Dace Aperāns is a composer of Latvian descent, born in Canada (where her parents emigrated after the Soviet takeover of the three Baltic countries). She studied composition at McGill University in Montreal with Brian Cherney and Bruce Mather and then moved to New York, where she studied at Mannes College and Hunter College in New York. Mrs. Aperāns has had numerous performances of her compositions in the USA and Canada. Since 1989 she has been frequently visiting Latvia and contacting numeroues Latvian composers. Since that time, her compositions have been frequently performed in Latvia, most recently a ballet based on the life of Edith Piaf, entitled "Edīte"("Edith"), commisioned by the Latvian Opera, and she has been actively promoting the music of Latvian composers, such as Pēteris Vasks, Pēteris Plakidis and others, inactivating performances of their works by American soloists or ensembles. In July 1994 she arranged the first summer music camp in Jaungulbene, I.atvia, which brought many musicians from all over Latvia as well as a number of Latvian Americans and Canadians, which featured courses, lessons, lectures and seminars by prominent instrumentalists, composers and performers. Since then the camp has been held in Ogre, small town in  about 50 minutes away on the train from Riga, every two years, the second camp took place in July, 1996 and the third happened in July, 1998. Dace Aperāne has been the artistic director for these three camps, and has been actively bringing together all of the best Latvian musicians to take a creative part in the camp. The 104 music students and 40 musicians came not only from Riga but from the whole country, many of them teachers in the Latvian Music Academy (i. e. Conservatory) named after Jāzeps Vītols in Riga, as well as in many music colleges throughout Latvia. The Camp was sponsored by the Soros Foundation, the British Council, the Riga Early Music Center, the Latvian Cultural Ministry and the Latvian Cultural Fund as well as by the Ogre Dome or City Hall and the Ogre Music School. In addition, a private donor, Brigitta Greenwood, a Latvian-American donated a substantial sum to the music camp. Also, all of the main Latvian orgaqnisations in Canada and the USA donated funds to the music camp; about 15 in all. Latvia, one or the three Baltic States which have obtained their independence from the Soviet Union in August 1991, is appearing on the scene of contemporay music with a number of interesting composers, who have started out their compositional career in the last few decades of the Soviet Union (after the cultural "thaw" of the 1960\'s when more unofficial writers, artists and composers were given a little more breathing space for their art in the Soviet Union) and who are now, after the demise of the Soviet Union, making their way into the international contemporary music scene, their music becoming more available in concert halls and CD stores in Europe and America. One of the most prominent composers in Latvia is Pēteris Vasks, who has had a rapidly increasing recognition as a composer, first among the unofficial composers in the Soviet Union (especially by such c<#nposers as Edison Denisov and Alfred Schnittke in Moscow) and then in the Western countries. He has made his\' USA debut in 1989 when Thomas Stacy performed his Oboe Concerto. In Europe where his music ir frequently performed and recorded on CD s, most of his compositions are published with Schott Publications. Other composers are also gradually coming out onto the international scene, including Pēteris Plakidis, Imants Zemzaris, Selga Mence, Imants Kalniņš and Maija Einfelde. Many of their works are performed in Germany as well as in the Scandinavian countries – Norway, Denmark, Sweden and Finland, with which they are developing a steadily increasing connection. Many Latvian composers have a very unique style of music, which can loosely be termed as Neo-Romantic, a lot of which features long, static tonal harmonies and a unique approach to texture and instrumentation, often connected to the organ tradition and religious musical genres. Many of the latvian composer\'s works, though tonal in harmony, can sound rather avant-garde in texture and instrumental usage and thus establish a connection to some avant-garde trends in European music as well as some of the avant-garde Russian composers, with whom the I.atvian composers were in close connection with when both countries were part of the Soviet Union. Some of this music remotely contains elements of minimalist technique, due to the static tonal harmonies, nevertheless their usage of the latter is totally different and distinct from the American minimalists Glass, Reich and Riley, due to the more emotional quality of the  composers\' music and in their case the minimalist technique remains a side feature and never becomes the central element of their work. Latvian music is also distinctive for its unique folk choral tradition, which many contemporary Latvian composers keep up, contributing to it with imaginative pieces and modifying it to suit their individual styles. A few Latvian composers also take up techniques from the European avant-gard tradition, sometimes concentrating on them fully and somtimes blending them with the Neo-Romantic Latvian tradition. During the last couple of years there has emerged a new "Society of Contemporary Music" the members of which consist of young composers, mostly in their 20\'s, who arrange a concert series of conemporary music and actively perform each others\' music as well as the music of their older colleagues and also develop connections with Western Eureopean musicians. Two of the cocmposcrs from this group, Rolands Kronlaks and Jānis Petraskevics, took part in the music festival, the former a lecturer and the latter as a student. The faculty members in the camp included Such prominet musicians as composer Selga Mence, who was the head of the program for composers, composer Pēteris Plakidis, who taught chamber music, harpsichordist Aina Kalnciema, choral conductor Juris Vaivods, pianists Teofils Biķis, a professor of the Latvian Musical Academy, pianist Ingrida Gūtberga, a Latvian living in the USA, pianists Uga Grants and Arthur Ozoliņš, viobnist Rasma Lielmane, A Latvian living in South Dakota and teaching in Mexico City, Latvian-American composer Imants Mc/a raups, born ir Philadelphia, a graduate of University of Philadelphia, a former student of George Rochberg and George Crumb, teaching in Latvia for the last two years, composer Rolands Kronlaks, a prominet 25-year old composer, who has been actively participating in the Western European contemporary music scene, musicologist Boriss Avramecs, director of the Riga Early Music Center who has been actively arranging the International Early Music Festival in Bauska-Rundāle, featuring Medieval music, as well as other festivals including those of Indian and Chinese music, musicologist Juris Grinevičs and host of others. Some of the musicians who came over from the United States and Canada included latvian-American baritone Paul Berkolds, a doctoral student ar UCSD, who has had a wide acclaim throughout the USA, clarinetist Eric Mandat, a faculty member at the University of Illinois and a member of the Transatlantic Trio (an ensemble consisting of piano, clarinet and cello, two member of which, Pēteris Plakidis and cellist Ivars Bezprozvanovs live in latvia, and the third member, Eric Mandat lives in Illinois; the ensemble meets together to perform as a trio regularly once a year when Eric Mandat comes to Latvia every summer), American composer Robert I>evin, composer Anton Rovner, Canadian composer Nic Gotham and others. In addition a number of composers, though not being active participants of the festival, came to Ogre to give presentations of their music as well as to attend the performances of their compositions – those included the famous composer Pēteris Vasks, who has been achieving immense success with his music throughout the world as well as composers Maija Einfelde, Imants Kalniņš, Imants Zemzaris, Ilona Brege, Vineta Lice and others. In additon the camp featured the Hilliard Ensemble from England, which "presented" a program of Medieval, Renaissance and Contemporary music, as well as an ensemble from Riga which performed Indian music on traditional Indian instruments. Each morning in the camp started out with a lecture at 9 AM in the main auditorium of the Ogre Music School, usually by prominet musicologists and composers and each evening ended with a concert at 8 PM in the same auditorium, where, for the most part, faculty members of the camp presented both traditional Classical repertoire and works by contemporary latvian composers. The rest of the day, between the morning lecture and the evening concert, was very tightly scheduled and featured more indidividual lessons, lectures amd masterclasses divided between each of the respaective groups of musicians. For the most part the faculty members who were instrumentalists and vocalists (most of whom were faculty members either in the Latvian Musical Academy in Riga or in the music colleges throughout Latvia) gave private lessons to students (most of whom were students either in the latvian Musical Academy in Riga or in the music colleges, of which there is a total of ten in laivia – but there were also some master-classes as well as cham ber-music coaching. The composition department of the camp, directed by composer Selga Mence, had a special individual program of its own composers. There were numerous lectures and presentations by composers, both those attending the camp in its entierety and those visiting the camp for one day. There were both individual presentations by composers of their own oeuvres and lectures read about wider topics in contemporary music. In addition the composers who were lecturers gave private consultations to the student composers upon the latters1 request. The composers who gave presentations of their works included Maija Einfelde, Ilona Brege, Rolands Kronlaks, Nic Gotham, Anton Rovner and others. Maija Einfelde, a composer of mostly chamber music and orchestral music who has recently received an award from the Barlow competition in the USA for her choral work "Pie zemes tālas"("Near the Far-Away Land"), presented a number of her choral works, including the composition which won the competition. Ilona Brege, composer and director of the Latvian National Symphony Orchestra presented her one-act opera "Dzīvais ūdens ("The Water of life"), based on religious, ritualistic story in an abstract mythical time and place, which has recently been premiered in Riga, Rolands Kronlaks, a graduate of the Latvian Music Academy, a composer following Western European avant-garde contemporary musical trends, who has participated in Franco Donatoni\'s master-classes in Mexico City and a number of contemporay musical festivals in Europe and who has been invited for one year to IRCAM in Paris, presented his orhestral piece "Under Pressure", his work for two pianos "Knots\'1 and his "Conversion" for oboe and string quartet. Canadian composer Nic Gotham, currently staying in  for one year, presented a number of different compositions of his including one chamber composition "Spirit of St. Ix)is" for violin, trumpet, piano and percussion and a fragment from his opera "Nigredo Hotel". Gotham sludied with the famous American composer James Tenney and his own compositional style features an organized blend of innovative "Dontown school" experiments folowing the tradition of his teacher Tenney with a traditional Romatic semi-popular operatic style. Anton Rovner shoved a diverse variety of his chamber compositions written during various years. One of the most talented students of the Latvian Music Academy, Jānis Petraskevics, presented a few of his works, including a solo cello work and a work for clarinet, viola and piano. Petraskevics, a follover of European avant-garde trends, has participated in a number of composers\' workshops in Europe including summer courses with Boguslaw Schaeffer in Vienna. He has received a grant to study at the Stockholm Conservatory with Magnus Linborg during the upcoming year. Some of the lectures about contemporary music of various kinds featured a wide variety of interesting music.  composer Imants Mežaraups read a lecture about American electronic music from a historical perspective, demonstrating the works of Vladimir Ussachevsky, Otto  Milton Babbitt and a host of others. He also demonstrated a variety of American new compositional and theoretical systems, such as Schenkerian analysis, set theory and others. Dace Aperāne presented a selection of contemporary American compositions, including a work by Christopher Rouse. Rolands Kronlaks read a lecture about Franco Donatoni and demonstrated his system to the interested audience. Anton Rovner presented a selection of works by contemporary Russian composers following European trends, including Edison Denisov, Dmitri Kapyrin and Victor Ekimovsky. These Russian composers were in very close and friendly relations with the Utvian composers (as well as composers from other Baltic countries) during the time of the Soviet Union, since both of these groups of composers had the common goal of finding new forms of expression, alternate to the prevailing "socialist realism". Since the breakup of the Soviet Union and the cooling down of relations between Russia and the Baltic countries, contacts between Russian and Latvian composers became fewer and more rare. Nevertheless some composers maintained contact with each other, certain Latvian composers visited contemporary musical seminars in Russia and vice versa. Anton Rovner also read a second lecture about the system of music education in the conservatories and universities in the United States, closely tying it with the various trends of contemporary American music, such as the Uptown school, the Downtown school the tonal composers, some of the systems of music theory and demonstrating the music of Andrew Thomas, Milton Babbitt, Roger Sessions, Charles Wuorinen and Daniel Goode as musical examples. Clametist Bric Mandat gave a lecture about aleatory music and improvisation, in the course of which the students had a chance to improvise with their instruments, following certain charts which Mr. Mandat placed on the board. Composer Robert Levin was teaching a course in African rhythm which was open for participation not only for composers but for all the instrumentalists. The composers\' lectures were held in in English and in few instances in Russian. The student composers, who attended the composers\' lectures, contained many talented young composers, who were primarily students or the Latvian Music Academy, as well as the various music colleges throughout via, most of which had their compostions performed in the composers\' concert-marathon. Among the young composers, some of the most prominet names winch could be mentioned are those of Jānis Fetraskevics, Solveiga Selga, leva Pane, Agita Zeļča, Agneta Krilova, Maija Homjakova, Mārīte Dombrovska and Sintija Deruma. They were very receptive to the new music demonstrated in the lectures and performed in the concerts and attended all of the events with great interest. The big lectures in the morning had a wide variety of topics which were presented to a larger audience than the composers\' lectures and seminars and, in general, often had a more scholarly and formal approach. They were read exclusively in Latvian. The first lecture was read by Dace Aperāne about the music of Pēteris Vasks. Other lectures included those about Baroque music, Medieval music, traditional and contemporary Latvian music, lectures by Boriss Avramecs about Asian music, a lecture by Latvian composer Imants Kalniņš about his 4th symphony and its performance in the USA and in the Utvian cities of Liepāja, Rīga and Cēsis, a lecture about the influences upon  music from Eastern Europe and Russia by-Arnolds Klotiņš and a lecture by Imants Mežaraups about contemporary American electronic music. They were well attended by the students and by the faculty members. There were two cases when the evening concerts contained lectures or presentations as part of them: the first time when Pēteris Vasks demonstrated a video recording of his new ballet "We Set Out Early...Visibility was Poor", choreapgraphed by choreographer Bill T. Jones to the music of his Symphony for string Orchestra "Voices", performed at the Kennedy Center in Washington DC in October, 1997, and the second, when Dace Aperāne presented a variety of contemporary American compositions (as well as compositions from other countries) of the most varied stylistic trends, including Christopher Rouse, John Adams, Ukrainian composer Valenti Silvestrov and a a host of others. The two  composers in the camp, namely Dace Aperāne and Imants Mežaraups, could be especially commended for their enthusiasm in bringing American compositions to Latvia and presenting them with American music and all the up-to-date information about the American musical scene in all of its diversity. The concerts in the evening were an important element of the music camp, their generous, saturating quality gave the camp a quality of a music festival. Many of the faculty members performed in them both as soloists and in ensembles, sometimes presenting recitals taking up half of the program of a concert and often performing together, one after the other in various combinations with each other and with the most varied program. Among the performers of the concerts one can name harpsichordist Aina Kalnciema who did a solo recital, violonist Rasma Lielamane, pianists TeoGls Biķis and Arthur Ozoliņš, the Transatlantic Trio (featuring Pēteris Plakidis, Eric Mandat and Ivars Bezprozvanovs), baritone Paul Berkolds and many other musicians. In addition to compositions from the Classical, traditional repertoire (among which especially impressive was Teofils Biķis1 performance of Scriabin\'s sth Sonata), the concerts featured many performances of  composers, many of which have been present at the concerts. Pēteris Plakidis has had his trio for clarinet, cello and piano entitled "Bezmiegs"("lnsomnia") performed by the Transatlantic Trio, Dace Aperāne has had her new composition, "Pastorale" for violin and piano performed by Rasma Lielmane and Arthur Ozoliņš, and also had her songs for baritone and piano and a cycle of pieces for two pianos entitled "Songs" performed. Imants Mežaraups performed a few of his short piano pieces "Miniatures" and also has had his composition for two pianos, celesta and percussion "Dalijumi un saites: zvani un sitieniI\'("Divisions and common bonds: bells and strikes") performed by a group of instrumentalists. composer Tālivaldis Ķēniņš "Partita for strings" was performed by the music camp\'s chamber orchestra with Mārtiņš Bergs conducting. I have already mentioned the group of instrumentalists who performed Indian music, accompanied by a 75 year old Lavian dancer Vija Vētra a reknowed specialist in Indian dance, who danced as if she was a young lady, as well as the Hilliard Ensemble from London, which performed a selection of Medieval and Renaissance music, as well as choral works by contempo rary composers Pēteris Vasks and Veljo Tormis. Two of the concerts at the end of the camp were marathons, featuring students – one of the concerts presented compositions by the student composers, performed by the student performers, which was a sort of contest, which lasted until 1 AM and at the end of which the composers who were on the faculty, i. e. the lecturers met and chose one of the compositions to be performed at the last concert, namely a piece for soprano and chamber ensemble by composer Agneta Krilova. The other concert was a similar contest for students who were performers, who performed traditional repertoire, several of which were chosen for the last concert. The final feature of the camp was an opera, staged during the last day of the camp, on July 18th, which was Albert Ixntzing\'s opera "Rehearsal of an Opera", staged by Guntis Gailītis and conducted by Viesturs Gailis and performed by the students of the camp – this performance brought the camp to a grandiose and festive conlusion. In general the music camp has had a very invigorating and creative atmosphere, its participants had a lot of enthusiasm in bringing all of this music out to the audiences as well as a lot of professional skills, and the broad variety of latvian music in the context of music from other countries, presented at this camp, has made this camp a major event in the scene of contemporay music in Eastern Europe and an important step in bringing Latvian musicians into a more distinctive position in the internatinal musical scene. □ member of faculty at the camp Anton Rovner,']
