Name: 4_russian_say_moscow_russia
Representative_Docs: ['Window on Eurasia: \'Russian Nationalism Will Triumph Only Together with Democracy,\' Moscow Historian Says Pols Goubls (Paul Goble) Staunton June 28 Russia nationalism currently in dee ideologica crisis, unab to mobiliz the Russia  a t i o because of its obsession with ou of date ideas and opposed by th regime which fears the comin to power of a Russian nationalism allied with democracy, accord in to an MGIMO historian. Indeed, Valery Solovey, a expert on Russian nationalism argues in an interview postec online last week, "nationalisn will triumph only if it is alliec with the ideas of democracy much as was the case in Easter Europe whose historical pat Russia "willy-nilly" seems rate< to follow, however much th regime opposes it (naz4em.jpfor/ texts/132). Supporters of Russia nationalism recognize, h continues, "the need for radica changes" in both their theor and practice, "but the ver process of these changes has no been completed and that in tur means still has not been transformēt into a new quality." While this process is likely t be prolonged, Solovey says, th democratic and human right trend in Russian nationalism are becoming "more evident And "it is coming to b understood that Russia nationalism is objectively bein transformed into an importan part of civil society, for it arose independently of the state and in a certain sense in opposition to it." For that to happen, Russian nationalists have to find "ideas capable of mobilizing society." Anti-Semitism and Orthodox Monarchy "weren\'t able to mobilize anyone even 20 years ago." Now, such ideas are simply laughable. Instead, Russian nationalist need to form "a synthesis of Russian nationalism with the principles of democracy and a social state." As Solovey points out, "historically nationalism and democracy if not twin brothers are in any case extremely close and in many things even correspond." Russian nationalists often do not see that reality because "the very term \'democracy"\' was "compromised in Russia, although the ideas it encompasses has not. Most Russians, the historian insists, "support the basic values of democracy: they are for honest and free elections, competition in politics, and a multi-party system, for everything in short that represents the content of democracy. And if nationalists want to achieve success, must be with their own people\' on that. ~~~Mo~re~Qver, Solovey ~šāyS7 Russian nationalism must focus "above all on the middle class," the class which is always the basis for nationalisms success. He adds that he "has not doubts that the Russian middle class would invest in nationalism if the political prohibition on that were lifted and if the current ban on any political and social activity in general were removed." What that means in turn, Solovey continues, is that "willynilly [Russians] will have to repeat the experience of the East and Central European countries where democratic changes took place in nationalist forms and where the national-liberal revolutions played out as democratic ones." Because of Russia\'s complicated history, Russian nationalism faces a greater challenge, the MGIMO expert says. In many respects, Russian history has been a playing out of "a conflict between the Russian people and the empire" in "a dialectical" fashion. Russians "not without reason considered the empire their offspring," but the empire treated them badly. "It was impossible to convert the empire into a Russian national state," he points out. Indeed, "even the equality of Russians within the framework of the empire – and it doesn\'t matter whether it was Orthodox or Communist – could not be guaranteed." But most Russian nationalists have refused to accept this. However, "while Russian nationalists were searching for a way out of this blind alley, Solovey says, "history decided everything for them: [With the end of the Soviet Union,] Russia is no longer an empire, and the task of nationalists is to build in Russia an effective and hilly national state." Asked about the North Caucasus as "a relic" of empire, Solovey responds in a way that will trouble many Russian nationalists. He says tnat "already during our lifetimes," the North Caucasus will separate from or be separated by the Russian Federation, an outcome he says "many now are thinking about but still have not decided to say aloud." The Caucasus is not worth what it costs, as those who have fought there know best. Unfortunately, Solovey continues, "the current elite [in Moscow] will never resolve this problem in a principled fashion." Instead, they will continue to throw money at the problem, not only to hold the place as part of the empire and its "mission" but to steal as well. Over the next few years, he suggests, the problem may even get worse: President Dmitry Medvedev has called for increasing the financing of the North Caucasus republics. But according to Solovey, "this is connected with the approaching Olympic Games [in Sochi, games that are set to become] the most expensive in the history of the Olympic movement." Medvedev is also talking a great deal about "the creation of some kind of common nonethnic Russian identity," Soloveys interlocutor points out, to which the Moscow historian responds that "just as someone who is ill talks all the time about health and an alcoholic about vodka, so too he who speaks about the need to form an identity has serious problems with that. "There is no all-Russian identity," Solovey argues; the level of civic self-consciousness is extremely low." Society is "atomized and people are not allowed to unite because "every form of civic unification is persecuted" by the powers that be. Indeed today, the only thing that unites the population of the Russian Federation is May 9. Solovey says that he sees Russia ultimately as a federation rather than a unitary state, not only because Russia is too diverse to be run from a single center and according to a single set of rules but because it is "a guarantee of democracy. Real federalism will only strengthen Russia" because "people will now that they really can participate in the life of their own region." But getting there is going to be hard given the nature of the powers that be, people who Solovey suggests are more interested in destruction" than in "creation" and who are capable of "sowing only chaos." And he adds that even in the 19905, the country was led better than know and the effectiveness of administration was higher" because there was less corruption. The current powers that be, he says, are "totally hostile to everything Russian, to everything national." Indeed, "the main opponent" of the regime is "the Russian people which objectively needs freedom and democracy.\' Those in power feel that and consequently strive to prevent Russians from organizing. And these powers know they can get away with almost anything as far as the West is concerned, and consequently "the Russian elite intends to hold onto power in Russia to the last – to the last ethnic Russian. If it has to use force, it will use it without a shred of conscience and without any restriction."', 'Window on Eurasia: National – Democrats\' Plan \'Beginning of the End* of Russian Federation, Kalashnikov Says Pols Goubls (Paul Goble) Staunton, December 19 – The program of the National Democratic Alliance, which calls for the immediate  separation of the North Caucasus from the rest of the country and the division of the federation into seven "ethnic Russian republics" is "the beginning of the end of the Russian Federation," according to a Moscow commentator. In an article on Forum-MSK.ru yesterday, Maksim Kalashnikov savs that the new unions idea, wnich he says "corresponds completely with the plans of INS6R," the liberal think tank that has provided ideas for President Dmitry Medvedev, represents "the confederalization of the country, a CIS-2, and a new 1991". Moreover, the Russian commentator says, this plan will lead to "the complete destruction of hopes for the conversion of the Russian Federation into a fullblooded unitary ethnic Russian state," all in order to permit "the current elite\' to remain unpunished and hide abroad" with "the trillions of dollars" they have SJmlsu* . In support of his contention, KalashniKOv appends to his brief remarks three documents, the Manifesto of the National-Democratic Alliance adopted in formation of the National – Democratic Movement of the Russian Civic Union slated to be adopted this weekend, and a commentary on the need to separate the North Caucasus. Although Kalashnikov almost certainly overstates the importance of these declarations – they are at most the views of one part of the Russian political class and not an indication of the steps Moscow is likely to take in trie near future – he is clearly right to point to them as an indication of the directions some in the Russian capital are now considering. In the draft manifesto scheduled to be adopted this weekend, the National Democrats declare that they believe in "the indivisibility of the terms nation\' and \'democracy,"\' in the "naturalness" of nationalism and democracy in Russian history, and in the \'necessity of a synthesis of civic, ethnic, and cultural nationalism." They thus call for the formation of a genuine Russian national state based on democracy, a free market economy, and genuine federalism and regionalism. In addition, they urge the formation of a socially-responsible state, the tightening of immigration rules, the defense of the environment and culture, and the transition to a professional army. With regard to the status of ihc republics ol the Nuilh Caucasus, the draft manifesto says that "the price which Russia is paying today for holding these territories is too high" and that the subsidies these republics are receiving need to be cut, the borders need to be changed to separate out ethnic Russian regions, and that the borders between them and Russia must be strengthened. In a commentary that first appeared on the Nazdem.org site, Moscow commentator Aleksey Shiropayev expands on these points ana concludes that Russia must separate out the North Caucasus and give that region its independence if Russia is to have a chance to develop as it should in the future. "It is obvious," he writes, that there were "three geo-political factors which made Russia an empire:" Ukraine, Central Asia and the Caucasus. After 1991, only the Caucasus remained inside the borders of the state centered on Moscow. And that must change or Russia will remain in terrible straits. Shiropayev recalls that Aleksandr Solzhenitsyn who "dreamed about the transformation of Russia into a Russian national state" wanted to get rid of Central Asia, although even the great Russian writer hoped to retain Ukraine and Belarus as "a continuation of the so-called \'Russian world." The current Kremlin rulers, Shiropayev continues, "much better than Solzhenitsyn understand the imperial essence of Russian pretensions in Ukraine. They fight for Ukraine precisely as a necessary component of the internationalist Eurasian empire, covering this struggle with demagogic comments about \'the Russian world.\'" But the Moscow commentator points out, "Ukraine just like Central Asia already is all the same foreign territory." And that in turn means that "the main thing which gives an imperial character to Russia is now the North Caucasus," defined he points out by the ethnic territories not by the lines of the North Caucasus Federal District. "Let us remember," Shirpoyev says, "with what began the restoration of the imperial paradigm after the destruction of the USSR – with the Chechen war unleashed by Yeltsin under hurrah-patriotic slogans." It was precisely that war wnich "put an end to the hopes for the transformation of Russia into a normal federation consisting of equal subjects. One must note, he continues, that "as a result of the Chechen wars, which have cost so much blood of ethnic Russian soldiers, both the Kremlin and Chechnya have achieved mutually profitable results: the central powers that be have \'demonstrated\' the necessity of their existence as guarantors of the muchballyhooed integrity of the country." And at the same time, Shiropayev points out, "Chechnya has obtained thechance for unlimited subsidies as a parasite on the Russian regions of the RE" But there is "just one small detail: neither the one nor the other has any relationship to ethnic Russian interests." Indeed, both contradict them. "The Kremlin needs the North Caucasus for its presence within Russia justifies tne existence of an enormous repressive Chekist cop machine and what is the main thing the very existence of the Kremlin as tne center of power." In fact, "the Moscowcentric Russian statehood" of today rests on the North Caucasus. And likewise, "the North Caucasus needs the Kremlin as a source of aid, concessions, and all possible special legal arrangements" and that is not to mention the unbelievably enormous "corrupt schemes "which connect the capital\'s bureaucracy and the North Caucasus elites." Given all that, Shiropayev says, "it is obvious that the main question for Russia, the Russian question, cannot be resolved while the imperial character of Russian statehood is preserved. And it will be preserved while the North Caucasus remains within Russia," an arrangement the Kremlin and the North Caucasus are vitally interested in seeing continue. "The starting point for the reconstitution of the Russian Federation" must thus be placing the national republics of the North Caucasus outside its borders. "It is finally time to openly and honestly acknowledge tne cultural, mental, and psychological incompatibility of the Russian people and other peoples of Russia with the phenomenon of the North Caucasus. "We are OTHER and we want to live in another way, in a European way, and not according to the laws of* the mountain taips. It is time to deprive the Kadyrovs of the possibility of living as parasites on the Russian and other peoples of Russia. And it is time to deprive the Putins ot the possibilities ot speculating on imperial patriotism." Finally, Shiropayev says, "it is time to put an end to the criminal union of the Chekists and the wild men."', 'Window on Eurasia: North Caucasus Highlights Problems with Notion of Non-Ethnic Russian Nation, Experts Say Pols Goubls (Paul Goble) Vienna, March 31 – The terrorist attacks in Moscow earlier this week and the new upsurge of violence in Daghestan highlight weaknesses not only with the Russian state but also problems with the notion of a non-ethnic Russian national identity, a notion that many have argued is essential if that country is to develop as a modern civil society. In today\'s "Nezavisimaya gāzēta," Aleksandra Samarina addresses this latter point via interviews with four leading Moscow scho lars, Valery Tishkov who heads the Institute of Ethnology and Anthropology, Gyorgy Mirsky of IMEMO, Dmitry Furman of the Institute of Europe, and Aleksey Malashenko of the Carnegie Endowment\'s Moscow Center. Tishkov, who perhaps more than any other individual has been associated with the promotion of the idea of a non-ethnic Russian [\'rossiisky"] identity, acknowledges that there are problems with that identity among many people in the Noi th Caucasus but insists progress is being made (www.ng.ru/politics/2010-03-31/l_kavkaz. Html). The process of getting people in the North Caucasus to accept such an identity is proceeding "with great efforts," Tishkov says, something all the more necessary because that region was "incorporated into the Russian state later than the Volga or the Russian North, Siberia and even the Far East." In addition, he says, the North Caucasus "experienced the Caucasus war, the difficult period of the establishment of Soviet power, the deportation of peoples, and the Chechen war." And consequendy, while "the majority" of people in the region consider themselves non-ethnic Russians, there are nonetheless armed people who "cast doubt" on that concept. Mirsky extended the discussion by observing that even though many people in the North Caucasus and especially in Daghestan use the Russian language as a link to the world, those who leave that region to live and work in Russian cities increasingly feel themselves viewed with distrust because they are Muslims. "The word \'Muslim,\' the IMEMO scholar says, "ever more is associated with the term \'terrorist"\' in Western Europe, the United States and Russia. In Russia\'s case, he continues, such negative attitudes toward North Caucasians are intensified by the false notion that Gastarbeiters are taking Russian jobs. This is "dangerous," he says, because "people who encounter such a situation inevitably translate their feelings to the motherland. And there ever more often the idea arises that the term nonethnic Russian does not mean anything." These feelings are fueled by the appearance of attitudes of Russians who want "Russia for the ethnic Russians ["russkiye\']." The peoples of the North Caucasus, Mirsky says, "do not feel themselves to be ethnic Russians. That is both impossible and unnecessary. And then the term \'non-ethnic Russian\' turns out to be so nebulous" that they are unable or unwilling to see it as a reasonable substitute for or addition to their own ethnic national identities. It is difficult to speak about the existence of a non-ethnic Russian nation, although "the idea that there is a single nun ethnic Russian nation, in which are included various peoples and various ethnic groups does exist," Mirsky adds. But communicating that to ordinary people is "very difficult." "For the majority of the population [of the Russian Federation], there is a precise distinction: Russians and in general the Slavs are one thing, and the other people, especially the southern ones, are something quite different" Muslims feel this and thus have few reasons to re-identify as Moscow wants. There is an additional reason for that, he says. "Under Yeltsin the term \'non-ethnic Russian\' was introduced but it was interpreted so that there were both ethnic Russians and non-ethnic Russians. Ethnic Russians were Orthodox and patriots, but nonethnic Russians were those who took a pro-Western stance and wanted to eliminate Russian uniqueness." The Institute of Europe\'s Furman points to other problems in the post-Soviet period. When the union republics became countries with the demise of the USSR, he points out, "it turned out that none of the autonomies had the right to leave," and explaining that to a Chechen or a Tatar was morally "impossible." At that time, Moscow was confronted in the North Caucasus by "normal nationalism," and if [Russia] had really agreed to the independence of Chechnya in 1991-92, this would have been a state. Certainly not a very well organized one to be sure, but step by step it would have proceeded along a more or less human path." Instead, by opposing such independence, what Moscow obtained was "a pure formality." The Chechens say "we are with Russia forever" and formally enter the Federation. But we remain with an extremist underground," one that provides no basis for negotiations and that can "only be killed." But with regard to terms, Furman says, one must begin with the reality that "the North Caucasus is not Russia. Polls show that for ordinary Russians, people of the North Caucasus are more alien than say Ukrainians or Belarusians," an attitude that is reflected in calls for limiting immigration not from the latter but only with the former and other Muslim areas. In cultural terms, then, the North Caucasus today is "an absolutely alien territory which it is impossible to integrate [into the Russian Federation] in a genuine way." That means that the struggle against terrorism will and should continue there for a long time to come. Moscow can restrain and retain the area, Furman concludes, but this will be only "formally." Finally, Carnegie\'s Malashenko provides an insight into why Moscow is going to find it so difficult to come to terms with this divide. On the one hand, he notes, studying the Caucasus has not been prestigious or highly paid, unlike the study of France, the United States or even the Arab world, and consequently few Russians have done it. And on the other, the Russian powers that be are so sure that they do not need to understand this part of Russia that in no Russian embassy abroad is there some Russian diplomat "who speaks for example Tatar." Moscow officialdom is certain it can "conquer" or "modernize" the Caucasus, a reflection of both "laziness and lack of concern."']

Name: 5_migrant_australia_australian
Representative_Docs: ['OUR NEIGHBOURS\' TALK... Migration planners must face this trouble now "Australia\'s great post-war migration scheme – the "doubleor quit" programme advocated by all political parties since the first year of the fight against Japan pointed out the inability of native Australians to defend their own empty continent – has, so far, been eminently succesful. But, if it is to continue successfully -as it must – we will have to face right now the problem of human relationships between new Australians and the old. The foreign born element in now beginning to make itself felt in Australian communities, and the first ressentment of the old timer for the newcomers is stirring. une of the greatest worries of the Menzies-Fadden Govern – ment – or of any subsequent Gowemment – may well be to find out how to maintain the immigra tion programme the Commonwealth so desperately needs for its own security, against an ever-gro – wing pressure of public opinion to slow it down. These opinions are not merely my own, based upon a few score desultory abservations and interviews among migrants and their workmates. They are sha – red by many officials in both the Department of Immigration ad the Department of Labor and National Service, whose wholetime job is to know what is going on One employment officer put it in terms of chilly realism when he said: "There is a difference between needing migrants and wanting them. An increasing number of Australians are admitting among themselves that they don\'t want migrants. Perhaps it won\'t be long before they convince themselves thay don\'t need them. The memory of the man in the street is very brief." While the present employment boom lasts – while the country is still desperately short of people to make bricks and bathtubs and jam-tins – resentment of the influx of New Australians will remain inarticulate. But once the flush of an unprecedented prosperity fades and an element of competition again enters" job seeking, īhe volume\' of protest may surprisepeople who have been preoccupied only with the ~ long-term signifiance of\' a rapid population increase. Until now, most European migrants have been under complete Government control. Newcomers enter into a contract to remain in the employment to which they ere directed for two years and, na – turally enough, they have been directed into industries where the labor famine is most acute and where their presence is most welcome. From now on, however, increasing numbers will be released ffom this system of polite indenture and gravitate to more profitable and, consequently, more competitive fields. The very excellence of stan – dard demanded of first-comers by the Commonwealth\'s recruiting offivcers in Europe is now complicating smooth assimilation. Vigorous, well educated men and women were sought, and tens of thousands of them came to Austra lia. They have been working obidiently and industriously at awacl rates, in those occupations to which the Department of Labor and Industry found it most convenient to direct them. Few of them, however, make any secret of the fact that they have fiund their jobs uncongenial. They were grateful for the opportunity to make " the bargain and they are willing to keep theirpyt of it in letter and in spirit. But when their "time is up they will get out and seek work more in line with their tastes and capabilities. Many of these people are, by easy-going Australian standarts, almost terrifyingly frugal, industrious and enterprising.They have re ver before in their li – yes known anything like the economic sewurity which Australia promises them, and they are almost savagely determined to make every post a vinner. They have no intention whats> ever of remaining in the labor market. They aim to become employers themselves, and they save every penny they do not need for food, clothing and shelter to achieve that end. When they do begin their small businesses and industries, they are going to provide the indigenous Australian with competition fiercer than anything he has ever known. The changed commercial cli – mate af areas in Sydney and Melbourne favored by free Jewish migrants in recent years is probably only a foretaste of what will happen when Mr.Calwell\'s first hundred thousand find their feet. It is all very well being roadminded about this and saying that a liberal inflection of people who work hard and save is just what the indolent inheritors of this continent need. The observation may in itself be justified – but truth does not alter the fact that indigenous Australians will for a number of years yet choose the government of their country and they will be in a position to prohibit or modify beyond re – cognition any migration policy which becomes actively unpopular If smooth assimilation-both social and economic-cannot be assured for the thousands ol people now coming into this oaurt ry by every migrant ship, the pendulum of political action will, inevitably, swing in time with the pendulum oi public sentiment. The problem is one oi tremen dous complexity. The ex-Minister for Immigration (Mr Calwell) in wight years of office built up a highly efficient ādminis – •trative machine for the recruiting transportation and intro – duction into the Commonwealth of the new population we need to survive as a selfdeterminative nation – but the real job is only just begun. The job is to convince Aus – tralians that they need these newcomers even more urgently than the newcomers need the liberty and security Australia has to offer. There is already some evidence of this becoming the ease. Nothing is to be gained and a great deal is to be lost by ignoring the early signs and pretending that everything in the migration garden is lovely. In country districts where a considerable percentage of seaso – nal labor is supplied from the migrant pool, the hostility engendered by prejudice and distrust of foreigners is already marked. Towns like Albury, Wodonga, Shepparton and Mildura are buzing with stories abour the immorality, cunning and acquisitiveness of the jumpy, inarticulate "Baits" and "Jews" who are being drafted our in hundreds daily from the clearing centres. Wisdom of a quality rare in democratic government will be needed to keep this petty,rustic scandal from growing to the proportions of public opinion. It is not likely to be acquired by setting bureaucrats to study statistics. If a solution is ever to be found, it will be found in the intelligent and imaginative study of the human elements in the situation; by making a more effective effort ro prepare the migrant for his country of adoption, and by preparing the Australian public for the discomfort of the population graft upon which the country\'s survival may, a century hence, depend." (Osmar White, The Melb.Herald). * In a further article Osmar White praises the organisation set up by the outgoing Minister for Immigration (Mr.Calwell).he point out, however, that the assimilation of the newcomers must proceed more smoothly and if possible troubles are to be avoided, the further programme of the Department of Immigration must embrace a nation-wide movement for assimilation of European migrants. Churches, social organisations etc. have to participate. Finally, he tries id answer the question given to him by a number of fellow-Australians: "Whom do you think are the best migrants for Australia?" – "The ones who learn quickest to think and behave like Austra lians -no matter where the come from."', 'BALANCE SHEET OF FIVE YEARS MIGRATION balance-sheet of five years\' migration. In five years since the war, 460,560 migrants have come to Australia. Of them, according to a Melbourne "Herald" repport, 230,000 are British, 4,989 American, and 225,571 European. ;"«■:■■■- „^ttr«H "The great mass of Europeans are displaced persons, rendered homeless, and in many cases stateless, by the Second World War, says the "Herald" special correspondent. Latest available figures on nationalities are to June, when there were 190,000 DP\'s in Australia. Poles headed the list with 55,330, followed by Italians (not essentially D.P\'s) 19,472, Latvians 16,379, Ukrainians 15,750, Yugoslavs 14,573, Lithuanians 9,293. The remainder is made of small groups from places like Estonia and Greece. How is Australia benefiting from the arrival of these migrants? Main immediate value of the New Australians is their contribution to the work force of the Commonwealth. They have been put into "tough" industries which Old Australians were not keen to tackle. A good example is the job of maintenancing the east-west railway line over the Nullarbor Plains. The following table shows the industrial distribution of the D.P. work force of #7,824 last November 24:— Primary Production. — Timber, 2.5 per cent.; rural, 5.2 p.c.; mining, 1.4 p.c; total, 9.1 p.c. Capital Goods Production. — Building, construction, \' 2.4 p.c; bricks, tiles, etc., 9.5 p.c; metals, engineering, vehicles, IJK7 p.c; roads, waterworks, electricity, 25.9 p.c; total, 53.5 p.c. Consumer Goods Manufacturing. — Food, 3.7 p.c; textiles, clothing, 7.2 p.c; rubber, chemicals, etc, 4.2 p.c; total, 15.4 p.c. Transport — Railways, tramways, 3.5 p.c; P.M.G., 2.2 p.c; health, 7.2 p.c; defence, 1.3 p.c; total, 14.2 p.c. Private \' Services. — Commerce, retail trade, 1.2 p.c; professional 0.1 p.c; domestic service, 6.8 p.c; total, 8.1 per cent. In the early days of post-war migration, union leaders were chary about welcoming New Australians, fearing they might swamp the Labor market. These fears have been proved unfounded. In November last year \'there were 125,000 vacant jobs registered with the Commonwealth Employment Service—a record. States which had absorbed the most D.P. workers had the highest number of vacancies. In some instances D.P. migrant contribution has been spectacular. In 1950 migrant labor produced 75 million bricks representing 5000 brick houses, and 10 million roofing tiles; 120 million super feet of milled timber (15,000 timber \'homes); 240,000 tons of cement (20 per cent, of total production), and 150,000 tons of steel (12.5 per cent, of total). Female migrants, by providing domestic labor, have prevented numerous small hospitals closing ir. several States, and have enabled others to be opened. This has been the case particularly with T.B. hospitals. On the credit side of the balance-sheet must go the culture migrants are bringing with them from the Old World to the New —ballet, music, art. Canberra authorities claim that nost migrants are independent ;ypes, who are eager to make :heir own way in a new land. Some are already succeeding. George Mezey and Frank Dsucksy arrived in Australia in Vfarch, 1949. They are now runling their own brick factory outside Canberra, supplying large ļuantities of bricks to the Works Department. They invented a nachine which incorporated a iovel compressed air brick-makng technique. The Eg family — father and ;hree sons — reached Australia :rom Denmark unassisted, less ;han two years ago. They were iercely independent and determined. They roused up enough English between them to ask where they could find work. Someone directed them to the cold stores at Pyrmont, Sydney. A single room was hired as sleeping quarters. For six months the sons worked double shifts while the father went to Newcastle to work in a fishing trawler. At the end of six months the sons joined the father, and between them they bought one of the biggest trawlers working out of Newcastle. They are now independent fishermen. Employers and immigration authorities agree that, generally, New Australians are good, conscientious workers. That is the credit side of the balance-sheet. Old Australians, who find fault with the new, lay charges of arte gance, and point to reports of crime — knife play in particular. To some extent these points are fair criticism. Migrants who carry knives must be taught that in Australia arguments are settled at the very worst with fists. One of the most complicated aspects of mass migration to Australia has been the separation of husbands and wives during the two-year directed labor period. Wives with children have remained in holding camps, while husbands have been sent to other States. In these conditions, loneliness and jealousy thrive and create trouble. In a recent New South Wales case, Detective-Sergeant Woodward said: "Forced separation of husbands and wives is the direct cause of much trouble among migrants." Considerable bitterness has been created by the forceful way New Australians have hunted accommodation outside camps Home-seeking Old Australians level charges of arrogance and bad manners. The eagerness of many migrants to get even rooms of their own is actually more pathetic than anything else. What they crave is a little privacy — a luxury many of them have no* known since 1939.." The "Herald" Special Correspondent sums up his article in urging his country-men to assist New Australians in every way so that they may find their place in the new community as quickly as possible: "The New Australians are here to stay. Animosity will only prolong the process of making them Old Australians."', 'The Why and How of Post-War Immigration By ARTHUR A. CALWELL, M.H.R., Deputy Leader of the Federal Opposition. Excerpts from a paper read at the 1953 Summer School of the Australian Institute of Political Science. It is literally true to say that, g no other immigrant receiving I tl country had such grave prob- P lems to solve as those which A faced Australia when the mil- t( lions of war-weary British n people, and the dis-possessed c and displaced millions wander- c ing in Europe on this side of tl the Iron Curtain, sought to es- a cape to the new world. It is si also true that no other country c faced up to its task, nor did r more for those who came to n seek its citizenship, than did ti Australia. Second only to the t< United States in the total num- g ber of persons admitted, Austra- 1 lia was more humane in its p consideration of each individual\'s s needs and gave more attention t to individual difficulties than r any other country. In addition, c people were admitted on com- p passionate grounds, whether they v were assisted or non assisted by r Government agencies, much f earlier than in any other conn- \\ try Australia also took more f dependent elderly and juvenile r close relatives than did any > other country. In short, Aus- r tralia\'s post-war immigration 1 policy has succeeded beyond the \\ most optimistic hopes of those s who were charged with the re- 1 sponsibiliiy or framing it and r putting it into operation, just £ seven short years ago. c The main purpose of this £ paper is not to deal with the r success and failures of the i scheme. There were some fail- c ures, but they were mostly un- £ avoidable. Nothing was ac- j complished according to a com- t plete detailed plan. There was 1 no time for its preparation. It s was, however, a time for imagi- t i nation, initiative and, above all, c for hard work. If, in the light s of the experience gained, this t country were again undertak- i ing the transfer of large num- < bers of people here, things 1 would, of course, be done some- ( what differently. Experience i has taught its lessons, but as \' this generation is never again < likely to be confronted with a i similar problem, it is somewhat i idle to talk of variations and ] improvements in methods for I the selection and transport of ; a vast army of new settlers un- » less, of course, another World I War leaves the same sort of ) aftermath as did Worldd War 11. ; The effects of the new environment on the migrants and : the problems they faced fitting into their new homes and (he ! Australian community is the principal contribution thia paper is intended to make. Tailing the aspect of environmental \' effects first, it is generally accepted that this is of less con- 1 sequence in the case of children, adolescents and young people than it is with those passed 30 years of age. Not that children have not had to meet certain difficulties, due principally to peculiarity of speech and dress. There have been cases in which they have suffered humiliations and distress at the hands of other children, but generally, after a little while they have all been accepted and have won positions in school and sport life on their own merits and, in some instances, have surpassed their Australian born classmates. Being bi-lingual and even tri-lingual, the new students have a distinct advantage. Few, if any, nave failed to learn and speak English quickly. With those under 30. and passed school age, the problems of adaption and absorption, are in most instances quickly overcome. But as people advance in\' age, so the difficulties of assimilation correspondingly increase. Success will vary from person to person, and in some cases will be small. However, where there are children in the family there need be no fear of any great unhappiriess for the parents, or even for the grandparents. The children will be the interpretive and the liaison force as they have been and are in the United States and Canada. The settled policy of the Department of Immigration is to discourage the segreition of racial groups and in tat respect they have well, owever, the attitude of the ustralian people themselves wards the newcomers is of ajor importance. Segregation in often be the result of ostrasm by thoughtfless sections of ie Australian people. Fortun,ely xenophobia has died down imewhat in our midst and the "ivironmental difficulties cor■spondingly diminished for the 2w people. In any case, Ausalia has never had to conmd with such large national roupings as America has had. i spite of such groupings, the roblems that have arisen from ich separate racial communies in the United States have ot prevented the first Amerim born generation of such eople from becoming loyal, deoted, Americans. The same ?sults will happen with the rst generation born of those •ho have decided to join their ite with ours and share a comīon destiny with us in the ears that lie ahead. This is ot to say that in the particuir tragic circumstances in rhich nearly 200,000 people aught asylum and a new homeand here, there will not be iany who will never be fully ssimilated. The poignant memries of depression and tyranny | t the hands of Nazi and Com- | īunist dictatorships, the hauntlg fears for the safety of loved I nes whom they may never see gain, the thoughts of happier I esteryears and the doubts as 3 the extent to which they will c permitted to play and fill a atisfying part in the profes- j ional, industrial and social life, f the community, and the deire to preserve their own culural values, all play a part in etarding absorption. These ften sad eyed people want to ie accepted and want to give f their best, but many of them emain uncertain and nostalgic. This is not unnatural in view f their tribulations and the nental tortures they have sufered as well as their natural •ride in the particular race to yhich they belong. With the oung, the problem is less than vilh adults. Considered broady, and with certain reservations, he environmental difficulties re not of economic origin. They belong to the spirit. At i recent All-Australian Latvian festival held in Melbourne, Proessor P. Jurevics spoke what s probbaly the general opinion >f the leaders of all the Euro>ean national groups that have :ome to Australia in the postvar years. Concluding his adiress he said:— "It takes many years, many generations to assimilate a different culture. If one has lost one\'s own culture, it does not mean that one has already adopted the culture of others—in most cases it simply means that one has no culture at all any more, that one has become a barbarian. For adult people it is almost impossible to adopt another culture—they are separated from it by all things—by) language, by entirely different customs, by a strange approach to things, by the lack of answers to their own problems. The situation is rendered yet more hopeless when, as is actually the case, we, as predestined "labourers" and "domestics," as a shipwrecked people, have not even access to the cultured circles . . . "Insofar as we save our cultural inheritance and possibly develop it further can we hope to preserve our full human dignity and not to slide into barbarism. That this should not happen is not only in our own interest, but also in the interest of the society in which we now live. Let us not forget that among the cultural values safeguarded by the national community are also the moral values. If a person has torn himself away from his old community, and at the same time has not been able to become a member of another, then that type of man is created who initiates all catastrophies—the completely isolated human being." Expressed in a sentence, the newcomers believe that man does not live by bread alone. Tolerance and a sympathetic understanding as well as a readiness to help and a determination to beat down prejudice will pay rich dividends for ourselves and for our new found friends in both the short-term and long-term view. The criticism of some Australians of foreign-born people speaking a language other than English in trams and trains and shops tends to create barriers. When Australians travel abroad very few of them can speak the language of each or, indeed, any country they visit in Europe and behave exactly as do the new arrivals here in conversation with their own nationals. Very often, the criticism heard comes from Australians whose knowledge of their own language or of any other language is by no means perfect. Migrants needs are capable of satisfaction and must be satisfied if they are to be happy and content. They want homes. If they are agricultural workers they want an opportunity to buy land at reasonable prices. If they are professional people, they want reasonable treatment in the recognition of their academic degrees and professional status. If they are tradesmen, or are possessed of any special skills or aptitudes, they want a chance to utilise their talents and their knowdledge to the best advantage of themselves and the community. By self help and hard work, quite a percentage of the migrants have acquired land and have built, or are building or are contemplating building, homes of their own. Their example is worthy of emulation by many Australians and by many British mi-I grants. I When it comes to professional people, frustration and unfriendliness have been their common experience at the hands of the Australian professions. Medical boards, dental boards, veterinary boards, engineers\' and lawyers\' institutes and all similar groupings of our Universitytrained people have been about as non-co-operative as they could be in according professional status to those among the new Australians who have sought it. Again, State legislation bars the way to a fair deal for the newcomers and the ] rights conferred by it are jeal-1 ously guarded by the practitioners concerned. Fortunately under the Australian Constitution, the Commonwealth Parliament has complete legislative authority over Commonwealth Territories, such as the Northern Territory, the Australian Capital Territory (Canberra) and the Territory of Papua and New Guinea. By virtue of this authority, the Commonwealth Department of Health four years ago appointed a number of Aus- t tralian doctors to examine more than forty European doctors. They selected over thirty, both J men and women, for service as medical officers in Papua and New Guinea. These European doctors have acquitted themselves very well, and their professional skill has been availed of by residents as well as by I natives. No Australian doctors ļ wanted to go there, so New Australian doctors had to be sent or the Communities left largely without doctors. But the interesting point is that! when the New Australians re-1 turn to the mainland they will, under existing conditions, find I themselves, to all intents and purposes, unable to secure registration in any of our States. America does not act this way and unless our professional people will make further advances on the slight progress registered so far in the recognition of the degrees of the new people concerned, many hundreds, and with their families some thousands, of our new citizens will feel great difficulty in fitting into their new Lomeland. As a contrast to the refusal of professional people to help carry out our immigration programme, there is the ready assistance given by servicemen\'s organisations in granting membership to those of the new arrivals who served in the World War 11. in armies under allied commands. But while this is pleasing, the attitude of the Navy and Air Force Chiefs to accepting into their services young people who are still tech-1 nically aliens is hard to under-1 stand. Even the Army which is not so rigid in its refusal to | accept enlistments from alien ranks, also places difficulties in the way of those wishing to serve Australia. Briefly put, there are still many discriminations against, and much passive resistance towards, our new citizens in the highest professional and defence quarters. There is in most other places a willingness to assist and co-operate which has made our bi-partisan Immigration scheme the most successful of any of those operating anywhere since World War 11. finished. This survey does not cover arrivals under the Dutch. Italian and German Migration Agreements negotiated since the Menzies Government took office at the end of 1949. It deals with those who came before that date, and those who arrived during 1950 and early in 1951 under arrangements which had been made by the Chifley Government, and the alterations made to the decisions by the Menzies Government. The migrants from Britain, and the relatively few from the other Dominions, and from America, and from Ireland, have not experienced the same difficulties as the migrants from European countries. The fact that there is a common bond of language makes it easier for such people to fit in more easily into our national framework. More important as a bond that binds than language, or a common cultural heritage, or degrees of consanguinity is the possession of common religious beliefs and so the kindly understanding and enduring work of the different religious groups in welcoming new arrivals is to be commended. The importance of it cannot be too strongly emphasised. To the extent that this work has been performed the benefit has been widespread, to the extent that it has been neglected, it is unfortunate. Happily, few new arrivals have not received the welcoming hand and the kindly gesture they would like to receive from their co-religionists, whatever the denomination. If it were not for the Churches the work of reception and settlement and assimilation would have been largely in vain. To all who have taken a part in the great adventure and given their services generously and often anonymously, the thanks of this generation, and of posterity, are due in full and handsome measure. The nation is the beneficiary of the Immigration scheme carried out by both the Chifley and the Menzies Governments and that, to all who participated in the preparation of the plans and their execution, is both enough reward and enough satisfaction.']

Representative_Docs: ['On the Bank of the Amata Arveds Svabe The Latvian folk-tales i. c. fairy-tales and legends were collected considerably later and written down in comparatively lesser number than the folk-songs. The elementary school teacher and writer Ansis Lerchis-Puskaitis (1859—1903) commenced the systematic collecting and publishing of folk-tales and collected 6000 legends and fairy-tales. His work (7 volumes, 1891—1903), however, remained unfinished. At the same time, the Latvian Learned Societies published some small collections of fairytales Unfortunately all these collections lacked system or the system adopted was based on theories (c. g. B. B. Tylor\'s doctrine of animism), already given up by scientists. In Northern Europe and in the Baltic, the scientific investigation of fairytales more and more followed the so-called Finnish School established by the Finnish folklorists X a a r 1 c X r oh n and Antti Aarne with their internationally known series of publications "Folklore Fellow Communications." A. Aarne\'s merit was that of succeeding in setting up, after a long investigation, the first general schedule for the types of fairytales (Verzeichnis der M&rchentypen, Helsinki 1910) which contained 485 types of fairy-tales and their variations widely known in Europe. According to this Aarne\'s schedule, I already in 1923 commenced to compile anew and to publish the Latvian fairy-tales (8 booklets), but I was prevented from finishing this work. The Latvian synologist Peteris Smits (1869—1938) succeeded in doing this. He compiled anew and published, from-1925 till 1938, the most complete collection of the Latvian fairy-tales and legends in 15 volumes (7636 pages) containing 7895 items including variations. This collection closely respects the principles set up by Aarne. It is advisable to find out the difference theoretically between legends and fairy-tales though practically it is difficult to draw the boundary line, for a legend can turn into a fairy-tale as easy as a fairy-tale can turn into a legend. The fairy-tale is a story about wonderful events occurring outside historical time and a fixed geographical spot. A formula like "once upon a time, once, long ago, in ancient times, in early times" etc is usually used to point out the time, or — the time of the events is not mentioned at all and merely the verbs in the past give an indication to the hearers that the activity is not of today. The spot of the events is pointed out by such formulas: "far, very far away," or "in the world, in a land, in a town, in a parish, on a hill, on an estate, in apoor cottage" etc In the most ancient fairy-tales this favourite formula is used: "Beyond three-timesnine (3X9) forests, three-times-nine seas, threetimes-nine hills in a land far away." As is ascertained now this formula used in the Latvian fairy-tales, folk-songs, and magic has originated in Asia Minor and is connected with the lunar caLATVIAN FOLK-TALES lendar based upon the phases of the waxing and the waning moon. Astronomy, cultivated by priests, imputed especial magical meaning to the figures 3, 9 and 3X9. Therefore in the oriental fairy-tales composed by the educated classes very often occur: 3 king\'s sons, 3 devils, 3 dragons, 3 snakes having 3, 6 and 9 heads which the hero has to hew off with a sword weighing 3, 6 or 9 "birkavs" — an ancient Latvian weight of about 3 cwt), before this having strengthened himself with a restorative from 3 vessels. The formula 9 or 3X9 was profaned later and what the ancient Latvians meant by it was that the numbers of things was so large that it was not possible to count them by the fingers of both arms c. g- the vessel cracked into three-times-nine pieces. The description of the heroes and other persons mentioned in the fairy-tales is in the same measure indefinite as the time and the space. These persons usually are not called by names, only their professions, social positions and relationships are mentioned: a king, the king\'s son or daughter, a man and his wife, a sister and her brother, three brothers, an old soldier, a beggar, an apprentice seeking his master, a traveller, an inn-keeper, a peasant, an owner of an estate, a step-mother and her step-children, God and the devil etc The heroes are not characterised in detail — only one or two physical or spiritual qualities are given (foolish — clever, bad — good, beautiful — ugly, strong — feeble) which then determine their activity and even their fate of them. When yet sometimes the heroes are called by names (c. g. Bearkiller, Pinedrawer, Hillroller etc) then they cannot be regarded as real proper names but only as those denoting the inherited qualities of the heroes: a man who is capable to kill bears, to pull out pinetrees, to roll away hills etc On the other hand, a legend is always connected with a fixed place or person. The fairy-tale tells about "a king,\'\' the legend tells about King Priam, Romulus or fCurbads, the hero of Kurzeme *(Couronia). The fairy-tale mentions "a castle", but in the legend the events occur at a fixed geographical spot c. g. Riga, the Daugava etc. Contrary to the fairy-tales where only little attention is paid to the previous life of the heroes, the legends try to explain where, when and why the event happened in such a way and not otherwise. The legend is also history, but history of a legendary kind. Therefore, contrary to the fairy-tales which follow international themes, legends handle national motives or try at least to localize travel motives. The authors of fairy-tales let their heroes act in the irrational world of magic together with or in opposition to miraculous forces and prove their action by rules of voluntary and emotional think ing, while the authors of legends try to solve their problems by means of rational, discursive thinking. Of the Latvian legends, we must mention the cosmogonial legends in the first place- They have originated partly under the influence of the Bible stories dealing with the creation of the earth, but in distinction from the Bible, God does not act single-handed but together with the devil in the Latvian cosmogonial legends. There are comparatively few folk-traditions of such a kind in Western Europe, but many in the Eastern regions of ancient Byzantium, Armenia, Mesopothamia, Syria. In the time from the Bth till the 10th century the Christian gnostics and other sectants brought them to the Balkan Peninsula especially to Bulgaria and Roumania. It is to be supposed that these Balkan cosmogonial traditions with the mediation of the Western Slavs (Poles) have travelled into the Baltic where they spread widely. The doctrine about God and the devil as two creators of the world, however, has not originated in Byzantium but corresponds to the Persian cosmogonial dualism according to which Ahuramazda, assisted by Ariman, created the world. There is. however, a difference namely that the devil in the Latvian myth does not help God, but aims to disturb and destroy his work, or to create evil beings which then try to exterminate God\'s beings. For example, the legend that the wolf is created by the devil is known to all Baltic nations md to the Russians, Poles, Roumanians and Albanians. Altough the cosmogonial dualism is not a Latvian doctrine the figures of God and the devil have gained real national features to such a degree that they cannot be deparated from history of Latvia. The sociological aspect of the Latvian devil is formed according to the prototype of the Baltic "baron," but the Latvian God is only an idealized old farmer and their mutual antagonism reflects the centuries long struggle between the Latvian peasant and the German estate owner. By way of contamination from some other cycles of fairy-tales in these cosmogonial legends God can be replaced by a clever or strong man (c. g. the clever brother, strong Bearkiller etc) and the devil — by an evil giant or monster (c- g. a dragon, a bear etc). The motif itself — about the fatal struggle between the just and the unjust, the good and the evil — remains unchanged- To illustrate this it suffices to remind how th Latvian fairy-tales and legends describe the devil- People call him ..Black Master" because, contrary to the Latvian peasant wearing gray homemade clothes, the German knights and feudals wore black tailored suits. The devil goes about in a luxurious carriage driven by a coachman or rides on a magnificent riding-horse to inspect his district- The devil has a large countryestate and a castle with many rooms, in the cabinet there is a bookcase containing many books in foreign languages, mainly German, there are old chronicles, luxurious works on arts and necromancy- The devils speak German among themselves though they speak Russian too, but with their servants the devils speak only Latvian. The devils engage the Latvians as their coachmen, servants or common workmen and they have to be skilled in all peasats\' works such as ploughing, sowing, mowing, thrashing, grinding and baking bread for his master s needs- When engaging a servant the devil demands that the servant should not know neither to read nor to write, nor to speak any other language but Latvian- It is dear that this devil of the fairy-tales is composed by the fancy of the people from elements which were to be found on the German knights1 estates. On the other hand — the devil is usually represented in a legend as an envious neighbour who tends to disturb the seasonable works of the busy and honest farmer — namely God — sowing weeds into his fields, strewing stones onto his meadows, clamming up rivers in order to destroy mills etc The so-called aethiological legends are closely related to the just named cosmogonial ones. These aethiological legends try to explain in a naive manner, often according to the pattern of the Bible, why some animals or plants have this or another shape, colour or function. For example the Latvian, Polish and Roumanian legends explain the well-known fact that the mole runs across the road with God\'s prohibition for it to run along the road because the mole did not obey the invitation to join the workmen once building the road. Likewise people tend to explain why the ash is called the blind tree, why the raven is black, why the fox has a white tail-top, why the cat catches mice, why the wolf has a black snout, why the hare has a split lip etc. Both these groups of legends gradually change into geographical and historical legends. Their international subjets, having magical, balladical or romantical arrangement are often localized. There are legends about hills made by devils; about lakes fetched by God from one place to another in the shape of a cloud in order to drown a castle or a farm to punish it; about mysterious churches on the shore of the sea, where a ship arrives in the night once a year and the priest has to marry a strange couple; about the church-bells sunk down in a swamp; about a cave in the rocks where a beautiful young woman is held imprisoned. Being prose compositions, legends generally are shorter and simpler in composition than fairy-tales. Legends whose kernel of the fable has grown up from a magical or religious belief can be considered as the elder, those where an anecdote or an adventure is arranged in the form of a short story of the Renaissance period can be taken for younger legends. On the other hand, fairy-tales as artistic prose compositions bear evidence of a certain literary technique, elaborated by unknown masters, the stylistic and compositional means of which are to be found in folk-epics too. The. fault of the earlier compilers and publishers of the fairy-tales was nut of considering each fairy-tale as an independent original with its homogeneous composition They separated only those compositions which differred from a type or cycle of similar fairy-tales as to their language (c. g. written in a dialect) or Couple of Latvian folk-dancers at D. P. Camp Meerbeck Display of national costumes at D. P. Camp Meerbeck style. But careful literary analysis proved that, in spite of the difference in language and style, many nations had common fairy-tales, and that the likeness existed not only as to their contents but also as to such trifles of their form as the so-called polygenesis theory, teaching that similar legends and fairy-tales were composed by various ancient nations without any mutual influence, could no longer be recognized. The anthropologists supporting this theory devoted their attention merely to the mythological contents of the fairy-tales, but kept their literary form in the background. On the contrary, the Aryan school, established by philologists, regarded fairy-tales as heritage common to all Aryans in the same way as the Indo-European languages. They thought that the fairy-tales had originated by way of the degeneration of myths, but myths according to them had grown up from the cult of nature. This they tried to prove with the writings of the ancient Hindoos. So this movement, contrary to the school of the anthropologists, ackonowledges monogenesis instead of polygenesis but only as far as it concerns the spiritual heritage of the ancient Indo-Europeans. The interest about the ancient Hindoo literature increased by the efforts of .linguists, urged the sanskritist Th. Benfey to establish a new theory about the origination of fairy-tales. Th. Benfey seeks the birthplace of fairy-tales in India, where they have been composed by the buddhists. Following the expansion of Buddhism, fairy-tales were brought first to China, later to Tibet and Mongolia, but during the time of the Crusade, together with the Arabian fairy-tales, also to the Balkan, Italy, Spain, where they were translated into Latin already in the 13th century and began their triumph over the whole continent. Thus contrary to the theories mentioned above, which sought the origin of fairy-tales in the time of the prehistorical myth, Th. Benfey considers them as comparatively young artistic prose works expanded over various countries by means of writings. No doubt, this theory exaggerates the part of India played in the origination and that of literature played in the expansion of the fairy-tales. The author himself had to recognize that India could not be the birthplace of legends but this was more probably to be sought in Greece, where Aesop composed the oldest samples in this branch. It is impossible, too, to prove that most of the fairytales have spread among the people by means of books, for during the Middle Ages the peasants and artisans did not know how to read. Thus, for example, the translation of „The Arabian Nights" appeared in Latvian only in 1866, but the Latvian arrangement of Aesop\'s fables in 1766 while the verbal traditions of the Latvians and other Baltic peoples knew fairy-tales and legends with contents resembling these much earlier. Th. Benfey*s theory had an important influence on the development of the science of comparative folklore. His theory taught the scientists to regard fairy-tales as artistic prose works and to look for their patterns not only in the area of the Indo-European culture, but also in countries of Buddhism and Mohammedanism, pointing out that the origination of fairy-tales under the influence of some other confessional system did not prevent the import of them into the world of the Christian civilisation. In possession of the chief idea of Th. Benfey s doctrine — about the spreading of fairy-tales — the Finnish folklorists\' school utilized it in its further research work proving that fairy-tales and legends as to their composition were easily to be separated into the original sujets and the literary motives which though typically similar with certain cycles of fairy-tales, but like a mosaic could be removed from them and used for another composition. Such a deforming of fairy-tales and legends can be explained by the fact that the tellers, knowing by heart 20—70 long tales, often forgot the beginning or the end of a tale or mixed the persons and contents of one tale with those of another thus causing defective text or contamination of it. Therefore, the Finnish school pays much attention to the critic of the text of the fairy-tales and legends and treats them in separate monographies with the usual philological methods of the history of literature. There are many instances among the Latvian fairy-tales showing how by forgetfulness the original text of a fairy-tale is altered. For example, the fairy-tale about the sorcerer\'s ring in its oldest version is to be found in India, and the kernel of it is what Buddhism teaches that beasts often can be more good-hearted than human beings: a Brahmin rescues from the gap a tiger, a monkey, a snake and a man; the beasts present him several worthy articles but the rescued man charges the Brahmin of theft so that the latter is sentenced to death. Then the snake rescues him. In this fairy-tale spreading from India to Finnland by verbal traditions the tropical beasts are replaced by arctical beasts, it loses Its dogmatical character too and the grateful beasts begin to play the main part until the ungrateful man disappears in the background and, in the end even vanishes. In some Latvian variations the beasts are forgotten too and the snake\'s present — the magical ring — becomes a remedy which enables the hero to gain the king\'s daughter. The opposite to forgetting is the amplification of fairy-tales by extending the beginning and the end of the tales with superfluous adornments or even with an episode from another analoguous fairy-tale. In the same manner as the short Latvian folk-songs often are strung into long chains, the teller of fairy-tales too joins many legends on the same theme (c. g. about God and the devil, about the fox and the wolf) in order to create a whole cycle of legends or even an epos. To escape some ocular dissonances which could be created by the alteration of something of little value, sometimes the whole contents of the tale has to be varied. These alterations are to be explained by the tendency of a talented teller to take an active part in altering the tale, to feel an independent author and to prevent the tale from becoming tedious. Even today actors are doing the same varying by altering and completing their rolls which they have to play many times. As mentioned before the poetics of fairy-tales and legends makes use of stylistic and compositional means used in the epic folk-poesy. In order to attract the listeners attention, the teller repeats three times a certain hero\'s action letting him overcome increasing difficulties gradually. Sometimes instead of such a gradation retardation is used in heroic tales, adding some unnecessary episodes to adjourn the expected solution. In some other cases when localization of the text takes place, a special or proper name is generalized (c. g. the Bulbul-Bird mentioned in the Arabian Nights, is called by the Latvians simply "magic bird" though its original meaning is that 6f a nightingale) or otherwise: an impersonal underground combatant receives the Latvian names — Kurbads, Lacausis, Dzelzs Martins (Martin Iron), Stiprais An*\'* (Jack the Strong) or simply Martins and Ansis. The real fairy-tales, however, respect the rule that the story must end happily so that the listener should remains thoroughly convinced that the good overcomes the evil, the just beats the unjust. As in the conscience of the Latvian nation, feudalism which made the German dominators of all legal and economic power, was the cause of nearly all evil and unjust, it is clear to every one of what socialpsychological importance the compositional manner of the folk-tales was to bring the dramatic struggle between the bright forces and the dark ones to such and end where the devil or his substitute was always defeated. The optimism of the folk-traditions strengthened morally the Latvian nation and maintained its belief that the divine truth, in fairy-tales represented by an old man in peasant\'s clothes (God) or by a national hero sent by God, sooner or later would help Latvia to regain all what was lost during her wars of liberation against the German invaders in the 13th century. This belief was connected with the conviction that in the world, besides power and cunning, reigns a good spirit, too, which, at the proper time and place, interferes personally or with his messenger (helpful beasts) into the struggle and; brings forth a favourable solution even in an apparently hopeless situation. Thus the divine stage-manager, by his intervention, prevents drama of the fairy-tale from turning into a tragedy ! It is a miracle from the view of discursive thinking, but we must keep in mind that the civilized! nations even now have belief in miracles and that the mentality supporting this belief even in times of our expressive rationalism has its firm footing; that some peculiar rules of thinking and some\' peculiar conceptions of causality are reigning there and it is the task of anthropology to clear them all up. It is true that nearly all primitive peoples! have their common beliefs in the human body and! soul, good and evil spirits, beasts and plants, their methamorphosis and the travel of souls. But the scientists are mistaken admitting that a myth, cult or belief can become a literary motif, to say nothing of their arrangement in the shape of a fairytale. Every fairy-tale is invented by an author. We do not know what inspired him to choose just such a compositional form and not another-this could be some visions seen in a dream, the nightmare of a sick man, visions caused by narcotics or the tendency of a talented artist to create his own artistic world. Be that as it may — when such fairy-tales composed by some individual authors pleased the listeners for the sake of their theme, arrangement or idea, they grew popular, spread from mouth to mouth, were remodeled and even vulgarized to suit the taste of uneducated classes but even then it is possible for the criticism of sources to trace the routes of the literary tradition and to discover their birth-places. This principle generally acknowledged in aesthetic folklore can be applied to the Latvian fairy-tales too: they usually have strange borrowed motifs and patterns, but as a literary commodity they circulate among people bearing their own national trademark. Translated by Adotfs Ermanovskis.', 'The great majority of Latvian writers went into exile towards the end of the Second World "War, and it is only since then that Latvian literature has been able to develop freely outside Latvia. Among the authors who had to stay behind, those of the older generation have fallen silent, except a few who had already in the past evinced leanings towards Communism. The Soviet occupation authorities have encouraged new literary talent, but the dictates of the Party have been too rigorous to allow any development of individual style or original thought among the young writers. Any genuinely gifted young author (Heislers, Laganovskis, Cielava, Puris) who has tried to pursue something of an individual path, has been severely reprimanded and brought back to the Party line. But the occasional rebellion of a courageous mind shows that the freedom of the spirit cannot be entirely smothered even under an absolute tyranny. The Soviet occupation authorities have done their utmost to prevent the works of Latvian exiles from reaching the reading public in Latvia. Only a veTy few works written in exile have been reprinted in Latvia (The Green Book by J. Jaunsudrabins and an essay by Anslavs Eglitis). Yet there is an enormous interest in Latvia for everything published in exile; the few books which By JĀNIS ANDRUPS LATVIAN LITERATURE IN A CHANGING WORLD (Translated from, the Latvian by Ruth Speirs) reach the country illegally pass from hand to hand, and Party-controlled Riga newspapers find it necessary to protest from time to time against modern trends in Latvian exile poetry. The Latvian writer in exile unfortunately has no means of satisfying his nation\'s eagerness for genuine literary values. He has to find his reading public among the 100.000 Latvians who live in exile, and has to hope and wait for the barriers to fall. At the beginning of this century, the great Latvian poet, Rainis, spent 13 years in Switzerland as a political refugee; yet the Czarist regime did not forbid his books to be circulated in Latvia. The present Russian regime imposes far worse oppression over things of the spirit than the Czars ever did. Latvian publishers in exile, although supported only by the relatively small number of refugees, have so far been able to ensure the publication of works by Latvian exiles. Novels and volumes of short stories continue to sell at about 2.000 copies each, and this covers the costs of publication, but books of poetry present rather a problem because they have a more limited reading public. Authors\' fees, both for books and for writings in magazines or the newspapers, have dwindled; the continuance and development of Latvian literature in exile has been made possible by sacrifices on the part of the authors themselves: they earn their living not by the pen but by other work of different kinds. The future of Latvian literature in exilei depends on the new generation both of writers and of readers. There is as yet no cause for anxiety as far new authors are concerned—fresh talent has emerged in exile, particularly in the field of poetry. But there are indications that the reading public is beginning to decrease because the young generation, now in their twenties, have received their schooling and education in various parts of the Western world where they have grown up speaking the language of their adopted country. Although the majority of these young people know their native language very well the fact remains that books written in Lavian are not so indispensable to ill em as to the older generation. Among the Latvian writers who went into exile towards the end of the Second World War theTe were some representatives of the generation of authors who had prepared the way spiritually for the independence of their country; such were Kārlis Skalbe, Janis Jaunsudrabins, Valdemārs Dambergs. Kārlis Skalbe, one of the most distinguished Latvian poets, crossed i lie Baltic in a fishing-boat to find exile in Sweden and thus came to Scandinavia for a second time—he had already spent the years 1907-09 at Oslo as a refugee from the Czarist regime. While in renewed exile, Skalbe only wrote a couple of poems and a fairy-tale for he died on April 15th, 1945. Valdemārs Dambergs, who followed a classical conception of form in his novels, lyrics and plays, spent his exile in Denmark where he died in the summer of 1960. Janis Jaunsudrabins, 84 years old, is the grand old man of Latvian literature. Being a great lover of water, he has settled down by Lake Mone at Korbeck, Germany; ho has also found a place in German literature through the German translations of some of his prose works. Jaunsudrabins continues writing about Latvian country life as seen through the eyes of a boy; his language is rich and vivid, and he has a very clear vision. Janis Veselis and Pēteris Ermanis belong to the generation of writers who contributed to  literature at about the time when Latvia gained her independence. Janis Veselis, in exile in the USA, has written further novels and teiksmas (legends), the latter being an art form he has adapted to his own purposes, and in which his considerable imaginative powers can find full scope, frequently leading him into the realm of mythology. Pēteris Ermanis, now in Germany, continues to write poetry whose themes are mainly derived from his memories of the past. The next generation of writers—Anslavs Eglitis, Knuts Lesins, Valdemārs Karklins, Girts Salnais, Teodors Zeltins—have contributed most to the further development of the novel and the short story. The best of what Anslavs Eglitis has written is to be found in a small, unpretentious volume of short stories, Burning City, which was published in 1946, in the early days of exile. Since then, he has rarely written short stories; one of them, The Best Seller, translated by Ruth Speirs, has recently appeared in "The Texas Quarterly", USA. Anslavs Eglitis has published a large number of novels in which we frequently find an element of satire as well as the device (often used by Somerset Maugham) of supplying his characters with features of recognizable prototypes. The highest achievement of Valdemārs Karklins, most of whose works have been written in exile, is also to be found in the short story (By the River of Time, a volume of short stories published in 1951). Three of his short stories, translated by Ruth Speirs, have appeared in "The Texas Quarterly" and in "The Hudson Review", USA. Valdemārs Karklins has not continued with the short story as his art form but has turned to the novel. His big trilogy (The Legend of the Seven Ships, God\'s Own Country, The Golden Bell) presents a vivid picture of the times and abounds in original characters; his novel, Nothing but Love, is the most comprehensive study of the problems of the Second World War that has so far appeared in Latvian literature. A volume of short stories by Knuts Lesins, The Wine of Eternity, translated by Ruth Speirs, was published by the University of Minnesota Press in 1957 and has had very favourable reviews in American papers. Knuts Lesins has avoided the deadlock which writers of psychological stories tend to reach, and the value of his latest short stories (Dead End Street, 1958) again lies in his ability to portray man by revealing his quiet inner life without introducing the noise and bustle of the outside world. Girts Salnais presents in his long novels^interesting types of people in a vividly realised milieu. These authors have broken away from the theme of traditional country life, a theme which had at times threatened to get bogged down in parochialism, and have greatly enlarged both the inner and the outer world in Latvian novels and short stories. Zenta Mauriņa, an essayist now domiciled in Sweden, is one of the few Latvian authors who have made themselves a name among readers of a different nation. Most of her writings in exile were originally in German and later published in Latvian, some of them also in Swedish. The English edition of her very remarkable book on Dostoievsky appeared in 1939, but it has since been revised and published also in German and Swedish. Her autobiographical trilogy (Die weite FaJirt, Derm das Wagnis ist schon, Die eisernen Riegel zerbrechen) has gained her good reviews and a large number of readers in Germany. Martins Zivrts, also in Sweden, is the most distinguished Latvian dramatist. He experiments with new forms in his plays, and presents tensely dramatic confliets with a minimum of actors on a stage which requires practically no decor or props. Although volumes of verse are the most unremunerative books to publish in exile, it is precisely in the field of Latvian poetry that we find new explorations and fresh talent. Throughout the ages, poetry has been the most vital and valuable part of Latvian literature, a medium in which the Latvian people\'s particular gifts and propensities have found their natural expression. The Latvians have about 200.000 published folk-songs which, in concentrated four-line stanzas, are a reservoir of poetic wealth. Their beginnings go back to the distant past, to the development of the Latvian people and their language, and they have constituted a potential source of inspiration for the modern lyric to draw on.
