As is the case with many other European nations, the Latvians did not develop their written poetry directly out of folklore but under the influence of foreign models, and this poetry has at various times been far removed from the style and the spiritual world of the folk-songs. But Latvian poets have always felt the challenge to bridge this gap and to compose poetry which is genuinely Latvian in style and content, with the folksong as its prototype. Such an attempt was made by the poet Janis Modems who evolved nine new Latvian metres by combining metrical elements taken from the folk-songs. Janis Medenis, who has written some of the most outstanding ballads and poems in Latvian literature, spent years in Kolima where he had been deported by the Russians. Released in 1956, he returned to Latvia broken in health, and died on May 10th 1961. Zinaida Lazda, who died in the USA in 1957, did not attempt to imitate the verse form of the folk-song but to revive its spirit in her own poetry in which problems of modern life are illumined by the spiritual world of the folk-song. Pēteris Aigars, another representative of the same generation, began by applying the techniques of urban poetry to the portrayal of Latvian country life. Later he turned more and more to the theme of the individual, writing easy , melodious verse with a wealth of colourful imagery. In recent years the lyricalepical element has\'again frequently appeared in his poetry; he has written many fables and satires. Andrejs Eglitis, who lives in Sweden, has a great diversity of style—he can be emotional and dynamic as he writes on present-day themes, and he can speak in muted tones when portraying individual experiences. His volume of poetry, On the Shield, 1946, contains war poems which have the hard, metallic form of French Renaissance verse, but in another volume of poetry, Otranto, 1958, he yields to the glittering rush of surrealist imagery. Veronika Strelerte, also in Sweden, has not sought to introduce new forms to increase the range of poetry. Instead, gifted with a very profound feeling for language, she uses1 carefully chosen words in new and unusual contexts. Her concentrated verse and economy of words have a certain kinship with the Latvian folk-isong. The first to explore new possibilities in Latvian poetry was Velta Sniķere, who lives in England. Her early poems, published about 1943, bore the stamp of surrealism. Her recent volume of poetry, Surmise Unceasing (Nemitas minamais; 1961), shows her outstanding gift for transmuting actual experience in poetic formulas which have the nature of magic incantation. The verse of Andrejs Pablo Mierkalns (resident in France) also bears the mark of surrealism, but combinedy with a strong element of satire and humour. Unfortunately, his life is overshadowed by illness, and he had to spend years in hospital. Closely related to him in their explorations are a group of young Latvian poets in the United States—Gunārs Salms, Linards Tauns, and Olafs Stumbrs. They have learnt much from modern American verse but are also followers of the Latvian poet, Aleksandrs Caks. (Caks began to write about 1925, under the influence of Mayakovsky and Imaginism, and developed a poetry individual in its content as well as in its imagery.) These poets are all engaged in a search for new forms, but each of them has distinct individuality and marked talent. Gunārs Salins has leanings towards epical poetry and a pronounced element of irony; Linards Tauns exults in an intoxication with life; Olafs Stumbrs has completely entered into the world of his personal poetic vision—like the Chinese painter of whom it is said that he, .collected his brushes and took up his abode in the picture he had just finished painting. Although these young poets have grown up in a non-Latvian community they have a very sensitive feeling for their own language and promise a further development of Latvian poetry. Poetry is the part of Latvian literature which could give most to a non-Latvian reader, yet "the deepest poetry is untranslatable and even incommunicable", and these poetic values remain locked up within the Latvian language itself. American, English, German, and even French verse has in the last decade ever more closely approached prose, becoming more international and easier to translate, and its intrinsic poetic qualities have been lost. But this has not happened in Latvian modern verse which continues to grow out of genuinely vigorous poetic sources.', 'Old Jelgava – Etching by A. Apinis Janis Rudzitis One of the things which European literature has inherited from the 19th century is the modern artistic fairy-tale. In Germany, favoured and warmed by the sunshine of Romanticism, this genre flourished during the first half of the last century. First of all the merits of the Brothers Grimm must be mentioned in this respect. Their collections of fairy-tales Kinder- und Hausm&rchen (3 vols, 1812—1822) and Deutsche Sagen (2 vols, 1816—1818) created a powerful impression. But the fairy-tale soon rises ,to an equally eminent place in literary creation outside Germany. In 1835 the Danish shoe-makers son Hans Christian Andersen publishes the first collection of his fairy-tales gradually becoming the most famous fairy-tale writer in the world. The modern fairy-tale crystallizes into its purest shape, however, at the end of the 19th century. The British writer Oscar Wilde, that sensitive adorer of beauty, is the man who, as soon as he takes up writing fairy-tales, feels bound neither by any didactic aims nor by a craving shared by vast circles of readers — the craving for characters of an unreally fantastic world and for an interesting plot. The brothers Grimm and Andersen have not been able to get quite free especially from didacticism; Wilde is interested only in the aesthetic possibilities offered by the genre of fairy-tales. He has not created much in this branch of literature, only two books: The Happy Prince (1888) and A House of Pomegranates (1889), but by their quality he becomes the master and the fulfiller of the aesthetic fairy-tale. The author adds a characteristic aknowledgement to his second book, namely that it was "intended neither for the British child nor the British public". Wilde died in 1900, and was followed in the literature of the large nations of Europe by no authors who could have taken over and continued his manner of writing fairy-tales. But incidentally it has come to pass that the year of Wilde\'s de*ath at the same time marks the birth of the modern fairy-tale of a small European nation. It is the Latvian writer Anna Brigadere who publishes her modern fairy-tale Mare (Mary) in 1900. In 1902 the writer Andrievs Niedra follows with his Zemnieka dels (A Peasant\'s Son), in the sub-title directly called by the author "a modern fairy-tale". The talent of Anna Brigadere has all the necessary qualities to make her a writer of fairy-tales. In her conception of the world, however, the ethical ideals hold too important a place, so the fairy-tale is not her final aesthetic aim, but only a means to culminate the idea1 of woman\'s unlimited ability to sacrifice herself for the sake of the man THE MODERN FAIRY-TALE IN LATVIAN LITERATURE she loves. The genre of the fairy-tale is later valued by the writer only in her dramas which, like Mare, fascinates most with its high ethical ideals. Andrievs Niedra was a pastor and an eminent orator who dealt in his sermons not only with biblical themes but also with social and national problems of modern life. Also his Peasant\'s Son in a certain sense resembles a widely-conceived sermon shaped after the models of Christ\'s parables, yet intertwining into the plot genuine national motives taken both from the folk-tales and from the features and events of modem life. The Peasant\'s Son of Anrievs Niedra as a new and peculiar feature in Latvian literature, increased his fame. Yet after this Andrievs Niedra wrote no more fairy-tales- . In 1904 there was written the first fairy-tale by Karlis Skalbe — Ka es braucu Ziemelmeitas lukotips (My Travels to the Daughter of Northland). Skalbe was only 25 years old then The heroes of the first fairy-tales of both Skalbe and Niedra are young men who are driven away by an inner unrest from their idyllic parental homesteads into the wide world, this wonderland of the unknown. But Niedra and Skalbe were entirely opposite characters. The first of them preached conservatism; he hoped to settle all problems by means of a calm and cautious evolution, so during the revolution of 1905 in Russia he opposed it. The Peasant\'s Son of Niedra does not find in the world what he has been longing for and returns home dejected and disappointed, whereas the fairy-tale of Skalbe is a direct answer to the central thought of Niedra It contains c prophetic picture of the approaching revolution: the seeker of the Daughter of Northland, after having left his parental farm, as if winged and elated comes to the conclusion that "the real life is wide as the world and high as the heavens". If, consequently, we have reason to consider this first fairy-tale by Skalbe tt> be a reply to Andrievs Niedra, we on the other hand, may conclude that this time also the fairy-tale from itself has been chosen by the young writer only to use the same form as his opponent has used. In the person of Skalbe we meet an author who is a born fairy-story teller and to whom the fairy-tale may become the final aim of an aesthetic form. It is sometimes said that the fairy-tale sets a man free from the chains of the laws of nature. If it is so, then Skalbe performs this not only by phantastic plot of his tales, but still more by his stylistic portrayal, which resembles a painting, so that nearly every sentence wipes out the limit between the real and the unreal world. With the passing years Karlis Skalbe becomes the Latvian "King of Fairy-tales". More than fifty of his fairy-tales are among the most valuable treasures of Latvian literature. It has been said that Andersen by his fairy-tales has made the literature of the small Danish nation known throughout the whole of Europe. If the Latvian nation and the Latvian language were somewhat better known, if two World Wars had not devastated Latvia, the name of Skalbe would undoubtedly be celebrated in Europe together with that of Oscar Wilde. It is remarkable that there is not only a causal contact between the fairy-tales of Skalbe and Wilde, but also a few parallel features in the biography and the character of them Wilde was 35 when he published his second collection of fairy-tales. After a few years he was in prison and he thanked the world for this period of his life with the "Ballad of Reading Gaol". Skalbe Illustration from K-Skalbe\'s Fairy-Tales, by N. Strunke also was in prison, for the court of the Tsaric Russia did not forgive him his taking part in the events of 1905. As Skalbe was acquainted with and fascinated by the fairy-tales of Andersen already, during his imprisonment .he got more closely acquainted with the fairy-tales of Wilde, translated them and also wrote his own fairy-tales. He, too, was 35 when having left the prison he published (in 1914) his ZLemas pasakas (Winter Fairy-tales) which comprise the best of his fairy-tales. By a careful comparison it is possible to find several motives whicrf Skalbe and Wilde have in common, .not only in this one, but also in other collections. That Skalbe has looked at Wilde\'s works as models in a certain sense, may be seen also from his manner in choosing the titles of his books. Wilde has "The Happy Prince and other Tales" or "A House of Pomegranates and other Tales"; Skalbe has the same: "Mulka laime un citas pasakas\'(lA Simpleton\'s Luck and other Tales"); "Mans bernibu dienu menesis" ("The Moon of My Childhood . . .") "Sarkangalvite" ("The Girl Red-Cap") "un citas pasakas", etc The chief thing, however, is that both Wilde and Skalbe use the same method of artistic creation, that they are akin to one another in their visualization of the world, in which life and nature are visualized in soft half-tones ranging from a quite naturalistic reality up to the glorious mysteries of deepest essential meaning of everything, at the same time widely opening the gates towards the inside of the human soul. Together with this we must remember their difference: Wilde is British with Irish blood in his veins, a hedonist man of the aristocratic drawing-rooms; but Skalbe is the son of a Latvian blacksmith to whom even the drawing-rooms in the capital of his own rustic nation have never seemed enticing. This means that each of them has his own world, his own favourite characters, his own mentality which has been influenced by their nationality and the surrounding society. Finally, one more resemblance between their lives should be remembered: Wilde, expelled from his society, left England and in great poverty died in Paris. Also Skalbe had to leave his native country in the autumn of 1944, and he died in Stockholm, on April 16th 1945. In the fairy-tales of Skalbe, various elements have been blended together and his characters make quite a mixed society. In comparison with the countries of Western Europe, in Latvia the ancient folk-lore traditions are much more alive still in the 20th century. To the Latvians the folk-songs, fairy-tales and myths have not yet become just ethnographic materials collected in books in which only a few specialists might have an interest. All of the educated people, all scientists and artists have grown up in those traditions. The fairy-tales of Skalbe, too, contain much of the Latvian folk-lore. We see there, for instance, the same Latvian orphan-girl whose virtues and handsomeness is praised in folk-songs and fairytales; we see the witty cowherd boy and the clumsily powerful giant as well as the Latvian mythological characters who, perceiving im – perceptible, enter the modern life and settle the destinies of people. Of course, in the fairytales of Skalbe there are also the traditional kings, princes, princesses and court-people with whose splendour the fantasy of this nation, the bearer of a really severe destiny, has been so much occupied since ancient times. But they are rather strange, these royal noblemen, and rather strange is also the etiquette of their court; many a citizen of a monarchy might get puzzled by it. These oddities are caused by the fact that a king with Skalbe usually is a herdsman or generally a rustic man by his origin who has come to his high office either owing to his special merits, by the nobleness of his character, or by his wisdom. This is a usual motive of the folk-tales which is extended and developed on to its consequences in the works of Skalbe. The life at this court generally has the same features, the same conventions, the same style of language and even the same troubles which art to be mit at a well-to-do Latvian farm; everything is only depicted in brighter colours here; the court life culminates by mentioning the glistening of gold and silver there. Skalbe is not fond of animal personifications and he has therefore used but few of the folk-tales about animals. Personified domestic animals, wild beasts and birds appear in his fairy-tales only now and then and just episodically; they have no importance in the story as a whole. Yet among the most brilliant works of Skalbe there is one fairy-tale where the personification of animals is made into a wide allegory. Its title is "Kakita dzirnavas" ("The Pussy\'s Water-Mill"). Transferred into the human reality, this allegory is a carefully compossed short story on social and ethical motives with a pure-hearted Herrnhuter as the central character. The fairy-tales of different nations have common characters and common motives. In a similar way also Skalbe has not absolutely eliminated foreign elements from his tales. Amid purely Latvian surroundings there appear also characters closely akin to the world of Andersen and the Grimms, and next to ancient Latvian deities we find also mythological beings of other nations. But those have already become quite assimilated with their Latvian surroundings. And if on one single occasion Skalbe *s fantasy has reached distant India (in the fairy-tale "Nabaga bramins" – "The Poor Brahmin"), the same has happened. The hero of the tale is a Hindu only outwardly. He expresses only Skalbe\'s own "religion of silence" his creed, with his testimony, about the beauty of the word untold, about the value of the heart and the sun. Skalbe, now and then, introduces his fairy-tale by an enlargened and stylistically adorned variation of the usual "Once upon a time" formula, endeavouring to transfer the events of his tale into those times "when people had still no idea of mineral coal and of the teaching about the roundness of earth". The background of such far-off times adds to the effectiveness of the depiction, but it .cannot make this addition consequent and constant. As a rule, the events and characters in the fairy-tales of Skalbe are nearer to the present-day life than to the ancient past A modern millionaire who builds a modern town; a poor Incessant Student, for ever thirsting for knowledge; a farmer who makes preparations for a wealthy celebration of New Year\'s Eve; a mother who has lost her son in the Great War; soldiers, partakers of the War of Independence; a stingy bookworm; a hangman who wants to drown his conscience in alcohol; even a wanton woman — a whole collection of such 20th century characters is to be met in Skalbe\'s fairy-tales, together with fantastic kings, pagan deities and orphan-girls. Not always a "fairy-tale" of Skalbe is a true fairy – tale from beginning till end. As F regards the construction, Skalbe is fond of the so-called "frame-technique", where the fairy-tale itself is nearly lost in the magnificent arabesque of : the literary framework around it; and this frame• work is nothing but a vision of life and nature, seen with the eyes of a romanticist, or a characterization of the author s present state of mind or soul in statu nascendi of his fairy-tale At other times the short fairy-tale is added as a supplement at the end of a considerably longer depiction. It happens, too, that the fairy-tale at the end proves to be a dream, beautified memories, sudden eruptions out of the depths of the subconscious etc. On the other hand, Skalbe\'s fairy-tales never begin and never end with a fantastic event. In one of his fairy-tales Karala krasnkuris — The King\'s Stoker) Skalbe introduces his story by characterizing a pre-historic time, undatable and very ancient, and this characterization may be a sample of his style: "The human beings and the animals lived in a close friendship ... And the people had broad ruddy faces as if they had looked too long at the moon in the evening. They still did not distinguish between living and lifeless beings. The darkness was full of spirits; every corner was full of hidden life as a gently covered mother\'s womb ... And the people understood all voices. They understood also things that by their nature were dumb or suffered from a severe hoarseness just as the late autumn winds do. They talked to their cattle and to the stars in the sky, to the clay water-jug and to the tiny knitting-needle; when a child awoke he talked to his arms and legs. And on the thrashing-floor there was plenty of hay and straw, and plenty of beautiful legends were born incensed with the fragrance of hay ..." A glorification of the primitive man? No, it is a romanticist\'s vision of the paradise of all souls lost by civilized man. But if a person is able to return by his visualization of ;rhe world, in other words by his style, to such a panpsychism of the ancient man, he is indeed opening the gates to a fairy wonderland. Illustrations, metaphors, and personifications in his fairy-tales are not only means of style, but a brotherhood with plants, animals and things, professed in a peculiar visualization of the world. In his lyrics as well as in his fairy-tales Skalbe has depicted a strange feeling —- as if a man were growing into a tree. Even a whole Forest of Souls has grown up and the little boy, not being able to distinguish any more which tree is his mother now, kneels and says his prayers to the whole forest. The human beings in the fairy-tales of Skalbe, by being transformed into trees, are growing The Forest of Souls; in the same manner things and phenomena whenever* they come under Skalbe\'s poetic visualization obtain a soul and begin to live in the same society with human beings. Only a few of Skalbe\'s fairy-tales will interest children because usually his plot is too simple, without any outward dramatism; but the world of souls, of things awakened to life, shown by the< style of Skalbe, will be perceived in its full beauty only by a literarily educated aesthete. The same may be said about the contents, the ideas of these fairy-tales: they will captivate only such readers as know life already and who like to seek its meaning. On speaking about the ideas of Skalbe\'s fairytales it is useful to remember the definition of Prof. A. Svabe, as to where the fairy-tales of Skalbe differ from those of Wilde: "The hero of the legends of Skalbe is Virtue. By this fact Skalbe differs from the great British aesthete Wilde whose hero is Beauty." The ideas of Skalbe\'s fairy-tales are always determined by a virtue of the moral, social or national life. In his youth Skalbe turned against the peace and contentedness of the civic middleclass society. It was the virtue of struggle and of alertness that brought the hero of Skalbe to the cognition of the immeasurably wide range of the real life. Later on the heroes of Skalbe begin to return from the wide to a narrower range, even to contentment because they are seeking deepness of soul instead of the wide range now. This seeking of deepness is combined with the Latvian, this rustic nation\'s mentality of adherence to that narrow strip of earth wher* one has been born and has grown up. Thus in Skalbe\'s fairy-tales love towards the home-country is made the highest virtue. The crowned peasants of Skalbe have everything their heart may desire, but they feel unhappy as they yearn for the water which they drank in their youth from a spring at their homeplace- At another time the unrest is caused by the wish to pay a visit to one\'s first love who has been a simple rustic girl. There are several fairytales in which Skalbe has depicted the longing of a peasant youth to go into the wide world and look for a well-to-do and easy life. But at its most sublime we find this theme in the symbolic fairy-tale about the giant, who can be cheated by every cowherd-boy in the wide world and who grows worn-out and loses his strength while roaming in the world, aimlessly and without work, because he cannot find his proper place any more. Only when he digs himself deep into the earth again and starts carrying a mountain on his back as before, the giant lives a full life and is happy again. Those who are familiar with the history of the Latvian people, will understand that love towards his native country to a Latvian has always been connected with the virtues of simplicity and contentment. In Skalbe\'s fairy-tales these virtues have culminated in asceticism. Thus Karlis Skalbe, this writer of a rustic people, using an imprisoned prince as his spokesman, declares the sunbeam, water and rye-bread to be the highest treasures of the world; but the fisherman Jurgis throws into the water his only wealth, a silver beaker, only in orde. to get rid of the devils temptations and to be able to go on loving his simple life. And let us not forget to mention with honour "the most delightful virtues" of the granny: "the Virtue of Knitting, the Virtue of Darning, and the Virtue of Giving Gifts". "And then there Was one more virtue; it looked as a squirrel with a bent yellow little tooth: it was the Virtue of Gnawing Bred-Crusts." Skalbe has not written any legends about the saints of Christianity although several fairy-tales have been written influenced by the church festivals, especially by Christmas and Easter, and are intended for shaping the feelings of those festivals into poetic characters. But the virtue of Christian charity has dictated the gospel-like fairytales about the little daughter of an executioner and a Pussy\'s water-mill. In the hoarse throat of the executioner a peculiar sound is vainly seeking expression; but the executioner\'s little daughter, who sacrifices her life for a hero sentenced to death, finds out this holy word of life. Ascending the gallows she will tell this word to the witnesses of the public execution. This word is — charity. In the other fairy-tale Pussy meets one of the peculiar kings of Skalbe. Pussy might be the-very model of those "blessed" ones mentioned in Christ\'s Sermon on the Mount, but the king might have the title "rex dolorosus", as he mourns for all the sufferings inflicted upon the human beings as well as the animals and the beasts in the world. Neither Pussy nor the king are able to fight against the evil of the world. But both of these characters, idealized to the highest degree, join in the convention that evil must not be remembered and that one must not increase sufferings in the world. It would be a misunderstanding to think that virtue being such a dominant power in determining the ideas of Skalbe\'s fairy-tales, the result might be didacticism. It is not so. Not moralization, but an aesthetic experience is the aim of Skalbe-If the reader, influenced by the qualitative contents of the aesthetic experience, makes up his mind to become better, this is only a testimony of the catharsis which since the times of Aristotle has been mentioned as the mark of every eminent work of art on the public. It does not matter whether this catharsis displayed by the fairy-tales of Skalbe is of a religious, a national, or of a social character: after one has been purified by it, one obtains the visa to that world of a soulful beauty about which the writer has told in his very last fairy-tale: \'There beside blue waters golden trees stand in blossom every wish is fulfilled there, and the days pass in smiles and in playing." This takes place even if one like the hero of this last fairy-tale — the sailor Gaigals, who has lost his native country, is persecuted everywhere, having no rights whatever any more ... As an author of fairy-tales Skalbe holds quite a peculiar and distinguished place in Latvian literature. Nowadays the fairy-tale as a branch of literature is confronted with no bright prospects. But it is able to show many a characteristic feature in the face of the writer\'s nation, perhaps in a certain sense better than the other branches of literature might do it. He who will have the opportunity of spending some time in the world of Skalbe\'s fairytales will make friends with the Latvian. There he will make the acquaintance with a nation which has a quite unusual sense of life and a consciousness of cosmic deity. Translated by Janis Baltaks.']

Name: 7_eu_european_foreign_minister
Representative_Docs: ['At GAERC meeting: Preparations for December European Council and debates on EU-Russia relations A meeting of the EU General Affairs and External Relations Council (GAERC) took place on 10 November in Brussels with the participation of Minister for Foreign Affairs Maris Riekstiņš. During the meeting, EU Member State foreign ministers prepared for the December European Council session, debated the future of relations between the EU and Russia, and reviewed current topics in foreign and security policy. As part of the GAERC session, a separate meeting of EU Member State defence ministers was held on 10 November, whereas on 11 November a separate meeting of ministers for development co-operation was cchediilpfi In preparation for tne  ouu..«i meeting of 11 and 12 December, EU foreign ministers agreed that the key issues on the agenda will be the Treaty of Lisbon, the international economic and financial situation, energy and climate change, external relations and security (including such topics as the Eastern Partnership, the renewed European Security Strategy, and the strengthening of EU defence capability). In the run-up to the EU-Russia summit due to be held in Nice on 14 November in Nice, France, the foreign ministers discussed future relations between the EU and Russia. During the debates, Foreign Minister Maris Riekstiņš stressed that it is crucial for the EU to speak in one voice with Russia, not only about the new EU-Russia Partnership and Co-operation Agreement, but also when resolving existing problems, including the situation in the South Caucasus. At the External Relations session, EU foreign ministers talked about topical issues with regard to the current state of affairs in Iraq, the Democratic Republic of Congo, and Zimbabwe. The Civilian Capabilities Improvement Conference also took place within the framework of the GAERC meeting, where foreign ministers adopted a declaration on the future development of civilian capabilities and the 2008 Progress Report on Civilian Capabilities as part of the European Security and Defence Policy (ESDP). At a joint session with defence ministers, the foreign ministers shared opinions on the current ESDP missions and operations, the future of the EU military operation EUFOR ALTHEA in Bosnia and Herzegovina, and the situation in Afghanistan. The GAERC meeting will continue on 11 November and as part of it, a separate sitting of ministers for development cooperation is also to take place. During the meeting it is expected to cover issues such as the EU response to the food crisis, the situation in Afghanistan, preparations for the Conference on Financing for Development taking place from 29 November to 2 December in Doha, Qatar, as well as the regional integration of African, Caribbean, and Pacific region states and Economic Partnership Agreements (EPAs).', 'Baltic Review PUBLISHED BY THE SYDNEY LATVIAN SOCIETY Foreign Minister discusses enlargement strategy of the Euro-Atlantic community at Brussels Forum From 14 to 16 March Minister of Foreign Affairs Maris Riekstiņš took part in the Brussels Forum, at which politicians and experts debate and exchanged views on current issues of transatlantic cooperation. On 15 March Minister Riekstiņš, Swedish Minister of Foreign Affairs Carl Bildt, the President of the Foreign Committee of the Rumanian Senate Mircea Dan Geoana and U.S. Senator George Voinovich participated in a panel discussion headed by Ronald d. Asmus, the Executive Director of the Transatlantic Centre of the German Marshall Fund of the United States. Topics of the debate included NATO and European Union enlargement, both in the context of the coming meeting of NATO heads of state and government in Bucharest, and also when evaluating future development perspectives of NATO and the European Union. The Foreign Minister noted that Latvia is able to share with present candidate states its unique experience which has been gained both on the way to NATO and the European Union, and also in its status as a full-fledged Member State of the Euro-Atlantic community. "It is important to be aware that the perspective of being a part of the Euro-Atlantic community is an excellent stimulus for candidate states to implement reforms, however, at the same time, when making decisions to admit new member states, a stable system for evaluating May, 2008 conformity with criteria and meeting of requirements must be followed," added Minister Riekstiņš. The Brussels Forum is an annual meeting of European and U.S. high ranking politicians, businessmen, academics, representatives of non-governmental organisations and of the media who discuss the challenges being faced in world politics. The agenda of this year\'s forum includes issues relating to the situation in the Western Balkan region, the enlargement of the Euro-Atlantic community, Russia\'s policies after the change of leadership, Afghanistan and China, as well as current questions of international trade policy and world economics, including energy security and climate change. The Brussels Forum is organised by the German Marshall Fund of the United States, in co-operation with partners the Egmont Institute, Daimler, Bertelsmann Stiftung, and also the Ministry of Defence of the Republic of Latvia. This year, with the support of the Latvian Ministry of Defence, participants and guests at the Brussels Forum will have the opportunity to sample Latvian national cuisine. UN Special Rapporteur encourages continuation of Latvia\'s policy in promotion of tolerance On 19 March, Doudou Diene, the United Nations Special Rapporteur on contemporary forms of racism, racial discrimination, xenophobia and related intolerance, presented a report on Latvia at the 7th session of the UN Human Rights Council. In his report the Special Rapporteur welcomes the fact that Latvia has adopted the necessary legislation and established a number of institutions with the purpose of tackling racial discrimination. Mr Doudou Diene also positively refers to how the Constitutional Court and the Ombudsman have contributed to fighting racial discrimination. The Special Rapporteur notes the Government\'s achievement in solving the problems that the Roma community faces in Latvia through implementation of the national action plan "Roma in Latvia 2007---2009". Special Rapporteur Diene calls on Latvia to continue its work on tackling intolerance and on promoting integration. The Foreign Ministry values the fact that the Special Rapporteur has become informed with Latvia\'s history and has analysed inter-ethnic contacts in relation to this history, mentioning also Latvia\'s centurylong traditions in the area of tolerance and the need to overcome the consequences of long occupation. Mr Doudou Diene previously visited Latvia from 20 to 24 September 2007 at the invitation of the Government of Latvia. During his stay, the Special Rapporteur met with state officials, representatives of NGOs and civil society, as well as visited the Olaine Prison and the Museum of the Occupation of Latvia. Foreign Minister and French Secretary of State for European Affairs sign memorandum on French language teaching On 31 March, Jean-Pierre Jouyet, the French Secretary of State for European Affairs, made a visit to Latvia. During the visit, Mr Jouyet met with representatives of the Foreign Ministry and discussed current events within the EU, including France\'s priorities for its presidency of the EU in the second half of 2008. At the meeting with Foreign Ministry State Secretary Normans Penke, the parties considered Latvia-France bilateral collaboration, especially noting the active ties in culture. Mr Penke and Mr Jouyet voiced satisfaction with the festivals Pārsteidzoša Latvija (Surprising Latvia) in 2005 in France and Francijas pavasaris (French Spring) in 2007 in Latvia, which resonated on an international level. The French Secretary of State for European Affairs provided information on France\'s priorities for its EU presidency. The parties shared opinions both on the newly established Reflection Group, with Vaira vlke-Freiberga as its Deputy Chairperson, and on the future prospects for Europe. The government representatives of Latvia and France exchanged experience on formats of regional co-operation, and their role in Europe. The Latvian side provided a briefing on its presidency at the Council of the Baltic Sea States (CBSS) and the upcoming summit of CBSS member states, which is due to take place on 3 and 4 June in Riga. Mr Jouyet, in turn, mentioned France\'s initiative of the Union for the Mediterranean and its key priorities in the areas of environment, civil security, and energy. Within the framework of the visit, Foreign Minister Maris Riekstiņš and the French Secretary of State for European Affairs signed the Agreement between the Government of the Republic of France and the Government of the Republic of Latvia on Mutual Protection of Classified Information and the Memorandum between the Republic of Latvia, the Republic of France, the International Organisation of the Francophonie, the Kingdom of Belgium, and the Grand Duchy of Luxembourg on the Multi-annual Programme of French Language Teaching in Public Administration Institutions of Latvia. "For the Latvian representatives, to be able to successfully work in EU institutions, as well in preparation for Latvia\'s presidency of the EU in 2015, the teaching of French takes an increasingly vital role in the education of civil servants," commented Minister Riekstiņš. Anders Fogh Rasmussen visits Latvia At the Riga Castle, President Valdis Zatlers welcomed the Prime Minister of the Kingdom of Denmark, Anders Fogh Rasmussen. During their meeting, the officials discussed the final documents of the NATO Summit in Bucharest and the compromises reached there during the debates, noting in particular the essential commitment alongside the military mission to strengthen the work of civil reconstruction in Afghanistan. The ratification process of the Lisbon Treaty in both countries was also a topic of discussion at the meeting, and the consequences of the ratification in areas such as the EU common foreign policy were considered, emphasizing the European Union\'s ability to address third countries "in one voice". The discussions also included the principles of the EU common energy policy relating both to energy efficiency as well as to source diversification. The officials also assessed the positive history of Latvian-Danish bilateral relations and considered further opportunities for cooperation in areas such as energy and within the Council of the Baltic Sea States. During his visit, the Danish Prime Minister also met with his Latvian counterpart, Prime Minister Ivars Godmanis. The officials discussed current issues of the European Union, including the Lisbon Treaty and its implementation, energy issues and climate change, regional energy-related projects and co-operation perspectives in the Baltic Sea Region. Prime Minister Ivars Godmanis meets with Finnish Prime Minister During his working visit to Finland, on 6 April 2008, the Latvian Prime Minister Ivars Godmanis met in Helsinki with Finnish Prime Minister Matti Vanhanen. During the meeting, the bilateral cooperation between Latvia and Finland regionally and within the EU was discussed, focusing on the energy issues and asking for Finland\'s support for Latvia in specific EU energy policy sectors. Prime Minister Godmanis confirmed that Latvia wished to promote bilateral economic co-operation in Latvia\'s priority industries. With respect to the relations with Russia, common issues within the EU were mainly focused on, such as vehicle queuing at the Russian border, Russia\'s discriminatory railway transit tariffs, and the increased Russian round timber export tariffs. The regional issues included work on CBSS reforms and on the new strategic priorities of the Council of the Baltic Sea States, and on the EU Baltic Sea Region strategy currently under preparation. Of the EU issues, the Prime Ministers exchanged views on the EU energy and climate policy. Prime Minister Godmanis\' working visit took place before the informal meeting in Lapland, Finland, of the prime ministers of the European liberal democratic parties, from 6 to 7 April 2008. Foreign Minister and German Federal Minister for Foreign Affairs discuss regional co-operation At their meeting on 9 April, Foreign Minister Maris Riekstiņš and German Federal Minister for Foreign Affairs Frank-Walter Steinmeier expressed satisfaction with the dynamic nature of bilateral relations between the two countries, and shared the opinion that regional co-operation should be reinforced. During the visit, Minister Riekstiņš briefed his German counterpart on the current news of Latvia\'s presidency of the Council of the Baltic Sea States (CBSS), including the summit of CBSS member state heads of government, which is due to take place in Riga on 3-4 June. As to the Treaty of Lisbon, both sides expressed the hope that it will be successfully ratified in all EU member states and will enter into force as expected – on 1 January 2009. The officials also acknowledged that the debate on the operation of EU institutions should be continued after the adoption of the Treaty. Similarly, when the implementation of the Treaty of Lisbon takes place, increased attention should be paid to issues that concern the establishment of the European External Action Service. The ministers also discussed current issues of international politics, including the NATO summit in Bucharest, which took place from 2 to 4 April. Mr Riekstiņš informed Mr Steinmeier about Latvia\'s contribution to the European Neighbourhood Policy, emphasizing in particular the reinforcement of relations with Ukraine and Georgia. At the meeting with Christoph Heusgen, the Advisor for Foreign and Security Policy to the Federal Chancellor, the officials discussed current developments in international politics. The conversation focused on Latvia\'s presidency of the CBSS and on the reform process at the organisation. Minister Riekstiņš noted that the present priorities of major significance for the future operation of the CBSS have been highlighted: energy, environment, economy, and civil security. Mr Riekstiņš also expressed the hope that Germany will actively engage in the reform process of the organisation. At the close of the visit, the Latvian Foreign Minister took part in the opening of the exhibition Geld in Lettland. Geschichte und Gegenwart {Money in Latvia. History and Today). The exhibition is being held as part of Essentia Baltica 2008, the German-Baltic year of culture. The Prime Minister meets representatives of the Swedish government and companies On 31 March, Prime Minister Ivars Godmanis of the Republic of Latvia, while on a working visit to Sweden, met Frederik Reinfeldt, Prime Minister of Sweden, Maud Oloffsson, Deputy Prime Minister and Minister for Enterprise and Energy, and Anders Borg, Minister for Finance of Sweden, as well as with the management of the TeliaSonera company and the management of Svenska Kraftnat. The focus of the Prime Ministers\' meeting was on Europe\'s common policy and issues to be addressed during the Summit of the CBSS Heads of Government in June this year. The Latvian Prime Minister noted that the markets of Latvia and Sweden are closely linked with each other. Sweden, in both a direct and indirect way, is one of the key investors in Latvia in various sectors – banking services, telecommunications and other areas. The Prime Minister and the Minister for Finance discussed operations of the Swedish bank subsidiaries in Latvia in the area of housing and mortgage credits. They also analysed the structure of these credits and concluded that the share of doubtful loans is very low – up to 0.6%. The focus of the meeting between the Latvian Prime Minister, the Swedish Deputy Prime Minister and representatives of the Swedish energy company was mainly on the issues of constructing a link between Latvia and Sweden. During the meeting with the management of TeliaSonera, the sides repeatedly stated their position regarding the sale of Lattelecom and LMT shares.', "Foreign Minister Maris Riekstins to take part in European Council meeting On 19 and 20 June, Foreign Minister Maris Riekstiņš will head the delegation of Latvia in the current meeting of the European Council in Brussels, which is the last meeting within the Slovenian EU Presidency. From 1 July, the functions of the EU presiding state are to be assumed by France. On 19 June, Minister Riekstiņš will participate in the EU working dinner, during which the prospects for the Lisbon Treaty will be reviewed following the results of the Irish referendum. During a parallel working dinner, representatives from foreign ministries will debate the situation in Kosovo after the coming into force of its constitution on 15 June. Latvia had already earlier expressed support to the EU assistance in the formation of Kosovo's public administration institutions, especially to the European Union Rule of Law Mission (EULEX) in Kosovo. The foreign ministry representatives are expected to exchange opinions on the issue of EU sanctions against Cuba, on the current situation in Zimbabwe ahead of the second presidential election runoff and on a joint EU-U.S. declaration adopted during the 10 June EU-U.S A. Summit. Among other items in the declaration, Latvia has expressed its satisfaction regarding the firm support by the EU and the U.S. to Georgia's territorial integrity. On 20 June, the European Council's working session will include a discussion on the Council conclusions. The most important topics highlighted in the conclusions are the Lisbon Treaty, the issues of the interior and the judicial affairs, the situation in the Western Balkans, the EU's progress towards the achievement of the Millennium Goals, as well as the European Neighbourhood Policy. One of the issues in focus of the debate will be the impact of the increasing food and oil prices. Latvia supports the intentions of trie Presidency and the European Commission to seek solutions for the mitigation of the effects of this price-hike. Latvia believes that the rising cost of food and its impacts should be considered in the context of discussions on future development of the EU's agricultural sector, ensuring equal conditions of competition among farmers in all the Member States."]

Name: 8_nato_security_alliance_membership
Representative_Docs: ['NATO PA RECOMMENDS INVITING 7 NEW MEMBERS TO THE ALLIANCE, INCLUDING LATVIA Leaders of the NATO Parliamentary Assembly (PA) have committed themselves to NATO enlargement and its centralrty to NATO\'s future and Europe\'s stability. The leaders of the Assembly\'s 19 member delegations have called on NATO to invite seven nations – Estonia, Latvia, Lithuania, Bulgaria, Romania, Slovakia and Slovenia- to join the Alliance at the NATO Prague Summit in November. The Declaration on NATO Transformation agreed by the Standing Committee of the Assembly on 6 October 2002 argues that the seven countries have proved their progress towards NATO membership by pushing through radical reforms at home, supporting NATO operations in the Balkans, and contributing to the fight against terrorism. \'There is a growing consensus on both sides of the Atlantic that enlargement is key to the Alliance\'s future," said NATO PA President Rafaei Estrella. The whole security landscape has qua\'ltativeiy changed, and NATO\'s response to new challenges will be a central focus at the Prague Summit The Importance of some issues such as NATO enlargement, the fight against terrorism, the closer co-operation with Russia, and the need to maintain peace and security in the Balkans are central to security and stability in the Euro-Atlantic area. NATO must make further profound changes to ensure that it can meet the security challenges of the 21st century, in particular after 11 September." "Our Declaration on NATO Transformation represents the collective parliamentary view on how NATO should meet those challenges," said Mr Estrella. US CONGRESS EXPRESSES SUPPORT FOR ACCESSION OF BALTIC STATES TO NATO On 7 October2oo2, the US House of Representatives approved resolution HCONRESII6, which recommends that the three Baltic States – Estonia, Latvia and Lithuania – be invited to join NATO as early as this year. The resolution evaluates highly political and economic developments in the Baltic States and affirms that the three states, on', 'CHICAGO LATVIAN NEWSLETTER U.S., Europe Grope For Expansion Policy As Baltic Membership Questioned The ongoing debate on whether to expand the membership of the North Atlantic Treaty Organization (NATO), which united the states of Western Europe with the U.S. and Canada during the Cold War, to include former members of the arch-enemy Warsaw Pact, is beginning to look like an intricate mating ritual played out on an international stage. All of the pieces are there: the young bride, portrayed by the countries of Central and East European, doing their best to secure a home in the strong embrace 0f... the reluctant groom, played by the U.S. and its NATO coalition partners, torn between desire to ask the countries of Central and East Europe to dance and fear of unforseen consequences as long as... the enraged spouse abuser, again-aggressive Russia and its current leaders, continues to rage about injustice and threaten dire consequences if the budding romance is consummated. Unfortunately, NATO expansion is no soap opera — it is an issue of vital strategic concern for both the NATO countries and the new democracies of Central and East Europe, especially the Baltic states. Allies Uncertain, Russia Unequivocal In fits and starts, the 16 member nations of NATO have reached a general consensus that expansion into the vacuum left by the crumbling of Soviet power is only natural. This consensus did not exist previously. Events such as the Russian invasion of Chechnya, the ongoing morass of Bosnia, and the rising level of aggressive nationalist rhetoric in Moscow have convinced many western leaders, significantly those of the United States and Germany, that expansion of NATO membership to include countries such as Poland, the Czech Republic, Slovakia, Hungary, Latvia, Estonia, Lithuania, Bulgaria, Rumania, Ukraine and Albania will, in time, become a reality. Even such die-hard Russophiles as Deputy Secretary of State Strobe Talbott have concluded that NATO must expand eastward to deal with a "pessimistic scenario" in Russia. The question of establishing criteria and timetables for expansion, has proved vexing, however. The refrain of "not if, but when" on the lips of U.S. policy makers has become something of a cliche in Washington. Ever since debate began in Congress last year on the NATO Participation Act,, which stipulated that the U.S. Government must get serious about setting criteria for NATO membership, most politicians have October 1995 — Vol. XV, No. 8 NATO Shuffle supported NATO expansion in principle. Few were willing, however, to press for the establishment of clear membership criteria. The November elections, which swept the Republican Party to power on Capital Hill, did little to clarify the NATO membership issue. Many new Republican members of the House and Senate proved responsive to isolationist arguments of "what business does the U.S. have in defending Bratislava?" Legislation was passed, however, by the House in January, and should have passed the Senate by now, which strengthened the NATO Participation Act. This legislation gives U.S. NATO representatives a clear roadmap of what the U.S. considers the minimum requirements any future NATO member must satisfy, including a commitment to democracy and human rights and a readiness to bear the burdens and obligations of NATO membership. The amended legislation will also force the President to give serious consideration to countries other than Poland, the Czech Republic, Slovakia and Hungary, which were marginalized int he original law — such as the Baltics. In the meantime, Russia has been absolutely opposed to NATO expansion, especially to countries such as the Baltics. In early September, Russian Deputy Foreign Minister Sergei Krylov asserted that, if NATO is expanded to include the Baltics, "the threat to our security will grow and we will have to take not only economic and political measures but also military ones." One can only imagine the concern such a statement caused in Tallinn, Riga and Vilnius — countries that are looking for all available means of bolstering their own sense of security against their huge Russian neighbor. Baltic Cooperation A Catalyst? Writing for the Estonian newspaper Eesti Sonumid on September 14, Ross Mayfield, an Independent Advisor to the Office of the President of Estonia and a researcher at the Baltic Institute of Strategic and International Studies commented on NATO expansion and the role closer Baltic cooperation could play in speeding up the process for Latvia, Estonia and Lithuania: "NATO membership should be a decision for NATO members only. However, NATO\'s procrastination and concern over Russian paranoia have left the door open for other parties to influence the decision. "By hesitating in the early stages of the enlargement debate, NATO made a critical mistake by not specifying admission criteria. "The recent signals from NATO indicating that th Baltic States will not be among the first to join a expanding NATO are another attempt to placate Russia intellectuals and policy makers, who argue that th dissolution of the Warsaw Pact should be met by th termination of NATO\'s role as a military organization. "A study by NATO strategists on who will join whe will be issued later this year and will presumably be th basis of the first concrete decision on enlargement. Dv at the December 5 meeting of the North Atlantic Counci it will come days before Russian general elections. "The Baltics, too, can and must influence th process. "In order to secure independence and assur sovereignty, they sang together, and the world listened Their voices were loudest when they called for Russian troop withdrawal. If they cannot continue to harmonize they might not be able to join the NATO and El) choirs "Baltic security and defense co-operation ha advanced well for three reasons: One, it is possible to defend the Baltic States, to the extent of deterring an invasion, but only through a joint Baltic response. Two a collective defense is economical. Three, in order to joi NATO you must have something to offer NATO. "Some believe that co-operation with non-NATO members may impede future co-operation with NATO members. This is humbug, as NATO is not going to wor with an isolated country. "Some point to the public\'s lack of familiarity wit the importance and implications of Baltic military co-operation in the context of larger foreign polic issues. More specifically, the Baltic people must understand the need for increased military funding – anc be willing to support it. Without support, military structures cannot continue to evolve. "A third obstacle is the growing superiority comple of Estonia due the early success of it reforms. It i evidenced by Mart Laar\'s recent statement that Estoni should distance itself in foreign policy from Latvia and Lithuania and instead focus on co-operation with the Nordic and Central European countries. "This is short-sighted. The security and developmen of Latvia and Lithuania is clearly in Estonia\'s best interest It will allow the Baltic states to develop as a common market for investors, a larger geopolitical entity for polic makers, and a more stable region as a whole. Enlargin NATO and the EU, due to the implications it has fo member states, will be a political decision that is based on their perception of the Baltic states as a unit. The governments of these member states are going to have the approval of their parliaments and peoples for these new partnerships." Mārtiņš Zvaners', 'THE CHICAGO LATVIAN NEWSLETTER NATO INVITES THREE COUNTRIES TO JOIN On July 8, in Madrid, Spain, 16 NATO countries invited Poland, Hungary and the Czech Republic to begin accession talks with the Alliance. The goal is for the countries to become full members by 1999. According to the NATO Declaration, issued by the various heads of state and governments, all those nations that have expressed an interest in becoming NATO members, but were not invited for accession talks, will remain under consideration for future membership. The Declaration also references the Baltic states as aspiring NATO members and recognizes their progress towards greater stability and cooperation. The Declaration stated that NATO will maintain an active relationship with those nations that have expressed an interest in membership. In keeping with the pledge to maintain an open door policy, foreign ministers of Alliance countries will continually review the process with a final review at their next meeting in 1999. The NATO Declaration illustrates support for countries not included in the first wave of enlargement; however it lacks specificity in how an active relationship will be developed between NATO countries and non-NATO members. After the Madrid Summit, Latvian President Guntis Ulmanis said he hoped the Baltic states would join NATO within five to seven years. He stated that Poland, Hungary and the Czech Republic had promised to support the Baltic states in their drive for membership. U.S. Ambassador to Sweden Thomas Siebert was quoted in the Swedish daily Dagens Nyheter, depicting U.S. policy in August 1997 Vol. XXVII. No. 17 BALTICS ARE ASPIRING MEMBERS the Baltic region, "We will not consider the expansion of NATO to be accomplished or successful unless or before the Baltic states\' ambitions are fulfilled. We will work with each of the three Baltic states to help them achieve their goal of becoming members of NATO. We will also support their desire to become European Union (EU) members," he said. The question remains, where does this leave the Baltic countries? In the present 105 th Congress, both the House and the Senate have passed legislation that designates the Baltics as being eligible for NATO funding to help them qualify for membership in the future. (The differences in the House and Senate bills must now be debated in conference committee). Congressional designation for assistance gives the Baltic countries the same status that Poland, Hungary, the Czech Republic and Slovenia received in the NATO Facilitation Act of 1996. Designation is the initial step of the NATO enlargement process. Continued support from Congress for Baltic security is a necessary element during the period of enlargement. Currently, the Baltic Freedom Caucus in the Senate, co-chaired by Senators Richard Durbin (D-IL) and Slade Gorton (R-WA), raises visibility for Congressional support for the Baltics. The Caucus will work closely with Baltic-American groups across the country and will address a wide range of issues, including security concerns and NATO membership for the Baltics, economic reform and development, assistance and trade. Before the Madrid Summit, in late April, Senate Majority Leader Trent Lott (R-MS) announced the appointments of 28 Senators to serve on a NATO Observer Group. According to Lott, "The NATO Observer Group will ensure that the Senate is fully prepared for the historic step of enlarging the most successful Alliance in modern history. It will work with the Administration, our NATO Allies and NATO aspirants to exchange views and information, and it will enable Senate concerns to be heard and considered as decisions are being made," he said. Senators from the Observer Group attended the Madrid Summit. Some of the lawmakers included William Roth (R-DE) who is the Group\'s Chairman, Barbara Mikulski (D--MD) and Jesse Helms (R-NC), Chairman of the Foreign Relations Committee. The Senate must ratify a NATO treaty by a two thirds majority, which will raise a vigorous debate. On June 25, a bipartisan group of twenty Senators wrote a letter to President Bill Clinton asking ten questions about NATO expansion. The Senators aim was to raise issues that will be the most contentious as the Senate debates the question of NATO expansion. According to the lawmakers, "The Senate\'s approval is no mere formality, and we believe the debate on the question should be intense and enlightening." The twenty Senators that co-signed the letter included the following: Dale Bumpers (D--AR), Byron L. Dorgan (D-ND), Tom Harkin (D--IA), Bob Kerrey (D-NE), Patrick Leahy (D-VT), Paul Wellstone (D-MN), Robert Bennett (R--UT), Conrad Bums (R-MT), Ben Nighthorse Campbell (R-CO); Larry Craig (R-ID), Lauch Faircloth (R-NC), Jim Jeffords (R-VT), Jesse Helms (R-NC), Tim Hutchinson (R-AR), Kay Bailey Hutchison (R-TX), Dirk Kempthorne (R--ID),Pat Roberts (R-KS), Jeff Sessions (R-AL), Craig Thomas (R-WY)and John Warner (R--VA). One of the questions raised pertains to the Baltic countries, "How will NATO expansion strengthen stability in Europe when the nations that face the greatest potential threats to their own security – including the Baltic states and several other nations – will not be included in the first NATO expansion? Are we creating a new dividing line that will breed instability and friction in Europe?" The U.S. Baltic Charter, which serves to alleviate some of the Baltics security concerns, is non-binding and serves more as a symbolic act. The Charter is still being negotiated and therefore the contents are not public. U.S. support for Baltic security was demonstrated by Secretary of State Madeleine Albright when she traveled to Lithuania after the Madrid Summit. In Vilnius, Albright told the three Baltic states that they were "serious candidates on the road" to NATO membership. She sought to reassure Estonia, Latvia and Lithuania that Washington supported their eventual membership even though a NATO summit last week gave no indication when they might be invited to join the Alliance. However, after talks with Baltic foreign ministers, she also made clear that if the three states were to join NATO they must improve ties with Russia, including their treatment of Russian minorities. Also following the Summit, President Clinton visited Denmark, which has been a strong advocate for Baltic security. Clinton stated, "On almost every issue we stand together, and on some of the most important issues, we stand together and almost alone." These visits by Albright, Clinton and the U.S. Baltic Charter symbolize U.S. support for Baltic security. This type of U.S. leadership in promoting stability in the Baltic region is essential in the international arena. What is necessary now is a U.S. assessment of the Baltic countries\' fulfillment of NATO membership criteria. Identifying areas of concern may be how an active relationship can be developed between NATO countries and non-NATO members, such as the Baltics.']

Who can vote: All registered citizens of the Republic of Latvia who have reached the age of 18 by the first election day, to include those residing outside of Latvia, are eligible to vote. Eligible voters should have received a mailing from the Embassy of Latvia and the ALA informing them about the 6th Saeima elections and their eligibility to vote in the Riga district. If you are eligible and have not received this mailing, contact the Embassy (202-726-8213). Where/How can one vote: Eligible voters residing outside of Latvia may vote either in person at a designated Latvian Embassy or consular office, or by mail. In the U.S., one may vote in person at the Latvian Ev. Lutheran Church of Washington, DC (400 Hurley Avenue, Rockville, MD 20850), between the hours of 8:00 AM and 8:00PM on either election day. A Latvian passport or proof of personal code form must be brought to the polling station in order to vote. In order to vote by mail, eligible voters residing in the U.S. must complete and mail a written request to vote by mail and their Latvian passport or proof of personal code form to the Embassy of Latvia (4325 17th Avenue NW, Washington, DC 20011) by September 10. The passport or proof of personal code form will be returned by mail to the sender along with official ballots and election materials. All ballots must be mailed on one of the two election dates, and post-marked on one of those dates (09/30/95, 10/01/95) or on the Monday following the election (10/02/95), to be considered valid. Who is elected: 100 deputies will be elected by strict proportional representation from five electoral districts: Riga – 27 seats; Kurzeme – 14 seats; Zemgale – 15 seats; Vidzeme – 25 seats; Latgale – 19 seats. A barrier of five percent of the votes cast nationally has been set for parties to be represented in the 6th Saeima. Former KGB agents and persons lacking full Latvian language skills cannot be elected to the Saeima. Individuals found to have been active members of either the Latvian Communist Party or the All-Union Salvation Committee ("Communists") after January 13, 1991 are scratched from election slates; those found to have been agents and collaborators with the KGB or other security forces ("Agents") are exposed publicly, but allowed to stand for election. Communists: none Agents: Maija-Nora Tabaka Western Latvians: none 17) Savienība "Latvijas Ceļš" (Association "Latvia\'s Way", poll: 17.4%; sth Saeima: 32.4%) Latvia\'s Way will not gain the overwhelming support it enjoyed during the sth Saeima elections, but continues to be a powerful force in Latvian politics. Success of Gailis government in addressing security and other issues should allow party to overcome banking crisis and ensure significant participation by LC in 6th Saeima.
