Communists: none Agents: none Western Latvians: Viesturs Pauls Karnups (Australia), Aristids Lambergs (US), Alfreds Ž i gurs (Canada) 19) Demokrātu partija (Democratic Party, poll: 1.0%; sth Saeima: -0-) Minor party with little chance of reaching 5% barrier. Claim to be successor of independence-era party of Latvian Presidents Jānis čakste and Gustavs Zemgals. Communists: none Agents: Gvīdo-Harijs Volbrugs, Harijs Verhovskis Western Latvians: Jānis Valdemārs Liepiņš (Germany) ♦', "CHICAGO LATVIAN NEWSLETTER Saeima Elections Lead To Victory For Latvia's Way The elections to Latvia's first post-communist, post occupation parliament, the sth Saeima, marked a turning point in the renewal of Latvian sovereignty and self-determination. A process begun just more than three years ago, on May 4, 1990, came to a triumphant close as Latvian citizens, both in Latvia and abroad, cast their ballots for candidates representing 23 election slates. Chicago-area Latvians were actively involved in the election process, with nearly four hundred voting in person and many more by mail. The elections also marked the end of the Latvian voter registration drive in the United States, which had been underway since last November. According to figures supplied by the American Latvian Association, more than 9,000 Latvians reactivated their Latvian citizenship, enabling them to vote in the Saeima elections. In spite of difficulties in obtaining registration codes for many who registered in the U.S., 8,064 people actually voted in the United States, or 90.44% of the total number of 8,916 to whom the Embassy of Latvia in Washington was able to distribute election materials. The elections, which took place June 5-6 in Latvia, and from May 29-June 2 outside of Latvia, resulted in a resounding victory for the center-right coalition Latvijas ceļš (Latvia's Way), which is made up of prominent Latvian and emigre politicians. An additional seven candidate slates, including Latvijas Nacionālās Neatkarības Kustība (National Independence Movement of Latvia-LNNK), Saskaiļa Latvijai-Atdzimšana Tautsaimniecībai (Harmony for Latvia-Renewal for the Economy-SLAT), Demokrātiskā Centra Partija (Democratic Center Party-DCP), Latvijas Zemnieku Savienība (Farmer's Union of Latvia-LZS) and Latvijas Kristīgo Demokrātu Savienība (Christian Democratic Union of Latvia-LKDS) also won more than 4% of the total votes cast, entitling them to seats in the 100-member Saeima. The Saeima political spectrum ranges July/August 1993 – Vol. XIII, No. 6 Chicago Voters Place Their Trust In LNNK from the nationalist Tēvzemei un Brīvībai coalition, with six seats, to the pro-Russian Ravnopraviye list, which includes among its seven deputies-elect former Communist Party boss Alfreds Rubiks, who is currently standing trial for attempting to overthrow the Latvian government by force in January and August, 1991 (election results follow in this issue of CLN). One of the interesting conclusions that can be drawn from the election results is the enormous faith placed in the emigre Latvian community by the Latvian electorate. Despite the fact that voters abroad only made up 1.6% of the total electorate, nineteen gained seats in the Saeima, 12 from the United States. Latvians living in the Chicago area were actively involved in both the registration drive and the election itself. According to Illinois registration coordinator and election commission Chairman Ilmārs Bergmanis, 837 people registered their desire to renew their Latvian citizenship at registration points in Illinois. Of this number, 377 actually voted in person at one of three precinct stations set up in the Chicago area, or in Rockford. The remaining persons who voted did so by mail, and were included in the aggregate totals for the United States. These results, along with those from 16 other countries outside of Latvia, were counted as part of the Riga election district. Chicago's results differed from the U.S. and Latvian totals in the victory gained by the National Independence Movement of Latvia (LNNK) slate. Of the 375 valid ballots cast, 166 went to the LNNK. Latvijas ceļš placed second, with 112 votes, followed by the Farmer's Union of Latvia (53 votes), Christian Democratic Union of Latvia (15), Tēvzemei un Brīvībai (12), Popular Front of Latvia (8), Democratic Center Party (3), Anti-Communist Union (2), Green List (2) and Latvijas laime and Economic Activity League (1 each). Provisional results, indicating the numVote Totals – sth Saeima Elections source: Central Election Commission VOTES % SEATS Latvijas ce\\š 362,479 32.38 36 LNNK 149,455 13.35 15 SLAT 134,282 12.00 13 LZS 119,134 10.64 12 Ravnopraviye 64,495 5.76 7 Tēvzemei un Brīvībai 59,994 5.36 6 LKDS 56,136 5.01 6 DCP 53,294 4.76 5 Other 999,272 10.73 0 TOTAL 1,119,390 100.00 100 % Of Electorate That Voted 89.87 ber of seats won by each slate, were available as soon as the early morning hours of June 3. Once this information was known, the difficult process of coalition building began. As this issue of CLN goes to press, it is becoming clear that Latvijas cels will form a ruling coalition with the LZS, with a total of 48 seats. Talks continue with the LKDS, DCP and LNNK in an attempt to secure for the coalition an outright majority of at least 51 seats. The sth Saeima is scheduled to meet in its first session on July 6, at 12:00 noon in the Saeima building. Among the first actions scheduled to be taken by the Saeima will be the election of Latvia's first post-occupation President. Candidates include two emigre deputies-elect (Gunārs Meierovics-LC and Aivars Jerumanis-LKDS) and the nephew of Latvia's last elected President, Kārlis Ulmanis (Guntis Ulmanis-LZS). Latvijas ceļš has also put forward candidates for parliamentary and government leadership positions. Current Supreme Council Chairman Anatolijs Gorbunovs will be nominated to continue this role, now with the title of Chairman of the Saeima. Current Deputy SC Chairman Valdis Birkavs will be nominated as Prime Minister, with Latvian Ambassador to Germany Egīls Levits to be tapped as Justice Minister, SC Permanent Economic Commission Chairman Ojārs Kehris as Economic Reform Minister, and Foreign Ministry State Secretary Māris Gailis as State Minister. Other minister candidates will probably become known as the coalition process continues. □", 'Results of 10th Saeima Elections The voter turnout for Latvia\'s 10th Saeima (parliament) elections on October 2, was 934336 or 62.63% of the electorate, according to the Central Election Commission. Voter turnout saw a slight increase compared to previous general elections in Latvia. In 2006, turnout was 60.91%. Preliminary results received from 1010 polling stations out of 1013, indicated the following: "Unity" (party alliance of New Era, Society for Other Politics, and Civic Union), represented by Prime Minister Valdis Dombrovskis, received 30.76% of votes or 33 seats in the 100-seat parliament. Coalition partners "Union of Greens and Farmers" (party alliance of Latvian Farmers\' Union and Latvian Green Party) received 19.41% or 22 seats and the Party association "All for Latvia" – "For Fatherland and Freedom / LNNK" received 7.56%, 8 seats. Together, existing coalition received strong representation in the new parliament with 63 members. "Harmony Centre" (association of Daugavpils City Party, National Harmony Party, New Centre, Socialist Party of Latvia, and Social Democratic Party) was the second highest vote-getter in thte election with 25.67% or 29 seats. "For Good Latvia" (association of People\'s Party with alliance of Latvia\'s First Party and Latvia\'s Way), had the lowest vote count among those who qualified, with 7.54% approval and 8 seats. Arnis Cimdars, Head of the Central Election Commmission, said: "No serious violations have been reported during Latvia\'s parliamentary elections that might change their outcome". The Organisation for Security and Co-operation in Europe observation mission in Latvia reported that elections generally met OSCE and international standards for democratic elections. "The election process provided voters with the opportunity to make a free choice among candidates presenting different political alternatives," said Nikolai Vulchanov, head of the ODIHR limited election observation mission. The Saeima elections were held in five constituencies: Rīga, Vidzemē, Latgale, Zemgale and Kurzeme. Each constituency has different candidate lists and voters can only select from the candidate lists from the constituency they are voting in. Voters residing abroad can participate in elections by attending any of the 64 polling stations established abroad or by postal voting organised by 24 of those polling stations. Voters abroad voted for the Rīga candidate lists. Only those lists of candidates which have received at least 5% of the total number of votes cast in all five election constituencies are elected to the Saeima.']

Name: 10_citizenship_law_amendment_citizen
Representative_Docs:
['ESTONIAN CITIZENSHIP LAW The Estonian parliament on 19 January adopted a new citizenship law by a vote of 60 to 6. Under the previous legislation, people could apply for citizenship after two years residency in the country and could be naturalized one year after submitting the application. The new law requires five years residency to apply for citizenship. It also bans Estonian citizens from having dual citizenship but allows such citizenship for ethnic Estonians who are citizens of another country. President Lennart Meri signed the new citizenship law on 1 February. Criticism by Russia's Foreign Ministry that the new legislation "would strip a significant part of the Russian speaking population of the possibility of receiving Estonian citizenship" was rejected by Estonian officials. They said Russia failed to understand the law and was issuing a "routine statement." The new law applies only to current arrivals to Estonia and does not affect Soviet-era immigrants, who can still apply for citizenship under the old legislation.', 'Special commission to study changes to Latvia's citizenship law Andris Straumanis – March 23, 2011 A special subcommission will be created to work out differences among three sets of proposed amendments to Latvia's citizenship law, including changes that could lead to allowing dual citizenship in certain circumstances. The Saeima's Law Commission decided March 23 to form the subcommission to study the proposed amendments as well as other issues related to the Citizenship Law, according to the parliamentary press office. "To codify all these initiatives and to take into account suggestions from experts and the public, we have decided to form a subcommission to prepare a bill," lima Čepāne, chair of the Law Commission, said in a press release. Since January, the Saeima has received bills from the National Association (Nacionālā apvienība "Visu Latvijai!" – "Tēvzemei un Brīvībai/LNNK") and from the Unity (Vienotība) bloc, as well as an initiative from President Valdis Zatlers. Čepāne, a member of the Unity (Vienotība) bloc, herself is a co-sponsor of one of the proposed amendments. Under Latvian law, dual citizenship no longer is allowed. From 1991 to July 1995, exile Latvians and their descendants were able to register as Latvian citizens without giving up the citizenship of their home country. Since then, pressure to reconsider restrictions in the law has increased both from exile Latvians who missed the 1995 deadline and from politicians interested in helping thousands of postindependence emigrants maintain ties with their homeland. All three sets of amendments seek to allow dual citizenship for wartime exiles and their descendants, as well as to former Latvian citizens who have become citizens of European Union, European Free Trade Association and NATO defense alliance member states. However, the proposals differ in several ways. While all three would allow dual citizenship for persons who left Latvia between the start of the Soviet occupation of Latvia in June 1940 and Latvia's declaration of independence from the U.S.S.R. in May 1990, only Unity's proposal has language that specifically mentions World War II exiles (trimdinieki), creating a specific paragraph in the law identifying them. All three proposals address Latvians now living abroad, especially in Europe. While the United States and Canada are not mentioned by name, they would fall under the proposals' desire to extend dual citizenship to Latvians in NATO member states. Only the National Alliance proposal specifically addresses Australia and Brazilcountries with significant ethnic Latvian populations. The president's amendments and Unity's bill could include Australia and Brazil under provisions recognizing dual citizenship through bilateral agreements. Only Unity's and the president's proposals include language allowing dual citizenship for persons who become citizens of another country through marriage. Only the National Association and the president's proposals mention granting dual citizenship rights to children adopted by Latvian parents. The president's initiative also includes language to make it easier for children born of non-citizens in Latvia to become citizens. The Law Commission will ask the Saeima to set a deadline in September for recommendations to be submitted for the legislation's second reading. Each fraction in the Saeima will be asked to appoint two representatives to the subcommission, according to the parliament's press office. Zatlers is expected to meet at the beginning of April with a group of ethnic Latvian youth in the United States interested in the dual citizenship issue. The president will be attending the annual conference of the Joint Baltic American National Committee in Washington, D.C.', 'Saeima committee reintroduces amendments to citizenship law

Andris Straumanis

-Nov. 08, 2011

Amendments to Latvia’s citizenship law that, among other changes, would again allow VVorld War 11-era exiles to obtain dual citizenship have been reintroduced in the Saeima.

The parliament’s Legal Affairs Committee, led by Ilma Čepāne of the Unity (Vienotība) party, on Nov. 3 brought back six bills that were left unresolved when the last Saeima was dissolved. One of those, the amendments to the citizenship law, was originally introduced Feb. 17. One amendment in the bill vvould single out exiles, allovving them to register as Latvian citizens. Under the proposal, the amendment vvould apply to persons vvho were Latvian citizens and vvho left their homeland betvveen the start of the first Soviet occupation on June 17, 1940, and May 4, 1990, when Latvia’s Supreme Council declared the country’s independence from the U.S.S.R. The descendants of exiles also vvould be allovved to register as Latvian citizens.

Also under bill Nr. 52/Lpll, dual citizenship vvould be allovved for: • Latvian citizens vvho have obtained citizenship in another European Union or European Free Trade Association member state. • Latvian citizens vvho have become citizens of another members state of the NATO defense alliance. • Latvian citizens vvho have become citizens of another country with vvhich Latvia has

a treaty recognizing dual citizenship. • Latvian citizens vvho have the permission of the Cabinet of Ministers to maintain their Latvian citizenship. • A child born outside of Latvia, if at least one of their parents is a Latvian Citizen and if under the law of the country in vvhich they are born a child automatically become a Citizen of that country. • A person vvho as a result of marriage becomes a Citizen of another country.

In the 10th Saeima, the amendments were referred to the Legal Affairs Committee, vvhich set up a subcommittee to study the proposed changes. The subcommittee set a deadline of Sept. 1 to take comment on the amendments, but by that datē the 10th Saeima vvas running out of time.

Likevvise, the 11 th Saeima’s Legal Affairs Committee on Nov. 1 decided to again form a subcommittee to look at the law.

One of the charges of the subcommittee, Čepāne said in a Saeima press release, will be to call on experts to provide input on the proposed amendments.

“The citizenship law has not been amended for many years,” she said, “and fundamental changes in it are needed in both content and technical points.”

The subcommittee will examine four separate areas, according to the press release: dual citizenship for exile Latvians; citizenship for children born abroad to Latvian citizens; situations in vvhich Latvian citizenship has been revoked because of current restrictions on dual citizenship; and the granting of Latvian citizenship to children born in Latvia to non-citizens.']

At a peace congress of the Catholic clergy in October 1951, Health Minister Plojhar spoke "in behalf of" the Catholics when he declared: "We Czech Catholics completely differ from I the Vatican\'s present policy wdiich fully serves world capitalism and imperialism and supports the crusade against the Soviet Union \' and the People\'s Democracies. . . . We have full right as Catholic j priests to disagree with Vatician policy. . . ." Radio Prague. March 27. 1952 ļ reported: "Adding its voice to rerent protests of Catholic clergy and laity, the Presidium of the Central Committee of Catholic Action of Chechoslovakia has appealed to Christians through the world to condemn the American imperialists\' use of bacterial warfare. . . ." The Communist regime in Czechoslovakia now openly admits ļthat "religious superstitution" is its principal foe. The "eradication of religious traditions" is to Ibe accomplished through stepped up activities of Catholic Action (creation of further schismatic leadership sympathetic to the State), the Central Action Committee (disseminationg of atheistic propaganda), and through the Czechoslovak Society for the Propagation of Political and Scientific Knowledge. BALTICS: Religious persecution in the Baltic countries is especially significant not only because it was the first example outside of Russia, but because it was ruthlessly carried out under the direct supervision of the USSR. In the brief I span of one summer the Soviets incorporated every Baltic state into the USSR. Red armies entered in June 1940 and in July the Communists had seized governmenttal control. Puppet parliaments were granted "admission" into the USSR in mid-August and forthwith adopted models of the Soviet Constitution. Among the many legal pretexts by which the USSR thereafter exploited the area was the insertion of portions of Article 124,  \'npter 10 ol t he parent Soviet locumont into each of the Baltic constitutions. Ironically, its language ensured freedom of religious worship, but also implied freedom of anti-religious propaganda. Using this single provision as a fuicrum, the Communists have since confiscated Church property, banned religious organisations and the press, prohibited religious instruction, imposed taxes for performance of Church rites, and denounced religion and clergy alike in a widespread propaganda programme. The extent to which Communism has pervaded religious life in the Baltic Countries may be vividly expressed in terms of pre-1940 religious influences in the three countries. LITHUANIA The dominant Church in Lithuania is Roman Catholic, which in 1940 represented approximately SO per cent., or 2,400,000 of the country\'s 3 million population. Its 1,202 Churches were served by 1.64G priests in 681 parishes organized in 2 archdioceses and 4 dioceses headed by 3 archbishops and 8 bisheps. Reformed Calvinist, Moravian and Evangelical Brethren. Episcopal-Methodist. Seventh Day Adventists, and Baptists. TECHNIQUES OF ASSIMILATION. Two techniques were used: seizure or nationalization of schools. Churches, seminaries, libraries, publications, religious and cultural organizations; arrest, imprisonment, deportation and murder of cultured elements in the population. Further, relations with the outside world were cut off, and particular stress was laid on severing connections with the Vatican. An MVD order in Lithuania stipulated that all organizations established before June 20, 1940 "jeopardized public security." The gfeaAe thereupon took over property, archives, membership rosters, and funds of all religious organizations. This nationalization of private property caused the expulsion of Marian, Jesuit and Franciscan friars from their premises. Confiscation of the religious press was aggravated by j seizure of all stocks of religious books found in shops, printing j offices and homes. In Estonia, a joint order of the ! Council of People\'s Commissars and the Central Committee of the Communist /-\'arty, published in Official Gazette No. 17, October 2, 1940, Article 192, nationalized lands belonging to Churches and 1 religious associations. Confisca-1 ! tion went hand in hand with na- > j tionalization and exicted clergy- ļ men from their parishes, forced 1 suspension of the religious press, and closed Tartu University\'s De-! oartment of Theology. A nationalization law affecting all private property was promulgated in Latvia on July 23, 1940. The Baptist Seminary in Riga, was expelled from its headquarters under this decree. Rent was charged by the State for the use of any Church which the regime had taken over. High taxes were imposed for the use of confiscated Church regalia and records, | making confirmation and baptism virtually impossible. A special MVD authorisation, costing 400 rubles and equal to an average monthly wage, was required for a Church wedding. Moreover services outside the Church itself, such as those in cemeteries, schools or private homes were forbidden. Decrees outlawing religious instruction were issued in all three countries and courses on Marxist theory were then introduced in the schools. In Lithuania priests were asked to sign the following form: "I . . . sign the attached as evidence that I was informed on April . . . 1941 that I am strictly enjoined not to give religious instruēt ion to school children . . . in Churches ... or in the homes of children or in my own apartment or elsewhere. Thus I have no right whatsoever to talk to them about religious matters. . . ." (To be Continued)', 'The Red and the Black (Continued from previous issue) MASS MURDER AND DEPORTATION. By June 1941 the Soviets had destroyed all organizations representing Baltic culture, had overwhelmed the population with I atheistic and Socialistic propa| ganda, and had imposed a general I state of terror. A widespread I programme of mass deportation and murder followed. Before their forced retreat from Nazi armies in late June, the U.S.S.R. deported 65,000 Lithuanians, including 26 Roman Catholic j priests; an additional 15 were murdered. In Latvia, 12 Evan! gelical Lutheran pastors were deported, 2 murdered; 37 Baptist ministers were deported; 4 Roman Catholic priests were deported, 7 murdered. A total of 9,730 were deported from Estonia, and of these 15 Evangelical Lutheran I pastors were included; 2 more murdered, along with 24 Church Council members. Six Greek Orthodox ministers were deported, 6 murdered. The terror which cnaracterised the last month of Soviet rule continued under Nazi occupation between 1941 and 1944. Arrest, murder and deportation were the Nazis\' principal weapons against clergymen and the population as well, but no \'concerted campaign to eliminate religious life in regard to schools, associations, Churches and traditions was apparent during their tenure. POSTWAR Methodical attacks against the Church were resumed and intensified by the Soviets when they retook the Baltic countries in 1944. ļ Since then all Churches have come under State administrations; many are closed. The laity is discouraged from attending services by replacing Sundays and religious holidays (declared illegal) with compulsory Communist celebrations. Religious ceremonials are forbidden to Communist Party members and their families. Marxist theory has an established place in all school curricula, supplemented by an intensive antireligious propaganda campaign directed against the population as a whole. Mass deportations and arrests affecting all segments of the Baltic population have continued both for religious and political [purposes, but it ts impossible-tr>\' give exact figures on the number of people involved nor is it possible to distinguish clearly the purposes of each wave of persecution. Exile researchers, by comparing 1939 census figures with such available data as the number of ballots cast in recent elections, have corroborated a number of reliable refugee reports, and some estimates can be made. In the first big postwar deportation, approximately 50,000 Latvians — mostly males from 16 to 60 years of age —were included. A second mass deportation took place in Estonia and Latvia in March 1949. It is estimated that at that time 80,000 Estonians and 70,000 Latvians were removed. In May 1948, 100,00 OLithuanians suffered the same fate and are included in estimates numbering 550,000 for the entire period 1945-1949—a total of six deportation cycles. Only 30 of Estonia\'s 250 Lutheran clergymen are still on their native soil; less than 340 Catholic priests are left of Lithuania\'s former 1600. Almost half of Latvia\'s 279 Lutheran pastors have escaped to the West; of the remainder, it is know that 21 were deported and 4 killed during the two Soviet occupations, and it is estimated that only about 30 per cent, still reside in Latvia. POLAND: For centuries religion has been an essential part of Poland\'s history and culture and the conflict of the Roman Catholic Church and the Communist State in modern times can be understood only in those terms. The Church\'s interpenetration of the life of the Polish community created specific problems in the Stalinist treatment of it. Moreover, the conflict can be truly comprehended only if the nationalist nature of I Polish Catholicism is taken for granted. Because Poland is almost completely Catholic (some 96 per .cent.), the Communists j were unable to assail the Church openly. Communist attacks was made even more difficult because both clergy\' and ChdriS had been ! anti-Nazi, part of the resistance j movement, and therefore popular with the people. Nevertheless, the battle was j undertaken between the sickle and the crozier in Poland, and it falls into two stages. From 1945 to 1948 there was a period of relative calm and quiet, with the Communists proceeding cautiously while they consolidated their political gains. From 1948 to the present, a new era of State-Church struggle was launched which is as yet unresolved. The campaign began slowly and the first step was revocation of the Concordat on September 2. 1945. Press attacks against the clergy and the Catholic Church\'s welfare organization, "Caritas," were invoked but not yet as a concerted campaign. Only when the Communists felt secure in \' their control of the goevrnmental machinery did they attempt to disrupt the clerical machinery and that was in 1949. The following chronology gives an indication of how rapidly the attack grew: August 5. 1949—Government decree published safeguarding freedom of creed and conscience. August 11. 1949—Government rouisitioned all birth records from parish churches. August 18, 1949—Intensive anti-Papal campaign begun in the Communist press. September 21, 1949- Church hospitals nationalised under Ministry of Health. November 23, 1949—Bulletin of the Minister of Public Administration imposes serious limitations on public worship. January 23. 1950—Catholic charity organisation, "Caritas," taken over in sudden government confiscation. March 20, 1950—Confiscation of 375,000 acres of Church property. From August 1949 to April 1950, press vilification of the clergy continued to increase in intensity. Charges of collaboration with the Nazis, espionage, bribery, immorality were alternated with accusations of clerical alliance with reactionary elements internally and imperialist elements externally. T-i the interim the Communists v \\d not been idle on the Trojan Horse front. A "patriotic priest" organisation and a lay organisation of "progressive Catholics" i had been called into being, their purposes to split clergy from episcopate, flock from Church, and simultaneously to create cadres ļ for a new "people\'s clergy." By i using elements from the Polish Army\'s Chaplain Corps, the regime mobilised the so-called "Commission of Priests" attached to the Union of Defenders of Fighters for Freedom and Democracy. These "patriot priests" were accorded special privileges by the regime in the way of finances, tax exemptions, confiscations of property, etc. Also, as early as June, 1946, the Communists had sponsored and propagated a "National Catholic Church" as a diversionary effort. Neither attempt at creating schism within the Church succeeded, however. THE APRIL AGREEMENT After the abrogation of the Concordat in 1945, the government and the episcopate conducted prolonged negotiations for an agreement to regulate Church-State relations. These negotiations began and continued for four years before they finally resulted in an agreement signed on April 14, 1950 by the bishops and the State Department of Religious Affairs. On paper the Communists had made a number of concessions, and the agreement received the support of all the bishops. Under this new arrangement, the Church agreed: (The April 22 episcopal statement on this agreement issued at a conference in Gniezno contained the complete text.) a. To teach respect for the State\'s law and authority among the faithful. b. To reprove anti-government activities. c. Not to oppose the govern-I ment programme of collectivisa- i I tion. j , d. To ask the Vatican for re-! i cognition of Poland\'s claims to the ļ so-called "Recovered" or "Wesj tern" territories, which had been ceded to Poland by East Gerļ many. c. To imbue the faithful with sentiments of brotherly love and peace. For its part, the government: a. Assured religious instruction in the schools. b. Agreed to the maintenance of Catholic schools, religious ministration in the Armies, hospitals and prisons. c. Conceded the right of the Church to conduct charitable works. d. Agreed to recognise the right to publish Catholic periodicals. c. Conceded that the Pope is the decisive and highest authority in matters of canon, faith and morality. The innocuous final point of the episcopate\'s concessions provided the Communists with the pietext for breaking the agreement. The regime asked the Church to support the Stockholm Peace Appeal and the Church refused. The Communists then insisted that the episcopacy had abrogated the agreement by not working "for peace" and proceeded to intensify their anti-religious persecutions. Under regime pounding the Church capitulated and agreed to sign the Stockholm Appeal, but the Communists remained unappeased. Many Catholic schools were closed; a hostile press campaign was invoked against both the episcopate and the Vatican; anti-religious propaganda was stepped up; the Catholic press was hamstrung by censorship and discrimination in newsprint allocation was used against its wide circulation. All these aggressive activities, of course, were directed at the central problem which the Stalinist regime wanted to solve; the problem of the Western territories. THE WESTERN TERRITORIES. One of the thorniest problems facing the Polish Church was that of the territories acquired from Germany after the war. Here were dioceses to which the Pope refused to appoint bishops, since Vatican policy has been not to create permanent bishoprics pending peace treaties and final disposition of territorial rights and claims. It was the Vatician refusal (point four in the April State-Church agreement) which actually was the Communists\' chief concern. An apostolic administration for the Western territories had been created by the late Cardinal Hlond, but it was a provisional arrangement. The Communists wanted permanent authorities installed, particularly after the signing of the Zgorzeled Agreement between Poland and East Germany on July 6, 1950, as a sign of the Vatican\'s recognition of the revised territorial rearrangements. On October 23, 1950, an ultimatum was sent to the Polish hierarchy in which the regime stated that the provisional Church administration in the Western territories could no longer be tolerated. A campaign of oppressive action, mass meetings, and press invective followed and on January 26, 1951, the regime issued a decree liquidating the temporary Church adminst ration and removing the priests who acted as apostolic administrators. Immediately, to avoid the possibilities of schism, the Church itself, according to canon law, elected its own diocesan administrators, capitular vicars, to function in these territories, and Archbishop Stefan Wyszynski (now Cardinal) immediately confirmed the appointments. In May, 1952, Primate Wyszynski granted bishops\' privileges to the capitular vicars of two of the dioceses, and established a curiae in Wroclaw. This problem of the Church administration in the Western territories was >the main object of regime attacks on hierarchy and Apostolic See, and the stabilisation of Church administration there was the best possible reply to Communist attack. In this issue the Communists have cleverly exploited Polish nationalist feeling, which overwhelmingly wishes to retain the Western territories, by accusing the Vatican of pro-German sentiments, thereby attempting to create a schism between the Polish Catholics and the Apostolic See. The numerical strength and the constancy of Polish Catholics has prevented the Communist anti-religious programme from being fulfilled as quickly in Poland as it has been elsewhere. Although curtailment of Church activity has been significant, there is still religious teaching in the schools (but the Communists are developing a parallel completely secularised school system run by the Communist Association of Children\'s Friends [the TPD]; the Polish Church has not been dispossessed; there are still theological faculties and seminaries; clerical press, encumbered and censored though it is, still exists and is circulated. A Catholic University is open; pastoral letters can be read from the pulpit; bishops may visit their dioceses; and there is no loyalty oath for clerg.\' (there is one, however, For newly appointed bishops and vicars capitular) as there is in Hungary and Czechoslovakia. Moreover, the campaign against the Church in Poland has had a minimal amount of the largejcale arrests, murders and deportations which have characterised sther Communist persecutions. Moslem 10,000 – THE ORTHODOX CHURCH The Romanian Orthodox Churcl has long been a unifying force ii the life of Romania and identifie< with the Romanian State mucl as the Church of England is iden titled with the British State Moreover, historically, it has beei a nub of resistance to foreign ag gressors who in the past hay« included Russians, Germans Turks, Greeks, etc. Because o; the large number of Orthodo? faithful (more than 70 per cent of the population) and because oi the wealth and influence of th< Church, it was a prime target foi the Communist anti-religiouj campaign. Most important, th< Communists saw the possibilitj of converting the Church and its clergy into an instrument for propagating Communism and controlling the population. The campaign instituted against Orthodoxy therefore moved for the subjugation of the Orthodox Church toward those ends. In spite of a publicly declared benevolence, the regime led by Petru Groza attempted its first step: control of the clergy through a "Union of Democratic Priests" led by the then Minister of Cults, Father Burducea. When this gambit failed, more drastic measures were taken. In 1947, two laws were passed by Parliament— one for pensioning priests and the second for redistributing sees and promulgating new rules for episcopal assemblies—which permitted the Communists to replace recalcitrant clergy with their own adherents. Over 30 per cent, of parish priests in towns and villages were thus dismissed from their parishes. The new rules for episcopal assemblies paved the way for election of three new \'People\'s Metropolitans" and in 1948. after the death of Patriarch Micodemus, his replacement with i Communist partisan, Patriarch Justinian Marina. The purpose of this pamphlet is: – to provide information about the Soviet government\'s v i o 1 a t i on s against the Church in the now occupied but formerly independent Latvia, – to document for the world\'s free Christian churches the threat that Communist regimes pose for the future of Christianity and – to appeal to the worldwide Church community to work for the survival of Christianity. Official Church Delegates from the Soviet Union who visit abroad are not an accurate source of information about the present conditions of the Church. These officials, as representatives of a state institution must comply with the orders received from the Committee for State Security in Moscow. A seemingly independent church hierarchy does exist, but only to guarantee that the State\'s official atheistic policy can proceed without interference. The same can be said of official exchanges between the Soviet Union and Western Churches. The representatives from the West have access to information prepared by propaganda authorities. Pastoral visits to congregations outside occupied Latvia\'s capital are prohibited. Therefore only the silent witnesses, the church structures themselves are a reliable source of information. But these churches are: – not under the jurisdiction of their true owners, the congregations, – used for non-religious purposes (concert-sport halls, clubs, farm annexes, museums and antireligious museums, etc.), – allowed to deteriorate or are vandalized. We would betray Baltic Christians if we remained silent and did not notice these ruins that both accuse and call for our help! "I tell you that if these should hold t^eir peace, the stones would immediately cry out1" (Luke 19,40) – said Jesus. Religious discrimination no less than racial discrimination is unworthy of man and opposes the will of God. CURRENT STATUS OF THE EVANGELICAL LUTHERAN CHURCH IN OCCUPIED LATVIA The Church has lost its legal rights. Paragraph 52 of the Constitution of the Union of Soviet Socialist Republics guarantees the separation of Church and the State. " The Church is separated from the State and the school from the Churc . All citizens are guaranteed the right to practice their faith and access to anti-religious propaganda." Thus coexistence between the Church and State is recognized as de facto, not de iure. The church remains subordinate to the State and bound by the State\'s interpretation of this separate status. Thus: (a) the Church is prohibited to intervene in State affairs, but (b) the State dictates internal Church affairs. As regards the question of religious freedom, the Church is deprived of its right to openly carry out its mission and defend itself against assault. Only official religious activities are allowed, ceremonies held at State determined sites by State controlled congregations. Proliferation of religious beliefs/materials or the defense against arguments of non-believers is not permitted. X The Communist Party actively encourages that recently introduced secular ceremonies replace traditional religious rites (christenings, burials, confirmations, weddings) and that these be preformed at locations that have special significance for religious communities, for exampl, cemeteries. Thereby legal right to choose between the religious or secular ceremonies is denied. Affiliation with a religious congregation and participation in its activities is considered by THESE RUINS ACCUSE A RECORD OF RELIGIOUS OPPRESSION OF THE EVANGELICAL LUTHERAN CHURCI IN OCCUPIED LATVIA Riga Dome Evangelical Lutheran Church. The Cathedral was built in the XII century. Now it is used as a concert nan. me altar was demolished, the inside of the building reconstructed. Soviet State officials as potentially subversive, a dangerous "anachronism" that delays society\'s development toward an ideal Communist State. This affiliation also prevents admittance to institutions of higher education and positions within the civil service section. Church officials must endorse the Soviet State and Communist ideology and defend its positions on questions such as the Vietnam war, the occupation of Afghanistan, Moscow Olympics at national and international forums. This way the "separate" Church becomes a "State Church" . . . ("ecclesia ancilla communismi"). A CHURCH WITHOUT JURISDICTION OVER ITS PROPERTY The 1929 and 1976 Soviet law pertaining to religious practices states that all religious sites and all religious implements belong to the State, but religious congregations, with at least 20 State registered members may request permission for usage from local authorities. This restricts religious activity to official sites where State regulations for usage must be followed. If the congregation loses this right, it thereby also loses the constitutional right to free religious practice. The effect of this law is clearly reflected in statistics of church buildings in Soviet occupied Latvia. In 1944 Lutheran congregations administered 300 churches including 50 filial churches but by 1980 the number had dropped to one half, 138 churches including 17 filial churches. This was accomplished by the Soviet State in the following manner: 1) Congregations were denied the right to use . the churches. 45% of all Ev. Lutheran churches 1 in Latvia were destroyed or damaged during WW 11. j Congregations had to undertake renovation with- \' out State subsidies. Therefore the State though the legal owners, did not always maintain its property. \' In many places, Bārta, Gramzda, Salgale(Kur- j land), Gaujiena, Salaspils(Livland) State con- J gregations were denied permission to restore churches damaged during the war. Of jr; registered Lutheran congregat .ons in 1980,6: were without places of worship and therefore hac to utilize churches of neighboring congregates or other denominations. Valmiera congregat-f) (Livland) meets in a Baptist nouse. 2) Revoking former utilization rights. – The  of Lutherans fluctuates In ue areas -f massive population relocation durina and a(> the war. In 1975 40" of occupied Latvia\'s pmilation consisted of people from other Soviet xcupied republics, especially from Russia, [f the congregation\'s membership slides under i], it automatically loses it; rights to a church, • State law dictates that congregations must pay rent a-i property taxes to the State according to a social higher tax rate calculated by the size cf the church – m 3. These taxes also apply to very Sid or architecturally significant churches under tfte protection of State authorities as historical sites. Orthodox Cathedral in Riga. The Crosses on the towers are sawed off and is now a planetarium and cafe. Thus criurches are expropriated from congregations although activity has not ceased. The number of caches not used by congregations contin ually grows, from – 63 or 30% of all confiscated churches to 120 in 1980, or 75% of all church es confiscated. 3) Churches confiscated from congregations are used for non-religious or even anti-religious purposes – Restaurants and clubs: Cepļi, Piksari (Livland) Sēme, Penkule (Kurland); Varakļāni (Lettgallen) – Cinemas: Abrene, Kārsava, Preiļi (Lettgallen) . Farm annexes: Citadele (Riga); AdaŽi, AllaZi, Baldone, Burtnieki, Carnikava, Jumprava, Ropaži (Livland ; Birži, Demene, Ecjipte, Glūda, Grīve, lie, Mežotne, Reņcje, Snēpele, Sniķere, Vecsaule (Kuriand i Daugavpils, Kacēni, Rēzekne, Vidsmuila, Zilut* lettgallen) -Sport "alls: Ilūkste (Kurland); Krāslava (Lettgallen) – Museums of Atheism – Mati Si (Livland) and Do)ele (Kurland); of Architecture/Provincial History – St- Peter (Rīga), Koknese (Livland); War Museum – oetalva (Livland) . Concert halls: Dome (Riga), Reformed c. (Riga); Straupe. Valmiera (Livland); Trinity c./Liepāja Kurland\'; Ludza (Lettgallen). AntV?ngious objects, reproductions and por:raits c Communist leaders are places on altars, ,s in the Museum of Atheism in Dobele Church Kurland and in the Dome church in Riga. \\) Vandanzed and damaged church buildings where ■eligious implements have been damaged or destroyEd are continually allocated to religious congregations. After burglarization 1) church interiors (pews, pulpits, organs, etc.) are totally destroyed at Gaigalava (Lettgallen); Bērstele, Saldus, Spāre, Valle (Kurland); ?.) windows are broken at Skulte (Liviand), Daudzeva (Kurland) and other places; 3) altar paintings damaged at Gaigalava (Lettgallen), Jaunsaule (Kurland); 4) Broken crucifix at Mazzalve (Kurland), and stolen at Sala (Livland). Congregations, whose churches have unaccountably burned down, find themselves in exceptionally difficult situations. For example:in lecava lecava (Kurland) burned down in 1968 V3rme (Kurland) " " " 1968 Zasa (Kurland) " " " 1968 Bolderaja (Livland) " ■ " 1970 Mežaparks (Riga) 1970 Liepupe (Livland) " " " 1971 Rūjiena (Livland) " " " 1974 Gipka (Kurland) * " " 1979 In many cases State and Communist Party supported atheistic propaganda has caused unrest leading to the destruction of these churches or the religious implements. MINISTERS ARE DENIED FREEDOM Official Vladimir ;KUROEOOV from the Soviet Union Council defines the Communist Party\'s views on the rights of religious practices and ministers (IZVESTIJA 1/13/76): "No citizen is forced to: a) believe or be an atheist, b) practice or not participate in religious ceremonies." But even though freedom to organize and lead religious ceremonies has not been denied, there exists in occupied Latvia restrictions for individuals who manage or fulfill religious ceremonial duties. The State intervenes and determines the contents and order of religious ceremonies and restricts, at times even represses, the work of believers. It is prohibited to ring church bells in many places throughout occupied Latvia and to purchase necessary ceremonial implements. The number of candles during the Christmas season is limited to prevent their use during Christmas Eve services, but candle burning is encouraged during the official New Year\'s Eve celebrations. For the same reason candles may not be included in gift packages from abroad. The leaders of congregations are subject to threats and repressions by local authorities at the registration of commencement and conclusion of activities. Parents are denied their rights to carry out religious training in the home through forced home visits and threats by school administrators and teachers. The Soviet State law prohibits minors to actively participate in religious training and services. The number of ministers demeans as a result of continual restrictions on a) congregations, b) the education of new ministers, retirement and death of pastors as well as deportations, arrests and suspension of clerical rights. Number of Ev. Lutheran ministers in occupied Latvia: 1945 1963 1969 1980 120 103 87 76* *49 of these where ordained before 1945. It has become very difficult for ministers to perform thei- pastoral duties. All parsonages have been expropriated by the State and, unlike churches, are not made available to congregations. Only 10 congregations retain rights to their parsonages. In Gaujiena (Livland) services are held in the parsonage. As a result: ~ . \' – only about 40 or one half of all ministers dwell near the congregation; ; , – a disproportionately large number (23) live in the capital city Rīga; . – 10 congregations are without a minister. Often ministers must travel great distances to hold church services as well as serve several congregations simultaneously: nc Neietas (Kurland) minister serves 8 congregations AraiSu (Livland) minister serves 7 congregations. S 30 or one half of all ministers are over Church workers/ministers are subject to >he  The Soviet State has established the Council for Religious Affairs with representatives in occupied Latvia. This Council receives directives directly from the KGB, supports Communist Party ideology hostile toward religion, and has taken over the right of the church: – to prohibit the minister\'s right to practice; – to scrutinize candidates for the ministry; – toMecide the appointment and transfer of ministers without consulting the congregations in question. All employees of the Church are responsible for transgressions of Soviet law pertaining to religious practices. According to this law, imprisonment awaits those who "resist anti-religious propaganda", or who "utilize religion as pretext for the distribution of anti-Soviet propaganda". The minister of Ļaudona congregation (Livland) received a 5 year sentence for breaking the former law, and H. Grīvāns, the minister of the congregation at Ventspils (Kurzeme) was sentenced to 10 years imprisionment for breaking the latter law. This sentence was extended for another 8 years after the original term. Ministers are forced to work under the threat of arrests, deportation, and involuntary placement in psychiatric hospitals even after their release. 90% of all ministers ordained before 1945, who have continued their work in the official Church have been subject to one of the above. THE CHURCH IS DENIED FREEDOM OF THE PRESS According to Soviet law, only the State i.e. Party possesses the right to print and distribute any literature whatsoever. This has greatly restricted the freedom of the Church\'s activity. Announcements concerning services and those of an official nature may not appear in newspapers nor may they be distributed by mail. Announcements may only be posted on church doors. The necessary religious books may be printed only with special permission. Each service song sheet requires a special permit. The only translation of the Bible in Latvian was published in 1960. It did, however, contain only the New Testiment and Psalms and was published in 5000 copies, a number that was too small already during the time of printing. These Bibles have not been available for a long time. In 1976 the Church of Sweden offered to provide the paper necessary for a new edition of the Bible, but as of yet has not received an official reply. Similarily, permission has not been granted to print the already completed Minister\'s Handbook (agenda). In 1954 a revised edition of the Latvian Ev. Lutheran Song Book was published. It included new special songs for the celebration of State holidays, while many well known Psalms were eliminated on the grounds that they were "suspicious". The Latvian Ev.Lutheran congregations in occupied Latvia are prohibited to publish a religious newspaper. The only periodical of the Church is the Ev.Lutheran calendar. It is prohibited to send or bring into the Soviet Union Bibles, Psalm books and scientific theological literature published abroad. At the same time atheistic literature printed in the Soviet Union may not be sent abroad, probably so as not to give evidence of the Soviet Union\'s hostile attitude toward religion. Aaugstrozes Evangelical Lutheran Church is now a granary']
