Representative_Docs: ['Important political concessions ,ICCDAt lts recent session the Supreme Soviet of the  approved a new farm tax law under which the state revenue from this tax will decrease by 4,137 million rubles, or 43 per cent, in 1953, while in 1954 it will bring in 2.5 times less to the state than in 1952. It it had remained in force in its original form this tax would have brought in to the state 9.6 milliard rubles or 1.8 per cent, of the total state revenue in 1953, as apparent from the report of A. G. Zverev, the USSR minister for finances; in its new form it will bring in 5.5. milliard rubles, or 1.0 per cent, of the total revenue. From the point of view of the economy of the Soviet state the tax was and is negligible. Its importance lies in the fact that it was never a fiscal tax but a political one and has served political purposes from the first day ol its introduction. Originally the state made use of this tax to ruin the private farmers. The tax was so high that they had the choice between two alternatives: either to join a collective lurr.i or to wait until they were ciiarged with sabotage for not paying the tax, with the inevitable consequeiKv of arrest and deportation. (For a detailed analysis of the agricultural tax of, NEWSLETTER No. 112, Vol. 111, 1949). When private farming was destroyed the edge of the agricultural tax was directed against tnose kolkhoz fanners who derived income from their garden plots consisting of O. 25-0.50 hectares and in a very few areas of up to one hectare of land, and from their private cattle the number of which is strictly limited by the kolkhoz statutes. The reason was the mortifying circumstance that on their paltry individual patches the kolkhoz members raised twice as much land as the kolkhozes on their large estates. When alter the recent war the government introduced a special cattle breeding plan and took drastic measures to increase the livestock in the kolkhozes, it taxed the private income of the kolkhoz members from their! garden plots and cattle most ! cruelly, raising the tax from | year to year. As the kolkhozes did not manage to breed the prescribed number of cattle, the cattle to the kolkhozes. To give a certain idea of the severity of the agricultural tax it suffices to mention that a j kolkhoz member owning one yoat paid on it a yearly , tax of 21 rubles and 60 kopeks. The agricultural tax was progressive. On an income oi up ļ to 2000 rubles per year the tax was 12 per cent, while the tax on that part of the income which exceeded 8000 rubles was 1 4S per cent. If a kolkhoz member nad a garden plot of 0.5 hec- i tares and a cow, his cash income from work on the kolkhoz was rarely sufficient to pay the tax j on his garden plot and cow. As a result of such taxation the number of kolkhoz members not owning any livestock has been growing from year to year and a large proportion of kolkhozes made little progress in cattle breeding. In the winter cattle perished wholesale as a result of fodder shortage and its productivity was extremely low. At the CP congress of last October A. Mikoyan, the minister for trade, admitted that the country did not produce enough meat and fats to satisfy the demand of the population <tespite the high retail prices of. these commodities as compared to incomes. This admission and , the present reduction of the agri- , cultural tax leave no doubt that the farming and cattle breeding plans of the state and with them the Communist ideological attitude with regard to farming have proved failures. The new agricultural tax differs from the old one in two significant respects: 1 Now only the land used by the kolkhoz member is taxed and not his livestock or his income from this livestock; 2. Instead of being progressive as heretofore, the tax is to be proportional with fixed rates per 0.01 of hectare independently of what is being cultivated on this land. In the Russian SFSR this rate is to be 3-14 rubles, in the Ukranian SSR the eastern territories 5-12 rubles, in the western territories 2-6 rubles in the Estonian and Latvian SbKs By Professor A. Aizsilnieks 4 rubles, in the Lithuranian SSR 3 rubles, etc., depending on the quality of the land. This implies that henceforth the kolkhoz peasant may increase his livestock to the limit imposed by the kolkhoz statutes without risking to pay any increased I tax for it. The only thing he would still have to do is execute the compulsory deliveries to the ttads i:om this livestock. In reporting to the Supreme Soviet on the new tax Minister ior Finance Zverev said: "Such agricultural taxation will promote the increase of livestock privately owned by the kolkhoz peasants.\'\' Considering that at present there are many kolkhoz peasants who own no livestock the new tax law provides for a reduction of the tax for such peasants- by 50 per cent in 1953 and by 30 per cent, in 1954 "in order that they might provide themselves with cows during that time." "The government have :!so resolved to cancel ail arrears of the farm tax which have accumulated in the past years." As evident, the government have decided to make use of the pi n ate initiative ol the kolkhoz neasants in order to increase the livestock and provide the country with meat and dairy products. The reason for this is plain: the kolkhozes cannot cope with this task, at least not as i)uickly as necessary. This step is a great concession to private initiative and an abrupt deviation from "the road to Communism." The question is. what forced the Soviet rulers to make it? As they will hardly reveal their reasons we can only make guesses. There is no doubt that the great shortage of meat and dairy products is causing considerable dissatisfaction among the population. Owing to heavy taxation the kolkhoz peasants have been able to produce and sell less and less on the kolkhoz markets during recent years. Lately the administrations of these markets have been contracting the kolkhozes directly as regards the sale of their produce on these markets as the Soviet trade network has proved incapable to satisfy the needs of the population. The food shortage and the ensuing discontent of the population must worry the Soviet government considerably as the German and satellite hunger riots might easily spread to the USSR itself which which would be a great danger considering the present discords on top government level. Secondly, the high taxes and bad harvests in many kolkhozes have reduced the kolkhoz peasants to starvation. In its turn this worries those members of their families who serve in the army or have been detailed to various essential works. It is significant that under the new farm\' tax law the peasant families whose members work in such industries as coal and ore mining, ihe timber industry, etc., are exempt from the tax and that the families of persons serving in the army also enjoy variout privileges. Finally, the Soviet government has never for a moment lost sight of the possibility of a new war. Experience during the last one has shown that the food produced privately by the peasants was a considerable source of supplies to the population, when the government supply system bro\':e down under the stress of war and was unable to provide even the food to which the rationing cards entitled the', 'Bankrupt Socialist Farming By Professor A. Aizsilnieks. For years and years the Soviets have extolled their "Socialist agriculture" as the ideal and most productive form of farming in existence and have even found believers. Quite recently, however, at a plenary session of the CC of the! CPSU on September 3-1, 1953, the deathblow was dealt to this legend by no lesser personage than the present Ist secretary of tne CPSU, N. S. Khrushchev. His report on \' Measures towards the further Development of Farmiag" and the resolution of the CC in connection with it showed, in the first place, that the socialist or collective system of farming is highly ineffective and wastelul and, secondly, that the centrally directed planning paralyzes it further as this planning not only does not remove the disproportions of agricultural production but even increases them. In his report, Khrushchev plainly stated that the productivity of Soviet farming is very low or, as he chooses to express himself, "grows very slowly." In connection with this there is "a manifest discrepancy between the rates of growth of large Socialist industries, the urban population" and its demand for consumer goods on the one hand and "the present level of agri- ļ cultural production" on the. other. As an illustration, Khrushchev mentions that "from 1913 to 1952 the gross output of ! the large industries of the USSR ; has increased in comparative prices 27 times, including the ; output of means of production ! which has increased 47 times." At the same time the urban population has increased three Limes between the years 1926 land 1952. However, the agri> cultural output has rissn so lit| tie since 1913 that Khrushchev , finds it awkward to mention it. j Instead, he admits that between 1940 and 1952 the agricultural j output had irsen by ten per ! cent, only while the industrial output had increased 2.3 times. This state of farming not only precludes the possibility of satisfying the demand of the I urban population but also "nandicaps the further development of the light and food industries." The reason for this discrepancy or disproportion, which for some reason has not been noticed ļ hitherto, is above all the unreal-1 istic Soviet planning. For years the Gosplan (Central Planning Commission) has been planning! an uninterrupted and consider- ļ able increase of agricultural output but the plans have not come true even with regard to grain,l a branch of farming in which the i Soviets consider themselves to ļ have achieved the greatest sue-1 cess. For a long time Gosplan has been rcckonii-£ with an increased crop capacity from year to year: actually its plans were not fulfilled on the greater part of the USSR territory. The resolution of the CC says: "Many ; kolkhozes and sovkhozes still 1 reap low harvests of cereal, oil, leguminous cultures and flax ļ fibre, especially in the raions of the non-black earth belt, the \' central black eerth oblasts, the , Volga area and Siberia." Two reasons are named as the main ones: (1) the unsatisfactory ; quality of cultivation and (2) ļ the insufficient quantity of fertilizer provided for by the plan ; (according to Khrushchev the present Soviet output of mineral fertilizers is 5.7 million tons, or about 30-40 kg. per hectare of arable land). The poor cultivation is blamed ; on the MTS (machine and trac-I tor stations) which have not had I properly trained specialists, SDare parts for the repair of I their machinery and whose other requirements have not been provided for by the plan. Cultivation has also suffered from the apathy of the kolkhoz j peasants, the inefficient management of the Kolkhozes and the shortage of manpower. The reļ solution of the CC says: "Owing 1 to the unseasononable and care| less attendance to cereal, oil (and technical cultures as well \' as losses during harvesting, a considerable proportion of the agricultural output is lost in many kolkhozes and sovkhozes." The same applies to potatoes and vegetables. The CC resolution states that "in many kolkhozes and sovkhozes the planting of potatoes is carried out very late, in unfertilized fields. Tardy and careless harvesting caus-s great losses.\'\' Large horned Whereof  Cattle Cows 1916: 58.4 28.8 1928 : 66.8 1941: 54.5 27.8 1953: 56.6 24.3 In comparing these figures it should be remembered that 1916 was a war year when part of the livestock was already requisitioned by the state for military purposes while 1928 marked the beginning of collectivization. The above data reveal that in the beginning of 1953 the Soviet Union still had considerably less livestock than before collectivization and even less large horned cattle while the number of cows was to be 24.7 million heads already by Jan 1, 19*8. (cf. IZVESTI A, Feb. 28, 1947). The increase of livestock has not proceeded according to plan; on the contrary, sometimes it has decreased catastrophically. The CC resolution of Sept. 7, 1953 says: "In 1952 alone the sum total of large horned cattle ir the country has been permitted Large horned cattle Whereof Cows millions per cent millions per cent Sum total, Jan. 1, 1953 56.6 100.0 24.3 100.0 Whereof owned by Kolkhozes 29.6 52.3 8.3 34.2 Sovkhozes 4.2 7.4 1.3 5.3 Others 22.8 40.3 14.7 60.5 The "others" mentioned in the above table as owners of large horned cattle and cows are the kolkhoz peasants and workers and employees who witherto were allowed to keep one cow per family. As appears from the above, the kholkhozes own little more than half of the cows. What they possess are mainly young animals who constitute three fourths of their livestock. It is plain that these young animals ! they do not obtain from their I own cows. At the order of the ; state they "buy" tkexn j peasants. The same thing happened in subsequent years and I the peasants were compelled to ! sell by means of intolerably heavy taxes, (cf. NEWSLETTER, |No. 340/342, August 7, 1953.). That the kolkhozes nevertheless have so few cows is caused by the fact that a vast number of cattle perishes every year on them. According to Khrushchev "in the last two years the kolkhozes of the Novgorod, Kostroma, Kurgansk oblasts and the Altai krai lost every third calf through murrain, in 1952 the kolkhozes of the Vologda and Ivanovo oblasts lost every second lamb, the kolkhozes of the Voronezh, Tambov, Rostov oblasts I every fifth and the Bielorussian j SSR every fourth young pig." Khrushchev also gave to understand that a not unsubstantial number of kolkhoz livestock is yearly pilfered by CP and government officials. Furthermore, the productivity of the kolkhoz cattle is exceeding low. Khrushchev informed us that "in 1952 on the kolkhozes of the Kirov oblast the yield of milk was 727 kg. per cow, of the Novgorod oblast 777, of the Vologda oblast 819, of the Kostroma oblast 906, of the Azerbeijan SSR 373, of the Geogian SSR 457, of the Kirghiz SSR 537, of the Armenian SSR 814, of the Bielorussian SSR 829 kg." As a Soviet paper once pointed out, this is the yield of a goat, not a cow. The main cause of this is the shortage of fodder. The plans have not provided for a j sufficient output of fodder for the sped-up increase of livei stock and even these inadequate plans have not been fulfilled. Moreover, part of the produced , fodder was pilfered and wasted, i Further reasons are indifferent t care for the animals, lack of ! suitable stables for the winter I and lack of interest for cattle ■ breeding among the kolkhoz • peasants. Even the management of the : kolkhozes, as appears from \' Khrushcchev\'s report, is extre; mely inefficient from an agro; nomic point of view. For the most part the kolkhozes are headed by CP members without . any appropriate training, i Of 94,000 kolkhozes only 16,600 , have chairmen with proper ag, ronomic training while in the , majority of kolkhozes the chair, men have only an elementary i education. Khrushchev also > says: "Almost every third chairi man has held his job for less than a year. There are many Moreover, in his report, Khrushchev informs us that "in recent years the gross yields of potatoes and vegetables not only do not increase but even fall off. The areas under vegetable cultures have decreased by 250,000 hectares as compared to 1940." However, in the most desperate or, as Khrushchev says, intolerable position is the Soviet cattle breeding. According to him there were the following millions of heads of livestock in the USSR at each Jan L: Sheep and Pigs Goats Horses 23.0 96.3 38.2 27.7 114.6 36.1 27.5 91.6 21.0 28.5 109.9 15.3 to decrease by 2.2 million heads and the number of cows by 550 thousand heads." Especially lamentable is the [ state of cattle breeding in the kolkhozes and sovkhozes. Khrushchev said about it in his re\\ port: "The outgo of 1.5 million ! cows in one year (1952) constitutes 18 per cent, of the large horned cattle in the kolkhozes. j Consequently the kolkhozes have 29.6 million heads of large hornied cattle. The sovkhozes, according to Khrushchev, have 1.3 million heads of large horned cattle. j The sovkhozes, according to Khrushchev, have 1.3 million cows which make up 31 per cent, of their large horned cattle. Hence they have 4.2 million heads of large horned cattle. This gives us the following picture of the state of Soviet cat; tie breeding: kolkhozes which have had two to three chairman in the course of one year. Many of them have been dismissed because they could not manage the work or admitted abuses. One might adduce plenty of examples of how the raion committees move from kolkhoz to kolkhoz, people who have proved failures and theives, forcing them the kholkhoz peasants." In order to squeeze whatever possible out of the kolkhozes desuite all their backwardness, the Soviet government has buruenfu them witn very high com* pulsory delivery quotas. Krushcchev gives some data on them, which are as follows: Marketable agricultural output in 1952-1953 was, meat 5 million tons, and milk, 13.2 million tons; while State requisitions in 1952 were, meat 3 million tons, and milk, ten million tons. For the compulsory deliveries the state has hitherto paid very low, not to say infinitesimal prices. According to Khrushchev these prices have now been raised by 30 per cent and for milk 1.5 times. Needless to say that both prices are still considerably lower than those obtaining on the kolkhoz market, which gives a certain idea of their level before the rise. The result of this system was that the kolkhoz peasants hardly got anything for their work, took no interest whatsoever in it and, as Krushchev said "took the first opportunity to move to town to work in some factory." In winding up, the first secretary of the Communist Party of the Soviet Union comes to the conclusion that about the most important reason for the backwardness of Soviet farming is the violation of "the principle of material personal interest" of the kolkhoz peasants. Krushchevs contention that "the principle of personal interest of an enterprise and every individual worker in the results of the expenditure of effort is one of the basic principles of Socialist economy" marks a great change in Communist ideology. When the Soviet system was being introduced in the USSR the Communist leaders emphasized time and again that the object of the system was to eradicate private material and personal interest. The Communist Party promised to show the world that an economic system based on the community interest is much more effective than the old "capitalist" system built on the selfish personal interest of the individual. A few short years ago Khrushchev himself was the most ardent advocate of the "agrotowns" calculated to wipe out the last vestiges of private farming in the USSR. Now the highest instance of the Soviet dictatorship have been compelled to capitulate and concede the ineffectiveness of their dogmatic tenets. Khrushchev\'s report admits eloquently enough that an economic system disregarding the personal interest of the individual is impossible even if, as in the Soviet Union, it is based on compulsion and protected by a police regime.', 'The Soviet price cut of April 1, 1953, has been widely popularized by the Western press as evidence of the "liberalization" of the Soviet regime and increased consideration for the needs of the population. llt has been taken largely on its face value as the Kremlin intended it to be taken. Actually the improvements this cut introduces are more seeming than real. The list of goods the prices of which Were cut is a long one, and, at first glance, makes the impression as if all articles sold in the retail trade had become cheaper. In reality a number of relatively expensive articles are not to be found on the list. For instance, the price of ready-made woollen clothes, both lor men and women remains unchanged, and artificial silk as well as clothes made from it likewise. The average monthly wage of an ordinary worker being S(X)-600 -rubles, he is bound to find a woollen suit costing 1000-1800 rubles rather expensive. Scythes and sickles, still widely used on the kolkhozes because it is unprofitable to hire agricultural machinery from the state, have become cheaper, but all kinds of fei filizers, the largest item in the rpoductional expenditure of the kolkhozes, remain as expensive as ever. The price cut decree lists a number of building materials that have become cheaper, but bricks, lime and timber are not among them, although they are the most important articles for kolkhoz building. The cut in the price of a number of articles has no real significance, e.g.. the prices of potatoes, cabbage, turnips and carrots have been cut by a whole 50 per cent. Unfortunately, the people do not profit from this. The Soviet press itself has mentioned a large number of important towns having only" 1-2 shops selling\' such goods in the centre and none in the suburbs, e.g., "Trud" (27/7/52) writes: "The population of Stalingrad is not properly supplied with vegetables. In the outskirts of the city, where the population consists of workers, there are no stores for vegetables, and no milk in the srCres." Or to quote "Pravda" (14/12/52). "The Briansk oblast is particularly rich in vegetables, fruit and berries. Nevertheless, in the stores and restaurants of this oblast it is difficult to get fresh vegetables, fruit, berries and mushrooms." In I the smaller towns, vegetables and j truit can be bought only on the ļ kolkhoz market, where prices are the USSR the low income classes grow most of their own vegetables on small plots rented from the state, working on them before and after working hours. In 1952, 18 million workers and employees, or almost half of all wage-earners, cultivated such small kitchen gardens ("Trud," 26/2/53». The prices of nails and cement have been cut by 25 per cent. However, at the end of last year the Soviet papers often wrote that kolkhozes had not been bale to build cowsheds for their cattle, or left them roofless because of the lack of the above articles. What is the use of a price cut if an article is unavailable? Such shortI ages are a daily ocurrence in many i places and apply to many goods besides nails and cement. As usual in other connections, the Soviet sources mention only percentages in connection with the price cut and never a single price The reason is that although the prices of some goods are cut every year, they remain exceedingly high as compared to wages. Ar example is given by "Izvestia" (3/4/53). In a report about tht Moscow department store, thf salesman in the department ol readymade clothes says to a client: "Take this dress, it suits you very well. . . Now it is 1(X rubles cheaper." This dress canont be either o! artificial silk or of wool or it< price would not be cut. Cottor dresses have become 14 per cent and silk dresses 12 per cent cheaper. Hence, if the dress is i cotton one it costs 615 rubles nov after the price cut and 733 ruble! if it is a silk one. At the same tim< the latest Soviet official data giv< to hand that the average monthl: wage in the USSR is 500 rubles. Many people wonder how th* i Soviets can cut their prices yea i after year while in the Westeri i world they rather soar than fall, i Actually, this is no miracle, bu • rather a propaganda trick. Th j Soviet prices include very higi \' turnover taxes which, in certaii ; years, and on certain goods, con : stituted 70 per cent, of the retai : prices and still constitutes mor • than half of the total revenues o I the state. Cutting prices, the gc : vernment cuts this tax on farm • i ing produce. In view of the fac 5 that the number of workers an : employees buying this produc 5 grows with every year, the tots • sum made by the government b : i means of this tax remains cor ; stant. The prices of industrif ■ goods (textiles, etc.), are cut b • means of pressing down the cost E of prduction. The costs of pit ;l duction, in their turn, are lowere "1 by means of a continuing increas The Price Cut of the quantity of owrk a worker must execute in order to earn a certain Wmmt. In other words. Ihc price cut is parallelled by a wage cut. This is the main reason why the USSIL never publishes any ligu res concerning wages and salaries. In the West, such a procedure would be impossible as the trade unions would not agree to any wage cuts. In the USSR it is one of their primary tasks to introduce such cuts. Hence, in the West wages rise alongside of prices while in the USSR prices tall as a result of a lowering of the wages I\'llE NEW PRK ES AMI WA(iES IN THE BALTIC STATES The Soviet maintain that the decree of March 31, 1953, cutting the prices of a large number o: goods by 5-50 per cent, has substantially raised the living standard of the population. "This decree is a shining example of the untiring care of the Soviet goveronment for the material and cultural needs of oqi workers," wrote the Soviet-Estonian paper "Rahva Haal" ot April 1, 1953, and all over the thre Baltic countries meetings o thanksgiving "lor the current raise of our living standard" were organised. Whether hte price cut raises the living standard or not may be seen from a comparison of prices and wages. Moreover, it must be remembered that even a number oi staple foods, such as milk, eggs, potatoes, vegetables, fruit, are not available in the state-owned shops but must be bought on the kelkhoz mai\'ket where prices are free, i.e., regulated by supply and demand. Likewise, persons who have left the Baltic states in January of this year maintain that readymade clothes and underwear, wool len : textiles, stockings, better quality shoes, nad a large number of household goods, are to be had in the shops only sporadically often with several months intervals. A factory worker\'s monthly wages in the Baltic states today range from 300-700 rubles, an ">()() rubles. At the same time the cheapest kind of black bread now costs 1.44 rubles the kg. (formerly 1.60 rubles), the cheapest wheaten bread 4.05 rubles (formerly 4.50), groats 3.10 rubles (formerly 3.45), cod, the cheapest fish 2.70 rubles (formerly 3.00). Potatoes and vegetables have to be bought on the kolkhoz market now as before where they cost: potatoes 1-2 rubles, cabbage 2 rubles, carrots 3 rubles, etc. All other foods is so expensive that the worker and clerk cannot afford them very often. The new price of castor sugar is 8.19 rubles (formerly 9.10) and of butter 27.90 rubles (formerly 31.00). Both articles appear in the shops i about once a month and only a restricted quantity (e.g. 200 groms I of butter) is sold to a customer at a time. Smoked ham costs 36.00 rubles (formerly 40.00), porl 17.00-32.20 rubles (formerly 20.00 38.00». beef 12.75-18.70 ruble (formerly 15.00-22.001. coffee 62.40 rubles (formerly 78.00). Milk costs 3.00-4.00 rubles on the kolk hoz market and eggs 1.50-2.00 rubles apiece. In a word, butter, meat, etc., i accessible only to persons wit high salaries and a reduction i their price will hardly affect th budget of the vast majority wit their low wages. The director of factory may enrich his bill of fare thanks to the new prices for hi monthly .takings are 3000-4500 rubles (a basic salary of 150( rubles plus various premia anc bonuses). They will, howeve bring no particular improvemen into the lives of a physicia (monthly income 600-1000 rubles or a teacher (monthly income 500 rubles in the country and 600 i the towns). The cut in the prices of tex tile goods, shoes and haberdasher> will not raise the living standarc of the low wage earners eithe for these articles remain just a much out of their reach as the used ot be. What they used t and will continue to buy is th cheapest cutton for 12.41 ruble the meter (formerly 14.60). Bet ter cotton costs 18.70 ruble woollen matreial 62.90-119.00 rubles (formerly 74.00-140.00 women\'s shoes 180.00-324.00 ruble (formerly 200.00-360.00). It hardly likely that a typist wit her salary of 450 rubles per mont I or a female bricklayer earning th ■ same wage, or a hairdresser <3C [ rubles monthly) will be able t • afford silk or kapron (Sovie nylon) stockings at 40 rubles th I pair (formerly 50). A femal lieutenant in the militia with i salary of 1000 rubles may buy sue • stockings occasionally, but for th I vast majority there are the chea the pair and the cheapest underwear at 60.45 riibles (formerly «5.00». The wife of a major in the aviation (monthly salary 6000 rubles) or some other high functionary, may now save 28 rubles on a pair of good underwear which used to cost 200 rubles and now costs 172. wAorker — even if he is a Stakhanovito earning 800-900 rubles monthly, or a foreman making 1000-1200 rubles — cannot buy a suit for his whole monthly wage. The price of readymade woollen clothes has not been cut, and they are very rarely found in the shops. He would have to order the suit and the material costs 213.75-380 rubles per meter (formerly 225-400). the best quality suiting being inferior to that used tor cheap reach-medown clothes in the West. The cheapest cotton socks cost 9.60-11.20 rubles the pair and shoes 180-360 rubles the pair. For everyday wear the workers wrap their feet in rags and stick them into heelless rubber boots which are very hard on the feet. Al lthe beter goods mentioned above — if they should be available—as well as hats (100 rubiest, gloves (50-75 rubiest, handbags (140-200 rubles), are only for the well-paid Soviet elite. For the lew kopeks they save per kilogram of bread or fish the broad masses must give — and keep promises to incerase their productivity and produce things over and above the plan as a token of gratitude to Party and government. hTe reports ol Baltic papers and radio substantiate our contentions. On April 1 the Riga radio said that trade had been lively as a consequence of the price cut. An old man told hte radio reporter in a department store that he had now been able to buy a kerchief each for his wife and daughter. On April 2 it was reported from Tallinn that more cheese, sausage, fish and butter had been sold than before the price cut. On April 4 the Latvian papers said that the demnad for groats, sausage, wheaten bread and sugar had increased with the new prices. Our idea is that none of these articles is a luxury — the first thing people buy whenever they really have money to spend. The population of any country should be able to buy them — price cut or no price cut — before there can be any talk of a decent living standard.']

Representative_Docs: ['The long-range policy OF THE SOVIET POLITICAL BUREAU Thin article is by a well-known Baltic educationalist and deals with a high official of the Bolshevik occupation authorities. It reads like a strange story. Still, it is a relation of facts, based on short-hand notes, and conveys an idea of the objectives and methods of the Bolshevik occupation. An attempt has been made to retain in the English translation the language used by the chief person -in this  * The Editor. A Soviet book on the postwar economic planning of the Soviet Union stated (in 1946) the following: "As long as capitalism exists,the basic reasons for the rise of wars are also pre sent .Under ccndiHons of imperialism, any war, even if small,"threatens to grow into a world war". Whatever evil things they are doing or planning to do, the Communists impute to others.This is also true of imperialism in 1946. The Political Bureau realised in 1946 that the Red imperialism will eventually meet with resistance and that any war, even if small, may grow into a third world war.ln order that the "last battle* end with a total victory of communism in the world, "Russia was and is today the only country in the whole world with a heavy industry larger than the light industry".The violent expansion of the heavy industry is, not meant to ensure national since it is perfectly clear that no country in**the rJorld plans to make war on Soviet Russia and sine? \'t -\'.s equally clear that the Soviet Union wishes to engage in the fmal battle which in the communist belief will lead to— the establishment of communist throughout the world. The results of tne Soviet are now being felt by the United States m Korea . A local small-scale war is being waged but may develop into a third world war if IRe Kremlin imperialists deem a universal war opportune. Leading statesmen in the western countries long refused to admit the existence of a Red imperialism. The war in Korea has opened their eyes. A dictator is like a man on a bicycle. He must pedal and move forwards in order not to fall to the ground. It is, therefore, a delusion to assume that there will be no large-scale war. It is approaching and cannot be avoided. Some idea of the detailed scheme and careful praparation of the Soviet long-range plans may be gained from the following story which deals with Comrade Siabitis, a high Communist official inSoviet -occupied Latvia, and his "philosophy". It will be noted that the story dates from 1941 when Soviet Russia still was Nazi Germany\'s ally and an instigator of World War 11. Should some people object,stating that the views of one Communist are not representative of the official Soviet policy, I need only referto the French author, Andre Gide, who spent considerable time and effort on studying Communism and who said: "Every morning it is decided by PRAVDA what tothink, know and believe. And it is not good to deviatefrom it. So every time you speak to a Russian, you feel on are speaking to all of them". Moreover, Comrade Shubitis was not just one of the 180 million inhabitants of Soviet Russia.He was more than that. WHO WAS COMRADE SHUBITIS? Biographical data on Comrade Shubitis are scant. His general education was limited to two years of elementary schooling. Somewhat too early hehad adopted as his guiding principle Lenin\'s slogan: "Rob the spoils!" In school, he had started confiscating the belongings of his comrades and teachers.Thishappened in 1910 and the teachers failed to understand that this represented communistic ideological activities and qualified it as theft. Shubitis was expelledfrom the school and sent to a reformatory. Its discipline did not appeal to him. He escaped and made his way to Russia. During the Russian Revolution he proved that he had well grasped themeaning cflenin s saying: "Many a villain may be of great use to the Party just because he is a villain". The Communist Revolution gave Shubitis his big chance. The Party justly appraised Shubitis\'talents and he was made Political Commissar (politruk) in the RedArmy.After the end of the Civil War in Russia,he served m the Secret Political Police (NKVD) and won recognition for Efficiency. He also graduated from the Red Propaganda University in Moscow. In 1940, when the Soviet troops occupied Latvia, Shubitis was sent to the normal school for teachers in Jelgava (Mitau) as a political instructor. I met him there and heard from him the few bits of biographical information given above. Don\'t judge a man by his appearance! He came to Jelgava in a shabby and dirty suit and looked like the ugly duckling. The new clothes he bought in Latvia did not fit him at allies silly talks – at that time \'both students and teachers believed his talks to be idiotic – made him a stock of derision. His language was coarse, somewhat incoherent, but had a quality of srylLstic originality – it was extremely direct and pithy. WHAT DID COMRADE SHUBITiS TEACH* AND HOW? Comrade Shubitis extolled work and the socialistic competition for the greater glory of the Soviet fatherland. I have preserved a few of his speeches in short-hand notes. Excerpts are given below.They may give some food for thought. "See, everybody works in the Soviet Union, young people and old people,men, women and children. We is Communists and therefore we works, we works in socialistic competition. We caßs this stakhanowism and you does not know it. You will also have to work. You must take pattern on Soviet fatherland,not England or America. Comrades, Stalin is our father, friend and our teacher. He wants everybody to work. We must do what father and teacher says. If not, I will report,l iis also with the NKVD and you knows it does not like fooling. Raise hand who wants to work with enthusiasm so that I know who is saboteur, who is renegade". Naturally, all students and teachers raised their hands, since none of them wished to become involved with the NKVD. This added new zest to Shubitis speechifying. He went on: "Good, good chat waircs to work You is good comrades. See, I tells you, if we all works voluntarily in the Soviet Union,everything will be fine. There (in the Soviet Union Transl.) even cowherds looks after cattle 8 hours a day,makes money and buys what they wants. Buys a car and drives it,or else if they prefers,buys vobla and eats it". I had not known before the meaning of the word "vobla". During the Soviet occupation I learned that a vobla is a fish. Large quantities of vobla are being caught in the Caspian Sea. Dried in the sun, it is used for food. A dry vobla has a repugnant smell. People eat it when they have nothing else to eat. It to reason that a cowherd makes enough money to buy a vobla every day since it costs only a few kopeks. What we did not understand was how a cowherd could buy a car since there was no private property in Russia and nobody could own a car,eien assuming that a cowherd could actually buy one for the money he earned. Comrade Shubitis taught us the following about the Communist moral code: "See, we communists has a moral code of our own. It is not the blackfrocks\'code in which old silly dames believes. The father of our code is old Jews with long bearus. Lenin taught the students: in the Communist moral code you sees what serves communist victory in the world; if serves,then is moral, if not, down with it. I will give an example from my own experience so that it will be easier for you to understand. You knows, comrades,doctors are necessary, but we communists need – communist doctors. Better no doctor at all than a doctor who is not communist. See, during the Civil War I had a pal, Lebedev, real communist. We both fought against the Whites. Comrade was wounded in foot. Doctor necessary to treat foot. It was winter. During two days I transported comrade on a sledge. Found a doctor in village but, imagine, comrades,doctor is bourgeois. I says: "You shall treat foot of my comrade?" Doctor takes a look and says: "Foot can\'t be treated, too late, foot must be cut off". "Comrades, can there be such thing as a communist with one leg? He cannot fight and kill bourgeois. I took out my Pistol, thrust it under doctor\'s nose and shouted: You will treat foot or else l"shootyju\' Now* imagine, the scOLmdrel was not frightened, said he was right, can t be done, leg must be amputated. Doctor was bourgeois and wanted a communist to be with one leg. And you knows comrades what 1 did? I killed the doctor ,yes.killedndmn If doctor is bourgeois, better not be at all.He harms communism,it is moral to kill him, get it, comrades?" A student asked what happened with Comrade Lebedev and his foot. Shubitis replied:"Whataquestionl* Comrade Lebedev died. There was no communist doctor who could treat foot. But a communist doctor canwork miracle?. See, another example from the Civil War .A shell tore off back of Comrade Vorobiov\'s skull.Comrade communist, doctor communist. Doctor takes and cuts off back of skull of a killed soldier,puts it on Comrade Vorobiov, so that brains should not fall out, and comrade still lives with anotherman\'s skull". Comrade Shubitis explained the functioning of the people\'s courts of justice as follows: "See, comrades, only in Soviet Union there is culture, there are Red Corners in every kolkhoze and there are people\'s courts. Dr. A.', 'Even very recently there have been discussions in the Press about the real purpose of the Soviet Unions\'s policy. In the majority of cases they used to lead to the conclusion that the Bolsheviks at the Kremlin did not want war. These conclusions are in no way founded on fact, but on theoretical assumptionr&e Soviet State is not yet ready for war; in the last war it suffered terrible losses, and its main object now is to restore its national life, etc. There is also another aspect, namely; the power of the Soviet Union is considered an indisputable fact that must be accepted ipso facto and which makes it necessary te find a modus vivendi and of delimiting the respective spheres of influence (Walter Lippman). The latter view is in directcontradiction to all that the United States of America has beenpromising the world in the name of Christian morals and human rights, but the former is based on a lack of knowledge of the real nature of the Soviet State and of Soviet activities in the course of recent years. The activities of the Kremlin prove something very different. Here we no longer have to deal with assumptions, but with actual events, well known to the world,but unfortunately very often disregarded. Let us go over them again. 1. It was the Red Army of the Soviet State,then concentrated in White Russia and the Ukraine which on 18 September,l939 broke into Poland, already defeated by the Germans. The direct purpose of this inroad was to wage an imperialistic war with a view to making conquests. Very soon after there followed an agreement between Nazi-Germany and Bolshevist Russia as to thcestabldghemont of a new boundary line between both of these huge aggressive States, which resulted in the division of Poland, a State of 30 million inhabitants. DOES MOSCOW WANT WAR? 2. Two months later the very same Soviet State, breaking existing treaties, attacked Finland, at the same time compelling the three Baltic States to suffer the establishment of Soviet military bases in Baltic territory. The Russo-Finnish war in the winter of 1939 showed the whole world how humiliatingly unprepared was the Soviet State even for a war against a people of 5 million and what incredible sacrifices it demanded from the Soviet Union; it was forced to carry out an important economic reorganization all over its vast territories, including severe restriction in railway traffic. Turing this war the Soviet State attempted to conceal this humiliatingful situation from its people by issuing propaganda pamphlets representing the Finnish people as ten Times more numerous and talking of a clash with a people of thirty million backed by imperialistic forces of the capitalist world. Thus, beginning its military operations with an inroad into Poland,the Soviet State was completely unprepared,having only weak support from its poorly organized socialistic state economy.lt was as late as during the Finnish war that important reforms were carried out in the top ranks of the Rod Army. Of course, the 160 millions of Stalin\'s Empire were able to force little Finland to capitulation in spite of the resistance of the Finns, which had called forth the admiration of all the world. But the proportion was 1:55! During the war the speaker of the Finnish radio expressed his disgust in the following terms: "We are not soldiers in this war, we are butchers"; the proportion of casualitics was 1:30 in favour of the Finns. The Russians broke the Mannerheim line only after the inhuman order was given that no lives were to be spared, whereupon the well fortified Finnish positions were rammed by setting in a tremendous mass of Soviet troops. 3. Forcing Finland into capitulation, the Soviet State continued its aggression.ln the middle of June,l94o,it broke into the Baltic territories,openly occupying the Three Baltic States: Estonia, Latvia and Lithuania, giving as its reason that these three small peaceful and nations consisting in all of 6 million inhabitants had joined a military union in order to attack the huge Empire of 160 millions!! Poland, Finland, the Baltic States and Bessa.abia – it was a thoroughly prepared plan of aggression,the realization of which Soviet Russia intended to bring about in the same way as in 1917 in Russia, i. c., by taking advantage of the exhaustion of other nations during the second world war and their desire to make an end to it, this time in order to accomplish the bolshevization of the world. Then also, just as today, much was being said about capitalist intervention (later on it was called "capitalist encirclement"),about instigators of war against the newly established "socialist home-country", about hostile imperialistic blocks, etc. And the poor giant of 160 millions was said to have no alternative in saving itself from 6 million Baltic аМЗтДlлоп Finnish people but to defend itself desperately,"unfortunately" being compelled to occupy these States! Thus, military unpreparedness and poor economic condit – ions are no obstacles to the Soviet State for starting a wan On the other hand, there is always a reason for war when world conditions seem propitious and when they can be rendered still more propitious by furthering civil wars wherever possible and by provoking permanent disturbances within democratic countries. So, at a recent meeting of the Ccminform it was again decided to continue. At the present moment the Soviet hope of achieving a universal communist subversion is founded on the tremendous mistakes made in the course of World War 11. This subversion would extend Communist power beyond all limits. Once again this aggressiveness is being applied under the disguise of slogans of peace: "Much as the war-mongers try to discredit our socialist country, they will not succeed in banishing from the mind of the common people the conviction that the Soviet Union is the sincere fighter for peace, and consistently protects this peace for the whole world;that our country, as Comrade Stalin says, is able to maintain a policy of peace, not Pharisaically, but honestly, openly,firmly and consistently, for international co-operation, and for friendship among all nations. The peace policy of the Soviet State is in agreement with the fundamental interests of our people and of all mankind". (Pravda, no. 54, 1950: Leader "Armed Forces of  Soviet Homeland"). The above mentioned events from recent history give the clearest evidence as to how this "struggle for peace in the whole world" looks in the light of truth: in all these cases the aggressive ends of the Soviet State were pursued by means of an open military invasion. The political aims of this State arc often presented to the world under the title of "international co-operation" and of "friendship among nations". Stalin himself did so on 5 different occasions which shows how important and necessary for Soviet politics this tactical move seems to be. 1. In September 1946 Stalin said to Alexander Werth, the "Sunday Times" Correspondent: "I doubt if the possibility of peaceful co-operation could diminish, on the contrary it could increase". , 2. In December 1946 he gave the same reply to Roosevelt, the Presidents son, who asked for Stalin sopmion on a possible peaceful co-existence of the United States and the Soviet Union: "Yes, of course. This is not only possible, but quite a reasonable and attainable thing". 3. And furthermore: in April 1947 in a conversation with Senator Stassen, in May 1948 in the well-known reply to Wallos the same thing was said, and in January 1949 in answer to Mr. Smith, Director of the International News Service, Stalin even expressed his willingness to discuss with U.S.A. a common Declaration (the Russian Communists arc very fond of discussions!) by which both Parties agreed to abstain from waging war on each other, to conclude a Pact of Peace, followed by gradual disarmament. (It should bo noted that Stalin calls "armament" not what he is doing by means of slave labor, but the measures Europe takes with a view to defending itself). And, referring to these five statements of Stalin, Malenkov repeats the same. As Stalin has been reiterating such statements almost every year since September, 1946, it is only natural to ask why when the democratic world led by the U.S.A. was without any doubt prepared to do everything towards securing peace and to establishing national rights did there follow no definite action in this connection on the part of the Soviet Union? Why did not this Power after all take any steps at the termination of the Second World War to enforce "international co-operation on a reciprocal basis according to all engagements assumed" as mentioned by if the Soviet Union really wished to act according to Stalin s Declarations?" The answer is: as these**statements have been systematically repeated since 1946, and not the slightest thing has happened towards making them come true, it is more than obvious that they are meant to serve other purposes, that they arc only the expression of certain tactics to produce an effect necessary for the Kremlin. When in 1946 Stalin for the first time declared ihe Soviet Uninonls readiness to go on living side by side with the capitalistic system, it had an amazing response in the world at large! – London: "Almost the only reassuring statement in foreign polities since the end of the war!" – Paris:"The statement was received with joy, and is considered as the turning point in the development of international relations!" This was the very effect Stalin was aiming at: a deceived world transported with new hope, but blind in regard to actial conditions created by the Soviet State, and at the same time weakened and neutralized in its capacity to resist communis! But – there followed the blockade of Berlin, the complete bolshcvization of Eastern Germany and all those events which are weH кпояп  with Staling declarations there has always \'been great communist action in one part of the world or other, as f.ex., in 1946, when Stalin assured A.Werth of a possible peaceful co-existence and of the fulfilment of international engagements, the Soviet Union started military action against Persia, terrorizing Turkey at the same time, surely a premeditated attempt to force the Near East to submission. Just as now Malenkovls similar statement has not been followed by its realization, but by the realization of declaration, which gives a perfect example of how Moscow understands this "living side by side"! Namely: the Korean people are being urged to liberatethemselves from foreign imperialists – in a peaceful way, i. е., without an armed clash. In ether words, the Koreans are being incited to rise against the American occupation power and to drive the Americans out of the country. In the meantime Moscow has already made its preparations for turning Korea, like the Baltic States, like Czechoslovakia and Eastern Germany,into an ordinary Soviet Republic. At this moment similar events are taking place in Germany. The so-called National Council of the National Front Movement has been established with the appeal that "the Anglo-Americans wished to make a colony outofGermany and that therefore Western Germany should be liberated by all means from foreign dependency and from the clutchof the Atlantic Pact". This statement is accompanied by typical attempts to bring the chauvinism of nazi-Germans to the point of ebullition (see "Pravda" of Febr. 4, 1950, p. 4). To divert the world\'s attention from these far – sighted actions ofthe Kremlin and to envelop mankind in a mist of new hopes for a compromise "between the great Powers", Stalin s statements are being repeated over and over again. Buta parallel action in the sense of Molotov\'s speech is carried outsimultaneously. Unfortunately and to the detriment of humanity these subterfuges of Moscow nearly always succeed, since there are enough people in the democracies spreading theseßussian statements as honest endeavours at furthering peace. To accomplish all this is the only purpose ofMoscows tactics. Never is there to be expected on the part of the Kremlin any other "international co-operation". All that is said for foreign use is only "tactics and strategy", and along withthis tho many millions of the Soviet Union are being prepared for the occupation of the free world. By means of its tactics Moscow has succeeded up to now in gradually extending the sphere of influence of the Communist system and in constantly pushing back the rule of democratic freedom. This will last untilthere is no way of retreat, because there will be no space anymore to retreat to. No treaties and no negotiations will evercnange these tactics of the Kremlin. . . Docs Moscow wish war? It wages war all the time,gainingone territory after the other. It has been preparing this war for 30 years. Whoever does not yet understand it, must be ready to become a Soviet "citizen" for the rest of his life, dedicating the future of his children to the camps of compulsory labour in the Soviet Union. А. Кrauer.', 'A NEW STAGE IN THE PERMANENT COMMUNIST WARFARB. The fundamental nature of Communism is unchanging: it is a permanent war against a world in which mankind wishes to live according to established principles of justice and freedom. No discords or failures of the Cominform or the various Communist parties outside the Iron Curtain ever change the essential character of Communism.There have been many internal quarrels, and, ad times, grave dissension within the Communist Party, from Trotzki to Zhdanov and other mysteriously disappeared favourites of a more recent date. But Communism has lost nothing of its essential nature. This is confirmed by the history of the Bolshevik State. 1. No sooner had Russian Bolshevism established its Power in Russia, no sooner had it proclaimed the slogan: peace without annexations and contributions, no sooner had it announced the leading principles of a reconstruction cf the Russian State, among which self-determination and the right of all peoples to decide upon their adherence to the Union of Soviet States or upon national independence were conspicuous, than in 1918, conscious of its newly acquired it made its first attack on Finland and on the recently proclaimed Baltic Republics. The reasons for this onset were openly discussed in the "Izvestia", the Soviet Government\'s official paper, of 25 December, 1918, where i.a. the following was said: "Estonia, Latvia and Lithuania are in Russia\'s way of communication with Western Europe, they are a hindrance to the Revolution besauso they separate Russia from revolutionary Germany. This wall has to be pulled down. The red Proletariat of Russia must carry revolution into Germany. The conquest of the Baltic Sea will make it possible to conduct propaganda for a social revolution in the Scandinavian countries. The Baltic Sea, therefore,has to be made the harbour of the Social Revolution". This assault on trn: Baltic countries was cloaely connected with bolshevik hopes of provoking an armed rising with the view to attaining power in Germany. Thus we sec that the aims which bolshevik aggression was pursuing in its earliest days remained the same later on when Moscow proclaimed the Eastern German State. 2. Just as today; by means of misleading tactical stratagems Moscow goes on extending its power in the direction of both Europe and Asia. While attacking the States round the Baltic Sea. her eyes were turned towards Asia, and her first tactical aim in this respect was to destroy the newly established independent Georgian Republic,which had not sufficient power of resistance and thus lost its independence in 1922. 3. On 30th December, 1922, at the first Congress of all Soviet Republics when Lenin moved a resolution with a view to establishing the Union of Socialist Soviet Republics, Stalin said in support: "By this resolution the existence of the separate Soviet Republics comes to an end. The Republics join in a Union, because the Soviet State, apart from thinking about its own existence, must try to become an important international power, able to influence the international situation and to change it for the benefit of the working classes all over the world". In other words: the Soviet Union should become a powerful State that is able to accomplish the ends of the universal social revolution. 4. In December 1924 an attempt of a Communist rising in Estonia is made. It is the last rehearsal of an aggression in Europe, planned for the future. Suspending its advance in the West, Bolshevism gives rise to a series of events in Asia: the attempt to create a Red China, the war with Japan, etc. Thereupon begins a new period of activity in the olshevik State: a time of internal concentration and reconstruction for the purpose of creating a mighty military Power which should be "able to exercise its influence upon historical events of universal importance". From an agricultural country Russia is being changed into an industrial Power capable cf producing war material unassisted and in sufficient quantities. To prepare the country for a smooth development in this direction, it was necessary to break in advance any possible resistance. The convulsions and the upheaval after the years of tension had to recede back to the natural middle linenormal, settled life. The time of strain was over for Bolshevism, Lenin was obliged to give way and introduce the NEP, the New Economic Policy, з compromise with yesterday. The basis of the natural middle line in Russian j.ife.vas the peasant-proprietor, the irreconcilable adversary of Bolshevism, called by the Bolsheviks "kulak" (the fist). Russian Bolshevism had reached the limit: it had either to suffer the normal evolution of the country, which would have meant a return via the NEP to the middle line,to the average citizen who claimed his right to live, or to force the country into absolute submission to the Bolshevik policy. The last alternative demanted first of all the extermination of any possible resistance. The Communist Party put forth the slogan about the "extermination of the kulak as a social class" which was carried out with an unequalled cruelty and involved an incredible amount of victims. It must be remcmbencd that the annihilation of the peasant-proprietor and his enforcement into collective farms, the kolkhos, was not performed for economic reasons,but under the necessity of creating political terror, in order to break the backbone of the average citizen with his claim to live. By exterminating millions of peasants-proprietors, the backbone of this average citizen was actually broken. Thereafter the Communist Party was free to "establish Socialism in one State" without fear of meeting with resistance, and bybuilding up heavy industry, to place itself in a position of being able to "influence historical oventsV In this respect the essence of Communism-Bolshevism remains invariably the same, and no evolutionary development is possible, in spite of all the bewildering changes and alterations of "tactics and strategy". The imperfect knowledge of the constant character of Bolshevik pursuits and its disregard by the leading Western Statesmen, when making decisions of historical import during World War 11, has led to mistakes which have resulted in conditions now prevailing in the world. We fear thatthese mistakes will be repeated over again ay the present time, since the Western Democracies were almost prepared toaccept the idea of a split world where, according to Russianstatements, two separate blocs could continue a peaceful existence side by side. Bolshevism means permanent war which is always waged in a combined way: as civil wars, or as extery nal wars with Russia\'s oam participation in them, or by provoking clashes in territories of other countries where at that particular moment sections are interested in Communist expansion. Now again at the beginning of .thesecond half of the 20th century Russian Bolshevism has made up its mind to start a new phase in its permanent warfare. The Reasons for this are still not dear, but they become and will be- – come clearer gradually, And there are facts confirming the new start of such and presenting Communism m a state of increased activity. Those who think that the 21st of December, 1949, was only the celebration of Stalin\'s 70th birthday wined be mistaken..A detailed inquiry into the course of these solemnities according to Moscow s own sources shows that -йю\' 21st December was first of all a mobilization of world Communism for a fresh start of aggression. The most authentic source is "Pravda", official organ of the Communist Party. Its Number of 21st December is an historical document ol eminent political importance, for it contains articles dedicated to Stalin by all of the present rulers of the Kremlin. In these articles only the expression of official flattery and the superlative epithets refer to Stalin, while the gist bears upon the political tasks of the Soviet State of today. It is important and interesting to notice first of all the sequence in which these articles have been placed. This detail gives to the Politicians and the Press of the Democratic world, among other things, an indication as to the possible successors to Stalin\'s throne. At any rate it reflects the existing relations of the mighty rulers of the Kremlin of today, which of course may change quickly? as was demonstrated by a recent example – the disappearance of Zhdanov, the principal favourite. And so, after the first article which is a collective message by both the Central committee of Ihe Party and the Cabinet of Ministers, follows Malenkov\'s article, then Molotov\'s, Beria\'s, Voroshilov\'s, Mikoyan\'s, Kaganovitch\'s, Bulganin s, Khrushtchev s, Shvernik\'s a.o. in the above order of succession. And the contents of these writings? All of them have one leitmotif: "Stalin\'s "classic works on Marxism-Leninism are the Encyclopaedia of revolutionary Marxism, from which the leaders of the – worlds working class draw the knowledge that is needed by them in their struggle for Communism". – "Stalin s Constitution has secured the victory of Socialism, has for the working people of all mankind become the attracting force, a beacon, the light of which flashes all over the world as a source of freedom and happiness". (Extracts from the message of the Central Committee of the Party and the Cabinet of Ministers). Endless appeals to the working people of all the world. But a complete program of the aggression of world Communism is given by Molotov: "The attention of all nations is directed towards the success of Socialism in the Soviet Union and towards the immense importance of this success as a factor of progress, since the growing crisis of the Capitalist system has become quite evident to since all new States desert it (?), and Capitalism has \'lost all its chances". "The extermination in Russia of all those who were against Leninism is of the utmost international importance, because it has disproved the  disbelief of the Social-democrats in the victory of Socialism, which disbelief all Communist parties have to fight in their countries". "The victory of Socialism in our country proves unmistakably that the so-called "ob\'iective conditions" for the introduction of Socialism had already ripened long since both in Europe and outside". "It is known that an extensive movement in defence of peace, democracy and progress considers the Soviet Union as its support and principal hope, and Stalin^s name as its mighty banner". "The world is now in such a position that in the event of a clash with the imperialists, events will take anolher course than hitherto, they will result in the liquidation of the system of Imperialism throughout the whole world". "The tactical and strategical principles worked out by Stalin are of great moment to the Communist movement in all countries, for it is the propelling force and the stimulus of all working people in the world and of all oppressed nations in their struggle". "Originally a national force, the October Revolution has become an international force which rouses the Western labourers and the oppressed Eastern Nations to a self-denying struggle. And it Is now evident what progress our country has made in this direction and how thoroughly international conditions have changed just in this respect". Molotov calls the situation an "historical mission of the Bolshevik Party, of the Soviet State, of us all". This is a definitely expressed program of the increased activity of world Communism. Moreover, the article occupies an exceptional place as second in order among ail the others. There is every reason to believe that there is some truth in the information about Molotov having been entrusted with the lead of Cominform activities, i.e.,the organization of communist disturbances in all countries and in all continents. A report in Issue N0.289 of the periodical "Czassovoy" edited by Russian emigrants in Brussels gives a good illustration of Molotov\'s Program. According to this report several very secret conferences were held at the Kremlin in 1949, in which not only members of the Politbureau,but also military and scientific experts took part. Soma of the highly important resolutions then adopted were: The conquest of China was to be accomplished at the latest in spring 1950; in 1950 the subversion of Iran; a civil war between India and Pakistan, and a clash provoked between Pakistan and Azerbeidzhan, on boundary questions;in 1951 Indo-China and other territories of Eastern Asia were to be occupied; in 1950 the construction of naval and aviation bases in the Far East were to be completed to enable their use for military operations against USA and Canada; towards the middle of 1950 11 special airborne landing armies of 500.000 men were to be set up; in 1950 the sovietization of Eastern Germany was to take place, to be followed by an intensification of the cold war and clashes, uproars and disorder everywhere. The Newspaper states to have obtained this news from "well informed sources". But just the same can be openly read in the "Pravda". In its Issue of 22 January an Appeal of the "Central Committee of the United Democratic Patriotic Front" to the Korean people is published, and there we read: "The common national task of the Korean people is to strengthen the fight for the re-union of the country in a peaceful way. For this purpose the obstacles which prevent the reunion must be cleared away. These obstacles are:the outrageous intervention of the Americans and the so-called United Nations Korean Committee which is nothing but the instrument of American aggression. We must sweep away these obstacles by our own means. Our power is great". And in the issue of 20 January,-a proclamation of the Malayan Liberation Army: "Chinese and Malayans join in fight. We shall firmly struggle for the Constitution of the Malayan Democratic Peoples Republic under the leadership of the Communist Party". Or, "unity and community of action", "siding with the Soviet Union"! demanded in the same Number of the"Pravda" (see Declaration of the Provisional National Council of the German Democratic Republic-).^ All this, compared with Molotov s Program, shows that it is one and the same consequent action subservient to one and the same purpose. The same ideas are brought forward in quite a number of articles since the Stalin celebrations. The leading article of 24 December "Stabilization of Peace between the Nations" reiterates that "it will never be possible to efface the conviction from the conscience of the common people that after all only the Soviet Union has been the defendor of peace". The issue of 25 December^is dedicated to the appeal towards new fights in Stalin s epoch "for the victory of Communism". The leader on 30December "The Invincible Soviet Union" repeats over again that the formation of all Republics into a Union was dictated by the necessity of making Russia a giant Power (dierzhava) and this has now proved to be the right it has now become "a beacon, distributing the light of Socialism, sending its rays to the working classes of all Capitalist countries, to all oppressed colonial and unfree peoples". Pointing repeatedly at and stressing the perfection and might of the Soviet Union, suggesting of the inevitable collapse of Capitalism, enumerating all the prerogatives of the Soviet State, asserting that the national problem has been settled there incomparably better than in any other country of mixed nationalities, that the Communist State is actually democratic, having adopted the institute of criticism and self-criticism; that there is no unemployment in Soviet countries and that there will be none; pouring forth interminable flatteries on the "popular masses"from which things should bo "learned",eulogizing the Russian people as the leading nationality throughout the Union, etc., the Communist Press besets the "destitute classes" throughout the world with the temptation also to contrive to establish such "an accomplished and happy regime" in their own countries, and certainly, by means of Lenin\'s and Stalin\'s tactics: by an armed rising. The fact that the communist "self-criticism" mentioned has nothing to do with democracy, but is only a whip with which to drive on and to hold the officials in fear and ebedience, is evident. In fact, such cases of criticism from below used to finish with the persecution of the critic and with the criticized person\'s revenge, since every official has always better connections than the critic from the "masses".Much has been written about this and on many occasions by the Soviet papers themselves. But it has never been heard of in connection with criticism applied to authorities higher than those placed in charge of a local district,not to speak of criticism of the Government itself or of the Central Administration of the Party. This self-criticism and criticism of others in State affairs is the same method as Stachanovism in factories and workshops. The mobilization of universal Communism for further activity was the gist and the real political intention of Stalin\'s Jubilee. This new activity has even got its паке: The Struggle for Peace. Just as in 1917 Lenin put forward his slogan "for peace and land to the peasant" to stir the bored and tired Russian people and became victorious in his October Revolution, so now do the Soviets profit from the state of mind of all nations which tremble most of all at the bare idea of a possible outbreak of another war. Demagogically speaking, there is no slogan that would sound better in the ears of mankind today. Malenkov himself has been given the task of confusing the "Capitalist world" and of distracting its attention from the new Communist activity by renewed statements of the possibility of a simultaneous and permanent existence of both Communist and Capitalist systems. Malenkov could devote himself to his pursuits with a feeling of confidence, since a many Europeans had been prepared to accept the conception imposed upon them by Moscow of the world being split into two camps and of the continuance of such a state of affairs. Now he repeats it again, supporting his opinion by statements to a similar effect made by Stalir himself five times before. But, as we shall remember from the occurrences, these Stalin statements were always followed not by the manifestation of his desire to live peacably side by side with other nations, but by some new intensified Communist action, such as forcing Czechoslovakia into submission to Moscow. Is it only a mere coincidence that now again, while Malenkov steps forward with his temptations, putting them before the "Capitalist world" as a tasty dish, agencies announce anxiety in Washington and London about... Finland? However, once more it appears that Stalin and Malenkov did not serve the dish in vain, since "World Affairs" Editor is already about tc "look for truth in Malenkov\'s article", and, failing to find sufficient proof, he searches for this "truth" in a speech held by Malenkov at a meeting of the Politbureau in November 1949! On that occasion Malenkov said that "his beloved country had had boundaries so just and strategically correct as then, and that there had never lived in the neighbourhood such peaceful and friendly people as at that time..." And this was why Malenkov was supposed to have expressed his genuine belief! It is of course true, Russia today lives exclusively among satelites conquered by herself, among "People\'s Republics" and her boundaries have been delimitated by only herself according to her will! In such a case one coned of course be satisfied with so profitable and accomplished "work"! But one thing remains incomprehensible; How could the Editor of a Periodical of a free country make of this situation a basis for his statement that: "if this is so, the world can really hope for a lasting peace..." For never, not after the most happily terminated war, had a military Power ever had such a profitable success as Stalin had after a war which had been prepared by methods that at this very moment are once again being applied to little Finland. In the same article Malenkov, dealing with another subject, reminds and stresses that Russia is a "Dictature of the Proletariat", but that this Dictature is controlled by one Party, the Communist Party, which does not share authority with any other party and which cannot do it". is the Communist Dogma. If such a dictatorial paruy uoes not share power with other parties inside its own territories, it is still more disinclined to give up this principle m the international arena where its attitude is watched by its adherents. For the principle of the Dictature of the Proletariat is equally true in international Communist warBut the idea of the two systems existing side by side is only a camouflage against the "Capitalistworld*, tactics and strategy" to secure victory of the dictature of the proletariat throughout the world. Mere "tactics and strategy" is also the action for peace, under the disguise of which the new activity in extending the power of the dictature of the proletariat is conducted. It is in connection with this tactical move, with the "action for the protection of democracy, peace and progress" that the communists baited the hook for all communists, communist followers and duped simpletons, living in the free world, by promulgating, during the Stalin Celebrations, the Ukaz of the Soviet Union\'s Supreme Council concerning the establishment of an International Stalin Peace Prize "for strengthening peace among the nations". "The prizes are awarded to citizens in any country inthe world for outstanding merits in fighting war mongers and in strengthening peace". The distinction will consist of 1. the Stalin Prize Laureate Diploma, 2. a gold badge with Stalin\'s effigy and 3. a pecuniary reward ofloo.ooo roubles. Five to 10 prizes yearly will be awarded on the 21st of December, Stalin\'s birthday. The first distribution will already take place in 1950. The purpose of the Prize is to break the morale of the ideologically weaker individuals and part of the educated classes in democratic countries. Moscow\'s suggestion that countries on this side the Iron Curtain should take part in sports competitions in the free countries must be interpreted as a kind ofmasked propaganda and, above all, as one of the manifold features of the new activity. The periodical – "Soviet Sports" underlines that Russia has no objection (!?) to participation in international contests. In no country are sportsmen selected for participation in international competitions submitted to such preparation and provided with such special instructions as is the case with Russian sportsmen. They are sent to represent only certain branches, they must be in the absolute top class of these branches, and they dare not lose. This is a still another peculiar situation where amateurs have to contest with professionals. No other country is able to demand from its sportsmen such a maximum of effort.That Russia is able to produce such prodigies in sport canbe understood, since out of 180 million Russians and of about 160 million Europeans in the conquered countries somewhat less than 10% belong to the ruling class,which makes a number of 25 to 30 million people who live invery good, even luxurious or, at least, endurable conditions, against 90% of the population who live m condir tions now known to the world. But the communists intention to turn sport to account is based on the calculation that the public on this side of the Iron Curtain would be inclined to identify the success of Russian sportsmen with Russia in general, thinking little or ignoring completely that these sportsmen were carefully selected from only a tenth part of the population. But if the majority of the public has the impression that these successes are all-Russian, the purpose is achieved: where there aresuch excellent sportsmen, there must be a good life! Even the sportsmen of the free countries and their leading men do not see this connection and rejoice that "Soviet Russia has opened her gates!" "World Affairs" looks forward to an unprecedented action for peace from Stalin in 1950. It must not be expected, it has already begun. But the purport of it is the following: a now universal Communist action against the world of freedom and justice, sot up under the cover of a struggle for peace, a cover spread over mankind to make it forget the danger of war, to make it cease thinking about it, and instead to dream of happiness; to let it forget that of all kinds of warfare the most sanguinary and terrible is civil the traditional Communist instrument for gaining power. Mankind should also forget that communists never apply civil war alone, but that alongside of it there are always sanguinary external wars. Since in the free world Stalin s statements of the possible co-existence of the two systems have almost become a reliable political barometer for pending events ofworld -wide importance, they will indeed be wise who consider occurrences in this light. А. Krauer']
