Representative_Docs: ['"WE OPPOSE, REJECT AND WILL REVIEW BALTIC DECISION" „There can be no justification for Australia to recognize dc jure the incorporation of the Baltie States into the Soviet Union. We oppose this decision and we reject it and when we come back to Government we vvill review it," the Leader of the Federal Opposition. Mr. B. M. Snedden said m Melbourne last week. Mr. Snedden vvas addressing a capacitv audience of more than 800 at the Latvian Hall. Elvvood. The meeting vvas organized by the Baltie Council of Australia, representing the Estonian, Latvian and Lithuanian communities the re. The meeting passed tvvo resolutions, one condemning the Federal Government for granting dc jure recognition to the Soviet Occupation of the Baltie States, and the other urging the Opposition to pursue the matter further m both Houses of Parliament. The follovving is the fuII text of Mr. Sneddens speech. ~Mr. Chairman, lādies and gentlemen, l\'m very happy to be nere vvith you today, bot l\'m sad to be here vvith you on an occasion such as this. Everybody has a great love for the country of their origin. I have constantly said and I vvill repeat it today that being an Australian has nothing to do vvith forgetting your links vvith your homeland. l\'m very glad to see lādies here m national costume. I hope that you vvill be able to keep this tradition going for as far into the future as the Scots have been able to vvear kilts – vvhich I do myself. There are many thousands of people m Australia today vvho vvere born m one of three Baltie Countries. It is not just the people born there, the people they married and their children, and it\'s a great tradition that you can be glad of and that you ought to carry on, — and that tradition is nota Russien tradition. (Applause.) Those people came here to this country to seek freedom for themselves, for their children and preserve the freedom that they came here vvith. Andas you vvill make a contribution to the people that you live vvith, so that the people you live vvith have got to make their contribution to your freedom. What we are concerned vvith today is the decision of Mr. VVhitlam to give recognition fn lavv to annexation of those three Baltie States by USSR; he has given recognition m lavv and that is vvhat vve are concerned vvith. Obviously, the fact is that by the force of arms and military strength, by police forces operating m their own way, the three countries are under the domination of the Soviet. That is a matter of fact that vve can\'t argue about, but vvhat vve are concerned vvith is that there should be given the right of lavv to an act of military domination and that vve don\'t agree vvith. It vvas a shameful decision and it became knovvn through the Russian authorities or someone m colusion vvith them revealing to them that this had oecured. This vvas our first knovvledge of it. Not only the first knovvledge of you or of mc or Australian people but the entire VVestern VVorld didn\'t know about it until it vvas revealed through a nevvspaper correspondent. VVhen that oecured I sent a telegram to the Prime Minister and to the Foreign Minister, raising 10 Specific questions to vvhich I re quested an ansvver. I recerved none. Later vvhen I asked again for an ansvver I received a reply m vvhich the Prime Minister said that it vvas all said by Senator VVillessee m the Senate. It took three vveeks to obtain that first — and only — response from the Prime Minister. It referred only to Senator VVillessee\'s quite inadequate statement m the Senate. At no time at all has there been any effort made by any spokesman of the Government, or by Mr. VVhitlam himself, to explain why he did it. Thev now have to acknovvledge that they did it but there has not been the slightest attempt to give any explanation as to why – and that vvas vvhat I vvanted. I made it clear immediately it became public knovvledge that the Opposition does not recognise this surreptitious decision to give dc jure recognition to the incorporation of the Baltie States into the USSR. It is novv quite clear that this decision vvas a decision by Mr. VVhitlam. I\'ve had this confirmed by the Foreign Minister, Senator VVillessee, vvho has at long last ansvvered my telegram. Indeed there vvas a knock on the door at 11.30 p. m. on Friday night (6th September. Ed.) ... I vvondered – but fortunately it vvas only a telegram man m uniform... (Laughter.) ILL-ADVISED, UNNECESSARY, UNJUSTIFIABLE We see no justification for the decision. It vvas ill-advised and it vvas unjustifiable and there has been no attempt to justify it. To this second there has been no attempt to justify it. We vvill probably be right m vvhat vve think vvas the cause of such an incredible action, but why cannot Mr. VVhitlam or the Government say – „we took this action for these reasons..."? What is vvrong vvith stating the reasons? — They are ashamed of them – that\\ why. That\'s why they vvon\'t say them. (Applause.) The decision svept aside m an insensitive an arrogant fashion the the deeply held feelings of the Baltie Community, but not only just the Baltie Community — the feelings of everybody m Australia vvho has an understanding of vvhat the consequences of such an act are. This actions consequenrps arp vvidespread. VVhat must the Polish people think, the Ukrainiarr. the Czechs, the Hungarians, the Jugoslavs, m fact, the vvhole of that area of Europe vvhich is under the dc facto domination of the military might of the USSR? (Applause.) It vvas an action taken m direct contradiction of Mr. VVhitlam\'s own undertakings given to you. There can be no clearer vvords; the clear vvords I remember vvere these: that the Labor Government m office vvill pursue the same policy as it\'s predecessor Government of the Liberal and Country Party. That undertaking vvas given, you are entitled to rely on it, and so far as I knovv, some of you, perhaps many of you felt that this Labor Government vvas perhaps trying to pull itself away from the socialist thinking, the authoritarian thinking. Some of you may have been misled into voting for them relying upon that undertaking, but novv the clarity of the unreliability of any vvord given by them is clear, (Applause, hear-hear\'s.) GOOD GRACES OF USSR It vvas Mr. VVhitlams purpose to ingratiate himself to the USSR m advance to his visit to that country m the near future. He vvanted to gain thegood gracesof that country. (Jeers.) It is a sad reflection on the chaos that is novv the Labor Government\'s foreign policy. (Aplause.) The mishandling of the plight of Evgeni Ermolenko is a similar example m that episode. The Labor Government advocated its responsibility as the protector of freedom of speech and the upholder of the right of lavv. They vvere recklessly uncaring about the right of that young man – about the freedom that he said he vvanted. They vvere uncaring about the liberty of the individual. The proper process of the lavv vvas blatantly denied, consituting the denial of a fundamental aspect of Austraiia\'s demoeratic process. The surreptitious dispach of Ermolenko from Australia m an RAAF aircraft to Singapore vvhile the issue vvas stili ADDRESS GIVEN BY FEDERAL OPPOSITION LEADER B. M. SNEDDEN ON BTH SEPTEMBER 1974 AT LATVIAN HALL ELVVOOD, MELBOURNE before the court, vvas indicative of the Labor Government\'s attitude to freedom of the individual. (Applause.r Similar attitudes can be seen m the Labor Government\'s assistance to the Provisional Revolutionary Government m South Vietnam. It planned to provide aid for libera tion rņovements m Africa. It said the aid vvould only be given for humanitarian purposes but any such aid vvill permit those organisations to give time and resources to purehasing and deploying equip-. ment for terrorism. This proposal is consistent vvith the Prime Minister\'s statement m December 1973 that the use of arms and the taking of life could be justifiable asa means of settling problems m South Africa. There are more examples of this trend; the ill-timed establishment FEDERAL OPPOSITION LEADER BILL SNEDDEN addressing capacity audience of 800 Latvians, Estonians and Lithuanians in the Latvian Hall, Elwood. bf diplomatic relations vvith North Vietnam and North Korea and the equivocal attitude of the Labor Government to the recognition of the Provisional Revolutionary Government m South Vietnam. Australia is part of the free vvorld and vve vvill stay part of the free vvorld. (Applause.) Not only vvill vve remam part of the free vvorld but vve are determined to remam free here m Australia. (Applause.) We Australians are one of the VVestern demoeracies, vve are the heirs to the oreat Dolitical philosophy of liberal parliamentary democracy. Australia is a member of the VVestern alliance and the Opposition parties vvill play a full part m that strūcture of this alliance for peace and security of the vvorld. (Applause.) The non-aligned vvorld is not an option open to Australia. (Applause.) VVe reject the non-aligned vvorld asa place vvhere vve can find the future of this country and this nation. The place for us m the VVestern vvorld. (Applause.) But vvith Labor\'s foreign policy, the way it is, it seems that novv everything can be contradicted if the mood suits you, and I can\'t feel c onfident, vvhile this Government is m the office, that vve vvill remam part of the VVestern vvorld. VVe vvill say that the non-aligned countries are not our natūrai place because the latter have not inherited as vve have, the great liberal philosophy of demoeratic Government. My party is the inheritor of the great tradition of liberalism; vve insist on the freedom of the individual, freedom of expression, freedom of association, freedom of religious belief; vve insist on the equality of all people for opportunity, equality before the lavv and equality before the people that make up society. But the individual must be above the State; the State is created to serve the purposes of people; the State is not created to enable a limited number of people to capture it and to impose the vvill of them, m the name of the State,. upon the vvhole Australian people or any other people. (Applause.) Austrai ia\'s foreign policy must be based on diplomacy for peace. It should not condone international violence m any form. VVhile vve may see a dc facto military domination, it does not justify giving sanctity of lavv to that fact merely because it existed for a long time. There must be an end to the Labor party\'s „shoot from the hip" diplomacy. Their foreign policy is m disarray and confusion, and this is reflected m their decision to accord this dc jure recognition of the incorporation of Baltie States into the Soviet Union. „WE OPPOSE IT, VVE REJECT IT AND VVE VVILL REVIEVV IT" In existing circumstances, there can be no justification for Australia, as one of the VVestern demoeracies, taking such a decision. VVe oppose it and vve reject it, and vvhen vve come back to Government, vve vvill revievv it. (Prolongued applause.) Thank you. Thank you. For the sake of completeness, I vvant to read to you the questions I asked and the ansvvers I was given by Senator VVillessee last Fridav nlght, 6th September at 10.33. In a telegram, I asked these questions: „l refer to reports that the Australian Government has recognised Soviet jurisdiction over Estonia, Latvia and Lithuania. If these reports are accurate, I request ansvvers to the follovving questions: 1) „When vvas this decision made?" — This, incidentally vvas asked in a telegram dispatched by me on 4th August. Just listen to this ansvver, received on 6th September (Laughs.): Ansvver to gueation: „The decision to extend de jure recognition of incorporation of former Baltie States into USSR vvas taken bn 3rd July." 2) ,,By vvhom vvas it made?" Ansvver: „The decision vvas made by the Prime Minister as acting Minister for Foreign Affairs." (Loud boos.) 3) „Why vvas this decision considered neece iry at that time?" Ansvver: „There vvas no special stgnificance to the timing of the decision." (Laughs.) 4) „Has it been approved by Cabinet and Caucus?" Ansvver: ,,lt vvas not considered neccessary tc efer the matter to Cabinet and Ciucus." 5) „When vvas the Australian Ambassador in Moscovv informed?" Ansvver: „The Australian Ambassador in Moscovv, Sir James Plimsoll, vvas informed of the decision on 16th July." — The decision vvas taken on 3rd of July, the Ambassador vvas told on 16th July, but we did not knovv about it until 4th August! „IMMEDIATELY CLEAR" 6) „When vvere the Soviet authorities informed?" Ansvver: „They vvere never formally informed,  the James visited Estonia from 28th to 30th July. Preparing for the visit, Sir James approached Soviet officials in Moscovv and" — listen to this — „the signifieance of thevisit vvas immediately clear to these officials." (Laughter.) 7) „Why vvere the Australian people and the National Parliament not informed of this change in foreign policy?" — Novv this is the greatest example of double talk I have ever encountered in m y life: Ansvver: „The course of action follovved vvas the same as that adopted by the Liberal-Country party Government in 1964 vvhen it recognised India\'s incorporation of Goa. (Laughs.) In reply to Press inquiries about Reuters report from Moscovv concerning Sir James visit to Estonia, a spokesman from my department confirmed the Governments decision on 4th August." I\'m not avvare that such a decision vvas confirmed on 4th August. It seemed an avvful long time and a lot of questions in the Senate before we got an ansvver as to vve ther that vvas a fact. — 8) Are people of Estonian, Latvian and Lithuanian nationality residing in Austraiia novv regarded as stateless by the Government?" Ansvver: „Most of former nationals of Latvia, Lithuania and Estonia novv residing in Australia are novv Australian citizens and therefore not stateless." (Laughter.) 9) ,,When vvas the Latvian Consul in Melbourne informed of this decision?" Ansvver: „The former honorary Latvian Consul in Melbourne vvas informed of the decision on 15th August." (Jeers.) — Novv just Iet us remember: the 3rd July the decision vvas made; 16th July the Australian Ambassador in Moscovv vvas informed. Then Senator VVillessee says that he confirmed a Reuters report on 4th August, — but the Latvian Consul in Melbourne vvas informed of the decision on 15th August! Daily Hansard Thursday, 28 February 1985 • Senator Peter B a v m c (N.S.VV.): By leave – 1 amend notice of motion No 2 standing m my name, and move: That the Senate – (a) havingregard: (i) to the desire of all people to live m peace and freedom and to enjoy basie human rights. ineluding the right to self-determination, (ii) to Australia a membership of the United Nations and regard for the principles of its Charter, the Universal Ueclaratiori of Human Rights. the United Nations Declaration on the Granting of Independence of Colonial Countries and Peoples adopted by its General Assembly on 14 December 1960 by Resolution 1514 (XV) and the Final Act of the Helsinki Agreement, and (iii) to the fact that, vvhilst the Australian people have acquired these rights and treedoms, other peoples are deprived of them, and (b) takingnote: (i) of he Resolution of the European Parliament on the Situation m the Baltie States (Estonia, Latvia and Lithuania) adopted on 13 January 1983, (ii) of the fact that the 3 Baltie States vvere independent sovereign republics ana members of the League of Nations during the years 1918 to 1940, vvhen thev vvere oecupied by the Soviet Union m consequence of an agreement betvveen the then German Government under Hitler and the Soviet Government under Stalin (the socalled Molotov-Ribbentrop Pact), (iii) of the fact that the oecupation of these states by the Soviet Union stili continues, but that m aecord vvith other (Jernocratic govemments the Australian Government has not recognized d c j v re their incorporation into the Soviet Union, (iv) that the peoples of Estonia, Latvia and Lithuania are being denied many basie human rights (as indicated m the unariirnous Report of the Parliamentary Joint Committee on Foreign Affairs and Defence entitled (Human Rights m the Soviet Union», nresented m November 1979), out that they have not given up their struggle for self-determination and freedom, and (v) that an intensive process of Russification is taking place m these oecupied countries airned at elimination of the indigenous languaJ2S and culture, ves: (i) that Australia, asa member of the United Nations, should fulfill its obligations to promote and encourage respect for human rights and rundamental freedoms, and should support the Euronean Parliament's Resolution m respect of the Baltie States, (ii) that m order to do so the Australian Government should, on its ovvn initiative, and by seeking the support of like-minded govemments bring the auestion of human rights and for the Baltie States before all appropriate forums of the United Nations, and especially before the United Nations Human Rights Commission, and (iii) that the I>partment of Foreign Affairs should be directed to take all necessaņ/ steps m order to implement this Resolution. I sought leave to amend this motion m a minor way, following discussions with the Government, the Australian democrats and Senator Harradine. The effect of the amendment is to shorten paragraph (c) (ii) and to take out certain words from the original notice of motion which related to a United Nations committee which has already met to which reference is therefor inapproproate. The motion vvhich I have moved and vvhich I hope the Senate will adopt concerns the Baltie States of Estonia, Latvia and Lithuania. small countries m north-eastern Europe, situated between the Soviet Union and the Baltie Sea. These nations were briefly independent but have now been forcibh/ incorporated into the Soviet Union. Senator L c w i s : They had an 800-year history of independence. Senator Peter B a v m c : I will talk about their history of independence, It was brief but glorious 20 years of independence. We seek to have Australia support the resolution of the European Parliament m relation to the Baltie states, a resolution vvhich puts on record m no uncertain terms the view of that body. We seek to have Australia do all it can vvithin the United Nations to promore the cause of the Baltie nations. We ask the Department of Foreign Affairs be directed to take all appropriate steps to implement any resolution vvhich the Senate may enact. That direction would come from the Government of the day, but the Senate seeks to ask that that direction be given. These three Baltie nations have had a long history of oecupation. Prior to the tvventieth century they were conquered and ruled by many foreign groups. During the nineteenth century they were ruled by Czarist Russia. Independence vvas aehieved when the Russian and German empires collapsed during the final stages of World War 1. Despite the Hp service paid before the Russian Revolution to the ideāls of nationaD self-determination, the new Soviet Russian regime lost no time m trying to re-conquer the fledging Baltie republics. Hovvever, the Soviet Union vvas repulsed and m 1920 peace treaties vvere signed. Those treaties put on record that the independence of those states vvould be maintained 4forever'. Yet despite treaties promising the independence of the Baltie States 'forever', the imperiālists of the Soviet Union and the nazi regime together m 1940 signed an infamous document, the von Ribbentropp-Molotov Pact, vvhich carved up north-eastern Europe and allocated the Baltie states to the Soviet sphere of influences, Relying on the seeurity of these theaties m 1940, the Soviet Union oecupied the Baltie States by foree. It oecupied the states of Estonia, Latvia and Lithuania, Fraudulent eleetions vvere held vvith the installation of Soviet puppet regimes. This vvas follovved by the formai incorporation of each of the states into the Soviet Union against the vvill of the people and subsequently vvith great suffering and loss of liberty. Since that time the Baltie people have suffered massive invasions of their human rights. They have experienced mass deportations, mass imp ri sonment. e\\ecutions and arbitrary terror. In fact, betvveen 1940 and 1953 good estimates are that the Baltie states suffered a population drop of 26 per cent. It is interesting to vvork our hovv that figurē came about. Was it asa result of direct vasualties of vvar? Was it because all the people fled to the West? Let us examine the figurēs. Three per cent of that figurē of 26 per cent related to casualties directly related to war; 5 per cent vvere the vietims of nazi persecution – mainly Jevvs – from the Baltie states. Four per cent vvere refugees vvho fled to the VVest, but the majority of the population lost, 14 per cent, vvere the vietims of Soviet execution. Soviet impri sonment and Soviet deportation. In moving this motion I express the concern of my Nevv South VVales Senate colleagues, Senators Sir John Carrick, Lajovic and Puplick, about vvhat is happening m these three countries. The invasions of human rights and the elimate of fear and terror vvich has operated m the Baltie states rank vvith some of the vvorst that nave bēeh experienced m human historv. Some senators joined mc last night m seeing a moving film abour one genocide that occurred a generation ago when six miilion Jevvs lost their Uves m Europe. The people who are concerned about that genocide might also concern themselves about vvhat has happened since then m the countries of Estonia, Latvia and Lithuania. The identities of these Baltie states have been submergcd to the interests of the conquering Soviet regime, vvith the elimination of all the Baltie national, political, militarv, cultural and economic institutions. This is not meant to be a merely historical account as these things are continuing today. I vvant to set out for the Senate some of the deprivations and atrocitics vvhich are going on today and vvhich have brought sucn horror to the people m the Baltie states. The Baltie States as subjugated countries have specialprolcms... There is an active program of Russification going on. The language and culture of the Baltie States is being suppressed. Representative_Docs: ["NEVAR VIENOTIES Tautas vietnieku nams 30 minūtes debatē par divām atšķirīgam rezolūcijām Baltijas valstu jautājumā, nevienu no tām nepieņemot Kā AL 1817. n-rā jau ziņots, Austrālijas parlamenta tautas vietnieku nams 20. februārī pusstundu ve/tija debatēm par divām atšķirīgam rezolūcijām, ko Baltijas valstu jautājumā bija iesnieguši strādnieku partijas dep. Klagmans un liberālu partijas dep. Radoks. Debates beidzās ar liberālu dep. Konolija aicinājumu nobalsot, ko valdības vairākums noraidīja. Tas nozīmē, ka debates rezolūciju jautājumā atliktas līdz brīdim, kad abas partijas varēs vienoties par kopēju rezolūcijas tekstu. No latviešu viedokļa, būtu reģistrējami divi komentāri: pirmkārt, jāpriecājas par katru iespēju, kad Austrālijas publiskajos forumos iespējams pieminēt Baltijas valstis un to likteni. Otrkārt, ir jānožēlo, ka nespēja vienoties par kopēju tekstu valdības un opozīcijas starpā rada iespaidu, ka Baltijas valstu jautājumā Austrālijā pastāvētu kādas bāziskas domstarpības. Par to vajadzēja padomāt rezolūcijas iniciatoriem pirms rezolūcija nonāk parlamenta apspriešanā. Katrā ziņā: ja arī nekas liels nav iegūts, nekas liels nav arī zaudēts, un lietas atrodas apmēram tur pat. kur tās bija pirms 20. februāra debatēm. Sniedzam debašu tekstu pēc parlamenta protokola pieraksta. • Dr. KLUGMAN (Prospect) – I move: That this House-Noting that – (a) the 3 Baltie States Latvia, Lithuania and Estonia, were independent sovereign republics and members of the League of Nations from 1918 to 1940; (b) m 1940, asa consequence of the Molotov-Ribbentrop Pact betvveen the USSR and Nazi Germany, the 3 States vvere incorporated into the USSR by military force and m a manner vvhich violated elcmentary principles of freedom, democracy and justice; (c) like other demoeratic governments, Australia does not recognize d c j v re the incorporation of the Baltie States into the USSR; (d) under Soviet rule, vvell-documented violations of human rights have occurred and continue to occur m Latvja, Lithuania and Estonia – (1J acknovvledges the concern felt by members of the Australian community over the situation m Latvia, Lithuania and Estonia, particularly the repression of individuāls engaging m the non-violent expressk>n of their ethnic and cultural identity and religious eonvietions; (2) commends the Government for its actkon m briging this concern to the attention of the United Nations Commission on Human Rights at the Commission's meeting m March 1985 and invites the Government to continue to pay attention to this important issue; (3) calls upon the Government of the USSR to end acts of repression against the Baltie peoples and to abide by the committments vvhich it has entered into under the various international covenants on human rights to vvhich it is a signatory, and (4) requests the Speaker to bring this resolution to the attention of the Supreme Soviet of the USSR. The motion m many ways is selfexplanatory and I doubt vvhether there vvould be many members of this House – or any members of this House – vvho vvould take objection to any part of the motion. I vvish to amplify it signifieantly, and l think it is important for people on both sides of the House to express their support for it as they did, 1 think some tvvo years ago, to a similar motion m the Senate. Because an amendment vvill be moved – as I understand it, notice has been given by the honourable member for Dundas (Mr Ruddock) – I vvill refer to the amendment before I go further into the motion. I regret that the Opposition is playing politics rather than giving maximum time to publicising the plight of the Baltie people, and for that matter, other inhabitants of the Soviet Union. We have,of course, brought the issue before the United Nations Commission on Human Rights and that is not referred to m the amendment proposed to be moved by a member of the Opposition. We have a lot of people, and notin the first time, on the Opposition side always talking about doing things about the Soviet Union but then never taking any stand on it. I remember vvell vvhen the previous Prime Minister vvanted to sell his vvool and vvheat to the Soviet Union vvhilc at the same time getting all rhetorical and paying lip service to the lack of democracy and the sorts of things that vvere happening m the Soviet Union. The same sort of thing happened back m about 1974-75 vvhen an inquiry into human rights m the Soviet Union was set up for a Sub-Committee of the Joint Commitee of Foreign Affairs and Defence. The motion vvas moved m this House by a member of the Opposition, but I think any person, even supporters of the Liberal party who look at how that Sub-Committee went about its work vvould say that the then Senator John VVheeldon. Senator Sibraa, my close colleague the honourable member for Havvker (Mr Jacobi) and I were the backbone of that Committee and used to attend those Committeee meetings, listen to the evidence and do all the work. The Liberāls never turned up. They just talked big about the Soviet Union. The Committee, m fact, held its hearings under the then Fraser Government, so the Liberal and National parties could have appointed a majority of members to that Committee. In fact it had a majorify of Labor members. I emphasise that it not only had a majority of Labor members but a huge majority of Labor members amongst its active members. The important thing to remember is that we on this side of the House are reall y concerned about freedom, unlike those on the other side who promise the world and then do nothing. I regret, because I consuder myself fairly friendly with the honourable member for Dundas (Mr Ruddock), that he haschosen today to take the line not, I assume, to oppose our final resolution vvhen his amendment is defeated but to go to the extent of moving the amendment. There are only tvvo significant differences, as I see it, betvveen our motion and the proposed amendment. One is the failure, I suppose, of the Liberal Party and Senate resolution to refer to what we have already done – that is, raise the issue – to refer to the promise that we vvould do so again at the current sittings of the Human Rights Commission, and to refei to an intensive process of Russification taking place m these occupied countries aimed at the elimination of the indigenous languages and culture. I think that our reference to the repression of individuāls engaging m non-violent expression of their ethnic and cultural identity and religious conviction is really the same. We can argue about it, but I certainly would not go to the wali about it. It is important for mc, because I have been one of the people who have been active on this issue for many years, to emphasise my point of v icu – and it is not neccessarily the point of view of all members of the Government – with regard to the Baltie States and many of the other problems concerned with the Soviet Union. Many of the objections to the Soviet Union are fairly natinalistic objections by ex-residents, for example, of by current residents, of course, of many of the Soviet republics. That is not my main objection. My main objection – and I conseider it to be the main problem as far as the Soviet Union is concerned – is that' the Soviet Union is a dietatorship. If the Soviet Union were run according to its own constitution, vvhilst the residents of the Baltie republics, the Ukrainian republic or any of the other republics might stili wish to secede, firstly, they vvould be able to do so under their own constitution and, secondly, they might wish – and I emphasise 'might' – to remam part of a union of demoeratic republics. We know that the Soviet Union is currently run like Tsarist Russia. The only thing that has changed is a different jargon to justify imperialism. That is a great pity about what is happneiing m the Soviet Union. I note the prominent members of the Baltie Council m the gallery of the House. I do not know what their view is on this, but it is my view that the best chance of getting freedom for the people of the Baltie States and, if necessary, for their national aspirations, is to change the Soviet Union from a dietatorship to a demoeratic country. I am not suggesting for one minūte that that is easy7but to my mmd it vvould be easier to do that than so somehovv persuade the current regime m the Soviet Union to give freedom to all the republics that currently make up the so-callcd Union of Soviet Socialist Republics. I wish to emphasise – I come back to the Government's policy as distinct from my own views – that obvioush/ the Government, unlike a previous Labor government which made a shameful decision never endorsed by either the parliamentary Party or the Party outseide, vvould not recognise dc jure the incorporation of the Baltie States into the Soviet Union. That was clearly and unanimously stated by the Federal parliamentary Labor Party. It is a matter vvhich I know is of great importance to c\\-residents of the Baltie States who are cuirently living m Australia. The Primc Minister (Mr Hawke) asked mc to convey a message on the occasion of the 67th anniversary of the Declaration of Independence of the Republic of Latvia m November last yeai. Inter alia, he said: «In my message of greetings last year I undertook that the Government vvould raise before international bodies its concern at violations of human rights vvhich continue, under Soviet ru? c, m Latvia, Lithuania and Estonia. The Australian representative did so before the United Nations Commission on Human Rights m March this year m Geneva. The Government vvill continue to pay attention to this important issue.» I have been assured that, m fact, it vvill be raised again by our delegātes at the Human Rights Commission, I think, m March this year. We have expressed those concerns, and I think it important that they keep on being expressed. We are also obviously concerned at the number of prominent Baltie dissidents vvho are m gaol or othervvise very unpleasantly dealt vvith by the Soviet Union. We have been extremely impressed by the action of Baltie organisations to maik the tenth anniversary of the Helsinki Accords. The Baltie peace and freedom eruise m July of last yeax and the tribūna! held m Copenhagen vvere vvell attended and received vvidespread and very favourable publicity m international nevvs media. The Government, m its statement, also said that it supported the idea of bringing the concerns of the Baltie community before Parliament and that the Government's aim is to endorse these concerns and express its ovvn position as clearly as possible vvithout, hovvever, raising unrealistic expectations about vvhat might be aehieved, for example, at the United Nations. Most of us are avvare of vvhat the numbers are at the United Nations. It is difficult to do anything at the United Nations vvhich vvould signifieanth/ help the Baltie States. At the same ■time, it is my vievv that it ought to be raised continuously; it ought to be raised by our delegātes to Inter-Parliamentary Union conferences; it ought to be raised by our delegātes at other organisations. The main thing is to keep on pointing out to our people, to people all over the vvorld, that the Soviet Union is a dietatorship, that it is, I am afraid. an cxtremely nationalistic dietatorship and that that, m turn, creates nationalistic objections to that dietatorship. I commend the motion to the House. I vvould be happier if vve could have come to some agreement to make some mild amendments to the motion rather than have to vote on it, vvhich vvould somehovv imply that there is signifieant disagreement on the basie points, because I am sure that honourable members on both sides of the House feel very strongly about the basie points incorporated m our motion. Mr DEPUTY SPEAKER (Mr Leo McLeayO — Is the motion seconded? Mr CHARLES (Isaacs) – I second the motion, Mr. Deputy Speaker... It is around four-and-a-halfdecades since the three States m the Baltie area vvere overrun and vve have seen the Russification of those three States ever since, vvith great pressure on their peoples, particularly m regard to social and cultural positions and their religious beliefs. It is a credit to the three nations of Latvia, Lithuania and Estonia that they have kept their religious beliefs through great pressure and over the last four-and-a-half decades of oppression by the Soviet Union. The Havvke Government has made a number of high level representations to the Soviet Government on behalf of family reunion for residents of the Baltie States vvith relatives m Australia and vve vvill continue to give priority to this matter, vvhich vve knovv is of great importance to the Baltie community m Australia. I have great pleasure m seconding the motion and recommend it to everybody m the House. Of course, religious practices are restricted. As to compulsory military service, Balts m particular, as victims of Soviet aggression, have to undertake police action, as ir vvould be termed I guess by the Soviets, m Czechoslovakia and also m Afghanistan today, many losing their lives asa consequence. What are the grounds for supporting the amendment? The amendment, it needs to be understood. adopts the vvords precisely of the resolution carried unanimously, that is, vvith all parties' support, m the Senate on 28 February 1985. The proposition can be seen to be the coll ective vievv of the Parliament only if it is carried m identical terms m the Senate and the House of Representatives. It also needs to be understood that the Soviet Union and its supporters migiit vve 11 read silent messages into the changed vvording that has been used by the honourable member m its motion if vve do not carry the amendment. I am concerned abouf the solace that the Soviets might find m the significant omissions m the motion drafted by the Government and proposed by the honourable member for Prospect What are the changes? Firstly, the preamble is deleted. It needs to be understood, that the preamble uses vvords from paragraph 10.1 of thereport of the Joint Committee on Foreign Affairs and Defence to vvhich the honourable member for Prospect himself vvas a signatory. It abandons the linking of Australia to other international protests about the situation m the Baltie States. While the motion of the Government is used asa vehiele to commend it for raising this question before the United Nations Commission on Human Rights — and for this I too commend the Government – and it is a far cry from the Whitlam Government's recognition of the incorporation of the Baltie States, it nevertheless offers no continuine commit tment and direction to Australia's Government to continue to raise the question before the United Nations generally and the Commission on Human Rights. The Government's reason for supporting this vvatered dovvn resolution is hard to understand, given the assurance by the honourable member for Prospect and m the message of the Prime Minister (Mr Havvke) to the Latvian communrty that this matter vvould be one of continuing attention for the Government. Finally, I vvant to make this point: The final and most significant difference is the deletion of the notation of the intensive process of Russification taking place m the oecupied Baltie States aimed at eliminating their indigenous Inguage and culture. Given the Government's eommitment to multiculturalism m Australia, one can only assume that its deletion of comment on the process of Russification coupled vvith onry a vague reference to a concern about repression of ethnic identity and cultural identity, vvas effected to signal a lack of concern on the Government's part about the concept of Russification. It is notas if the honourable member for Prospect is unavvare of this issue. He vvas a party to the Foreign Affairs and Defence Committee report vvhich I cited earlier and vvhich at paragraph 4.30 concluded particularly – I vvill not read the vvhole quote – that to a large degree the impact on minority cultures vvas duc to the steady Russification of the non-Russian Soviet Socialist Republic. It is notas if the Government vvas unavvare of the concern of the Baltie communities at this vvatered dovvn proposal of the Government. The Baltie Council of Australia has had continous contact vvith the Government and has put its vievv to it. TheCouncil's vievv is that, vvhile matters covered by the motion of the honourable member for Prospect are commendable, the form of resolution adopted by the Senate is preferable for its more detailed preamble and its stronger eommitment to future activity by the Government I put it strongly as possible that the reasons I have advanced should cause all honourable members to support the amendment and to carry it today. Tajā simfoniskā orķestra un solistu atskaņojumā dzirdējām 5 latviešu komponistu darbus: Jāzepa VTtola Dramatisko uvertīru, op. 21, Tālivalža Ķēniņa Astoto simfoniju ( Sinfonia Conccrtata ) ērģelēm un orķestrim ar Anitu Rundāni kā ērzeļu solisti, Jāņa Kalniņa Koncertu klavierēm un kamerorķestrim ar solistu – pianistu Artūru Ozoliņu, Jāņa Mcdiņa Četras latviešu tautas dziesmas ( Dzeguze kūko, Rikšu bēri es palaidu, Aiz ezera augsti kalni, Tauticšami roku devu ) ar solisti Ileanu Pētcrsoni, un Jāņa Mediņa Trīs latvju dejas. Gribētos šo koncertu apzīmēt par spožāko pērli visu līdzšinējo svētku koncertu garajā virknē. Tā sarīkošana ļoti piemērotas telpās, iespējami teicamā izpildījumā, bija Šo svētku kalngals. Littler) rakstīja: "Ar tautas deju uzvedumu Maple Leaf Gardens arēnā un prasīgu simfonisko koncertu Toy Thompson Mali zālē 8. latviešu dziesmu svētki Kanādā beidzās uz augsta snieguma pakāpes. Un ja jūs brīnāties, kā tik maza zeme kā Latvija var radīt tik daudz drāmas, dzejas, deju un mūzikas, ko dzirdēja aizvadītās 6 dienās, nacionālais lepnums dos jums atbildi. Nekas tā neliek māksliniekiem pulcēties ap karogu, kā drauds kādas kultūras iznīcībai, kāds piemeklējis tik bieži iekaroto Baltijas valsti tās vēsturē, pat ja karogs plīvo tikai trimdā. Abi, J. Kalniņš un T. Ķēniņš, latviešu komponisti trimdā, Kanādā ieceļoja 40 gadu beigās un 50 gadu sākumā, un mūsu mūzikas laukā tiem ir nozīmīga vieta..." "Kalnins has always been the more idontifiably Latvian of the two men, by virtue of his stronger allegiance to his traditional musical roots, including the spirit of folk song that expresses itself in the new concerto\'s slow movement. Kenins, with his neoclassical French training, has been much more cosmopolitan and in recent years especially, much more contemporary in his musical language. There is nothing contemporay at all in the language of the Kalnins Piano Concerto, a lighthearted virtuoso romp, honoring the tradition of decorative piano writing embodied in the concertos of Saint-Saens and Prokofiev. It nonetheless proved an ideal vehicle for Arthur Ozolins, the Latvian-parented Toronto pianist, notonly because of the ease with which Ozolins\' technique coped with the runs and ornamental flourishes of the opening movement but because of his sensitivity to the unabashed lyricism embodied in the lovely folk song melody of the succeeding "Lento espressivo". As for the rhythmically jaunty finale, I couldn\'t make my mind up whether it reminded me more of the spirit of Prokofiev\'s Peter and The Wolf or the background for an animated cartoon. Now in his 80s, Kalnins appears to be grinning his way toward the grave. On the evidence of the Eighth Symphony, Kenins is still frowning. This is a serious work, three movements and 25 minutes in length, with subtitle ( "Sinfonia Concertante" ) alluding to the way an organ is drawn into dialogue with the orchestra. In actuality, and despite the strong presence of as strong a player as Anita Rundans at the console, the organ supplied more spice than meat or carrots to the stew. The percussion battery generated – as it has in much recent Kenins – more of the score\'s personality..." Pārējiem darbiem kritiķis pieskaras it kâ garāmejot un apzīmē tos par ultratradicionâlu mūziku. Viņš piemin solisti Ileânu Pētersoni ka simpātisku mecosoprānu. Globe un Mail mūzikas kritiķis J. Kraglunds, galvenokārt, apskata J. Kalniņa un T. Ķēniņa darbu pirmatskaņojumus. "...First of these works was Tālivaldis Kenins\' Symphony No. 8 ( Sinfonia Concertante ) for Organ and Orchestra, commissioned with assistance from the Ontario Arts Coucil. Kenins has had numerous new works premiered in this city, but he is setting something of a record this year with premiers in four other centres, including Paris, Bucharest and New York. His Eighth Symphony is eminently listenablc. The organ, played by Rundans, seemed more an orchestral member than a virtuoso solo instrument – a part played more effectively by the timpani. The outer movements roused excitement with an urgency in the dominating rhythmic patterns. Lyricism got full play in the middle movement, memorable for the gradual unfolding of the long opening crescendo. But much of the work\'s success could be attributed to the controlled use of dynamic and tonal contrasts. Janis Kalnins\'s Concerto for Piano and Chamber Orchestra, with Ozolins as soloist, is a brilliant composition of a lighter  than the Kenins piece and also more romantic and less modern in its style. Ozolins played it brilliantly, setting the style and mood, as the piano was given long solo introductions and cadenzas. He did not manage to avoid the sentimental tendencies of the slow movement, but otherwise inspired orchestra and conductor to precise, often dramatic heights. Peterson, who took part in last Wednesday\'s concert at the Toronto Convention Centre, was featured in four of Jan Medins\' Latvian folk songs. The most expressive of these were Sister\'s Lament for the Homeland and the equally plaintive There Are Hills beyond the Lake. They best revealed the emotional aspect of the singer\'s art, but Peterson seemed equally at home in the robust rhythms of the other two songs. As Prime Minister, I would have gone if there could have been some substantive moves m improving relations and I had wanted to aehieve a trading arrangement with the Soviet Union vvhich vvould have resulted m rehable trade betvveen Australia and the Soviet Union. They are knovvn novv m our marķēts as «opportunity bidders» – thev might be here today and then be , out for two years, and that\'s disruptive of the market. And vve had officials vvorking to try to see if it were possible to chieve a trade arrangement. Nothing came of it. Umm... maybe it all ended when the Ambassador vvent to see the Head of Department of Foreign Affairs m a slighdy aggressive fashion, saymg «There\'s an invitation for Fraser to go to Moscovv. He needs to be there m two vveeks.» LATVIAN NEVVS :When was that? FRASER: In May, oh, it must have been 1977 – early. And he vvas told that no agreement had yet been reached betvveen officals. The Prime Minister had indicated that he vvould go if there can be a substantive agreement that indieates oositive progress, but not just for a visrt ?°i?you likf, not just for a tounst vmt. And he said that his masters had told him that the time vvas m tvvo vveeks. LATVIAN NEVVS:That vvasn\'t terribly diplomatic. FRASER: It vvasn\'t diplomatic. ..and I didn\'t go, and I think the officials m the Department of Foreign Affairs realised that that approach vvas offensive, and vvasn\'t paying any attention to the fact that all Heads of Government are busy, vvhether they are great, large nations, or little-ranking countries like Australia. LATVIAN NEVVS: VVhile vve\'re on the subject, vvhat do you think of the present Foreign Minister\'s trip? FRASER: I don\'t mmd him going to the Soviet Union, but the factor is vvhat he says vvhen he gets there. I vvas heavily criticised for a speech I made m 1976 about the Soviet Union on foreign policy but I believe that as time has passed, it has become more and more evident that vvhat vvas then said has been proved right. Even the present Government is saying that tiiey are concerned about the arms build-up m the Pacific and m the Indian Ocean – and that\'s a change from the Labor Party of earlier days. And I suppose a change that I vvelcome because I think it\'s realistic. They should be concerned – about the naval povver and buildup of facilities m Vietnam at Cam Ran Bay and Da Nang, for example. LATVIAN NEVVS: So you vvould not criticise the present Government\'s attitude tovvards the Soviet Union? Could one deseribe it asa moderate one? FRASER: VVell, yes, I think that\'s right. I vvouldn\'t have lifted the restrictions asa result of the invasion of Afghanistan. LATVIAN NEVVS: VVhich they have done. FRASER: VVhich they have done. It\'s inevitable that they vvere going to. But I\'ve got to say that I\'m delighted that the Prime Minister especially, and more so than Mr Hayden, has emphasised the importance of the American relationship, the importance of the American relationship, the importance of the ANŽUS alliance, has emphasised the neēd for collective defence and indicated a concem at the Soviet build-up m, if you like, our hemisphere. You vvouldn\'t have found a VVhitlam Labor Government shovving those concerns. Even since I\'ve left ParSament, I\'ve made it plain that I vvasn\'t going to be party to partisan politics anymore – that\'s m the Parliament, and I\'m glad that there has been some continuity of policy m important strategie matters m the present goveenment and my government PARALLELS BETVVEEN TVVO BOYCOTTS LATVIAN NEVVS: One is tempted to dravv parallels betvveen the tvvo 01ympic boycotts and betvveen the situations m 1980 and 1984. Has the vvorld become a more dangerous place since 1980? FRASER: Yes, it\'s more dangerous novv – much. The boycott by the Soviet of the Games m America vvas inevitable. You knovv, even if it\'s tit for tat But inevitable for a number of reasons – partly the general relationship betvveen the United States and the Soviet Union, partiy because of vvhat happened m 1980, and all of that, for examnle. emphasises that the Otympic Games ought to go back to 01ympia m Greece. They are so expensive that only the great, the povverful, the wealthy nations can mount them. Even a country like Australia vvould have to think three times, six times, ten times, before they undertook that responsibility. And therefore it is a matter of national competition betvveen large and povverful nations, and that\'s vvrong, for the 01ympic Games. You can only remove that v you remove the international element and get it back to competition betvveen sportsmen and sportsvvomen. LATVIAN NEVVS: So you see a solutkon m Ofympia? FRASER: In 1980, my governnent had said that vve vvould be prepared to contribute financially to establishmg a oermanent international centra for the Olvmpic Games. And I vvould hope that Mr Havvke\'s government vvould do the same thing. Because othervvise rt s a matter for international nvalry of the worst kind. You\'ve asked if I thought the vvorld waS a more dangerous place, and it is more dangerous, I think, for tvvo mam reasons. THE VVORLD – A MORE DANGEROUS PLACE President Reagan, very rightlv, began to rebuild America\'s strength. It had been run down. That m itself isgoing to create a period of danger because the Soviets \\vUl be askingtfor 10 to 15 years the Americans have run dovvn their strength, why are they now rebuilding it? And unless American objectives are spelled out very clearly, there vvould be a doubt m the Soviet mmd. I don\'t think those American objectives, vvhich is the survival of VVestern civilization, have been spelled out clearly enough. There have been statements from the United States vvhich almost talk m religious terms about the liberation of Eastern Europe and the sacred duty of America to see that all people lived m liberty and democracy vvith a government of their ovvn choice. Novv that\'s the vvorld vve vvould like to see, but to talk about achieving it and maybe even relating that to an arms buildup is to talk about mutual anniliilation, because vve all knovv m practical terms it\'s not possible. And maybe I could sum up tlie centrē of the danger by saying that I think that the VVestern policy is bound to be conducted m ideologicai terms rather than m practical terms. We have our ovvn ideology, vve believe m freedom, vve believe m democracy and people making their ovvn choice and deciding their ovvn government, but vve ought also to knovv that if vve seek to impose that on people vvho don\'t have that benefit, the vvhole vvorld vvill blovv up. SOVIET EXPANSIONISM OR DISENTEGRATION? Foreign policy cannot be conducted on the basis of ideology. It\'s got to be conducted betvveen the United States and the Soviet Union on the basis that they exist and vve exist, and hovv vve can Uve and let live, each recognising the legitimate interests of the other. The Soviets knovv very vvell that if they seek to push out and expand their interests, they vvill be opposed by all the strengths of the United States and the VVest. There has been an admission to that over the past 20 or 30 years, but there is all the difference m the vvorld betvveen opposing Soviet expansionism vvhether directly or indirectly, and talking as though the Soviet system can be upturned. One is legitimate and not inconsistent vvith our ovvn safety, and even necessary for our ovvn safety, and the other is fraught vvith the greatest danger to everybody. LATVIAN NEVVS: Don\'t you think that there is a third possibility – that of desintegration of their empire and the colonies m Eastern Europe? FRASER: I do think there is a possibility of disintegration, but the more they are attacked from vvithout the more they appear to be under threat from vvithout, the longer they vvill survive. The disintegration vvill come ultimateh/ from vvithin. LATVIAN NEVVS: That is vvhat I\'m getting at. FRASER: But if the Soviet leaders can point to the United States or, if you like, VVestern Europe, as posing a current and major danger, that vvill enable them to do things that enable the Soviet empire to survive, I don\'t think you can change the Soviet system from vvithout. I doubt if the tvvo or three most povverful people inside Russia could change it, except marginally. I think it is a question of time, patience, and meanvvhile the VVest needs the strength and the determination and the capacity to sustain a courese so vve can live alongside, if you like, a very dangerous country – I vvas faing to say, a very dangerous animal, ut I suppose I shouldn\'t. Co-existence is necessary ho vve ver much vve might dislike it if vve are to survive. Therefore co-existence is an absolute necessity. BALTIC QUESTION AND THE UNITED NATIONS LATVIAN NEVVS: You might remember that on one of your last intervievvs, vvhen m office, mention vvas made of the European Parliament resolution urging to take up the Baltie States question m the United Nations. Meanvvhile European Foreign Ministers have expressed the opinion, that they fear such step could be counter-produetive, and that it vvould not help one bit, let us say, to take the Baltie States\' question to the Special Committee of 24, or even to the Committee on Human Rights because, quite possibly, the numbers aren\'t there VVhat are your vievvs on the matter? Should vve aim to do something m one of the United Nation forae, or just let ir slip? FRASER: Oh, I don\'t think vve should let anything slip. I don\'t think you should expect too much or much at all from the United Nations. But at the same time you have a situation m vvhich the United Nations is avvare that this Xment exists, that there are people are concerned about the lack of human rights and the subjection of people vvho ought to be independent, vvithin the Soviet empire. I think that it is a good thing to continue this argument but not to expect too much vvithin the short term. I vvouldn\'t like to see the argument put aside and forgotten. LATVIAN NEVVS: Turning to local matters: do you foresee a change of government this year? In 1987? FRASER: VVell, since the last election I haven\'t spoken on partisan political issues, and I don\'t really want to start novv. LATVIAN NEVVS: Of course not. VVith the benefit of hindsight – and one vvould imagine that the question vvould have been asked a number of times – vvhat do you consider as your greatest personai achievement m the years m office, and, secondh/, vvere there any errors vvhich you novv see as such and vvhich you vvould not vvish to repeat? For example, the 1983 election date – vvas that an error? ACHIEVEMENTS FRASER: VVell, achievement – I think if you listen to the vvhole economic debatē today, it vvould not have been possible vvithout the economic arguments that vve vvere putting 7 years ago and vvhich vvere opposed by the Labor Party vvhen they vvere m opposition. Mr Havvke has accepted most of the economic arguments that vve put. Hovv successful he vvill be m pursuing those points of vievv, those policies, I m not too sure but that\'s another ques.tion. The economic debatē has been changed and, I hope, permanently changed for the better asa result of the debates that vvent on betvveen 1975 and 1983. And that, I think, is an achievement. In foreign policy, I believe, there vvas a greater element of realism and vvhat a country like Australia could do, a greater sense of independence, and therefore a greater sense of the importance of the relationship vvith the United States and the United Kingdom. In older days that relationship vvith those major povvers miglu have been the be-all and end-all of our foreign policy. That\'s certainh/ not so novv, and I believe getting that all into perspective vvas an important achievement of those years. In other terms I believe that the ideal of multiculturalism vvhich vvas given birth m my period of government vvas given very substantial expression by many actions of the government and I think therefore helped to build a more unified, more united Australia. I think the greatest compliment I vvas ever paid vvas vvhen an Australian vvho had been here for, I suppose, 20 odd years but stili spoke vvith an substantial foreign accent, said to mc that under my government for the first time he had ceased to look over, or feel the neēd to look over, his shoulder to see vvhat vvas happening. In other vvords, he felt part of Australia, accepted for vvhat he is asa person. I think m that sense this country has grovvn up a lot, and by and large vvithout too much passions, vvithout too many problems betvveen people of different races and origin, and I think it vvould be a tragedy if this vvas broken dovvn. THE VVISDOM OF HINDSIGHT There are obviously things vvhich you vvould do differently if you had the vvisdom of hindsight. I suppose, if you vvere calling an election vvhich you knevv you vvere going to lose you vvouldn\'t call it unless you absolutely had to, and it\'s interesting that m all the people vvho vvere advising mc at the time to have an election at that time, novv say «I vvish you had follovved my advice and had an election at the end of the year», there is not one adviser left to advise mc to have an election then, but at the time there vvasn\'t one vvho vvas advising to vvait until the end of the year, and that\'s the way political life goes. In the event, that vvas obviously a mistake, although vvith the knovvledge avaflable at the time I suspect that the judgement vvas right. The 1983 Budget has often been hailed asa mistake m budget, based on the desire to have an election, and I think that is one of the grossest distortions that ever existed, because vve knevv that Australia vvas going into a recession, there had been a massive drought, and there vvas the vvorld recession that vvas affecting us hard. And it vvas a question of vvhether Australia should or could do some thing to relieve some parts of that hardship or vvhether vve should say because revenue is dovvn vve are going to spend less, and that vvould have resulted m an even greater increase m unemplovment. I vvould be orenared to argue m Ed. Keisa domu graudi: Lai mājas viss butu kartība, pašam viss labi jāuzmana. VAI ATCERATIES VEL MALKOMU FRĒZERU? any economic forum m the vvorld that the basis of the 1983 Budget brought us through the recession and enabfed a recovery to begin m 1983, much much quicker than vvould have been the case if vve had the kind of budget that vvas pressed upon us by some of our advisors. I think that, as events turned out, it vvas a very vvell balanced budget and one ideally paced for the time. I suppose there vvas a failure to get that message across to the public, and maybe if I had had... I think Mr Havvke is a master salesman, and I sometimes think that he could sell absolutely nothing and make people think they had bought something vvorth a million dollars. Novv if I had had one half of that capacity, I suspect vve vvould have vvon the election m 1983. LATVIAN NEVVS: And vvhat of the present and future – immediate plāns and those for the more distant future? May vve see you returning to politics at some future datē? FRASER: I hope the circumstances vvill not occur m vvhich a return to politics vvould be practical or m vvhich people vvould vvant mc back. After the last election, the Liberal Party vvill vvant a nevv leader. They forget that vou\'ve vvon three elections and only remember that you\'ve only lost one, and I vvanted therefore to establish the circumstances m vvhich the Liberal Party could elect a nevv leader vvithout bitterness or difficulty. And the only way to do that vvas for mc to resign from leadership. Then I carried the argument further that if I stayed m the Parliament and if things vvent the way I believe they vvould, my presence m the Parliament vvas likely to become adivisive factor vvithin the Liberal Party. There vvould be those vvho vvould say I should be leader. There vvould be those vvho vvould say I should not, and the only real way to remove that item of debatē from the opposition benches vvas for mc to get right out of the Parliament. LATVIAN NEVVS: That\'s all m the past, of course, but as you have said yourself – a vveek is a long time... FRASER: VVell, a vveek is a long time but the circumstances m vvhich I vvould be vvanted back vvould be ones m vvhich the government vvould start to make a real mess of things, maybe because it started to conceed too much to the Left, and that of course is a possibility after maybe another election victory. On the other side of theequation- circumstances m vvhich the Liberal Party is conrinuing to do not vvell, and I don\'t vvant that to occur, so m other vvords I hope the circumstances don\'t arise m vvhich people might start to say: vve vvant Fraser back. It means: 1. the government has done badly; 2. the opposition is not seen asa viable alternative and 3. people start to look around for somebody else. LATVIAN NEVVS: For an Australian dc Gaulle, perhaps? Do you see that? FRASER: VVell, may be I\'m too close to it. LATVIAN NEVVS: You are stili m your prime years. FRASER: I\'m stili younger than the Prime Minister. I\'m not sure that he likes mc pointing that out. LATVIAN NEVVS: Thank you for your time, Mr Fraser.
