Name: 16_price_inflation_consumer
Representative_Docs: ['INFLATION HITS RECORD LOW IN JULY According to the State Statistics Committee, in comparison to June, in Jury consumer prices went up only by 0.3%. Thus, Latvia reports the lowest July inflation rate in the Battle States; Estonia recorded an inflation rate of 1.7%, while Lithuania reported a 2.7% hike. When comparing prices to December 1994, in the first 7 months of 1995, prices have risen by 15.2%, but compared to a year ago the rate of inflation has been 25.4%. The prices for food stuffs in total dropped 1.2% in Jury. From those areas with the most notable drops in food prices, while fish prices decreased by 1.8%, and milk and dairy products by 1 %, egg prices fell 42%, butter prices 8.4% but fresh vegetables topped the list with a 14% seasonal drop in prices. The consumer price index, however, was also negatively affected by water supply and sewage charges that went up 8% in July, while public catering or food services became 3.4% more expensive.', 'INFLATION IN ESTONIA AND LITHUANIA The Estonian State Statistics Office has announced that the consumer price index in January increased by 3.5% over December. Prices of goods rose by 2.9% (food by 3.8% and manufactured items by 1.6%), while the price of services increased by 4.2%. Meanwhile, the Lithuanian Statistics Department reported that inflation in January reached 5.7%, mainly owing to a 8.7% increase in food prices. Prices for tobacco and alcohol increased by 2.7%, for transportation and communication by 2.5%, and for housing expenses by 2.2%.', 'INFLATION IN BALTIC STATES According to the Lithuanian Statistics Department, inflation increased from 0.9% in December to 3% in January. Higher value-added tax on some food products contributed to a 5.2% increase in food prices. Several days earlier, the Latvian State Statistical Committee announced that consumer prices in January rose by 1.9%, compared with a 0.6% rise in December. Food prices increased by 1.7% and services went up by 2.8%. The Estonian State Statistics Department, meanwhile, reported that consumer prices increased by 1.4% in January, double the figure for December. Food prices rose by 1.3% and services by 1.6%. Twelve-month inflation rates in all three countries, however, have declined.']

Name: 17_population_000_census_birth
Representative_Docs: ["Latvia's population to lose 19,000; emigration, birth rate are top causes Latvians Online – Dec. 27, 2010 Latvia's population will have dropped by 19,000 individuals come the end of this year, which is a larger decline than experienced in 2009, according to the Central Statistical Bureau in Rīga. The forecast is based on analysis of provisional data from the Office of Citizenship and Migration Affairs (OCMA) and from civil registry offices that record births, deaths and marriages, the statistical bureau's Elmīra Senkāne announced Dec. 17. At the close of 2010, Latvia's population will be 2.229 million, the data suggest. Continued emigration from Latvia and a drop in the birth rate contributed to the population decline. The total number of emigrants this year could reach 10,000, OCMA data show. Already in the first 10 months of 2010 the number of emigrants—B,Boo—topped last year's total of 7,400. Meanwhile, immigration to Latvia has slowed. At the same time, the number of children born in Latvia is decreasing. From 2004-2008, according to Seņkāne, a slight increase in births was recorded. The number of births in 2008 reached 23,900, the highest number since 1994. However, last year the number dropped to 21,600. This year, the statistical bureau warned, births will not top 20,000. Also registering a continued decline is the number of marriages. While the drop this year compared to 2009 will not be large, from 2008 to 2009 the number of marriages in Latvia decreased by 3,000 to about 9,900, according to the data. Death appears to be the one constant, according to the data. The number of deaths in Latvia this year figures to be about 29,000, or about the same as in 2009.", 'Population Changes in Latvia in Consequence of World War 2 At various times in the course of history Latvia has suffered great deciminations in population as a result of devastating war action by neighboring great powers on Latvian soil. Subsequent periods of peace, however, enabled the Latvian nation to recover, to redeem the population losses and to grow in numbers. During the long period of peace, before the outbreak of World War I in 1914, the population of Latvia had reached its peak of 2,552,000. This figure, however, included also Russian officials of the czarist regime, Russian soldiers stationed on Latvian soil, visitors to Latvian health resorts and other temporary residents. The actual number of permanent inhabitants of Latvia was probably somewhat lower. When the German armed forces of World War I invaded Latvia in 1915, there occurred a great exodus ol refugees and expellees, particularly from south-eastern Kurzeme and Latvia\'s capital, Riga, which swept 7-800,000 people into the interior of Russia. After the establishment of the independent Republic of Latvia, a quarter of a million Latvian refugees returned to their native land. When repatriation was completed in 1925, the population of Latvia still amounted to not more than 1,850,000. According to Soviet statistics, in 1926 some 150,000 Latvians were counted in Russia. What happened to the remainder of refugees is unknown. In 1939 the population of Latvia had grown to a full 2 million. Of these 1,520,000 (76 per cent.) were Latvians; but the remaining 460,000 ( 24 per cent.) were composed of so-called national minorities—Jews (94,000), Germans (60,000), Russians ( 207,000), Poles (49,000) and others (70, 000). Both world wars inflicted great suffering and devastating losses on the Latvian nation. But there is a fundamental difference in the consequences: While Latvia regained its independence after the first war, as a result of the nation\'s ceaseless struggle for liberation, World War 11. brought. renewed bondage of Soviet occupation, which continues to the present day. Concerning the question of population losses through deportations, emigration, mass murder, war casualties, etc., we find accurate data only for the period ending with 1944-45. From there we must base our calculations on unverified reports from Latvian underground sources, prisoners of war repatriated from Russia, other occasional accounts given by eye witnesses who chanced to escape to the | West, and whatever facts we may derive from Soviet statistics and announcements, weighing and balancing them in the light of facts and reason. On occasion of the so-called "eletcions" in Soviet Latvia in December 1950, a total of 1,504,-650 voters was registered. This number must comprise practically the entire population over 18 years of age, since in Soviet countries everyone must "vote" for the single Kremlin-approved slate of the "people\'s representatives." According to Prof. P. Stares, the total number of minors under 18 years would amount to about 35 per cent, of the adult population. Thus we arrive at the conclusion that Latvia at p-esent is inhabited by slightly i. ore than 2 million people, which was Latvia\'s population before the war. Prof. A. Schwabe, editor of the Latvian Encyclopaedia (Stockhom, 1951) arrives by somewhat different calculations at a slightly higher figure. At the beginning of World War 11. the entire number By R. G. Shillers (60,000) of former Latvian citizens of German nationality emigrated voluntarily to Germany, on the basis of a special agreement between Germany and Latvia. During the year of the first Soviet occupation 34,000 Latvians were deported or murdered by the Soviet political police. Of these, 30,000 have been identified and are listed in the files of the International Red Cross in Geneva (compare "These Names Accuse," introduction, pp. 35-36, Stockholm, 1951). During the German occupation (1941-1945) the majority of Latvian Jews, having amounted to 94,000 in 1939, was brutally exterminated by German – Nazi executioners. The number of Jewish survivors who were deported or fled to Soviet Russia in 1941 is estimated at 20,000. Some 4-5 thousand may now be living in Latvia. We have no exact data on Soviet deportations of Latvian citizens of Russian, Polish and other nationalities. But, according to the Latvian Encyclopaedia (p. 479), at least some 12-16,000 were deported to forced labor in Germany in 1942. During the German occupation many thousands of Latvians were imprisoned, killed, placed in concentration camps, or deported to forced labor in German war production. Beginning with 1943, Latvian men were compulsorily inducted into the armed forces. All together 25,000 may be listed as execcuted, missing or killed in warfare. But when Red forces once again invaded Latvia in 1944-1945, streams of refugees poured westward, and others were forcibly evacuated by the German occupation authorities. Most were compelled to proceed in the direction of Germany. In 1947 not less than 110,000 Latvian displaced persons and refugees were counted in Western Europe. Prior to that date their number had been much larger; but many thousands of Latvian refugees were intercepted by the advancing Reds in Eastern Germany, Poland and Czechoslovakia in 1945, and most were deported to Soviet slave labor camps. A few thousands of Latvian displaced persons in the West were later "repatriated." Adding 5,000 Latvians who had manged to escape over the Baltic Sea to Sweden, we arrive at a total of 115,000 Latvian refugees and DP\'s, of whom most have already emigrated to overseas countries—45,000 of them to the United States. Though we have no exact data on deportations and population shifts in Latvia since the second Soviet occupation, it is wellknown to us that, immediately after the re-occupation of Latvia, the Soviet MGB proceeded with its "filtering" actions, arresting and scrutinising all males of military age, as well as all "suspects." It is assumed that no more than half of these were released and that the rest was shipped to Soviet concentration camps. Adding to their number thousands of slave laborers of both sexes, driven earlier to centres of Soviet war production, students and other nationalists arrested and deported, we arrive at the overall figure of some 60,000 deportees and other victims of Soviet atrocities during the period from 1944-1949. Apart from the above mentioned, no other mass deportations were reported prior to March 1949, when total collectivisation of Latvia\'s agriculture was suddenly inaugurated and carried out in one stroke. It POPULATION LOSSES AND SHIFTS IN LATVIA SINCE THE BEGINNING OF WORLD WAR 11. Latvian Germans, who emigrated to Germany in 1940-41 60,000 Latvian Jews, murdered by the Nazis, deported or evacuated to Russia, excepting returnees 90,000 Members of other minority groups deported .. . . 15,000 Total of national minorities, deported, removed, murdered or missing 165,000 Latvians deported or killed during first Soviet occupation 34,000 Latvians, who perished during German occupation 25,000 Latvian refugees and displaced persons .. .. 115,000 Latvians, deported, killed or missing during second Soviet occupation 136,000 Total of Latvians, deported, exiled, missing or killed since the start of World War II 310,000 Total loss of original population in Latvia to the present time 475,000 It has been pointed out above that the overall population of Latvia today is the same (or slightly above) as in 1939, at which time it amounted to 2 million. Since at that time 1,520,000, or *76 per cent, were of Latvian nationality, it appears that some 1,210,000 Latvians may still be living on Latvian soil, constituting 60.5 per cent, of the population. The percentage of non-Latvians has, accordingly, increased from 24 per cent to 39.5 per cent, consisting at present chiefly of Russians and various Soviet Asiatics. Apart from Latvian citizens of Russian extratcion, they are composed of functionaries of the Soviet regime and the political police, Red soldiers, for-, mer Russian prisoners of war who remained in Latvia, and new settlers from the East. It appears that there are today more than three times as many Russians in Latvia than before World War 11. There are whole districts where Latvians today are in the minority, particularly the border regions and along the ~ea coast. However, while the political power presently completely rests in the hands of Moscow directed Soviet Russian functionaries (including the MGB), Latvians are still predominant in all fields of cultural, social and economic activities. may easily be imagined what that meant to Latvian farmers. It meant new mass deportations of the rural population, hitting hardest the so-called "kulaks," i.e., farmers who were holding 75 acres of land, living on individual homesteads. (Since the inauguration of a sweeping agrarian reform in 1922, independent democratic Latvia had done away with large agricultural estates). According to the best information available (compare with the Latvian Encyclopaedia, p. 480), it is presumed that not less than 50,000 Latvian "kulaks," including members of their families, fell victims to mass deportations during the ominous period of compulsory Soviet collectivisation. Aiding to the above figures a reasonable margin of other victims of Soviet deportations, carried out individually, as well as other conceivable population losses during the period of the second Soviet occupation to the present time, figuring it tentatively 26,000, we arrive at the following overall statistical picture: The figures provided in tihs article can by no means claim to be accurate, because the Soviet occupation regime, which alone could reveal the extent and depth of its crimes of genocide, is not inclined to open the files of the NGB for free inspection. In appraising Latvia\'s population losses, we also must take into consideration various fluctuations, as well as possible changes in the birth rate of Latvia, in consequence of the upheavals of war and foreign domination. Many former inmates ot Nazi concentration camps, or persons pressed into German war production, have later joined the masses of displaced persons. On the other hand, thousands of Latvian refugees were trapped in Eastern European regions behind the Iron Curtain, as has been mentioned above. Latvian functionaries of the Soviet Russian regime, as well as a part of the Soviet sympathisers, who had joined the flight of the Red Army in June 1941, and also survivors of such evacuees as Latvian transportation workers, soldiers inducted into the Red Army, etc., were undoubtedly later repatriated with the return of the Soviet occupation regime. Our figures comprise men, women and children. Even though the death rate in Soviet slave labor camps is appalling, having amounted in some instances to 70 per cent, within one year (compare with "Human Freedom is Being Crushed," Washington, D.C.. 1951) it is, however, obvious that a great many Latvian deportees are still alive, particularly among the expellees who have been settled in the Soviet East, without having been convicted to hard labor, this group being composed primarily of women and children. Any survivors, who may, will undoubtedly return to Latvia after its liberation, just as was the case after World War I. Some authors on this subject have put the total of Latvian population losses considerably higher than is estimated here, without however substantiating their claims, as has been attempted in this survey. Our estimates correspond in general with the calculations of more prudent authors on this subject. The Polish writer, K.M. Smogorzevski, for instance, maintains in his pamphlet "The Russification of the Baltic States," that Latvia has today a population of 2,100,000, of which 1,222,000, or 58 per cent., are Latvians. This corresponds closely to our own figures. This article aspires to serve evidence that the actual decimation of the Latvian nation has, up to the present day, been less than the population lowes caused by World War I, regardless of any other effects of the criminal Soviet occupation regime, which cannot be appraised ir mere figures. Latvia is at present still subjected to the yoke of unprecedented barbarian Soviet bondage. But the spirit oi the nation has not and wil; not be broken. It has withstood thr test of history for several thousand years. When liberation comes, the Latvian peopk will be quite capable of recovering and managing their own affairs as free people, just & they did during the past 2* years of Latvia\'s independence.', "LATVIA'S POPULATION DROPS TO 2.375 MILLION Latvia had a population of 2.375 million on 31 March, 2000 which is 10.9% less than in 1989, according to preliminary data of the population census, released on 7 November by the Central Statistics Office. The population has decreased by 291,000 since the previous population census in Latvia conducted in 1989. The portion of people older than 60 years has increased by 9% or 39,000 since 1989 while the number of residents younger than 14 years has decreased by 146,000. The number of residents aged between 15 and 59 years has decreased by 186,000. The urban population has decreased by 13.5% while the rural population dropped by 5.1 %. The western Latvian town Uepaja has seen the sharpest reduction in its population, falling by 22%, in Riga by 16%, followed by the central Latvian town Jelgava whose population has decreased by 15% since 1989. The preliminary data show there are more women than men in Latvia, the number of women on 31 March being 1.282 million while the number of men was 1.093 million. About half a million or 21% (502,300) of the population were older than 60 years, according to preliminary data. 424,000 or 17.9% of the popu iation were younger than 14 years while 1,448,300 or 61% of the population were in the age group between 15 and 59 years. The portion of children has rapidly declined in the towns – in Liepaja by 36%, Riga 34%, Ventspils (western Latvia) 32%, Rēzekne (eastern Latvia) 30%. The sharpest growth in the portion of elderly people was seen in Daugavpils (eastern Latvia) by 20%, Ventspils 17%, Rēzekne 15%, Riga district 29%. The tenth population census in Latvia was conducted from 31 March 10 29 April. The Statistics Office said previously the population census covered some 95% of Latvia's residents. The next population census in Latvia will, most likely, be conducted in 2010. As a result of the natural movement of the population and migration, the percentage of Latvians in the total population has increased to 57% compared with 52% in 1989; the respective increases for Poles was from 2.3% to 2.5% and for Lithuanians from 1.3% to 1.4%. Over the same period the percentage of Russians has decreased from 34% in 1989 to 29.6% in 2000; the other decreases were: Belarussians from 4.5% to 4.1%, Ukrainians from 3.5% to 2.7% and Jews from 0.9% to 0.4%. The analysis of the indicated native language shows that 62% of the population have indicated Latvian as their native language. This figure is even higher than the percentage of Latvians in the total population (57.6%). The Russian language holds second place among the native languages – 36.1%. This percentage is also higher than the percentage of Russians in the total population (29.6%)."]

Representative_Docs: ['THE CHICAGO LATVIAN NEWSLETTER POLITICAL EVENTS, PICKETS AND DEMONSTRATIONS In April Latvia has had its share of pickets, demonstrations and unstable relations with Russia. The mayor of the city of Moscow Yuri Luzhkow has recommended Russian businessmen not to buy any goods from Latvia. In spite of this fact, the ambassador of Russia in Latvia denied that any economic sanctions on Latvia will be imposed. The ambassador also admitted that the relations between Russia and Latvia have declined. The governor of Yaroslav had also asked the Russian businessmen and entrepreneurs to boycott the goods from Latvia as well as the service industry. Many pickets were held at the Embassy of Latvia in Russia and many well known Russian citizens had taken a part in those pickets. On April Bth, the President of Russia announced that the government of Russia has made a decision to impose economic sanctions on Latvia, however, a day later his Press Secretary announced that no sanctions will be imposed. The Prime Minister of Latvia Guntars Krasts had asked the Ministry of Economics to prepare a report about the economic damages Latvia will face if Russia decides to impose sanctions on Latvia. According to this report Latvia exports to Russia 27% of food products, 17,1 % – electronics, 11,8% – textiles, 9,3% – chemical agents. In 1997 Latvia exported goods to Russia totalling 203,6 million Lats which is 21% of the entire export of Latvia. In case of imposed sanctions about 12,500 people may lose their jobs. The Prime Minister of Latvia Guntars Krasts has asked the Minister of Economics Atis Sausnītis to resign from his duties. Guntars Krasts considers that Sausnītis has passed misinformation about the relations between Latvia and Russia. A day later, the party "Saimnieks", where Sausnītis is a member, has resigned from the government and now the talks about possible crises of government are seriously considered. On March 17, the movement "Līdztiesība" (Equal Rights) and the Socialist Party of Latvia organized a procession in order to call attention to the slow process of issuing non-citizen passports. The participants were mostly noncitizens who were demanding that the Latvian government extend the use of the U.S.S.R. passports in Latvia. On March 24, the government of Latvia eased the concerns of many aliens residing in Latvia. After the aliens had held many pickets in March protesting the canceling validity of the U.S.S.R. passports, the government of Latvia extended the date of validity of the former U.S.S.R. NEWS FROM LATVIA May 1998 reported by passports for entering Latvia from March 31st to July Ist. The date of validity for leaving Latvia to travel to the CIS countries has also been extended from March 31st to October 31st. Up to March 19th, 145,390 people have received the aliens passports, which means that of all the aliens in Lavia, only 22,3% of them have obtained the passports. Most of those who have received these passports live in Preiji – 34%, Rēzekne – 33%, Daugavpils – 27,9% and Rīga -20,6%. The embassy of Russia in Latvia had not displayed the flag of the Republic of Latvia on March 25th – the Memorial Day of politically repressed persons. Even though embassies are not obligated to commemorate significant dates of the country they reside in, all the other embassies, out of respect for this day, had displayed the flag of Latvia. EXPLOSIONS In the early morning of April 2nd, there was an explosion at the Jewish Synagogue in Rīga. The Jewish community in Latvia has announced that this is a clear instigation and it will not damage the good relations between the Jews and the Latvians. To help to determine the cause of the explosion two FBI agents from the Baltic office in Tallinn have arrived in Rīga. Another explosion took place at the Embassy of Russia in Latvia. This also is being considered an instigation. TWO MEN ACCUSED IN GENOCIDE AGAINST THE NATION OF LATVIA On March 20, llja Mashokin was taken into custody on the grounds of suspicion that he has taken part in genocide against the nation of Latvia. The General Prosecutor\'s office has found out that in 1949 llja M. had signed resolutions for deportation of families that, included representatives of many different nationalities, llja M. was born in 1915 and is a citizen of Russia who permanently resides in Latvia. The General Prosecutor\'s office has worked on his files for over a year. If he will be found guilty, he will be the second person accused of genocide against the nation of Latvia. The first one Alfons Noviks, was sentenced to life in prison and died soon thereafter. In April, another former KGB agent has been arrested by the General Prosecutor\'s office. He is accused of genocide against the nation of Latvia. Trofims Jakušonoks is a citizen of the Republic of Latvia. Šarlote Līduma in Rīga NEW POLITICAL PARTIES On March 14, the Ist Congress of the new party called "Jaunā partija" took place. Raimonds Pauls was unanimously elected chairman of the party by all its members. When asked, why the newly elected chairman has not joined the party formed by ex-prime minister Andris Šķēle, R. Pauls replied, that two great spirits can not live peacefully together. When journalists asked him how many seats the new party is planning to get in the next parliament, R. Pauls answered that he is sure of only one seat (most likely meaning himself). A week later he announced that he will support the new party untill the elections and then he will see. There have been no comments from his party members about his announcements. The Ist Congress of the new party called "Tautas partija" or "Šķēles partija" will take place in Rēzekne on May 2nd. According to different polls, this party has the highest rating among other political forces in Latvia. ON INDEPENDENCE OF LATVIA A group of deputies from the Russian parliament Duma geopolitical committee is preparing a draft document that would declare all the legislative acts that were passed before the collapse of the U.S.S.R null and void. Should this be passed, the Sept. 6,1991, document which states a formal recognition of the independence of the three Baltic states, will be null and void as well. On April 2nd, the Latvian parliament was debating a project according to which the Latvian government would request the official opinion of other countries regarding Latvia\'s occupation by Russia. The debates were heated, no consensus was reached, and the final decision was tabled. One of the independent Russian parliament representatives Aleksandr Golubev again asserted to the press that no occupation ever took place. INTERESTING STATISTICS According to the statistics agency "Latvijas fakti", the most popular politician in the country is Raimonds Pauls (66,7 points). In second place is the President of the Bank of Latvia Einārs Repše (48 points) – (he hasn\'t taken part in politics since the regaining of Independence of Latvia). In third place is the current President of the Republic of Latvia Guntis Ulmanis (36,5 points), followed by Jānis Adamsons (29,9 points), Juris Bojārs (19,6 points), Andris Šķēle (23,3 points). The most negative ratings received Juris Kaksītis (-12,2 points), Atis Sausnītis (-6,3 points) and Vladimirs Makarovs (-4,9 points). The Institute of Women\'s Rights has summarized the situation of women in Latvia. According to the results of their poll taken this year, the women in the age bracket – 30 to 49 have the most difficult time finding a job. Unemployment among women is rising. In the next four months time the Women\'s Rights Institute will summarize all known data and will try to pass a bill about measures that need to be taken in order to ensure equal employment opportunities for women. According to the Latvian Central Agency of Statistics, the household budgets have been declining in 1997 for about 0,6% in comparison to 1996. In the countryside the household budget is 13,7% lower than in the cities. The highest income has been fixed in the region of Rīga and in Zemgale, the lowest – in Latgale. 1/3 of the non-citizens of Latvia consider that the process of naturalization is humiliating. 82% of citizens think that the children who were born in Latvia should be the first to be granted citizenship. In the event of an armed conflict, 77% of the citizens will be on the side of Latvia, of the non citizens – only 24%. 35% of the non citizens want Latvia to join the CIS, 44% are against it. 46% of non citizens want Russian to be the second official language of the Republic of Latvia. 17% (almost every sth!) of non citizens know somebody who has illegally obtained the document that certifies his/her knowledge of Latvian. Latvia still has one of the highest rates of suicide among all European countries. In 1996 – 758, in 1997 – 746 attempts of suicide have been registered. In comparison, in Helsinki, which has the highest suicide rate in Europe, about 605 suicide attempts a year have been registered. Russia is clearly intent on bullying Latvia. Yevgeny Primakov. Russian Foreign Minister, told a meeting at the Moscow State Institute of International Relations that in regard to its relations with Latvia, Russia will "use its size and power to insist that Russia\'s smaller neighbours conform to its demands." He also invoked, what has now become an established Moscow doctrine in relation to its so-called near abroad, namely that Russia has a right to protect \'our people1 in foreign countries (Russia Today, "Bear is Back in Latvia Relations", April 23, 1998). Primakov\'s words were echoed by Yeltsin\'s spokesperson Sergei Yastrzembsky, who stated that liitvia\'s changes to its citizenship law in conformity with the cecommendations of the USCb> were not sufficent to alter Russia\'s plans to punish Latvia economically (Associated Press quoted in The Washington Post, April 23.1998). Thus, we return to the issue of whose interests do these incidents serve? An answer to this question leads to a consideration of Latvia-Russian relations since the restoration of Latvian independence in August of 1991. As one analyst has remarked: Since the restoration of Baltic intependence, even a Russia in transition to demoracy has given numeroues indications of a desire to impose her will on the democracy and ensure that it remains within her sphere of influence. (A. Thomas Lane, "The Baltic States, the Enlargement of NATO and Russia", Journal of Baltic Studies, Vol. XXVIII. no. 4 (Winter 1997)) At that time, some 40,000 Russian troops were still stationed there, and Russia controlled its early warning radar station at Skrunda, which it still wants to keep operational despite an international agreement that it should be disbanded in 1998, as well as a communications monitoring station near Ventspils. For Latvia to claim its independence and sovereignty, the troops had to leave as quickly as possible. Yet, despite the fact that this was the Kozyrev\'s watch at the Russian foreign ministry and the romantic\' period in Russia\'s relations with the West, a troop withdrawal treaty was not signed until August 1994. Only after the intervention of the U.S. and the Russian expectation that it would gain financially from the agreement persuaded Russia to sign. The ostensible reason for the delay was Russia\'s insistence that Russians and then expanded to include all Russian speakers\' be given automatic citizenship. Initially, Russia expected that the West would assist Russia in forcing such a policy on Latvia, but the West refused and allowed a sovereign state to decide its own laws, through various international and regional organizations sought to guide Latvia\'s citizenship policy. By this time, the Kozyrev honeymoon with the West was largely over and Russian policy toward I.arvia and the Baltics came to be viewed through Russia\'s policy toward the \'near abroad\', the newly independent states of the former Soviet Union (For an analysis of this period and the turn towards a hardline Russian foreign policy under Primakov see, Leszek Buszynski, "Russia and the West: Towards Renewed Geopolitical Rivalry?" Survival, vol. 37. mo. 3, 1995; James H. Brusstar, "Russian Vital Interests". Strategic Forum, Institute for National Strategic Studies, November 6, September 1994.). The outward expression of this policy was Russia\'s insistence at various international fora and in its press that Latvia, along with Estonia, was systematically abusing the human rights of Russian speaking inhabitants of Latvia. The number of different organizations with a human rights remit that have visited Latvia must have broken some sort of international record: the United Nations Human Rights Commission, The Council of Europe, the Organization of Security and Cooperation in Europe\'s National Minorities Commissioner, the Council of the Baltic Sea States" Human Rights Commissioner, not to mention the U.S Slate Department, as well as private organizations such as Helsinki Watch. All reported that there were M systematic or government supported forms of human rights violations (The deputy head of the OSCE mission in Latvia, Pirkka Tapiola was quoted in the Baltic Times, Nov. 28- Dec. 4,1996: "During the three years that the mission has been here, we have not seen any massive or systematic violation of human rights. Problems have been of an adninistrative nature characteristic of transition countries,"). In 1994, the OSCE had even accepted Utvia\'s citizenship law, though it was identified that some of the provisions could be too difficult to satisfy. Yet, despite such reports that the human rights of Russian speakers were not being violated, Russia has persisted in making such claims. Why? It cannot be because Russia has an exemplary record of observing human rights and would want others to emulate it.; its record is universally recognized as being abysmal. It cannot be because it fears that the some 700,000 Russian speakers in Latvia will all leave for Russia and create a burden for it. Surveys have shown that despite not having the rights of citizens, Russian speakers are quite satisfied to remain in  the prospect of returning to Russia under the present circumstances must indeed be sobering. Is it because Utvia wants to deny Russia its ports and other transport facilities in order to hinder Russian trade with western Europe. Latvian ports have consistently seen a growth in the volume of trade tliat passes through them (Lionel Barber in the Finacial Times, April 15, 1998 refers to an unpublished Finnish government study that makes reference to Russia\'s dependency on Baltic ports.). Latvia would hardly seek to voluntarily decrease its income from this lucrative service. The answer does not lie in any of these explanations. The answer lies elswhere. Over the course of 1997, Russia\'s Baltic policy has taken a more definite shape. It should be seen in the context of Russia\'s overall military and security policy which over the last several years has been increasingly articulated by nationalist and populist politicians and the military, and implemented by Yeltsin and Primakov. These policies are predicated on Russia reasserting its great power status, of seeking to reintegrate the space of the former Soviet Union. One can point to the Belarus-Russia agreement that stopped just short of merging the two countries, but which with the help of Lukashenko is clearly aimed at creating a security zone separating Russia lrom NATO. Though Russia has had less luck with Ukraine, there is every, however, indication that Russia will continue io put pressure on Ukraine to keep it from completely escaping Moscow\'s reach. Ukraine has sought for the moment to balance its relations between Russia and the West. It is both a member of the Commonwealth of Independent States and is signatory to the Ukraine-NATO Charter. In the Caucuses and Central Asia, Russia pursues reintegrationist policies that focuses on Russia\'s interests coming first (Ariel Cohen, "Russia Won\'t Be I,eft Out", The Washington Post, Sunday, March 8, 1998. Russian officials are explicit about their determination not to lose their sway over the region. Andranik Migranian, a leading Russian policy analyst and a member of the Russian Presidential Council, recently co-authered an article in a leading Moscow newspaper, explicitly calling for destabilization of the \'near abroad\', the Russian term for the countries on its border. The article called for the encouragement of ethnic conflict in Georgia and Azerbaijan."). In 1997, there were already clear signs that Russia had not given up on its objective of bringing the Baltics back into its fold, of creating a dividing line in Europe that separates the space of the former Soviet Union from the rest of Europe. While Moscow\'s efforts to reintegrate the space of the former Soviet Union have not been as succesful as it would like to be, it is the clear aim of Yeltsin and his foreign minister Primakov to wage a foreign policy that gives priority to such integration. This policy should be set against Russia\'s larger strategic goals, which have been described as folows: • Russia\'s main goal is to avert the establisment of a monopolar world, with America at its center; • Russia must facilitate the development of a multipolar world, one in which Russia is one of the major powers; • Russia will be able to maintain and promote its major power status only if it stregthens it\'s sphere of influence – the entire territory of the post-Soviet space (read – the former USSR) – and if it manages to limit the influence of other large countries, especially the United States, in this region; • A policy of integration in the former Soviet space is one way in which Russia can accomplish its mission; • The success of these integration efforts will play a major role in determining the extent to which Russia manages to maintain a more or less equal relationship with the United States (Aivars Stranga, "Baltic-Russian Relations: 1995 – Beginning of 1997", in Atis  and Zaneta Ozoliņa, eds., Small States in a Turbulent Environment The Baltic Perspective. Riga: Latvian Institute of International Affairs, 1997. For the role of Russian Foreign Minister Evgenii Primakov in advancing this strategy see, Ariel Cohen, "The "Pimakov Doctrine": Russia\'s Zero-Sum Game with the United States", FYI No. 167. Dec. 15, 1977, The Heritage Foundation.). How this policy would affect Russia\'s relations with the Baltic states was enunciated on February 12, 1997, when Russia\'s long-term Baltic policy was unveiled in Washington, D. C, by the Russian Embassy as Yeltsin\'s statement on the Subject (Kmbasy of the Russian Federation, Washington, D. C, Press Release, No. 6, February 12, 1997.). This statement, while proclainung that it was issued with the objective of creating a climate of \'goodneighbourliness\' with the Baltic states, in fact expresses Russia\'s determination not to allow the Baltics the freedom to decide their own security policy and to keep them within her sphere of influence. The statement opposes Baltic entry into NATO and wants that such action could be \'a serious barrier between these states and Russia\' and would have \'an extremely negative impact\' on long term cooperation in the region. But by staying outside any \'blocks (ie., remaining neutral) there could be \'very concrete\' steps taken that could dispel any fears that the Baltic s may have for their security. As we shall see, this would appear to an allusion to the security guarantees that Russia was to offer the Baltics later in the year. With respect to Russianspeaking residents of Estonia and Latvia, Russia expects that they all wil] be granted citizenship. In economic relations with the Baltics, Russia expresses frustration that the transit routes for shipment of Russian energy and non-ferrous metals have been used to the detriment of Russia. Though this accusation is not clarified, Russia expresses a veiled threat that Russia will diversify the channels of transit, presumably to the detriment of the Baltics. Moreover, it advocates strenthening Russian capital in the Baltics and creating favourable transport conditions for the Kaliningrad region. Russia rejects I Latvia\'s \'underground" territorial claims and Estonia\'s desire to withdraw the border issue from the development of bileteral relations and ties signing of border treaties with improvements in the situation of Estonia\'s Russian speaking population. Most astonishing, the document speaks of eliminating the criminal threats against Russia from the territory of the Baltic countries, forgetting the fact that by some estimates 50% of Russian businesses are tied to the Russian mafia. Finally, Russia promises to eliminate fears of Russian \'cultural imperialism\', by seeking to create closer (sic) cultural ties with the Baltic States. The document is unshamedly one-sided in its approach to Russian-Baltic relations. Russia provides a specific list of what the Baltics shoul do to improve relations, what concessions they should make, but Russia provides no concessions on its part. Russia expects the Baltics to compromise their sovereignty and independence, while Russia gains the ability to control Baltic security and even domestic policy. In short, it is a zero-sum game, where the Baltics loose and Russia gains. As Stephen Blank writes: ... Russian elites believe that for Russia to be Russia\' it must have exclusive and uncontested dominance over the CIS and accomanying sphere of influence over the Baltic States (Blank, op. cit., p. 7.) Yet, the Russian Ambassador to Latvia, Alexander Udalcov claimed that the document was the basis for the start of a new period in Russia\'s relations with the Baltics and that it should be interpreted \'positively\' and that it would not harm Baltic-Russian relations {Latvijas Vēstnesis, February 17.1997 (http://www.lv-laiks.lv/1997/049050/kr.htm) This hypocritical attitude underlies Russia\'s aim to "blackmail those states with permanent exclusion from the NATO and possibly the EU... unless they surrender vital aspects of their sovereignty regarding legislation and foreign policy (Blank, op. cit., p. 12.). □ /To be concluded in November issue of MLB Ziņotājs)']

Representative_Docs: ['CHICAGO LATVIAN NEWSLETTER Populists Surge, Conservatives Purged In National Elections The 6th Saeima elections opened many people\'s eyes, not least in Latvia. Although opinion polls prior to the elections on September 30-October 1 predicted success for the soviet-era nomenklatura filled Democratic Party Saimnieks (DPS), no commentator predicted the rise of the populist Popular Movement Latvijai (Siegerist\'s Party) or the Unity Party of former Gorbachev protege Alberts Kauls. As significantly, no one foresaw the collapse of the coalitions formed by both the Farmers Union or the National Independence Movement of Latvia, which were so confident of victory that they agreed prior to the elections to form a governing coalition afterwards. The Saeima that will meet at high noon on November 7 will be much different than that which last gathered for a plenary session just two days before voting began. Voters Protest Pain, Choose Promises Despite gloating and post-election spin doctoring by the "victors" of the election – the DPS, TKL and LVP – the message sent by the Latvian voters is one of pain, protest, and naivete. To their credit, the Latvian voters took advantage of the available literature and ever-present radio, television November 1995 — Vol. XV, No. 9 Saeima Election Surprises and print ads to educate themselves on the platforms of the nineteen parties and coalitions standing for election. Unfortunately, Too many of the voters, in my honest opinion, took those campaign promises much too literally. Sickened by the pain of reform brought to them by the politicians of the sth Saeima, the voters who took part (neariy 73% of the electorate – a significant drop from the nearly 90% participation rate of the sth Saeima elections, but still enviable considering recent experience in the U.S.) generally rejected those parties they considered to be "to blame" for their agony – especially the LZS and LNNK, but also Latvia\'s Way (which saw its support at the polls drop by half and number of seats cut by more than 50%) and their coalition partners, the small Economists Political Association. Who did the Latvian voter support instead? A mixed bag of populists, each of which generally vied with each other in expansiveness of the promises they could make. The Democratic Party Saimnieks (translated as "Master" or "Steward", depending on the source) gathered to itself most of Latvia\'s late-model upwardly mobile Soviet nomenklatura, led by one-time Latvian Communist Youth leader, former Interior Minister Ziedonis Čevers. The 35year old Čevers parlayed his Interior Minister\'s portfolio, which during his tenure under Prime Minister Ivars Godmanis was rife with former Soviet security personnel, into a successful private personal and business security enterprise, the fund Drošiba. The DPS candidate list was filled with many "formers", to include Latvia\'s last Soviet-era PM, Alfreds Čepānis; Ilmārs BiŠers, who resigned in disgrace as a result of corruption charges in the Godmanis-era Finance Ministry; Ivars Ķezbers, a Soviet diplomat who was declared persona non grata in Sweden; and Dainis Turlais, who prior to becoming commander of the Latvian Army served as Soviet Army Gen. Pavel Grachev\'s second-in-command in Afghanistan. Prior to the elections, Čevers often claimed to have information about corruption within Latvia\'s Way, which he threatened to reveal if elected. He also claimed to have similar information about prominent Western Latvians, to include LNNK leader Aristids Lambergs and LC members Gunārs Meierovics, Olgerts Pavlovskis and Valdis Pavlovskis. To date, no evidence has been revealed, but the claims were used to back up Čevers\' assertion that only he and the DPS could "clean up government" and bring about instant prosperity. Čevers has also taken to claiming that he will rule in Latvia "for the next ten years". Balm to a voting populace which grew up during the stable and predictable times of Brezhnev and Khrushchev, but not a reflection of democratic reality or the power of the ballot. The Popular Movement Latvijai, led by German-Latvian Joachim Siegerist. Siegerist, is built on the anti-Communist, pro-Latvia posturing of its leader. Siegerist, a failed radical conservative politician in Germany, came to Latvia and built up a following among the poor and dispossessed, to whom he offered humanitarian aid in exchange for loyalty. During the sth Saeima elections Siegerist bankrolled the LNNK party\'s campaign, and was himself elected to serve as a deputy. His radical politics and lack of Latvian language were his undoing, as Siegerist was kicked out of the LNNK and later the Saeima. In forming the TKL, Siegerist gave his loyal followers ā means of protesting the pain of transition by supporting him and his lieutenants. Often playing loose with the truth, Siegerist was able to claim that he alone cared about the oppressed ethnic Latvians suffering from the burdens of "communists and their useful idiots". The success of TKL was, perhaps, the most surprising to political analysts in Latvia and abroad, for his rhetoric and tactics had made him seem nothing more than an itinerant carpetbagger. Since the elections, Siegerist has brought his party of anti-Communists into the fold of the DPS leftist alliance, which may well, after November 7, be elected (with the help of the largely unreformed former communists of the socialist Party) to govern in Latvia. He echoes Čevers\' conviction that he will rule for the next decade. Prior to Alberts Kauls\' involvement, the Unity Party of Latvia stood no chance of gaining seats in the Saeima. With Kauls\' support, and that of his wide universe of political and financial contacts, the Unity Party became a major player in Latvian politics. As Siegerist targeted the LNNK as insufficiently nationalist, Kauls, a former chairman of the famed Ādaži collective farm, targeted the Farmers Union as uncaring of the plight of the farmer. Promising prosperity "like we had at Ādaži, Tērvete, Druva" (all Soviet-era collective farms), Kauls guaranteed to "feed and clothe the nation" if his party won and he became Prime Minister. What\'s Next? In the final analysis, no one party will be able to lead decisively based on the results of the 6th Saeima elections. Two evenly matched blocs of parties have developed, each of which will put forward a proposed cabinet of ministers and government program. President Guntis Ulmanis will then charge the leader of one of the blocs (either Čevers or For Fatherland and Freedom leader Māris Grinblats) to assemble their cabinet and secure Saeima confidence in their choices. The National Bloc, led by Grinblats and his surprisingly powerful party, also includes the LNNK and LZS coalitions. Latvia\'s Way has pledged to support a National Bloc government, and will sign a formal agreement with the bloc if it is given the opportunity to form a government. The bloc, together with Latvia\'s Way, can count on 47 votes out of 100; for the National Bloc to secure Saeima approval, they will have to find several defectors from the leftist coalition parties. This process has already begun, with TKL deputy Guntis Eninš declaring his intention to serve as an independent in the Saeima due to his disgust with Siegerist\'s choice to join with Čevers. The National Reconciliation Coalition, led by čevers and the DPS, also includes Kauls\' Unity Party, the Popular Harmony Party of former Foreign Minister Jānis Jurkāns, and Siegerist\'s TKL. This coalition has already signed a cooperation agreement long on promises and opportunities to spend money, but short on concrete plans and sources of national income. The NRC currently also has 47 votes, but may be in a better position to win approval for their government depending on the position taken by the Socialist Party (5 seats). On October 20, the NRC published the names of its its proposed cabinet of ministers: Prime Minister Ziedonis Čevers (DPS) Agriculture Roberts Dilba (LVP) Culture (Raimonds Pauls?) Defense Odisejs Kostanda (TKL) Economics Joahim Siegerist (TKL) Education and Science Modris Plāte (TKL) Environment and Regional Development Maris Ikaunieks Finance Aivars Kreituss (DPS) Foreign Affairs Jānis Jurkāns (TSP) Interior Jānis Ādamsons (LC) Justice Juris Kaksītis (DPS) Transportation Ēriks Kaža (DPS) Welfare Andris Saulītis (TKL) Ādamsons has already expressed to Latvia\'s Way leaders the his surprise at being included by čevers in this proposed government, although he has consistently maintained since the elections that he would abide by his party\'s decision on the matter. Lastly – it is worth noting that a "three strikes" rule does exist for forming a government in the Republic of Latvia. If President Ulmanis proposes three Prime Ministers in succession who fail to gain Saeima support for their governments, the President must dissolve the Saeima and call new elections. Stay tuned. #Martioš Zvaners Elected to the 6th Saeima (in order of preference, from the Riga, Vidzeme, Latgale, Kurzeme and Zemgale voting districts except where noted): From the Democratic party Saimnieks (18 seats): Ilga Kreituse, Valdis Krisbergs, Juris Zaķis, Ivars Ķezbers; Ilmārs Bišers, Aivars Kreituss, Dainis Turlais, Alfrēds čepānis, Ziedonis Čevers; Ernests Jurkāns, Ēriks Kaža, Leonards Teniss; Juris Kaksitis, Valdis Nagobads, Atis Sausnitis; Juris Celmiņš, Modris Goba, Jānis Rāzna. DPS received 144,758 votes (15,1% of votes cast). From Latvia\'s Way (17 seats): Indulis Bērziņi, Imants Daudiss, Aivars Endziņš, Andrejs Panteļējevs; Valdis Birkavs, Māris Gailis, Anatolijs Gorbunovs, Jānis Ādamsons; Pēteris Apinis, Vilis Krištopans, Antons Seiksts; Jānis Lagzdiņš, Kristiāna Libane, Dzintars Ābiķis; Romāns Apsitis, Jānis Bunkšs, Andris Tomasuns. LC received 139,929 votes (14,6%). From the Popular movement Latvijai (l6seats) Andris Rubins, Māris Rudzītis, Elmārs Zelgalvis; Oļģerts Dunkers, Ojārs Grinbergs, Odisejs Kostanda, Andris Saulitis; Jānis Strods, Kārlis Cerāns; Gunta Gannusa, Edmunds Grinbergs, Jānis Kazāks; Guntis Eniņs, Janina Kusnere, Jānis Mauliņs, Modris Plāte. Siegerist\'s party received 142,324 votes (14,9%). From For Fatherland and Freedom (14 seats) Māris Grinblats, Leopolds Ozoliņš, Normunds Pēterkops, Aleksandrs Petersons, Juris Sinka; Oskars Grigs, Guntars Grinblats, Jānis Straume, Juris VidiņS; Roberts Jurdžs; Aigars Jirgens, Roberts Zile; Vents Balodis, Andrejs Požarnovs. TB received 114,050 votes (11,9%). From the Farmers Union coalition (8 seats) Paulis Kļaviņš (KDS), Kārlis Jūlijs Druva (LZS); Aida Prēdele (KDS), Pauls Putniņš (LZS); Andrejs Naglis (KDS), Anta Rugāte (KDS>, Māris Vitols (KDS); Ervids Grinovskis (LZS). The coalition – made up of the LZS, Christian Democratic Union (KDS) and Democratic Party of Latgale (LDP) – received 60,498 votes (6,3%). From the Unity Party (8 seats) Gundars Valdmanis; Raitis Apalups, Roberts Dilba, Viesturs Gredzens; Viktors Kalnbērzs, Anatolijs Tučs; Edgars Bāns; Jānis Rubulis. The LVP received 68,305 vēlētāji (7,1%). From the LNNK-Green Party coaluon (7 seats) Juris Dobelis (LNNK), Aleksandrs Kirsteins (LNNK), Andrejs Krastiņš (LNNK); Indulis Emsis (LZP), Anna Seile (LNNK), Pēteris Tabūns (LNNK); Jānis KalviņS (LZP); Arisuds Lambergs (LNNK). The LNNK-LZP coalUon received 60,352 votes (6,3%). From the Popular Harmony Party (6 seats in Riga, Vidzeme and Latgale) Vladilens Dozorcevs, Jānis Jurkāns, Ludmila Kuprijanova; Andris Ameriks; Leonards StaSs, Jānis Urbanovičs. TSP received 53,041 votes (5,55%). From the Socialist Party of Latvia (5 seats in Riga and Latgale) Oļegs Deņisovs, Modris Lujāns; Aleksandrs Bartaševičs, Martijans Bekasovs, Aleksandrs Golubovs. The socialists received 53,325 votes (5,58%)', 'NEW GOVERNMENT CONFIRMED On 21 December, 94 out of 100 Sae//7?a deputies participated in the confidence vote for the new government, formed by Andris Skele. By 70 votes for and 24 against the government was approved. The government is: Prime Minister Andris Skele (entrepreneur); Deputy Prime Minister Ziedonis Gevers (DPS-Democratic Party "Master"); Deputy Prime Minister and Minister of Defence Andrejs Krastins (LNNK – Latvia\'s National Conservative Party "LNNK"); Minister of Foreign Affairs Valdis Birkavs (LC – Alliance "Latvia\'s Way"); Minister of Economy Guntars Krasts (TB – Union "For Fatherland and Freedom"); Minister of Finance Aivars Guntis Krertuss (DPS); Minister of interior Dainis Turlais (DPS); Deputy Prime Minister and Minister of Education and Science Maris Grinblats (TB); Deputy Prime Minister and Minister of Agriculture Alberts Kauls (LVP – Latvia\'s Unity Party); Minister of Transport Vilis Kristopans (LC); Minister of Welfare Vladimirs Makarovs (TB); Minister of Justice Dzintars Rasnacs (TB); Deputy Prime Minister and Minister of Environment Protection and Regional Development Maris Gailis (LC); Minister of Culture Ojars Sparitis (LZS – Latvia\'s Farmers\' Union); Minister without Portfolio for EU Affairs Aleksandrs Kirsteins (LNNK); Minister without Portfolio for Local Governments Ernests Jurkans (DPS) State Ministers: Ministry of Foreign Affairs, State Minister Juris Sinka (TB); Ministry of Economy, State Minister for Industry, Property and Privatisation Ēriks Kaza (DPS); Ministry of Finance, State Minister for State Revenues Aija Poca (LC); State Minister for Investments and Credit Policy Sarrnite Jegere (non-party); Ministry of Education and Science, State Minister for Education Janis Gaigafs (LC), State Minister for Higher Education and Science Pēteris Cimdins (KDS – Christian Democratic Union); Ministry of Agriculture, State Minister for Cooperation Janis Muižnieks (LVP), State Minister for fisheries Ivars Amolins (LVP), State Minister for Forestry Arvids Ozols (LZP – Latvia\'s Green Party); Ministry of Welfare, State Minister for Labour Andris Berzins (LC), State Ministerfor Health Juris Vinkelis (non-party); Ministry of Environment Protection and Regional Development, State Minister for Environment Indulis Emsis (LZP) The new government\'s declaration says that a long-term goal of the state economic policy is to integrate Latvia into the European countries\' economic system. The main objective of Latvia is to join the EU and draft the economic legislation according the EU standards. The government should also promote the creation of a common Baltic market The government will continue the course towards NATO membership and actively participate in other international organisations. The government will develop stable neighbourly relations with the Russian Federation and continue negotiations on determining the state border according to international norms and historical developments. The government will support strict monetary policy – it undertakes to introduce the most severe austerity regime regarding state budget expenditures, sharply reduce expenses for state institutions by eliminating duplication of functions, cut the staff of state institutions, as well as reconsider the usefulness of embassies\' expenditures. The main goals of the government\'s agricultural policy are to ensure that Latvia\'s residents are supplied with Latvian-made basic foodstuffs, as well as observe Latvian farmers\' interests when integrating into the EU. The government also plans to improve the mechanism of granting subsidies and credits to farmers. In the sphere of education, the government promises to increase teachers\' salaries gradually to the level of average salaries of civil servants.', 'CHICAGO LATVIAN NEWSLETTER Latvia Gets Best Christmas Gift Of All: A Stable Government The 100 deputies of Latvia\'s parliament gave their country the best Christmas present it could hope to receive on December 21, when they voted overwhelmingly (70 yea, 24 nay) to approve a "grand coalition" government led by successful Latvian businessman and political neophyte Andris Šķēle. A spectacularly unexpected breakthrough by most of the parties competing for influence in the Saeima allowed Latvia to end nearly three months of political paralysis and avoid the very real threat of parliamentary stalemate and new elections. The creation of a "grand coalition" of six moderate parties of both the right and left was made possible by the failure of both camps to secure approval for their proposed governments, as well as by President Ulmanis\' consistent and, to this observer, principled and proper rejection of radical extremist Joachims Zīgerists. Čevers Tries, Fails Following the failure of Tēvzemei un brīvībai leader Māris Grinblats to form a center-right cabinet consisting of ministers from the National Bloc (TB, the LNNK/LZP faction and the LZS/KDS/LDP faction) and Latvia\'s Way, President Ulmanis took six days to nominate Ziedonis Čevers, leader of the Democratic Party Saimnieks, as his next choice to be Prime Minister. Much of the delay, no doubt, can be attributed to Ulmanis\' wariness of offering the cabinet to any party bloc which could potentially include Zīgerists. Ulmanis stated in nominating Čevers on November 29 that the existence of two solid and equally strong blocs in the Saiema drove his selection-in interviews both before and after the fall of the Grinblats\' cabinet, more than ten potential candidates had rejected Ulmanis\' offer to become Prime Minister because they felt they could not gain support in the Saeima. One day earlier, both Andrejs Panteļējevs of Latvia\'s Way and Aleksandrs Kiršteins of LNNK said that they felt such a "third party" candidacy for the Prime Minister\'s seat could become a reality only after a failure by Čevers and his center-left bloc to set up a cabinet. In spite of statements that he would unveil a cabinet "within hours" of selection, Prime Minister-designate Čevers was forced by dissention within his bloc to revisit his proposed cabinet of ministers and government platform (see CLN 11/95). Both Popular Harmony Party (TSP) leader Jānis Jurkāns and members of the Latvian Unity Party (LVP) faction expressed their wish for an expanded cabinet which could be supported by more than the four parties of the Čevers bloc and the five members of the unreformed-communist Latvian Socialist Party. Jurkāns also reiterated his opposition to the inclusion of Zīgerists in any cabinet. To Čevers\' dismay, he was unable to secure outside support, and as a result also failed to become Prime Minister, In fact, Čevers was abandoned during the vote on his cabinet on December 7 by two members of the LVP. Although Čevers received a plurality of the votes cast (50 yea, 45 nay, 5 abstained), Saeima rules require a simple majority (50% + 1 of deputies voting) for approval of a Prime Minister. Because of the tie vote, a second ballot was held, with no change in the result. Predictably, Čevers was supported only by his party bloc and by the Socialists, and opposed by the opposing party bloc and Latvia\'s Way. However, the opposition was joined by LVP deputy Viesturs Gredzens, who voted no. and by LVP deputy Viktors Kalnbērzs, who abstained-thus guaranteeing Čevers\' fall. "Who Is Andris Šķēle?" After the collapse of the Čevers candidacy, discussions began in earnest about the creation of a "grand January 1996 — Vol. XVI, No. 1 coalition" government, which would cut across the two opposing party blocs and unite as many factions as possible in support of a "neutral" Prime Minister. Although the first new names to be mentioned as candidates included former Prime Minister Māris Gailis and former Saeima Chairman Anatolijs Gorbunovs (both LQ, the rival Tēvzemei un Brīvībai and Democratic Party Saimnieks factions soon reached agreement on an alternative candidate, whose best quality would be that he was not a member of Latvia\'s Way-prominent businessman and presidential advisor Andris Šķēle. As Šķēles name was heard more often as a potential Prime Minister, questions arose about his background and past activities. Bom in 1958 in Ape, near the Latvian-Estonian border in northeastern Latvia, Andris Šķēle graduated from the Latvian Agricultural Academy in Jelgava in 1981. After college, Šķēle worked for the "Stars" experimental agricultural facility until 1990, when he joined the Agricultural Ministry as a Deputy Minister in the government of Ivars Godmanis. After the fall of his superior Dainis Ģēģers in May 1993, Šķēle served as acting Agricultural Minister until September 1993. Šķēle was acting director of the Latvian Privatization Agency, and served as an economic advisor to President Ulmanis. Just weeks before his selection as a Prime Minister candidate, Šķēle had been elected chairman of the board of the huge state joint stock company Latvijas kuģniecība (Latvian Shipping). Šķēle is best known in Latvia as an extremely successful businessman, who has created a food services empire which has been estimated to encompass one half of all privatized food services and processing companies in Latvia. Although Šķēle personally claims to hold a controlling interest in only one company (46% of the bread company Baitmaiznieks), through his financial-production company Aye Lat, Šķēle made use of the privatization process to become a major investor in 15 different milk, egg, bread and meat processing facilities, as well as the champagne producer Rīgas vini, confectionery manufacturers Laima and Uzvara, and many others. In spite of intense scrutiny, none of Šķēles business activities has been found to be at all illegal. In fact, šķēle is widely considered to be an exceptionally competent and hard-working manager. These qualities must have swayed both TB and DPS, as these two parties both recommended Šķēle as a compromise candidate to President Ulmanis, who announced that Šķēle would be his candidate on December 14, during a stopover in Frankfurt, Germany while traveling to Germany and Spain for the EU summit. New Government Large, But Stable The new coalition government approved by the Saeima is the largest of any in the short history of modem Latvian parliamentary politics. Created to reflect the balance of power in the Saeima, each of the parties which joined the coalition (TB, DPS, LC, the IJWK/LZP faction, LVP and the LZS/KDS/LDP faction) was given cabinet portfolios commensurate to their strength in parliament. A party board will be created to help secure consensus on all issues before the government, and should any minister leave, their position will be filled by a candidate from the previous minister\'s party. Political responsibility for the actions of their ministers has also been taken by each of the parties represented. Latvia\'s previous governments were headed by persons representing the majority party, which then also took responsibility for the actions of the government. Šķēle is a political outsider, although during the 6th Saiema elections he and his business associates supported all parties except for the unreformed Communists of the Latvian Socialist Party and the Popular Movement for Latvia, headed by radical populist Joachims Zīgerists. The government platform agreed to by the coalition parties is an amalgamation of the plat-forms offered by both Grinblats and Čevers during their failed attempts to form a cabinet. It emphasizes economic liberalism while addressing social issues as well, and reflects Šķēles personal priority of developing a strong market economy and securing a positive investment climate for both foreign and domestic businessmen. As a reflection of the difficulty of forming a cabinet from so many parties, the current law regulating the structure of the government will be amended within the next few weeks to allow for more persons to be designated as Deputy Prime Ministers. In time, Māris Grinblats will step aside as Education and Science Minister and maintain only his Deputy PM portfolio. Grinblats will be replaced by current State Minister and former education and Science Minister Jānis Gaigals (LC). At that time, as many a* five persons may become Deputy PM\'s-Čevers, Grinblats, Kauls, Māris Gailis of Latvia\'s Way anc Andrejs Krastiņš of LNNK. #Mārtiņš Zvanen Prime Minister Andris Šķēle Deputy Prime Minister Ziedonis Čevers (DPS) Agriculture, Deputy PM Alberts Kauls (LVP) State Minister for cooperation Jānis Muižnieks (LVP) State Minister for fishing Ivars Amoliņš (LVP) State Minister for forestry Arvīds Ozols (LZP) Education & Science, Deputy PM Māris Grinblats (TB) State Ministers Jānis Gaigals (LC) State Minister Pēteris Cimdiņš (KDS) Culture Ojārs Spāritis (LZS) Defense Andrejs Krastiņš (LNNK) Economics Guntars Krasts (TB) State Minister Ēriks Kaža (DPS) State Minister J.
