Name: 22_astra_court_skulme_sentence
Representative_Docs: ['Astra freed Gunārs Astra among recent ]y reķiased Latvian jpgJitical ļ i is oners. Cunārs Astra, one of 1 he best known and longest imprisoned Latvian human rights activists, has been released and returned to his home in Riga. Astra, 54, was released on February 1 after serving over 3 years of his 7 year prison term. Added to his previous sentence. Astra had spent a total of 18 years in Soviet prisons. Astra has been one of the most eloquent and outspoken of Latvia\'s human and national rights activsts. Astra has demanded the annulment of the 1939 Molotov-Ribbentrop Pact, publically denounced the illegality of the Soviet annexation of Latvia, and translated Baltic human rights documents. He was arrested in 1983 for the possession of illegal Western books, including George Orwell\'s "1984". In his final statement before the Soviet court on December 15, 1983. Astra said, "It is love and dignity for my people that has brought me to the defendent\'s dock, as I oppose the brutal acts against my people and demoralization of my nation\'s soul." His wife, Livija Astra, has been an active and outspoken defender of her husband during his incarceration. She has met with U.S. Congressional aides in Rip.n and and appeared on Canadian television. Although Astra\'s immediate plans are not known. Western sources hope to interview the Latvian patriot shortly While other aspects of Gorbachev s glasnost\' policy have been met with skepticism in the Baltic States, the release of Baltic political prisoners has continued In recent months. 09-year old Latvian Six Democrat Juris Bumeisters was pardoned after serving 7 years of ■ 15-year sentence for allegedly "passing military secrets\' to the West. Another Latvian activist. Žanis Skudra lias also been released free prisan. With the release of Ast jn, HTiineisters and Skudra, all of the well known Latvian political prisoners have beOD released.', "Latvia's leading freedom fighter Gunārs Astra, dies at the age of 56. Gunārs Astra, one of Latvia's most outspoken and respected nationalists, a man who spent 19 years of his life in Soviet prison camps and was looked upon by Soviet authorities as the Sakharov of Latvia, is dead at the age of 56. Astra died on the morning of April 6, several days after undergoing a 7-hour heart operation in a Leningrad hospital. While all the facts surrounding Astra's sudden illness and subsequent death are not clear, this iuch is known: Although he still had two years to go on his 7-year prison sentence, Astra was released from prison and returned to his home in RTga, Latvia in February of 1988. He was one of the last of Latvia's well known political prisoners to be released as part of Gorbachev's recent amnesty policy. In the beginning of March, Astra and his wife Līvija took a train from Riga to Leningrad, in order to visit friends there. Astra became sick en route, and was brought by his wife to a hospital in Leningrad. A great deal of confusion surrounded the diagnosis of his condition and treatment. He was variously diagnosed as suffering from Dneumonla. endocarditis and finally blood poisoning News of Astra's sudden illness reached the West immediately. The World Federation of Free Latvians made arrangements through Lufthansa and the West German government to supply Astra with broad spectrum antibiotics not Gunārs Astra igļi -1988 available in Leningrad. Sen. Paul Siaon (D-IL). Sen. Don Rlegle (D-MI). Cong. Steny Hoyer (D-MI). and Cong. Edward Pelghan (D-OH) all sent telegrams to Soviet authorities at the hospital, urging them to make every effort to bring Astra back to health. At his own request. Astra was moved from the Botkin hospital in Leningrad, to a military academy medical center, considered to be the best medical facility in the city. Physicians there apparently determined that center, considered to be the best medical facility in the city. Physicians there apparently determined that Astra's heart had been seriously damaged by the blood poisoning and recommended open heart surgery. On March 25. as thousands of Latvians were gathering in Riga to honor the victims of the 1949 deportations, Astra underwent a 7-hour heart operation, where two of his damaged valves were removed and replaced with artificial ones. His chance of full recovery was listed at 50-50. Astra's condition remained stable for the next few days His wife Līvija was at his side through much of the recovery period. At approximately 4AM. while sleeping soundly, he began to have trouble with his breathing. Although he received immediate medical assistance, he died at 6AN. An autopsy, performed by Soviet authorities a day later, attributed his death to pulmonary embolisms (blood clots in the lungs.) According to Livija Astra, one of her husband's last requests was that a foreign (non Soviet) pathologist be allowed to participate In his autopsy. Livija Astra conveyed this request to the Soviets and friends In the West. At the invitation of the World Federation of Free Latvians. Dr. Jdrgen Thomsen, from the Danish University Institute of Forensic Medicine agreed to make the trip to Leningrad on April 14. Although Soviet authorities never denied Dr. Thomsen permission to perform the autopsy, he was unable to get a visa to the USSR. While there is no hard evidence that foul play was involved in Astra's death, many questions have nevertheless been raised. Astra was not known to have heart problems while in prison, and had not been ill during the 2 months after he had been released from prison. According to former Latvian political prisoner and Astra family friend. Gunārs Rode, now living in Stockholm. Astra had been called in for questioning by the KGB several hours prior to his departure for Lenigrad. During the interrogation, Astra reportedly drank mineral water given to him by his interrogators. According to Rode's sources in Latvia, Astra first started feeling ill several hours later on the train Diplomatic representatives of the U.S. Consul General in Leningrad had contacted Mrs. Astra after her husband's death, and reportedly informed Soviet authorities of her desire to have a Western pathologist review the autopsy findings. The Latvian human rights group HELSINKI 86 has announced plans for a public funeral for Astra when his remains are returned to Riga. A tentative date of April 19 had been proposed.", '" I am a Latvian" by Ojārs Kalniņš Gunārs Astra was not a dissident. The dictionary indicates that a dissident is someone who disagrees with the opinions of a group to which he belongs. He was not a Soviet dissident, because he did not belong to the Soviets. He lived, and died, a Latvian. Nor was he a Latvian dissident, because his views were shared by the majority of the Latvian people. Gunārs Astra was many things a former political prisoner, a freedom fighter, a human rights activist, a student of language and philosophy, a father and husband – but above all, as he stated at his own trial in 1983, "I am a Latvian." He was highly respected, not only by Latvians, but by all those who knew of him in the human rights movement. He was a man fought for his beliefs, and stood up defiantly to his nation\'s oppressors. His wife once said that even if he were released from prison, he would not remain free very long, because he would continue to speak out about the same Issues that originally landed him in the Gulag. He would make no deals, sign no agreements, recant no statements.his wife said.  who fight for national and human rights. Gunārs Astra was schooled, trained and educated in the Soviet prison camps. He was born in Latvia on October 22, 1931 and as a youth worked at the VEF electronics factory in Riga. While working he enrolled in After completing his term, Astra returned to Riga in 1976 where he grew increasingly interested in Latvian history, culture, traditions and literature. While working at a factory, he spent his free time studying English and philosophy. He also began collecting Western books and magazines, as well as material published during Latvia\'s independence. Astra was incensed over what he saw as a deliberate Soviet Russian attempt to destroy the Latvian language and history. In 1983, during a major KGB crackdown on Latvian religious and national rights activists, Astra was again arrested and sentenced to 7 years imprisonment and 5 years exile. His "crimes" included possession of forbidden Western books (George Orwell\'s "1984" for example), photocopying historical monographs, and distribution of illegal Latvian books and materials. Astra was also accused of having translated the text of the secret protocol of the 1939 Stalin Hitler Pact. At his trial, Astra made a dramatic final statement to the Soviet court, setting forth his personal views on Soviet rule in Latvia, Russlfication and censorship. He condemned the "deliberate abuse of the Latvian language", stating that "It hurts and humiliates me to see my native language slowly pushed out of the way." In a passage that seemed to predict the nationalities unrest which presently besets the USSR, Astra denounced "Russian communist expansion and domination" in such non-Russian countries as Latvia. Moldavia, Bielorusia and Ukraine, and cited Napoleon\'s observation that "All imperialisms fall due to disorders in their digestive systems." "It is love and dignity for my people that has brought me to the defendant\'s dock, as I oppose the brutal acts against my people and the demoralization of my nation\'s soul, " Astra told the court. As fate would have it, Astra was imprisoned in 1983 for activities which have become tolerated, if not legal, in Gorbachev\'s USSR. In his final statement, he made an eloquent defense of freedom of speech and information: correspondence courses at the Latvian State University where he learned English and German. In time, desiring to practice his newly learned English, he befriended some Amerians he had met at a trade exhibition In Moscow. Because he continued contacts with these Americans, in 1961 he was arrested and tried for passing information about his job to foreigners. He was sentenced to 15 years in a strict regime labor camp. During his time In prison, he befriended many political prisoners of different nationalities, all of whom had been falsely charged just as he had. He began to develop contacts in the human rights movement, and participated in protests against the mistreatment of prisoners. "Nothing can Justify our being kept in the dark. People, being intelligent life forms, need to continually receive information about the real world – the more widespread and varied, the better. It is a well established fact that the opportunity to air opposing viewpoints facilitates choosing the best alternative in any given situation. A lack of information leads to the eventual inability to make rational judgements and promotes mental atrophy. A person who is incapable of rational thought becomes less than human He becomes a puppet and a slave, to be manipulated by the state." While such a statement was viewed as subversive in 1983, today it could have just as easily come from the mouth of Gorbachev and his fellow proponents of \'glasnost\'. At his trial, Astra was accused by the prosecutor as being Andrei  "comrade in thought." Back in 1983, Sakharov was still in exile and in disfavor with the regime. Today, Sakharov is free and speaking openly to the Western press about human rights, freedom of the press and democracy. Gunārs Astra, on the other hand, is dead. While Astra was deeply hurt by the suffering of his people and nation, he never gave up hope, and ended his 1983 court statement with a defiant optimism: "I fervently believe that these nightmarish times will end some day. This belief gives me the strength to stand before you now. Our people have suffered a great deal and have learned how to survive. They will outlive this dark period of their history." I join Gunārs Astra in this fervent belief. He was a living symbol of Latvian courage in the face of suffering, and although he did not survive his tormentors, his memory, his conviction, his fiery patriotism and love of Latvia will live on. Gunārs Astra was a hero. The Soviets turned him into a martyr. The Latvian people will not forget that. Ever.']

Name: 23_export_import_million_lat
Representative_Docs: ["LATVIAN FOREIGN TRADE VOLUME GOES UP According to the State Statistics Committee, the volume of Latvian, foreign trade has increased in the first five months of 1995, compared to the same time period in 1994. The export of goods has risen by 32.6% and reached 269 million lats. The total volume of imports has increased by 34.2% for a total of 347 million tats. The difference between the total volume of imports and exports has Increased; in the first five months of 1995, imports exceeded exports by 29%, while in the same time period in 1994 the difference was 27.5%. The relative importance of the European Union countries has increased in Latvian foreign trade figures, while the role of the CIS has decreased. In the first five months of 1994, Latvia exported 35.8% of its goods to the EU, but in 1995 this figure has already risen to 47,3%. Import from EU countries reached 50% of the total imports for Latvia to date this year, compared to 35.8% last year. Exports to the CIS added up to 44.5% of total exports fast year and 36.8% this year, while imports equalled 35.2% in 1994 and 29.1 % in 1995. In the first five months of 1995, timber and wood products occupied the most significant place in Latvia's exports – 28.6%. The share of total exports enjoyed by textile fabrics and goods was 13.9%, followed by foodstuffs at 8.9%, mechanical and electrical appliances at 8.3%, metal and metaiwork at 8.2%. In comparison to 1994, the export of timber and wood products has increased by 37.1 million lats, while the export of textile goods, as well as agricultural produce, has gone up by 7.3 and 5 million lats respectively. The export of transport vehicles, footwear and millinery goods has considerably decreased. Mineral products occupied the largest share of Latvian imports in the first five months of 1995 at 22.3%, mechanical and electrical appliances represented 17.9% of the total, chemical industry and related products 11.9%, textile fabrics and goods 8%, and transport vehicles 7.7%. When compared to the first five months of 1994, import of mechanical and electrical appliances and equipment has increased most rapidly – by 26 million lats. The import of chemical industry and related branch goods went up by 14.5 million lats, and the import of textile fabrics and goods increased by 13.4 million lats. The import of mineral products to Latvia decreased by 7.7 million lats and the import of meat and agricultural products by 2.5 million lats.", "LATVIA'S IMPORTS EXCEED EXPORTS BY 28.9% According to Latvia's Central Statistical Bureau, Latvia imported 28.9% more goods in the first eight months of this year than it exported. Between January and August, Latvia exported 437.4 million lats worth of goods and imported 563.8 million iats worth. In comparison to the same time period in 19943 export increased 26.8%, while the volume of imports grew 33.2%. Similarly, in the comparable period of 1994, imports exceeded exports by 22.8%. In 1995 Latvia's primary imports were mineral products such as oil and oil products, natural gas and electricity, totalling 20.9% of all imports, or 117.7 million lats. Other examples of items Imported and their respective value and percentage of total imports are as follows: machinery and equipment worth 100.6 million lats equalling 17.8% of all Imports; 65 million lats in chemical goods or 11.5%; 50.5 million lats in transportation vehicles or 8.9%; 45.3 million lats in textile goods or 8%. The largest growth was observed in the import volume of timber which increased 2.9% or 3.5 million lats. In the lead among exports was timber and timber goods, accounting for 27.5% of all exports, or goods valued at 120,3 million lats. Other exports included 60 million lats in textile goods or 13.7% of all exports; 41.5 million lats in food stuffs or 9.5%; 37.6 million lats in machinery and equipment or 8.6%; 36.2 million iats in metals or 8.3%. The export of cattle and meat products increased by 9.5 million lats or 2.1 times when compared to the first eight months of 1994. The export of timber, cardboard and paper grew 2.3 million lats or 94%, while the export of stone, concrete, glass and ceramics increased 4.3 million lats or 93.4%.", "STRONG RISE IN TRADE TURNOVER WITH EU The proportion of Latvia's foreign trade with the European Union has grown through the first six months of this year, while that with the CIS has decreased. In the first half of 1994, the EU accounted for 36.3% of Latvia's total export, while for the same period this year the figure was 47.2%. From the EU, in 1995 Latvia imported 50.9% of its total import, as compared to 35.5% last year. Meanwhile, exports to the CIS have shrunk from 42.9% last year to 37% this year, and imports from 32% to 28%. In the first half of the year, Latvia exported 327.7 million fats in goods, which Is 27.8% more than the same period in 1994. The total volume of imports was 418.6 million lats, again a 26.6% increase over last yeas- Between January and July the trade gap narrowed, with the difference between the total value of exports and imports slightly reduced. In the corresponding time period last year the negative difference was 29%, while fortius year imports exceeded exports by 27.8%. According to figures from the State Statistics Committee, Latvia's main export was timber and wood products, constituting almost one third or 28% of total exports. The next most important export goods were textile products (14%), food stuffs (9.1%), machinery and equipment (8.2%), and metals (8%). There has been a most striking growth of timber exports, to the value of 41.6 million lats or 83.3% over the first half of the previous year. The list of the main imports was headed by mineral products (21%), followed by machinery and equipment (17.9%), chemicals (11.8%), textiles (8.3%) and transport vehicles (8%)."]

Name: 24_korea_korean_chinese_united
Representative_Docs: ['The United States last week charged the Soviet Union with "actively assisting the aggressors in Korea on a scale which makes possible the continuance of that aggression " •U\'j*cdelegate Heni*y Cabot Lodge, Jr., told the JSI* State* Seneral assembly\'s political committee: The rulers of the Soviet Union can stop the war whenever they want to, and Mr. Vishinsky knows it 99 The U.S. representative led off the debate on the issue of Korean armistice as the second part of the seventh U.N. generaal assembly\'s first full day. Put before the 60-nation body ten facts which the assembly should face concerning Korea: "Fact No. 1 is that planning started the original aggression, which was subsequently maintained by Soviet training and equipment, notably Soviet air- ļ I craft, Soviet artillery, Soviet j T-34 tanks, and Soviet autoi matic weapons. "Fact No. 2 is that the North Korean forces which were virtu- \' ally destroyed after the Inchon landing in the fall of 1950 fled across the Yaul River, behind the screen of the Chinese communists who were then entering Korea. They reappeared on the battle front in later winter, reconstituted, reorganised, and almost completely equipped with weapons of Soviet manufacture. "Fact No. 3 is that the flow of Soviet equipment is constant and steady, accounts for | the increase of combat effec- ■ tiveness of the enemy over the past year. "Fact No. 4 is that the Chinese communists who entered the fighting with normal , light equipment are now fight- 1 ing with heavy equipment, ! supplied by the Soviet Union. ļ "Fact No. 5 is that naval i mines in large numbers which have been picked up or washed ashore off the Korean coast I have been mines of Soviet manufacture. "Fact No. 6 is that the pianos which the United |Na- i tions Command has been en- * countering over North Korea . have been MIG-15\'s and other aircraft, manufactured and sup- 1 plied by the Soviet Union. "Fact No. 7 is that recently United Nations forces have * encountered a new type of Soviet plane, the IL-28, which * is now a part of the air forces 1 of the communist aggressors 1 over North Korea. 1 "Fact No. 8 is that in spite e of heavy losses, the Chinese 1 communist and North Korean * air forces have grown until r they today have some 2500 air- c craft of which half are jets, all manufactured and supplied g by the Soviet Union. [ "Fact No. 9 is that the Soviet e Union provides the replacements a for these aircraft which United Nations forces shoot down over F North Korea. Our experts 0 estimate that the Soviet Union c has contributed in excess of a 4400 planes to fight against the t United Nations in Korea. "Fact No. 10 is that the anti- t aircraft guns in North Korea, including many which are radar controlled, are of Soviet origin." a Challenging the Soviet repre- c sentatives to disprove these ten v facts, Lodge declared: "the t whole world knows the truth; e that except for the active aid t i furnished to the North Korean i and Chinese communist aggre- d Soviet determined to carry on Korean war "to last Chinese" sors by the Soviet Union, the war in Korea would now be over. "Like the ostrich who sticks His head in the sand and thinks thnt no one sees him, the rulers of the Soviet Union are so bemused by their own propaganda that they do not recognise the exent to which world opinion holds them responsible for aiding and abetting this great crime against the peace of the world." The U.S. delegate recalled that on last February 4, Chinese communist foreign minister Chou En-Lai spoke of the Korean war and said that it "has greatly reinforced the strength and influence of the world camp of peace and democracy headed by the Soviet Union." Lodge called Chou\'s words "an unwittingly candid admission ... as to the identity of those whose words he heeds." "Mr. Chou En-Lai\'s description of the Korean war as a positive good to communism "hocks us," he said. "We hate it. We think Chinese people would repudiate it if they were free to do so." Lodge added: "Apparently, the Soviet Union, with its own special brand of magnanimity and generosity, is determined to carry on the war to the last Chinese." The American representative described the words of the Chinese communists and the actions of the Soviet Union as "a monstrous answer" to the assembly\'s adoption of |the Indian resolution on the Korean question. For these reasons, he said, "there is little point in reformulating at this session the principles on which that resolution was based." Lodge said that every element of American life is eager for peace. "It was for that reason," he explained, "that my predecessors at the last assembly voted for the Indian resolution — a resolutior which spared no effort to meet the issues; which declared unequivocally for peace; which was passed by a vote of the general assembly which was overwhelming. "If ever an action represented the conscience of the world and the striving of suffering humanity for peace it was the passage of that resolution. "It seemed impossible that there could be rulers in this world who would object to this! offer, but it soon appeared that] there were. The regimes of China and of North Korea have turned it down, and. of course, when they did it, they must have known that they were telling the world irj effect; \'we wish to continue the bloody struggle in Korea; and therefore, tell you that you can only I solve the Korean problem on our terms.\' "We, who are determined to end the war on a just basis, must also note this development with regret and seek the j reasons for it."', '"Sacrifices in Korean War justified" U.S. Secretary of State, John Foster Dulles, said last ?ek that the outcome of the Korean war justified the :rifices made. Addressing the American Legion convention and a nation-wide radio audience, the Secretary of State said: "This administration intends to act realistically to win the battle for peace. "Since there was a war in Korea, it was vital that the outcome should justify the sacrifice made. It was important to establish the fact that aggressors, hostile to the Free World cannot go on enlarging themselves by the conquest of small nations until they become bloated with power. This fact has been established in Korea. "The aggressor, which initially overran all of Korea except a small beachhead at Pusan, has been thrown back to and beyond his point of beginning. He now controls 1,500 fewer square miles than when he started, and has incurred casualties totalling about 2,000,000. The cost to the aggressor has been colossal. HLs gains have been nil. "Some persons seem to ieel that our men who fought in Korea fought uselessly. That is a cruel mis-statement of the situation. The troops rolled back aggression in Korea. Forces, which, if unchecked, would have gone on to imperil the United States. "The terms of the Korean armistice also established another principle of great importance to us. The right of enemy prisoners to enjoy political asylum. "In Europe, after the end of World War 11, many who were claimed by the Soviets were forcibly returned, except those who elected to commit suicide as a preferable fate. The communists wanted the Korean armistice to elevate that practice into generally accepted International law. We refused. "This time, no Red Army prisoners who want freedom will be sent back to captivity. A new and healthy principle has been established. As a result, from now on, the Red Armies will be less dependable as tools of aggression. "When I was in Freedom Village last month meeting returning prisoners, there were reports that the communists intended to require a number of of men to serve r>rison sentences. The communist official radio was declaring that Geneva Convention gave them this right. "We have now received word from General Clark that the Military Armistice Commission, representing the two sides, has agreed that all captured personnel who desire repatriation shall be returned without regard to alleged offences. This is good news. We on our side shall, of course, conform to this understanding. "Regarding deterrents to new aggression: We can all take satisfaction in the fact that President Eisenhower has found it possible to top the fighting on honorable terms. Now we turn to the political aspects of the problem. "I headed a delegation that went to Korea last month to discuss these maters with President Syngham Rhee. We acted with a background of regular consultation with Congressional leaders, both Democratic and Republican. The Republic of Korea and bur delegation then agreed on the terms of a security treaty. It will not, of course, be effective until the Senate gives its consent. "The proposed Korean security treaty is another step in the development of a Pacific security system. The treaty would complement the earlier treaties which I negotiated in 1951 with the Philippines, Australia, New Zealand and Japan. Also, the Korean treaty would prevent any recurrence of the enemy miscaluculation of 1950 which brought about the Korean war. The proposed new security treaty will constitute a clear warning. It will make i it unlikely that the Republic of Korea will be subjected to another act of unprovoked military aggression which would again involve the United States. "Since 1950, the forces of aggression have been supplied, equipped and protected by air . . . from unmolested bases in China, just north of the Yalu. If, however, the communists desire to resume the war, they now know that they could no longer count on this "privileged sanctuary." "Korea is the primary subject of the political conference. "The United States voted against extending an invitation to India. In doing so, we had the support of good friends. Also we regretfully split votes with some other good friends. In the end, India withdrew. "Our action involved no reflection upon India. This administration has great respect for India and we seek friendship with her. We have already shown that in unmistakable ways, and we intend to go on showing it. "But the forthcoming conference is a serious business. It is a prolongation of the struggle in which over 25.000 Americans laid down their lives, and in which nearly 125,000 more became casualties. We owe it to these Americans to be as disciplined in our political thinking as they were disciplined in the cause of liberty. "We have come to another conclusion regarding the post-Armistice conference. We shall not sit on it indefinitely. "There is a long record of communist negotiations which have been dragged out merely in order to give the communists a cover for achieving ulterior purposes. "We are always ready to negotiate in good faith. But we expect good faith to be mutual. We shall not lend ourselves to communist manoeuvers designed to win their ends through guile. "So, if the Korean conference discusses Korea for three months without making genuine progress toward the settlement of the Korean business, we shall pause to take stock of the situation. We shall consult with the Republic of Korea and with our other friends. If, as a result of these consultations, we conclude that the conference is serving no useful purpose, we shall expect to withdraw from the conference. "We believe that, in negotiating with the communists, we should always have in mind a terminal point. "We seek no pretext for turning Korea into a United States base on the Asia mainland. We seek dnly the long-proclaimed goal of the United Nations, namely, the peaceful unification of Korea under a representative form of government. "A single Chinese communist aggressive front extends from Korea on the north to Indochina in the south. The armistice in Korea, even if itj leads to a political settlement in Korea, does not end United States concern in the western Pacific area. As President Eisenhower said in his April 16 speech, a Korean armistice would be a fraud if it merely released communist forces for attack elsewhere. "In Indochina a desperate struggle is in its eighth year. The outcome affects our own vital interests in the western Pacific, and we are already contributing largely in material and money to the combined efforts of the French and of Vietnam Laos and Cambodia. "There is the risk that, as in Korea, Red China might send its own army into Indochina. The Chinese communist regime should realise that such a second aggression could not occur without grave consequences which migh not be confined to Indochina. I say this soberly in the interest of peace and in the hope of preventing another aggression miscalculation. "We want peace in Indochina also. The political conference about to be held relates in the first instance to Korea. But growing out of that conference could come, if Red China wants it. an end of aggression and restoration of peace in Indochina. The United States would welcome such a development."', '52 UNITED NATIONS CO-OPERATE TO STOP AGGRESSION IN KOREA Amid the confusion and frustration surrounding the Korean peace talks the world can recall on United Nations Day, October 24, one fact about the Korean aggression which history will remember: That this is the first time in history when 52 nations of the world have risen in united action to defend one small nation against aggression. It has been undeniably established with this first United Nations war that "collective security" is no longer a dream, a promise or a hope. With the Korean war, it became a reality. Viewed in this light, June 25, 1950, may well become the most important date of the twentieth century. For it was on this day that the North Korean army invaded the Republic of Korea. It was on this day that the United Nations Commission in Korea gravely wired Secretary-General Trygve Lie: "Commission wishes to draw attention to serious situation developing . . . assuming !| character of full scale war and may endanger the maintenance of international peace and security." A.nd, it was on this day that the Security Council of the United Nations by a 9/0 vote promptly placed the blame on North Korea and asked that no help be given North Korea but that all help be given to the United Nations in order to stop this aggression. First to respond to the UN request was the United States which two days later on June 27, 1950, | put United States troops at the disposal of the United Nations to help South Korea. By July 14, 1950, Tryve Lie announced that 52 : nations had endorsed a U.S.-spon- i sored resolution (June 27) recom- ; mending immediate military mea- \\ sures by the United Nations to ļ restore peace in Korea. By autumn 17 nations had contributed direct military aid in the form of planes, warships and troops. These nations were: Australia, Belgium, Canada, Colombia, Ethiopia, France, Greece, Luxembourg, Netherlands, New Zealand, Thailand, Philippines, Republic of Korea, Turkey, Union of South Africa, United Kingdom and the United States. While the rest of the 52 nations have contributer great quantities of money, blood plasma, military bases, food, clothing, rice, sugar, lumber and in a number of cases have offered troops which have not yet been used. These contributions to the United Nations\' cause in Korea have come from almost every land and every race; from such widely separated areas, for instance, as Sweden, Costa Rica, Iceland, Israel, Lebanon, Argentine, Liberia, Pakistan, India and Free China. Such a tremendous step, of course, was not taken ill-advisedly or hastily. Korea had long been the subject of dispute and disagreement. A short history of the Korean situation shows that as far back as the Cairo Conference (December, 1943) and the Potsdam Declaration (July 26, 1945) the great powers had agreed upon Korean independence from Japan. Yet, when Japan surrendered in 1945, temporary military expediency divided Korea at the 38th parallel, with the Soviets occupying the northern half (August 12, 1945) and the United States troops occupying the south (September 12, 1945). Real disagreement came when, despite an agreement (Moscow, December, 1945) between the USSR, the United States and Britain to set up a provisional Korean democratic government, the Soviets began to spurn or shackle all efforts to give Korea either unity or independence. In November, 1947, for instance, the United Nations called for a general election in Korea supervised by the United Nations Temporary Commission on Korea (UNCOK). Under the plan, the representatives\' elected would have constituted a National Assembly, which with UN help would be [charged with the drafting of a democratic constitution for a national government. The Soviet Union, however, frankly stated that Korea was none of the UN\'s business, and refused to admit UNCOK north of the 38th parallel. Thus, the May 10, 1948, elections were confined to South Korea, where the government of the United States assisted the United Nations programme. North of the 38th parallel, the Soviets set up their one-party communist government which they called the "Democratic People\'s Republic of Korea," and which claimed jurisdiction over all Korea. This regime lived, as it was created, in complete defiance of the United Nations. The United Nations had further trouble with the Soviet Union in 1948 over the matter of the withdrawal of troops from Korea. The United Nations had asked (December 12, 1948) that all foreign troops be withdrawn from Korea as early as practicable. The United States compiled by June 29, 1949, and threw its zone open to UN inspection. The Soviets, however, while claiming to have withdrawn its troops in December, 1948, refused to open its zone for USSR at the 38th parallel, and an iron curtain was drawn by the United Nations inspection. Thus, what had been a temporary line on a map, the Soviet Union now made a permanent barrier. In co-operation with the United Nations, the United States had meanwhile not only tried to unite Korea, but had appropriated more than 623 million dollars for Korea\'s welfare. This included 356 million dollars! in |funds |made available for aid in 1946-49 and 110 million dollars in Economic 110 million dollars in Economic Co-operation A d m i n i stration (Marshall Plan) funds in 1950. These funds were for public health, schools, communications, roads and for a constabulary force to aid the Koreans in controlling i border incidents caused by Communist guerillas in the North. This is how things stood until June 25, 1950, when the puppet. army of the satellite, North Korea, suddenly invaded the Republic of Korea. At the time, the North Koreans glibly placed the blame of the agression on the South, but the UN Commission on Korea, which only two days before the attack had made a complete tour of the South, was in a position to know the truth. The Commission, in fact, had reported that "the South Korean Army is organized entirely for defence, and is in no condition to carry out an attack." Later, documentary proof was discovered when the United Nations troops captured two North Korean ; Army documents: one document ; was a reconnaissance order which gave detailed instructions on the "enemy" before and during the attack, was dated June 18, 1950, seven days before the attack; and the other document captured was the battle plan for the invasion of South Korea written in Russian. Warren Austin, United States delegate to the United Nations, at a UN session on September 18, 1950, ; exhibited Soviet-made weapons ; captured from Communist troops. North Korean and Chinese Communist planes are also of Soviet design and manuufacture. The Korean crisis, of course, is , not yet over. And, the truce talks from time to time bog down in what seems hopeless frustration. Yet, for history, the real meaning of the Korean conflict is the fact that it was declared in the first place. The great meaning lies in the fact that men of many nations, races and religions are willing to unite for peace and fight aggression. No longer merely a pious hope among men, worldwide collective security is at long last a reality.']

Representative_Docs: ['5.000 gather at Latvian Monument of Freedom In Riga for first peaceful non- Communist nationalist demonstrat lon In Soviet history. On Sunday. June 14, nearly 5,000 Latvians gathered at the Monument of Freedom In Riga to honor the victims of mass Soviet deportations. Although there was a heavy police presence, the solemn, peaceful ceremony took place without incident. It is the first such openly organized demonstration to take place in Soviet occupied Latvia and the largest known peaceful. non-Communist political gathering in the history of the Soviet Union. The demonstration had been announced two weeks earlier by the Latvian human rights group Helsinki 86. After word of the planned demonstration spread throughout the country, leaders of Helsinki 86 were called into the Soviet Army reserve and Riga officials hastily scheduled a child mi\'s Festival near the monument, in an attempt to block access to the site. Nevertheless, at 4PM Sunday, several hundred people gathered at nearby Bastej Kalns park. Leading Latvian political activists addressed the crowd, demanding the end of Russification in Latvia and the release of political prisoner Gunars Astra. The group was also addressed by an Estonian nationalist. By 6:30 the crowd had swelled to over 1.500 people. Garbed in native folk dress, Eva Bitenieks. 19, daughter of Helsinki 86 founder, Raimonds Bitenieks, and Rolands Silaraups. 21, lead the group in a procession to the Freedom Monument. Crowds lining the street applauded the marchers. When they reached the monument they were greeted by several thousand others who had filled the square and surrounding parks. Although police and KGB agents were present, they restricted their activities to photographing and videotaping the participants. A National Re-awakening At the monument, a sign was placed at its base that read. "In Memory of the Victims of June 14." An aisle was formed in the crowd leading to the monument. Over the next hour a steady stream of people solemnly walked down the aisle and heaped flowers at the base of the monument. A banner placed atop the flowers read. "For Fatherland and Freedom". A second displayed the words. "God Bless Latvia", which is also the title of Independent Latvia\'s national anthem, banned in the country since the Soviet occupation of 1940. *t one point, loudspeakers, erected nearby for the children\'s festival, began blaring pop music toward the solemn gathering at the monument. The crowd began to sing "Put Vejin" (Blow Wind), a highly emotional national song, successfully drowning out the loudspeakers. The gathering broke up peacefully, although people continued to linger in the square and place flowers at the monument late into the night. At IAM the police finally began clearing the area and detained 11 Individuals for minor offenses. That night. Riga TV showed footage of the demonstration, although news commentators claimed the large group had gathered to honor Communist war veterans. In a TV interview. Anatolijs Gorbunovs, member of the Latvian Communist Party Central Committee, admitted that an "extremist group" known as Helsinki 86 did exist in Latvia, but claimed that it had a following of only 200 people. The next day. individuals continued to place flowers at the monument. By evening, groups of Latvian youths gathered in the nearby park and sang Latvian folk songs. Several more were detained by police and a water cannon was brought in to clear the square and surrounding parks of people.', 'US Congress and Latvians face? off against Soviets on the «ost historic November 18th in 69 years. With the two successful mass demonstrations behind then and the unanimous support of the U.S. Congress giving them courage, the people of Latvia were determined to publically observe Latvian Independence Day for the first time since the Soviet occupation over 4 decades ago. The Soviets were equally determined to stop them, and put thousands of armed Soviet militiamen, plainclothes KGB agents, civilian vigilantes and police dogs between the Latvian people and their goal. Perhaps it didn\'t happen in they way they had hoped, but Latvians did succeed in making this November 18, 1987 one that will never be forgotten. Many details remain to be confirmed, but here is a preliminary report of what happened: An angry I8th SEE YOU ON THE 18TH! The first indication that Latvians intended to publically observe Latvian independence day on November 18 came as early as August 23. As the crowd of 10.000 people who had gathered at the Monument of Freedom in KĪga that day broke up, people waved to one another and shouted, "See you again on the 18th!" Although the Latvian human rights group HELSINKI 86 had called for the June 14 and August 23 protests, they hesitated on taking a position on the 18th. Their leadership had been decimated by expulsions and arrests, and they were reluctant to urge others to do something they themselves could not participate in. There was also a new element of danger. While the Soviets reluctantly tolerated the previous two rallies, November 18th was out of the question. To commemorate the founding of the independent Latvian republic a republic destroyed by the Soviets was treason in the eyes of Moscow. It could not and would not be tolerated. THREATS. WARNINGS AND OMINOUS RUMORS The Soviet campaign to nip any Nov. 18 rally in the bud began as early as September 17 when Anatolijs Gorbunovs. a high ranking Latvian Communist Party leader warned that approaching the Monument of Freedo. on Nov. 18 would be viewed as an "anti Soviet act. This warning was repeated over and over again, in the press, in schools and workplaces. As Nov. 18 approached the pressure grew. Parents were warned that they would be held responsible for their children on this day Students were asked to sign documents promising they would not participate. Vigilantes, called "citizen security squads" were recruited among loyal Communists in the country. Rumors, possibly initiated by the KGB, indicated that ex cons, Afghan war veterans and an assortment of thugs were being brought into Latvia and were being trained in small scale terrorist tactics. The word quickly spread that "blood would flow" if the Latvians tried to come out on the streets that day. While some elderly Latvians, especially those who had survived the Stalin era, grew apprehensive and feared a return to bloody repressions. Latvian youth became defiant. The full-scale Soviet effort to thwart a Latvian display of nationalism merely angered and inflamed tJieir passions. "Making some kind of a statement" on November 18 became a «atter of national pride. THE US CONGRESS STEPS IN While tensions continued* to grow in Latvia, members of the United States Senate and Congress decided to take action. The Soviets were put on notice that their actions in Latvian would not go unnoticed. Cong. Uoug Bereuter (R NE) was joined by Dennis Hertel (D MI) and Don Ritter (R PA) in introducing H. Con. Res 209. which expressed support for the right of the Latvian people to peacefully observe their national day. In the Senate, stalwart Baltic defender Don Riegle (D-MI) was joined by Bob Dole (R KS). Robert Byrd (D WV) and Paul Simon (D IL) in introducing a similar resolution. Both resolutions passed by unanimous consent. This action, which could be considered provocative by some, was historic in its own right. It not only had unanimous bi-partisan support, but its co-sponsors included Presidential candidates from both parties, Dole and Simon. Prior to passage of the House resolution, coutless legislators took the stand to eloquently defend it. Among them were the leading human rights advocates in the country: Tom Lantos (D-CA), Steny Hoyer (D-MU), Henry Hyde (R-IL). Gerald Solomon (X NY) and John Miller (R W A). One Congressman, James Bilbray (D NY), was so outraged by Soviet plans to thwart the Nov. 18 demonstration, that he suggested the US Congress send an official delegation to Riga that day to observe the events. He even applied to the Soviet Embassy in Moscow for special permission to make a 48 hour visit to Riga MOSCOW SEETHES All of this was too much for Moscow. Knowing that the Latvian people were aware of all this support through Voice of America and Radio Free Europe broadcasts, they lashed out in anger at "U.S. interference in Soviet affairs." Loyal Communists stood up in meet ings in Riga and condemned the United States. With teachers standing over them, students were forced to sign petitions protesting the Congressional resolutions. Similar petitions appeared outside of workplaces in the hands of Party loyalists. Soviet anger was so great, they sent a delegation of Latvian Communist Party leaders, complete with a Soviet TV crew, to the US Embassy in Moscow, where they planned to lodge a protest with US Ambassador Jack Matlock. They should have known better They were never allowed to see Matlock. After standing on the sidewalk outside the embassy for 45 minutes an embassy official came out and gave the delegation a >rief political and history lesson. He explained the \'.S. nonrecognition policy toward the illegal Soviet annexation of the Baltic States. He said they were olcome to come in as private individuals and talk bout what they wanted. But anyone claiming to be an ■fficial representative of the government of "Soviet atvia" was not welcome. For reasons that are hard to fathom, TASS and Soviet TV later angrily reported this embarassing rebuff froi the US Ambassador. Everyone in the Soviet Union learned what happened. One Latvian in Rīga was asked how Latvians reacted to this incident. "We laughed" he said. NO ROOM AT THE INN The Soviets were not laughing. The Moscow incident, coupled with the Congressional resolutions, had generated a high degree of interest in the world press. A story was brewing and Riga was the place to be. The Soviets responded to this by trying to close off Riga to the outside world. Moscow correspondents were told that Riga\'s hotels were packed and trains were sold out. "There\'s no room in the city," they were told. Oddly enough, it was reported that on November 17, tourists were being bundled up and rushed out of Riga in an attempt to empty the Intourist hotels of potential eyewitnesses. (Perhaps the rooi was needed for the militia and vigilante forces that were being brought in.) NOVEMBER 18 – A STATE OF UNDECLARED MARTIAL LAI» The first reports from RTga came as early as widriight, the night of the 17th. As the first minutes of Latvian Independence Day ticked off, the first flowers already began to appear at the base of the Freedo» Monument This continued sporadically throughout the »orning. By 11.00 AM several hundred gathered at the «onuaent, prompting the militia to appear. One witness reports that around 40 people were arrested. There are also reports that several hundred people had been put under house arrest that same morning Two activist ministers. Juris Rubenis and Māris Ludviks were detained and later put under house arrest. Lidija Doronina was detained on the outskirts of RTga Roberts PoirTtis was taken off the street, bundled into a car and driven around for 8 hours. Janis Baikāns, acting leader of HELSINKI 86 was alsu detained. By noon, Riga came under the full trappings of an undeclared but otherwise comprehensive state of martial law. The city center was delcared a "green zone", meaning that unauthorized persons were subject to immediate arrest. Thousands of armed lili*l3 marched in and formed living barricades around the Freedom Monument. Lenin Monument, and National Theater. City cemetaries, including the gravesite of Latvia\'s first president, Jānis Čakste, were closed off to the public. Despite this extraordinary police presence, thousands of Latvians continued to gather near the Freedom Monument, milling around in the parks and the streets outside the double line of militia. Several eyewitness claim that as many as 7.000 people surrounded t c militia barricade. Some individuals tried to brea through but were stopped. A rumor spread through crowd that a US Congressman was about to «ake an appearance. (Cong. Bilbray never did receive an answer from the Soviet embassy, and thus could not go AN "OFFICIAL" DEMONSTRATION At 4:00 PM the officially sponsored and organized anti-American demonstration began at the Monument to the Red Riflemen near the river. Estimates range from 400 to 2.000 participants in this government-organized rally. These individuals too were surrounded by a phalanx of militia with police dogs. One observer claimed the militia seemed to be there to protect the Communist demonstrators from the Latvian onlookers. Several eyewitnesses claim that Latvian youth, outraged at the anti-American demonstration, tried to break through the militia to get at the Communist protestors. Fights broke out, women were thrown to the ground and during the arrests, many appeared to be injured. Based on information we have to date, this seemed to be the bloodiest confrontation of the day in Riga. PRISON VANS AT NIGHTFALL Sporadic incidents of violence seemed to pick at nightfall in various locations. One report, given by a Russian dissident in Moscow, claims that prison vans, loaded with people were seen rushing through RTga streets in the evening. Other public vehicles had also been commandeered to handle the apparently large load of prisoners. VIOLENCE IN LIEPĀJA RTga was not the only site of unrest in Latvia on November 18. According to Eva Biteniece, daughter of HELSINKI 86 member Raimonds Bitenieks and participant in the June 14 rally, approximately 900 Latvians held a solemn procession to the North Cemetary in the Latviaji port city of Liepāja. (f.iepa\'ja is the birthplace of HELSINKI 86 and its founders, Linards Granting, Martins Bariss and Bitenieks) While the procession began peacefully, at one point a young Latvian emerged from the crowd waving a huge ■aroon and white flag of independent Latvia. The • ilitia closed in to arrest him and bedlam ensued. There were bloody battles between Soviet militia and Latvians. According to Biteniece, 3 busloads of arrestees were taken from the scene. SUMMARY The above reports were all received within hours of the demonstrations. Some came from Latvian eyewitnesses in the city, including Georgs Rubenis, father of the Rev. Juris Rubenis. Despite Soviet attempts to put a news blackout on Riga, some Western reporters, including Washington Post Moscow correspondent, Gary Lee, were able to report on the events. (Lee was present in RTga at the Jūrmala Conference last year and has interviewed Rolands Silaraups in Moscow.) Nevertheless. Soviet efforts to stifle the rally and li«it reporting did succeed in part. HELSINKI 86 »e»bers. who coordinated reports on the previous rallies, were under house arrest or otherwise unable to reach the West. It is also not know if any Western tourists were in the city. If so, it is hoped that in UH coming weeks we will get a fuller picture of what tQok place on this historic day.', 'A CHRONOLOGY OF EVENTS IN LATVIA -1988 The political and social upheaval that took place in Latvia in 1988 actually began in 1987, when the Latvian human rights group \'Helsinki 86\' staged the first unofficial public demonstration at the Latvian Freedom Monument in June. By August of last year mass rallies occured in all three Baltic capitals, also in defiance of official wishes. While these events were unprecedented, not much else had changed in Latvia when 1988 began. \'Helsinki 86\' was the only openly active political group in Latvia and was denounced by the authorities as a radical extremist group. The Latvian national flag and hymn were outlawed. The Latvian language had no official status. Latvian newspapers faithfully followed the Party line and the Latvian people had little say in their own affairs. In the beginning of 1988, the four decade-long Soviet stranglehold on Latvia began to lose its grip. Here\'s a brief summary of what happened: January The Latvian Communist Party was shocked to learn that one of their own, Dr. Juris Vidiņs\', chief physician of the city of Rēzekne, had joined \'Helsinki 86. VidiņŠ was subsequently fired from his job and thrown out of the Party. Shortly thereafter, \'Helsinki 86\' submitted a comprehensive 10-point proposal to the Latvian Communist Party, calling for radical measures to restructure the Latvian economy, preserve the Latvian language, establish an independent press and protect religious and minority rights. February Gunārs Astra, Latvia\'s foremost human rights activist was released from prison after spending 19 years in Soviet prison camps. Astra had once been denounced by Soviet Latvian authorities as "the Andrei Sakharov of Latvia." March \'Helsinki 86\' leader and founder, Linards Grantiņs was forced to emigrate to the West after serving a 6-month jail term. Grantins chose Dr. Juris Vidiņs to replace him as the head of the human rights group. However, in an unprecedented display of \'glasnost\', a Latvian newspaper revealed that over 43,000 Latvians had been deported by Stalin in 1949. In a ceremony supported by the Latvian Writer\'s Union and sanctioned by authorities, over 15,000 Latvians participated in a March 25 memorial service for the victims of the Stalinist deportations. April During a visit to Leningrad, Gunārs Astra took ill and subsequently died on April 6 following a major heart operation. His wife questioned the circumstances of his death and asked for a Western pathologist to perform an autopsy, but her request was denied. On April 19 over 8,000 people attended his funeral, an extremely emotional event that was highlighted by the singing of the banned Latvian national anthem and the draping of the outlawed Latvian flag over his coffin. Six days later over 12,000 participated in a rally to protest proposed plans to build a subway in Riga. The rally was organized by the Latvian Environmental Protection Club (YAK). The YAK had existed quietly for several years and had a large following among Latvian youth, but this was their first mass public rally. May A Latvian Communist Party official revealed that Latvians now comprised only 47% of Latvia\'s population – the first time in their history Latvians had become a minority in their own homeland. June An explosive month that broke many taboos and shook the very foundations of Communist Party rule in Latvia. On June 1 the Latvian Writer\'s Union held a historic meeting where among other things, Latvian TV commentator Mavriks Vu If sons told a shocked crowd, including Party leaders, that Soviet rule in Latvia came about in 1940 not through a popular revolution, but instead a Stalinist occupation. A week later the Writer\'s Union passed a dramatic resolution asking that Latvia be made a sovereign state with its own citizenship, military units, foreign ministry and national symbols. The NY Times described the resolution as "one of the boldest appeals ever issued by an official Soviet group." On June 14, 50,000 Latvians gathered in Riga to honor the victims of the Stalinist deportations in 1941. A year earlier 5,000 Latvians had defied Soviet authorities by holding the first such rally at the request of \'Helsinki 86. This year, the rally was approved by authorities. The rally, however, turned into a massive nationalist demonstration, as speakers called for Latvian sovereignty and carried signs denouncing Soviej rule in Latvia. For the first time since the Soviet occupation, the outlawed maroon and white Latvian flag was unfurled at a public rally and displayed at the Freedom Monument. Later the flag carrier led thousands of Latvians in an emotional procession through the streets the Riga. Within days, the Latvian Communist Party held a stormy meeting where the leaders and events of June 14 were denounced by top Party officials. Unknown to the participants, their secret meeting was recorded and transcripts were released to the West, revealing that Latvia\'s top Party bosses were outraged by the new openness Gorbachev had imposed on their republic. On June 21, a coalition of 17 nationally known Latvian Party members, intellectuals, clergymen and Political activists announced plans to form a "Latvian opular Front", which would seek to support and implement the resolutions of the Latvian Writer\'s Union. July The momentum begun in June continued building in July as a former underground group known as the \'Latvian National Independence Movement" went public and put forth 20 demands, including an end to the Russification of Latvia, independent political parties and a re-establishment of a sovereign Latvia. On July 13 a Baltic folk festival in Riga turned into a major nationalist rally as the outlawed flags of independent Latvia, Lithuania and Estonia are prominently displayed at all concerts and events. Three days later 30,000 Latvians gathered at an outdoor amphitheater to demand the legalization of the former Latvian flag. Speakers included representatives from Armenia and the Crimean Tatars as well as Lithuania and Estonia. A week later 4,000 flag carrying supporters of the Latvian Environmental Protection Club disrupted an official Party rally organized to commemorate the 48th anniversary of Soviet rule in Latvia. Many of the Russian workers that were bussed in to till out the crowd of the official rally fled at the approach of the Latvians. Pressured by the crowd, the Party officials reluctantly allowed Arvids Ulme, president of the environmental group to address the rally. In front of the Party bosses, Ulme blamed Latvia\'s government for all the country\'s problems. August Having already conceded that Stalinist deportations had indeed occured in in the Baltic States, Soviet authorities were now forced to reckon with the 1939 Stalin-Hitler Pact that paved the way for the Soviet occupation of the Baltic States. On August 23rd, the 49th anniversary of the signing of the pact, over 50,000 Latvians held a rally in Riga, waving Latvian flags and demanding an anullment of the pact. During the rally, a letter sent by 27 U.S. Senators to the Baltic people was read out loud to the crowd. The letter, originating from the office of Sen. Don Riegle (D-MI) had been written in Latvian, Lithuanian and Estonian and had been sent to the three Baltic capitals prior to the rallies. Later that month, \'Helsinki 86\' Juris Vidiņs went to Moscow to meet with U.S. Ambassador to the USSR, Jack Matlock, to discuss developments in Latvia. September On Sept. 3 a massive anti-pollution rally took place in all three Baltic States along the shores of the Baltic Sea. In Latvia alone over 45,000 people formed a human chain over 36 kilometers long. The rally was organized by the Environmental Protection Qub and was supported by international environmentalists in Europe and the U.S. Plans were also being made for the founding congress of the Latvian Popular Front, which was scheduled for early October. In a vote of 8-6, the organizing committee agreed to invite representatives of Western Latvian groups to the congress. October Throughout the year, Soviet authorities in Latvia had backed off each time they were challenged by the nationalistic fervor of the masses. In October the old Party leadership was pushed right out of the picture. Just days prior to the founding congress of the Latvian Popular Front, Moscow replaced the First Secretary of the Latvian Communist Party and the President of Supreme Soviet of Latvia with "reform-minded" Latvians. The Latvian Supreme Soviet then legalized the formerly outlawed Latvian flag and made Latvian the "official" language of Latvia. On Oct. 7 over 150,000 Latvians held an dramatic rally in Riga to celebrate the founding of the Latvian Popular Front. Over the next two days the newly formed Popular Front worked out a far-reaching program, calling for economic sovereignty, environmental protection, greater civil rights, an end to immigration, a free press and freedom of religion. This last demand was dramatically realized when authorities gave the Popular Front permission to use the historic Dom Cathedral in Riga for a church service to "bless" the new movement. This was the first religious service in over 30 years in the Cathedral, which had been converted to a concert hall by the Soviets. Despite all these advances, opposition, this time coming from the Russian migrants in Latvia, began taking shape. An "International Front" of mostly Russians was formed to oppose the Latvian Front. Reports of Russian gang attacks oh Latvian youths in Riga also increased. November As Latvians formed their Popular Front, similar mass movements also emerged in Estonia and Lithuania. In October representatives of all three movements met in Riga to develop a joint strategy. On Nov. 11 the Latvian mass rally reached a new pinnacle when an estimated 200,000 people flooded Riga to commemorate \'Lāčplēsis Diena\' [Bearslayer\'s Day], to honor the veterans of Latvia\'s 1918-19 War of Independence. On that day the maroon and white flag of independent Latvia was ceremoniously raised atop the Riga Castle, where authorities agreed it could now remain \'forever*. Another Soviet taboo was smashed on Nov. 18, when hundreds of thousands of Latvians throughout the country celebrated Latvian Independence Day. Just one year earlier thousands of Soviet miltia had been stationed in Riga to block any ceremonies on this day. In 1988 Latvian Independence Day was celebrated nationwide. Most noteworthy was an official Popular Front sponsored concert, which was held in the Latvian National Theater, site of the signing of the Latvian Declaration of Independence in 1918. During November several Latvian informal groups, including the Popular Front, issued statements condemning proposed changes in the Soviet constitution which would reduce local rule in the Baltic republics. Unlike the Estonian Supreme Soviet, which openly defied Moscow and declared sovereignty, the Latvian \'parliament* took a more conciliatory position. It did so at the urging of its new President, Anatolijs Gorbunovs, who had just returned from a meeting with Soviet officials in Moscow. Nevertheless, Latvians joined Estonian and Lithuanians delegates in protesting constitutional changes at the congress of the Supreme Soviet in Moscow. In 11 months several remarkable things had occured. The traditional symbols of independent Latvia, long banned under Soviet rule, become legal again. The Stalinist crimes in the Baltic States were openly discussed and condemned in the Latvian press. Journalists and historians even began to challenge the legality of Soviet rule in the Baltic States – and their comments were openly published and broadcast for all to hear. The Latvian human rights group \'Helsinki 86\', once viewed as a radical extremist group, seemed almost establishment in comparison to the new nationalist groups that had emerged during the year. Ironically, the \'radical\' proposals it presented to the Communists in January, were the foundation of the demands of the Latvian Popular Front in October. Many in the West have attributed all these changes to Gorbachev\'s policy of \'glasnost\'. While partially true, it should be noted that all the gains made by the Latvians in 1988 came about through the will of the people. The people were not \'given\' their flag, anthem or language – they demanded it. They were not told to hold raļfies – they simply took the streets and forced the authorities to accept them. The Communists did not create the Latvian Popular Front, but simply joined it when they realized they could not stop it. Gorbachev tried to create a revolution from above – the Baltic people ran way ahead of Gorbachev and initiated it, far beyond Gorbachev\'s expectations, from below. 1988 is now over...stay tuned for 1989. The Chicago Latvian Newsletter would like to thank the American Latvian Association and the World Federation of Free Latvians for providing information used in this article. We\'d also like to thank the people of Latvia, Lithuania and Estonia for having the courage to do the things that we\'ve written about']
