Representative_Docs: ['News from the Front On October 8, 1988 the Latvian Popular Front held its founding congress in Riga, Latvia. The «ass movement claimed a following of over 130,000 people and made sweeping demands for greater autonomy from MoscowHow It began The concept of the Latvian Popular Front (LPF) was first proposed on June 21. 1988 by 17 well-known figures in Soviet occupied Latvia, representing a broad cross section, of Latvian society. What made this initiatory group unique was a membership that included leaders of informal Latvian organizations such as HELSINKI 86, the Environmental Protection Club (YAK) and the Lutheran Movement for Rebirth & Renewal, as well as Latvian cultural figures who were Communist Party members in good standing. The initiatory group criticized the existing Soviet-controlled government in Latvia, and called for sweeping reforms in the country\'s political, economic, social and cultural\' life. They emphasized that solutions to the nation\'s problems would have to come from the "people", for Latvia\'s present government had proven itself incapable of ruling. A program and party support As similar events unfolded in Estonia and Lithuania, the Communist authorities in Latvia jumped on the "Popular Front" bandwagon and extended its support. Janis Peters, head of the Latvian Writer\'s Union became actively involved in the Front and helped arrange plans for a founding congress in October. On September 8. the official organ of the Latvian Communist Party Youth League. \'Padomju Jaunatne\', published the proposed program of the LPF on its front page. The program called for "the radical reconstruction" of Latvian society, "radical reform of the political system" and the establishment of a "sovereign" Latvian republic within a Soviet federation. While the program did not openly call for a secession from the Soviet Union, it reflected, almost point for point, demands which had been proposed by the Latvian human rights group HELSINKI 86 over 9 months earlier. At that time, HELSINKI 86 and its demands were considered "extremist" by the authorities. Now, HELSINKI 86 demands and members were a leading force in the Latvian Popular Front Formal and informal fronts As Latvians prepared for the October founding congress, debates ensued over the significance of the LPF. Some nationalists expressed concern over the Communist Party participation in the Front, claiming that what had begun as a true "peoples movement" was now being coopted and controlled from above. Some groups, such as the Latvian National Independence Movement, agreed to participate in LPF activities, but chose not to become formal members, since their goal total political independence for Latvia – was not shared by LPF leaders. An "Informal Latvian Popular Front" was also formed, not to oppose the formal LPF but to serve as a faction within it. According to this group\'s members, "what the Party gives, it can also take away." Their goal was to serve as a parallel popular front independent of Party control or involvement. If the formal LPF ever were to collapse, they would continue its work. Guests from the West? In September, Valdis Pavlovskiy, president of the American Latvian Association was informed by HELSINKI 86 leader, Dr. Juris Vidins. that the LPF would be extending invitations to Western Latvian organizations to participate in the October founding congress. The LPF invited seven Western Latvian leaders, including Mr. Pavlovskis, Linards Lukss, president of the World Federation of Free Latvians and Latvian organization leaders from Australia. Great Britain, and West Germany. They were told that the invitations had been endorsed by Janis Peters and supported by Soviet authorities in Latvia. Travel arrangements were made and all systems were go for what would have been an historic event: the first visit of Western Latvian organization leaders to occupied Latvia. While Latvians on both sides of the Iron Curtain were largely united in support of this plan, Moscow apparently had other ideas. A week prior to the October 8 congress, the Western Latvians learned that their visa requests had been denied by the Soviet Union. The Party rushes in As popular support (and expectations) for the Oct. 8 congress grew, the Soviet authorities made some, dramatic moves to steal the thunder from the growing nationalist movement. On Oct. 4 the unpopular First Secretary of the Latvian Communist Party, Boris Pugo, was "promoted" to Moscow and replaced by Janis Vagris, an equally unpopular, longtime Party bureaucrat. Anatolijs Gorbunovs, a native Latvian who had gained popularity in the country in the last year for his support of Gorbachev\'s reforms and national rights, was appointed President of the Supreme Soviet of the Latvian SSR. With Gorbachev\'s ascenclon to the presidency in Moscow, the previously meaningless post was suddenly given stature and apparent signifiance in Latvia as well. The newly crowned Gorbunovs was being treated as the true leader of the country, while Party boss Vagris receded to the background. In a dramatic move to win popular support, on Oct. 6, the Supreme Soviet of the Latvian SSR, under Gorbunov\'s leadership, unanimously voted to make Latvian the official state language of Latvia. That same day, HELSINKI 86 leader Vidins. joined representatives from the Environmental Protection Club, the Lutheran Rebirth movement, and former political prisoner Juris Ziemelis, on a live radio talk show which was broadcast throughout Latvia. LPF leader Janis Peters also announced that a church service would be held on Oct. 9 in the Dom Cathedral to "bless" the LPF. The Dom Cathedral had been converted to a concert hall following the Soviet occupation of Latvia and had not been used as a church for over 3 decades. The movement gains mass On October 7, a day before the opening of the congress, a crowd exceeding 150,000 people filled the outdoor Neza Park amphitheatre to participate in a manifestation" to celebrate the founding of the Latvian Popular Front. In a scene comparable only to Latvian song festivals, dozens of regional choirs In their native folk dress filled the stage and led the massive audience in the singing of Latvia\'s most patriotic folk songs. Unlike recent song festivals, this huge rally was punctuated by dozens of maroon and white flags and fiery speeches pronouncing the rebirth of the sovereign Latvian nation. One of many huge banners in the crowd read. "LENIN RECOGNIZED US – STALIN OCCUPIED US – GORBACHEV ???????" The Front is founded ©n Saturday. October 8, 1,083 delegates convened in Riga to formally found the Latvian Peoples Front. Some reports claim that as many as 300 Western journalists were present. The tone for the meeting was dramatically set by Ēvalds Valters, a former Latvian Rifleman now in his 90 s, who quoted the Latvian poet Rainis. We have not come here to beg for what is ours, but to demand it!" The two-day meeting, compared by Western reporters to an American town hall meeting, was filled with speeches, debates over procedures and lively, sometimes raucous exchanges. "Everybody is still learning how to be democratic" said Sandra Kalniete, an LPF organizer Speakers advocated complete economic independence, the right to create Latvian currency, the right to establish independent relations with other countries and an end to the teaching of atheism. Laimonis Gaigals\', a Latvian filmaker told the delegates. "The time has come for us to take back control of our own land because the loss of a true Latvia is no longer a threat;\\t is a real and pressing d anger."', 'LATVIJAS REPUBLIKA DECLARATION OF THE SUPREME SOVIET OF THE LATVIAN SSR ON THE RENEWAL OF THE INDEPENDENCE OF THE REPUBLIC OF LATVIA The independent state of Latvia, founded on November 18, 1918. was granted international recognition in 1920 and became a member of the League of Nations in 1921. The Latvian Nation\'s right to self-determination was implemented in April, 1920. when the people of Latvia gave their mandate to the Constituent Assembly chosen by universal, equal, direct and proportional elections. In February, 1922. the Assembly adopted the Constitution of the Republic of Latvia, which is still in effect de Jure. The ultimatum of June 16. 1940. from the Stalinist government of the USSR to the Latvian Government, asking for its resignation, and the following military aggression, constitute international crimes, which resulted in the occupation of Latvia and the liquidation of its statehood. The new government of Latvia was formed by the dictate of the government of the USSR According to international law, this government did not represent the sxecutive authority of the sovereign Republic of Latvia, since it represented the interests of the USSR instead of those of Latvia. The elections of June 14 and 15. 1940, to the Parliament (Saeima) of occupied Latvia were held under conditions of political terror after an illegal and unconstitutional election law had been adopted. Of the 17 lists of candidates submitted, only one was permitted in the elections — the list of the Working People\'s Bloc. The pre-election platform of this bloc did not include any demand to establish Soviet power in Latvia or L.o join the Soviet Union. Moreover, the results of the elections were falsified. Thus, the Hegally and fraudulently formed Parliament did not represent the will of the Latvian people. It had no constitutional powers to change the governmental system and iquidate the sovereignty of Latvia. Only the people had the right to decide on these natters, but no referendum was held. Hence, according to international law, the ncorporation of Latvia into the Soviet Union is Invalid. Accordingly, the Republic of Latvia continues to exist de jure as a subject of international law and is recognized as such by more than 50 nations of the world. Taking into account the "Declaration on the Sovereignty of the Latvian State " idopted by the Supreme Soviet of the Latvian SSR on July 28. 1989; the "Declaration on he Question of the Independence of the Latvian State" adopted by the Latvian Supreme Soviet on February 15. 1990: and the "Appeal of the All-Latvia Meeting of People\'s Deputies" of April 21. 1990; Observing the will of the inhabitants of Latvia, which was unmistakably expressed yy the election to the Latvian Supreme Soviet of a majority of deputies who had scpressed the determination to restore the independence of the Republic of Latvia; Being determined to restore de facto the free, democratic and independent Republic of Latvia; The Supreme Soviet of the Latvian SSR decides: 1. To recognize the supremacy of the fundamental principles of international law over national law and to consider illegal the treaty of August 23. 1939 between the USSR and Germany, and the subsequent liquidation of the sovereignty of the Republic of Latvia on June 17. 1940. which was the result of Soviet military aggression. 2. To declare null and void from the moment of inception the decision of July 21. 1940. by the Parliament of Latvia: "On the Republic of Latvia\'s Joining the USSR." 3. To re-establish the authority of the Constitution of the Republic of Latvia, adopted by the Constituent Assembly on February 15. 1922. in the entire territory of Latvia. The official name of the Latvian state is "The Republic of Latvia." abbreviated l_ "Latvia." 4. Until the adoption of a revised constitution, to suspend the Constitution of the Republic of Latvia, except for the articles expressing the constitutional and legal foundation of the Latvian state, which, according to Article 77 of the Constitution, can be changed only by popular referendum: ARTICLE 1 — Latvia is an independent, democratic republic. ARTICLE 2 — The sovereign power of the Latvian state belongs to the people of Latvia. ARTICLE 3 — The territory of the Latvian state shall consist of Vidzeme, Latgale. Kurzeme and Zemgale, within the boundaries stipulated by International treaties. ARTICLE 6 ~ The Saeima is elected by universal, equal, direct and secret vote on the basis of proportional representation. The application of Article 6 will follow the renewal of those state and administrative structures of Latvia which guarantee free elections. 5. To set a transition period for the re-establishment of the de facto independence of the Republic of Latvia, which will conclude with the convening of the Parliament of Latvia. During the transition period, supreme state power in Latvia is held by the Parliament of Latvia. 6. During the transition period, to implement those constitutional and other legal acts of the Latvian SSR which are in effect in Latvia when this Declaration is adopted, insofar as they do not contradict Articles 1.2.3 and 6 of the Constitution of the Republic of Latvia. Conflicts of law are resolved by the Constitutional Court of Latvia. 7. To form a commission for revising the Constitution of the Republic of Latvia, so that it corresponds to the present political, economic and social situation in Latvia. 8. To guarantee citizens of the Republic of Latvia and those of other nations permanently residing in Latvia social, economic and cultural rights, as well as those political rights and freedoms which are defined in international human rights instruments. To apply these rights also to those citizens of the USSR who express the desire to continue living in the territory of Latvia. 9. To develop relations between Latvia and the USSR in accordance with the Peace Treaty between Latvia and Russia of August 11. 1920, which is still in force and which recognizes the independence of Latvia for all time. To establish a Government Commission for conducting negotiations with the USSR. This act takes effect from the moment of its adoption. A. Gorbunovs Chairman of the Supreme Soviet of the Latvian SSR. I. Daudišs Secretary of the Supreme Soviet of the Latvian SSR May 7, 1990. in Riga', 'LATVIAN POPULAR FRONT CALLS FOR INDEPENDENCE! News from the Front: * Popular Front of Latvia (LTF) calls for total political and economic independence * LTF joins with Estonian and Lithuanian movements at historic \'Baltic Assembly\' in Tallinn * LTF board discusses strategy with Western Latvians at summit meeting in France * LTF leaders speak out for Latvian independence The Popular Front of Latvia (LTF), a massive, officially sanctioned popular organization boasting the support of over 1/4 million Latvians, including many Latvian Communist Party members, has called for total political and economic independence for Latvia. The decision came on May 31, when the board of the directors of the LTF voted unanimously to issue an appeal to the Latvian people to support them in this departure from their previous program. At its inception, in October 1988, the LTF had spoken only of seeking Latvian sovereignty within a Soviet federation. The May 31 declaration (printed in full in this issue) states that growing opposition "from the center and internal reactionaries\' made sovereignty within the USSR unlikely. Therefore, the board will seek to establish total Latvian independence through a "parliamentarian struggle" to alter the existing political structure. The LTF call for total Latvian independence is all the more remarkable in that a majority of its board members are also members of the Latvian Communist Party. Since the founding of the LTF, independent LTF board members, representing the National Independence Movement of Latvia (LNNK) and Helsinki 86 have attempted to move the LTF board in a more radical direction. Some Western observers had expected a split between the so-called "Radical Union" of the LTF and the more moderate Communist Party members. Instead, it appears that the moderates have come over to the side of the radicals, forming a unified front in support of independence. Baltie Assembly calls for independence One explanation for the shift in the leadership of the LTF, is the Baltic Assembly, held in Tallinn, Estonia on May 13-14. During this historic two-day conference, leaders of the LTF joined with the Estonian Popular Front and the Lithuanian Reform Movement Sajudis, in approving a series of strong resolutions concerning self-determination in the occupied Baltic States. Among the resolutions adopted by the Baltic Assembly were: l]a call for independent Baltic States within a "neutral and demilitarized zone of Europe"; 2Ja declaration of Baltic economic independence 3]an appeal to the members of the Commission on Security and Cooperation in Europe, and the UN General Secretary calling for the Soviet Union to declare the 1939 Stalin-Hitler Pact null and void. Prior to the meeting of the Baltic Assembly, of the three \'officially sanctioned\' Baltic fronts, only the Lithuanian Reform Movement Sajudis had spoken of total independence. In Estonia and Latvia, the Fronts had taken more moderate positions on sovereignty within the USSR, leaving the calls for independence to unofficial groups like the Latvian LNNK and the Estonian Independence Party. As has routinely happened in the past, the Baits appear to have followed the lead of their boldest and most outspoken member, in this case Sajudis. Having endorsed the resolutions of the Baltic Assembly, the LTF seemed to have no choice except reflect those positions in their own program. Summit meeting in France While some members of the LTF leadership were meeting with Estonians and Lithuanians in Tallinn, other LTF board members met with Western Latvian leaders in France. During the week of May 14-18, leaders of the LTF, National Independence Movement of Latvia (LNNK) and the Rebirth Party of Latvia held formal talks with the board of directors of the World Federation of Free Latvians (WFFL) at the Latvian Cultural Center \'Abrene\', near Chinon, France. The Abrene Summit was the first formal meeting between the board of directors of the LTF and the WFFL. [The WFFL board included Latvians from the U.S., Canada, Australia, West Germany, Great Britain and Sweden] In addition to the WFFL other Western Latvian participants included representatives of the Latvian Social Democratic Party, Daugavas Vanagi, European Latvian Youth Association, Latvian Popular Front Support Groups of Canada and several Western academicians and community leaders. The two groups discussed possibilities of joint cooperation in political, economic and cultural activity. They also discussed the development of a coordinated global campaign to promote sovereignty, self-determination and democratic reforms in Soviet-occupied Latvia. The WFFL for instance, agreed to assist the LTF in achieving representation in such international forums as the United Nations, CSCE Conferences and the International Olympic Committee. Outspoken leadership Two leaders of the LTF, both of whom returned to Latvia from recent visits to the West, have distinguished themselves in the last month with powerful statements on behalf of Latvian independence. Dainis Ivans is president of the LTF and was recently voted "the most popular person in Latvia" by a poll taken by the magazine \'Liesma. Ivāns, a writer, environmental activist and Communist Party member, had just returned from Moscow where he served as a delegate to the National Congress of the USSR, and made a moving speech during a mass rally on June 14 in Riga. Said Ivans: The inevitable collapse of the empire cannot be stopped by clubs, shovels nor tanks, for although the old regime does have power and fanatical priests, it no longer has ideas which can unite and control independent thinkers. [This regime J also does not have the desire to divide power according to principles of decency, justice and equality; this can and must be done by us, in order to guarantee the physical, national, social, political, economic and ecological safety of Latvians and Latvia\'s national groups, so that we can protect our women and children in an independent Latvia. In Moscow we were convinced: freedom cannot be obtained by begging or asking – we must seize and realize it ourselves." 4 Valdis Steins, a former chairman of the LTF board, particpated in both the Ganonoque (Canada) and Abrene (France) summits with Western Latvians, and has been one of the strongest proponents of Latvian independence in the last month. On May 27, Steins spoke at the congress of the National Independence Movement of Latvia (LNNK) in Riga. He called for the formation of a commission, consisting of World War II allies (the USSR, US, France, Great Britain) that would draft an international treaty "concerning the restoration of a free, democratic and neutral Latvia." Steins also emphasized that Latvia\'s citizens alone had the right, to determine the fate of Latvia. According to Steins, the only legal citizens of Latvia were those "who were Latvia\'s citizens on June 17, 1940 and their progeny." Several Latvian groups have begun forming "citizens committees" of individuals who meet the above criteria. It is believed that these groups will then hold a referendum on Latvia\'s future status. Until now, the Popular Front of Latvia, based on the positions of the program which was ratified at its founding congress, has based its activities on the first alternative: the federative principle. However, the events of the last month testify that all Baltic and Latvian efforts to realize this goal have come up against growing political, economic and ideological opposition from the center and from reactionary internal forces, including the open use of brute force, as was demonstrated by the events of April 9 in Tbilisi. Even the developments at the USSR Congress of People\'s Deputies demonstrate that a conservative majority has formed, which has taken a position against the aspirations of the union republics for total national sovereignty. As a result, the Board of Directors of the Popular Front of Latvia has proposed that all branches and member organizations of the Popular Front of Latvia consider a question of vital importance to our people: should the Popular Front of Latvia join the struggle for Latvia\'s total political and economic independence? We see the future Latvia as a democratic nation, where all citizens, independent of their national or social membership, political and religious beliefs, are guaranteed equal rights. Latvia must be a humanitarian nation, ruled by law, economic growth, and the opportunity for broad spiritual and cultural development for all ethnic groups. Relations with other nations, including also the union republics, must be developed on the basis of treaties guaranteeing mutual benefits and equal rights. It is the view of the Board of the Directors of the Popular Front of Latvia, that the Latvian people themselves mustplay the determining role in achieving independence. The Board of Directors of the Popular Front of Latvia foresees the road toward achieving this goal, as one which would involve a parliamentarian struggle, utilizing and modifying the existing structure of the Latvian SSR, according to the demands of the time and the will of the people, as well as one which would involve direct manifestations of the popular will. The goal of the Popular Front of Latvia is to achieve Latvian national independence, and to achieve legal guarantees which would allow the people to choose for themselves the form and mechanism of political power. It must be understood, that the road toward achieving this goal will be difficult, and will demand political responsibility and unwavering dedication from all Latvian patriots, regardless of their national or social membersnip. Our immediate task is to ensure that the June 16 session of the Supreme Soviet of the Latvian SSR ratify a declaration for Latvian national sovereignty, and approve changes in the LaSSR Constitution concerning property rights in the Latvian SSR and ratification of USSR laws, so that the Supreme Soviet of the Latvian SSR can immediately approve a law concerning Latvia\'s economic autonomy. With a view that positive election results are the main prerequisite for paving the way to the development of political freedom and indeoendence, a coalition of all progressive forces must be achieved toward this end. The Board of Directors of the Latvian Popular Front perceives and understands that the idea of the creation of Latvian Citizens Committees is a result of the ultimate consequences of totalitarian activities and as a necessary tool for the protection of the rights of Latvians and other native residents. However, the expansion of activity by these committees will be necessary only then, if Latvia\'s right to national independence and self-determination cannot be guaranteed through parliamentarian measures. The Board of Directors of the Latvian Popular Front']

Name: 27_conference_american_association_congress
Representative_Docs: ["ALA in Chicago 36th annual American Latvian Association (ALA) Congress scheduled for April 30-May 3 in Chicago. Who runs the American Latvian Association? What do they do? What are their plans for the coming year? Chicago Latvians will have an ideal opportunity to get a firsthand answer to all these questions at the 36th Annual Congress of the American Latvian Association this April. Delegates representing nearly 200 secular and religious Latvian American organizations throughout the United States will be gathering at the Sheraton International near O'Hare airport April 30 through May 3. The yearly meeting is held to elect the board of directors, review activities of the last year and discuss plans for the coming year. Aristids Lambergs. president of ALA for the last two years, heads up the board and its various cultural, educational, informational and other bureaus. The business part of the Congress (reports, debates, etc) will be held in the Latvian language, although parts of the opening session will be in English. In addition to Chicago-area dignitaries, John Wallach, Foreign Editor of the Hearst Newspapers and Coordinator of last year's Chautauqua Conference in Jūrmala, Latvia, will address the group. The ALA is a non profit organization dedicated to representing the interests of the Latvian – American community, and promoting the preservation of the Latvian culture and language. The organization supports Latvian-language schools and cultural activities, provides scholarships to Latvian American students, publishes books and provides English-language information about the Latvian people to the U.S. government, press and public. Ilmārs Bergmanls, Vice President of the United Latvian Associations of Chicago, is chairman of the 36th Annual Congress Coordinating Committee. For additional in formation: – about the Congress in Chicago, contact Mara Erkmane. (312) 588-2085. – about hotel reservations, contact Sheraton International, (312) 297-1234. – about the American Latvian Association, contact Anita Tērauds. (301) 340-1914.", 'THE CHICAGO LATVIAN NEWSLETTER THE AMERICAN LATVIAN ASSOCIATION DEFINES PRIORITIES AT THE 46TH ANNUAL CONGRESS IN CHICAGO On May 2-4 in Chicago, the American Latvian Association (ALA) organized its 46th annual Congress. Ilmārs Bergmanis, head of the Chicago Latvian society, stated that even though the Latvian community has become smaller over the years people are still active in their communities. Latvian culture and traditions must be introduced into the rest of the world, and passed onto the next generation of Latvian leaders, he concluded. Jānis Kukainis, President of ALA, emphasized the themes of the Congress which were to uphold the Latvian culture in the U.S. and to educate American Latvians about Latvia. In the political realm, Kukainis focused on NATO enlargement and Baltic security as Latvia\'s priority. Kukainis stressed the need for Latvia to solve its own domestic problems. He stated the importance of accentuating the positive when addressing Latvia to the international world. The positive attributes being political stability and low inflation. Einaris Semanis, Counselor from the Latvian Embassy, read a letter from Ojars Kalnins, the Latvian Ambassador to the U.S. According to the Ambassador, NATO unites Europe and the U.S. and Latvia\'s focus is to advance its interests in European structures. The July NATO Summit in Madrid is the beginning of a process, where countries will be invited for accession talks, the Ambassador concluded. In late May, in a letter to Baltic American leaders, President Clinton reaffirmed his commitment to NATO enlargement. "I am committed to pressing our allies to assure that NATO\'s doors will not close behind its new members. NATO should remain open to Europe\'s democracies ready to share the responsibilities as well as the benefits of membership. No country outside NATO will have a veto; no nation will be automatically excluded from joining the June-July 1997 Vol. XXVI. No. 16 alliance. We must not allow a gray zone of insecurity or spheres of influence to reemerge anywhere in Europe." The question still remains, what is the strategy for including countries into the second wave of enlargement, what happens after the Madrid Summit? Thus far, the Administration\'s strategy includes enhancing Partnership for Peace (PfP) and continuing dialogue with countries. In the President\'s letter to Baltic American leaders he remarked about the NATO-Russia Charter. "The NATO-Russia partnership that I have in mind will not in any way damage the interests of other nations. In my trips to Central Europe I have learned one phrase in many languages that sticks in my mind: \'Nothing about us without us.\' This is a good and important principle that the United States respects." The Russia U.S. NATO Charter was signed by Presidents Clinton and Yeltsin on May 27. This is also non-binding, however it serves as a forum for Russia\'s participation with NATO, not in NATO. There is a section in the Charter that outlines the principles that NATO and Russia will base their principles, "respect for sovereignty, independence and territorial integrity of all states and their inherent right to choose the means to ensure their own security." At the conclusion of the ALA Congress, delegates decided to support first tier NATO enlargement for the Baltic countries. Following the ALA Congress, on May 23 in the U.S. Congress, Senator Slade Gorton (R-WA) introduced Senate Concurrent Resolution (SCR 29). This resolution recommends the integration of the Baltic countries into NATO at the earliest possible date. This nonbinding resolution expresses Congresses view that the Baltics are to be commended for their efforts in establishing democratic and economic reforms. This is the Senate version of Representative Gerald Solomon\'s (R-NY) House Concurrent Resolution 10. On June 11, the House of Representatives passed the European Security Act (H.R. 1758). This bill includes text from the Gorton and Solomon resolutions, which recommend Baltic integration into NATO. The European Security Act designates Romania, Estonia, Latvia and Lithuania as eligible to receive NATO transition funding under the program established under the NATO Act of \'94. The passage of the European Security Act in the House demonstrates Congressional support for the Baltic countries before the July NATO Summit in Madrid. At the Summit, NATO Allies will reach a consensus and will invite certain countries to begin accession talks. On June 12, the Clinton Administration chose Poland, Hungary and the Czech Republic as the first countries eligible for NATO membership. However, other European allies support the inclusion of 5 countries, adding Romania and Slovenia, in the first wave of enlargement. Thus, a consensus must be reached by the July Summit. As for the Baltics, the Administration remains committed to completing a U.S. Baltic Charter before the July Summit. This Charter will be non-binding but serves to alleviate some of the Baltic countries security concerns. The exact contents of the Charter are unclear at this time. The Administration also supports the passage of the European Security Act. The House passed this legislation parallel to the time that the Latvian Foreign Minister Valdis Birkavs was in Washington, D.C. for a three day visit. Foreign Minister Birkavs met with Secretary of State Madeleine Albright, U.S. Senators and Congressmen to urge that Latvia, Estonia and Lithuania are included in the next wave of NATO enlargement. After talks at the State Department, Albright repeated assurances that the first new members of NATO would not be the last and said the Administration\'s "goal is an integrated Europe and that Europe includes Latvia." The two leaders signed an agreement to cooperate in halting drug trafficking, money laundering and fighting crime. Speaking at the National Press Club in DC, Birkavs said Latvia was ready to share the burden of NATO enlargement and contribute to security in Europe by helping in peacekeeping operations. He said Latvian troops were serving in Bosnia alongside U.S. and Russian forces. Birkavs expressed satisfaction with President Clinton\'s announcement that the United States will allow only three countries– Hungary, Poland and the Czech Republic —to be included in the first wave of NATO\'s enlargement "I hope the summit will not be a once-in-a-lifetime event but more like the Olympics, where the principle is not to be first but to participate every two or four years," he concluded. On June 11, Birkavs addressed Latvian American community members in Rockville, Maryland about Latvia\'s security concerns. He said his meetings with Senators and Congressmen were supportive and highly positive. Some of the Senators he met included Senators Richard Lugar (R-IN) and Richard Durbin (D-IL) and some of the Representatives included Gerald Solomon (R-NY) and Richard Armey (R-TX). Birkavs reminded community members that they should contact their legislators by writing letters regarding their concerns for Latvia. Birkav\'s call for political action is already underway in the Latvian American community. The delegates at the ALA Congress in Chicago reached an agreement to contact their Members of Congress to support legislation for the Baltics. However, in reaching consensus ALA delegates lacked adequate representation and input from the younger generation. The themes of the ALA Congress should not only include passing on Latvian tradition and culture onto future generations and prioritizing foreign policy, but also engaging the youth to participate in the U.S. political process which promotes stability and security in Latvia, Lithuania and Estonia. Aija Straumane', 'Tf;e clhiiitauqua Conference in occupied Latvia This past September seven Latvian – Americans joined over 270 Americans in a historic "town meeting" between U.S. and Soviet officials in the Baltic resort city of Jūrmala. In Soviet-occupied Latvia. Ojars Kalnlns, Public Relations Director of the American Latvian Association and editor of the Chicago Latvian Newsletter, participated in the conference and gives this special report: TH X IKtBIHIIN <; On Nay 7. Dr Olgerts Pavlovskis. President of the World Federation of Free Latvians and I. were called to the State Department in Washington. D C. to discuss what had been described to us as a potentially "historic event" scheduled to take place in occupied Latvia. The "event" was the Chautauqua Conference: a privately sponsored \'town meeting" where US and Soviet spokesmen could debate superpower relations. A similar conference, involving high ranking U.S. and Soviet officials had already taken place in 1985 in Chautauqua, New York. This year, in a gesture of reciprocity, the Soviet-U.S. Friendship Committee in Moscow had invited the Chautauqua Institute to participate in a followup conference in the Baltic resort riiy of Jūrmala. Latvia. Mark Palmer, then Deputy Assistant Secretary of State and John Hallach, foreign editor of the Hearst Newspapers and coordinator of the conference, explained their views on the event. While they were in favor of such a conference in principle (it promoted the "people to people" exchanges Reagan and Gorbachev had agreed on in the Geneva Summit), they had a problem with the location. The U.S. has never recognized the legitimacy of Soviet rule over Latvia, and generally does not send high ranking officials to the occupied Baltic States. Although this was a private undertaking, the perception of U.S. officials debating Soviets on occupied Latvian soil could be negative. We were told that the State Department had initially refused to approve U.S. government participation at the conference in Latvia. However, after Soviet refusal to change locations, the State Department began to reassess the situation. What If, they asked vi, the US. side agreed to participate in the conference, but would use this opportunity to dramatically call attention to the non recognition policy and other Baltic issues? Into the Lion\'s Den! Wallach and Palmer offered us a number of proposals which they felt would ensure that Baltic interests would be protected. U.S. speakers would clearly restate the U.S policy toward the Baltic States during the debates The American Latvian Association could send ten individuals of their choosing to the conference, who could raise questions and participate in the debates. We would also have total access to the press, speakers and delegates prior to the conference so that we could brief them on our concerns In their view, by openly challenging Soviet rule of Latvia in such a forum, the conference would bring the nontTcognit ion policy "out of the closet" and generate unprecedented press coverage in the West. Palmer saw it v a perfect followup to last years\' Baltic Peace and Freedom Cruise, with one critical difference: Baltic issues would be raised directly to the Soviets on occupied territory, not outside of it. tohm Wallach. as coordinator of the project, gave his personal word that every effort would be made to safeguard Baltic intercuts. In follow up meetings with ALA president Aristids Lambergs. Wallach made a startling offer. To prove his sincerity, he gave the ALA veto power over the entire project. If we could not approve the conference, the project would be called off. It wits an intriguing proposition. Throughout history Latvia had been caught in the middle of Major [tower conflicts Suddenly the ALA was in the middle of a historic event involving the two modern superpowers, only this time the decision of "go" or "no go" rested with us DOUBTS AND DECISION Al.A\'s first decision was "no go". Although disappointed. Wallach kept his word and informed Moscow that the conference could not take place in Latvia. While it seemed that the project had been killed. Lambergs. after conferring with ALA VP. for Public Information. Valdis Pavlovskis. decided to re evaluate the situation. Wallach. the State Department and even the White House (we were told) wanted this conference to take place. We were assured that Latvians would only gain by approving it Weighing the potential pluses and minuses, Lambergs and Pavlovskis ck*:tded that perhaps this was the timetry a bold experiment. For years we had been de-fending" Latvian interests in the West: why not go on the- offensive" and directly confront the oppressor at the scene of the crime, on occupied Latvian soil? They developed a new plan and subsequently presented the State Department with a detailed proposal. If the State Department could guarantee our requests in writing, the ALA would "not protest" the conference. Within days Lambergs received a letter from Deputy Secretary of State John C. Whitehead wriii h met all of ALA\'s requirements. The conference was on PLANS & STRATEGY Over the next three months, we actively began to prepare for the conference, devising an overall press and public relations strategy which would maximize the potential positive benefits of this unprecedented foray into occupied Latvia. We prepared comprehensive briefing books for the American delegates and the press, met personally with Amb. Jack Matlock. Jean Kirkpatrick and other scheduled "debaters" and briefed fr\'ends In the U.S. Congress. During the pre conference negotiations. John Wallach also won two important concessions from the Soviets: per Lambergs\' request, the debates would be simultaneously translated into Latvian as well as English and Russian. Second, the Soviets agreed that the majority of the local audience would consist of Latvians. The key to our strategy was the element of surprise. As far as the Soviets were concerned, the Haiti* issue was not a part of the agenda, and the ALA\'s participation in the conference was not publicly known In our view, the Chautauqua delegation was a Trojan horse entering into occupied Latvia with a bellyfull of Baltic sup rises As a i-esult, the biggest problem we faced was winning the support of the Baltic American community without tipping our hand to Moscow While we did In ief key Baltic American loaders, many in the community had only sketchy information about the conference and were understandably disturbed Even BOOT who knew about the arrangements remained skeptical. As word got out. criticism of our "failure to protest the conference" began appearing in the Latvian American press Whileit was troubling to hear this criticism from our fellow Baits, it did serve a purpose: it was what the Soviets expected and thus kept them in the dark about on r rea I plans. ON TO RIGA\' On Monday, September 15. over 270 Americans and 2,000 Soviets and Latvians gathered in a conference hall In Jūrmala. Latvia to begin what had been billed as "A Journey in Open Diplomacy" Among the American delegation were- seven Latvi,in Americans: Dzintars Kalniņa, Nils Melngailis. Louise Praaaar, Viesturs TJarots. Martins Hildebrants. Benita JaunclaJdei is and myself While none- of us had been horn in Latvia all wen- fluent in the Latvian language and active in the Latvian American community Our task was to help fulfill three objectives: I To protect the U.S. nonrecognit ion policy. 2. To generate press coverage about Latvia and the Baltic States, and 3. To bring a message of support to the people of occupied Latvia. During the following week, amid high drama, deep emotion and a numl>er of unexpected developments, these objectives were fulfilled beyond our wildest hopes and ex pectat ions THE I) EBAT i: S Each day the debates were divided into two parts. In the morning, in an open air theater before an audience of well over 2,200 people, speakers from each side would make opening statements, respond to opponent\'s remarks and then answer written questions submitted from the audience. In the afternoon, before a smaller audience in an enclosed theater, speakers would raise new issues and then respond to oral questions from the audience. While various subjects were discussed U.S Soviet relations, arms control, regional conflicts, etc two unscheduled subjects generated the greatest controversy: Nicholas Danlloff and the Soviet occupation of the Baltic States. The Baltic question surfaced immediately on the opening day of the conference in the ki?ynote address of Ambassador Jack Matlock. As Special Advisor to the President on Soviet Affairs in the National Security Council, Matlock was the official head of the U.S. delegation. He began his speech in fluent Latvian a gesture which was enthusiastically applauded by a delighted, if somewhat suprised Latvian audience. Matlock\'s opening comments contained a subtle reference to a theme that every U.S. speaker would repeat throughout the conference. Praising the Latvian people and nation. Matlock stated that "All of us who ate visiting your land, lie they Americans, Russians or others, owe you a debt a gratitude for your hospitality." It was during the Russian language portion of his spei-ch that Matlock clearly and unequivocally stated the U.S. policy toward the occupied Baltic States "The use of force and the absence of freely given consent are the reasons the United States has neverrecognized and will never recognize the legality of the forcible Incorporation of Latvia. Lithuania ml Estonia into the Soviet Union ." The statement dropped like a bomhshell on the startled audience. In an angry response. Deputy Soviet Foreign Minister Vladimir Petrovsky dismissed Matlock\'s comments as "provorat ion" and accused Ihe U.S of meddling in internal affairs\' of the Soviet Union by referring to the Latvian issue. Yet Ureal Latvians in the audience seemed intrigued by Matlock\'s statement In a written question Matlock was asked why the U.S held such a position. Matlock repeated his earlier remarks, adding that the illegal occupation of Latvia was "a fact "']

Name: 28_gdp_growth_quarter_year
Representative_Docs: ["GDP increases by 9,5% in the first six months in Latvia With the ongoing growth of Latvia's national economy, the GDP has increased by 9.5% in the Ist half of 2005 if compared to the corresponding period of the previous year. In the first quarter, the GDP increased by 7,4%, but in the second quarter this year it grew by 11.6%. As advised by the Central Statistics Bureau, the GDP growth in the second quarter has been the largest since regaining independence in the early part of 90-ies. The increase in GDP in the first and second quarter this year has been due to the rises in the following sectors: 17,1% and 18,7% in trade, 15,2% and 18,8% in transport and communications, 3,7% and 7,8% in manufacturing, 16% un 15,8% in construction. In the first six months of 2004, the GDP increased by 8,2% and by 8,5% during the whole year of 2004. The GDP growth ratio in Latvia in the second quarter has been 11,6%, which is the highest in the Baltic's, compared to a 10% GDP growth in Estonia and an 8% increase in Lithuania. For more information about the GDP growth in Latvia in the Ist half of 2005 please see the home page of the Central Statistics Bureau.", "LATVIA'S Q3 GDP GROWTH HIGHEST IN BALTICS RIGA, 20 Dec, BNS – Latvia boasts the highest gross domestic product (GDP) growth among the Baltic states in the third quarter of 2001, reported the Latvian national statistics office. In the third quarter of 2001, Latvian GDP grew 6.3% while provisional estimates give Lithuanian GDP growth at 5.1% and Estonia 4.9%. Latvian GDP grew most rapidly also in the other two quarters in 2001. In the first quarter the growth was 8.3% in Latvia, 5.8% in Estonia and 4.4% in Lithuania, in the second quarter of this year Latvian GDP grew by 9.2%, Lithuanian by 5.7% and Estonian by 5%. Annual GDP growth in 2000 over 1999 was 6.6% in Latvia, 6.9% in Estonia and 3.9% in Lithuania.", "LATVIA'S GDP GROWTH EXPECTED TO EXCEED 5% IN THE SECOND QUARTER Latvia's gross domestic product (GDP) in the second quarter of 2002 is expected to have been higher than in the first quarter and to have grown by more than 5%. in its forecast, the Bank of Latvia indicates that the rising GDP can be attributed to industrial growth and rapidly climbing retail trade, as well as the more traditional growth sectors – construction and communications. At the same time, GDP growth will be tempered by a decline in the transit sector, notably in port and transportation operations. Although the handling of goods at the Riga port has increased somewhat, this will not offset the overall negative effects on the transit industry from the shrinking cargo turnover at Ventspils, the largest port of Latvia. GDP growth in the first six months of 2002 could be around 4.5%. This estimate, however, may have to be revised once the results of the services sector are assessed and included in the compilations. The Bank of Latvia forecast for annual GDP growth in 2002 remains unchanged at 5%. In the first quarter of this year Latvia's GDP was up 3.8% year-on-year, mostly due to an 8.9% growth of trade (accounting for 19.3% of GDP), a 6.8% growth in construction (4.2% of GDP), a 4.1% growth in commercial services (11.2% of GDP) and a 2.9% growth in processing indusby (11,2% of GDP). The growth of Latvia's GDP in the second quarter of 2001 was 9.3% whiie for the entire year it was 7.7%."]

Name: 29_australia_sydney_australian_community
Representative_Docs:
['Latvia’s 71st Anniversary Celebration

The vicepresident of the Latvian Federa tion of Australia and New Zealand, Mr Vidvuds Koskins, hosted a reception on the 17th November at the Latvian Centre on the occasion of the 7 Ist Anniversary of the dec laration of independence of the Republic of Latvia. The reception was attended by members of the Australian Latvian community and invited representatives of the wider Austra lian community a total of 120 people. Among the distinguished guests were: Mr Leo McLeay MP, Speaker House of Representatives, representing the Prime Minister, the Hon. Bob Hawke; Dr Bob Woods MP, member for the Federal seat of Lowe, representing the Leader of the Federal Opposition, the Hon. An drew Peacock; The Hon. John Johnson, President of the Legislative Council;

Mr Paul Zammit MP, member for the State seat of Strathfield, representing the Pre mier, the Hon. Nick Greiner; Mr Wes Davoren MP, member for the State seat of Lakemba, representing the Leader of the State Opposition, the Hon. Bob Carr; Senator Michael Baume, senator for New South Wales; Mr Philip Ruddock MP, member for the Federal seat of Dundas, shadow Minister for Immigration and Ethnic Affairs; Mrs Tiiu Kroll-Simmul, president of the Joint Baltie Committee (of NSW). The reception was addressed by Mr Leo McLeay, Dr Bob Woods, Mr Paul Zammit,Mr Wes Davoren, the Hon. John Johnson and Mrs Tiiu Kroll-Simmul who also proposed a toast to the Republic of Latvia. The four Par liamentary Leaders had sent messages with their respective representatives.', 'ESTONIAN DELEGATION RECEIVED BY PRIME MINISTER An Estonian Delegation — consisting of the President of the Organising Committee of the Council of Estonian Associations in Australia, Mr. A. Lond, Hon. Sec. of Estonian Club "Eesti Kodu Linda," Mr. V. Niineorg, both from Sydney; the President of Estonian Society "Kodu," Mr. V. Maidre, from Melbourne and the Chairman of New Australians Liberal and Country Movement, Estonian Branch, Mr. M. Ots, from Melbourne—on December 19, 1951, handed over an address to the Prime Minister, Mr. Menzies. Mr. Lond addressed the Prime Minister in a short speech. The text of the address to the Prime Minister was:— The Right Honorable R. G. Menzies, K.C., M.P., Prime Minister of the Commonwealth of Australia, Parliament House, Canberra. In the Jubilee Year of the Australian Commonwealth, all Estonians now living in Australia — those who have been living here for tens of years as well as those who have arrived only a few jears ago as political refugees — nave the honor and great pleasure of bringing you, Mr. Prime Minister, their most cordial congratulations and wishing you the best of success in your work for the welfare of Australia. The Estonians have warmheartedly followed your political activities and self-sacrifices for the benefit of this risirig country, and they have witnessed the fulfilment of many of your worthy endeavors, the results of which will not only be enjoyed by this generation, but by many other generations in centuries to come. Thanks to your foresight, Australia has been able to preserve its freedom. You have succeeded in saving this country from that threatening danger, which already has spread its system of horror over many countries, holding their people in its iron grip and carrying in its wake terror, slavery and death for millions. The majority of Estonians, who have come to Australia, know from painful personal experiences the hardships and sufferings inflicted by that dictatorial power, and we are therefore able to appreciate most sincerely your efforts to keep this country out of that threatening grip. We also carry the conviction that you will unhesitatingly do our utmost to help the countries and peoples—among them our beloved native country, Estonia —> which have been deprived of their freedom to self-determination and of the power to conduct their own life and affairs, for freeing themselves soon from foreign occupation and rebuilding their life, culture and economy in independent states. We, the Estonians, who have settled here, promise to contribute our very best efforts for the welfare of this country. On behalf of the Estonian Organisations in Australia T. VARIK, President of the Estonian Society V. NHNEORG Honorary Secretary. Sydney, December 19, 1951. MR. MENZIES' REPLY In his answer, Mr. Menzies thanked for the thoughtfulness of the Estonians and said inter alia: "I would just like to say to you how thankful I am for this address, not only for the beauty of its work, but also for what you have said in it. "Yoi come from a land a long way from Australia. You come from a much older land. You come from a land in which people like yourself have exactly the same love of freedom as we have in Australia. And I have some idea of how it must have felt for all of you to leave your homeland, to leave the country in which you were born, in which you grew up, with all the sights and sounds that are gathered into ou> minds when we are children. That must have brought great sorrow to ydu and to those like you. But I would like to say on behalf of Australia that when you come to this land you come also to a country in which we like free people; and therefore you have a great welcome here. We know that you are going to be great Australians that we shall all be fellow-Australians together, making no distinctions among ourselves. "Indeed, it would be interesting, would it not, it some day a son or grandson of a man who came from Estonia to Australia sat in this chair of mine to receive a message of goodwill from an Estonia liberated and once more a member of a group of free nations in the world. It would be interesting, and not impossible. "I would like to say to you that you may rely on the friendship and goodwill of the Australian people. If, occasionally, you meet someone who seems to disagree with you, I want you to remember that I myself meet many more people who disagree with me. In Australia, we don't always agree about things. It is one of the principles of our freedom. That is why you come here—because you want to live in a free country and be able to disagree with other people and still be able to live at peace with each other. "So I value this address, and through you I would like to send my good wishes to your Estonian fellow-countrymen who have made Australia their new home."', 'LATVIA’S 72nd ANNIVERSARY CELEBRATION

The vicepresident of the Latvian Federation of Australia and New Zealand, Mr Vidvuds Koskins, hosted a reception on the 16th November at the Latvian Centre on the occasion of the 72nd Anniversary of the declaration of independence of the Republic of Latvi a. The reception was attended by members of the Australian Latvian community and invited representatives of the wider Australian community in excess of 100 people. Mr Paul Elliott, MP, Federal Member for Parramatta, representing the Prime Minister, the Hon. Bob Hawke A.C. Mr Philip Ruddock, MP, Shadow Minister for Immigration and Ethnic Affairs representing the Leader of the Opposition, Dr John Hewson. The Hon. Sir Adrian Solomons, MP, Deputy President and Chairman of Committees, Legislative Council, represen ting the Premier, the Hon. Nick Greiner. Mr Kevin Moss, MP, Member for the State seat of Canterbury, representing the Leader of the State Opposition, the Hon. Bob Carr. The Reverend Canon Dr Lance Shilton, AM, former Dean of Sydney and Mrs Shilton.

Alderman Eve Dutton representing the Mayor of Strathfield. Alderman Raftan, representing the Mayor of Burvvood Aid. Geof Howe. Mr Aivars Mednis, Chairman Baltie Council of Australia. Mrs Tiiu Kroll-Simmul vice president of the Joint Baltie Council Olaf Pihlak Federal President of the Estonian Australian Community. The reception was addressed by Mr Paul Elliott, Mr Philip Ruddock, The Hon. Sir Adrian Solomons, Mr Kevin Moss and Mrs Tiiu Kroll-Simmul who also proposed a toast to the Republic of Latvia. The four parliamentary Leaders had sent messages with their respective representatives. Message from the Prime Minister It gives me great pleasure to send my greetings to members and friends of the Latvian Federation of Australia and New Zealand on the occasion of the 72nd Anniversary of the Declaration of Indepen dence of the Republic of Latvia. Latvian-Australians are a long established community within our society, well noted for their enterprise and initiative, which are reflected in the signifieant contribution they have made to the

economic life of our country. As well they have ķept up their strong cultural traditions which further enrich our multicultural society.

While you have settled so successfully into your Australian lives it is natūrai that you have also retained an interest in Latvia. The momentous changes that have taken place in Eastern Europe over the last year have led to a fundamenta! transfor mation in East-West relations. Australia has recognised for many years the right of Latvia and the other Baltie States to exist as independent nations. Like many other vvestern governments, Australia has refused to accept in International law their incorporation into the Soviet Union in 1940, rejecting self-proclaimed Soviet sovereignty overthe m. I am heartened by prospects of negoti ations betvveen Moscow and the Baltie States, and I hope they will lead to a settlement which will meet the aspirations of the Baltie people for economic and political independence. I look forvvard to a just and peaceful resolution of this long standing issue. I congratulate you on this important occasion and join with you in the hope that the future will bring security and prosperity, not only for members of the Latvian community here in Australia, but also for families and friends in your former homeland.

R. J. L. HAWKE']

Name: 30_budget_tax_lat_million
Representative_Docs: ["LATVIAN DRAFT NATIONAL BUDGET TO HAVE 1.9% DEFICIT IN 2001 The Latvian draft consolidated national budget provides for a fiscal deficit of 79.23 million lats (SUS 129.88 mln) or 1.9% of gross domestic product (GDP), Finance Ministry spokeswoman Baiba Melnace reported on 5 September. At the same time, the financial deficit is set as 66.86 million lats. Revenues to the budget are planned in the amount of 1.43 billion lats and expenditures as 1=498 billion late. Under this year's consolidated national budget target revenues are set at 1.374 billion late and expenditures at 1.44 billion lats on a fiscal deficit of 80.499 million lats and a financial deficit of 66 million lats. (SUSI = LVL 0.610)", "LATVIA'S STATE BUDGET DEFICIT 24.1 MILLION The Ministry of Finance reports that the state budget deficit amounted to 24,193,000 lats in the first half of 1996. Under an agreement between the Government of Latvia and the International Monetary Fund, the budget deficit cannot exceed 25 million lats in the first half of the year. The state budget deficit was 52.5 million lats in the first six months of 1995.", "Consolidated total budget deficit was 1.8% of GDP last year The total budget revenues from the national budget last year were LVL 2.11 billion, LVL 214 million up from 2002. The total budget expenditures stood at LVL 2.24 billion, and fiscal deficit – at 103.1 million. Therefore, the consolidated total budget deficit was 1.8 percent of the GDP last year. The state budget's total revenues in 2003 amounted to LVL 1.73 billion, LVL 18 million more than projected in the Law on the budget, and LVL 188 million more than in 2002. The state budget's fiscal deficit therefore was LVL 90.8 million, 1.6 percent of GDP and less than the LVL 169 million deficit envisaged in the Law on the year 2003 budget."]

Name: 31_crime_war_criminal_evidence
Representative_Docs: ['Canada first ... for zeal The Deschenes Commission report is upon us. Where do we go from here? What, in fact, the Deschenes Commission finally said was that stories of thousands of Nazi criminals in Canada have been exaggerated, and the authorities should look seriously into 20 alleged criminals and further investigate another 218. It looks as if Justice Minister Ray Hnatyshyn and Judge Deschenes have managed to carry out a reasonable damage control exercise on the harebrained idea of Prime Minister Mulroney\'s to set up the Inquiry into the Commission of War Crimes in the first place. This mania of our PM for making public policy decisions based on circuses and opinion polls is the attitude of a man unable to lead or govern. Meanwhile, the idea of looking into 218 cases strikes me as absurd, but I suppose the commission had to come up with something after two years of investigation. It strikes me as absurd because it is absolutely clear that Canada has no big fish Nazis. It may have some small fry, much as we have some small fry communist horrors here. But just as there seems little point in digging up some pale, flaccid Hungarian who got through our screening procedures in 1956 and now lives comfortably here trying to forget what he did in the cellar of the Budapest secret police so it seems pointless to try unimportant ex-Nazis. Bringing some small-time concentration camp guard or foot soldier of the Third Reich to trial now would do nothing but make him a scapegoat. It would suggest that his crimes were an aberration in the Third Reich. In fact, all of Germany was mad at the time, gripped by a terrible disease in which the rules of law were suspended. Israel itself has no interest in bringing such people to trial. Many of its leading jurists (notably ex-chief justice Chaim Cohen) have even argued against holding such trials as the Eichmann and Demjanjuk trial now going on. I have no idea why Canada has to outdo everyone in the world for zeal, and misplaced zeal at that, and twist its laws to hold war crimes trials for Nazi minnows. When the idea of holding war crimes trials was even raised in Britain, the home secretary dismissed it on the spot as inconceivable. A man is brought to face justice where he committed his crimes and where he may be judged by a jury of his peers. Canada has no jurisdiction in crimes committed in Europe during the war and all the Criminal Code amendments in the world will not wash that abstract ideal away. The justice minister\'s response strikes me as sober and appropriate. He has rejected the illiberal concept of retroactivity in citizenship without proving fraud in obtaining it. He seems unlikely to want to broaden our extradition treaty with Israel, which Israel is unlikely to press for anyway. He has stuck BARBARA AMIEL by the need to use Canadian standards of evidence in any trials, which effectively eliminates Soviet evidence. Since most war crimes were committed in areas of Europe that are today under Soviet control, the most important evidence for any trials Canada may hold can often only be collected in the Soviet Union. This raises an important issue. The real need now is to heal the rift between the East Europeans and the Jewish community in Canada. It is my opinion that the rift is in part the deliberate work of the Soviet Union. This is mere speculation, but there are some signposts. Several years ago, a front-page story in the Los Angeles Times said a Soviet official had warned justice authorities in the U.S. that American courts were being set up by phony testimony from Soviet witnesses in war crimes trials. There is no way of finding out whether this was true or not but it raised the question of why would the Soviets want to get false testimony into the trial of accused ex-Nazis living in Europe or North America? The answer, I suppose, is to create tension and dissension between the Jewish and ethnic communities. The Soviets have suddenly begun leaking evidence on alleged ex-Nazis after some 40 years. But they will not allow that evidence to be properly tested scientifically (in the case of documents) or through proper cross-examinations (in the case of eyewitnesses.) Much of the Wiesenthal Centre\'s evidence comes from Soviet sources although the Wiesenthal Centre seems curiously coy about this and will not say where its evidence is obtained. Once someone like Soi Littman, the Wiesenthal representative in Canada, starts pointing the finger and accusing a country like Canada of harboring "thousai.ds" of war criminals, he puts pressure on the government to act in ways that can only inflame the delicate relationships between ethniccommunities. This may not be Littman\'s intent This is too often the result. It is in the Soviet Union\'s interests to sec that attitudes toward Israel are hostile and that the western countries have internal difficulties with rival ethnic communities. The East Europeans and Jews  a history that is inextricably intertwined. Whatever the past unhappinesses, today both are victims of the same oppressor. They must not let the past evil of the Third Reich play into the hands of the present evil of the Reich of the USSR.', 'Šht J&ril THE GLOBE AND MAIL, TUESOAY, MARCH 4,1986 CANADA\'S NATIONAL NEWSPAPER SOVIET VILLAINS OMITTED A flaw in Canada\'s search to uncover war criminals Crosbie: put matters to rest. BY RON VASTOKAS and LUBOMYR LUCIUK Mr. Luciuk is a post-doctoral fellow at the University of Toronto. Mr. Vastokas teaches anthropology at Trent University. [|L I 1979, Joe Clark, who was then I \\| Prime Minister, set up a task force I I on alleged war criminals in Canada. One conclusion of the task force report, prepared by Martin Low, was that "evenhandedness would require that any policy to deal with \'war criminals\' should apply to any person regardless of where or when the wrongdoing took place." Inexplicably, the present Government ignored this recommendation when It set up the Commission of Inquiry on War Criminals in February, 1985, limiting its mandate to the investigation of war criminals related to the atrocities of Nazi Germany. To Canadians of East European background, the decision to investigate only alleged Nazis is perceived as selective, even arbitrary. In their view, the Soviet Union is as guilty of genocide as Germany, with which it signed a non-aggression pact in 1939. In the Baltic area, executions, massacres and deportations began during the first Soviet occupation in 1941 and continued when the Soviet army returned in 1944. In Lithuania, some 600,000 men, women and children, a quarter of the population, were shipped out of die country in sealed freight cars and, in the words of Soviet dissident author Alexander Solzhenitsyn, "tipped like dung into the frost-bound soil." A similar fate met half a million Estonians and Latvians and, in proportionately greater numbers, the people of Ukraine. The Soviet secret police, die NKVD, masterminded these mass arrests and executions, set up the concentration camps and even pioneered the use of gas chambers, at Vorkuta in 1938. The NKVD was responsible for die massacres at such places as Katyn, Vinnytsia and Lvov, where a total of some 30,000 Poles and Ukrainians were executed. Together with SMERSH killer battalions, the NKVD also liquidated many of the more than two million people forcibly repatriated to the Soviet Union in 1945 by the United States, Britain and Canada. None of the men responsible for these atrocities has ever been brought to justice. Despite these facts, the Ukrainian Canadian Committee and other East European organizations publicly expressed their support for the decision to establish the Deschenes Commission, believing that the Government would bring all war criminals, regardless of background, to trial in Canadian courts. Unfortunately, this position was not given much attention by the Canadian media. The way the mandate has been interpreted has not allowed the introduction of evidence on Soviet war crimes, although there are lists of alleged Soviet war criminals available and some of them may well be living in Canada. Since the Soviet Union and Nazi Germany were allies from August, 1939, to June, 1941, when many of the atrocities were committed, Uiese lists should be investigated. The contention of Soviet spokesmen that only Nazi Germany can be held accountable for crimes of genocide, or that evidence of Soviet crimes against humanity is "fabricated by Fascist reactionary emigrēs," is nothing more than disinformation. Meanwhile, little thought or consideration seems to have been given to the concerns of the East European community by those who set up the inquiry, despite the fact that Mr. Low\'s report said, "most, if not all, tfie persons against whom accusations (of complicity in Nazi war crimes) have been made came originally from Eastern Europe." There were no consultations with any of the East European communities before the commission was set up. The question of how many Nazi war criminals might be in Canada appears to have been significant in creating the inquiry. Justice Minister John Crosbie, at a press conference announcing the commission, remarked that "they say there are 3,000 (war criminals) in Canada and we say, no, as far as we know there\'s no more tban 30 or 40. Many groups do not believe the federal estimates ... so by having this commission ... this should put the matter to rest." Regardless of numbers, many East European community leaders would agree with Professor Irwin Cotler, counsel for the Canadian Jewish Congess, that "if there is one Nazi living in Canada, that is too many," since many of their people also perished in the Nazi concentration camps. The position of David Matas, counsel for the B\'nai B\'rith, that "all war criminals .. . should be brought to justice" is even more acceptable, since all Canadians would agree that one Soviet war criminal living in Canada is also one too many. However, the selective mandate of the commission, its focus on Eastern Europeans, particularly Ukrainians, the unsubstantiated allegations about thousands of war criminals to be found in these communities, and the decision to seek Soviet evidence of Nazi war crimes, have engendered a perception among many Canadians that die entire process is little more than a witch-hunt. A bitter controversy has also been fanned in the Jewish, Baltic and Ukrainian ethnic press. A full-page advertisement in The Globe and Mail last Sept. 28, based on die Martin Low Report and asking, "Why Discriminate?" evoked a harsh reply in a Canadian Jewish News editorial. It charged that Canadians of East European origin and descent were "harboring" and "protecting persons in their midst who are accused of murder." A November issue of Ukrainian Echo contained a countercharge which read, in part: "You know and we know that a number of Jews worked willingly with the Soviets ... when will we see your community admit to this?" The tragic irony of all this is that the leading participants in the debate, Canadian communities of East European and Jewish origin, have both suffered repression and genocide. All came to this country to build new lives. Yet suggestions that an accord be reached between these groups have been frustrated by the anger generated by media coverage, which has revived old charges. For example, according to Rabbi W. Gunther Plaut, one particularly sore point in Ukrianian-Jewish relations is "the accusation by many Jews that a Ukrainian SS division took part in vicious butcheries of Jews" in the Second World War. This claim has been made before. In 1950, the Canadian Jewish Congress said it possessed "actual documentary proofs" of the Galicia Division\'s involvement in war crimes. The Ukrainian Canadian Committee asked that these documents be produced. They were not. The controversy surfaced again last year and all the familiar innuendos were repeated. At least this issue has been resolved, for as Yves Fortier, counsel for the Commission on War Criminals, noted on Oct. 3: "I am pleased to put on the record ... that if the only allegation against a resident of Canada is that he was a. member of the Galicia Division, that is not an individual that we consider should be made the subject of an investigation." If the mandate had not been so selective and if careful attention had been paid to the complexities of this issue by the media, then the controversy that now envelops this inquiry might have been avoided or diminished. Again, Mr. Low\'s report presciently said that "there is ... a serious problem of perception and definition which would have to be resolved in the development of any policy to deal with \'war criminals\' in Canada." Representative_Docs: ['ANDREW WITER. M P. PARKDALE-HIGH PARK EXTERNAL AFFAIRS—REQUEST FOR LISTING OF HONORARY BALTIC CONSULS Mr. Andrew Witer (Par kdale—High Park): Mr Speaker, on April I i directed a question to the Secretary of State for External Affairs (Mr Clark) with regard to the listing of honorary Baltic consuls in the External Affairs directory of diplomatic, consular and other representatives in Canada. The Minister responded that the question was under review by his Department. This matter has been in limbo since 1972 and clearly should have long since been resolved. For 10 years prior to 1972 the consuls were listed in the directory and it was only on the death of the acting Honorary Consul General of Estonia that year that the listing of the consuls were removed from the directory. The federal Government has received countless representations from the Estonian Central Council in Canada, the Latvian Federation in Canada, the Lithuanian Canadian Community, the Captive Nations Committee in Canada and the Baltic Federation, yet their efforts have produced no results Despite Canada\'s de jure nonrecognition of the illegal occupation of the Baltic states of Estonia, Latvia and Lithuania by the Soviet Union, the Baltic consuls are still not listed as the representatives of the pre-Soviet occupation, freely and democratically elected governments of the Baltic States. Over the years there have been many answers given as to who is responsible for the listings. On different occasions the Baltic communities have been informed thai the listing is at the discretion of the Secretary of State for External Affairs, that the responsibility lies with the Parliament of Canada in persuading the Secretary of State for External Affairs to list the Ba/tic consuls, that the question of listing of Baltic consuls must be decided by the Cabinet, and that the question of listing the Baltic consuls should be decided by the inner ( \'binet. With all these conflicting responses, it is small wonder that the Baltic communities are left confused and concerned as to how this matter can be resolved when it cannot even be established who is responsible. In the past, two Secretaries of State for External Affairs have commissioned reports to study the issue, neither of which were ever made public. The Minister\'s assurance that the issue is under review can therefore be of little comfort to the Baltic communities. In discussions with representatives of the Department of External Affairs, representatives have been told that the listings would have a negative effect on "other areas". Such statements have led to the conclusion that the Department of External Affairs is concerned that the listing of consuls would anger the Soviet Union and. consequently, allows that fear to determine our policy on this important matter. There are those in our Department of External Affairs who advocate that Canada should not do anything to ruffle the feathers of the US S R They always recommend the path of least resistance and are proverbial fence-sitters. Such a position is an abrogation and betrayal of our principles as a free and democratic sovereign nation. For us to appease a morally bankrupt Soviet regime, whose only trump? are a mighty military apparatus, an aggressive stance toward the rest of the world, and their traditionally arrogant rhetoric, would constitute a betrayal of all those who gave their lives for freedom and democracy, all those who came to this country believing that Canada was a country which stands for fundamental freedom and human rights, including respect of territorial integrity and the right of self-determination. If Canada continues to sit on the fence by refusing to list Baltic consuls, the Soviets will tell the people of the Baltic States that while Canada pays lip service to the principle of withholding de jure recognition of the captivity of their countries, in practice Canada has conceded Soviet sovereignty and Soviet right to enforce its illegal annexation. This is a discredit to our entire system of government. The Government allows the acting honourary consuls of the Baltic States to continue exercising their duties in Canada, although it does not formally recognize them since honournry consuls are not appointed by a government with which Canada maintains diplomatic or consular relations These consuls are Canadian citizens and for this reason have no immunity nor diplomatic privileges. We have been accustomed to thinking of Canada's multiculturalism exclusively in Canadian domestic terms – the variety it offers; the challenge of drawing the best qualities from different cultures; the tolerance it can teach us here at home. But there is an international dimension to Canada's multicultural character. It provides us direct access to the multitude of countries and societies from which Canadians come. Usually, that helps us understand those countries better, and we can apply that understanding in the exercise of Canada's historic international role as a moderating nation, and a leader in the practical pursuit of human rights. But it can also, in critical times, give us an influence that is not available to countries which lack our links of family and culture and language. Sometimes those links can create problems, as, for example, when foreign disputes are imported into Canada. But more often, they create opportunities, and we should act on them. Take the case of the Baltic communities in Canada. There once was some fear that the presence in Canada of citizens with origins in Central and Eastern Europe would limit our ability to encourage glasnost or perestroika. The opposite is the case. Thousands of Canadian citizens speak Estonian, Latvian, Polish, Hungarian, Serbo-Croat, Slovak or Czech. They give Canada direct and personal connections with the activists of Solidarity, nationalities of the Baltic States, or the faithful seeking to practise the principles of the  Church, or of Judaism in the Soviet Union. Those arc not obstacles to Canadian foreign policy. They are considerable assets, if we have the imagination to make the most of them. Tomorrow, as the Government continues our consideration of our response to the terrible events in China, I am convening a Roundtable of Canadian experts from outside the Government, who have direct experience in China. This will include Canadians of Chinese origin, as well as scholars, business persons, and others who have lived in that society we seek to influence. We are taking the same approach to the different changes that are occuring in the Soviet Union and Eastern and Central Europe. The Prime Minister and I seek the advice of the business leaders who are actually pursuing joint ventures in the Soviet Union. I meet directly with families, like Ala Wclfson or Ivan Shumuk who are trying to bring Refusniks or formerpolitical prisoners to Canada. And we believe there is a creative constructive opportunity to make use of Canada's human links to Europe. We must be sensible and deliberate in the way we use that asset. We must steer between old memories and new enthusiasms, and always be guided by the pragmatism that makes foreign policy effective.
