But we can help encourage new thinking in the Soviet Union and Eastern and Central Europe by displaying imagination here at home. That is why I come to you this evening – not just out of courtesy to a significant Canadian community, nor simply to celebrate your culture and your history. I am here to ask your help in Canada's contribution to positive and permanent changes in the Soviet Union, and in Eastern and Central Europe. This past year has been momentous in relations between East and West. It has brought elections in Poland and the Soviet Union and the promise of the same in Hungary. In January we signed the Conluding Document A KEYNOTE SPEECH. Joe Clark, to the Vienna Conference on Security and Cooperation in Europe. New conventional arms negotiations for Europe have begun. And last month in Brussels, Prime Minister Mulroney and other Heads of Government commemorated the 40th Anniversary of NATO in a Summit that set out nfy directions for the Alliance. President Bush's dramatic proposals have regained the negotiating initiative for the West. The Prime Minister's proposal of a new scholarship plan – to help sitizens of Central and Eastern Europe learn about democratic institutions – helps assure the dynamism of an Alliance that has safeguarded Western security for four decades. This is a time of profound change in the lands from which many of you and your families came, tike all change, it can be difficult and controversial, and will require both imagination and compromise. We are not spectators in these events. We are part of them – as a nation whose citizens share languages and heritages with Europe; as a country prepared to exercise our influence internationally; as a cociety whose economic system and political traditions offer encouragement and examples to those seeking changes in Europe. As we assess these changes, some of you will counsel caution, and we will want to weigh your advice carefully. Others will see opportunities that do not leap to the eyes of our officials. The developments in China are instructions about how change has been introduced in the USSR. President Gorbachev recognized early reality which has not penetrated the leadership of China economic reform cannot occur without political reform. You cannot ask people to accept the sacrifices of economic reform without also giving them a say in how it will occur. President Gorbachev knew from the outset that the command system had failed. He identified the main cause of failure – all the levers of power were being pulled by individuals protected by repressive laws and police practices. It was impossible for all but the bravest to challenge the system. There were brave challenges. We know the names of many of those heroes – Father Svarinskas, Father Tamkevicius, Mart Niklus, Petras Grazulis, Enn Tarto, Anatoly Scharansky, Josef Begun, Yuri Shukheyvich, Josep Terelya, Stepan Stepeiiak, to name only a few. We worked together – you, other Canadians, and concerned people around the world – to secure their release from prison, from exile and even from the USSR. You kept them in the public eye before there was glasnost. Their courage contributed to the progress we are now observing as glasnot and perestroika because they showed their compatriots how to retain hope, gave them the courage to resist erosion of their cultural identity. Now Soviet authorities are acknowledging that they need the cooperation, not the pasivity, of their population. To gain that cooperation, they are prepared to make concessions. How far is the Soviet leadership prepared to go? I do not know. Perhaps they do not either. But they have already permitted, and even encouraged, expressions of sentiment which no one Wpuld have dared to hope for a couple of years ago. Fundamental change has already occured. More can – and should – be expected. But change should not be so rapid as to provoke a reaction undermining all the successes of the past year. The leaders of the Popular Fronts in the Baltics have demonstrated wisdom in a volatile situation, seeking a peaceful transition to independence. We hope their efforts will be crowned with success, so that Soviet authorities will gradually accept that their best interests are served by granting maximum fredoms to the people of the Baltic States. They have to be encouraged to understand this. The arrival of new leadership in the USSR provides solid grounds for believing that the will of the people can and will be expressed, and that it will have some impact on policy. There is still much to do. But there can be no doubt that great progress has been made in the USSR towards reducing the limitations on individual freedoms. We welcome the release of political prisoners in the Soviet Union. Religious tolerance is increasing and should be enshrined in the new law on religious practice which the Supreme Soviet is expected to pass this year. The effects of this law will be keenly felt in the Baltic States and will be of key interest to us in terms of its impact on the Ukrainian Uniate Church. We will continue to urge the maximum freedom of religion for all. Already, the situation has improved beyond anything we could have expected as little as three years ago. Who could have predicted that more than 1600 new religious congregations would be registeredin 1988 ( as opposed to 104 the year before ), that Jewish cultural centres would be formed in communities around Soviet Union or that religious texts could be imported legally in to the Soviet Union? Bnigration from the Soviet Union is higher now than at any time in the past 10 years. In the first four months of this year, 57,000 exit permits were granted for emigrants. And this before the new laws on emigration have been enacted by the Supreme Soviet. We look forward to even higher figures once that legislation is approved. Canada has no unresolved family reunification cases with the USSR. Private visits from the Soviet Union to Canada have multiplied – from 5,000 in 1986 to 12,000 in 1988. President Gorbachev seems determined to create a society based on law and he is drawing on the experience of Western countries. The Soviet Supreme Court ļftsitadthe Supreme Court of Canada last autumn to discuss a wide range of legal practices, particularly the role of defence counsel. A return visit is planned. Ultimately, of course, it is not the laws but how they are implemented which counts. We will watch closely how the spirit of the new laws is observed. While the democratization process initiated by President Gorbachev is a major step forward, the results are democratic in the sense that we would use that word. But the fact is that the Soviet people were given a choice of candidates for the newly-created Congress of People's Deputies. Where no choice existed, they were given the option to reject the single candidate. This they did. The result wa6 that most of the deputies from the Baltic States were representative of local feelings and local views. That is true also of some deputies from the Ukraine. Many deputies are clearly reformers, most obviously Boris Yeltsin and Andrei Sakharov. But there are dozens more. There will be those who argue that President Gorbachev has secured for himself a compliant Supreme Soviet which will do as it is told. I do not agree. It is not a revolutionary body, but it is reform-minded. Furthermore, the voices of deputies to the Supreme Soviet, and for that matter of deputies to the Congress, will continue to be heard. The politicization of Soviet society is only beginning, but it is a good beginning. No one can fail to appreciate the difficulties facing the Soviet Union. The challenge of reforming the economy, the tragedies of Armenia and the train disaster in the Urals, the legacy of Chernobyl, and the ethnic violence of Armenia-Azerbaijan, Uzbekistan and Georgia. There is a rising tide of demands for greater recognition of the rights of nationalities. These come from around the Soviet Union, from the Ukraine, Belorussla and Moldavia, but most significantly in the Baltic States. Perhaps the most remarkable development in a unique year in the history of the Soviet Union has been the flowering of the popular movements of the three Baltic States and the acceptance of large parts of their agenda by the local Supreme Soviets. The election of popular front representatives to the Congress of People's Deputies gives them a national forum for the pursuit of their objectives. Live television coverage of the entire session of the Congress showed Baltic representatives demanding a renunciation of the secret protocols of the Moiotov-Ribbentrop Pact and independence for the Baltic States. This must have been an eye-opener for most of the country which would have been made aware of Baltic concerns for the first time. The agreement to set upa commision of the Congress to look into the Moiotov-Ribbentrop Pact was an admission, at last, that the issue must be addressed. In the meantime, the Baltic States insist on their independence, refusing to recognize Soviet all-union laws unless they have been approved in their own Supreme Soviets. They have made their languages the official languages of the Baltic States. The Baltic Assembly of Popular Movements which met in Tallinn last month will contribute to the sense of joint purpose of the three Baltic States. The outcome of the contest of wills between Baltic Supreme Soviets and Moscow is still far from clear. The aspirations of the Baltic peoples will not be met all at once. But there has been real progress. This Government applauds the efforts of the Baltic peoples to assume responsibility for their own destinies. Clasnost and perestroika provide a context in which a new reality is unfolding, from which we hope there will be no turning back. Canada continues to refuse to recognize de jure the force incorporation of the Baltic States into the Soviet Union. We give substance to that position in a number of important ways: – First, federal government Ministers and Ambassadors do not travel to the Baltic Republics. – Second, Estonia, Latvia and Lithuania remain acceptable designations as places of origin on Canadian passport and immigration documents, and – Third, as a result of a policy I initiated last year, a disclaimer now appears on all federally-produced maps of the Baltic region reiterating our policy of no de jure recognition of the Soviet annexation. Our position of principle on this matter will not change. The legitimate aspirations of the Baltic peoples must be met. The basis on which the Soviet Union incorporated the Baltic States was illegal and immoral. The situation must be put right. There is now reality in the Baltic States and together we must determine our response to it. Canada continues to recognize facto Soviet control over the Baltic States. To recognize it is not to accept its legitimacy. It is the reality. But we have a choice. We can turn our backs on the Baltic region altogether, thereby denying courageous people the encouragem* ent and contact we can offer. Or we can maintain our de_ position and work within the realm of the possible to improve the life of the people there, many of whom are family to you here tonight. Large numbers of Canadians, particularly those of Baltic origin, are seeking more extensive contacts with the Baltic States, for personal, family, cultural, sports, or commercial purposes. Many of them seek the leadership and assistance of the Canadian Government. So do members of the popular fronts whom officials of my department have met over the past few months, both in Canada and in the Baltic States. A private exchange, under which 28 Estonian businessmen are studying the capitalist market system at York University, has been established and funded by Canadians, most of Estonian origin. Joint ventures between Canadian and Baltic Interests are being pursued. There will be a demand for more exchanges to permit closer contacts and establish joint ventures, so that the people of the Baltic States can rebuild the market skills they traditionally possessed and, broaàen people-topeople contacts. Apart from the key izssues of independence and economic autonomy the Baltic States face three basic problems: the need for economic modernization and development; the threat from environmental pollution; and the need to preserve their national identities. Canada can help with some, perhaps all, of these issues. Our focus could first be commercial and economic, whether in the area of management training, joint ventures, or purely commercial transactions. Some of you are already involved in such activities and my Department is ready to help you. The enviroment may be another area where our expertise could be useful. We do not have established programs of enviromental cooperation with the Baltic States. But Canada would be prepared to share the experience we have developed in an area so crucial to the quality of life. Canadians of Baltic origins are proud to reinforce the sense of national identity of the peoples of the Baltic States. You have shown that they and their cause are not forgotten. You have maintained contacts to the limit of the possible. Now those contacts will be permitted to grow. You should take advantage of the opportunity. Whether the contacts take the form of family visits, now less difficult to arrange, or whether they are sports events, cultural, media or academic exchanges and meetings, you will strengthen the resolve and confidence of peoples whose fate concerns us all. This Government has been paying increasing attention to the Baltic States. More officials from my Department and others will travel to the region to ensure that we have the best possible understanding of developments there. We want to ensure an open two-way flow of information with you, too, for as I said, you represent an important asset in the formation of new Canadian foreign policy responses to the changing Baltic scene. My Parliamentary Secretary has been meeting with you for instance, recently addressing th* Annual Convention of Baltic Veterans League in March, and the Baltic Womens Federation in April. We will not, however, jeopardize our policy of non-recognition de jure. Our role there will be to enhance the potential for contact and communication between the people of Canada and the people of the Baltic States. Joint ventures can greatly assist the development of the market ethic, a crucial element in achieving economic progress under perestroika. Some Canadians have been quick to see the potential of such ventures and more will do so in the near future. There are already 10 Canadian-Soviet joint ventures set up since 1988 and another six are close to completion. A group of Canadian businessmen will travel to the Ukraine in October to identify possibilities there. A Canada-USSR Business Council has been formed led by such companies as Olympia and York, McDonald's Restaurante, Lavalin, Foremost, Francmaster and Alta Cenetics. More than 100 firms should be members of this organization before its first meeting in Moscow in November. More joint ventures can be expected in the Baltic States as new opportunities are identified. The training of managers and the planned folow-up undertaken by Enterprise York are precisely the kind of private initiatives we want to encourage. In November, the Prime Minister will visit the Soviet Union. He plans to take with him a delegation of business people who are keen to identify commercial opportunities. While some of them will find a ready market for Canadian products, many will be looking to the longer term. They will be particularly interested in areas which are likely to enjoy greater freedom to make economic decisions and already have skilled labour forces and a strong work ethic. They should be encouraged to look to the Baltic States. President Gorbachev has shown increasing understanding of the necessity to permit the countries of East Europe to find their own way. In the case of Poland, we have every reason to rejoice in such a decison. That country's problems will not end now that a partially democratic election has been held. But it is already apparent that the people have a new sense that, just maybe, there is hope that the Party will share power with the people and that a way can be found out of the political and economic dilemma which has faced the country for so many years. The decision by the Party in Hungary to permit multiparty elections offers hope for that country as well. Canada and the West will have to seek creatively new methods of encouraging those trends in East European countries which offer a real chance for the people to participate in the decision-making process. At the same time, we must stimulate other East European countries whose leaderships have not yet inhaled the reform spirit to acknowledge that the world has changed and they are out of step. Canada has played a constructive role in creating the atmosphere of reduce East-West tensions which in turn has contributed to greater freedoms in the USSR, in the Baltic States, and in East Europe. Whether on arms control issues, confidence building, Alliance building, or in the CSCE process, the voice of Canada carries weight and conviction. We have a reputation for telling frankly those countries in the Warsaw Pact whose human rights record is wanting, that they cannot be fully accepted as reliable members of the international community until they live up to their commitments. But we now face a new situation. Some Warsaw Pact countries are beginning to make such substantial changes that we are obliged to rethink our relationship with them. We do not forget the human rights concerns which remain to be satisfied; we do not forget the aspirations of nationalities such as those you represent. But we must recognize change where change has occured. We must seek new ways to strengthen the processes already under way We must encourage the Soviet Union and its neighbours to realize that their best interests are served by living within a family of nations, where people are ruled by governments of their own choosing, associated freely according to their own identified national interests. During the seventeenth Baltic Evening at the Confederation Hall, West wing of Canada's Parliament in Ottawa, the legal counsel of the Baltic Federation in Canada Mr. William J. H. Hough also delivered an impressive address which we hope to publish in our next English Section of MLB Ziņotājs. The musical program of the evening was provided by the internationally well known Latvian pianist Arthur Ozolins. Many senators and MPs attended the splendid event. Over a hundred of Estonians, Latvians and Lithuanians had a golden opportunity to meet them. The organizing committee had done an excellent job indeed."]

Name: 33_garezers_camp_summer_university
Representative_Docs: ['Summer High Schools A traditional right of passage for Latvian American youth has been attendance at one of three four-year summer high school programs in the U.S.. Garezers, the oldest and largest such program; Beverīna on the East Coast or Kursa on the West Coast. Although Beverīna is no longer active, both Garezers and Kursa continue to offer high-quality, six week residential programs for Latvian American high school students. These programs have been joined by the Saulgrieži program in Latvia, which is an effort to integrate emigre Latvian educational experience, and Latvian American youth, with their counterparts in our now-free homeland. Garezera vasaras vidusskola (GW) Director Ilze Kreišmane, 3266 Rinda Ln, Cincinnati, OH 45239. Tel: (513) 923-1239 The school session runs from July 7 through August 18. Drawing, stained-glass work, jewelry making and pottery craft are among the extracurricular activities offered to students. The camp is located at the Latvian Center Garezers (67732 Lone Tree Rd, Three Rivers, MI 49093). Kursas vasaras vidusskola Director Biruta Zommere, PO Box 44, Arch Cape, OR 97102. Tel: (507)436-1708 The school session runs from June 30 through August 10. Special attention will be given to students with minimal or no Latvian language skills. The school is located at the West Coast Latvian Education Center, located in Washington state (PO Box 1221, Shehon, WA 98584). Saulgriežu vasaras vidusskola Director Gundega Michele, 1503 E 56th St, Chicago, IL 60637. Tel: (312) 288-3889 The four-week program will run from July 1-July 28, at the Apgulde agricultural school 12km south of the Latvian town of Dobele. Swimming, horseback riding and other sporting activities ivill be available at the school, which is conveniently located on a rail line from Riga. Family Camps Another Latvian American summertime tradition is the Trīsreiztrīs (3x3) camp. These camps offer Latvian Americans of all ages, as well as Latvians in Latvia itself, a relaxing week of companionship with other Latvians as well as the opportunity to revitalize one\'s "Latvianness". 3x3 camps have come to play a i/ital role in the efforts of many young Latvian American families -vishing to maintain their special ethnic heritage and pass it on to their children, while living in societies that value conformity. This summer, Trisreiztris camps will be held at the Latvian Center Garezers in Michigan and the New York Latvian Ev. Lutheran Church Camp in New York state, from August 18-August 24. Camps will also be held in Latvia, at Viļaka in northeastern Latvia from July 7-July 14, and Jelgava from July 21-July 28. Information on all of these family camsjs available from Program Director Līga Ruperte (2141 Brunsink NE, Grand Rapids, Ml 49503; tel. 616-456-8023). #', 'Summer is almost here – have you decided where to send your children for summer camp? The ALA Office of Education has compiled a list of Latvian summer camps, both for primary- and secondary-school aged children. For more information on a specific program or to request application materials, please contact the camp director listed, for more information on Latvian educational opportunities in the U.S., call the ALA Office of Education at (301) 340-8719. Childrens Camps Garezers Childrens Camp Director Lelde Brunovska, 2329 Warrensvllle Center Rd, University Heights, OH 44118. Tel: (216)871-9659 The six week season is broken up into three sessions: from July 7-July 20, July 21-August 3, and August 4-August 18. The third session has traditionally focused on children with minimal or no knowledge of the Latvian language. The camp is located at the Latvian Center Garezers (67732 Lone Tree Rd, Three Rivers, MI 49093). Catskilts Childrens Camp c/o New York Latvian Ev. Lutheran Church, 564 Second St, Brooklyn, NY 11215 Latvian speaking primary school children can spend one, two or three weeks at this camp, from July 14-August 3. Non-Latvian speakers are the focus of the last two weeks of the camp season, from August 4-August 17. Younger children are invited to participate in the īkšķīši (Tom Thumb) program, for one week periods from July 21-July 27, and July 28-August 3; Latvian nonspeakers get special attention from August 3-August 10. Celebrating its 40th year, the camp is located on the grounds of the Latvian Church Camp (Elka Park, NY 12427). Mežotne Summer Camp Director Jānis Rogainis, 15119 63rd Aye W, Edmonds, WA 98026. Tel: (206) 742-8954 Mežotne, whose name is taken from a famous ancient settlement in Latvia, will be in session for one week, from August 11-August 18. Campers are divided into two groups: children from 6-13 years old, and so-called Mazputniņi (Little Birds) of 3-5 years of age, who are accompanied by at least one parent or relative. The emphasis of the camp is on the Latvian language and traditions within a traditional camp environment; as a result, the camp director does ask that potential campers begin attending local Latvian schools prior to coming to camp. The camp is located at the West Coast Latvian Education Center in Washington state (PO Box 1221, Shekon, WA 98584). Summer Camps Recharge Latvian "Batteries"', 'So, how do you spend a summer with your children if you don\'t want to, can\'t afford to, or don\'t know how you\'d spend a few summer months with your children in Latvia? How can you get them to hear and speak Latvian for a week, two, three or six? Why you can send them to a Latvian summer camp! There are a few Latvian camps, from coast to coast in the US and Canada. So for those who want a Latvian cultural "booster shot" of sorts, summer is the time to tray el and go to camp. From mid-June to mid-August Latvian parRita Pelēce, nts and their children are on the road traveling to camp in hopes that their children will make some new friends and speak a little bit more Latvian than they do at home. There is nothing quite like camp life that can forge life long memories and "forever" friendships. And many of the children do try a bit harder to speak Latvian, if only to other adults and authority figures. But most of all, camp is fun! I never attended the summer camp there, I went further up the road to the outskirts of Holland Michigan, and went to a quaint little camp called "Mežvidi". It was idellyic or at least that is how it stands out in my mind. Even as a young child of 8 I could almost see the entire camp perimeters upon entering. As we drove into the camp down the dirt road the first thing I would see was a large oak tree. It really was old, maybe Mineapole There is a lot more to being Latvian not what they call a "dizozols" in Latvia, but definitely big and old enough that it took about 3-4 of us kias to wrap our arms around it! Under the oak was a crooked bench. At the oak, the road split, but only to get around it. Once we were around the oak it was time to park the car. No one had vans or rocket-boxes back then, but a few smart Latvian families had station wagons and conversion vans to haul us kids from Minneapolis to Holland for 10-12 hours at 55 MPH, the legal highway limit at the time. "Mežvidi" or the "Middle of the Woods" had a few old buildings, a house where the girls slept up on the hill. And very cool multi-car garage that haa been converted minimally to house the boys. Now that was a cool place to sleep! Past these two buildings one could spot the flag mast, where we\'d go each momeing to "salute" the flags of Latvia and America, hear a short morning greeting by the camp director, someone\'s grandmother, I was sure at the time. But the best part was the morning greeting! Looking back it was very "scout" or "militaristic". We\'d march in a line with our cabin mates to the flag clearing and greet the director, who was always there already waiting for us and any other group whod beat us to the punch. LABRIT SORIT!" (Good morning, this morning!) was the main call, but every now and then the older boys or girls would march up they would add "Lai bikses kritf (May your pants fall down!) I couldn\'t wait until I was old enough to punch out that line! About a quarter way around the lake a traditional Latvian sauna, a "pirts", was built. It looked like it had been modeled after a pirts from a Latvian novel or play. My eyes could hardly believe it was real the first summer I saw it. It even had a well next to it, with a hanging bucket. (Though I don\'t remember that being functional. We just went to the lake with a bucket for water instead.) What I do remember is going in the pirts for the first time, and being told to leave my swimsuit on. It was a bit of a shock as I grew up going to a neighboring Latvian family\'s pirts on Saturday nights and knew that one did not wear a polyester swimsuit into the pirts. Thinking back about "Mežvidi" I really think it was the best place one could send their child of 8 or 12 for two weeks 500 plus miles from home. Then comes Garezers. I started going to Garezers when I was old enough to go to the Vidusskola, the Middle School, or High School. Vasara 2011 And frankly, it was quite shock. It was big. It was a real ha mile walk along a tar road to th mess hall. No more dirt roads The Viddusskola was separatee from the camp and it felt like its own world. Except for when w went to eat or swim. We ate i shifts, and I would see my young er sister waiting outside the mess hall as I exited for the Kantine fo treats. She was at camp, I was i Vidusskola. Never the two sha meet. At least that\'s how it fe back then. Fast forward 20 years and I\'m back in Garezers. But this time have my mother, a grandmothe now, and my daughter of 4 alone We have siened ud for 3x2 ily camp, ar so they say. Its really a language and culture camp, and three generations are able to attend. In reality, if you have infants and toddlers along, it turns into is a marathon of sorts. We wake up to the sound of Latvian music being played over the speaker system, someone talking about tne upcoming days events and a gentle push to get out of bed, and get to breakfast at the mess hall. Camp activities start at 9am and go until noon, but if you have a young child I quickly learned to drop any expectations of learning to throw a pot in ceramics or to get informed on the latest political Nometne at Garezers musings from Latvia. By the time we ate, changed a pull-up and walked up to the child-care area, I was ready for a break! (And some "real coffee!) I would drop off my daughter, and a few years later, my son, and take off back to the Vidusskola for a break. I would pick up the room, make myself a stronger cup of coffee and either nap or read until lunchtime. Because after lunch the kids went swimming and I wanted to go along and get some sun and have some fun as well. I could go on about my years of going to 3x3 as my children have grown from infant to preteen, but basically each time it\'s been über tiring and extremely fun and rewarding at the same time. Over the years I\'ve learned to just enjoy hand building with clay a famand made some new and meaningful friendships. At some point I decide I will not bring my children to Nometne at Garezers. What do I do instead? I head to the Catskills in NY state. A friend of mine there, whose children are of similar age, has summoned me there ana I am only too happy to go on this wild three day road trip with my mother and three small children. (Bless your heart mom!) I know that Katskili are smaller than Garezers only due to the fact that I attended a Latvian World Wide Scout and Girl Guide Jamboree at the age of 12. It rocked! Big woods and a small lake I could almost see from the welcome gate. It was like Mežvidi, small and cozy. I decided that it is better for my kids to be at a small camp for their first overnight camp experience and for me to hang with a dear friend for two weeks in the woods. I was right. Katskili was a great camp experience for all of us, grandma included! I do it all again the following year, only this time I look for cheap flights and sack the 3 day drive from MN to NY. Also a good choice. Fast forward another 5 years or so and I am back at Garezers again. This time to send my 12 year old extravert to Sagskola, a middle school version of camp. Not Viddusskola and not Nometne, but a new development, probably started by some smart parent-teacher-volunteer that knew the research on Middle School development. Good call. Like a typical hormonal Middle Schooler she dragged her heels into the van telling me that she never had intendedto go to camp at Garezers. My middle child is in Nometne and with a close friend from our city of Minneapolis. He is hard to please, but in general, even with a broken collar bone and arm in a sling, the boy toughs it out. The result? The two weeks were a hesitant thumbs up. Perfect. My youngest, always trying to keep up with the older two announces to me on day two of the Preschool day-amp, Bizßiz berni, that "this is for babies" and she wants to march straight over to the sleep over part of the camp. Perfect. Or is it? All is perfect, except that I have not registered her for the sleepover camp, but the day camp tor a full 2 weeks. She is only 6 after all and how could I have expected her to sleep in a cabin with girls she didn\'t know for 2 weeks? Never mind that I am in Atbalsis (a private Latvian lodging area just down the road and not a part of Garezers. In fact, people can Rita Pelēcis and Jauno Laiks editor Dainis Mjartāns at 3x3 camp in Garezers fļo to Garezers for years and never md out about Atbalsis. Its a secret resort of sorts. And quieter at night than Dzintari, an area of Garezers. Oops! Now you know. ) and plan to stay the entire 2 weeks they are in camp. I have my mom and our one year old small dog, Rimi (named after our trip to Latvia last summer) along. So enough with day-camp says the 6 year old and she convinces me to "go ask" when she can start sleeping over at "real" camp. So, off I go to speak to the Camp Director. Lucky for us there were a few other 6 year old girls whose parents only signed them up for the first week of the two week camp. We get one of their slots and after a few tears, mainly mine, they are all sleeping away from me. WTiat to do? What to think? How to spend the rest of my time at Garezers and Atbalsis? I really hadn\'t planned for this much "free" time. The next day I took a little walk and saw someone sporting a Tshirt with the wording "Kas notiek Garezera, Lai paliek Garezera". Interesting. A direct translation from "What happens at X, stays at X." A very American quote in my mind and not Latvian at all. Later I see that same Tshirt for sale at the Kantine, a dark little shop with half empty shelves and loads of artificially colored candy and Schwann\'s ice cream for sale. All for the kiddies after lunch while at camp. Is this Tshirt trying to tell me something? Is it precisely this reason I have stayed away from Garezers all these years? There are areas in Garezers, more persisely, Dzintari, that have quite the reputation for heavy partying on weekends. Especially 4/2 Volleyball weekend. I had this "Been there, done that" attitude all these years about Garezers and especially about Vollevball weekend. I just didn\'t fed like I had to go back to all that drinking and partying. And I didn\'t. After I graduated from Vidusskola, I went to work at Kursa, the Washington State High School camp. There is a lot more to Garezers. And there is a lot more to being Latvian. And speaking in Latvian, or at least trying to speak it while in Garezers or trying to learn it, trying to improve your vocabulary, trying to read it (try and modern novel like a Dan Brown translation!), trying to write, trying to keep up on current events and elections and politics (There is a lot to keep up on right now!), trying to learn new songs (from Gustavo\'s rap, Ilgi or Suitu Sievas), is a large part of that. Trying just a little bit, even if its hard. Or because it is hard. Drinking a case of beer and sing ing "Alus Ezeriņš" just doesn\'t cut it in this Latvian\'s eyes. Culural identity is very personal and can be very profound. We all have our own stories and our own voices and we can chose how to use them. Because "Kas notiek Garezera" (What happens in Garezers.) is a lot of really neat stuff that many, many people put a lot of effort in to all year long, not just from June to August. "Lai paliek Garezera" (Let it stay in Garezers.) is cutting ourselves short. Bring it home, wherever your home may be, and be that proud Latvian all year, not just for a few summer weeks. And be grateful that you have a place like Garezers to come to and maybe even take it a step further one summer, and find a summer camp in Latvia.']

Name: 34_withdrawal_skrunda_russian_radar
Representative_Docs: ['Negotiations between the Russian Federation and Latvia on the withdrawal of former Soviet armed forces from Latvian territory may finally be reaching a decisive stage, if the events of the past few weeks are any indication. Between the visit to Latvia of U.S. Secretary of State Warren Christopher, approval of a resolution renewing the U.N. General Assembly\'s call for the speedy conclusion of agreements, including timetables, for an "early, orderly and complete" withdrawal from Latvia and Estonia, and the Russian delegation\'s proposals to move up their preferred withdrawal date in return for Latvian flexibility on the issue of the Skrunda radar base, the long-stagnant negotiations now seem to have a new urgency. Secretary of State Christopher was in Riga October 26-27, at the end of a tour which took him to Belorus, Ukraine, Kazakhstan and Russia. In Riga, Christopher met with President Ulmanis and Prime Minister Birkavs, all three Baltic Foreign Ministers (Andrejevs, Trivimi Velliste of Estonia and Povilas Gylys of Lithuania), and with representatives of Latvia\'s Russian-speaking population. Christopher\'s trip emphasized America\'s keen interest in seeing a solution found to the Russian troop issue, as Christopher repeated the U.S. position of supporting the immediate, complete and orderly withdrawal of the Russian military from Latvia. However, Christopher was also careful not to take a public stand concerning the Skrunda early warning radar base, saying that was an issue for Latvia and Russia to resolve amongst themselves. During his meeting with Birkavs, Christopher did discuss Skrunda, how long it could take to fully disassemble the radar and needed international guarantees of Latvian security during the period of disassembly. Christopher also tried to walk a tightrope on the sensitive issue of Russia\'s accusations of discrimination against Latvia\'s Russian speakers. Christopher praised Latvia\'s readiness to accept the opening of a CSCE mission and noted that international observers have yet to discover any human rights violations in Latvia. At the same time, he said the U.S. hopes that Latvia will soon deal with "practical issues" tied to the Russianspeaking minority. In light of the fact that Christopher had come to Latvia from Moscow, it is not surprising that he would want to learn more about issues Troop Withdrawal Talks Reach Decisive Stage? raised there, including the discrimination issue. However, one hopes that the Secretary\'s meeting with the Russian-speakers will not be used to lend credence to Russia\'s discredited "human rights" campaign against Latvia and Estonia. Prime Minister Birkavs tried, and succeeded, in returning the focus of debate to the troop withdrawal with a speech before the U.N. General Assembly on November 15, to introduce a resolution on the "Complete withdrawal of foreign military forces from the territories of the Baltics States." Birkavs said "the main obstacle to successful development and the most potent factor of instability in the Baltic region still remains the presence of Russian troops on the territory of two sovereign states — Latvia and Estonia." Birkavs also noted that, in spite of Russian promises and Latvia\'s good faith efforts to reach a reasonable solution through bilateral negotiations, Russia had not yet made "significant progress... in fulfilling these requirements." Russia\'s demands have, in fact, often contradicted their promises: Russia has yet to agree to a withdrawal timetable, blocking an early withdrawal; facilities that Russia has evacuated were left in anything but an orderly condition; and Russia has maintained its demand to keep several bases, giving the lie to the promise of a complete withdrawal. Birkavs paid special attention to the Skrunda radar, noting that "these \'strategic\' facilities are perceived [in Latvia] as military bases which could once again serve as a pretext for the occupation of Latvia, as was the case in 1940." However, Birkavs said Latvia would consider extending the relocation deadline of any such base if it is provided convincing international security guarantees and "a reasonably tight internationally controlled schedule of such relocation." The Latvian government\'s willingness to consider such a compromise was given an additional boost by proposals made by Russia during the latest negotiating session. The Russian delegation, led by chief negotiator Sergei Zotov, said that Moscow was willing to remove all of its troops from Latvia by August 31, 1994, four months earlier than its originally stated deadline of December 31. Russia also dropped its demand to keep bases in Ventspils and Liepāja, while reducing the period of time it wanted to keep the Skrunda radar from 10-12 years to six. As Latvian chief negotiator Mārtiņš Virsis stated, "The Russian delegation has for the first time really made concrete positive proposals." Latvia seems very aware of the limits to i flexibility, however. Prime Minister Birkavs stated that "we do understand Russia\'s strategic need and we are flexible enough to discuss a transitio period, but it cannot be until the end of th century... The way we see it in Latvia, the Skrun da station is a mousetrap, Russia is the cat anc Latvia is the mouse." Latest word has it tha Latvia has extended this latest round of talks unt December 3, in order to fully consider its positio on this latest proposal. As the rhetorical fur continues to fly o the troop issue, the evidence is pointing toward a potential break in the two-year old talks. Bot sides seem willing now to take the steps necessary to secure the withdrawal of Russian troops from Latvia, which will remove an 18,000 person strong barrier to the resolution of critical issues such as Latvia\'s economic crisis and the status of Latvia\'s many non-citizen residents. As long as international pressure remains strong on Russia to withdraw its troops from Latvia, one can finally begin to hope that Latvia will be free of foreign occupying armed forces by the time we celebrate Mārtiņš Zvaners', 'CHICAGO LATVIAN NEWSLETTER Latvia, Russia Initial Troop Withdrawal Accords The two year odyssey of on-again, off-again negotiations between Latvia and Russia on the withdrawal of former Soviet armed forces from Latvia has come to an end. Negotiating teams representing Latvia and Russia initialed on March 15 the "Republic of Latvia and Russian Federation Treaty On The Regulations, Terms and Procedures For The Complete Withdrawal Of Russian Federation Armed Forces From The Territory Of The Republic of Latvia And The Legal Status Of These Armed Forces During The Period Of Withdrawal". Side agreements on the legal status of the Skrunda early-warning radar station and the social security of retired Russian military personnel and dependents and of active duty Russian military personnel and dependents until their withdrawal were also initialed. Despite several points of intense controversy and lack of clarity, the initialing of the treaty brings the final removal of. the armed forces that have illegally occupied the territory of Latvia since World War II that much closer to becoming a fact. The treaty, initialed by Latvian Chief Negotiator Mārtiņš Virsis and his Russian counterpart Sergei Zotov, commits Russia to the complete withdrawal of its armed forces by August 31, 1994. The withdrawal is to take place according to a timetable prepared by Russian authorities, who say the timetable is already being used to regulate the ongoing troop pullout. A copy of the timetable will be delivered to Latvia when the agreements are signed, possibly in late April. After being signed by the two Presidents, the treaty and side agreements must still be ratified by the respective parliaments — which is not at all assured. Skrunda Base Agreement Questioned The troop withdrawal treaty itself, as significant as it is for the removal of Russia\'s remaining 12,000-13,000 troops and officers, has been overshadowed by intense criticism in Latvia of the three side agreements — especially that concerning the Skrunda radar facility. April 1994 – Vol. XIV, No. 4 The side agreement on Skrunda, which would enter into force on September 1, 1994 only if all Russian Federation armed forces are actually withdrawn from Latvia, would allow Russia to continue operating the radar facility for four years, or until a replacement facility is brought on line, whichever comes first. Russia would then be allowed an additional 18 months to dismantle the facility and remove it from Latvia. During the period covered by the agreement, the radar facility would be treated as a military facility under civilian control, which will be leased from Latvia by Russia for an as-yet undetermined annual payment — not as a military base. The radar facility will be staffed by 798 personnel, of whom, to begin with, 599 would be military specialists and 199 civilian employees. The agreement calls for Russia to work towards replacing the military specialists with qualified civilian employees who are Latvian citizens or permanent residents. External security at the facility will be carried out by Latvian personnel, while Russia would be allowed to employ up to 89 military specialists as an internal security force. Oversight of the Skrunda agreement would be entrusted to a Joint Commission made up of both Russian and Latvian representatives, as weH as a representative or representatives of a mutually acceptable international organization, in which both countries are members. The Joint Commission would have the power to arbitrate any issue referred to it, with its decisions binding on both sides. Both scheduled and surprise inspections by the CSCE will also take place, with no more than two of each per year, as well as three days after the closure of the facility and on the day that the treaty loses force. Surprise inspections would take place on the initiative of Latvia. The Tēvzemei un brīvībai movement has called the Skrunda side agreement a violation of Latvia\'s law on international agreements, which states that the Saeima alone has the right to allow a foreign military force to remain on Latvian soil. It considers the maintenance of the Skrunda facility to represent just such an agreement. Twenty two members of the National Bloc in the Saeima also sent a letter to President Guntis Ulmanis pointing this out. Their letter called on Ulmanis to refuse to sign the treaty and agreements, and instead turn the decision on the treaty and agreements over to the people through a referendum, which could be held in conjunction with the upcoming local government elections. Social Guarantees Unpopular The side agreements on social guarantees are causing no less controversy than the Skrunda agreement. The issue here is the decision by Latvia to allow Soviet military pensioners, who retired prior to Russia assuming control of the armed forces in Latvia on January 28, 1992, and their dependents to reside in Latvia. Latvia would recognize the rights of these people to continue to use their homes, as well as confirm their rights to ownership and, if Latvian law will allow, to purchase their homes. Russia will receive a one-time payment from Latvia for living space freed up by military personnel returning to Russia, to support the housing of these people in Russia. In addition, Latvia will help Russia to build apartments and other facilities with funding from international organizations and third nations. The exchange of living quarters between retirees and military personnel leaving Latvia and Russian Federation residents coming to Latvia will also be encouraged. Russia will continue to pay the pensions of military retirees in Latvia. However, if a military retiree decides to take advantage of any pension they may have earned from the Latvian government, they forfeit their Russian military pension for as long as they receive payments from Latvia. Military retirees would be entitled to the same medical and dental services which Latvia gives its retirees. These services would be paid for by the Russian government, which would also allow Latvia to take over military hospitals in the Riga area for use to serve military retirees. Betrayal. . . Or Realism? The Latvian National Independence Movement (LNNK) board called the agreements a betrayal of Latvian national interests, as they legalize the occupation of Latvia and give Soviet military pensioners who retired before January 28, 1992 the opportunity to remain in Latvia and, in time, apply for Latvian citizenship. LNNK Saeima faction leader and Foreign Affairs Commission Chairman Aleksandrs Kiršteins told Diena on March 17 that "the issue is in the Latvian Constitution. Many have forgotten that four paragraphs of the Constitution were renewed on May 4,1990, which asserted that Latvia is an independent state... In reference to the officers, this treaty is simply unconstitutional.,, The treaty and side agreements were welcomed by the CSCE and European Union, as well as the U.S., British, Finnish, French, Polish, Swedish and Estonian governments. The European Union said it "considers the agreements...an important step towards achieving the complete withdrawal of Russian troops from the Baltic States called for in the 1992 CSCE Helsinki document, and towards the establishment of good, cooperative relations between Latvia and Russia." Prominent Latvian government officials acknowledge how difficult the agreements are for Latvia to accept, but note that the treaty and side agreements offer a last best hope for securing the withdrawal of Russian armed forces and finalizing Latvia\'s break with Moscow. Justice Minister Egils Levits noted that Russia has, by initialing the agreements, firmly fixed a withdrawal deadline and agreed to turn over the two new buildings of the uncompleted phased-array radar at Skrunda to Latvia immediately after the agreements are signed. Levits called the treaty "a notable achievement of Latvian diplomacy... I think future historians will term this treaty an achievement, much as has happened to the 1920 Russia-Latvia treaty." Foreign Minister Georgs Andrejevs, interviewed by ITAR/TASS during a trip to Italy, referred to the international aspect of how agreement was reached. "As far as possible, we heeded to pieces of advice from across the world, above all from the United States. We know that this compromise version was worked out at the Moscow summit between Presidents Boris Yeltsin and Bill Clinton. Then came our visit to Washington. During this visit, we received necessary explanations about why we should not be scared with this version." Andrejevs recognizes how painful the Skrunda agreement is to Latvians, but hopes that realism will win out. "We have the bitter experience of 1939 related to military bases stationed in Latvia. It was not very long ago. However, we need to change Latvian people\'s mentalities. We should explain that times have changed, and Russia needs the Skrunda radar only as a means of obtaining information and not as a military installation. There is quite strong opposition in Latvia... Nevertheless, we should display political realism in our onward advance." Mārtiņš Zvaners', 'The Embassy of Latvia, 4325 17th St. NW, Washington, D. C, U. S. A. «202-726-8213, has released the following: U.S. GOVERNMENT STATEMENTS CONCERNING THE LATVIAN-RUSSIAN TROOP WITHDRAWAL AGREEMENTS Washington – President Clinton congratulated Latvian President Guntis Ulmanis and Russian President Boris Yeltsin April 30 for their agreement on Russian troop withdrawal from Latvia by August 31. 1994 Following is the text of the statement issued by the Office of the Press Secretary of the Whiute House "I applaud today\'s agreement signed by Latvian President Guntis Ulmanis and Russian President Boris Yeltsin that will lead to the withdrawal of Russian military forces from the territory of the Republic of Latvia by August 31, 1994. I have contacted both leaders to offer my personal congratulations for their vision and statemanahip in concluding this historic accord. Since the early days of this administration, among my highest foreign policy priorities has been promoting agreement on an orderly withdrawal of Russian forces from the Baltic countries. I discussed this frequently with President Yeltsin and President Ulmanis. The United States has played an active role with both parties during the course of the Latvian-Russian negotiations. I believe that our engagement with both aides, along with the support provided by other countries, in particular Sweden, has played a constructive role in bringing this agreement to a successful conclusion. Over the course of their negotiations, both the Latvian and Russian governments displayed a pragmatic approach to resolving their differences. The understanding the this document embodies, including the continued operation of the radar installiatlon at Skrunda as a civilian facility, are testimony to the determination of both sides to conclude an agreement that responds to Russian concerns while affirming Latvia\'s full and unrestricted sovereignty and promoting its Mnegration into the world community. The agreement between Latvia and Russia opens the door to a more normal relationship between the two countries. It constitutes an important contribution to overall stability In the Baltic region and to European security as a whole. I hope the this agreement also will help stimulate a speedy conclusion of the troop withdrawal negotiations between Estonia and Russia. " US Department of State spokesman on Latvian-Russian troop withdrawal agreement: "We are delighted that on April 30 Latvian President Guntis Ulmanis and Russian President Boris Yeltsin signed an agreement m Moscow on the withdrawal of Russian troops from Latvia by August 31. 1994. We applauded both the Latvian and Russian Government for taking a constructive, pragmatic approach to resolving their differences We look forward to agreements implemented smoothly The U S. actively promoted the withdrawal of all Russian forces from the Baltics It is our hope that the Russian-Latvian agreement will pave the way for an agreement soon between Estonia and Latvia President Clinton and Secretary Christopher were personally engaged in contributing to an early resolution of the troop withdrawal issues In order to promote the troop withdrawal talks, the U S last year also established a 160 million program for the construction of housing for demobilizing Russian officers returning from the Baltics and elsewhere. Under this program housing vouchers will also be made available fot immediate use by retired Russian officers who want to depart Latvia and Estonia to resettle m Russia A key breakthrough in the negotiations was President Clinton\'s suggested compromise for the continued operation and subsequent dismantlement of the Skrunda radar facility, which provided the basis for the draft agreements that were initialed on March 15 The United States has also commited $ 2 million to assist with the prompt dismantlement of the large phased-array radar at Skrunda Sweden already has commited $ 1 million in the international effort for the same purpose We believe the signing of the agreement is a maio< contribution to legional stability and harmony and also a succesfull example of a concentrated effort both by individual states and by the CSCE to help bring a difficult issue to a satisfactory resolution These statements clearly indicate the pressures which the super powers have applied to The Republic of Latvia No doubt the debate about these signings will be harsh and emotional. Particularly because some of the agreements take effect immediately. The agreements must be ratified by the Russian and Latvian parliaments It is not clear how soon this will be done but it appears that passage in the Latvian Saeima may be secured relatively easily Time will tell. Your comments on the matter are most welcome Ed.']

Name: 35_olympic_chess_champion_game
Representative_Docs: ['THE CHICAGO LATVIAN NEWSLETTER 33 LATVIAN ATHLETES GO TO NAGANO The Latvian Olympic committee confirmed the members of the Latvian National teem for the Olympic games in Nagano. The team includes 33 athletes and their coaches plus 12 accredited members who will not live in the Olympic village. This year s Olympic team is the largest yet from Latvia to participate in the Olympic games. SANDIS PRŪSIS HOPES TO GET A MEDAL IN NAGANO In the opening ceremony of the XVIII Olympic games the Latvian flag was carried by Sandis Prūsis from the Latvian bobsledding team. So far only the bobsledders have had the honor to carry the National flag in the Winter Olympic games. So far, he is the biggest hope to get an Olympic medal for Latvia. JĒKABS NĀKUMS FINISHES FOURTH. In the fifth round of the World cup in Biathlon in the Italian village of Antersalev the Latvian biathlonist Jēkabs Nākums from Alūksne took the fourth place in the 20 km distance. THE NEW PLENARY SESSIONS AUDITORIUM On January 15th the new Plenary Sessions Auditorium at the Parliament was reopened. The oldest parliamentarian deputy Guntars Grinblats from "Tēvzemei un Brīvībai\'VLNNK was the one to carry the flag of the Republic of Latvia into the newly renovated auditorium. After the National Anthem was played, the auditorium was considered to be opened. The reconstruction of the auditorium cost 655 thousand Lats. THE HOMEPAGE OF THE CHARTER BETWEEN USA AND THE BALTIC STATES The U.S. Information Agency (U.5.1.A.) informed that the home-page address of the NEWS FROM LATVIA March 1998 reported by Charter between USA and the Baltic countries is www.usia.gov/regional/eur/baltics/ homepage.htm. One can also find materials regarding this matter on the home-page of U.S.I.A. in Riga. The address of the U.S.I.A. is www.usis.bkc.lv. ONE MORE NEW POLITICAL FORCE IN LATVIA Representing 64 different professions a new political party "The New Party" (Jaunā partija) has announced the date of it\'s foundation. It will take place on March 14th. Many businessmen are linked to this new political force. The members of the party will include such prominent people as composer Raimonds Pauls, the executive director of Varner/Hakon Invest Ainārs Šlesers, neurosurgeon Aris Auders, the former Minister of the Republic of Latvia Jānis Āboltiņš and many others. So far there are 500 active supporters of \'The New Party ", but they hope that they will be the biggest political force in Latvia and will reach 10 000 members. This however seems a very unrealistic number, because the biggest party "Latvian Way" (Latvijas ceļš) has only about 400 members. So far two former KGB agents have joined the supporters of "The New Party". THE MEETING OF PRIME MINISTERS OF THE BALTIC SEA COUNTRIES. On January 22nd and 23rd 11 Prime ministers from the countries around the Baltic Sea arrived in Rīga to the annual conference. Two of them – the Prime Minister of Germany Helmut Kohl and the Prime Minister of Russia Viktor Chernomirdin – arrived in Rīga for the first time. This was one of the most important meetings held in Rīga. The Minister of Justice Dzintars Rasnačs while meeting with the Prime Minister of Russia discussed how Latvia could get back the KGB documents related to the deportations of Latvians. in Riga Šarlote Līduma THE OCCUPATION OF LATVIA According to the letter of Russian Minister of Foreign Affairs addressed to the Duma (Valsts domei) there was no such thing as the occupation of Latvia. RUSSIANS ATTACK LATVIAN HIGH SCHOOL ON THE DAY OF COMMEMORATION On January 20th the main activities of commemoration of the January 1991 barricades took place. The President of the Republic of Latvia Guntis Ulmanis, the chairman of the Parliament of Latvia Alfreds Čepānis and representatives from the government and the town council of Rīga placed flowers at the Monument of Freedom. Many other related activities took place, such as the opening of the Commemoration center of 1991 .This is the first time when the commemoration of the barricades was officially celebrated. The same afternoon about 30 – 40 Russian teenagers armed with sticks and clubs attempted to attack the Latvian high school Nr. 25 on Maskavas St. Thanks to the timely arrival of the National Guards nobody was hurt. LITHUANIA JOINS THE CENTRAL EUROPEAN TIME ZONE Lithuania will join The Central European time zone on March 29th at 2 a.m. After that Lithuania will be in the I Time zone according to the Greenwich time. This time zone includes such countries as Germany, France, Belgium, Netherlands, Italy, Spain, Austria, Switzerland, Norway, Sweden, Denmark, Poland and Czech Republic. For the time being Lithuania as well as Latvia, Estonia, Finland, Belarus, and Ukraine are in the II time zone. The Lithuanian government considers that the Central European time zone will positively affect Lithuania\'s relationships with other countries in the I time zone. Lithuania already was in the I time zone before the occupation by U.S.S.R. in 1940. PRESIDENT NEEDS A NEW BILL The President of the Republic of Latvia might have a right in the future to the amnesty of persons sentenced to death penalty even if they will fail to submit the request for amnesty. The parliament of Latvia, Saeima has to pass this bill as soon as possible because since the changes included in Satversme in December 1997, the process of amnesty has been stopped. Up today the President of the Republic of Latvia has received more than 200 requests for amnesty. However, until the new bill is passed he can not review them. THE NEW ALTERNATIVE TO MANDATORY MILITARY SERVICE The Ministry of Defense has introduced a project which contains a curriculum for teaching the basic military skills in the higher academic institutions. It is supposed to be an alternative to the mandatory military service. If a student will regularly attend the military classes, he will not have to do the mandatory service. The earliest when this project will be effective is 1999. Prior to that, the precise data about the need for specialized military personnel in Latvia has to be gathered. RĪGA WILL HOST MORE THAN 3000 INTERNATIONAL FINANCIERS It is confirmed now that the European Bank of Reconstruction and Development (EBRD) will hold its annual meeting in Rīga, reports the Ministry of Finances of the Republic of Latvia. The proxies of the EBRD almost unanimously voted to hold the meeting in the capital of Latvia. This meeting will take place on May 21 and 22 in the year 2000. Some 3000 – 4000 influential financiers from more than 50 countries are expected to arrive in Rīga for the meeting. THE ALLIES RECOGNIZE LATVIA DE lURE 77 YEARS AGO On January 23rd the celebration of the 77th anniversary of recognition of the Republic of Latvia de iure took place. On August 26, 1921 the independence of Latvia and Estonia de iure was acknowledged by The Supreme Council of The Allies which at that time consisted of England, France, Italy and Japan. The Minister of Foreign Affairs of the Republic of Latvia and other representatives from the Ministry of Foreign Affairs laid flowers in the Meža kapi cemetery at the monuments of J.Čakste and Z. Meierovics. MURDERED LATVIANS HONORED NEAR MOSCOW On February 3rd in the city of Butov, near Moscow the first meeting was held to honor the 263 Latvians shot during the Stalin\'s campaign of annihilation. The relatives of these Latvian victims, as well as Latvians living in Moscow and the representatives from the Embassy of Latvia in Russia honored the victims. 73 000 RUSSIAN LATVIANS MURDERED DURING STALIN\'S ETHNIC CLEANSING On February 3rd the Commission of human rights and public sector supported the proposal by the organization of The Latvians in the East about declaring the first Monday of December as a commemoration day of Latvians killed in Russia. However this proposal was not accepted because there is no precise information about the date of December 1937 when the actual genocide against Latvians took place. According to the commission of Latvian parliament Saeima, in a two year period some 73 000 Latvians had been killed because of their nationality. ALFREDS RUBIKS VISITS MOSCOW The former Latvian communist leader Alfreds Rubiks is on a visit to Moscow. In the train station he was met not only by his supporters in Russia but also by Russian top communist leaders. During his visit he met with the chairman of The Duma Genadij Seljeznow and the mayor of Moscow City, Yurij Luzhkow. After a meeting with the fraction of Communists in The Russian Parliament A. Rubiks announced the renewal of a united communist party. THE CONSUMPTION OF ALCOHOLIC BEVERAGES IN 1997 According to statistics during the first nine months of 1997, 61% of alcohol consumed in Latvia was vodka and liqueur, 20% – beer, 10% wine, 7% cognac and brandy and 2% – champagne. Amount of consumed alcohol per capita has been 5,4 liters. Altogether the consumption of vodka and wine have decreased, but the consumption of cognac and brandy increased. EU IS INTERESTED IN THE LATVIAN CRAYFISH During the summer of 1996 within the European Union\'s program PHARE research was con■ ducted about the prospects of fish breeding and crayfish breeding in Latvia. The experts came to the conclusion that the crayfish breeding is one of the most prospective fields of aquaculture. Latvia is rich with lakes, rivers and ponds where top quality crayfish abides. Due to the vast areas environmental pollution the population of crayfish remaining in Latvia has decreased. For now there is only an insignificant number of crayfish left, which possibly could be harvested in a single summer. Now a center for crayfish breeding has been founded and this fall crayfish will be available to people who want to begin crayfish breeding. A LATVIAN MOUNTAIN CLIMBER ROBBED IN ARGENTINA Latvian mountain climber from the city of Liepāja, Zigurds Ivanovs was robbed in South America during his climbing of Mt. Aconcagua, which is the highest mountain in South America. The belongings were taken by local Indians who usually are hired to carry the equipment of mountain climbers. The local ranger was informed immediately about the theft, but all search results were negative. A round-trip ticket from Latvia to Argentina which was taken with the equipment, cost $3000 US and the equipment itself was worth another $ 2000 US. However, Z. Ivanovs doesn\'t regret this accident because thanks to this he has met people who are planning to go to Antarctica. As it turned out, to reach the Southern Pole is one of the goals for the Latvian explorer. He also is considering to go to Argentina and try to climb Mt. Aconcagua once again. NEW OPERA On January 23 and 25 the premiere show of "Aida" took place in the National Opera of Latvia. On the opening night the Chinese tenor Warren Mok performed as Radames.', 'The last two weeks of February saw many of us glued to our television sets, enjoying a once-in-alifetime opportunity to renew our quadrennial Olympic fantasies two years early. Due to a decision by the International Olympic Committee to hold the Winter and Summer Olympic Games in separate years, the XVII Winter Olympic Games in Lillehammer, Norway are taking place only two years after the previous Olympics in Albertville, France. The Lillehammer Games have also proven to be an unique opportunity for Latvia, Estonia, Lithuania and the many other nations emerging from the Soviet shadow to try their hand again at establishing, or, in the case of the Baltic states, reestablishing an independent name for themselves in the world\'s premier sports competition. Latvia recognized by CBS Latvian, Estonian and Lithuanian athletes have yet to medal in the Lillehammer Games and, as a result, have not been prominently featured in CBS\' television coverage. They continued to build on the respect they earned during the 1992 Albertville and Barcelona Games, their first Olympics since renewing their independence in 1991. In a pleasant departure from Albertville, CBS made sure to avoid another faux pas by prominently featuring the Latvian Olympic delegation as it entered the Opening Ceremonies in Lillehammer\'s ski jumping stadium. The Latvian flagbearer was gold-medalist Zintis Ekmanis, who with fellow Latvian Jānis Ķipurs won the twoman bobsled competition during the 1988 Calgary Games, while Latvia was still occupied by the Soviet Union. The CBS commentators also ensured that viewers were aware of the special role many of the Baltic olympians played during the struggle to renew Baltic independence. Ekmanis\' experience as one of the many Latvians who defended Latvia\'s freedom in January 1991 was noted during the Opening Ceremonies, and any time Baltic athletes were shown during event coverage, viewers were told that the athletes represented countries that had broken away from Soviet occupation and domination. The team The team that represented Latvia at the LilleLatvian Pride At Lillehammer Games hammer Olympic Games consisted of 31 athletes and 11 delegation members. Although to date (February 21) Latvian athletes have yet to win a medal, they have competed well — in some cases, surprisingly well. Figure skating — Latvia sent three skaters to the Olympics: Andrejs Vlasčenko in mens, and Jeļena Berežnaja and Oļegs šļahovs in pairs. Berežnaja and šļahovs placed ninth among among 18 pairs competing for the gold medal, while Vlasčenko tied for 21st among the 25 skaters competing for the mens title. Luge — Latvia\'s luge teams repeated their strong performances of Albertville. Anna Orlova led the women with a ninth place finish, followed by Evija Šulce (16th) and Ilūta Gaile (17th). Aivis Švāns and Roberts Suharevs took 11th place in the mens doubles competition, followed by teammates Juris Vovčoks and Dairis Leksis in 12th. In mens singles, Agris Ēlerts took 18th place, while Vovčoks placed 21st. Bobsledding — The four-man race will take place on the last day of the Olympics, February 26. But in the two-man competition, Calgary Games gold medalists Zintis Ekmanis and Aldis Intlers placed 10th. Ekmanis and Intlers are joined on the bobsled team by Boriss Artemjevs, Egils Bojārs, Rodžers Lodziņš, Andris Plūksna, Sandis Prūsis, Otomārs Rihters, Didzis Skuška and Juris Tone. Cross Country Skiing — Latvia sent two skiers to Lillehammer – Jānis Hermanis and Ineta Kravale. Neither expected to be in medal contention in the races they entered – rather, they felt that simply finishing would be achievement enough. LOC President Baltiņš explained their inclusion on the team with the phrase "for faithfulness to sports." Hermanis placed 85th in the 10km classic race and 69th in the 30km freestyle race, while Kravale finished the skm classic race in 61st place and the 15km freestyle race in 52nd place. Speedskating — Latvia\'s one speedskater, Honda Lūse, earned 25th place in the 3000 m race. Biathalon — Latvia sent five biathaletes to the Lillehammer Games: leva Volfa, Aivars Bogdanovs, Ilmārs Bricis, Oļegs Maļuhins and Gundars Upenieks. The level of competition in Lillehammer was exceptional, especially in figure skating, where several former champions returned from professional skating careers to try again for Olympic gold. The Latvian Olympic team proudly and ably demonstrated their commitment to sports and again served as shining exponents of the spirit of Olympic competition. Mārtiņš Zvaners', 'COULDN\'T THIS POSSIBLY INTEREST YOU? During their comparatively short period of independence between the two World Wars, on several occasions the Baltic nations could distinguish themselves in different fields of international sports. ■Estonia. In the European sports press Estonia frequently was called "the land of the strong men", for reason of the achievements in heavy athletics by its nationals, who were among the strongest in our continent indeed. The once world-famous professional wrestler George Lurich has become an almost legendary symbol of colossal strenght, and has set a splendid example for the younger generation of Estonians. At the Olympic games and European Championships, the Estonian boys nearly always won laurels comparable to those of the Finns and Swedes. During the last Olympic games at Berlin in 1936, the Estonian heavyweight Christian Palusalu could claim the title of the "topmost strong man", for reason of his scoring a double victory in wrestling Greek-Roman style and American free-style as well. This is by no means an isolated instance; the fame of other wrestling champions-Vali, Neo, Puttsepp, Kotkes, etc.-in sporting circles is perhaps on nearly the same level. Quite conspicuous are the achievements of the Estonians in weight lifting and boxing too; c. g., in 1938 at Dublin, Raadik became boxing champion of Europe in the middleweight class. The Estonians won quite a number of victories in field and track events too. Shortly before the Second World War, the only serious competitors of the brilliant Finnish javeling throwers in Europe were the Estonians G. Sule and J. Issaac. Next to them came the outstanding shot putters A. Vflding, the champion of Europe in 1938 A. Kruk, and A. Maalstein. In 1945, the last named, now beeing a DP in Germany, still was one of the best in Europe. After the First World War, the Estonian A. Klumberg was the decathlon world record holder; in 1920 at the Antwerp Olympic games, the marathon runner J. Lossman was second, closely behinf the "father of the Finnish runners" H. Kolchmainen. Next to miraclous was the success of the Estonians in shooting; from 1935 till 1939, this small nation could boast of such a list of world record holders and champious as even the Finns and Angentinians were not able to surpass. The Estonian basketball and voleyball players were among the best in Europe. World famous was their young chess champion P. Keres, one of the best players throughout the world. Outstanding were the performances in ice-yachting; several times the European Championship meets took place in their country. In 1937, the European wrestling (Greek-Roman style) Championship meet was held in Tallin, Estonia. Latvia. The Latvian people, counting less than 2 million, have contributed to the list of outstanding athletes several big names. The national sport of the Latvians was soccer football, although they have achieved mor notable results in other sporting events. The former multiple world re. cord holder in walking events, J. Dalins, has spread the fame of his native country throughout the sporting world. Nearly all European star walkers came to compete with him to the Latvian capital Riga. Now this fame has passed over to the Swedes, who once were the most gifted disciples of the notable Latvians-J. Dalins, P. Bernhards, A. Liepaskalns, A. Bubenko, etc. At the Los Angeles Olympic games in 1932, J. Dalins was second behind the Englishman Green in the 50 klm walking event; in 1934 at Torino, he became champion of Europe for the same distance. Another brilliant Latvian was the decathlon athlete J. Dimza who was deported by the Russians in 1940. Perhaps not a few American sporting enthusiasts may still remember his misfortune at the Los Angeles Olympic games in 1932 when, being amongst the leaders after the first half of the decathlon events, he fell so unluckily during the pole vault competition that he had to be carried off the field badly injured. The star page of the Latvian sports roll includes the following names: K. Klava, shooting champion of the world in 1937 at Helsinki-Finland. E. Bietags, wrestling (Greek-Roman style) champion or Europe in 1934 at Rome. A. Berzins speed skating champion of Europe in 1938 at Riga, Latvia. The Latvian national basketball team, the first champion of Europe in 1934 at Geneva. The achievements of J. Dalins were mentioned already. At the Berlin Olympic games in 1936, A. Bubenko was third in the 50 klm walking event, but the lightheaveyweight E. Bietags second in wrestling Greek-Roman style. Quite notable in chess circles ar the Latvian players. At the Paris Olympic games in 1924, the Latvian H. Matison was first, and his compatrist J. Apsenieks second. Several important sports meetings were held in Latvia, c. g.p the wrestling (Greek-Roman style) competition for the European champion-ship in 1926, the basketball, competition for the European championship in 1937, the speed skating competition for the European championship in 1938; besides, several times the European championship iceyachting meets took place there. Big international chess tournaments were organized in Biga, and in the renowned spa Kemeri not far from the beautiful Latvian seaside resorts. Lithuania. Of all kinds of sporting activities, basketball has attained the highest popularity with the Lithuanians. Twice they were champions of Europe, and before the war next to all Lithuanian boys and girls played basketball. To this enthusiasm have mostly contributed the USA star players of Lithuanian origin: Puzinauskas, Norkus, Andrulis, Baltrunas, etc., are names held in highest esteem by all European basketball fans. Good progress was also made in soccer football and other sporting events, but all that had to be abandoned with the outbreak of war. The biggest sports meeting in Lithuania took place in 1937: in thedir capital Kaunas the European basketball championship were held. In exile. At present the athletes of the Baltic states, as displaced persons, are dispersed and dwell in several countries, mostly in Germany and Sweden. With the help of their old and true protectors and friends, the American and English, they try, as far as circumstances permit, to continue their sporting activities, in order to conserve and develop the strenght of their youth for the days to come, when our innermost dreams, of returning to the free and democratic republics of Estonia, Latvia and Lithuania, will come true. His small merry face shrank with a sudden sorrow. "I\'m sorry you don\'t speak Czech." "I wish I did, Love", I said, "it it could help you". "Sure, it would help me", sighed Love, "I\'m running after DPs all time but in vain. No Czechs come my way. A Czech is all I need. That\'s why I endured the ETO pretty wellhoping to meet a Czech." This was the time to get suspicious. "Say, what\'s so marvellous about a Czech? What has a Czech .got other DPs haven\'t? Can\'t someone else do as well?" "No", Love said in a rather resigned tone, "not while I\'m married to this wife." "Come, Love, explain it", I patted him on the shoulder, having not the slightest idea of what use the missing Czech could be to Love. He seemed to be so fond of the two Loves he had home already. Yet, who knows? Arthur Thomas Love from Texas raised his eyes. I met a roguish look framed in horn. "You know, my wife is a Czech. She has the habit of calling me names in Czech. You know, she uses them any time, bad names and good names. I\'d just like to know what names exactly. And I\'d like to call her names in Czech too. But there is no hope for me, no Czechs around who could teach me. I\'ll go home and she\'ll kiss me and call me names in Czech." Love pulled a wallet out of his pocket and let me see his wife who called him names in Czech, a smiling American beauty. Then he asked me, "What would you do if you were I?" "Well, Love, I hope you realise your wife can\'t call you Love because everyone else does. So she has to be different. That\'s why. But just to help you, why not think up names to use any time she calls you names in Czech? Tell her it is some European lingo. I bet she will quit speaking Czech". "That\'s an idea," Love seemed to cheer up. "Hm, hm, quite an idea". Then he meditated a little, tilting his lovely nose up with one lovely finger. "Yes", he decided at last. "I\'ll do it if I won\'t find a Czech. Thank you." Private Love got up, knocking over his champagne-glass. He hurried across the room towards the door, and out into the cold German winter night, straight on, as if fate waited for him outside-or a Czech. Love came unexpectedly, Love disappeared unexpectedly. Why didn\'t grandmother tell me?']

Name: 36_montreal_raudsepp_show_club
Representative_Docs: ['The tenth anniversary Baltic philatelic and cultural exposition in Montreal took place on the weekend of May 15 and 16, 1999.
