This year\'s show was held at the Notre Dame Porte de l\'Aurore – Our Lady Gate of Dawn Lithuanian church hall – a large church with a wonderful hall big enough to play basketball in (which thev regularly do). The weather was excellent, the spirits were high, and with over 700 pages of exposition and good attendance on both days, Saturday and Sunday, it was in every way a cuccess! People from both the English and French speaking communities of Montreal were in atten dance and several car loads down from Ottawa, Kingston, and parts in-between. Dr. Rolfs Sers from the Maritimes and a member of our club and an exhibitor as well came from the greatest distance away. The exhibition\'s "Cafe" was open from 9 AM to 6 PM on both days, with Giselle von Dehn as well as Nora Saletés and Malda Skaistkalns (our members from Cornwall) in charge. The ladies had outdone themselves, with superb cakes and sandwiches, several kinds of teas and coffees available, at phenomenally low prices! The best he has ever seen at any stamp show, in the judgement of Mr. Eric Jānlepp who had driven down from Ottawa for the anniversary show. The Official Canada Post cancel, used on letters mailed at the show, designed by Dr. Francois Brisse (of the University of Montreal) featured two liands open wide with the names of the three Baltic States being gently held. It was very popular and received much usage during the show. The Commemorative envelope of this anniversary Exhibition, in 500 copies, was hand printed in gold leaf by Mr. von Hahn of Montreal West – a beautifylly executed work indeed! With over 700 pages of exhibits, in 14 major collec – tions, to evaluate, Mr. Benoit Carrier, as the chief judge and Henrik Mouritsen helping had their work cut out for them. Ten medals were awarded, with silver medals going to Mārtiņš Štauvers (1) and Dr. Rofs Sers (2 medals), and silver bronze to four other collectors. The medals were designed by our "Grand Old Man" Oskars Vindedzis, who suprised us this year with an exquisite design. BALT-EX X Two exhibits, totally in French, with great computer graphics by club member Francois Rochon were parti cularly commented on; as well as an interesting short exhibit on "Love" by the club president. The most talked about exhibit was again by Dr. Rolfs Sers, our club member froms Antigonish, Nova Scotia, whose "Early Latvian varieties" won the Most Popular Exhibit award. Thematic exhibits were several, the younger viewers particularly liked the one on "Hockey"; the older ones \'Baltic Folk Costumes"; and in the general open class, an exhibit of "Romantic Postcards from Estonia in the PJ2o\'s and 1930\'5". An Extensive Lithuanian exhibit on the "Nobel Prizes" yu Romas Verbyla also was excellent. This year also, we had a dozen "one-pages" – exhibits <>l oji£ Piig£ only, and three pagers or "tryptychs" which seemed to be popular, and will be repeated in the future. In the "Court of Honor" was shown an International level display of Denmark philately (photo copies) by Henrik Mouritsen which was in a class by itself. For our 10th anniversary show, the Montreal City Hall sent the Pro-Mayor, Monsieur Pierre Lapointe, as mayor Bourque\'s official representative, who affably officiated during the Medal Ceremonies held on Sunday – his friendly presence was much appreciated. Next year we will have another 10th anniversary to celebrate – that of the founding of The Montreal Baltic Philatelic Club itself, an amalgamation of those interested in promoting Baltic culture through our club activities and yearly exhibitions. We believe this work to be useful in bringing information in a practical way to the attention of Canadiens of all origins. The club needs your support – should you wish to support this work, give us a call at 486-5717! □ J. J. Raudsepp, President Left to right: Mārtiņš Štauvers, vice-president, J. J. Raudsepp, president, Monsieur Pierre Lapointe, Dr. Rolfs Sers Diploma Ceremony: from left to right – Francois Rochon, Romas Verbyla, J. J. Raudsepp, president, Henrik Mouritsen, Dr. Rolfs Sers, Elmārs BārdiņŠ (representing Mārtiņš Štauvers) Oskars Vīndedzis and Monsiur Pierre Lapointe, Deputy Mayor of Montreal The Pro-Mayor of Montreal Monsieur Pierre Lapointe with the organizers of the "Exhibition Cafe"(l. to r.) – Nora Saletes, Giselle von Dehn, the Pro-Mayor, and Malda Skaistkalns President J. J. Raudsepp presents free stamp envelopes to young collectors A hot discussion of philatelic and exhibition matters', 'The 7th annual Baltic Philatelic & cultural exposition look place in Montreal, on May 25 and 26. 1996. With over 4HU pages of exhibits under glass and several tables of material, the show was a complete success! Held in the N. D. G. St. John\'s Estonian ev.-luth. church hall, it was attended by French and English, as well as Balticpeople. An excellent "Cafe" with Kathleen Young, Nora Saletés and Malda Skaistkalns in charge, served a variety of tea, coffee and juices and excellent cakes and sandwiches, allowing people at the show to sit down and chat. l\'hère were guests from Ottawa, Toronto, and from as far away as Edmonton, Alberta. Representative_Docs: ['Roberts Laiviņš The March 16tn gatherings honoring the memory of the men of the Latvian Legion have increasingly become a subject of much debate, and have come under international scrutiny. As there is a great deal of available information about the Legion, there are also many misconceptions, some of which have been purposely perpetrated via propaganda campaigns. Some of this misinformation has made its way into international media, with some news outlets unintentionally reporting erroneous items as genuine. To understand why the March 16tn gatherings are such a point of contention it is important to take an objective look at the story of the Legionnaires and the significance of the 16m of March. What was the Latvian Legion? The Latvian Legion is a name given to two divisions of young men conscripted to arms by Germany, which had invaded Latvia during the Second World War. As the result of the Molotovvon Ribbentrop pact, a secret deal was forged between the Nazis and the Soviets. This enabled Soviet Russia to conquer the Baltic States, while Germany, the only other great military power in the area, would do nothing to interfere. In the first year of Soviet rule, many men of the formerly independent Latvian Army were executed; the remainder was absorbed into the Red Army. Additionally, the Soviets conscripted men of All about Latvian Legionnaires and March 16 service age into their war machine. The Nazis turned on their erstwhile allies and counterinvaded. In order to fuel their war against Russia, the Nazis then conscripted the remaining able-bodied young men. Were the Legionnaires Nazis that participated in war crimes? No. Because of the racist policies of the German High Command, only true Germans could be members of the National Socialist Workers\' Party (Nazi) or the regular Army (Wehrmacht). Latvians were considered "human material" that could only wield weapons as members of a Protective Squadron (SchutzstajH). These multi-ethnic units Tiad specific tasks, such as mountaineers, cavalry, infantry, and tanks. The two Latvian divisions were both assigned as grenadiers. Aside from Latvians, other divisions consisted of Dutch, Norwegians, Estonians, Valonians, Ukrainians, Byelorussians, Croatians, Romanians, Hungarians, as well as French and Russians. The Legionnaires were never part of the Gestapo, the Sicherheitsdienst (SD) or Deaths Head units responsible for the overwhelming majority of war crimes and the holocaust. Additionally, no Latvian Legionnaire was ever convicted at the Nuremberg War Trials. Were the Legionnaires mercenaries that willingly served the Nazi causer No. Although the two divisions were considered freiwilligen (volunteer) their conscription was mandatory. Those that did not report when called were rooted out and persecuted, in some cases sent off to concentration camps or executed. The Latvians were mainly deployed near the Baltic frontier and, at the end of the war, to defend Berlin. At the end of the war, the Latvian 19tn Division still held onto a large section of the Latvian province of Kurzeme (Courland). Of the 146,000 Latvians conscripted into the Legion, some 50,000 were killed, 60,000 wounded and 30,000 taken prisoner. The Nuremburg War Crimes Tribunal specifically exonerated the Legionnaires, considering them as men "who were drafted into membership by the State in such a way as to give them no choice in the matter, and who had committed no such crimes." Did the Legionnaires pose any threat to the Western allies? No. The Latvian 15tn Division, which was commanded to defend the German Reich to the last man, defied the suicidal orders. Colonel Vilis Janums took the remnants of the Latvian units in the area and advanced westwards to the Elbe River, specifically in order to surrender to the Americans. After the war, Latvian POWs were vetted by d with the Western allies. Many Latvians even served in transportation and guard units under Allied leadership. The American Displaced Persons Commission famously issued the following statement: "The Baltic Waffen SS Units (Baltic Legions) are to be considered as separate and distinct in purpose, ideology, activities, and qualifications for membership from the German SS, and therefore the Commission holds them not to be hostile to the Government of the United States," echoing positions previously voiced by the US High Commission in Germany and the US Secretary of State. What is the significance of March 16th? March 16th 1944 marks the date when both divisions of the Latvian Legion fought beside each other under the command of Latvian officers. The battle over a hill by the River Velikaya took place less than 25 miles from Abrene region, which was taken from Latvia and annexed into Soviet Russia, with much of the local ethnic Latvian population displaced or deported. In a cruel irony of war, among the Red Army units that engaged the Legion in battle there were ethnic Latvian riflemen units forcibly conscripted into the Soviet Russian war machine. Thus brother was forced to fight brother. How did Latvians first come to commejmorate March 16°*? At the conclusion ol World War 11, some 25,000 Latvian Legionnaires were held in Prisoner ofWarcamps throughout war-ravaged Western Europe. 12,000 men in the Zedelgem POW camp, near Brugges, Belgium, formed a veterans\' organization named Daugavas Vanagi (The Hawks of the River Daugava) in December 1945. They were eventually permitted to contact other veterans in POW and Displaced Persons\' camps in the Allied Occupation Zones throughout Germany. These vets felt a need to honor their fallen comrades and decided that this date, conveying a sense of unity, was the most appropriate. Why are the March 16tn gatherings important to Latvians? As the veterans were resettled to all parts of the globe, they founded chapters of the DV in cities with large Latvian concentration. The commemoration day became an important date to gather and pay homage to their brothers in arms. A great many Latvian exiles viewed the Legionnaires as having defended Latvian soil from the onslaught of Communism and Soviet Russian forces. Aside from providing mutual welfare, the DV organization actively supported Latvian exile civic activities-churches.community centers and schools. In time, they actively sponsored education programs, such as scouting, summer camps, and youth organizations. Thus, many subseuuent generations of Latvians have some connection to these veterans. (Turpinājums sekos)', 'The concept of the Latvian Legion, its creation and history Reference material supplied by the Ministr/ of Foreign Affairs, Republic of Latvia A. The latvian Voluntary SS Legion On January 23, 1943 the Fuehrer of the German State Ādolf Hitler „approved the idea of creating a Latvian Voluntary SS Legion and ordered such a Legion to be set up". In accordance vvith the order on January 24, 1943 the four Latvian self-defence batallions, included m the 2nd SS Brigāde, vvere joined together under the name of the Latvian Voluntary SS Legion. The formai vvritten order by Ādolf Hitler on the establishment of the Latvian Voluntarv SS Legion was executed only on February 10,1943. In order to clear up all misunderstandings connected vvith the introduction of the term "the Latvian Legion", Himmler had to state the follovving: "The name \'The Latvian Legion\' īsa collective term for all Latvian military units included m the Waffen SS and the police. The Latvian Legion reports to the Reichsfuehrer SS." (See note 1). At this point – May 1943 -- the follovving military units vvere established: t The Latvian SS Brigāde; 2. The Latvian Voluntary SS Division; 3. The Latvian police batallions. (By the order of the Reichsfuehrer SS from May 24, 1943 the Latvian self- defence batallions duc to their steadfastness m action had been honoured to be renamed "the Latvian Police Batallions" and included into the Latvian Voluntary SS Legion.) B. Latvian units m the Vfaffen During the war two Latvian Waffen SS divisions vvere set up. The Latvian Voluntary SS Division vvas set up the first (the establishing order vvas signed on February 15, 1943. From the end of 1943 the unit was involved m military action at the front). From October 1943 until June 1944 knovvn as the 15th Latvian Voluntary SS Division, it later became knovvn as the 15th Waffen SS Grenadier Division (Latvian No. 1). As of December 31, 1943, the total manpovver of the division vvas 15192 men. In June 30, 1944 ir comprised 18413 men. The 15th Division moetly was engaged m defence and retreat battles inßussia: from 18.11.43 til* 5.1.44 it fought m defence battles near Nevel; from 14.1.44 till 15.2.44 it vvas involved m battles north of Nevel. From 4.3 till 12.3.44 the Division vvas engaged m the first defence battles at Ostrov and Pskov, from 26.3 up to 11.4.44 it fought to the north of Opotchka, but from 24.4 till 21.6.44 it vvas involved m the stabilization battles m the territorv of the Commando Group Nord and later – m the retreat battles m the term to ry of Latvia. In September 1944 the remains of the Division vvere relocated to a militarv training centrē m the Western Prussia to be restructured. At the end of 1944 the 15th Division vvas battleready once again. In 1945 it participated m heavy fighting m Vvestern Prussia, m the vicinity of Danzig, m Pomerania, Mecklenburg and Mark Brandenburg. By the end of the vvar a significant part of the soldiers of the division and its supporting units managed to escape from the Eastern front and to capitulate to the VVestern AHies m the north of Germany. The second Latvian division vvas set up on the basis of the Latvian SS Brigāde vvhich, according to the Himmler\'s order from January 7, 1944, vvas reconstructed into the 19th SS Division. From 24.1.43 till 15.3.43 the Brigāde vvas knovvn as The Latvian SS Legion, but later – until 22.10.43 it was knovvn under the name of the Latvian Voluntary SS Brigāde. Then its name vvas changed onoe again and till 7.1.44 it bore the name of the 2nd Latvian Voluntary SS Brigāde. As of December 31, 1943 the to tai strength of the brigāde was 8033 men. Up to May 1944 this military unit was called the 19th Latvian Voluntary SS Division but after that it was known under the name of the 19th Waffen SS Grenadier Division (Latvian No. 2). As of June 30, 1944 the to tai manpovver of the division was 12592 soldiers and on October 20, 1944 it consisted of 12507 men. The Brigāde or the Division was involved m the battles at the Leningrad Front. After the end of the siege of Leningrad some units of the division fought at Wolchow and m the battles south of Pskov. From July till October 1944 the division was involved m the rearguard action at the Latvian border and m the territorv of Latvia itself. In October 1944 the 19th Division was relocated to Kurland vvhere it participated m fiveoutof the six serious battles m Kurland. It fināli}\' capitulated to the Red Army m May 1945 m Kurland. C. Conscription m the Waffen SS m Latvia The soldiers for the Latvian Waffen SS units were recruited by the following methods: L They were recruited by the inci usion of the Latvian self-defence batallions (2) of the Latvian Voluntarv SS Brigāde into the 19th Latvian Voluntarv SS Division. (The self-defence batallions generally were set up by volunteers m 1941 – m to tai sic Latvian batallions were united m a single Latvian brigāde.) Through inclusion of the Latvian police batallions m the Latvian Waffen SS division the Waffen SS acquired 5 additional batallions. The police batallions consisted of volunteers, the Latvian policemen, as wyll as of conscripted persons After the eetting up of the l&th Waffen SS Grenadier Division m August 1944 its personnel consisted of the Latvian border gvards, the Latvian voluntary policemen as well as members of the Latvian professional police (as of 14.5.44 the policemen vvere C\' iced under the command of the tvian Voluntary SS Legion). 2. The conscription [ mobilization (3) ] of whole age groups In the territory of Latvia mobilization effort was continued m the period betvveen March 1943 and August 1944 (m the territorv of Kurland it was also continued m September and October 1944) (4). The first wave of mobilization came m the period betvveen March and August 1943. During this period Latvian citizens from the age group 1919 to 1925 vvere mobilized. The mobilization was carried out according to the regulations of the obligatory labour service m the occupied Eastern territories adopted on December 19, 1941. Under the auspices of the Labour and Social Administration of the Generalkommisar a special mobilization headquarters was set up Lists of the people subject to the mobrlization orders vvere prepared by the police. The follovving figurēs vvere given m the conscription plan: SS Legion needed 15500 men (29.1%); the Wehrmacht had to receive 22000 men (41.5%) and civilian service (obligā-  labour programmēs) -- 15600 (29.4%). According to the mobilization guidelines 0f26.2.43 (the third, amended version of the guidelines, adopted on 16.2.43) the people subject te the obligatory labour service should be able to choose betvveen the service m the Legion, the Wehrmacht or m the civilian service. Nevertheless, already on March 8,1943 the "free choice" clause vvas cancelled because the practice of the mobilization process shovved the unvvillingless of the conscripted persons to choose the military options. Under these circumstances the second version of the guidelines from 16.2.43 vvas set m force. These guidelines stated that "the mobilization authoritv has to convince the conscripts to choose the service m the Legion, and only if the offer is cathegoricallv rejected, to appoint them to the voluntary auxiliary service (5) or to the labour units m the spheres vitallv necessary for the war effort." The heads of the mobilization offices had to divide the mobilized persons according the needs of the three abovementioned institutions. The only restriction for the conscription m the V/affcn SS units vvas the rninimum height demand of 168 cm for the conscripts. The first wave of the mobilization gave the follovving result: the SS Legion received 17971 men (mainly the Vfaffen SS units) (50.3%) – thus significantly overfilling the initial demand; the Wehrmacht got 12979 men (36.4%) and thetivilianlabour service conscripted 4769 men (13.3%). During the second mobilization campaign (October-November 1943) the rest of the age group 1919-1924 was conscripted and the conscription of the men from the age group 1923-1925 vvas started. During the third vvave of mobilization (December 1943-January 1944) the men from the age group of 1915 to 1924 vvere conscripted. It must be said that during those mobilization vvaves the initial allocation of the conscripts to different services as vvell as the observanoe of the planned mobilization figurēs vvere cancelled. The mobilization vvas carried our by the SS-Ersatzkommando Ostland vvithout any "legal defence considerations". In the beginning of 1944 the three Baltie reģions vvere exclusively reserved for the Reichsfuehrer SS as reservoirs for supplementing the SS Leģions by means of mobilization and conscription. (The mobilization ordera gave the Latvian divisions ca. 12000 men.) During the follovving months (Januarv-Apni  men iroui tiw age groups 1906-1916 and 1919-1921 vvere conscripted and the mobilization of the men from the age group 1922-1924 vvas continued. This time the conscription vvas orgānized under the auspices of the nemv established SS-Einsaizinspektion Ostland (SS-Ersatzkommando Lettland) and it vvas carried out by the Inspector-General of the Latvian Voluntary SS Legion. (During the period 46600 men vvere conscripted and only ca. 1000 of them vvere allocated directly to the SS Divisions.) In July 1944 the total mobilization vvas announced m the territory of Latvia whereby all men from the age grpup from 1906 up to 1926 vvere Labie for conscription. By September 1944 ca. 19000 men vvere conscripted. There are no exact data about the division of the conscripts among the different services. One part of the conscripts for other military units later on vvas assigned to the 15th Waffen SS Grenadier Division (Latvian SOLDIERS OF THE LATVIAN LEGION in defence positions at the Velikaya River, near the Latvian border, March 1944. As the conscripted persons and the volunteers had to serve together m the same so-called "voluntary military units", the statistical data did not mark the "true volunteers" separateh/. (Even m the case thoee persons had voluntarily joined the military units (for example, the self-defence batallions), their incorporation m the voluntary SS divisions vvas not done according to their own free vvill (vvith the exception of the deserters). Even the possibility of a free choice betvveen the services for the greatest part of the conscripts vvho vvere mobilized m the first vvave of conscription from 26.2 until 8.3 1943 vvas a seeming freedom to choose. Summarv The Latvian Voluntarv SS Legion (m an unofficial abbreviation – the Latvian Legion) was the common term for the Latvian militarv units organized under the ausptcesof the Wat}en £6 utid  luīh and the 19th Latvian Voluntary SS Divisions belonged to the Waffen SS. (9.2-1944). Kārlis Kangeris, Scientific Researcher, Baltic Studies Centre, University of Stockholm April 27,1994.', 'The Latvian Legion (1943-45) Material supplied by the Ministrv of Foreign Affairs, Republic of Latvia * Recent news reports have erroneouslv associated membership m the Latvian Legion (1943-45) with Nazi atrocities committed during the Nazi Germany occupation of Latvia (1941-45). Historical evidence shovvs that the conscripted combat units of the Latvian Legion (designated by Berlin as the Waffen SS) could not and did not participa te m these atrocities. L The Latvian Legion was formed m 1943 after the Nazi occupation forces had completed their systematic destruction of latvia\'s Jewish population. The last major killingoperation against Jews m Latvia took place m March 1942. 2. The Legion consisted of combat units (Waffen SS means VVeapons SS m German) which fought on the Eastern Front against the Soviet forces from 1943 until 1945. 3. Nazi occupation forces m Latvia created the Latvian Legion m 1943 through forced conscription, m violation of the Hague Convention. 4. Individual Latvians did voluntarilv collaborate with the Nazi occupation forces and participated m atrocities against Latvia\'sJewish population from 1941-42. Some of these individuāls did join the Latvian Legion m 1943 m order to disguise their criminal activīties. However, the Legion itself was not involved m actions against the Jewish population. Of the 140,000 men who served m the Latvian Legion 50,000 were casualties m combat against the Soviet army on the Eastern Front. On November 28, 1950 the U.S. Displaced Persona Commission Headquarters m Frankfurt, West Germany, APO 757, acknovvledged the above circuinstances m a memorandum concerning Baltie Waffen SS Units. The memorandum states: "In the case of the Baltie Waffen SS Units [the Commission J received evidence shotving membership m these units was duc to conscription, a method by the Hitler regime, and that members of the Baltie SS were impressed fbr the most part fbr actual ftghting on the Eastern front. After careful consideration, the Commission finallv decides that the Baltie Wafjen SS units were nota hostile movement within Section 13 of the Act, and membership m these units was nota bar per se." Robert Corkern, tIS DUplaced Persona Commisßion Coordiiiator for Kurope According to Harry Roeenfield, Comrnissioner of the US Displaced Persons Commission, the Commission also approved the follovvingstatement: "That the Baltie Waffen SS Units (Baltie Leģions) aretobe eonsidered as separate and distinct m purpose, ideology, activities and aualifications fbr membership from the German SS, and therefbre the Commission holds them not tobea movement hostile to the Government of the United States under section 13 ofthe Displaced Persons Act, as amended.9 Additional facts about the Vfaffen SS: Despite the "SS" designation, V/affen SS units were not associated with SS activities m the infamous Nazi death camps, nor did they participate m any actions against civilian population. These vvere combat units, often of an international nature. For example, m 1943, Latvian Waffen SS combat  Russia, In effect, these Latvian units had two identitk*s, dependingon one\'spoint of reference. Nazi German authorities called these units Waffen SS. Latvian members of these units looked upon themselves as the Latvian Legion. The decision of the United States Displaced Persons Commission m 1950 reinforces this distinetion by stating that the units ofthe latvian Legion "are to be eonsidered as separate and distinct m purpose, ideologv, acitivities and qua!ifications for membership from the German SS." Historical buckground: The Latvian government and armed forces vvere dissolved m the summer of 1940 follovving the Soviet Union\'s occupation and illegal annexation of independent Latvia. Tens of thousands of Latvians vvere deported and/or executed by the Soviet regime by June 1941. Nazi Germany invaded Latvia m July 1941, forcing a Soviet retreat to Russia. A nevv vvave of mass executions and deportations was initiated by the Nazi regime, focussing mainly (but not exclusively) on Latvia\'s Jevvish population. As the Nazi Germany invasion of the Soviet Union began to collapse m 1943, Hitler ordered the conscription of Latvian nationals m combat units known as the Latvian Legion (Waffen SS). Latvian eonseripts vvere sent to the Northern Sector ofthe Russian front m order to cover the retreat ofthe German Wehrmacht. The Legion was forced back to defensive lines on Latvian territory, vvhere major engagements took place m 1944. Latvian troops eventually retreated to Germany vvhere they became POWs under either US-British or Soviet jurisdietion. At no time did units ofthe Latvian Legion engage m combat against United States\' or other VVestern Allied forces. On the gathering of latvian Legion veterāns m Riga on March 16,1998: L The gathering of Latvian Legion veterāns on March 16 vvas organized by the veterāns themselves. It vvas not endorsed by the Latvian government. 2. The purpose ofthe veterāns\' gathering on March 16 vvas to pay tribute to fallen "brothers-in-arms", i.c. to all those fellovv combat soldiers who fought and died on the Eastern Front against the Soviet Red Army. It vvas nota political demonstration. 3. March 16 was ehosen because this date commemorates the March 16, 1943 Battle of Velikaya River (m Russia), vvhere the 15th and 19th dividions ofthe Latvian Legion held off a much larger Soviet force for several days. 4. Leaders ofthe gathering made it clear that there vvere not, nor have they ever been "Nazis". No Nazi flags or insignias appeared at the gathering. 5. Individual members of the Latvian parliament and Latvian military did participate m the gathering. They did it as .ndividuals, not on behalf of the government. Their purpose vvas to pay tribute to Latvian soldiers vvho had fought and died m defence of their homeland.']

Name: 38_tourism_tourist_development
Representative_Docs: ['Latvian Tourism Development Programme for 2006 – 2008 approved On 4 July, the Cabinet of Ministers approved the Latvian Tourism Development Programme for 2006 – 2008 drawn up by the Ministry of Economy The goal of the Programme is to promote development of the tourism sector, to ensure annual valueadded increase of 10% -15% and an annual increase in the percentage of tourism sector in GDP of 20%. The Programme was prepared to ensure the achieving of the goals outlined in the Basic concept of Latvian tourism development for 2004 – 2006 and in accordance with the course of action set out in it. The Programme includes events to enhance the image of Latvian tourism and to promote its recognition, to develop and improve the tourism infrastructure, to promote the development of tourism products and services and to improve cooperation between the state and the private sector and on an international level. The programme also anticipates the carrying out of tourism research which would help better determine new courses of action', 'LATVIA\'S MINISTER FOR ECONOMY: A HUGE POTENTIAL FOR TOURISM IN LATVIA On 3 December 2003, the National Agency for Tourism Development and the Ministry of Economy organized the first conference for representatives of tourism enterprises. Focusing on successful utilisation of EU Structural funds, the conference was entitled "EU Support Programs and Their Role in Tourism Development in Latvia". Minister of Economy juris Lujans, who opened the conference, emphasized that there is a great potential for the development of tourism in Latvia. Today tourism can be considered one of the most important sectors of Latvia\'s national economy. Tourism should become a cornerstone of the country\'s economy in the next 5 to 10 years. Under the oversight of the Ministry of Economy since March 2003, tourism has been chosen by the Ministry as a priority area for development in 2004 and will receive LVL 1, 042,000 state funding, which is 2.5 times more than In 2003. The Ministry insists on efficient use of the increased funding and has allocated money for specific projects: maintenance and development of a unified tourist information network and development of a system of informative road signs for travellers. Mr Lujans stressed that the "tourism should comprise a larger portion of the GDP; at the moment it constitutes 2 3% of GDP, and the goal is to reach 5-7% of GDP". According to the Minister, membership of the European Union should accelerate Latvia\'s economic development, including the tourism sector.', 'Latvia\'s main task in the tourism sector for 2005 is consolidation of country\'s tourism image According to the Latvian tourism development plan for 2004 drafted by the Ministry of Economics, the country\'s main task in the tourism sector for 2005 is consolidation of Latvia\'s tourism image, including airing of an advertisement clip on CNN, publication of a wide range of tourism information and other tasks. Director of the State Tourism Development Agency (TAVA), Uldis Vitolins, told that the airing of the advertisement clip on CNN is agency\'s main project for this year. It is planned to air the 45-second clip on a regular* basis from March 15 to April 15. The choice of this time of year, Vitolins explained, was based on the presumption that this is the time when tourists make their last decision on where to spend the holiday season". The clip will be broadcasted two to three times a day. Director of TAVA explained that the main idea of the clip is to promote Latvia as a country "where you want to return again and again". Airing of the clip will cost Latvia four thousand lats and it is developed by the advertisement agency "Metro Leo Burnett&Cuba Studio". Another tasks for 2005 include improvement of tourism-related web resources about Latvia, participation in international tourism fairs and organisation of forums and seminars on tourism opportunities in Latvia. 300 roadsigns informing the travellers about sightseeing objects and tourism facilities will be fitted on Latvian roads along with 35 tourism information stands, setting up of special network of parking lots for tourists and other improvements. The Latvian government has allocated more than 310 thousand lats for restoration of cultural monuments. 3 million lats will be allocated to development of infrastructure of green tourism (European Union participates with a 2.2 million co-funding), while the funding for motorway infrastructure improvements amounts to 29.1 million lats. The Central Statistical Bureau informs that in the nine months of 2004, Latvia was visited by 19.6% more tourists than in the same period of 2003, crossing the Latvian border 2.308 million times.']

Name: 39_flight_airport_passenger_airßaltic
Representative_Docs: ["Riga International Airport maintains leader position among Baltic States Riga International Airport in 2005 managed to retain its leader position among the three largest Baltic airports, based on passenger numbers, number of flights, and freight volumes. In terms of passenger numbers and freight, Riga International Airport led throughout the year, with Estonia's Tallinn Airport leading in some months in the number of flights serviced. Last year Riga International Airport served almost 1.9 million passengers, 77.1% more than in 2004. It serviced 34,552 flights, an increase of 26.4%, and loaded 15,896 tons of freight, 81.6% more than in 2004. In December of 2005, Riga International Airport served 160,571 passengers, 52.7% more than-in December 2004, serviced 2,638 flights, an increase of 14%, and loaded 736 tons of freight, 18.9% more than in December 2004.", "In January Riga Airport records highest number of passengers and flights among Baltic States. Riga International Airport in January recorded the highest number of passengers and flights among Baltic States, while the most freight was loaded in Tallinn. In the first month of this year, Riga International Airport served 160,378 passengers, 585% more than in January 2005, serviced 2,639 flights (16.3% more than January 2005), and loaded 524 tons of freight (32.3% less than January 2005). Tallinn occupies the leader position in terms of freight with 527 tons loaded (up 46.1 % on last year). Riga International Airport managed to retain its leader position among the three major Baltic States' airports, based on passengers served, number of flights, and freight loaded. Last year almost 1.9 million passengers were served at Riga International Airport, 77.1% more than in 2004. Riga", "LATVIA'S RIGA AIRPORT SERVES MORE PASSENGERS IN 8 MONTHS RIGA, 3 September, BNS – Latvia's Riga International Airport reported a year-on-year growth of 11.6% in the number of passengers served by the airport in the first eight months of this year. The airport operating in the Latvian capital Riga served 425,160 passengers over the period, an increase by more than 40,000 passengers over the same period in 2000. From January to August 2001 the Riga airport served 12,688 arriving and departing flights, up by 5% or 600 flights year-on-year. In August the airport served 67,041 passengers, up by 19.4% year-on-year, when this figure was 56,140 passengers, and 1,839 flights, a growth of 11.4% from August 2000 when it served 1,650 flights."]

Name: 40_unemployment_unemployed_rate_registered
Representative_Docs: ['UNEMPLOYMENT IN LATVIA STILL 7.6% RIGA, 9 Nov, BNS – The unemployment rate in Latvia this October stayed unchanged from September at 7.6%, reported an official from the National Employment Sen/ice. The unemployment level in Latvia has stabilised, said the Unemployment Records, Analysis and Forecast Unit head Ilga Upeniece. As of 1 October 2001, there were 90,560 people officially recognised as jobless in Latvia, down by 703 month-onmonth. The number of registered job seekers was 92,977, a reduction by 541 from September. Among the unemployed, 24,609 persons have been unable to find a job for more than a year. As usual, the unemployment rate Is highest in the eastern part of Latvia. At the beginning of October the rate was 26.8% (26.9% on 1 September) in the county of Rēzekne, 21.6% (21.7%) in Balvi and 20.5% (20.8%) in Krāslava. The Latvian capital Riga still boasts the lowest unemployment in the country – 3.6%, no change month-on-month. In early January this year the unemployment rate in Latvia was 7.8%. By April the rate grew to 8.1% but has been falling gradually since then.', 'UNEMPLOYMENT IN LATVIA DOWN TO 8.1% The unemployment rate keeps falling in Latvia and was 8.1% as of 1 September, 2000, the record low rate since 1997, the National Employment Service Unemployment Records, Analysis and Forecast Unit head Ilga Upeniece said on 11 September, In early August the unemployment rate in Latvia was 8.2% of the economically active population. At the beginning of July this figure was 8.4% and in early June 8.6%. The unemployment rate in Latvia was the lowest in 1997 when this figure was 7% on average. In recent years the rate was usually above 9%. In April 1999 a record-high unemployment (10.2%) was registered in Latvia because many people became jobless as a result of the Russian crisis. In January and February 2000 the unemployment rate was 9.1% but in March and April 9%.', 'UNEMPLOYMENT IN LATVIA The Latvian Statistics Bureau reported that on 1 July 98,426 residents of Latvia (6.4% of the economically active inhabitants) were looking for work, BNS reported on 1 August. The highest unemployment rate (21.5%) was in the Krāslava district where 4,386 people were seeking work. High unemployment rates were also registered in the Rēzekne (18.7%), Balvi (18%), and Preiļi (18%) districts. The districts of Ventspils, Jelgava, and Ogre had unemployment rates of 3.3%. In the largest city, Riga, the unemployment rate improved from 2.8% on 1 June to 2.6% on 1 July. According to data from the Register of Employment, as of August 2 there were 96,581 people looking for work, which is 6.5% of the active work force. There are 86,181 officially registered as unemployed and 34,000 who have been looking for work for an extended period of time(over 6 months. In comparison to the previous month, the unemployment figures have slightly increased; in July the unemployment was 6.4%. In Riga the unemployment rate has also increased -18,055 individuals are looking for work, which is 2.8% of the active work force.']

Name: 42_baltie_state_senator_motion
Representative_Docs:
['COUNCIL OF THE BALTIC STATES ON THE WITHDRAWAL OF TROOPS

The Council of the Baltie States acknow ledges the commencement of dialogue between the Baltie States and the Russian Federation on the issue of the withdrawal of the former USSR military troops, currently under the jurisdietion of Russia, from the territory of Estonia, Latvia and Lithuania. At the same time, the Council of the Baltie States is forced to acknovvledge that this dialogue is proceeding unsatisfactorily and with this, there stili exists a threat to the State sovereignty of the Baltie States and to the peace and stability in this region. Even now, the October 5 statement of the Council of the Baltie States request for the immediate withdrawal of military troops from the Baltie capitals, as well as the withdrawal of offensive troops, namely paratrooper units and strategie air force units, has continued to be ignored. The January 5,1992 appeal to the heads of State of the members of the Commonwealth of independent States to recall their citizens who are serving in the former USSR’s armed forces which should be withdrawn from the territories of Estonia, Latvia and Lithuania has remained unansvvered, with the exception of Azerbaijan.

The former USSR military forces, which are to be withdrawn from the territories of the Baltie States, continue to act as conque rors and disregard the sovereignty and legislation of Estonia, Latvia and Lithuania. This is furthered by the lack of their clear subordination to the Russian Government. VVithout notice to and the approval of the Governments of the Baltie States, the above mentioned military troops relocate their units and continue to hold exercises in artillery firing and land and sea shelling. The military troop units continue to cause damage to environment. The military troops have begun illegal business activities, namely, the sale of vveapons and property and the clearing of forests. The Council of the Baltie States expresses its protest against the above mentioned violations of the sovereignty of Estonia, Latvia and Lithuania and requests Russia to undertake the most decisive measures to terminate such activities. The Council of the Baltie States catego rically protests against the continued presence, in the sovereign territory of independent States, of the former border guard units of the former USSR which hinder the realization of the defense of the

State borders of Estonia, Latvia and Lithuania with the Baltie States’ own forces. Estonia, Latvia and Lithuania stili con tinue to demand material compensation for the property, arms and equipment of their armies, which was seized in 1940 by the Soviet Union, and which is presently necessary for the renewal of their State defence capability, as well as compensation for other losses caused by the presence of the former USSR military forces in the Baltie States territory. The Council of the Baltie States invites Russia, which has proelaimed itself suc cessor of the USSR’s rights and obligations, to realize a policy which would promote the strengthening of regional and European security, the reduetion of tensions and the inerease of confidence. The aforementioned is contradicted by the intention to station a great number of military troops, which have

been withdrawn from Central and eastem Europe, as well as from the Baltie States, in close proximity to the borders of Estonia, Latvia and Lithuania. The Council of the Baltie States eapres ses its hope that the Russian Federation wi!l demonstrate its goodwill and will do everything to actually and immediately withdraw military forces currently under its jurisdietion from the territory of the Baltie States. The Republics of Estonia, Latvia and Lithuania, on their part, are ready to eonelude an agreement on the terms and procedures for the withdrawal of the military forces.

Signed by the Chairmen of the Supreme Councils of Estonia, Latvia and Lithuania, A. Ruutel, A. Gorbunovs and V. Lands bergis, respectively.', 'CSCE CONFERENCE IN COPENHAGEN FAILS BALTIC STATES

BALTS RECEIVE CSCE MORAL SUPPORT, BUT NO OBSERVER STATUS The built-in eontradietions of the Helsinki process became quickly apparent at the Copenha gen meeting on “The Human Dimension” of the Conference on Security and Cooperation in Eu rope, this June. The formai request of the Baltie Foreign Ministers- Lennart Mēri of Estonia, Jānis Jurkans of Latvia, and Algirdas Saudargas of Lithuania- for observer status for their countries was politely denied. An earlier application by Lithuania was also rejected on the grounds that it would not get the needed consensus from the 35 CSCE members because of Soviet objections. That status was promptly granted to Albania. Ali the signatories to the 1975 Helsinki Final Act, among them the Soviet Union and the United States, committed themselves to “respect the equal rights of peoples and their right to self determination.” Hovvever, the Helsinki Act also guaranteedthe existing borders, ineluding those of the Soviet empire eneompassing the occupied Baltie countries. The consensus arrangement for the Helsinki process provided the Soviet Union witha Virtual veto powerconcerning membership. On the Baltie issue, the “new” Soviets in Copenhagen spoke and acted very much like the old familiar ones. Soviet deputy foreign minister Vladimir Petrovski told a press briefing on June 8 that “the Baltie States have no legal grounds” for req uesti ng membership because they are “part of the Soviet Union”. Soviet Ambassador Yuri Reshetov went on to deny that in 1940 the Baltie States were annexed to the USSR by force. The Baltie cause received U.S. verbal support at the Copenhagen meeting. On June 6, U.S. Secretary of State James Baker expressed con cern over the Soviet policy tovvards the Baltie States. U.S. Ambassador Max Kampelman made a strong statement in favor of Baltie f reedom at the plenary session on June 12. U.S. delegāte John Evans reminded participants on June 14 that on that day 50 years ago the Soviet Union occupied the Baltie States. He said he wished to remind the Soviet Union of Principle Five of the 1975 Helsinki Accords, which says that no country shall occupy another or use direct or indirect force against another State, and that any occupation of acqui-

sition of another State by force will not recognise as legal. Soviet restrictions of travel to the Baltie States were criticised by the deputy head of the U.S. delegation Jane Fisher on June 27. Hovvever, the U.S. interventions stopped at the line drawn by VVashington’s cautious Soviet policy. Outspoken support came from the delegātes of the smaller countries. Iceland’s Foreign Minis ter Jon Baldwin Hannibalsson said on June 6 that any Soviet use of force against the Baltie States could endanger progress toward European se curity. He emphasized that the only solution to the Baltie problem “that is compatible with the Hel sinki process (is) full recognition of the Baltie nations’ rightto independence.” Norway’s Foreign Minister Kjell Magne Bondevik received the Baltie foreign ministers in Oslo on June 9. They were accompanied by rep resentatives of the Baltie World Council. Mr Bondevik said he would raise the question of Baltie observer status at the next CSCE confer ence late November in Paris and expressed his confidence that it would be granted. The Baltie issue, he said, would also be raised atthe Nordic Council (Iceland, Norway, Denmark, Svveden and Finland). Similar sentiments were expressed by Den mark’s Foreign Minister Uffe Elleman-Jensen who received the Baltie foreign ministers and other representatives in a private audience on June 12. In his speech, heemphasisedthe impor tance of the restoration of the independence of the Baltie States and recalled that Denmark never recognised the legality of their occupation. Mr Elleman-Jensen promised his support for the Baltie efforts to gain observer status with the CSCE. Support forthe Baltie case was also voiced by the Austrian, British, Canadian, Dutch, Irish and other delegātes. On July 19, Lithuania, Latvia and Estonia again applied for observer status wit h the Helsinki process at a CSCE preparatory meeting in Vienna. The request, signed by Estonian minister Endel Lippmaa, said the Baltie States see no alternative to full independence from the Soviet Union.', 'Senate censure over Baltics THE SENATE Wednesday, 18th September 1974 PART 4 Senator DAVIDSON: ln short, the Minister for Foreign Affairs, representing this Government, is prepared to say, that because the people of the Baltie states have never had freedom "as we understandit', they should never have it or have an opportunity to have it. Later m the debatē Senator James McClelland deplored the fact thet the Baltie states had been taken over by the Soviet Union. All day long Government senators have deplored the fact that the Soviet Union had taken over the Baltie States. Yet not one, either by his words or his contribution to the debatē, has said that the Government might at least have adopted a more sympathetic, a more understanding and indeed a more honest approach when making a decision on this matter. I turn to the subject raised m the second paragraph of Senator Greenvvood's motion. It refers to the Baltie states. Reference has been made to it by Ministers and by otherhonourable senators who have spoken m this debatē. Almost daily members of Parliament are confronted with problems and issues that arise from the actions of the government of the day. Some of these matters affectpeople m away which may not totally please them. But the Government's decision to recognise the incorporation of the Baltie states m the Soviet Union is the most extraordinary decision I have heard of for many years. It affects many tens of thousands of people m Australia. Of course it affects a great many more people m the states of Lithuania, Estonia and Latvia. Maybe there was no Government decision. From my recollection of the ansvvers vvhich were given to questions that 1 asked m the Senate, I do not know who made a decision, if a decision was made or how the decision came about. The decision, such as it was, was not related to trade or economics or defence or even to international aid. It related to nothing but the people who lived vvithin the Baltie communities. THEIR GOALS ARE VERY HIGH" The Baltie people who live m Australia represent a substantial segment of the population. They are hard-working people. They are succesful and they are contributing to our Standard of life. Their goals are very high- They are people who were driven from their homeland and who re tai nod the hope that one day their homeland at least would be free and that if m the fullness of time this hope was not fulfilled m the way m which they vvanted it to be fulfilled, at least the peoples of the free vvorld would recognise the situation m vvhich they were placed. These people were driven aeross the vvorld liy^CQieJ^eyasni^jnd^he^vjannK ment, for no apparent reason, has by its decision condoned this cruelty. It has accepted the circumstances by vvhich the Baltie people were driven out of their country. It has accepted the circumstances by which the Baltie people were forced to creep dangereously by night and to hide as best they could by day. If honourable senators opposite have ever shared m the experiences of these people as I have, they will know that the things which I say are true. The Government has placed itself m the company of the invader. It has placed itself m the company of the tyrant, the dietator, and the prevented of freedom. It is no wonder that when the event took place I deseribed the decision to recognise the incorporation of the Baltie states by the Soviet Union as cruel and unnecessary. The Government by its decision greatly undermined the work vvhich already had been done by the VVestern powers and this is a very important point for the Senate to remember. The work of obtaining more freedom m intemational arrrangements and undersnatdings by the VVestern powers has been greatly undermined. In the future it wiU make it more difficult for the VVestern powers to continue to work to aehieve the freedom and independence of the smaller states. All the work that had been done m the past has been greatly damaged by the Australian action. "EXAMPLE TO ALL AUSTRALIANS" Because of the involvement of the migrant policy, the Government's decision has denied its own stated policy of ensuring the happiness, reunion and peace of all migu has iett the Estonians, Larvians and Lithuanians completely isolated and completely unrecognised. Senate censure over Baltics Anyone who has had anything to do with the Baltie communities will know that the emphasis which these groups place on freedom is an example to all Australians, and their reminder of the price to be paid for freedom has been completely disregarded by this Government. Most serious at all, the Government has shovvn its own attitude ot the tragie events which led to the dominance of the Baltie states by the Soviet Union. The Government's attitude – and this has been exemplified today – has been one of indifference to smaller states which have been overrun by a merciless great povver. The Government will see tomorrovv what these people think of the Government's decision m this matter. Some 2,000 people will assemble m Canberra to give effect to their own feelings m this matter and to let the Government knovv how strongly they feel about the Government's decision to recognise the incorporation of the Baltie states m the Soviet Union. It is no wonder that many of us have been constrained to deseribe the decision as being cruel and unnecessary. It has caused a great deal of heartache and grief to the Baltie communities throughout Australia. Those people – and I was one of them – who went from spccial meeting to special meeting of Baltie communities on that particular Sunday afternoon will know how these loyal, hardvvorking, honest and decent Australians – former Lithuanians, Estonians and Latvians – were reduced to grief, anxiety, great sorrow and great disappointment because the nation vvhich they had trusted had let them down. VVhen Parliament reassembled 1 sought to get some August last I asked the Minister for Foreign Affairs a number of question m the Senate. The replies to Oiose questions appear from page 781 onvvards of the Senate Hansard of that date. Other questions m relation ot the Baltie sommunities were asked by my colleague from South Australia, Senator Young. I was not at all satisfied with the Minister's ansvvers. I sent telegrams to the Prime Minister (Mt. VVhitlam), and the best that he could do was to send mc a phot ostāt copy of pages of Hansard containing the ansvvers to vvhich I have referred. Among these ansvvers the Minister said: It is unlikely that thes Soviet Government vvill grant independence to these territories. Here is an admission by the Government that the Soviet Union is acting against the charter of the United Nations. One sees m the platform of the Labor Party that its members sobseribe to the policies of the United Nations charter. Here is an admission by the Government that the Soviet Union is acting against the charter of tfie United Nations dealing vvith the self-determination of people. It is also an admission by the Government that the Soviet Union is acting against its ovvn constitution vvhich provides, amongst other things, that every union republic shall have the right freely to secede from the USSR. Surely the Minister for Foreign Affairs vvas not serious vvhen he said that the recognition of the incorporation of the Baltie states by the Soviet Union vvill facilitate consular activities. particularly m relation to the reunion of families. Does he really beUeve it? Surely he must knovv that m relation to any emigration from the Baltie states permits to emigrate are issued not by the puppet governments m the Baltie states but m Moscovv itself. The reasons put forvvard by the Minister m connection vvith both these issues are, m my, vievv, completeh/ unconvincing. I am of the vievv that they dishononr, cleaj\ undertakings. They, . are our of step with the rest of the world. They recognise and condone gravē injustices that have been commited. They deny the Government's claim to champion small nations. To cap it all, they led to a decision vvhich was carried out, as the motion says, furtively and shamefully. So the whole issue is one thet gives reason for sondemnation not only because of its cruelty but also because of its betrayal. When there is betrayal there is cause for very great concern. I now want to read out a letter from the Prime Minister. This letter was vvritten to the President of the Council of the Lithuanian Community. It is written on 17 May which was the day before the last election. It was m reply to an carlier letter from the President of the Council of the Lithuanian Community m Australia. In the opening sentence there is reference to an earlier communication from the Council. The Prime Minister went on to say: The policy of the present Australian Government is that vvhile not formally recognising the incorporation of Lithuania, Latvia and Estonia into the Soviet Union, it must be cognisant of the dc facto situation and deal vvith the government vvhich has effective control of the territorv m question. On a number of occasions the Government has made its vievvs knovvn on the question of civil liberties and on fundamental human rights, its own adherence to them and its vvish that those rights embodied m the Universal Declaration of Human Rights be extended evervvvhere. In an address at the United Nations Association on the occasion of Austraiia's celebration of the 25th Anniversary of the Universal Declaration I said, inter alia, . . . 'the Declaration has come to be recognised as one of the enlightened events m modern history' and that it is a . . . fundamental objective of the Labor Government to ensure that Australia's policies are soundly based on respect for and on the protection and enhancement of civil liberties and basie humaņ rights'. The Soviet Government is avvare of Australian Government's position m this matter, and vve vvill continue to seek opportunities m the United Nations to promote respect for and observance of basie human rights by all members. Senator GEORGES (Lab., Qld.): VVho signed that letter? Senator DAVIDSON: It is a statement by the Prime Minister of this country. It is a statement by the Leader of the Government m this country. It is a statement by the man who, according to the Hansard, from vvhich I have quoted, made the decision m relation to the Baltie states. According to page 784 of Hansard of Tuesday, 13 August, vvhen I asked Senator Willesee vvhether he made the decision he said: 'No, the decision vvas made vvhen I vvas out of the country.' The inference vvas that the decision vvas made by the Acting Minister for Foreign Affairs. Yet here m this letter the Prime Minister conveyed to this Council, vvhich represents some 40,000 to 50?000 peoplem Australia anda vvhole lot of vvell vvishers beyond, an assurance that the Labor Government vvould promote respect for and observance of basie human rights by all members of the United Nations. It is no vvonder that there is also support for the third part of the motion vvhich is novv before the Senate, that is, that the foreign policy alignments are not serving and vvill not serve Australia's interests. The ACTING DEPUTY PRESI DENT (Senator Marriott): I call Senator Greenvvood vvho vvill be closing the debatē. Senator GREENWOOD (Liberal, Victoria) (10.32): . . . The second legof the motion states: (ii) m breach of a clear undertaking to the contrary given by the Prime Minister the Government shamefullv and furtively extended recognition to the incorporation of the Baltie States m the U.S.S.R., the Minister vvithholding any announcement or explanation of the decision. What the Government has failed to acknovvledge m the course of this debatē is why an undertaking given m May vvas dishonoured m August. The basie proposition vvhich vve assert is that m May the Prime Minister (Mr. VVhitlam), m response to questions asked of him, said that the Government did not propose to change the existing policy. STATEMENT TO LATVIAN NEVVS I shall quote from a letter from the editor of the 'Latvian News' vvhich vvas vvritten to the Australian Press. I quote from the Hobart 'Mercury' of 6 August 1974. The Prime Minister vvas asked: Q: Do you plan to touch on the question at ail, during your coming cisit to Moscovv? A: No. Q: So you are, m fact, saying that your policy m the matter is the same as that of the previous Government? A: Yes. Q: Do you intend to change it? A: No. That vvas stated prior to the election on 18 May. Why then did the Government change its policy? VVe have not been given an ansvver, and the anly attempt at an ansvver by the Minister vvas that the Government recognised the realities. VVere not those realities vvhich the Government novv relies upon the realities of May 1974? Has anything heppened betvveen May and July vvhich vvarranted a change? Senator CAVANAGH (Labor, S.A.): Yes, the people voted. Senator GREENVVOOD: Senator Cavanagh says 'yes\ VVhat vvas it? Senator CAVANAGH: The people voted. They locked you out. Senator GREENVVOOD: Senator Cavanagh may be gfving a revealing ansvver. VVhen the Government got the vote of the people it felt it eould make any decision it pleased and so it repudiated the promise. That is the fundamental basis of the Opposition's motion vvhich says that the Minister is deserving ot censure because a promise to uphold the ideal of freedom, cherished by people who came to this country and by theri descendents, was utterly and shamefully repudiated. Therefore it is a matter vvhich we say is deserving of a censure... There is one final point to vvhich I refer. It has been suggested by Senator Steele Hall that he found himself m a predicament m regard to this resolution. As he savv the position – and he expressed it asis his right – he felt that there vvas merit m the second leg of this three-pronged motion and vvould have voted if he had the opportunity against the First and third legs but m favour of the second leg. I suggest to him that it is not easy to be the independent or the individual senator m this chamber. The motion vvhich the Opposition has moved is a resolution of the Opposition parties and m putting it forvvard I am discharging an obligatiok vvhich I have as Deputy Leader of the Opposition m the Senate to put forvvard the Opposition's vievvpoint. If Senator Steele Hall desires to eliminate from the motion any part of it to vvhich he objects he may move an amendment to the motion. But he has not moved an amendment. Alternatively he may ask that the matter be taken sepaeately and if he had 1 am sure that we vvould have conceded the right of an independent senator to have that approach accepted. But as I understand it, it is a matter for the Senate ultimately to decide. Senator DOUGLAS McCLELLLAND (Lab., N.S.VV. – Minister for the Media and Manager of Government Business m the Senate) In vievv of the remarks just made by Senator Greenvvood I suggest that the motion moevd by him be put m three separate parts. Senator GREENWOOD: lf the Minister proposes it or if Senator Steele Hall proposes it vve vvill not oppose it. VVe have put our motion as vve decided. Senator CAVANAGH: I rise to order. After a motion has been moved and the moved has replied, is it not your duty, Mr. President, to put the question? Can the Senate then rearrange the motion? I spoke m this debatē but if the motion is going to be separated it may vvell be that I vvill vvant to speak on it as it vvill be put and m this case it vvill be a different motion from that to vvhich I spoke. Senator VVITHERS: I rise to order. As I understand standing order 130 you have the right to divide a difficult question. That is your prerogative. The Opposition is not asking you to divide the question but I understand that Senator Douglas McClelland... Senator STEELE HALL: I rise to order. 1 understood that Senator VVithers vvas speaking to standing order 130.1n the debatē I expressed the vievv that the motion vvas a complicated one vvith 3 quite divergent vievvpoints. I ask under standing order 130 that you divide the motion into its constituent parts so that the Senate can give an individual and properly based vievv on reach part. The PRESIDENT: I do not classify the motion asa complicated motion and if Senator Steele Hall feels inclined to divide the question I ask him to seek leave to do so as he raised the matter here. Senator STEELE HALL: I seek leave to divide the question. The PRESIDENT: Is leave granted? Senator GEORGES: No. Honourable senators seem to disagree vvith mc. I vvould like an explanation before I vvithdravv my objection. V/hat I object to about this procedurc, although I am prepared to go along vvitht the Senate, is that vve have debated the coropo&ite motion all day. Although the point vvas made by Senator Steele Hall that that part of the motion dealing vvith the Baltie states vvas hidden, complicated and confused and made ridicnlous by the other parts of the motion, nevertheless vve debated the motion asa vvhole. If Senator Steele Hall then vvants to proceed vvith another urgency motion dealing vvith the Baltie states or take some other action he is entiUed to do that, but why make this appear ridiculous? I do not vvant to be standing out like an odd person not granting leave if the rest of the Senate is prepared to grant leave, and if that si the case I vvill vvithdravv my objection. Senator DOUGLAS McLELLLAND: I vvish to speak to the point of order. A colleague of mine anda member of the Government is under attack by the motion that has been moved by Senator Greenvvood. Senator SIR KENNETH ANDERSON (Lib., N.S.VV.): In 3 parts. Senator DOUGLAS McLELLLAND; In 3 parts and they are on 3 different issues. I have discussed this matter vvith the Minister for Foreign Affairs, vvho is the subject of the motion, and he has expressed the opinion that questions should be put on each of the 3 issues. Therefore I suggest that the Senate should express its opinion on these 3 issues sepatareh/. The PRESIDENT: I have asked the Senate to give leave to Senator Steele Hall. I vvill put it again. 1s leave granted? There being no dissent, leave is granted. Senator STEELE HALL (South Australia – Leader of theLiberal Movement) (10.50): I vvill move if that is the appropriate thing to do, that this question be divided into 3 constituent parts and taken seratim m that fashion. The PRESIDENT: I vvill novv proceed to divide the question into 3 parts. The preamble vvill apply separatelv to each part. That the preamble and the first partof the motion be agreed to. The Senate divided. Ayes – 28. Noes – 28. Ouestion so resolved m the negative. AYES-29,NOES-27; OUESTION RESOLVED IN THE AFFIRMATIVE Question put: That the preamble and the second part of the motion be agreed to: That the Minister for Foreign Affairs is deserving of censure and ought to resign beacuse: (ii) m breach of a clear undertaking to the contrary given by the Prime Minister the Government shamefulh/ and furtively extended recognition to the incorporation of the Baltie States into the U.S.S.R., the Minister vvithholding any announcernent or explanation of the decision. The Senate divided. Ayes — 29, noes — 27, majority — 2. Ouestion so resolved m the afTumative. (Ayes: Anderson, Baume, Bessell, Bonner, Carick, Chaney, Cormack, Cotton, Davidson, Drake-Brockman, Durack, Greenvvood, Guilfoyle, Hall, Jessop. Laucke, Lavvrie, Marriott, Martin, Maunsell, Rae, Scott, Missen, Sheil, Sim, Townley, Webster, VVithers, Young, Noes: Bishop, Brovvn, Button,Cameron,Cavanagh, Coleman, Devitt, Everett,Gietzelt, Grimēs, Keeffe, McAuliffe, McClelland D., McClelland J„ Mclntosh, McLaren, Melzer, Milliner, Mulvihil, Murphy, o'Byrne, Primmer, Walsh, V/heeldon, VVillesee, NVriedt, Poyser. Pairs: Wood, VVright, Georges, Drury.) Question put: That the preamble and the third part pf the motion be agreed to. The Senate divided. Ayes – 28, noes — 28. Ouestion so resolved m the negative. Senate adjourned at 11.8 p.m. End.']
