Representative_Docs: ['Radio programmes for N. Australians EASY LESSONS FOR BEGINNERS BROADCAST BY THE AUSTRALIAN BROADCASTING COMMISSION Easy Lesson No. 4, Saturday, 26th July, 1952. In this broadcast lesson Mary, John and Paul will teach you how to use some words and sentences which are both simple and necessary. Listen to Paul and John having a telephone conversation: John: Paul? Paul: Yes, yes. This is Paul. I am here. John: It is all right, Paul! This is John here! Paid: Oh, hullo, John! John: Is Mary there, Paul? Paul: No, Mary is not here. John: Oh, well, she is coming this morning. Are you learning-English? Paul: Yes, I am learning English . . . John: That\'s good. Well . . . I am coming to the studio. Paul: Are you coming here? John: Yes, I am coming now. So cheerio! If you listen to a Metropolitan Station, you will hear this broadcast every Saturday at 12 45-1 p.m. (South Australian Stations at 12.15-12.45 p.m.). Country Stations and 6WN Perth broadcast the programme at 7 a.m.-7.15 a.m. Other programmes for more advanced learners are broadcast as follows:— _ _ \' Easy Lessons (27-52). Saturday, 1.45 p.m. (S.A., 1.15 p.m.; W.A., 7.15 a.m.) Harder Lessons: Sunday, 7.4b a.m. (S.A., 7.15 a.m.; W.A.. 7.45 a.m.). RADIO PROGRAMME #FOR NEW AUSTRALIANS" BROADCAST BY THE AUSTRALIAN BROADCASTING COMMISSION Saturday, 26th July, 1952. Paul is a good learner; he learns well. Do you know when to say "good" and when to say "well"? You can learn about these two words with Paul if you listen to this lesson. John: Doesn\'t Olga like your saxonhone, Paul? Paul: No. And I play very good! Mary: Oh, you musn\'t say that, Paul! Paul: What, Mary? Don\'t I play the saxophone good? Mary: No, no, Paul! You play the saxophone well, very well, but NOT "good!" Paul: Oh! I play the saxophone well. You sing well. John drives the car well. I must learn that. John: Yes "She is a good singer." "She sings well." Mary: You are a good pia.fst. You play the piano well. Paul: I like good music. That is a good film. But, Mary teaches English well. John plays the piano well. This is only a small part of the Radio Lesson. You can see that its an easy and entertaining way of learning English. You can have a Radio Booklet posted to you each month by writing to the Department of Education in the State where you live. The Radio Booklets are FREE and contain Broadcast Programmes in the form of English lessons. You may also get information about Continuation Classes and Correspondence Lessons from the Department of Education. If you listen to a Metropolitan Station, you will hear this broadcast every Saturday at 1.45 p.m. (South Australian Stations at 1.15 p.m.). Country Stations and 6WN Perth broadcast the programme at 7.15 a.m. Other programmes are broadcast as follows: Easy Lessons (1-26), Saturday, 12.45 p.m. (S.A- 12.15 p.m., W.A., 7 a.m.). Harder Lessons, Sunday, 7.45 a.m. (S.A., 7.15 a.m., W.A., 7.45 a.m.).', 'Radio Programme For New Australians BROADCAST BY THE AUSTRALIAN BROADCASTING COMMISSION (A) Very Easy Lessons, 1-26 Easy Lesson, Number One (1). Saturday, 4th July, 1953. The Easy Lessons start again with this broadcast. If you begin with this broadcast you will learn English very quickly. John: Come in! Come in! Paul: Good morning! John: Good morning! Oh, you are Paul! Paul: Yes, I am Paul. John: Come in! Hullo, Mary! come in. Mary: Hullo, John. John: Here is Paul, Mary. Mary: Hullo, Paul. Paul: Good morning, Mary. John: Paul is learning English, ļ Are you Australian?— Yes, I am. No, I am not. Am I European?— Yes, you are. No, you are not. Easy Lesson Number Two (2). Saturday, 11th July, 1953. In this lesson Mary, John and Paul will teach you how to .use the simple words and sentences which are used every day in Australia. John: What is this? Paul: It is a match. John: And what is that? Paul: That?—Oh that? Not this? It is the telephone. John: Yes—and it is ringing! This is here . . . That is there. This cigarette is here . . . That cigarette is theer. Easy Lesson Number Three (3). Saturday, 18th July, 1953. Paul is learning to use the words and phrases that are used in Australia. Listen! You can learn them too. Mary: No, no Paul! You must say: "Where—is—he? John is a man . . . You must say he, not it! Paul: Oh, dear! Where is he? Mary: Well, he is coming. Listen . . . Yes, he is coming now. That is John\'s car . . Open the door, please, Paul. John: Good morning, Mary, it ļis a beautiful morning. We are ! going to Melbourne in the car. And you, Paul. Paul: We? Mary: You—and John—and I. We are going to Melbourne. Easy Lesson, Number Four (4). Saturday, 25th July, 1953. Paul is practising his English Lesson. Heis learning to use— I . . . You . . . He . . . phe . . . We . . . Listen . . . Paul: I am a man. You are a man. She is a woman. We are men. Oh dear! The telephone is ringing. Yes? Hullo? John: (on telephone): Paul? Paul: Yes, yes. This is Paul. ... Oh, hullo, John! ... No, Mary is not here. I am learnļ ing English. Are you coming i here? John: Yes. I am coming now. So, cheerio. Easy Lesson, Number Twenty-Seven (27). Saturday, 4th July, 1953. Paul has a good sense of humour. That\'s one reason why it is enjoyable to learn English with him. Mary: Well, tonight we\'ll all go to the pictures. There\'s a very good film. Paul: When Olga and I go to ļ the pictures, Mary, we go by i bus. It stops at the theatre and ! I say: "Well, let\'s go in." Then i Olga says: "Paul! Where are I the tickets?" John: And where are the tickets? Paul: At home! On top of the radio in my room! Mary: Paul, you\'re terrible! I\'ll never give you the tickets, quickly. John, put them in your pocket quickly. John: All right, Mary. There they are—in my pocket. Paul: And now John will put on a good coat to come to the pictures and the tickets will be in the pocket of his old coat hanging behind the door of his room. Radio Programmes for N. Australians Easy Lesson, Number Twenty-Eight (28). Saturday, 11th July, 1953. Paul can write letters in Eng- j lish now . . . And practise his j English at the same time. Paul: 1 must write a letter. Can you give me an envelope and some writing paper? Mary: Yes, of course. Here\'s an envelope. The writing paper ■is under that box of chocolates. Paul: There\'s my letter. I\'ll post it when I go home. Mary: Sorry, Paul. I can\'t hear you. The radio is too loud. I\'ll turn it off. That\'s better. John: Mary! Paul! Can I come in? Mary: Come in, John. We\'re in the lounge. Come to the fire and get warm. John: Brrr! It\'s a very cold night. What a big fire! Mary: Yes. Paul likes chopping wood. John: That\'s lucky. Easy Lesson, Number Twenty-Nine (29). Saturday, 18th July, 1953. This is a very important lesson because Paul learns to make questions with the words "do" and "does" ... Mary: Yes, Paul, John and I want to teach you a hard lesson today . . . We\'ll tell you all about "do" . . . First—l say: I can speak English. Question: Can I speak English? Now—l say: They like chocolates. Question: Do they like chocolates? John: We learn English every day. Mary: Do we learn English every day? John: Men put their money in their pockets. Mary: Do men put their money in their pockets? We learn—do we learn? You want—do you want? But—does he, does she, and does it! John: Paul drinks coffee every day . . . Mary: Question: Does he drink coffee every day? John: Olga learns English by radio. Mary: Does Olga learn English by radio? Paul: I see ... I take the "s" from drinks and I put it on "do"—Does he drink? Does she drink? Does it drink? Easy Lesson, Number Thirty (30). Saturday, 25th July, 1953 Paul is a good learner; he learns well. In this lesson you will learn when to say "good" and when to say "well." John: Doesn\'t Olga like your saxophone? Paul: No. And I play very good. Mary: Oh, you mustn\'t say that, Paul! Paul: What, Mary? Don\'t I play the saxophone good? Mary: No, no, Paul! You play the saxophone well, very well— but not "good!" Paul: On! I play the saxophone well. You sing well. John drives the car well. John: Yes. "She is a good singer. "She sings well." Paul: I like good music. That is a good film. Mary teaches English well. John plays the piano well. And now—"do" and "don\'t" . . . "does" and "doesn\'t"— Do you smoke?— Don\'t you smoke? Does Olga like chocolate?— Doesn\'t she like chocolate? Do they listen to the radio?— Don\'t they listen to the radio? These are only parts of the radio lessons. You can see that it is an easy and entertaining way of learning English. The radio lessons are broadcast at the following times from A.B.C. Stations. Metropolitan Stations Very Easy Lessons (1-26): Saturday, 12.45 p.m., 2CN, Canberra, 12.45 p.m., S.A., 12.15 p.m., Perth, 6WN, 7 a.m. , Easy Lessons (27-52): Saturday, 1.45 p.m., 2CN Canberra, 1.45 p.m., S.A., 1.15 p.m., Perth 6WN, 7.15 a.m. Harder Lessons: Sunday, 7.45 a.m., 2CY Ganberra, 7.45 a.m., S.A., 7.15 a.m., Perth, 6WN, 7.30 j a.m. Country Stations. Very Easy Lessons (1-26): Saturday, 7 a.m. Easy Lessons (27-52): Saturday, 7.15" a.m. Harder Lessons: Sunday, 7.45 a.m. (S.A., 7.15 a.m., W.A., 7.30 a.m.) Radio Booklets The Radio Lessons are printed in a booklet. You can have a booklet posted to you each month. Write to the Department of Education in the State where .you live. The RADIO BOOKLET is FREE. You may also get information about EVENING CLASSES and CORESPONDENCE LESSONS from the Department of Education.', 'Radio Programme For New Australians RADIO PROGRAMME "FOR NEW AUSTRALIANS" BROADCAST BY THE AUSTRALIAN BROADCASTING COMMISSION . (A) Very Easy Lessons 1-26 Lesson 18, Saturday, May 2,1953 Paul is learning "isn\'t" and "aren\'t" . . . Paul: Mary, you say: Isn\'t that right, John? \'Isn\'t\' must be \'is not,\' but how must I use it? Mary: It works like this, Paul ... we can say: That\'s not right . . . OR that isn\'t right . . . Paul: Ye-es . . . Olga is not oioadc . . . Clga\'s not blonde . . . Olga isn\'t blonde. Mary: Yes, that\'s it . . «. Then: That is not right . . . That isn\'t right ... Is not that right? . . . Isn\'t that right? Paul: Yes, I understand that. Listen . . . Can not Olga speak English? Can\'t Olga speak English? . . . Must not John telephone? Mustn\'t John telephone? . . . Is not that John\'s car? Isn\'t that John\'s car? . . . • Lesson 19, Saturday, 9th May, 1953. Paul, Mary and John are shopping. Paul learns about "Can," "Must," "Want." Grocer: Good morning, Mrs. Bennett. What can I do for you? Mary: I want two pounds of sugar, please . . and a pound of tea . . . I like the tea in that packet—yes, that one. Thanks . . . and half a pound of butter. Grocer: Is that all, Mrs. Bennett? Mary: Yes . . . No, I must buy coffee. You like coffee better than tea, Paul? Paul: Yes, I like coffee very much. Mary: Then I\'ll buy a pound of coffee. Lesson 20, Saturday, 16th May, 1953. In this lesson, Mary and John teach Paul about "mine," "yours," "his," "hers," "ours" and "theirs." John: Oh, those are my matches. Paul: No, it\'s a new box. They are mine. John: But mine is a new box, too. I am sure it\'s mine. Paul. Paul: Mary! These matches are not his . . . They are mine! John: And I am sure they are mine, Mary! Mary: Really, you men! Where are those matches? Thank you, Paul . . . Yes, of course! . . . Paul, they are not yours . . . and John, they are not yours . . . the matches are mine! Lesson 21, Saturday, 23rd May, 1953 When you listen to this lesson, you can learn about that little word "of." Paul: Mary—this box of chocolates is for you . . . Mary: How very nice, Paul. Thank you very much! . . . But you must" say "of," Paul— a box of chocolates . . . Paul: Wrong again! (B)—Easy Lessons, 27-52 Lesson 44, Saturday, 2nd May, 1953 Mary and John are pleased with Paul. Paul speaks well now. Now he can learn harder things. John: Paul speaks well now. Mary: I know, John! Do you think we must teach him the past now? John: Yes, I do, Mary. I\'m sure he can learn the past all right . . . Paul: And what is the past? Mary: The words for yesterday and before yesterday, Paul. Mary: Listen very carefully . . Now, you are here, in my house . . . Yesterday you WERE at work. John: Today, I am here in Mary\'s house. Yesterday I WAS at work. Paul: I WAS . . . YOU WERE . . . Mary: I am . . . you are . . . I WAS . . . You WERE Lesson 45, Saturday, 9th May, 1953. Paul learns more about the past. This time he learns about "had." John: We had a very good time, last year. Paul: Had? Had? I don\'t know thai word. Mary: Past of \'have,\' Paul . . . Very easy, too. Always *had\' ... I had—we had—he had— they had— Paul: Oh, that is easy . . . I have a bicycle now and Olga has a bicycle now . . . And last year—l had a bicycle and she had a bicycle . . . Lesson 46, Saturday, 16th May, 1953 In this lesson Paul learns about the past of LEARN, COOK, WORK and MAKE. Paul: You are a very good cook! Where did you learn to cook, Mary? Mary: I learned a long time ago. I learned during the war. Paul: Learned—learned . . today I learn English—yesterday, I learned . . . 0.X., I understand. Mary: That\'s right. I was in the Red Cross during the war —from 1940 till 1945. I worked in a Red Cross Canteen. For the first few months I cooked easy things—l made—remember make?— Well, I made tea and coffee and sandwiches. Lesson 47, Saturday, 23rd May, 1953 Paul knows the past well now. He can tell Mary about yesterday. Yesterday Paul and John were on a farm. Paul: The calves wore always so hungry. I took the big pails of milk for Nancy. Mary: It was very hard work for her . . . so many hungry ens and the calves and pigs, too! John: Well, we washed up after lunch and dinner, Bill and Paul and T. Mary: i rial s gouti . . . – Paul: Then in the evenings we smoked and talked, or we played cards. . . . Lesson 48, Saturday, 30th May, 1953. Listen carefully with Paul. Mary and John are teaching him about clearly, slowly, well, badly, quickly. Mary: My voice is clear. I speak clearly. John: And I speak slowly, very slowly. I want Paul to understand. Paul: I think I understand. John drives the car slowly, but Mr. Smith drives quickly. He will have an accident. Mary: Right! Paul: And I remember this, too. John is a good driver. He drives the car very well. John: Quite right! Mary: Hmm! Yes . . . he\'s not a bad driver. He doesn\'t drive the car badly. These are only parts of radio lessons. You can see that it is an easy and entertaining way of learning English. The radio lessons are broadcast at the following times from A.B.C. COUNTRY STATIONS Very Easy Lessons (1-26): Saturday, 7 a.m. Easy Lessons (27-52): Saturday, 7.15 a.m. Harder Lessons: Sunday, 7.45 a.m. (S.A. 7.15 a.m.; W.A. 7.30 a.m.) RADIO BOOKLETS The radio lessons are printed in a booklet. Representative_Docs: ['BOOK REVIEW Dr. H. Biezais\' Review of H. Schreiber\'s Land im Osten (Dusseldorf: Econ-Verlag. 1961), 418 pp. It cannot be said that there is a lack of activity in the investigation of the history of Latvia. The generation of historians, who grew up in independent Latvia, carries on its work both here in exile and in the homeland to the extent that existing circumstances permit. Next, it is Baltic-( ierman historians that exhibit the greatest activity. This group has at all times shown a lively interest in the country where they themselves as well as their fathers have lived. To these c.;n be added yet a third group which is neither Latvian nor Baltic-German and thus is often motivated by purely academic interests. Members of it are to be found in Europe, the USA, and elsewhere. The interests, methods, and points of view of these three groups are so diverse that it is impossible to identify any even partially unified notion of the history of our land and people. The author of the work considered here belongs to the third group. (He is an Austrian and a doctor of the University of Vienna.) Writing of our country, he has significantly entitled one of his chapters "The Much-Desired Bride," an expression which, although figurative, is not without a profound insight into the heart of the matter. His basic concern is the question of German expansionism in the east and its development through the preceding century. He has endeavored to see matters as they are, without any whitewashing of either one side or the other, and to find an answer to the question why the Germans have been so utterly unsuccessful in their eastward ambitions, which have culminated in our day in the greatest catastrophe of their history. The book is written in an engaging style. One has to admire the author\'s by Prof. Dr. Phil, and Dr. Theol. Haralds Biezais THE MUCH-DESIRED BRIDE bibliography and the justness of his conclusions, all the more so. because the differences among Eastern European nations and the conflicts of their interests are so complicated that it is very difficult for an outsider to see through them. Schreiber. however, has managed this commendably. Eschewing a technical style and yet standing on solid foundations, he is capable of interesting even the least initiated reader. Understandably, we are greatly interested in what the author has to say about Latvians. Having discussed the ancient geographical boundaries of the Baltic nations and their situation before the crusades of the Teutonic Order, he first depicts in very clear strokes the tragedy that took place in the conquest of the country of the Old Prussians and in their annihilation. In characterizing this old kindred nation of ours and especially in evaluating their religion as well as the cruelty with which Christianity was forced upon them, Schreiber is far ahead of many of our own scholars. "The religion of this people was just as much or little pagan as, say, Islam, whose adherents the crusaders likewise called pagans. After centuries of pure nature worship, there had developed among the Prussians the cult of a few personified gods who were worshipped in sacred forests and beside sacred waters" (p. 73). After countless destructive battles, the Prussian ration succumbed, but it never accepted the new faith. "The few remaining living warriors did not embrace Christianity; after the quarter-century of desperate battles it remained to them the religion of a savage enemy, a religion whose commandments the Prussians simply could not believe in. The men who after this long war had yet remained alive wandered kiver to Lithuania." (p. 82). The author rfdds: "And despite this, there are historians who dare to maintain that the Prussians were not annihilated but only Christianized and Germanized." (Ibid.) Not without bitterness and irony he concludes the chapter on the Prussians with these words: That which the Germans expelled from these regions in 1944 and 1945 experienced and suffered has been recorded on thousands of printed pages of paper — a testimony of great sorrow and great injustice. The traces of another injustice, which the German lords had perpetrated seven hundred years ago, only the dumb earth has retained in its fateful position between Middle and Eastern Europe..." (p. 84) The author has not lacked courage to look truth in the face and to draw the necessary conclusions. based on the Chronicle of Henry of Livonia, when he comes to speak about Latvians. He arouses admiration for the Baltic nations in their hundred years\' long struggle against the technically better armed German knights, who enjoyed the backing of all of Christian Europe and of the Pope. Henry, the priest, erred when he wrote in his chronicle intended for Western Europe about "the awakening of the idolatrous Livs from their idol-worship and sin through the fire of love" which the German knights had brought. Henry implies that the Livs were backward and isolated, but actually, together with other inhabitants of Latvia, they were already known in the Baltic Sea area as good fighters and pirates on a par with the vikings. They had already established connections with both East and West. Tt was not long before the Pone was forced to send his legate William to settle the quarrels between the Teutonic Order and the bishop, because they tried to take away from each other and acquire for themselves the greater part of the jointly conquered land of the Latvians. This "fire of love" was so strong that even the legate was unable to extinguish it. When in 1226 the legate stepped aboard his ship, he had gained an insight into the ambiguous position in which bishops of Ostland found themselves. Christian, the first bishop of Prussia, died embittered and without achievements in 1245. The bishop of Livonia, Albert, made himself a name of shining glory which no knightly order could darken. However, at bottom their fate was one and the same and had to be, for the men of their times were not yet ripe for a battle with spiritual weapons. Christianity in its lofty medieval form was still burning with a clean flame uncontaminated with bloody vapors from the heretics at the stake; nevertheless, the nations for whom this salvation was destined saw only the blood-red crosses and swords. The men saw an enemy, the women feared the new religion which dissolved their heathen marriages. And of all classes the knights were the one least suited to dispel hatred and fear, to inspire faith..." (p. 105) Further on, Schreiber speaks about how the knights found worthy opponents in the Estonian sea pirates as well as in the cunning Latvian tribe, the Semigallians. He concludes that it was the knights themselves who deprived Germanism of a future in this region. In the thirteenth century and also later, they showed their often obvious incapacity to adapt themselves to their task; they all merely brandished the sword and left thinking to their tutors. Such methods succeeded if clever men led the order, but through the centuries they did not suffice to turn the country into a German province. The knights forever remained foreign masters, and in all countries of the world the days of foreign masters are numbered. The chivalric migrants to Livonia, whose names in later centuries often acquired a high ring, lived under the fateful delusion that one can make the ground fertile with blood, (p. 108) Schreiber corrects also R. Wittram\'s assumption that at least the towns of Livonia were places where the "natives" had free opportunity to work alongside German artisans. He writes: "It is possible that to those who drove to the market the gates were opened; but as citizens in the three largest, and even in the smaller, Baltic towns we find almost exclusively Germans, a few Danes, later Swedes and Russians." (p. 109) In order to characterize the conditions prevailing in later centuries, the author quotes extensively from travel descriptions from the seventeenth and eighteenth centuries. The impressions of such travelers are in no respect flattering to the rulers of the country. If in some instances the travelers devote too much attention to minor oddities that strike a stranger\'s eye, they cannot for any reason be suspected of ulterior motives. In this connection, Schreiber has devoted much attention to Gottlieb Merkel\'s work on the Latvians and also to those exceptional original attempts to improve the lot of the Latvian peasant linked with the names of K. Schulze, O. Ekesparre, and others. Characterizing the situation of the Latvians in the 18th century, Schreiber writes that it did not have its equal in any other land where Germans lived. "The serfs were sold and traded like slaves; the manor lord who thereby parted a marriage was merely penalized with a money fine. In the beatings, even torture, not only did the lord\'s wife take part, but sometimes their children as well, as if they could not learn something like that early enough." (p. 266) Those are grave words, which, did they not flow from the pen of an Austrian, could easily thought to be chauvinistic. Baltic-German historians have always pointed to such evils as exceptions. It seems that the author was aware of this. His interpretation is almost the direct opposite, namely, that the positive instances are the exceptions: "In certain manors and in certain families the striving old soldiers had achieved a quite considerable personal culture, but on the whole, as a class, they were still unable to see beyond the tips of their swords." (p. 266) The author devotes special attention to the well known German humanists whose names are closely tied to the dissemination of the ideas of 18th century Enlightenment in Latvia, namely, Hamann, Herder, and Merkel. The time had become ripe for the manor lords to realize that through their idleness and simple exploitation of the economic advantages given them by the serf system they had fallen far behind the rest of Europe. Thus an increased migration of tutors from Germany began. With a sense of humor Schreiber writes that the Baroness Barbara Helena yon Budberg, "a lady of sense, beauty, and 20,000 Albert dollars," hired for a few dollars a tutor for her son Waldemar. He was brought to the estate in a four-horse carriage, preceeded by a man on horseback. The tutor was Hamann. Herder and others followed in his wake. These humanists learned to know quite well the life and thought of the country nobility. "The lords had not yet realized that a civilization consisting of towns, towns\' rights, taxes, and laws was not everything. They could not understand why the miserable tutors whom they had hired for a few dollars would rather listen to the songs of the Latvian serfs than to the conversation by the fireplaces of the nobility." (p. 267) Schreiber, in speaking about these carriers of the new spirit, says correctly that "each of them felt that he could not be silent." (p. 264) And, as we know, these men did speak and were therefore forced to leave Latvia or were, like Merkel, condemned to silence for a whole century. But what did the oppressed nation itself do? Perhaps the most significant point if one looks at the book with the eyes of a Latvian is that the author does answer this question, namely, that the nation created its very vast and complex folklore in which it expressed its notions of what life should be like if it were free. As far as I am aware, this is the first instance where a non-Latvian has seen it and expressed it so clearly. Thereafter the author shows that there developed slowly that German type which regarded himself as a superman and the other eastern nations as submen of higher or lower rank. He illustrates this copiously with examples of National Socialist inhumanity in the so-called "Eastern Realm." The author\'s thesis it that through the centuries of their eastward expansionary ambitions, in meeting other nations, the Germans have never seen them as people equivalent to themselves. Often they have been people with character traits superior to the German colonizers. The book is richly illustrated with photographs of Latvia. There is an expressive scene of the entrance way to a Latvian farmhouse with a fireplace from the end of the 18th century. The book contains also many well drawn maps, an extensive bibliography, and an index of persons and places. This is certainly a unique work which deserves attention. It sparks many reconsiderations, because we have not been used to such open language. It is true that now and then among Baltic-Germans one can hear voices which attempt to revise the old overlord mentality. We need recall only the recently voiced opinions of Dr. Marniz and others. We should mention especially G. Rauch, who, in his epoch-making address during the 1961 Baltic-German Culture Days in Liibeck, has tried to turn a new leaf in the understanding of Baltic history. But let us note that these are only single voices without echoes in the official Baltic-German organizations and society. Let us not forget that at the same time the Superintendent of Berlin, P. Kahl, has published a book about the meeting of German Protestantism with Russian Orthodoxy in the Baltic region, wherein he expresses regret that "temporarily" this meeting has been disrupted. Why could he not continue this meeting right there in Berlin? These are circumstances which make one glad about the appearance of Schreiber\'s work. In a provocative manner, it forces the reevaluation of the old and ever-new problems of the history of our native land. We may now do this much less passionately than was possible only a few decades ago, but the problems have not therefore lost their power to attract the interest even of foreigners. Translated by Ojārs Krātiņš', 'A MAP OF LIVONIA By Col. L. Lieven ACCORDING TO HENRY THE LATVIAN AND AT THE TIME OF THE BISHOPS AND MASTERS OF THE ORDER. (THE PERIOD OF ABOUT 1200 — 1562 A.D.). This map is undated. It was designed by W. C. Triebe, drawn by J. W. Krause, and engraved by F. Ramberg. The country of origin of these men is not stated. The left top corner shows a female figure holding a balance, the upper plate of which contains a parchment scroll with the words "Rights of Nations." The scroll is shown as partly torn at the end. A cross lies on the lower plate of the balance. The left bottom corner depicts Latvians or Estonians gathering for a uprising. The background shows a Latvian or Estonian castle earthwork or hill-fort (pilskalns). A cut-down oak tree (perhaps a symbol of the liberty of the Baltic people or their ancient religion, where holy trees played an important role) is growing new branches. The Latvians or Estonians are being joined by Russians, apparently coming to their aid. The top right corner has a table explaining conventional signs showing properties of the Archbishop of Riga and their vassals, of the Bishops of Reval (now Tallin), Dorpat (Tartu or Tērbata), Oesel (Saaremaa or Sāmu sala) and their vassals, of the properties or the Livonian Order (the northern branch of the Teutonic Order) and its vassals; castles of Germans; fortified towns with a castle; minor towns with a castle; as well as the pre-conquest period wooden hill-forts (pilis) of Livonians, Latvians, and Estonians. The left bottom corner shows a heathen inhabitant kneeling before a sacred oak which has been cut down but which is driving new shoots. His hand is stretched in a pleading, protecting gesture over the stump of the tree. A Bishop is interceding for him with a Knight of the Order, behind whom stand some sinister-looking figures with sardonic expressions. The shaded area of the map, excluding the triangle based on the Daugava (i.e., excluding the southeastern part of Latgale), is marked "Lettland" (Latvia; the older, latinized version of this name was Lettia). The narrow coastal area to the west of "Lettland", from the Pernaa river in the north to Kokenois (Koknese) on the Daugava is marked "Livland" (Livonia, in a more narrow sense of the word, i.e. the *) Comments on the period shown by the map, the eastern borders of the Lettigallians (Letts), and the selection of the quoted passages from Henry the Lett\'s chronicle are by Dr. Nikodēms Bojārs. area actually inhabited by the Livs in the 13 century). Curonia (Kurzeme) and Semigallia (Zemgale), south of the Daugava, are left almost blank. Only a few places are indicated there, as the map covers only "Livonia" (i.e., the area of the Baltic nations conquered by the Brethren of the Sword or Fratres Militiae Christi and by their successors after 1237, the Teutonic knights, until about 1242, before the subjugation of the Cours and Semigallians). This, then, approxiamately answers the question as to what period of history is shown by the map; as nearly as one can tell, the political situation of about 1237 is shown. The reasons for this conclusion are the following: 1.) Couronia and Semigallia are not yet conquered by the Teutons. 2.) Estonia is already conquered, including the northern part; however, North Estonia was ceded to the King of Denmark in 1238, and was regained by the Master of the Livonian Order, Burchard yon Dreyerlewen, only in 1343 (de facto, by occupation) or in 1346 de iure, by purchase from the Danish King). Since the Danish possessions are not marked as a separate political unit, the map refers to the situation before 1238. 3.) The Bishop of Riga and the Order took possession of Adzele (Terra Adzelle, or the northeastern part of Lettigallia) in 1224, and this part of Adzele is already shown within the boundaries of "Livonia" (in the wider sense of the word). The Dukes of Pskov dominated the eastern corner of Adzele until about 1340, when they were defeated by the same Master of the Order — yon Dreyerlewen, and the Lettigallian regions of Abrene (now Pytalovo), Augšpils (now Vysgorod), and Sarkani (now Krasnogorodsk) were regained by the state of Livonia. This region of Abrene was again conquered and joined to Russia in 1464-1476 and again after 1945. The map shows this eastern part of Adzele as well as the southeastern part of Latgale as belonging to the Russians; therefore the period after 1224 but before 1340 is shown. 4.) After their defeat in the battle of Saule in 1236, the few surviving Brethren of the Sword joined the Teutonic Order of Prussia in 1237. This situation is shown by the map; the initial German conquests in Courland (1231-2) have disappeared after the battle of Saule. The second important question is the age of the map itself. Is it possible to date it approxiamately ? And who inspired the subject of this map? The map was shown to a specialist, and he dated it approximately late in the 18/ A century. This conclusion also helps to answer the question, as to who inspired the subject of this map. If the dating is correct, then it would fall into the period of the "Enlightenment", a philosophical movement of the 17th and 18th centuries, based largely on rationalism and influenced by the earlier spiritual trends of the Renaissance. The Enlightenment was the time of Diderot and the Encyclopaedists. The source might have been a liberal-minded German Baltic nobleman, but if so, he might have hot made himself too popular among his own because of the nationalist and partly anti-clerical nature of the subject. To Latvian readers this needs no further explanation. It is rather as though an 18th century American had produced a map claiming for the American Indians the right to their ancestral lands. Could the author of the map have been a Russian? First of all, the eastern borders of Livonia (including Lettia) are drawn favorably for the Russians, since the actual borders of Livonia during most of the time of its existence were really farther to the east. Besides, the Russians are shown to be hurrying to help , the uprising of the Letts or Estonians. There were at the end of the 18th century a good number of men and women in Russia who were strongly under the influence of French and English liberalism and anti-clericalism. This movement was headed by Empress Catherine II herself. It is doubted, however, whether these "enlightened" Russians were particularly interested in the rights of minorities at that time. The Empress certainly did not do anything to create an independent state of Latvia or Estonia. The Russification was rather encouraged by the rulers of Russia. If neither a German Baltic nobleman nor a Russian ordered this map to be designed, drawn and engraved, and as the country of its origin is unknown to the commentator, we will have to look elsewhere for a possible source. Who could this person have been? In 1791 Herder published his work on the "Ideas on the Philosophy and History of Mankind", and in this he referred to the Baltic people, which corresponds to his chapter on the Slavonic people. He also published Latvian and Estonian songs in his collection of folk songs.* Another friend of the Latvians and Estonians of that period was Garlieb Merkel. He was born in 1769 as a son of a German Pastor in Livonia who had been born in Weimar, Germany, and was therefore a newcomer to the Baltic. Garlieb Merkel published a book in 1796 with the title "The Latvians". This was the first book in which the nationhood of the Latvians, their rights and traditions as an ancient people, as well as their future formed the theme. He fought passionately all his life for the rights of the Baltic people in the spirit of the Enlightenment. Merkel was a devoted admirer of Catherine II because of her liberal ideas and her intended reforms for Russia. In the first sentence of his introduction to his book on the Latvians, Merkel says: "Reason has triumphed, and the Century of Justice begins." This volume had effects on the German nobility in the Baltic lands. In 1838, Landrat R. J. L. yon Samson confessed that he had experienced a sense of guilt when he read the passionate works of Dr. Merkel.** The commentator has, therefore, no doubt that it was Garlieb Merkel to whom we owe this remarkable document showing the Latvians and Estonians as peoples with a future of freedom before them, after centuries of oppression. It now only remains to explain why the Latvians (Letti seu Lettigalli of the 13th century chronicles) are shown as occupying the southeastern corner of Vidzeme (Idumea of the chronicles), and why Russians are coming to the aid of the Baltic insurgents. This may be due to the fact that the map was based on the annals of Henry the Latvian (Henricus Lettus of the 13th century; a new English translation of his chronicle has appeared recently***). Therefore, the map shows the situation as it was in the first half of the 13th century. At that time, the city-states of Pskov and Polotsk attempted to claim all Estonia and Vidzeme (Livonia) as their sphere of influence, and opposed the Germans as invaders. Besides purely aggressive motives and the tribute which the Russians sometimes exacted from a few minor states of the Baltic people (such as Tālava), the Russian Dukes had a commercial interest in the Baltic. The Russians desired to control this area through which the trade passed from Sweden to the Black Sea, the Volga, and thence beyond to the Far East through Central Asia. Pskov and Polotsk constantly urged the Livonians, Latvians and Estonians to rise and supported these uprisings with armies. However, those or the Lettish (Latvian) princes or castleelders (virsaiši) who concluded treaties of mutual assistance with the German Order, must have regarded the Russian* as a greater menace, because of the interminable warfare of the Lettigallians against the Krivichi tribe in the east (as witnessed by the existence of the "defense zone" of about 300 hill-forts on the Lettish eastern border). Nevertheless, there were some struggles of the Letts against the Order, although not as extensive and tenacious as in Couronia, Semigallia, and Estonia. These struggles and the claims of the Dukes of Pskov and Polotsk were well known to John IV, the Terrible, who tried to "reclaim" and conquer for Muscovy the "lost" Baltic lands. *) R. Wittram, Baltische Geschichte, Munchen, 1954, p. 151. **) R. Wittram, op. cit., p. 157. ***) Henricus Lettus, Origines Livoniae sacrae et civilis, Engl, transi, by J. A. Brundage (Madison, 1961). It may be of interest to discuss why the heathen Latvian, Livonian or Estonian shown on the map appeals for protection to the Bishop against the Teutonic knight. Possibly, because Bishop Albert Buxhoevden, the Bishop of Riga in early 13. century and a Spiritual Prince of the Holy Roman Empire, a friend of both Frederik (Friedrich) II Hohenstauffen and of Pope Innocent 111, was described by Henry the Latvian as a great medieval statesman, and he sought the cooperation of the local population against the Russians. Frederik II declared in 1224 that the converted people of Livonia and Prussia had become the citizens of the Holy Roman Empire and therefore they were free men and not serfs of kings, dukes or princes. The Pope Innocent 111 tried to abolish the slavery in the whole Christian world and defended in his messages the possessions of the Baltic people from the rapacity of the conquerors, while his successor Honorius 111 admitted the Christianized Baits in the community of free peopļev (in libertatem filiorum Dei) in 1225. Bishop Albert, according to Henry the Latvian, "received back .as sons" both Vetseke, the leader of* the Livonians, and some of the Latvian leaders, after their joint and unsuccessful rising against him. The attitude of the Teutonic knights was certainly different. The history of this uprising (in 1212) is best described in the words of Henry the Latvian himself:**** "A great contention arose between the Brothers of the Militia from Wenden and the Letts of Autine, the latter being in the Bishop\'s area, over their fields and their bee trees. Some of the Letts were wounded by the Brothers and the quarrel came before the Bishop. The Lord Bishop rose up with the venerable Lord Philip, Bishop of Ratzeburg, and called the Brothers of the Militia and the Livonians and Letts to a hearing so that, by checking the quarrel, he could restore them to their former harmony. They wrangled bitterly for two days and were unable to enter into any peaceful reconciliation among themselves. The Livonians and the Letts thereupon lefļt the Germans and took oaths among themselves... The Bishop promised to restore everything that had been taken unjustly. As for the things that the Brothers had taken, as they justly deserved, on the account of the Livonians\' excesses, the Bishop made no promise to restore them. The Bishop, acting on the advice of prudent men, demanded the Livonians\' boys as hostages, lest they abandon the Christian faith. The Livonians, however, would neither give up the hostages nor obey the Bishop or the Brothers of Militia ... He (Bishop Albert) called together the pilgrims, the master of the Militia and his Brothers, and the Rigans and Livonians who still remained faithful. Taking all the necessary supplies with them, they marched to Treiden and besieged Dobrel\'s fort... The prince and elder of the latter (Livonians) was Vetseke. The Livonians left the fort from the rear and, after wounding some men in the army, they took to their horses and loot arid returned to the fort, saying: "Take heart and fight, Livonians, lest you be slaves to the Germans." They fought and defended themselves for many days... Others (the Germans) put up a tower which the wind knocked to the ground the next night. At this there was a great noise and rejoicing in the fort and the Livonians sacrificed animals, paying honor to their gods according to their old customs ... The tower was put up more strongly, another wooden tower was quickly strengthened, it was pushed across the moat, and the fort was sapped from beiow. From the highest point in the fort, mean- j while, Russin (the Latvian prince and \\ elder Rūsiņš) called Berthold, the master of Wenden (Cēsis), draugs, that is, his fellow. Russin took off his helmet, leaned down from the wall, and uttered words about peace and their former friendship. Suddenly a bolt from a ballista struck his head. He fell and shortly thereafter he died ... The Bishop, in order to persuade them to return to the sacraments of the ****) Henricus Lettus, op. cit., transi, by J.A. Brundage, sub anno 1212, Chapters 3, 4. faith, sent his banner into the fort... The Bishop had pity on them... They asked the Bishop, as Alabrand had instructed them, to confirm them fully in Christ\'s faith and to lay upon them the annual payment of the tithe as satisfaction for their misdeeds ... The Bishop, however, was at last overcome by their importune begging. He agreed to their request and received them as sons. After giving them peace, he confirmed their promise that henceforth they would be faithful and would pay the annual tithes." It is very possibly this very uprising which is shown on the corners of the above described map. The Pope sent to Livonia Bishop William of Modena who reported adversely to the Pope on the oppression of the native population by the Brethren of the Sword (Fratres Militiae Christi). The hopes and plans of the Holy See to improve the situation of Latvians, Livs however, since the actual power of the Popes in Terra Mariana (Livonia) decreased through the centuries, and the Teutonic knights even ignored a ban of the Church (excommunication) directed at them in the next (14th) century. If the authors of the above map have used other sources besides the well-known Chronicle of Henry the Latvian and the Rhyme Chronicle of the 13th century, it is possible that a careful study might reveal new information such as the exact location of some ancient Latvian, Livonian i and Estonian hill-forts. For example, it ( seems that the capital of the Lettish state of Tālava, Beverīna, has not yet been located with any reasonable degree of certainty. *****) A. Schwabe (Švābe), "Les illusions dv Saint-Siege" in Histoire dv people Letton (Stockholm, 1953), pp. 84-94.', 'Too little has been written about the historiography of the Latvians, and what has appeared suffers from pronounced prejudices or inadequate knowledge. The early period of Latvian historiography was entirely dominated by alien historians who represented the interests and power politics of their respective countries. In their presentation of Latvian history the periods of domination of their own countries were strongly emphasized, periods of domination by other powers ignored and/or belittled, and the Latvian nation as such scarcely mentioned. These observers of historical events were neither psychologically nor nationally related to what they observed. The period of national revival of the Latvian nations during the nineteenth century aroused the interest of the people in their native history. During this period the first academic historians of Latvian origin sought to diffuse knowledge both of the outside world and of the past glory of their country in the hope of stimulating national revival. Latvian historiography, in the scholarly sense, could develop only after 1918 when free Latvia came into being. One of the most distinguished Latvian historians and the founder of the Latvian school of historiography was the above-mentioned Arveds Svabe (who also used the spelling "Schwabe"). Born in Lielstraupe on May 25, 1888 Svabe was already well-known in LatHistorian—ARVEDS ŠVĀBE by DR. EDGARS ANDERSONS vian intellectual circles by the age of twenty-five. At fifty, he was widely recognized as a distinguished scientist of broad interests. A historian and jurist by profession, he was a writer and student of folklore by avocation. His knowledge of languages was impressive, his capacity for work enormous and his personality extraordinary. At the time of his death on August 20, 1959, in Stockholm, he had published more than fifty books and approximately a thousand articles and essays. As a student Svabe sought a solid basis in the natural sciences. Unsatisfied, hi then explored the fields of history, law, sociology, and aesthetics. Later he received rigorous training in historical method at the Shanavsky University in Moscow and studied the publications of British, French and Russian writers of folklore and ethnography, considering it necessary to understand the mental characteristics of nations in order to trace their origins and development on the road to statehood. At the same time he gained success as an orator and as a powerful urbanist in poetry. In 1918 Arveds Svabe became a member of the Latvian National Council in the Far East. After returning to Riga, he became an official of the Foreign Ministry and a member of the Constituent Assembly of the new Republic of Latvia. The War had caused Svttbe to revise traditional judgments and direct his attention away from antiquity to the recent past and current events. As a member of the Constituent A«semh!v he was active in educational reform work and in the campaign to erve historical monuments and docmrnN. The Assembly provided for rrpirinn of the State Library and State Archives. Tempted to continue his historical studies, he was faced by a Department of Histcy in the University of Latvia dominated by Baltic German professors, who, being hostile to the new state and the Latvian national movement, were heavily emphasizing classical studies and the auxiliary studies of history without offering a single course in the history of Latvia. There Svabe, choosing the long way around, entered the more liberal Department of Law. But in 1936 he became the head of the Department of History. With the Latvian nation now thoroughly "history conscious," cities and communal centers organized museums, established local archives and enlarged their libraries. The people demanded texts reflecting not only the history of the aristocracy and ruling minorities, but of the nation as a whole. The Baltic German and Baltic Russian historians,, writing from the angle of their respective nationality and class interests, had ignored the vital problems of the national majorities in the Baltic area. The harm that such one-sided history had already done to the Baltic nations was incalculable. Svabe, after receiving his M.A. in jurisprudence, had been appointed to the chair of History of Latvian Law. He had already published A History of Latvia in three volumes (1921-1923) as a text for schools, two volumes of The History of Latvian Culture (1921-22, the third volume remained unpublished) as well as several articles. Realizing that the former overlords and now the enemies of the new republic, entrenched irļ their ideological positions, were still seeking to control the nation\'s history, Svabe was determined to fight them with historical research. To him this was a continuation of the war of liberation, in a different field, with different weapons. The Latvian agrarian reform was causing friction between the landed gentry and the government. for s< "y of i 1 Svabe wrote his Agrarian History of Latvia (published in English, French and German, 1927-1929) to clarify the bacground for the agrarian reform in Latvia. In the fall of 1926 he finished his History of the Rural Community. After 1927 Svabe devoted much energy to editing the Latvian Encyclopedia of which 21 volumes had appeared by 1939. The Ph. D. degree had been conferred on him in 1932, his thesis topic being The Oldest Feudal Code in Latvia. He was then made full professor of the history of law. While the historians of the old school remained entrenched in the Department of History, a new breed of them was growing up in the Departmets of Law and Economics at the University of Riga. In 1936, however, Svabe accepted the chairmanship of the Department of History, and proceeded to improve seminars and reorganize the curriculum. A new and well-trained generation of historians was prepared to replace the old. He also helped to organize the State Institute of History, assisted in the publication of historical journals and source books, attended international congresses and worked in foreign archives. On his fiftieth birthda} his friends and students honored him with a festschrift entitled To the History of the Nation. Young Latvian scholars published monographs based on new and rich archeological and documentary discoveries. Their activities raised a storm of violent, even scandalous protest from the Baltic barons and their friends—the Nazis, who launched their attacks on Latvian historiography. Nonetheless, they won acknowledgment among scholars in the West. \'From 1939 to 1940 Dr. Svabe was Vice President of the Latvian State Institute of History. He promoted the publication of the monumental History of Latvia and the collection of sources, Monumenta līistorica Latviae. World War II interrupted his work. The Russians allowed him to lecture at the University, but accused him of serving the "rotten democracy." When the Nazis took over they kept Svabe out of the lecture halls. From 1941 to 1944 he was nominally President of the Institute of Historical Sources, but was not permitted to use the archives. His only important production was two more volumes of historical sources. In 1944 he was deported to Germany, where he was rescued by the Allies. After the war he remained in exile. Elected President of the Latvian Central Committee in Exile, he helped to relieve the plight of refugees. Finally, Svabe settled in the Swedish capital where, in association with the University of Stockholm, he devoted himself to historical research. He published several books in English, French and Swedish, completed a three-volume special edition of the Latvian Encyclopedia, and finished the first volume of his monumental History of Latvia, 1800— 1950 {Latvijas vesture, 1800—1944). In the course of his life Svabe worked in five different fields: folklore, the history of law, agrarian history, medieval history and, particularly, modern history. His work in all these areas was unified by the historical approach. It was his initial interest in folklore that led him into history. His History of Latvian Culture combines the study of customs and institutions along with that of documents and relics of historical significance. He traced the evolution of Latvian culture from prehistoric times through to the Middle Ages. Svabe spent much time in the medieval period of Latvian History, exploiting many Baltic, Scandinavian and German legends, and striving for objectivity and candor in handling dangerous problems. Yet his work caused unfriendly critics to accuse him of lack of realism. Reference should be made to his essays in Currents and Sources (two volumes, 1939-40). He brought to light much interesting material concerning Scandinavian-Baltic relations, the struggle between the Teutonic order and the Pope in the Baltic area, and the life and institutions of ancient independent Latvian territorial units as well as the policies of their rulers. Svabe\'s Agrarian History of Latvia bears some resemblance to the works of some German historians, but much of the text results from his own findings. Among many essays dealing with more modern problems the most interesting may be his article on Sigismund Augustus\' Livonian policy. Since he was mostly interested in rural development, few of Svabe\'s essays dealt with the development of Baltic cities, and science and technology were neglected. His later works reveal a growing interest and expertise in diplomatic and political history. One may mention his Histoire dv peuple Letton (1953), The Story of Latvia and her Neighbors (1947, 1949), Baltic Autonomy Until 1918 (1954), Genocide in the Baltic Countries (1949), History of Latvia 1800—1914 (in Latvian). He edited and published two volumes of Fontes Historiae Mcdii Aeii (1937, 1940), Quellen zur Wirtschaftsgeschichte Livlands 1553—1618 (1941) Quellen zur Rechtsgeschichte Livlands 1336—1551 (1941) and the somewhat less important Chrestomathy of the Sources of the History of Latvia 1300—1500 (1940). Some of his critics have maintained that Svabe\'s historical outlook was restricted by provincialism and isolationism and that he evinced no interest in the factors of international co-operation. In fact, he projected on a carefully analyzed, well-balanced and skillfully presented international background. However, he was not expert in some of the questions he treated, and he did overestimate legal, social and economic factors while underestimating the political, diplomatic and military ones. He has been accused of a priori concepts and idees mattresses. Yet his idee maitresse was to discover truth in a violently misused and misinterpreted field of history, hitherto appropriated by certain alien elements and their allies, a caste hostile or indifferent to the Baltic nationals. These accusers have been more violent nationalists than Svabe himself. Undeniably, however, a lack of moderation in his attacks on these elements sometimes seriously weakened the effectiveness of his arguments. He himself admitted he could never be either a diplomat or a politician. Svabe established a new school of Latvian historiography based on modern principles. He greatly raised Latvian standards through his own teaching and writing; he discovered and preserved significant new materials. He wove Latvian history into the pattern of World history. His books and articles have been profoundly influential ; and even his Nazi and Communist enemies have been forced to admit, in their own ways, that Svabe has shed new light on the history of Latvia. He admitted, however, that historians should reflect the prevailing views of their time, their class or their nation. As a combined jurist and historian, Svabe. however, was always critical with his sources, sometimes caustically criticizing other scholars for the misreading of documents. In his writings he followed the rules of simplicity, factual presentation and cautious interpretation. He considered the narrative art indispensable to the writer of history, but never at the expense of truth. His philosophy of history is not original, most of its elements being borrowed from certain French, German, Russian, and British historians. He was particularly indebted to Fustel de Coulanges, Kluchevsky and Aulard. However, Svabe\'s practical contributions to Latvian historiography are so great that he must be regarded as the most important Latvian historian up to the present. ABOUT THE AUTHOR: Mr. Edgars Andersons, Ph. D., is professor of History at San Jose State College, California. Dr. Andersons is magazine "ZINTIS" historical section editor. He also is associated with the international magazines Historical Abstracts 1775-1945; The American Historical Review, The Journal of Central European Affairs, and is associate editor to The Guide of Historical Literature (Scandinavian and Baltic historiography section). His latest work is The Courlandians and the West Indies—The First Settlements — Caribbean Quarterly, June 1959. At the present Dr. Andersons is working on a longer study: British Policy Toward the Baltic States 1918-1920. The morning is wiser than the evening. Estonian proverb Don\'t praise a day before the evening, /i woman before the morning. Lithuanian proverb ZINTIS recommends the following Lithuanian magazines in English: LITU ANUS Lithuanian Collegiate Quarterly Published by the Lithuanian Student Associaton, Inc. 916 Willoughsby Aye., Brooklyn 21, N.Y VILTIS (HOPE) A Folklore Magazine Published by V. F. Beliajus, P.O. Box 1226, Denver 1, Col. VYTIS (THE KNIGHT) \' Published monthly by the Knights of Lithuania Editor Dr. J. Leimonas 52 Thomas Park, So., Boston 27, Mass. A Peek at ZINTIS\' Second Issue The Winter 1960 issue of ZINTIS will contain prose works of Zenta Mauriņa, Janis Veselis, Rita Liepa and other writers. There will be an article about the Latvian Dainas and K. Barons, an interview with composer Tālivaldis Kenins, an article about Janis Rainis, one about J. Endzelins, an illustrated folk dance, art work, and a synopsis of diploma work (Gundar King), and other works. The Editor']
