Representative_Docs: ['Swedish newspaper supports Vīķe-Freiberga for EU president Andris Straumanis – November 05, 2009 A leading Swedish newspaper has thrown its support behind former Latvian President Vaira VTķe-Freiberga to become the next president of the European Council, which is the European Union\'s main decision-making body. "Europe needs a strong, respected and unifying figure as a new, permanent president," the newspaper Dagens Nyhetersaid in an editorial published Nov. 5. "Her name Vaira VTķe-Freiberga." VTķe-Freiberga, who served two terms as Latvia\'s sixth president, is one of several candidates—including former British Prime Minister Tony Blair—mentioned for the post. With the Czech Republic\'s ratification on Nov. 4 of the 2007 Treaty of Lisbon, changes to the structure and operation of the European Union are expected to take effect Dec. 1. Among them is the selection of a president of the European Council to serve a 2.5-year term. Currently the presidency rotates among EU member states every six months. Sweden holds the presidency now, while Latvia would have been scheduled to take over in the first half of 2015. Among reasons the newspaper supports VTķe-Freiberga is that it believes the time has come for the European Union to elect a woman as president, as well as to select someone from the EU\'s newest members. Latvia joined the EU in 2004. In its editorial, Dagens Nyheter argued that VTķe-Freiberga would be a good representative for Europe because of her speaking skills, her fluency in five languages and because she "has a strong commitment to Europe." VTķe-Freiberga was born in Latvia in 1937. Her family fled during World War II to Germany and then relocated to Morocco. After settling in Canada, VTķe-Freiberga completed her education and became a professor of psychology. She returned to Latvia in 1998 to head the Latvian Institute and was elected president in 1999. The newspaper acknowledged that her candidacy could be troublesome for some critics because of Vīķe-Freiberga\'s strong ties to the United States, because Russia might object and because some people believe someone who has grown up in Canada should not be president of the European Council. Dagens Nyheter dismissed the arguments. Meanwhile, support for Vīķe-Freiberga\'s candidacy is growing online. One Web site, A Woman to Head Europe, has gathered nearly 2,800 signatures supporting the former Latvian president. "An exceptional character, Ms. Freiberga is a woman of peace, a committed, determined European," according to the Web site. The Web site was registered Oct. 28 by David Juni of Saint Mande, France.', 'THE CHICAGO LATVIAN NEWSLETTER A NEW PRESIDENT FOR LATVIA After four unsuccessful tries, Anatolijs Gorbunovs, the some-time ideological chief of the Latvian Communist Party, but since then a staunch supporter of democracy and the people\'s choice for President, Raimonds Pauls – the well-known composer, Vaira Paegle – an immigrant and some-time President of the World Association of Free Latvians, Jānis Priedkalns – another immigrant, Latvian Envoy to the UN, the Latvian Saeima (Parliament) with a three-party consensus managed to elect Mrs. Vaira Vīķe-Freiberga as the second Latvian President of this independence period (the sixth all together). Mrs. Freiberga moved to Latvia in 1998 from Montreal, Canada in order to assume the Presidency of the Latvian Institute. She has a doctorate in experimental phychology, speaks Latvian, English, German, Spanish and, during her first news conference, stated that she would also learn Russian. She firmly announced that Latvia\'s movement to the European Union and NATO are her first objectives. Russia immediately objected for not being mentioned. Touchy, those Russians, or what? She was inaugurated on July 8, 1999. In her fine speech, she urged the Latvian people to care for each other and to enrich each other\'s lives. That night an inaugural ball was held for 900 at the Rundāle castle (qv.). LATVIA\'S GNP DROPS The Latvian GNP, which was upward-mobile last year during the first quarter of 1999, has dropped by 2.3%, when compared to the first three months of 1998. However, both Latvian and EU authorities are not disturbed by this, and expect the GNP to rise to + 1.5 – 2.0 by the end of the year. THE SEA BORDER TREATY FINALLY SIGNED The Prime ministers of Latvia un Lithuania – Vilis Krištopans and Rolandas Paksas – signed the long overdue agreement on the sea-border. It is a 22,2 km (13.9 mile) broken line boder, that does not concern economics or the possibility of oil on the bottom of the Baltic Sea. FIRST AND ONLY BROADCAST PRESS CONFERENCE The Prime minister Vilis Krištopans (The Latvian Way) staged a news conference on television during prime SEPTEMBER 1999 NEWS FROM LATVIA – reported by Laimons Streips in Rīga time, with his press secretary next to him and then selected media representatives to ask questions. The questions for the most part were vicious attacks on the Krištopans government, and this ascerbic man managed to answer them all with a smile and very gently. THE NEW LANGUAGE LAW IS PASSED The new Language law, which has been debated internally and with the European Union for five years, was passed by the Saeima. Since then the law has been severely criticized by Russian authorities as well as by Russians residing in Latvia, of which many simply do not wish to learn Latvian. Moreover, the European Union has also questioned some of the paragraphs of the law. The new President returned the bill to the Saeima, outlining some paragraphs that have to be clarified. ANDRIS ŠĶĒLE -THE NEW LATI VAN PREMIER On July 11, 1999, the President, Vaira Vīķe-Freiberga, asked that Andris Šķēle, the Chairman of Tautas partija (The Folk party) form the new Latvian Government. In another positive move, the reigning parties – Tautas partija and Tēvzemei un brīvībai (the Folk party and for Fatherland and Freedom) – asked that Latvijas ceļš (the Latvian way) join the government. Šķēle is the sole owner of the largest Latvian food, sweets and liquor producers. He has promised to yield control to another party for the duration of his premiership. Latvian law so far has nothing like a blind trust. As the first act, the new government changed the State budget, eliminating much of income and expense, in order to balance the books more closely. The government also increased excise taxes on alcohol, tobacco, black oil as well as gambling. Since the government coalition has a solid majority in Saeima, it is expected that all these provisions will become law. "GREENS" ATTACK LITHUANIA\'S OFFSHORE PUMPING STATION The Latvian "Greens" delayed the opening of Lithuania\'s offshore oil pumping station at Būtiņģe. They occupied the platform and hancuffed themselves to it, but were later arrested by Lithuanian police. The "Greens" accuse the Latvian government of ignoring the dangers inherent in Būtiņģe. Next day the oil pumping station was duly inaugurated with many speeches, and a tanker was filled, but the Lithuanian police were on the lookout for more protests. UNIVERSAL TESTING METHOD FOR HIGH SHOOL SENIORS Four years ago the Latvia\'s education system instituted a universal testing method for high school seniors. During the four years, media have reported that the tests may be bought at the "Laima" clock (a very common meeting place for all sorts of people) in downtown Rīga for about Ls 20.- (appr. 35.- USD). This year four shools were accused of failing math tests, because 80% of the students answered the same questions wrong. Clearly something is rotten, since the tests were sent to school principals in sealed envelopes. The Public prosecutor\'s office has begun an investigation, and several hundred students, who say that they did not cheat, have begun a class-action suit, and are also complaining to the Citizen\'s Protection Authority. Some 2,500 students are reported to have cheated. SUMMER CAMPS FOR LATVIAN ORPHANS Several hundred orphans will have the opportunity to get some sun and fresh air this summer in camps provided for them. This is part of an effort to get these children off the streets. RUSSIAN POLICY PLAYS HAVOC WITH LATVIA\'S ECONOMY The Russian political attacks against Latvia for misusing the colonists they themselves shlepped into Latvia, and which of course is arrant nonsense, was soon coupled with an embargo against Latvian food imports, especially by the Mayor of Moscow, Yurii Luzhkov. And then the collapse of the Russian economy made it a tripple whammy against Latvia. While there are some Latvian food exporters that have successfully made agreements with some governors of Russian provinces, this is of little help. Heretofore 90% of Latvian food production could be exported to Russia. FISHING INDUSTRY HIT SEVERELY Most severely hit by the embargo is the fishing industry. Many fish canneries have gone out of business, and fish production has dropped to below 50% since 1995. Food producers fault the government for not signing a free trade agreement with Ukraine with whom we have no political problems. The 40 million Ukrainians would certainly make a difference in fish production industry, had a free trade agreement been signed. FOOD PRODUCTS TAINTED Health matters mostly come from the European Union. Chicken, eggs, milk and all sorts of products made from these have come from Belgium, tainted with a class of Chemicals known as the dioxins. At higher concentration, dioxins may be carcenogenic. It appears that Belgian authorities sat on this discovery for a while. Belgian, French and German food products of the kind mentioned were culled from our stores and samples sent to Germany and Finland for dioxin analysis. So far this contaminant has been found in imported beef liver. Right on the heels of the dioxin scare appreared another one, again, originating in Belgium. Coca-Cola drinks made several hundred students ill. The explanations of the company that has manufactured Coke cans for many, many years, were quite unconvincing. Again, Latvia removed Coca-Cola products that were manufactured in France and Belgium from the store shelves, but not those made in Lithuania. What is wrong with those Belgians anyway? EU officials commended the Latvian agencies for their fast and complete work removing the tainted products from the market. AMERICAN MILITARY TO ADVISE LATVIAN DEFENSE FORCES It is reported that Latvian defense authorities have invited a group of American military for the purpose of instructing Latvian defense forces on NATO requirements. The tenure of this group might be from 6 to 12 months, although the Latvian Defense minister Ģirts Valdis Kristovskis has said that the time might be much longer, since not all Latvian officers know what is required of them. LATVIAN WOMAN IN THE AIR FORCE ACADEMY A young Latvian woman, Pfc. Ērika Neimane will begin studies at the US Air Force Academy this fall. SUGAR BEET CROP Latvia is again going to have a bumper crop of sugar beets, but – alas! – 30 thousand tons of sugar of unknown origin have come into the country and are being sold in unmarked plastic bags, which is against the law. This is presently being investigated by the authorities. LATVIAN FARMERS PRODUCE TOO MUCH FOOD FOR LOCAL CONSUMPTION It seems that Latvian farmers produce twice the amount of food that Latvia\'s inhabitants are capable of consuming. Obviously the rest has to be exported, preferably to the vast Russia, quite familiar with Latvian products from years past. However, Russia cannot pay, even though several Russian provinces have entered into agreements with Latvian food producers, which are mutually acceptabe, and by paying in kind. Latvia also exports food to the European Union and other countries, even to the USA, for I myself went to Maier\'s Delicatessen in Chicago and bought Latvian manufactured sprats (šprotes). Latvia\'s excellent beer is also exported to the USA and sold for outlandish prices. Likewise – Latvia\'s champagne. Latvia was the first country to mature this bubbly wine in stainless steel tanks. Several other countries have followed suit. I do not think the French will buy this manufacturing manner. CRAYFISH – LATVIAN AND CAJUN DELICACY It has been reported that in Latvia the crayfish are now going to be grown commercially. A gentleman by the name of Egils Tinte – the Vice-president of the Latvian Crayfish Growers Association – is quite involved in this project. In his ponds he has harvested about 70,000 young crayfish this year. It apparently has begun well, since this Association has been invited to join the World Crayfish Growers Association. SANITARY OFFICIALS CLOSE THE FISH PAVILLION State Sanitary officials descended upon the Fish pavillion of the Great Market halls in Rīga. The air temperature in the pavillion at that taime was 31 °C (88°F). They demand that the temperature be lowered to 10° – 15° C (50° – 59°F) even though to air-condition such a huge edifice may not be an easy proposition. They shut all operations in the pavillion because the freezer temperatures which should have been -15° C (5°F) were found to be on the order of -2,9° C (27°F) – a world of difference for \'The very easily spoilable fish products", as the leader of the State Sanitary group said. MUSIC FESTIVALS IN LATVIA Latvia – like, to be sure are many other lands, – is a land of music festivals. But, since you are reading this, you have some affinity to Latvia, not Edinburgh or Verona. The first is the Ballet Festival, followed by the Rīga Opera Festival in June; a month later is Sigulda Opera Festival, held in the ancient Sigulda castle, which shows the best of Latvian opera singers and the young European singers; Rīga Dome Cathedral Organ festival, as well as the a Chamber music festival in August. In the fall, there is the Baroque festival, which is held in Rastrelli\'s built castle at Rundāle; Jazz festival, Rock festival in the provincial town of Valmiera. You name it, and Latvia probably has it. PĒTERIS VASKS\' 2. SYMPHONY IN LONDON The 2. symphony of the Latvian composer, Pēteris Vasks will be premiered at the Royal Albert Hall in London. This work was commissioned by BBC. It will be broadcast from a recording by the Latvian "Klasika" radio on August 26. SKONTO IN EUROPEAN SOCCER CHAMPIONSHIP The Latvian leading team in soccer started the World Championship first level well, beating Luxemburg\'s Jeunesse Esch 2:0 (0:0) in Luxemburg. The return match was played at the very, very wet Daugava stadium in Riga, and this time Skonto beat the Luxemburgers 8:0 (2:0). Skonto next played Bucarest\'s Rapid and in Bucarest managed a draw – 3:3 (2:1). They won again in Rīga 2:1 (0:1) and then for the third round played against the premier-league team Chelsea of England. Skonto is the first Latvian soccer team to go to a third round. EUROPEAN YOUTH FIELD AND TRACK CHAMPIONSHIP IN RĪGA The European Youth Field and track meet was held in Rīga\'s Daugava Stadium. This is the first time such an event was staged in a former Soviet-block country. AUTO-SPORT TRAGEDY There was a sports related tragedy in the Latvian provincial town of Madona, where two competing cars ran into the crowd, killing five on the spot, two died on their way to the hospital and two are still on the critical list. The blame seems to be with the arrangers of the event. The Latvian Automobile Federation (LAF) has decided not to allow any auto races before all the tracks are inspected and certified. The accident claimed another victim – a 9 year old boy, who never regained consciousness. The two drivers who caused this tragdey left auto sport and one of them is creating a fund to help victims of accidents caused by sporting events. The LAF has barred five of the officials and referees from the sport for life. WWF INTRODUCES WILD HORSES IN LATVIA At a behest of the World Wildlife Fund (WWF) a herd of wild hoses of the genus Konik polski were introduced at Pape in Latvia. For fence building and animal maintenance WWF earmarked $30,000 – this year and again for the next two years. It has been intended to add other animals to the horses, e.g. the blue cow that has stemmed from aurochs that lived in Latvia in earlier centuries. Another species for introduction is the presently living aurochs (in Poland); however, for these animals, that weigh nearly a ton, a large territory and very stout fencing is needed. Latvia is the only European country where alongside grass eaters live predators, such as wolves, foxes and lynx. THE RĪGA CARD IS AVAILABLE Attention all travelers to Rīga! The city now has a Riga Card that features discounts at sundry restaurants, travel on public transportation, including trains, free admittance to museums, e.t.c. The card may be obtained at the airport, Old town, tourism agencies. It will cost Ls 8.- for one day, Ls 12.- for two and Ls 16.-for three days. There are also children\'s cards. THE RĪGA CANAL WILL BE DREDGED The Riga Canal, which meanders so very prettily through the city in its 3,2 km length, is going to be dredged (last time it was done, was in 1932) to a depth of 2 m. Nowadays, the depth in some places is only 70 cm. There are construction debris, car parts and – who knows – perhaps the skeleton of a suicide victim! The city also plans to introduce stabilizing vegetation and to improve the banks as well. LIBERAL PARTY LATVIAN WAY LOSES POWER During the presidential election, the here-to-fore most potent political force, Latvijas ceļš (The Latvian Way) lost much of its power. A three-way consensus by the far-right party Tēvzemei un brīvībai (For Fatherland and Freedom), Tautas partija (Folk Party) and the Social Democratic Workers Party allied themselves to elect a fine President and may play a more formal role in the future. And, indeed, Mr. Krištopans, the Prime minister, resigned on July 5, 1999, accusing his coalition partners of treason. LATVIAN SOCCER TEAM PLAYS BRASIL The Latvian National soccer team was invited to a friendship game against Brasil. The game duly took place in Curitiba. The Brasilian National team won by a score of 3:0 (1:0). According to sports news, this was a demonstration lesson for the very young Latvian team, where some teens were on the team for the first time. The Brasilians, however, considered the Latvian National team as worthy sparring partners and would like to expand relationships. FK VENTSPILS IN THE EUROPEAN INTERTOTO SOCCER MATCHES More successfull was the Latvian soccer team FK Ventspils, which in the European Intertoto matches beat Norwegian Valerenga with a two-game total of 2:1. They next played the Turkish team Kocaelispor to a draw 1:1 (0:0) and lost a game in Turkey 0:2 (0:1). Thus they were eliminated from further games. CHICAGO, ILL: Governor George H. Ryan and Illinois First Lady Lura Lynn Ryan hosted the 1999 Ethnic Media at the James R Thompson Center on July 22. Over 60 ethnic groups attended the reception where Governor Ryan issued a proclamation declaring July 22.1999 as Ethnic Media Day in Illinois. Children representing ethnic communities presented Mrs. Ryan flowers.', 'LATVIA HAS A NEW PRESIDENT On June 17, 1999 (on the same day in 1940 – 59 years earlier – The Soviet Union occupied latvia under a secret amendment of The Ribentropp-Molotov pact) the Latvian parliament – Saeima – elected a new president – Vaira VĪķe-Freiberga. She received 53 votes on the seventh ballot. Memorable is the fact that Vaira Vīķe-Freiberga is the first woman in the Eastern Europe to hold the office of President of the State. She took office on July 8, 1999. The current term is 4 years. She replaces President Guntis Ulmanis who had served his 2 terms allowed by the Constitution of Latvia. On that same day Saeima passed a historic Language Law. Both events have been widely covered in the European and Western media. On July 10, 1999 "Globe and Mail" covered both in a lead editorial Titled "Latvia imports a Canadian President" and subtitled "Can Vaira Vike-Freiberga serve a a role model for language tolerance?" The editorial comment follows: Vaira Vike-Freiberga isn\'t canola or telephone parts, but she still has the potential to be one of Canada\'s most important exports. After living 44 years in this country, the former University oif Montreal psychological professor returned last year to her native latvia. This June, with the country\'s presidential race deadlocked, she was asked if she would stand for the largely ceremonial office. She did, and she won. Now Ms. Vike-Freiberga talks about bringing a Canadian sensibility to a country whose history bears the scars of conquest and colonization. Since 1237, with only few interludes of independence, Germans, Poles, Swedes, Russians, Germans again, and Russians again have ruled the Baltic country. Russian rule after the Second World War was particularly brutal, with 60,000 Latvians deported to Siberia and hundreds of thousands of Russian colonists brought in. Since 1991, Latvia has been free and strugling. The most trying question has been how to amalgamate the 40 per cent of the population who are Russianspeaking with a Latvian speaking majority. The Latvians initial instinct was vengeful. They passed a law proclaiming that only those Russian speakers who themselves, or whose ancestors, had been in the country m 1940 could become citizens. Everyone else was a stateless, non-voting resident. These provisions have since been reversed, but almost simultaneously with Ms. Vike-Freiberga\'s inauguration the latvian Parliament passed a Draconian language law. Among other things, it requires all signs, posters and advertisments to be written only in lavian. It demands that public speeches be only in Latvian. It severely restricts the ability of Russians to conduct business with the government in Russian and prohibits showing films to  children unless the movies a dubbed in latvian. The law is seen as so discriminatory that latvia has been warned by several European countries that its provisions may torpedo its hopes of joining the European Union. The legislation has also produced protests from Russia and Russian-speakers. The latter have marched under ominous placards announcing "A Ruling Dictatorship I>eads to Kosovo." Enter Ms. Vike-Freiberga. She has declared that Russians are welcome members of the state. She says she will enroll in Russian language classes so she can speak with the minority, and as well as show them that even a 61-year-old can learn a new tongue. She has also argued that Russians must learn the "state language" of Latvian. She likens this to people living here learning either English or French. All of this is good and soundly Canadian. But she also says she will never give a speech in Russian while president She has declared that the Russians ar colonizers who did not do the honourable thing and return to their homeland after the end of empire. In this too she sounds Canadian, but, alas, of the Lord Durham-Parti Québécois militant school. Ultimately, she and Latvia are going to have to decide wluch Canada they will be – the place where two language groups make peace with one another or the place where one kind of linguistic colonialism attempts to supplant another. It is a choice which will determine whether Latvia prospers in a region where it will always have to accomodate its huge and nationalistic Russian neighbour. And it is also a choice which will indicate how good this country has been at teaching Vaira Vike-Freiberga the intrinsic worth of tolerance. □ President V. Viķe-Freiberga has invited two-time premier Andris Šķēle, the leader of the People\'s party CTautas partija) to form the Bth gouvernmet of the renewed Latvian State. It will be a majority gouverment supported by the 3 major parties (Tautas partija, Tēvzemei un Brīvībai un Latvijas ceļš) in the Latvian Parliament (with 62 combined members ol 100). U appears that this will happen quickly and will form a political stability for some time to come. It will replace the minority gouvernment of Vilis Kristopâns. who resigned upon learning that one of the junior parties of the coalition was talking to the oposition.']

Name: 57_project_lat_fund_eu
Representative_Docs: ["EU SUITABILITY FOR LATVIAN INSTITUTIONS COSTING 1.2 BLN LATS Latvian institutions will need 1.2 billion lats (SUSI.9 bin) in the next ten years to fully meet European Union (EU) member status, according to calculations made by the Finance Ministry. The Ministry of Finance, by order of the European integration Council, has collected for the first time information on the activities and costs of all 12 government ministries required by Latvia according to the National EU integration program and promises made during EU accession talks. Integration costs this year and in 2002 are expected to be the highest, when the implementation of EU requirements and standards will cost 268 million and 296 million lats respectively. A sharp fall in costs is expected in 2003, which undoubtedly shows the Latvian government's intention of completing as much as possible before the next round of EU expansion in 2004. The largest part of these costs is expected to be covered by the state budget – 646 million lats. Only 168 million lats are expected to be received as financial assistance from the EU and most of this is expected to come from EU structural funds after accession, mainly for environmental projects. 202 million lats are expected to be received as bilateral assistance, including donations and co-financing from other countries. The rest will be obtained from loans. In terms of costs, top of the list is the Ministry of Environmental Protection and Regional Development, planning to spend 800 million lats on putting in order the country's garbage collection industry, ensuring tap-water quality and cleaning of drainage water, as well as the control of air and industrial pollution. Almost half of these funds are expected to be received from the state budget, most of the costs expected to be charged to local governments. Considerable costs will also be required by the Ministry of Welfare for carrying out EU requirements, planning to continue the reform of health care, social security and assistance systems, and to ensure the public with healthy and environmentally friendly food over the next four years. The lowest costs are planned by the Ministry of Transportation, set to increase the control of hazardous cargo and establish a plane-crash incident investigative service. In terms of planned costs at least, the Defence Ministry doesn't seem to be focusing too much attention to the development of the common EU external and security policies, as the ministry plans to spend only 440,000 lats over the next 10 years for establishing a Latvian coastal observation system prepared to link up with similar systems in Estonia and Lithuania. Edited by Vidvuds Koskins", 'EU structural funds finance approved for 5,000 projects in Latvia By the end of last year, 5,000 projects had been approved in Latvia to receive financing from EU Structural Funds. Agreements had been signed for over 4,000 projects. 1,318 projects had been approved to receive European Regional Development Fund (ERDF) financing, 1,160 projects had been approved to receive financing from the European Social Fund, 2,452 projects had been approved for financing from the European Agricultural Guidance and Guarantee Fund (EAGGF), and 333 projects will receive financing from the Financial instrument for Fisheries Guidance (FIFG). 95.6% had been committed from the total Structural Fund financing available to Latvia until the end of 2006, and 32.6% had been paid out. By the end of last year, 46 projects worth EUR 710 million, (the total amount available), had been approved to receive financing from the Cohesion Fund financing available to Latvia.', 'European Commission approves eight Cohesion Fund projects for Latvia The European Commission has approved eight Latvian projects to receive a total of 126 million euros from the European Union Cohesion Fund. The financing from the Cohesion Fund covers 70-85% of the total cost of the projects. The approved projects include development of supply roads to the Ventspils and Liepāja ports, extension of a runway and development of the lighting system at Riga airport, development of the Riga water management system, household waste management in the coastal region of centralwestern Latvia, development of a hazardous waste management system, technical assistance to the Transport Ministry and management of the Cohesion Fund in Latvia. Implementation of the projects is expected to take 2-4 years, with completion expected by 2009. All projects will be implemented with the help of the finance from the Cohesion Fund allocated to Latvia for 2004-2006. The EU Cohesion Fund supports implementation of large transport and environmental projects in Latvia and offers the largest amounts of funding of all EU funds available to Latvia. To date, the European Commission has approved 35 projects to receive a total of 530 million euros from the Cohesion Fund, equivalent to about 75% of the Cohesion Fund finance available to Latvia for 2004-2006.']

Name: 58_aid_defence_defense_gdp
Representative_Docs: ['PRO BALTIC AMENDMENT TO US FOREIGN AID BILL\n\nThe US House of Representatives passed a Foreign Aid Bili on June 20 with an amend ment linking aid to USSR with the situation in the Baltie States. According to the amendment, the US would vvithhold aid pending a USSR pledge to distribute the aid equally among the Baltie States and Soviet Republics. The USSR would also have to return property seized in the Baltie States since January 1. Half of the proposed $3O million in technical aid for 1992 and 1993 would go directly to the Baltie States, and the other half would be sent to democratically elected local USSR governments and other organizations deemed “eligible” for aid.', 'Senate proposes $25 million in foreign aid – House kills it during all-night committee meeting. It was Oct. 26, the last day of negotiations between House and Senate over the 1991 Foreign Aid Bill. It was 5:00 AM, the last hour of a heated all-night session in the Foreign Operations Appropriations conference committee. It was the last chance for the U.S. Congress to approve a Senate recommendation that $15 million in AID grants be provided to the Baltic States. It looked like a lock. Senator Robert Byrd (D-WV) had introduced and fought for the Baltic aid. The entire Senate Foreign Relations committee had backed it. All it needed was approval from the House members of conference committee. The approval never came. The House members rejected the Baltic amendment, and went home to run for re-election. Actually, two Senate amendments for aid to the Baltic States had been offered to the joint House/Senate conference committee. Originally Sen. Alfonse D\'Amato (R-NY) had introduced an amendement calling for $10 million in humanitarian aid for the Baltic States, while Sen. Byrd asked for an additional $15 million in economic assistance. Since there had been no Baltic aid amendments originating from the House side, the D\'Amato amendment was rejected out of hand by conference committeee chairman Cong. David Obey (D-WI). This was initially viewed as a House/Senate compromise, since the Senate had offered two Baltic bills while the House had none. It was thought this would guarantee that at least the $15 million Byrd amendment would go through. Yet even this second proposal was further compromised when some committee members complained that U.S. aid to the Baltic States would appear to support \'secessionist\' movements in the Soviet Union. Thus, an agreement was reached to split the $15 million: $7.5 million would go to the Baltics, and $7.5. to support democracy and privatization in other Soviet republics. This way it would not appear that the Congress was singling out the Baits. Ironically, this compromise was also rejected, because other committee members did not want to give aid to the Soviet Union. Among those who opposed the compromise were Cong. Charlie Wilson (D-TX), a major supporter of the Afghan freedom fighters, Cong. Robert Mrazek (D-NY) and Cong. Matt McHugh (D-NY). CONGRESS REJECTS FOREIGN AID TO BALTICS While all the details of the committee debates and dealings are not known, observers of the comittee proceedings feel that Cong. David Obey directly, and Cong. Lee Hamilton (D-IN) indirectly, worked to prevent the Baltic aid package. Obey sprang the compromise proposal on the committee without forewarning, perhaps knowing that conservatives would reject any amendement that included aid to the Soviet Union. Hamilton, while not a committee member, has a great deal of influence on House Democrats, and reportedly argued against any foreign aid that would appear to destablize Gorbachev and his Soviet government. By combining Baltic and Soviet aid in one package, conservatives and liberals gave a 1-2 knockout punch to Baltic aid. Aid to the Baltics was bad because it would weaken the Soviet Union. Aid to other republics was bad because it would strengthen the Soviet Union. The solution: no aid for anyone. "I HAVE NOTHING FOR YOU MY DEARS"', 'FROM RFE/RL DAILY REPORT $ US SEMTE LINKS 3USSIAN AID TO BALTIC TROOP WITHDRAWAL. On July 1 the US Sen.ite voted 92 to 2 to impose restrictions on aid to Russia after a year unless made significant progress toward withdrawing its troops from the Baltic States, Reuters reports. By a vote of 60 to 35, the Senate defeated a stronger alternative bill that would have imposed the restrictions immediately, without the 12 month grace period. The bill authorized $ 620 million ( $ ISO million for 1992 and $ 470 million for 1993 ) for economic aid to Russia plus $ 18 million in State Department ezpenses and $ 6.8 million for US Information Agency programs. The bill also included $ 400 million aid for 1992 and $ 450 million for 1993 to Eastern Europe nations, including the Baltic States. The Senate also approved authorization of a $ 12 billion contribution to the IMF that will be part of its $ 24 billion aid program to Russia ( Saulius Cirnius ). * LATVIAN, RUSSIAN EXPERTS DISCUSS TROOP WITHDRAWAL. At their third meeting, which began in Jūrmala on June 30, Latvian and Russian experts continued discussions on the withdrawal of ex-USSR troops from Latvia. Radio Riga reported on July 1. Latvia\'s deputy defense minister Dainis Turlajs said Russia was disregarding previous accords; he noted that in the February 1 communique, signed by representatives of both countries, Russia had agreed to discuss the withdrawal of all ex-USSR troops, adding that now Russian experts insist the communique had only mentioned the pullout of some troops. Latvian group member Juris Dobelis said that the Russian side had, however, presented a list of 84 military objects in Latvia that the military could vacate soon and said that this would be compared with a similar list compiled by the Latvians. Dobelis said that Latvia wants to take advantage of any positive steps made by the Russians ( Dzintra Bungs ). § 450 PEOPLE TURNED BACK AT ESTONIAN BORDER. Some 450 people were refused entrance into Estonia at the Narva border with Russia on July 1 because they did not have Estonian visas. BNS reports that four train loads of people wishing to enter Estonia were checked for documents at the Narva rails station. Estonia began requiring visas of former Soviet citizens on July 1. All otherforeigners have been subject to a visa requirement since last October. After Estonia\'s currency reform, the cost of a single-entry visa jumped to $ 60 ( RUna Kionka ). * POLISH-LATVIAN TREATY OF COOPERATION SIGNED. While visiting Riga, Polish Foreign Minister Kryzaztof Skubiaszewski signed a treaty of cooperation between Poland and Latvia – the first such treaty to be signed with one of the Baltic States – and accords on trade, travel and legal protection for citizens of the two countries. Skubiszewski told the press that he was pleased with the development of Latvian-Polish relations. He praised in particular Latvia\'s generous policies toward its many national minorities. While in Riga, the Polish foreign minister also met with representatives of the approximately 60,000 Poles living in Latvia, Radio Riga reported on July 1 ( Dzintra Bungs ). * LATVIAN FILM DIRECTOR JURIS PODNIEKS FOUND DEAD. Radio Riga and Reuters reported on July 1 that earlier that day the body of Juris Podnieks had been found in Zvirgzdu lake, in the vicinity of Kuldiga. Podnieks disappeared on June 23 while participating in a fishing and underwater exploring expedition of Latvian lakes. Although the cause of death was not announced, initial findings suggest that he probably died as a consequence of an accident. Podnieks gained international acclaim with his film "Is It Easy to Be Young?"; the film, a searing indictment of the Soviet system, consists of interviews with young people who speak openly about their disillusionment and their indifference toward the future. In Latvia Podnieks was best known for his documentary films, especially those chronicling the path toward the restoration of Latvia\'s independence ( Dzintra Bungs ). # SWEDEN TO CEOMPENSAIE ESTONIA, LITHUANIA FOR THEIR GOLD. Sweden has decided to compensate Estonia and Lithuania for gold that the Swedish government had transferred to the USSR in 1940, after the Soviet Union occupied the Baltic States. The gold, now worth about 46,6 million dollars, had been deposited in Sweden before World War II by Estonia and Lithuania. The Swedish government statement of July 1 said that the two countries can decide if they want repayment in gold or in cash and indicated that Sweden would like to complete the transactions as soon as possible. Estonian and Lithuanian officials indicated that they would like to have the gold back, Western agencies reported on July 1 ( Dzintra Bungs ). THE MONTREAL BALTIC STAMP CLUB held its Exhibition "BALT-EX-111" on May 23 & 24, 1992 at the Montreal Estonian St. John\'s Lutheran Church hall, Marcil Avenue, NDG. It featured the new stamps of Estonia, Latvia and Lithuania. The visitors judged Dr. R. Sers study of the Latvia\'s Nr. 1 as the best in the show. M. Stauvers\' "Latvian Postal Saga 1990-1992" was a close second ( by one ballot ) and by far the most extensive ( 75 pages ).']
