Name: 60_bank_banka_baltija
Representative_Docs: ["THREE LEADING COMMERCIAL BANKS TO AMALGAMATE On 17 March, the principal shareholders of three leading commercial banks in Latvia-Banka Baltija {Baltic Bank), Latvijas Depozītu banka (Latvia's Deposit Bank), Centra banka (Centre Bank) – made a strategic decision on amalgamation under one name – Banka Baltija. This agreement in principle wilt be put before ail the shareholders of the respective banks in the near future. All three banks are in Latvia's top ten, and Banka Baltija is in fact the biggest financial Institution with statute capital of 26.6 million lats. After amalgamation, the statute capital would increase 1.5 times.", 'Baltic Banking Crises Rumble On; Lithuania, Lavents Dominate News Banking instability finally hit the third Baltic state, Lithuania. After suffering the closure of several smaller banks and beginning to feel like the worst may be over, the Lithuanian central bank suspended operations at Lithuania\'s two largest commercial banks, Innovation Bank and Litimpex. The two together hold one quarter of the country\'s total bank deposits, according to an article in the latest issue of The Economist (96.20.1). The article linked the banking crises that have swept each of the Baltic states now since 1992 with a series of causes, ranging from inexperience on the part of new bankers, overly lax regulation (especially in Latvia), and the fact that many of the new commercial banks simply lived beyond their means. Many banks were set up as a mechanism to provide cheap insider financing for large companies; others offered unsustainably high interest rates in an effort to lure new depositors. Some, like Latvia\'s Banka Baltija and its boss, Aleksandrs Lavents, did both – fostering the collapse of about 40% of Latvia\'s banking industry last summer. The Economist does not paint an absolutely bleak picture of banking Baltics style: the newspaper cites Estonia, the first to sustain a banking collapse in 1992, with reforms so successful that banks there "are now arguably the soundest of any ex-communist country." Latvia and, belatedly, Lithuania are hurrying to join their northern neighbor in toughening bank capital requirements and other banking regulations, while banks in each country engage in badly needed consolidation. Baltija Failure Lingers Speculation and cheap political theatrics continue, however, to dog the worst offender in Latvia\'s banking collapse, Banka Baltija. Months after the Bank of Latvia and the government of former Prime Minister Māris Gailis took over the failed bank, jailed Oversight Council Chairman and factual bank boss Aleksandrs Lavents continues to find politicians willing to make accusations that the man who single-handedly wiped out the savings of tens of thousands of Latvian citizens is being held behind bars simply because he is a Jew (Siegerist Party leader Odisejs Kostanda). Popular Harmony Party member Mavriks Vulfsons echoed this accusation, and presented a Lavents-offered plan to bring Baltija out of bankruptcy that would only serve, according to the Bank of Latvia, to further increase the failed bank\'s debt load while cheating depositors out of any chance to receive any of their funds. The Bank of Latvia, in a statement released January 22., called on Lavents instead to return the nearly 100 million dollars currently estimated to have been transferred from Baltija accounts to his personal holding company, in order to reimburse creditors and depositors. The Bank feels that the bottom line of the various plans offered by Lavents and his supporters to bring Baltija out of deserved bankruptcy are nothing more than ploys to get Lavents out of jail. On a related matter, the new government of Prime Minister Andris šķēle has decided to forgo continuation of compensation payments to Baltija depositors until additional funds are recovered by the bank\'s appointed administrator, Uģis Grūbe. In fact, because state budget funds were used to begin the payments (just before the October Saeima elections), accusations are now flying that former Prime Minister Gailis used the payments as a political ploy to regain voter confidence, and votes, for his party, Latvia\'s Way. The net result: 17 seats in the 6th Saeima for Latvia\'s Way and one million lats of borrowing from the state budget to compensate 6,000 Baltija depositors. The banking situation may be stabilizing, but the revelations are only beginning. Stay tuned. #', 'CHICAGO LATVIAN NEWSLETTER Was Banka Baltija Blown Up With Skrunda "Monster"? May A, 1995 was a day for celebration in Latvia, with the spectacular demolition on Latvia\'s fifth anniversary of renewed independence of one of the most infamous symbols of the Soviet military occupation, the unfinished long range passive array radar station at Skrunda. However, recent reports in Moscow and Latvia have led to speculation that the final destruction of the Skrunda "monster", as the radar was "affectionately" known, may have played an indirect role in the downfall of Latvia\'s largest commercial bank, Banka Baltija. Banking Big, Banka Baltija Biggest Latvia\'s economic rebirth has been largely based, so far, on phenomenal growth in the financial services sector. As part of its drive for independence, Latvia claimed the role of the former Soviet Union\'s banking center—rivaling, in ambition at least, the bankers of Switzerland. More than sixty banks opened throughout Latvia, each competing with the others for deposits, customers and a share of the huge amounts of capital flowing out of Russia. Several have either been declared bankrupt, or come perilously close to bankruptcy, over the past half-year—among them Tautas Banka, Centra Banka, Latvijas Depozītu Banka and, most spectacularly, Lainbanka, which barely survived a run on its deposits following the arrest of senior bank officials on fraud charges that were later dropped. However, Latvia\'s biggest bank was, perhaps, also its most vulnerable—Banka Baltija. Built by Chairman of the Board Aleksandrs Lavents and President Tālis Freimanis, Banka Baltija was the largest commercial bank in Latvia, with 37 branches throughout the country. Banka Baltija controlled assets in excess of 2\\l million lats—nearly three times the assets of the next bank, the Latvian Universal Bank. As of April 1, more than 196 million lats were on deposit. More than 500,000 residents—20 percent of the total population of Latvia— had an account at Banka Baltija, drawn by the extremely high interest rates on deposits offered by the bank. Depositors included such prominent politicians as Prime Minister Māris Gailis, former Finance Minister Andris Piebalgs and Saeima chairman Anatolijs Gorbunovs, who had "hoped to get my 50 percent on May 20." In order to support the high interest payouts, Banka Baltija aggressively pursued loan business, managing just less than one third of the total volume of loans distributed by all of Latvia\'s commercial banks combined. Banka Baltija took on a number of risky, but lucrative loans, which often caused borrowers to default and become dependent on the bank for their survival. State stock companies such as the electric monopoly Latvenergo, the natural gas monopoly Latvijas gāze, Latvian Railways, July/August 1995 — Vol. XV, No. 6 Latvian Airlines and the Dautex chemical fiber plant in Daugavpils, Riga Central Market owner Turība, and the popular newspaper Labrīt have all suffered as a result of loan defaults to Banka Baltija, By this spring, rumors were circulating in the halls of the Saeima and government about cash flow problems at Baltija—but no one was prepared for the implosion of the banking colossus. Baltija Collapses, Officials Gut Bank The first visible signs of trouble at Baltija began to appear in mid-May. Following the murder of a minor stockholder in the bank, Moisejs Gurēvičs, bank officials began to make engage in an unusual series of interviews and public relations efforts. On May 15, Diena columnist Aivars Ozoliņš speculated as to whether the interviews were an indication of the involvement of Baltija officials in Gurēvičs\' murder, and noted an unconfirmed report in the Russian-language paper SM-Segodnja that Bank of Latvia President Einārs Repše and then-Finance Minister Andris Piebalgs had been asked by Baltija officials for an 80 million lats in financial aid. The request quickly became, by May 20, a deal in which the Latvian government would bail out the bank in return for 51% of Banka Baltija stock and total control of all bank operations. Latvian American Uldis Klauss, a consultant with the Bank of Latvia, was appointed the bank\'s new chief executive. On May 22, having spent the weekend examining the bank\'s financial reports, Klauss shut down the bank, and Repše suspended all banking operations. By May 23, the government bailout had become a full-scale takeover, in order to give the government unquestioned authority to restructure Baltija. The next week were spent exposing illegal transactions by bank officials, which emptied the bank\'s coffers before the bank was taken over by the Latvian government. Lavents and Freimanis became the subjects of an investigation into the malicious destruction of their business on June 1, based on the sale of 80 million lats of Baltija loans to a small bank in Moscow in exchange for Russian state treasury bills with a current market value of only 24 million lats. Other deals were completed either just before or after the government agreed to bail our Baltija: the bank mortgaged all of its physical, real and intellectual property as a guarantee to secure nearly 45 million lats in long-term deposits from the state shipping monopoly Latvian Shipping Company. Loans worth 6.3 million lats were sold to Pareks banka, while another 5 9 million lats in loans were sold to the Riga Oil And Chemical Bank. By June 5, it was clear that losses at Banka Baltija were at least in the range of 115-196 million lats. Klauss termed the situation at the bank "a pit that keeps growing deeper and deeper". Repše called the actions of the Banka Baltija officials deliberate sabotage for which, if convicted in the United States, Lavents and Freimanis "would never get out of prison... They openly lied about the bank\'s shortterm problems while, at the same time, signing agreements which emptied the bank. They managed to deceive regulatory agencies and international audit companies. It is villainy and sabotage against the Latvian state." Was Russia Involved? Several sources point to the involvement in the Banka Baltija crisis of "not just the first/second person at Baltija [Lavents and Freimanis—MJZ) but also of third, fourth and even fifth parties." The daily Monitor, published by The Jamestown Foundation, reported on May 30 that massive withdrawals of Russian funds from Latvia led to the collapse of the Baltija Bank. According to a report on May 23 in the Moscow paper Delovoy mir, Russian depositors were angry about Latvia\'s destruction of part of the Skrunda radar site and what it said were Riga\'s anti-Russian policies. The fact that the funds were drawn out all at once suggests that there might have been some coordination by the Russian government itself. When asked about the possibility of a "Russian Scenario", several commentators in Latvia were skeptical. However, high-level officials within the Bank of Latvia are well aware of how real such a scenario is, in light of Russia\'s oft-expressed willingness to use economic means to destabilize the Baltic countries. "It looks like Baltija has been robbed bare. Literally... The total emptying [of bank assets) took place just recently. Estimated losses are nearing 200 million lats. It is not possible that something like this could be done by accident or stupidity. Either a well organized mafia is involved, or the Russian secret services (if they aren\'t one and the same). Everything has been carried out in a cold-blooded and thorough manner..." The unnamed high-ranking Bank of Latvia official stated that the fate of Lavents and Freimanis will serve as a litmus test for the involvement of other parties—"lf we are able to arrest and convict them, then it is very possible that they are simply criminals. If they flee, are vindicated or are removed\', then it is likely that [securityl services are involved." The official agreed that "there is very good reason" to suspect the involvement of Moscow in the downfall of Banka Baltija. The Latvian government has set June 26 as its date for developing a plan to halt the crisis that exploded in its financial system in the wake of the Banka Baltija collapse. Bankruptcy proceedings have begun against the bank in Latvian Commercial Court. In the meantime, the Baltija crisis has brought local government activities to a standstill, threatened pension payouts and caused the respected Financial Times to comment that the "Latvian Zurich" has now become a banking Las Vegas. The government is trying to restore normalcy to the once-thriving Latvian financial sector. But the question remains—is Latvia\'s economic security buried in the rubble of the Skrunda early warning radar station? *MJZ']

Name: 61_omon_interior_beret_riga
Representative_Docs: ['BIŠERS AND VAZNIS MEET WITH USSR INTERIOR MINISTER PUGO RIGA, LATVIA. Ilmārs Bisers, First Deputy Prime Minister, and Aloizs Vaznis, Interior Minister, from the Republic of Latvia, held a press conference on January 22 to discuss their January 21 meeting with USSR Interior Minister, Boris Pugo, in Moscow. They described four main points of a verbal agreement which they reached with Pugo regarding the omon Commando Unit ("Black Berets") located in Riga which attacked the Latvian Interior Ministry building on January 20, resulting in 4 dead and 10injured. This verbal agreement consists of the following points: 1. Omon must be removed from all of its guard posts around the buildings they have been "protecting" (such as the Press House, the Latvian SSR Prosecutor Building), must leave the city and return to their bases. 2. Omon must return all weapons they have seized during their various raids (especially their attack on the Minsk Militia School in Riga). 3. A special investigation group must be formed to investigate the criminal charges filed against Omon and other legal violations by Omon. This group to consist of representatives from the USSR General Prosecutor, the Republic of Latvia General Prosecutor and from a neutral Republic; the Latvian SSR Prosecutor\'s Office (appointed by Moscow) cannot be included due to its involvement in the Interior Ministry attack of January 20 (Omon soldiers shooting from the Latvian SSR Prosecutor building). 4. All guilty parties must be brought to justice and Omon units in Latvia must be disbanded or removed from Latvia. Pugo also swore that he had not give any order or directions to the Omon in Latvia to take the actions at the Press House, the Minsk Militia School, Interior Ministry attack, and other acts of violence.', 'CHICAGO LATVIAN NEWSLETTER January 20, 1991 – As we go to press, over a dozen people have died in Lithuania, barricades are up in Riga, and all three Baltic States are bracing for an imminent assault on their people and governments by the Soviet armed forces. With new developments every day and every hour, it is difficult to prepare a newsletter that will only be read weeks later. The following issue will try to provide some background and additional information about events in Latvia during the first part of January. Late last year, Latvia\'s Foreign Minister Janis Jurkans predicted that 1991 would be the year of the Baltic States. In spite of the tragic events that have already occurred in the Baltic States, or perhaps because of them, it can only be hoped that 1991 will also be the last year of the Soviet Union... A new year, new fears The dress for rehearsal for Lithuania\'s Bloody Sunday (Jan. 13) took place in Latvia on January 2, when Black Berets used force to seize the Latvian Press House in Riga. While there were no fatalities, force FEBRUARY 1991 Vol. XI No. 2 BLOODSHED IN THE BALTICS was used and some shots were fired. The shooting occured on Vansu Bridge, and was directed at a vehicle belonging to the Latvian Interior Ministry. On January 7, Soviet Defense Minister Yazov announced that paratroopers would be deployed to the Baltic States and other repubics, in order to round up individuals that hadn\'t reported for the draft. After discussions with the Latvian and Estonian governments, the deadline for the paratroopers was set for January 13. Troops begin arriving in Lithuania immediately. On January 10 Soviet President Mikhail Gorbachev gave an ultimatum to the government of Lithuania, demanding that it repeal all "anti-Soviet" laws and abide by the Soviet constitution. An estimated 10,000 anti-independence demonstrators rallied in Riga. On the following day, Soviet troops moved into Vilnius. The first gunshots were fired, the first buildings were seized, the first victims fell. In the early morning of January 13, the assault of the Lithuanian TV tower in Vilnius began. As the casualty count in Lithuania rose, Latvia\'s Vice President Dainis Ivans went on national TV with an appeal to the populace to come to Riga to defend the Latvian government. On Janury 13, over 700,000 people gathered on the banks of the Daugava River in Riga, in a demonstration of support for the Latvian Government. No incidents were reported and no paratroopers were seen. By Monday, hundreds of trucks, buses, snow plows and other vehicles had been brought to Riga from around the country in order to protect the government buildings. Tens of thousands of Latvians took up a 24-hour vigil on the barricades, prepared to resist any assault. On Tuesday a coalition of Communist Party members, Soviet military personnel and other Moscow loyalists – held another anti-government rally in Riga. This time, only 8,000 participated. Later than day, Soviet troops attacked and seized the Latvian Military Academy in Riga, seizing weapons and beating some of the students. On January 16 the first Latvian casualty was confirmed. Roberts Mūrnieks, a driver for the Latvian Ministry of Transportation, was shot in the back of the head and killed by Black Berets. That same day, Andrejs Krastins, Vice President of the Supreme Council of the Republic of Latvia stated, "The Latvian Government wants the world to known that Gorbachev is personally responsible for orchestrating this manipulated chaos." An appeal for help On January 14, the government of the Republic of Latvia issued the following appeal: "The events in the Baltic States are entering a tragic and decisive stage. Ignoring all legal and moral rules and norms, the reactionary circles of the Communist Party of the USSR and Soviet Armed Forces are preparing a murderous coup d\'etat and liquidation of existing constitutional systems of the Baltic States. From the information available to the Government of the Republic of Latvia, it follows that the coup in the Baltic States can take place in the nearest 24 to 48 hours. Armed troops under the central administration of the USSR have started to disarm the militia of the Republic of Latvia, thus making the government of the Republic unable to maintain order and exercise control over gangs of hooligans which are being formed by the adversaries of the legal government. Irrespective of the fact that support for the Government of Latvia was expressed by over one fourth of the population who took part in the manifestation on January 13 in Riga, and in written form, by over one million people of various nationalities, the aggressive forces of the Communist Party are ready to restore totalitarian dictatorship at any cost. The actions to be taken are bound to produce violence, even bloodshed, a new enslavement of the nation. The threat of danger is corroborated by the present policy of the leaders of the USSR. Besides, the bulk of mass media in the USSR spread deliberate niisinformation about processes taking place in the Baltic States, thus deluding society in the USSR and in the world. At this severe hour we are addressing all the national and governments of the world with an appeal not to allow another Kuwait to happen, with an appeal to prevent murderous international crime in the Baltics. We ask all the governments of the world who praise values common to all mankind, who praise ideas of humanism and democracy to oppose unanimously the reactionary forces and support the rights of Latvia, Lithuania and Estonia to survival and restoration of their statehood in a democratic way." The Government of the Republic of Latvia, January 14, 1991. How the world reacted If Gorbachev had calculated that the world\'s preoccupation with the Gulf crisis would give him a free hand in the Baltic States, he was greatly mistaken. While no concrete action was immediately taken to halt the attacks on the Baltic governments, there was no shortage of condemnations and threats to cut off economic assistance. On January 11, the European Economic Community (EC) expressed its first concerns and urged Gorbachev to begin negotiations with the Baltic States. NATO held an emergency meeting and issued a similar expression of deep concern. The United Kingdom\'s Foreign Office summoned the Soviet Charge d\'Affaires and told him there was no justification for the Soviet action. Poland\'s government and populace expressed strong support for neighbouring Lithuania. The Polish parliament denounced the Soviet action and large anti-Soviet demonstrations were held in Warsaw and Krakow. Denmark issued a stern warning to the USSR and the Canadian Government expressed strong opposition to the use of military in the Baltic States. Prime Minister Mulroney expressed "the abhorrence of all Canadians at the unwarranted violence used against the people and democratically elected Government of Lithuania. Sweden backed its words of concern with a concrete expression of support for the Baltic States when it allocated 1 million Kroner to cover the expenses of Baltic government officials visiting Stockholm. This money paid for a trip to the United States by Latvian and Lithuanian vice presidents, Dainis Ivans and Bronius Kuzmickas. The United States weighs in The United States also made its feelings known in no uncertain terms. Sen. Bradley (D-NY) introduced, and the Senate unanimously passed, a Sense of the Senate Resolution in which it called upon President Gorbachev "to refrain from further use of coercive tactics against the democratically elected governments of Lithuania, Latvia and Estonia. Such coercive tactics are unacceptable among the community of democratic nations and especially so at a time when the world, including the Soviet Union itself, is united in opposition to the forcible annexation of another small nation, Kuwait, by its brutal neighbour, Iraq." It should be noted that the Senate interrupted its debate on Iraq at 2AM in order to pass the resolution. On January 13, both President Bush and Secretary Baker made statements on the Baltic situation. "I have been following the situation in Lithuania and the Baltic States closely. The turn of events there is deeply disturbing. There is no justification for the use of force against peaceful and democratically elected governments. And the brave people and the leaders of the Baltic States have, indeed, acted with dignity and restraint." said President Bush, adding, "We condemn these acts which could not help but effect our relationship." The President came close to what could be considered recognition of the Baltic States when he said, "I urge the Soviet Government to return to a peaceful course of negotiations and dialogue with the legitimate governments of the Baltic States." Speaking from Turkey, where he was preparing for the allied action against Iraq, Secretary of State James Baker said that he was "deeply disturbed and saddened by these developments", reaffirmed that "the United States has never recognized the forcible incorporation of the Baltic States into the Soviet Union" and offered this warning to Moscow: "Only with continued reform will progress and hope for the Soviet Union be possible and in the end, enduring U.S.-Soviet cooperation, indeed partnership, depends upon continued reform -- for partnership is impossible in the absence of shared values." To monitor the rapidly developing events, the State Department set up a special Baltic Working Group, which was manned around the clock and headed by Paul Goble, the State Department\'s Desk Officer for Estonia, Latvia and Lithuania. Goble, who put in an average of 18-20 hour work days, worked closely with the American Latvian Association, Joint Baltic American National Committee, Lithuanian organizations and the Lithuanian, Latvian and Estonian Legations. On Monday, Jan. 14, the Charges d\'Affaires of Estonia, Latvia and Lithuania met with numerous State Department officials, including Ray Seitz, Assistant Secretary for European and Canadian Affairs. The empire acts up There was also a strong response from within the Soviet Union as well. Demonstrations in support of Lithuania were held in Moscow, Leningrad and Kiev. Independent trade unions and coal miners called for a strike. Many Soviet deputies, including Moscow\'s Deputy Mayor condemned Gorbachev\'s action. The most forceful and significant show of support came from Boris Yeltsin, President of the Russian Republic. The day after the first assault on Lithuania, Yeltsin went to Tallinn, Estonia and signed a mutual cooperation treaty with the leaders of Lithuania, Latvia and Estonia. Then, Yeltsin went even further and issued a statement to all soldiers of the Russian Republic based in the Baltic States, urging them not to use their weapons against unarmed civilians. "Before attacking civilian targets in the Baltic States, remember your home – think about your republic\'s and people\'s present and future. Violence – against the rule of law, against the Baltic nations – will likewise bring about new serious crisis in Russia, as well as crises for Russians living in other republics." Yeltsin warned the soldiers not to become "pawns in the hands of dark reactionary forces," and added, "We believe in you, the officers and soldiers of Russia, who cherish high moral values, honesty, bravery, boldness, idealism and patriotism, as did the previous generation of the Russian military. We turn to our experience of history which tells us that a misstep today may affect not only those who take the step, but also forthcoming generations." Yeltsin\'s appeal had its affect. A Soviet tank division based in Vitebsk, Bieolrussia had been deployed to the Latvian city of Dobele when the crisis began. But when they were ordered to move on Riga, they refused. There are reports that many tore up their Communist Party cards and that their commander defected. There is also a report that one of the victims in Vilnius was a Soviet soldier, who was shot to death by the Soviet troops when he refused to participate in the massacre and stood in front of a Soviet tank. And in Riga, eyewitnesses report that many Russians came to barricades that had been set up around the Latvian government buildings and offered food and drink to the defenders. It is also worth noting that on January 13, the day after the assault on Vilnius had begun, over 700,000 people rallied in Riga in support of the Latvian government. Latvia has a population of 2.5 million. Of those, 1.5 million are ethnic Latvians. On January 15, the National Salvation Committee – a coalition of Communists, Soviet army officers and other Moscow loyalists – held a rally to call for the resignation of the Latvian government. They claimed to be defending the interests of Latvia\'s non-Latvian nationalities. Of the 1.1 million non-Latvians they were supposedly defending, only 8,000 showed up. Riga alone has over 600,000 non-Latvian residents who could have simply walked or hopped on a bus to the rally. The figures speak for themselves. The pen, the fax and telex are mightier When the Soviets seized the Latvian Press House they moved against one of Latvia\'s most powerful weapons in the war for independence. The Press House, located across the Daugava River from downtown Riga, houses all of Latvia\'s main newspapers and printing facilities. The building had been illegally controlled by the Communist Party for many year. When the new Latvian government was formed it passed a decree which made this facility the property of the Republic of Latvia. The Press House was converted to a stockholding company and was made available to all publications, including the Communist press. Communist Party Boss Alfreds Rubiks vowed to retake the building, and on January 2 carried out his threat. Nevertheless, the Latvian press continues to operate from alternative facilities. It has also played a critical role in providing information to the west during the January crisis. Inexplicably, telephone and telex lines to the west remained open, and Latvian journalists as well as the government were able to provide almost hourly reports on events in the country to Western journalists in and outside of Latvia. This information flow was coordinated with Latvian organizations in Europe, the American Latvian Association (ALA) and the World Federation of Free Latvians (WFFL) in Rockville, MD, and the Legation of Latvia in Washington, D.C. The ALA and WFFL maintained a 24-hour watch from their offices and were able to convey information almost instantaneously to the State Dept., Congress and press. When Roberts Mūrnieks was pronounced dead in a Riga hospital, the Legation of Latvia had a press release out to the wire services 15 minutes later.', 'CHICAGO LATVIAN NEWSLETTER i .1 J MARCH 1991 Vol. XI No. 3 ill On the night of January 20, Soviet Interior Ministry troops, known as the Black Berets\', attacked the Latvian Interior Ministry in Riga. They sprayed the building with gunfire, then occupied it, and after several hours, left. When they were through, four people lay dead and several were injured. Among the dead were Latvian film maker Andris Slapins, and two members of the Latvian Interior Ministry – both ethnic Russians. Several weeks later, Gvido Zvaigzne, another Latvian cameraman struck by the gimfire, died from his injuries. At the time of the attack, authorities in Riga were convinced that this was the beginning of Moscow\'s attempt to seize and topple the Latvian Government. Black Berets had been terrorizing Riga for several days, firing on buildings and assaulting rivilians. Although one Latvian had been killed by the Black Berets earlier, the attack on the Interior Ministry on January 20 was the first full scale assault on a Latvian government institution. The attack started and stopped within a matter of hours. Why did the Black Berets attack the building, only to leave it shortly thereafter? According to the Black Berets, they had no intention of seizing the building that night. They had gone to there, they say, to discuss the alleged rape of a Black Beret\'s wife. Upon arriving at the building they were fired upon, and assuming that the fire came from Latvian Militia, attacked the building in self defense. In a further investigation of the incident, it was confirmed that the Black Berets had indeed come under fire, but not from the Interior Ministry. Sources within the Latvian prosecutor\'s office claim that KGB sharpshooters located at nearby Bastejkalns and on several neighboring rooftops, had opened fire on the Black Berets. It is believed that KGB bullets were the ones that killed the Latvian film makers. WHO DID THE KILLING IN RIGA? Although there is no confirmation of these reports, some in Latvia believe that the KGB did indeed instigate the incident – firing upon the Black Berets and the bystanders – in order to provoke the gunfight. Their purpose was to give the impression that Latvia\'s militia was attacking the Black Berets. There is also evidence that the Black Berets may have been part of the charade. Peter Maass of the Washington Post reported on Feb. 18, that a Black Beret machine gun nest had been set up across from the Interior Ministry, and that a flare was fired just before the attack, presumably as a signal to begin. What is known, is that the Latvian militia did not provoke the attack, and that sniper fire from nearby locations did occur. Whether it was the KGB, Black Berets or someone else who pulled the triggers, the responsibility for the attacks lies with Moscow. A blessing in disguise? by Qjars Kalnins Most people view the January bloodshed in Lithuania and Latvia as a failed attempt by Moscow to topple the democratic governments of those two countries. According to such hardline defenders of the Soviet empire as Col. Viktors Alksnis, Mikhail Gorbachev is fully responsible for the failure: he had promised to depose the governments, but lost his nerve when resistance proved greater than expected. For people in Lithuania and Latvia, these events were tragic. At least 21 people lost their lives, and scores were injured. But it is becoming clear that those 21 did not die in vain. Their deaths have strengthened the Baltic independence movement, in a way that was unimaginable last year. At the end of 1990, while the rest of the world was pre-occupied with the crisis in Kuwait, the Baltic governments were pre-occupied with their own economic and political struggles. Still committed to independence, the Baltic governments were stiffering a crisis of confidence, as their restive populaces began grumbling about economic problems and the stalemate with Moscow. Few, if any Western reporters or government officials were paying much attention to the Baltic States. The January crackdown changed all that. When the tanks rolled into Vilnius, the Western press followed. As the death count rose in Riga, so did the anger of the world community. The Baltic people rallied behind their governments and took positions behind the barricades. In a matter of days, despite the outbreak of war in the Persian Gulf, the Baltic States were back on the front pages of newspapers around the world. If Moscow thought it could rely on the Gulf War to distract the world from its attack on the Baltics, it was dead wrong. Columnist William Satire said it all when he wrote, The free-world media will not treat the story as secondary news in light of the gulf crisis; we are able to cover two wars and chew gum at the same time." With hundreds of print and electronic media reporters in Vilnius and Riga, every shot fired against the Baltic people was heard and seen around the world. And every foreign government which once had expressed nominal symbolic support for Baltic independence, was looking for ways to express its outrage in the strongest possible terms. President Bush condemned the Soviet actions. The Canadian Government and European Economic Community suspended aid to Moscow. Sweden allocated funds to support the activities of Baltic government officials in the West. Iceland took steps to recognize Lithuania and establish diplomatic ties with all three Baltic governments. Delegation after delegation, from the US Congress, European Council, Eastern Europe and the Soviet-ruled republics, began arriving in a steady stream in the Baltic States. For the first time ever, world government leaders began insisting that the status of the Baltic States was an international issue which had to be settled on an international level. Cautious, pragmatic statesmen, who had sought to walk a fine line between their support for the Baits and their friendship with Gorbachev, began, for the first time, using the T word: independence. During the first two years of the Bush Administration, no Administration official had ever openly supported Baltic "independence." But on February 10, Secretary of State Baker told a nationwide TV audience, "WeVe made it very clear that we want to see the aspirations of the Baltic people for independence fulfilled." By cracking down in the Baltics, the Kremlin thought it could crush Baltic independence once and for all. It accomplished the exact opposite. William Safire, whose writing on the Baltic States has been consistently brilliant, wrote, "The terrible truth is that fre  is most often won on the barricades, after sacrifice, when real soldiers come to the rescue of countrymen being savaged by dictator\'s killers, and when outsiders are shamed into diplomatic support." Safire then added this quote from Thomas Jefferson: "The tree of liberty must be refreshed from time to time with the blood of patriots and tyrants." Mikhail Gorbachev could have paved the way to Baltic independence through peaceful negotiations. He chose to drench the streets in blood. Those who made the ultimate sacrifice in the streets of Vilnius and Riga have made the tree of liberty stronger than ever. U.S. Congressional delegation in Riga On February 12, the largest U.S. Congressional delegation to ever visit the Baltic States arrived in Riga, Latvia. A dozen members of the U.S. Congressional Commission on Security and Cooperation in Europe (CSCE), headed by Chairman Steny Hoyer (D-MD) met with Latvian legislators and political leaders, and then headed for similar meetings in Vilnius and Tallinn. The visit was prompted in part by a request made in Washington two weeks earlier by Dainis Ivans and Bronius Kuzmickas, Vice Presidents of Latvia and Lithuania. The two had testified before the CSCE and met with Secretary of State Baker. Both Latvian and U.S. officials viewed the visit as meaningful and productive. The CSCE members also met with Alfred Rubiks, Ist Secretary of the Communist Party in Latvia. Four hours after the meeting, a bomb exploded in the Communist Party headquarters where the U.S. legislators had spoken to Rubiks. Latvian authorities said it was similar to bombs placed in other locations in Riga, and attributed it to anti-independence forces in Latvia. Latvia\'s shuttle diplomacy During the height of the crisis in Latvia in January, a number of Latvian government officials headed to Western Europe and the United States to solicit support for the beseiged country. Latvia\'s Vice President Dainis Ivans spent a busy week in Washington, meeting with Secretary of State Baker, members of the National Security Council and numerous U.S. Senators, including Senators Kennedy (D-MA), Biden (D-DE), Bradley (D-NJ), Warner (D-VA) and Wallop (R-WY). He also appeared on NBC\'s Today Show, the McNeil-Lehrer Newshour, and National Public Radio. Ivans\' visit was also featured in the PBS-TV program "Frontline", hosted by Hedrick Smith. The program, entitled, "Guns, Tanks and Gorbachev" was broadcast nationally on Feb. 19. Ivans also made a one-day trip to Ottawa, Canada where he met with Foreign Minister Joe Clark. While Ivans was making the rounds in the Northern Hemisphere, Latvia\'s Foreign Minister Janis Jurkans was covering the European Continent and Great Britain. He met with British Prime Minisler Major, Germany\'s Foreign Minister Genscher, France\'s President Mitterand and with the foreign ministers and political leaders of Sweden, Denmark, Holland and Belgium. Jurkans\' numerous visits received extensive TV and newspaper coverage throughout Europe. Rubik s Cube Last year, Alfrec s Rubiks received a rar honor in Latvia. The 1 Secretary of th Communist Party Latvia came in first in poll conducted by th Latvian youth magazin liesma. He was votec Latvia\'s most despisec personality. Through the years, Rubiks has done a great deal to deserve this prestigious honor. You could say he was a visionary. Back in 1987, when Gorbachev wad building a case for glasnost, Rubiks, then the mayor of Riga, suggested that tanks be brought in to stop peaceful street demonstrations. It took Gorbachev 3 years to act on Rubiks\' suggestion. Rubiks was eventually voted out of office and retained the only position that didn\'t require democratic elections: the leadership of the Communist Party. He watched in frustration as his Party split and then was forced out of power in the spring 1990 elections. But that didn\'t stop him. Throughout 1990 he vowed he would retake the power that was taken from him. In December he went on Latvian TV and predicted that Latvian TV and radio "would soon be our hands again." On January 2, Soviet Black Berets, operating under Rubiks\' orders, seized the Press House in Riga. A week later, as Soviet tanks began crushing civilians in Vilnius, Rubiks assumed the leadership of the socalled "Latvian Salvation Committee" and announced that he was ready to take over the Latvian government. As usual, he was ignored by the populace. Within days, Rubiks announced that his Salvation commitee had already taken over the government. But the democratically elected Latvian government was still in place. Later, after the Black Beret assault on the Latvian Interior Ministry, Rubiks held a press conference and announced that he had been misunderstood. His Salvation Committee, he said, never really intended to seize the government. It just wanted to have a voice in Latvia\'s affairs. Rubiks denied having any role in the Black Beret terror campaign in Riga. Soviet President Gorbachev also denied responsibility for the violence. Gorbachev even denied having anything to do with Rubiks or his Salvation Committee. Shortly thereafter, Latvia\'s president, Anatolijs Gorbunovs went to Moscow to meet with Gorbachev and discuss the crisis in Latvia. When he arrived, Gorbunovs found two people sitting across from him at the table: Mikhail Gorbachev and Alfreds Rubiks. Perhaps Gorbachev found something in common with his fellow Communist Party boss. Gorbachev, you see, also has this problem with democratic elections and popularity polls. Chicago has a heart Usually, infants like Krista Liepniece rarely have a chance to survive in Latvia. Born with a rare form of congenital heart disease, Latvian doctors concluded that they could do little to help her. Her x-rays were sent to major cardiac surgery centers in Europe and the United States, and specialists there concurred. But Dr. Christopher K. Zarins, a Latvian American Professor of Surgery at the University of Chicago never gave up hope. He examined the two-year-old Krista during a medical mission to Latvia in December 1990, and returned to Chicago with an appeal to the University of Chicago for assistance. Dr. J. Deane Waldman, Chief of Pediatric Cardiology, and Dr. Robert B. Karp, Chief of Cardiac Surgery at the University of Chicago agreed with Dr. Zarins. On January 23, thanks to the University of Chicago, Scandinavian Airline Systems and the Chicago-based Latvian Mercy Lift, Krista and her mother Inga arrived in Chicago. Successful heart surgery was performed on Krista on Feb. 1 at Wyler Children\'s Hospital in Chicago. Chicago condemns crackdown On February 6, Mayor Daley and the Chicago City Council passed a resolution condemning Soviet violence in Lithuania and Latvia. The lengthy resolution called on Gorbachev to cease the use of force in the Baltics, and stated, "we support President Bush\'s condemnation of the recent Soviet use of force and intimidation in Lithuania, Latvia and Estonia, and call on him to convey this message directly to the Soviet Government, to make this issue a priority item on the agenda of future summit meetings between the United States and the Soviet Union." The City Council urged the Soviet government to "enter into peaceful and meaningful negotiations" with the Baltic governments, and suggested that the CSCE utilize "new conflict prevention and resolution mechanisms" to resolve the Baltic crisis. Reiters — The 700-Year History of the Latvian Flag Historical references to the red-white-red flag of Latvia can be found as far back as 1279 in the German language Reion chronicle. The use of the red-white-red colors was revived by Latvian students at Dorpat University in 1890 and used in the National Latvian Song Festival in 1910. During World War I, a commission of artists was formed to decide the "official" shade of red and the flag\'s proportions. This "official" national flag graced the stage of the National Theater at the declaration of statehood ceremonies on November 18, 1918. NEW AND UNEXPECTED PASSPORT CHANGE In 1992, the Republic of Latvia released the new Latvian passport. Now it has turned out that the Latvian passports do not meet the international safety requirements. The new passports will be issued as soon as the year 1999. RĪGA SLOWLY BECOMES WHEELCHAIR ACCESSIBLE Finally at the Central Train Station in Rīga there is an elevator installed for people with moving disabilities. So far for people in wheelchairs the only way to get to the train was by being carried by somebody up the stairway. BUSINESS NEWS According to the magazine "Business Central Europe the total investment of foreign capital in Latvia last year was 635 million Lats (about 1,07 billion USD), slightly more than in Estonia or Lithuania but significantly less than in some Eastern European countries. In the influential numismatic magazine "Deutches NEWS FROM LATVIA September 1998 – reported by Muenzenmagazin" it is mentioned between the 15 best coins of 1997. in the international exhibition of seafood products in Bremen, Germany "Fish \'98" many wholesale seafood dealers from Czech Republic, Germany, Norway, Netherlands, USA and Canada were interested in buying the products of Latvian seafood companies "Kaija" and Salacgrīva \'95V More than 800 enterprises were presenting their seafood products at this exhibition. LENIN SOLD AT SOTHEBYS The monument of former Soviet dictator\'and tyrant Vladimir Lenin from the city Preiļi, Latvia was sold in May at Sotheby\'s for 23,350 British pounds. LAILA PAKALNIŅA\'S MOVIE AT CANNES FILM FESTIVAL During the 51st International Film Festival in Cannes, France in the category of the "Golden Camera" the film of Latvian director Laila Pakalniņa "Shoe" ("Kurpe") was shown. STATISTICS In 1997, 79,2% of Latvians were registeres as living, below the accepted level of the standard of living, which is 72 Ls per month. In the last four years the number of foreign citizens working in Latvia has increased 1,6 times. Most of them are employed in Rīga and Ventspils – the two economically more developed cities. The highest number of illegal workers has been registered near the boarders of Latvia and mostly in the summer months. The fine for the employer, if caught, employing illegal imigrants is 150 Ls. It is too small a fine to stop employers from hiring illegal workers. So far there is no legislation in Latvia that controls the number of foreign employees entering the work force. JUNE 14TH – THE DAY TO REMEMBER THE VICTIMS OF COMMUNISM On June 14th – the day of Commemoration of the victims of communism, many events took place. It will take 7-11 years to dismantle it and the whole process will cost 50 million USD. RUSSIAN TEACHERS The Russian teachers were picketing at the Ministry of Education and Science in Rīga against the regulation which states that starting September Ist this year the Russian teachers are required to speak Latvian fluently. Out of 19,190 non-Latvian teachers only about 2,200 have attended Latvian language classes which were free of charge. It is estimated that about 500 teachers will find it difficult to pass the Latvian language examination. NON-CITIZENS ARE SLOW TO EXCHANGE THE OLD USSR PASSPORTS More than a half of the non-citizens of Latvia have not bothered to obtain the non-citizen passports. According to the law, the old USSR passport has to be exchanged for the non-citizen passport of Latvia by the year 2000. Currently there are over 650,000 non-citizens in Latvia. Only 221,517 of them have obtained the new passports. IN PARLIAMENT On June 22, the Latvian Parliament passed a bill to grant citizenship to all children who were born in Latvia after August 21st, 1991 to non-citizens. These parents can request the citizenship to be granted to their children up to the age of 15. In that case the children don\'t have to take the examination in the official language, which is Latvian. However, if the parents decide not to request citizenship for them, after the age of 15, the children themselves have the right to request the citizenship. However, then they will be requred to take the examination in the official language. 38 deputies opposed this bill. According to the Latvian Constitution, if 34 members of the Parliament oppose a bill, the President must put a stop on the bill for two months, so that changes can be introduce. It needs 10% of the voters to support the idea in a form of a referendum. A MOVIE ABOUT THE BALTICS A new documentary film titled "One world: the Baltic countries" is being made about the Baltic states. The movie is produced by "Ward Television Corporation" and it will be aired by the Public Broadcasting Stations in the United Stated of America and by some national TV stations in other parts of the world. One of the sponsors is Motorola Company. COST OF LIVING STATISTICS Of 172 different cities around the world Rīga was ranked 45th in regard to the cost of living. This research project was conducted by scientists in Geneva, Switzerland using the prices of more than 200 products and services on the scale from 1 to 100,100 being the most expensive. Rīga scored 88 points. However, according to this survey Tallinn, the capital of Estonia, has one of the lowest cost of living ratio. SONG FESTIVAL 31,000 singers, folkdancers and musicians participated in this year\'s Song Festival in Rīga. The City Council of Rīga spent 18,000 Lats to refurbish concert halls and parts of the city for this event, and the state granted about 390,000 Lats for the purpose of staging the Song Festival. MISS AND MISTER LATVIA On July 4th, a pageant of "Miss and Mister Latvia *98" was held on National TV. Evija Ručevska from Rīga was crowned "Miss Latvia \'98" and Kaspars Patašs from Jelgava became "Mister Latvia \'98". NEW REGULATIONS REGARDING REFUGEES On July 4th, the Cabinet of Ministers set new regulations on the allowance for refugees. If a person has obtained the status of a refugee and doesn\'t have any other source of subsistence, she or he is entiled to receive a 50 Ls allowance as well as additional 35 Ls for acquiring basic knowledge of the Latvian laguage. "BALTIC CHALLENGE \'98" On July 16th, near Klaipeda, Lithuania an international military training called "Baltic Challenge \'98" took place. Eleven countries participated in this training. Latvia participated with two military companies "Latbat" and "Baltbat" and the national guard "Zemessargi" unit. LATVIAN PEACE-KEEPERS – BEST IN THE BALTICS The Latvian peace-keepers from the Baltic Peace battalion "Baltbat" have been rated the best guards in the Baltics. Especially the Latvian mechanics have been praised for their knowledge of repairing a variety of armored vehicles. For Latvians this was not the first peace-keeping mission that they have participated in. MONETARY REFORM On July 20th, the Bank of Latvia has put in circulation 500 Lats domination notes. This concludes the monetary reform that in 1992 introduced the Lats as the Latvian currency. The 500 Ls notes were printed in Germany at the printing house "Giesecke & Devrient". Besides all the safety marks featured in all paper bills of Latvia, this 500 Ls has an additional safety mark – a change in color – depending on the angle from which the light is coming. ENVIRONMENTALISTS AGAINST OIL TERMINAL The Latvian environmentalists, mostly from The Green Party are protesting against Lithuanians building an oil terminal along the Baltic Sea coast, stating that the coast of Kurzeme will be totally destroyed. People can pettition and voice a protest against the building of the terminal. A number of the Green Party members have gone on a hunger strike near the Lithuanian embassy in Rīga. Many well known politicians have signed the petition. AN AWARD PRESENTED TO THE FORMER U.S. AMBASSADOR TO LATVIA The Council of Orders awarded the former ambassador of the United States of America the Honorable Larry Napper with the Second degree or the Three Star Order. LATVIAN ROCK BAND GETS THE GRAND PRIX From July 16th – 18th, the rock festival "Baltic Song Contest" took place in Sweden. In a fierce competition with bands from Sweden, England, Poland, Norway, Lithuania, Netherlands and Denmark the grand prix was awarded to the band from Latvia "Prāta vētra" ("The Brainstorm"). The lead singer of the band Renārs Kaupers was praised in Swedish press and was even named the new Jim Morrison. ROLLING STONES IN TALLINN On August Bth, the legendary rock group the "Rolling Stones" had a concert in the Tallinn, Estonia. According to the border guard information about 6000 Latvians crossed the border to hear and see the group perform.', 'THE CHICAGO LATVIAN NEWSLETTER AT THE END OF NOVEMBER – A NEW GOVERNMENT ESTABLISHED On November 26th the new government formed by Vilis Krištopans was accepted. 59 deputies voted for the government, 24 were against it, the deputies from the communistically orientated party "Tautas Saskaņas partija" didn\'t participate in the vote, thus guaranteeing acceptance of the new government. THOUSANDS OF CANDLES LIT IN REMEMBRANCE OF LATVIAN FREEDOM FIGHTERS In memory of the battle against the army of Bermont which took place on November 11, eighty years ago, many events occurred all around Latvia. The most moving event was when people gathered on the bank of Daugava river near the President\'s Palace to light candles in remembrance of the Latvian fighters for freedom. THE CELEBRATION OF NOVEMBER 18TH On November 18th the 80 anniversary of the proclamation of Latvia\'s independence was celebrated. This year more than before, various events dedicated to the anniversary took place. The day started with religeous service in the Doma Cathedral, then the army parade took place. One of the main events was the concert in Latvian National Opera house. Many official delegations from abroad congratulated Latvia on it\'s independence day. Because of the sudden drop of the temperature (it was about 8 degrees Fahrenheit even during the day) there were some problems with the fireworks later in the evening. It turned out that some fireworks don\'t work when it s cold, but nevertheless thousands and thousands of people showed up and enjoyed the "remaining" fireworks display. THREE STAR ORDERS PRESENTED On November 17th the Three Star Orders (Triju Zvaigžņu ordeņi) were presented to many people who have honored the name of Latvia. Among them was also the President of the Republic of Latvia – Guntis Ulmanis. THE NEW CHANGES IN THE LAW OF CITIZENSHIP TAKE EFFECT After the new changes in the Law of the citizenship NEWS FROM LATVIA – January 1999 reported by took effect the number of people asking to grant them citizenship of Latvia has increased. During the first week of the Law 280 inhabitants of Latvia asked for the country\'s citizenship. Before the Law took effect there were only about 100 people weekly applying for the citizenship. Most of the new applicants for the citizenship are in the 41 – 50 age bracket. EUROPEAN PARLIAMENT ON LATVIA The European Parliament has voted on Latvia entering the EU as soon as possible. However, it only means that next time Latvia will be invited to join the EU, most likely will will be a year from now. LATVIAN POLITICIANS GO TO MOSCOW The delegation of the pro-communistically orientated party – "Tautas Saskaņas partija" – have gone for a visit to Russia by the invitation of the mayor of Moscow. The Minister of Foreign Affairs Valdis Birkavs is of the opinion that it is inadmissible for the deputies of TSP to meet with such high officials of Russia and that those kinds of visits give Russia the opportunity to interfere in Latvia\'s internal affairs. The politicians from TSP have agreed with the Mayor of Moscow that Moscow will collaborate with businessmen from Latvia who support the TSP. This is an open example of corruption and the other deputies of Saeima have expressed their indignation of such a move by their colleges. EUROPE GETS INTRODUCED TO THE HISTORY OF LATVIA. An exhibition "Latvia is returning to Europe: from Occupation to the Independence" was held in Europarliament in Brussels. It was opened by a speech of Prime Minister at that time, Guntars Krasts who informed that the intention to make such an exhibition was to introduce other European countries to the recent history of Latvia. The exhibition was organized largely due to the efforts of the deputy of Europarliament Ernesto Kakavale who is responsible for reports about Latvia. It was his idea to organize such an exhibition. The Latvian government gave 40 000 Lats towards the installation of the exhibition. It is planned to show the same display in Strasbourg and in other cities of EU countries. Šarlote Līduma t in Rīga LATVIA THE THIRD IN TERMS OF FOREIGN INVESTMENT In 1997 the amount of foreign investments in Latvia took the third place among the Central and Eastern European countries. The amount of investments on one inhabitant of Latvia in 1997 has been 169 USD. The first place with 208 USD per capita went to Hungary. Estonia with 180 USD on each inhabitant of the country took the second place. In terms of investment into a foreign country Russia received the first place with investments worth 2,5 billion USD, Hungary – 431 millions USD and Estonia – 130 millions USD. NEW BOTTLES-NEW BEER Starting mid – November the Latvian beer brewery "Aldaris" has changed the old bottles to a new and prettier design. It has also put out a new beer just for Christmas season and it is called the "Christmas beer" (Ziemssvētku alus). 22 YEARS TOGO The living standard in Latvia could reach the level of the poorest country of European Union – Greece – only in 22-25 years as reported in "Business Central Europe". For Estonia the prognosis is to reach the level of Greece in 16-18 years, for Lithuania – 32-33 years. STATISTICS During the last three years the death rate in Latvia has decreased. It might be the first indication of positive changes in the Latvian economy. There are 59,4% people of working age living in Latvia (ten years ago – in 1989 – there were 57,7% people of working age in Latvia). The ethnic situation has also changed. In 1989 there were 52% Latvians living in Latvia, this year the number has increased to 55,5% Latvians. Currently in Rīga 38,7% residents are ethnic Latvians. The average age for marriage is 23,6 years of age for women and 25,7 years of age for men. The number of marriages per year has increased by 0,5%, however, the amount of divorced marriages is also on the rise. In comparison with EU countries, the accidental death rate is large and it makes 11,6%. One of the main reasons of death still is the hindered circulation of blood. VISAS FOR RUSSIAN MILITARY MEN LENGTHENED AGAIN The visas for 205 Russian military men currently stationed at the Skrunda radio locations unit were extended by the Latvian Immigration service till the end of September 1999. For 7 Russian military men with their families who were formerly working at a Skrunda Radio location their visas had expired, but since they claim that they have no place to go in Russia their visas have been lengthened again, this time till January Ist 1999. THE FIRST HELP FOR PEOPLE WITH LOW INCOME In November the first "social house" – an apartment building for the low income people – has been opened. So far only 10 one bedroom apartments and 40 studios are available but soon four other buildings for such purpose will be renovated in Rīga. The rent in those houses will be very low (0,04 Lats per one square meter) and the costs for heating in the winter time will be lowered by 25%. Till the middle of November 75 people had already applied for rights to live in this house. However many others are considering to apply later, because the information about applications has not been out for more than a couple days before the completion of the building. IN LATVIA – COLD WHEATHER DOMINATES This November and December have been unusually cold in Latvia. The first snow which fell on the ground in the end of October hasn\'t melted yet. The temperature in November was mostly 17 to 8 degrees Fahrenheit. In the period of time from November first till November 18th 11 people were found frozen to death. On November 18th – the day of the Proclamation of Latvia\'s Independence – temperature this low was registered only more than a hundred years ago. The snow on the city streets has piled up. The Rīga City Counsel spends about 11 thousand Lats each day to clean the snow from the streets. PEOPLE ARE RESPONSIVE TO DONATING The Fund for the Reconstruction of the Monument of Liberty (Brīvības piemineklis) has informed that the largest amount donated to the Fund is 10,032,83 Lats. It was collected from November 14th till November 17th in the "Statoil" chain of gas stations. Each "Statoil" customer donated half a santim for each liter of gas purchased. When the four day campaign of money collecting for the Monument began, the number of "Statoil" clients increased by 16%. THE DEMARCATION ON THE LAND BORDER – COMPLETED On November 27th the demarcation between the land border of Latvia and Lithuania was completed in its entirety. The land border between the two countries is 540 kilometers long. GOOD NEWS FOR EMPLOYEES During the last year the number of enterprises which provide health insurance for their employees has doubled. LATVIAN CHILDREN GET HIGH PRIZES Two gold medals and three silver medals were won by the children of Latvia at an international arts contest in Taiwan. The event gathered several thousand contestants from over thirty countries. Altogether 60 drawings from Latvia were entered in this contest. BRONZE MEDAL IN LAHTI In the championship of weight lifting in the Finnish city Lahti, Viktors Ščerbatihs from Dobele in Latvia won the bronze medal in double contest (divcīņa) and the small bronze medal in pull-lifting (raušana). Both his results are new records for Latvia as well. SONORA VAICE\'S SUCCESS IN AUSTRIA The performance of Latvian soprano Sonora Vaice in Austria has been recognized as a great success. She performed as Violetta in Verdi\'s "La Traviata" and with the first aria got the ovations of opera goers. The Austrian critics like her voice as well as her appearance. In all the articles that have been written about Sonora it is always emphasized that she comes from Latvia. TRAIN ACCIDENT RULED SABOTAGE A leak of 59 tons of diesel fuel after an explosion at Mangaļi train station outside Rīga on November 29th was caused as the result of sabotage. The country\'s Forensic expertise center has determined that TNT explosives were attached to a cistern causing the blast. The fuel polluted the surrounding area and caused 30,000 Lats damage. DENMARK HELPS LATVIAN CHILDREN The Latvian branch of Save the Children Foundation and the Christmas Charity Marathon staged by the Latvian state television received 200,000 Lats donation of medical supplies from Denmark\'s Maersk Medical company and Sollerod\'s Lions Club. FISHERY LAYS OFF WORKERS Due to the financial crises in Russian the "Kaija" fish cannery will lay off 220 employees. Their warehouse is stacked full with unsold goods which Russia can\'t afford to buy. LATVIA PREPARES FOR FULL MEMBERSHIP IN WORLD TRADE ORGANIZATION. Additional 7,000 Lats are needed for Latvia to officially join WTO. Currently, Latvia pays 17,000 Swiss franks per year to WTO as an observer nation. Full membership, however, requires the additional money in annual fees. The membership brings access to the markets of 131 member states as well as reduced tariffs with many countries.']

Name: 64_undp_human_right_latvia
Representative_Docs: ["MINISTER OF FOREIGN AFFAIRS ADDRESSES UN COMMISSION ON HUMAN RIGHTS On 21 March in Geneva, the Minister of Foreign Affairs Mr Indulis Berzins participated in the 58th Session of the UN Commission on Human Rights (UNHCR). In his speech the Minister noted that protection and development of human rights is among the priorities of today's international relations, and expressed confidence that the UNHCR has to continue with an effective work in this area worldwide, Mr Berzins noted that Latvia appreciated the assistance and support provided by international organisations during the process of streamlining legislation, and expressed satisfaction over the decision reached at the end of last year by the OSCE Participating States that the presence of the OSCE Mission in Latvia should be discontinued. In his speech, the Minister also emphasised that the priority of Latvia's Government is the development of an integrated society. The National Programme Integration of Society in Latvia tirat covers various fields – citizenship, language training and education – has been adopted to facilitate this process. The Minister of Foreign Affairs informed the participants that in the previous year, Latvia's Government launched the information campaign Citizenship of Latvia: Make Your Choice, tine aim of which was to familiarise the genera! public with the opportunities of naturalisation, as well as introduced the activities of the Society Integration Foundation, established in 2001. The Minister noted that the Foundation would be one of the key mechanisms in facilitating tie building of a harmonious society.", "MINISTER OF FOREIGN AFFAIRS ADDRESSES 58TH SESSION OF THE UN COMMISSION ON HUMAN RIGHTS On 21 March 2002 in Geneva, the Latvian Minister of Foreign Affairs Indulis Berzins participated in the 58th Session of the UN Commission on Human Rights, where he gave a speech. In his speech, the Minister emphasised that Latvia's work, from 1999 to 2001, in the Commission on Human Rights, which is one of the most significant international human rights bodies, has proved Latvia's commitment and ability to be an active participant of international processes and to contribute to the decision-making concerning global issues. Mr Berzins noted that protection and development of human rights is among the priorities of today's international relations, and expressed confidence that the UN Commission on Human Rights has to continue effective woric in that area in any part of the world. Mr Berzins noted that Latvia appreciates tine assistance and support provided by international organisations during the improvement of legislation, and expressed satisfaction over the decision reached at the end of last year by the OSCE Participating States that the presence of the OSCE Mission in Latvia should be discontinued. In his speech, the Minister emphasised that the priority of Latvia's Government is the development of integrated society. The National Programme Integration of Society in Latvia is the means to achieve that objective, and it covers various sectors – citizenship, language training and education. Mr Berzins informed the participants that, In the previous year, Latvia's Government iaunched the information campaign Citizenship of Latvia: Make Your Choice, the aim of which was to familiarise the general public with opportunities of naturalisation, as well as give an introduction to the activities of the Society Integration Foundation established in 2001. The Minister noted that the Foundation would be one of the key mechanisms in development of harmonious society.", 'THE CHICAGO LATVIAN NEWSLETTER UNITED NATIONS DEVELOPMENT PROGRAM IN LATVIA The United Nations Development Program (UNDP) assists people in 174 countries, including Latvia, in promoting sound governance and market development and helping to rebuild societies in the aftermath of war and humanitarian emergencies. The UNDP focuses in the following areas: 1 )democracy building 2)transition to market economy 3)environmental regeneration, 4) conflict resolution and s)the advancement of women. In administering its programs, UNDP relies on the expertise of developing country nationals and non-governmental organizations, the specialized agencies of the UN and research institutes in every field. In the fall of \'93, UNDP prepared a proposal for democratization in Latvia which stimulated many initiatives in the area. DEMOCRACY BUILDING An important element in promoting democratization is to facilitate the involvement of non-governmental organizations (NGO\'s) in civil society. UNDP will provide assistance to Latvian NGO\'s involved in social and human development. UNDP technical support is being combined with assistance from the American Bar Association\'s Central and East European Law Initiative, the United States Agency for International Development (USAID), the United States Information Service (USIS) and the Soros Foundation to establish a Judicial Training Institute under the Latvian Judges\' Association. The goal is to provide training for new and experienced judges in law and judicial practice. TRANSITION TO MARKET ECONOMY A World Bank-financed study on economic management, executed partly by UNDP, provided an analysis of the economic management of the Latvian public sector. As a follow-up, UNDP is helping finance a capacity-building program with the main objective of enhancing the analytical capacity of the government in the implementation of economic policies. ENVIRONMENT According to UNDP reports, the principal environmental problems in Latvia consist of air and water pollution. The two main bodies of water- the Gulf of Riga and the Daugava River- are heavily polluted. In the early 90\'s, 80% of urban settlements and about 95% of rural villages failed to provide for high-grade drinking water and adequate sewage purification. March 1997 – Vol. XXIII, no*l3 Soil and ground water have been severely contaminated near military bases, due to chemicals and petroleum products. Thus far, information about UNDP efforts to reduce water and air pollution has not been available. In the energy field, a joint initiative with Latvia, Lithuania and Estonia is underway with the International Atomic Energy Agency. Protection from radiation and nuclear safety are the main focus points. CONFLICT RESOLUTION The recent passage of the Latvian Citizenship Law requires that people seeking to naturalize have sufficient knowledge of Latvian. UNDP is assisting the government in the development of a comprehensive national program for Latvian language training. This project is intended to facilitate the integration of the Russian minority. ADVANCEMENT OF WOMEN The Gender in Development program (GID) emphasizes women\'s roles and needs in all operations-not just in traditional women-oriented projects. GID is an underlying element of all UNDP supported programs. Specific GID programming efforts under way in the Baltics include: 1) establishment of national Women in Development (WID) units for capacity-building, to defend women\'s interests and to assist in women-oriented legislation; 2) development of gender statistics; 3) support to NGO\'s and other advocacy groups in civil society; 4) training and the retraining of women for the transition to market economies, and in environmental protection. Many governments in the world contribute to UNDP funding. Recipient country governments cover more than half of total project costs through personnel, facilities, equipment and supplies. Funding for UNDP programs from \'92 to \'96 in Latvia totaled over $3 million. Thus far, the 97-99 programs in Latvia have been allocated over $1 million. For more information regarding UNDP and their programs contact: UNDP, Regional Bureau for Europe and the CIS, 304 East 45th Street, NY, NY 10017. "Change is under way. This doesn\'t just mean doing things differently. It means breaking the mold, starting over, and questioning everything that was believed to be real. It means taking great risks and charting unknown territory," UNDP Administrator. —Aija Straumana']
