Name: 66_art_painting_artist
Representative_Docs: ['AN INTERVIEW WITH VERONIKA JANELSINA Interview by TĀLIVALDIS ĶIKAUKA A well known Latvian painter comments on current trends of modern art, including the "school of Pop Art." Her own ideas about various -isms, forms of abstract and non-objective art, are well formulated and show a sincere and penetrating approach. Her oil paintings, creating their own idea of space, are intense with light, and often produce an inimitable glow that fascinates the spectator. Her advice to young painters of today: "to be true to oneself." And the truth is always so simple ... Artist Veronika Janelsiņa in her work room Q: Do you find that living in California has in any way influenced your painting? A: I don\'t think that painting today is influenced by environment. Q: Can one speak of a Western U.SA. art? Do the current trends appeal to you? A: At the moment, it is difficult to speak about any regional "schools" in the United States. There are only individual painters, who occasionally find groups of followers. For a while, the Western U.S.A. was under the influence of deKoonig and Rothko. Q: Don\'t you think that too much has been said about abstract art? Is all art abstraction, perhaps? A: Philosophically speaking, all art is abstract, but for practical reasons we may differentiate. Q: Can one really separate the non-objective art from other kinds of visual representation? A: It all depends as to what we call an object. A geometric form, a line or an area can also be called "objects", e.g., a painter could paint a jug the same way he would paint an area of color. Q: What, in your opinion, is the main purpose for an artist today? A: The crowd and individuals, educated in art, make up today\'s public. If one is to follow the tastes of the crowd, there is no point to speak of freedom of painting. The painter hopes, however, that he will be understood, at least to a degree, by those who have studied to discern. Q: Are you concerned what the public thinks of your paintings ? A: Only about that which is said by specialists. Q: Do you think that your paintings can be ascribed to some school, or any other mode of expression? A: My paintings basically derives from the "school of expressionists" of Riga (close to the Flemish school, Antwerp Expressionists), but over the years my style has changed to lean towards the non-objective painting, with emphasis on pure color, treatment, composition and abstract space. Q: Observing Latvian painters now working in many lands, could one say that there is some "collective subconscious", a certain, even lyrical or somewhat restrained common approach in painting, avoiding the current extremes of "dynamic expression" ? A: Latvian painters, including abstractionists, almost always possess a certain common frame of reference, but it is not always a lyrical one. Q: Do you have any favorite colors? or combinations of colors? Similarly, do you "hate" any colors? A: I do not hate any one color, but my favorite colors are the warm ones: the yellows, olives and browns. Q: Is color more important than form? i.e. must a definite composition be retained, or can it become confluent — even a mere texture? A: In painting, even when non-objective, there is always the composition. Even the treatment of the texture depends upon variations of the composition. Q: Are you concerned about perspective in your work ? A: Even a line, drawn on the canvas, creates an illusion of space. Q: What are your favorite techniques ? A: Oils. Q: Can one say that some of your paintings have a chiaroscouro effect, in the sense that you have created a specific light which encompasses your painting? A: By using warm color schemes, one can always speak of the effects of light. Q: Do you think that for a professional painter today certain rules, art canons and respect for materials must be observed ? A: I like purity of materials. Somehow I cannot regard a collage as a completed work of art. Collages can only be means of forming a composition, and they should remain in the artist\'s studio. Even the Pop Art has only a very slight link with serious art and pure painting. Q: Can one speak of some luminosity in your painting? Could this be the effect of using transparent glazes in contrast with impasto strokes? A: The intensity of color, "luminosity" can be increased by means of the surrounding hues. This is one problem of painting that fascinates me the most. Q: Don\'t you think that true art can be quite simple? A: Every art that is good, is simple, and every good art has its own excellence, even the nonobjective. Q: When you have painted enough, do you like to do something quite different? A: I\'m interested in literature and the theatre arts. Q: What would be your advice to a young painter today? A: To be honest to oneself.', 'LATVIS A GROUP OF LATVIAN PAINTERS AND SCULPTORS IN CANADA Established in 1954, the Latvis society of painters and sculptors has 21 members and 13 associated members. The center of activities is Toronto, where annual group showings take place, but membership extends as far as Montreal and even Halifax, Nova Scotia. In Spring, 1964, a large exhibition was held in Montreal with a total of 103 works, and this showing, held at The Gemst Gallery, won unanimous praise from the critics. What is rewarding to the spectator . visiting a group showing of the "Latvis" painters and sculptors is the discovery of some common, basic underlying art truth. It seems as if at some levdl of inspiration or moment of creation, there is a universal acknowledgement of similar ideas. This common frame of reference may be the "social subconscious", as Jung describes it, since all the members were born in Latvia and came to Canada as political refugees. Such generalizations, however, are not useful in trying to denote a common quality of art form, which pervades the canvasses, bronze, stone, ceramics, wood, prints, watercolors and drawings. As if stimulated by a similar experience the works have a cohesive thread, a sense of common reverberation in the act of seeing. The artists know their craft and have mastered their techniques. What is also meaningful to the critical observer is a feeling of communication: the work of art is not only the product of the artistic process — but also speaks to the spectator, reveals new, unseen worlds, sometimes mythical, strange and personal. In the paintings the polychromatic colors are strong and lively; they sparkle in a variety of compositions, textures and glazes. They speak of clarity and truth. The discoveries, feelings and sensations of the artist are communicated to the spectator, who will not be shocked or stare in horror, but, on the contrary, will be greeted and drawn closer. This amiable quality of restrained, lyrical coloring is an element often forgotten today, when the acrobatics with a paint brush or palette knife display a sardonic countenance. The artistic quality of the "Latvis" group of painters and sculptors, when seen as a whole, constitutes a totally congruent collection of works of art and as critic Raymond Heard (Montreal Star, Montreal) noted: "I found the standard of work surprisingly high." Other critics attempted to use a criterion of style and as Carol Chapman (Globe and Mail, Toronto) puts it — "some (artists of the group) are primarily stylists, some haven\'t attained this, others transcend it." A less significant and rather heteroclite attribute is the sign of an "ebullient extrovert" attitude that, according to one critic, "puckishly peeks through." (D. Pfeiffer, The Gazette, Mont.). This curious observation reflects the philosophical attitude of an artist, who is not swept along the path of modern extremes, even if these happen to be the slogan of the day. With the "Pop art" high on a pedestal, the apostles of anti-art are undermining the few remnants of artists\' craft, as if expressing a complete degeneration of art as a skill and profession. Such spasmodic outbursts in the name of "dynamic expression" are not the trademark of the "Latvis" artists. It may be due to their inheritance of ideals and ideas practiced in Latvia that there is a universal respect for materials, techniques, style and composition. The modern trends in Latvian aft at the turn of the century saw the advent of post-impressionism, futurism, influences of the French symbolists as opposed to EDUARDS DZENIS, born in Latvia, has travelled to Belgium, France, Germany and Sweden. He loves to depict swift movement of forms, penetration of light and perhaps, most convincingly, stylized horses. By using a variety of techniques of which a mixed dry point is most original, he engraves a flowing, decisive line that explores the possibilities of depth. In addition, mottled areas increase the surface quality and provide a rhythmic cadence. A nonchalant, stylized expression is based on a thorough understanding of oils, tempera and emulsions. JĀNIS ZUNTAKS, born in 1906 and once Head of a Department of the Art College in Riga, works alia prima and with a polychromatic palette depicts landscapes — snows, trees, skies. His work is characterized by a tonal perspective, harmony and contrast. His most recent paintings show a distinct transfiguration and new harmonies. Swift in execution he produces pulsating and vibrant oils that are always in demand by the collectors. the Russian school of realism (Narodniki). Following the First World War, painters J. Kazaks and J. Grosvalds were the leaders of the new Parisian school of cubism, expressionism, but in a typical Latvian manner, avoided calculated uncertainty and non-motivated deformity that marked the "fin de siecle" of the postimpressionism era. The trends of constructivism, neoplatiscism and purism were experienced, modified, and often discarded as they tended to be flat, poster-like and imitated mechanical models. The years during the twenties and thirties saw a gradual consolidation and sobriety, avoiding surrealism and nonobjective experimentation. Such general background was experienced by the older members of the group when they received their training at the Academy of Fine Arts in Riga, Latvia. ARNOLDS NULITIS, has exhibited in Germany, France and Holland; he also belongs to the Colour and Form Society of Artists in Toronto. His everchanging apnroach explores abstract, non-objective compositions that change from angular, gothic, contrasting lines to frottage-like experiments of texture. In "the Nets" he depicts an interplay of geometric forms, well-balanced and intelligible. ZIGFRĪDS JURŠEVSKIS, b. in 1910, once headed the Department of Ceramics at the Art College in Latvia. His stylized, monumental clay figures are plastic, clearly defined. Using a unique technique of sculpting clay on a turntable, he achieves geometric clarity and symbolic meaning, but there is also an earthly, jocund softening of what otherwise might have been classical severity. PĒTERIS POLNA, b. in 1912 sculpts and carves in several media. His "Old Troll", a bronze, with an elongated, simplified torso reminds one of Don Quixote — it is a languid figure with a definitely literary connotation. HUGO MERCS, b. in Riga, exhibits floating, polished sculpture in wood. VALIJA MĒRNIEKS, has just returned from Paris. On her canvasses she clearly defines areas and subjects them to a massive composition, rationally constructed. Her careful delineation of geometric forms speaks of vigor and solidity, which is sometimes modulated by grisaille-like effects. LILIJA TREIMANE, has her ceramics studio in Val David, Quebec. She has exhibited framed terra cotta plaques, utilizing glazes over a surface indented with lines. EDGARS KRŪMIŅŠ, b. in 1922, earns his living as a television scenic designer in Toronto. His paintings and, even more so, his watercolors manifest dreamlike apparitions, often kaleidoscopic glimpses of city roofs, birds and sail boats. A poetic, transparent, delicate coloring sifts through an extremely personal landscape. His imagery is simple, but not overt and bombastic. In contrary — his lyrical restraint and deeply felt decorative elements provide a fascinating paradox. VISVALDIS REINHOLDS, b. in 1924, has built his studio on high land overlooking a deep cataract, known as the Devil\'s Glen, near Duntroon, Ontario. In his secretly placed studio, surrounded by dense bushes, he works on his non-objective canvasses, using imprimatura, followed by broad impasto strokes. His art transcends style, his colors speak of Northern lights with an unusual luminosity and reflection. An architectonic clarity defines the composition, sometimes quite severe and yet simple. Rectangular areas of thick color are unmistakenly placed with precision. R. does not have a high output: his work seems to be completely conceived in advance, after a period of meditation. A new concept of space encompasses his canvasses and he transforms shapes into structures that often seem as reflections of another world. ĒRIKS DZENIS, b. in 1925, can be envied for his immaculate draftsmanship. Endowed with an ability to do an effortless rendering, he takes particular interest in the human, figure in motion. Swift in execution, his pastels display an instantaneous vision precise and balanced. Today, when the ability to draw accurately is gradually disappearing, his drawings are a pure delight. ALFRĒDS BRŪVERIS, b. in 1922, is a painter for whom expression is a necessity. flii nervous, abrupt, coarsely laid brush strokes speak of anguish and sometimes of sheer force. KARL LANGENFELDS, b. in 1925, is employed as a scenic artist for television. He likes to portray objects of genre: still lives, clowns, owls, devoting his interest to textures, tonal contrasts in terms of the spectrum. His style is planimetric and his knowledge of materials methodically proper. The theatrical element is interwoven with his rendition of living objects. An element of "joie de vivre" permeates his visual transcriptions. DAINIS, b. in 1929, who for simplicity signs only his first name, is an instructor at the Ontario College of Art, in Toronto. His complicated techniques of watercolors take him to secluded regions of Northern Ontario, where he executes a formidable number of watercolors in a month\'s time. His romanticism evokes nature as an essence for existence and for this reason his pathetic orchestrations of nature are really metaphorical allusions about the grandeur of this world. His approach is direct, intuitive, without premeditated composition or rationalization. His method, using the wet technique, requires absolute sureness of execution — no corrections are possible afterwards. ILGVARS ŠTEINS, b. in 1925, lives in Halifax, Nova Scotia, where he is employed as a TV artist. His graphic art, of which multicolor wood cuts are typical, is fascinating. It borders on surrealism, and at times is satirically grotesque. Occasionally he vivisects the human folly and draws demon-like, strange, uncouth creatures that depict the dehumanization of our age. A careful and unceasingly ardent engraver, etcher and draftsman, he has his favorite imagery that he has mastered to the greatest detail. His knowledge of anatomy of figures and animals is invariably erudite, technically without any flaws. His greatest talent is his everchanging invention of new subject matter: it has a life of its own. ĢIRTS TILTIŅŠ, b. in 1935, shows a tonal, plastic quality but without reverberating nature forms into a "grande paysage". In contrary — he reduces nature to basic nomenclature: a branch, a leaf, a stem; these constitute a tree. His portraits are solidly constructed without chiaroscuro effects, sometimes thinly painted. His showing at the Roberts Gallery, in Toronto, prompted one critic to say that he has the technique "to make his love contagious in conservative realism." GUNTA UPĪTE, b. in 1933, prefers delicate, restrained coloring that finds its best application in genre, and still life. AGRIS ANŠMITS, b. in 1938, young and talented, paints impressionistic landscapes using a heavily loaded brush. He achieves a thick impasto, vibrating texture that saturates his canvasses with a heavy atmosphere. He likes a variety of greens, whites and some of his landscapes are reminiscent of the Dutch masters. A. PUĶĪTE is an able watercolorist, K. KRONBERGS shows balanced composition and luminosity, A. KALNIŅŠ paints stylized still lives, L. KALNIŅŠ has exhibited landscapes, A. KERANS has been noted for his miniature plastic sculpture, A. JAUNZEMS has exhibited concentrated, dense renderings in tempera. It is impossible to account for every artist in the group, since new painters and sculptors annually join the Latvis society of artists. Canadian art today, with few exceptions, is greatly influenced by U.S. art, and the new "formulas" are eagerly imitated. One only has to attend an annual exhibition or see the works of the graduating class of the Ontario Art College to confirm this view. In contrast, the exhibition of the Latvis society of painters and sculptors, when viewed as a whole, is un-Canadian in the sense that the general outlook does not reflect the "New York" or "Chicago" schools. This difference has been noted by several critics, and one is almost sure that we are looking at a European standard of art for what it is worth. Standing from left: P. Polna, A. Kerans, J. Kronbergs — "Sun In The Forest" — Oil', 'Janis Silins The purpose of this article is to give a concise characteristic of the development of Latvian art during the latest period, i. c. beginning with the eighties of the last century up to the present time. During these sixty years Latvian culture as a whole, and painting, sculpture and the graphic arts as essential branches of it, succeeded in shaping independent national features of their own and in fitting in to the common pace of evolution of the spiritual life in Europe. The making and development of these arts took a length of time, and they have a history of their own. * Coming from the West, the official period styles in art, from the Romanesque style onwatjd, including the rich unfolding of the baroque and ncoclassic art, used to reach Latvia, with some retardation. In ecclesiastic and profane architecture, in sculpture and painting, the forms of Western Europe acquired, in our country, a certain local colouring which corresponded to the feeling and mentality of the Latvian people At the same time when foreign masters, hailing usually from Germany, but since the Renaissance period also from Sweden, Poland and other Western countries, continued to come to Latvia, local schools and art centres were gradually developing, particularly m the Duchy of Kurzeme (Courland) (1561—1795) and in Riga. Wars and fires have destroyed countless monuments of Latvian art and for the same reason literary data about our early artists are scarce and scanty. Nevertheless, from the 15th century onward, but particularly in the 18th century, traces and indications of Latvian artists and craftsmen are numerous. Besides the period art which had some influence on the development of the Latvian nation, the importance of popular art must be stressed as having been far greater. The roots of it go back as far as pre-historic art tradition exists. This rich source of artistic inspiration has not dried up yet in spite of the ever growing strength of the forms of modern civilised life. We have but to mention the rural architecture of Latvia, so expressive in its simplicity and matter-of-course objectivity, the everyday Latvian household furniture and equipment: the earthenware and working implements, the rich textile fabrics, brilliant in hues and abundant in their peculiar national ornamentation and design, and the silver-wrought jewelry. The Latvian surface ornament discloses in its strictly geometrical design, fine colour scheme and rhythm of the separate elements, a world of its own, differing from both Slavic and Scandinavian ornamental design. It is this tradition which, rooted in and grown out of the life of the people, consciously or subconsciously inspires the present artists\' work and serves as a starting point to modern applied arts and crafts. The same close community exists between the works of the modern THE NEW LATVIAN ART Latvian painter and sculptor and the manifestations of Latvian popular art. They have both one\' root in common: the peculiar Latvian conception and aspect of life and life\'s phenomena, formed by the fundamental structure of the Latvian soul. Let us try briefly to characterise the spiritual trend of the Latvian artist and its expansion in terms of form, by defining the common and unifying elements which despite all idiosyncrasies permeate the artist\'s personal style. We are aware, . in the mentality of the artist, of two contrarious elements which are welded together in a queer way. One is his rustic proximity to the soil, to the world of realities. He never loses touch with life, even if he is carried away by the images of his dreams or by abstract form. But once adrift outside his own country the Latvian artist will invariably get uprooted, lose his artistic sap, and wither away. The tendency for realism interlaces the whole of our art. It accounts for a certain heaviness evident in the works of Latvian art. But it is this unsophisticated approach towards the natural phenomena and life\'s events which enables the Latvian artist to view and interpret them in that warm light of intimate simplicity and sincerity which prevails in the works of our best masters. But there is yet another spiritual attitude which has contributed to the forming of the character of Latvian art. Besides the ability for treating and artistically expressing life from a realistic angle there is a strong propensity conceivable for a soaring of the artistic mind into the realms of transcendental and visionary ideas. This specific northern quality of plunging into meditative absorption, of intensifying the expression of sentiment, of conceiving the monumental entity of Nature lends a note of exceptionality to the artist\'s images, of everyday subjects and sights. This peculiar dualism in Latvian art is most characteristic of some of our poets, too (Rainis, Poruks, Skalbe), who like to interweave reality with threads of their artistic imagination. To the Latvian artist life means a full-sounding reality, and, at the same time, a symbol. Under such an angle the peculiarities of form in Latvian art are more easily comprehended- The Latvian sculptor simplifies the forms of Nature. Heavy and massive, as the solids of K. Zale\'s "Dying Horseman" \'in the Warriors\' Cemetery, Riga, and the figures and heads of T. Zalkalns\'s Latvian Mothers appear, they represent a fusion of symbolic simplicity and realistic form. The rustic in the painter and sculptor tends towards- creating and shaping compact, clear and simple forms, a tendency typical also of the Latvian national ornament and rural timber architecture. A constituent principle in Latvian sculpture and painting is the characteristic rhythm of forms, line and surfaces, binding together the contrasting elements. The Latvian painter has a positive liking for diffused outdoor light which seems to penetrate the bodies and emanate from them, enwrapping everything in. a soft silvery twilight, thus creating that lyrical mood so frequently hinted at in the folk-songs whenever the "silvery grove" is mentioned. This does not, however, imply that Latvian painting is lacking the shine of bright, unmitigated sunlight which, breaking forth, touches and lights up the forms in glowing yellow and warm pink and reddish hues. Let us now turn from these general reflections on our artists\' mentality to the historical outline of the development of Latvian art. In the 19th Christ on the Cross, by J. Rozentals The Jelgava Market, by J. Valters Vernal Waters, by V. Purvitis century Latvian art was strongly affected by the social  which took place in Latvia as well as elsewhere in Europe. The culture of the courts and privileged nobility circles which had reached a climax in the Duchy of Kurzeme (Courland) came to an end, and the middle-classes began to stress their claims and rights. The prevailing cultural forms gradually changed in expression according to a new conception of life. In this period of transition the native artists, living in narrow, provincial circumstances, had to fight hard to subsist, and, as often as not, the more talented went west or east to make their way there, rather than put up with the narrow-minded and artistically uninterested society at home. As long as the thirties of the last century, when a neo-classic tendency in art was prevalent, Jelgava, the principal town of the province of Kurzeme, had the lead in artistic life. Towards the forties it shifts to Riga, the metropolis, where a more realistic view, tinged by romanticism, begins to develop. The middle of the last century produced a number of master-painters of a romantic-realistic trend who gained recognition both in Western Europe and in the Russian capital. It is interesting to note that while pursuing their studies in the Academy of Fine Arts in Petersburg they were oriented towards Western European art. Of the most important painters of that period ought to be mentioned Karlis Huns (1831—1877), a painter of historical subjects and portraits, and Julijs Fedders (1838—1909), landscapist. After graduating from the Petersburg The Mower, by T. Uders Academy with the Large Gold Medal, Huns visited the art centres of Europe in 1863, including England, and settled down in Paris where he became influenced by the art of Delaroche and Descamps, and soon drew attention by his historical paintings representing scenes of St. Bartholomew Massacre. In his "Old Catholic Warrior" who tries with clumsy fingers to fix the sign of the White Cross on to his casque, Huns combines an accurate draughtsmanship with colour effects. The realistic way in which he presents the figure, interior and accessories, are typical of the taste of late romanticism. Huns\'s works appeared on view both in the Paris saloons and in Vienna and at international shows in London. Some were acquired by the art museums of Petersburg and Moscow. However the most touching are his intimate sketches depicting the simple Latvian peasant taking his meal, or folkfestivals and market scenes. At the age of forty Huns receiv;d a professorship at the Petersburg Academy of Fine Arts. Julijs Fedders, then a student of the Academy, was strongly influenced by the Barbison group. In his paintings an atmosphere of romance, silvery-green hues and blue and violet-hazed distances prevail in his landscapes representing the scenery of his country: the picturesque valley of the river Gauja, the banks of the river Daugava, the lonely parish cemeteries. His style is realistic, but blended with a poetical interpretation of Nature. Incidentally, the paintings of Fedders met with success at the International Art Exhibition, London, 1874, and they are to be found in several museums in Russia (Petersburg, Moscow, Odessa). Some decades had to elapse before a new enthusiastic generation rose to create modern Latvian art. From the seventies onward the spiritual life of the Latvians developed at a vigorous pace despite the resistance and opposition met on the part of the minority of the German inhabitants. A drift of Latvian country people into the towns, especially Riga, set in. They hoped to secure an economic footing there. This rush to the towns resulted in the development of an independent. Latvian urban culture. A new approach to art opened wider aspects, and promised richer experiences to the young artists, mostly peasant youths eager with an intrinsic desire for developing their artistic abilities. They would leave their farmsteads in order to go to Petersburg to get their education there. The majority of the Latvian painters of that period were born in the seventies. Fostering the desire for an art of their own they developed their national mind and enthusiastic spirit in a luring light of national romanticism which swept Latvian literature at that time and tinged in a peculiar way the spiritual life of the Latvians. Petersburg at that period witnessed a heyday of art. A host of talented artists, unsatisfied with the routine of a stagnating academic naturalism, were striving after fresh impulses and new ways of artistic interpretation and were ready to submit to the influences of Western European art. Art exhibitions of the most notable masters of the Western and Scandinavian countries took place in Petersburg, and it was particularly the young Finnish art which showed signs of a peculiar national vigour. In the nineties a national romanticism, led by such eminent painters as Zorn, Berg, Edelfelt, Jarnefeld and Gallen-Kallela, was prevalent in Swedish and Finnish art and proved that even such small nations as the Finns were Smokers, by J. Kazaks able through their art to gain world fame. All this was apt to inspire the young Latvian artists, fill them with enthusiasm and hopes, and strenghthen their belief in the future of their country\'s art They turned wholeheartedly to studying and de picting their country, its scenery, its popular types, its rural life, scenes of the glorious struggles for freedom in the past, and of fabulous motifs. The father and ideological leader of this movement was Adams Alksnis who was born in 1864 and died prematurely (1897). He emphasised the importance of national topics in painting. In his drawings and water-colours Alksnis shows the toiling Latvian peasant, one of his favourite motifs being the ploughman and the wood-cutter who on a winter morning, with his shouldered axe, is crossing the snow-covered fields on his way to the forests. In these pictures the human figures melt into one with the milieu, embodying the virtue and will to work of the Latvian peasant. Alksnis also likes to dwell on the raids by sea of the ancient Cours (kursi), the struggle for freedom against the Teutonic Order, he even rambles occasionally into the world of fairy-tales, depicting a rather goodnatured and silly sort of devil. Unfortunately, most of Alksnis\'s work remained unfinished. His contemporary, Richards Zarrins (1869—1939) was a • known graphic artist, sometime Head of Department at the Imperial Russian Mint and Bank-Note Printing Office, Petersburg, afterwards professor at the Latvian Academy of Fine Arts, Riga His favourite media were the engraving-plate, the lithographic stone, and charcoal. Basically romantic, he discloses the fairy-world of the Latvian folklore, visualising the mysterious dusk of wood From the Oriental Cycle, by J. Grosvalds thickets, the abode of witches, hobgoblins and bogies, where Kurbads, the hero, subdues the nineheaded devil and accomplishes his numerous feats. Among the Latvian artists Alksnis and Zarrins enjoy the reputation of national romanticists of local significance. Another group of that generation of artists is represented by a number of painters, whose artistic maturity grants them a place on a level with other masters of European fame and standing. Three of them. Janis Rozentals (1866—1917), Vilhelms Purvitis (1879—1945), and Janis Valters (1869—1932), were graduates of the Petersburg Academy of Fine Arts. Coming of indigent country families, the first two had, during their early age, to suffer life\'s hardships, but possessing true Latvian perseverance and toughness, they managed to push their way and succeeded in attaining their aim. Endowed with rare impulses, they stirred the social circles of their time by the freshness of their artistic temperament and by the sureness of their ideas. Their style of painting is a realistic and decorative impressionism. In the later decades of their development Valters and Purvitis turned to a more expressive and free manner in painting, characterised by a crescendo of decorative colour effects. Exuberant in creative impulses and spirit was the talent of Janis Rozentals, painter of genre and portraitist. At the so-called Spring Exhibitions in Petersburg, where young artists used to make their debut, he" drew attention by his refined colour harmonies and the lyrical atmosphere of his paintings. Jointly with Purvitis, they were, for several years successively, appointIlze, by V. Tone Ed members of the Committee of Jurors of that exhibition. The evolution of Rozentals\'s art was determined by Finnish, not Russian painting. land became his second home-country. He frequently sojourned there, and there he died too. Rozentals excelled in painting scenes from the life of the Latvian farm-people, giving visions, in scales of silvery greenish, pink and bluish hues, of the atmosphere of sunlit summer Sundays, with the country people lingering between whiles, after service, in front of the church and in the churchyard. He has produced quite a gallery of rural types\', presenting them, full of warm sentiment, in a romantic atmosphere. His genre has become classic and left a lasting imprint on Latvian art. Rozentals\'s figures are for the. most part taken from among the peasantry and reflect the rhythm immanent in the soul of the rustic Latvian and the serene sincerity of his life. Rozentals\'s portraits are alive with an expression of deep feeling which communicates itself in a direct way to the looker The artist had a wonderful ability to com bine the bodily reality and psychological likeness ofc his model with a highly expressive and decorative colourfulness. Rozentals has also painted quite a number of fine landscapes and several altar-pieces which have become widely popular. The works of Vilhelms Purvitis, one of the most remarkable landscape-painters in Europe of his time, are to be found in nearly all the large* art museums of Europe and in many private collections (in London, Paris, Bruxelles, Leningrad, Moscow, Stockholm, Oslo, Malmo etc)- Under the influence of his masterful pencil and highly Mother and Child, by O. Skulme artistic personality several generations of Latvian landscape-painters have developed and worked out their style, creating a "Purvitis school" in painting. The son of a miller, Purvitis studied painting in the Petersburg Academy of Fine Arts\' in the studio of Kooindzhi, and in 1897 got the Rome Prize for his competition work "The Last Rays". Very soon the \' young painter gained a reputation in Western Europe. His landscapes were awarded first prizes at the World Exhibition in Paris (1898), in Paris in 1900, gold medals at Munich (1901) and Lyons, with Cross of Honour (1902) for his "Northern Night" and "Day in March." In 1905 the "Studio" published an article about him. In 1913 the Petersburg Academy of Fine Arts conferred on him the title of Acade mician for his outstanding merits as an artist. Purvitis began in a plein-air manner of. painting, turning afterwards to a decorative impressionism and creating a peculiar style of his own of a synthetic comprehension of Nature. Latvia\'s scenery acquires in his perception a solemn sublimity, and the most commonplace object: a clump of birchtrees, the bank of a brook, a snow-covered stretch of land, seen with his eyes and interpreted by his brush, reveal the deeper essence of Irbene Lane, by K. Ubans Nature\'s life Purvitis\'s melting snow and vernal waters, and particularly his birch-groves, are wide ly known as some of his most favourite and finest topics. So are his stretches of hilly countryside alternating with brook valleys dwindling out of sight towards far distances and still chequered here and there with patches of half-molten snow; or river-banks and meadows covered with thawing snow and ice glowing in the slanting rays of the afternoon sun in March; or lowlands flooded by water-courses overflowing their banks; or, again, a cluster of white-trunkcd and purple-topped birchtrees set off against the deep limpidness of a blue vernal sky — each motif a glorious hymn to the majesty of Nature\'s revival. To his subtle composition of lines and surfaces the master adds a luminous colouring. In the last decades of his lifetime Purvitis sought for less conventional landscape motifs, which he interpreted in an uncommonly vigorous and dynamic style. As a pedagogue Purvitis held the post of director of the Riga Municipal School of Art from 1909 to 1915 and became the head of the\' Studio of Landscape Painting at the Latvian State Academy of Fine Arts in Riga from 1920 to 1934. Under his efficient guidance a great number of young Latvian, Estonian and Lithuanian painters received their artistic training. janis Valters, a contemporary of Rozentals and Purvitis, was an exceedingly sensitive master of hues. He exhibited in Petersburg, Munich, Berlin. Impressionistic in treating light and colour problems and in composing form, Valters imbued his genre- paintings, portraits and landscapes with much intimate feeling, half-suggested im – pressions and an undertone of colours. We find among his genre- paintings a market scene, rope-makers, sheep-shearing, bathing boys on a sunlit bank, and interiors in subdued harmonies with delicate female figures reading, playing music or lost in reverie in a dusky room. Valterss artistic conception is more akin to the contemporaneous French and English art of painting. His excellent portraits are of a refined colouring and admirably convey that sensitive and soulful atmoAt the Seaside, by A. Annuss sphere in which his models were living. A certain boldness which he sometimes knows to restrain, coupled with a charming poetic feeling, is evident in his treatment of the glitter of the March sun, the play and reflexion of light on shallow waters, or when he paints the soft greenish trunks and orange foliage of autumnal trees. Valters spent the last decades of his lifetime abroad where he began to appreciate expressionismTwo main directions in conceiving and giving shape to form were prevalent among the national romanticists: the one — a lyric and intimate style, the other — dramatic, agitated and rather harsh Rozentals and Valters are the most conspicuous representatives of the first style of painting- The latter is best perceived in the works of Teodors Uders (1868—1915) who was an artist of very strong individuality. His expressive charcoal-drawings mostly treat types of fishermen and peas ants. The figures of his mowers, boat-builders, young fisher-women are monumental,\' full of elementary vitality, and seething with passion. Being a philosophical-minded symbolist, Uders views life as a process of motion, as an unceasing struggle. His types, genuinely Latvian, one feels, are imbued with the tragicalness of life. His figures are earth horn, rustic people who are suffering under the yoke of Inexorable Fate. Uders discloses in his drawings a great deal of his personal religious credo, in depicting sufferings and the. solitary and forsaken figure of Christ on the Cross. The generation of national remanticists was followed after a short interval by a number of young Latvian painters born around 1880. Though still impeded by the adverse attitude of the Germans in the Baltic, Latvian art gradually gained ground, developing a new multiformity and activity. 1910 saw the first show of paintings by Latvian artists in Riga on a more extensive basis. It was followed soon after by three more exhibitions of paintings and one of graphic arts which gave an opportunity to the rising talents to make their public appearance. Some of them, like Janis In the Field, by K. Miesnieks Roberts Tilbergs (born 1880) and Aleksandrs Romans (1879—1911), continued to move along the paths of academic tradition. Tilbergs, a well-known portraitist, attains a pleasing outward arrangement in composition by a refined flow of line and grouping of surfaces and succeeds in enhancing the attractiveness of the persons he portrayed. In his style of painting he is rather close to some English portraitists, for instance Sir John Layer. Grand in conception is Tilbergs\'s portrait of Rainis which shows the great Latvian poet as a solitary walker, resting in a meditative attitude on a hill-top. Romans is rather mournful and dreamy in his moonshine landscapes and white horses by the riverside, and sensitive, discriminating and delicate in colouring in his portraits. The works of the rest of the contemporaneous painters more or less reflect the restlessness of the life of that time upset by the revolution of 1905 which in art gave rise to a wave of mysticism and decadence. This unrest particularly characterises the "Bohemians" of that period, of whom Voldemars Zeltins (1879—1909) with his agitated colour scales is perhaps the most inciting. Another kind of artistic temperament is represented by the artist-dreamer who seeks to retire from reality to a visionary realm of imaginary beauty. Rudolfs Perle (1875—1917), a, water-colour painter, is one of them. He displays in his watercolours a colourful phantasmagoria of roses, lilies, fairy-tale rocks and castles. The Paris studies by Peters Kr as tin\'s (born 1879) reveal influences of the French "1 c s fau v c s." A melancholy mood permeates his misty Latvian bogs and delicately framed female figures. Voldemars Matvejs (1877 -1914) was the first to introduce the aspirations of expressive modernism to our art of painting. Archaic in manner; he used dim and transparent colours to evoke visions of the Latvian folklore: youths on horseback on their way to the rose-coloured castle of Romance. The beauty of bygone times is sump tuously represented in Janis Kuga\'s (born 1878) painted scenery for Rainiss and Aspazija\'s dramatic plays. His stage-decorations initiate a new phase in the development of the Latvian theatre Mythological Painting by A. Cirulis Fairy-Tale illustration, by N. Strunke During the years of Latvia\'s independence he greatly contributed to the enrichment of the stage productions of the Riga National Theatre and Opera by creating a series of stage-decorations, and conducted the Studio of Decorative Painting at the Latvian State Academy of Fine Arts, Riga, holding the office of Rector since 1934. Professor Kuga is also a landscape-painter who looks for his favourite motifs on the rocky banks of the Daugave which disclose vistas of far, violet-hazed distances. The first world war marked the beginning of a new period in the development of Latvian art. A young generation, born about 1890, which had declared war to the academic naturalism and pro claimed the ideas of post-impressionistic modernism, was in the making. It was yet a period of fermentation for them, with the striving after new means of expression, a focussing of their creative minds on pure artistic values, spiritual experience and a free display of imagination rather than on the reflexion of realities. These young artists oriented towards modern French painting and were passionate in defending the right of unimpeded creation. During the war many of them were fighting for Latvia\'s independence, alternately using their rifle and their pencil to prove their idealism and enthusiasm, and their belief in the future of their nation, thus providing strong impulses to the growing new art. Two artists who died prematurely clearly reflect in their works the spirit and atmosphere of this period of fighting and the manner of their early efforts in expressionistic painting: Jazeps Grosvalds (1891—1920) and Jekabs Kazaks (1895 to 1920). Grosvalds introduced new thematic problems to Latvian art. With a taste for monumentality, he depicted the hardships and sufferings of the Latvian refugees in simplified synthetic forms, as, for instance, the greyhaired Latvian peasant, huge, imperturbable in his spirit, looming on a background of houses on fire, and consoling a boy who, frightened, is clinging to his side. Seeing the events with the eyes of a true artist and being a soldier himself, Grosvalds reproduces the glorious fighting of the Latvian Riflemen. As an officer of the British expeditionary forces Grosvalds travels through Mesopotamia and the Iran, and it is there that he creates his magnificent Oriental Cycle. Kazaks, who died at the age of 25, is more expressive and feverish in his paintings of the life of refugees and soldiers. In the last years of his lifetime he turned to study colouristic problems. During the two decades of independence Latvia\'s art experienced a rise unknown before. The newly Etching by V. Krastins founded Academy of Fine Arts in Riga gave the future artists an opportunity of moulding their artistic faculties in their own country, thus laying the foundations for the development of independent traditions in Latvian art. The number of active Latvian artists may be estimated to some 60 at the outset of the first world war, this number having increased to about 500 in 1940. Exhibiting abroad, our most proficient masters obtained notable successes at Helsinki, Tallinn, Warsaw, Cracow (1936), Vienna, Budapest, Prague, Kaunas (1937), Copenc hague (1939), and Paris and London (1939). Their works were acquired by foreign art museums. In a comparatively short time the new Latvian art went through its stage of pioneering, and joined the living art of the senior European nations. To go into details of the complex process of the making of Latvian art would mean to exceed the compass and scope of this survey. A few most characteristic examples will therefore do to illustrate this period of Latvian aitf. 1920—1924 marked the period of a general enthusiasm for abstract constructive art. The Latvian master-artists, however, being firmly rooted in their native soil, did not become wholly engulfed by purely formal experimentation, nor did they get lost in the void of abstractions. In 1924 Latvian art veered towards post-expressionistic realism. The interpretation of reality became the task of the artist. The significance of personality and the elaboration of mastery grew essential. Konrads Übans (born 1893) is an impressive painter of lyrical, intimate landscapes. Refined colouring and an elaborate technique qualify him as one of our best modern landscapists. He excells in the dreamy silence of Latvian country-side scenery, the provincial towns and outskirts. Like Übans, Voldemars Tone (born 1892), too, has a troop of enthusiastic disciples among the younger generation of artists. Tone is a portraitist who delicately shapes his models, dipping them into a visionary atmosphere of subdued deep brown and grey shades, contrasted and brightened up by yellow and red accents. His female figures and heads, merging into shadowy forms, are filled with a hushed melancholy. If Übans and Tone represent the more lyrical mood among the younger painters, Leo Svemps (born 1897) and Janis Licpins (born 1893) go in for an expressive dramatic and dynamic style. Both have common lines of contact with Vlaminck and the modern, sensually tense, Belgian painters. In his landscapes and flower-pieces Svemps indulges in a display of shining colours and picturesque Wood-Cut by A. Junkers strokes. In just the same way*he handles his portraits. Liepins is harsher, more melancholy, when depicting the poise and heaviness of his peasants or potato-diggers, or in his still lives. Somewhat in the tradition of Belgian art are the paintings of the romantic and phantastic Janis Tidema ni s (born 1897). If Tidemanis is led by his instinct, Ludolfs Liberts (born 1895) is pushed by his will which sets the aims to his romantic temperament. Liberts is a versatile and productive master of the younger generation. He is equally successful in painting Latvian scenery, German landscapes and Paris boulevards at night with a brilliant skill. Lately he has turned to portraying. Liberts is a well-known painter of stage scenes, and in his decorative settings for the operatic and ballet stagings in Riga and abroad he has developed a style of his own. Liberts has exhibited in Paris, Bruxelles, Berlin and Stockholm, and won a gold medal at Barcelona and a Grand Prix in Paris (1937). Quite a number of his paintings are to be found in foreign museums and collections. In the genre field Karlis Miesnieks (born 1888) and Augusts Annuss (born 1893) have contributed a number of large compositional paintings. Miesnieks, a painter of an unsophisticated, rustic mind, sincere and observant, favours in his works scenes from the Life of farmworkers and peasants. He is also quite a good portraitist; he represents his landscapes in a condensed manner, very often in the colourful and warm red glow of the setting sun. Annuss likes to paint healthy fishermen\'s wives in hues permeated with light, or farmers and their folks taking their afternoon meal, and the sinewy and sturdy bodies of longshoremen. There is quite a lot of delicate lyricism in his painting and a tendency to the essential and monumental in his composition. Looking at the world with the eyes of a child is Ansis Cirulis (1883—1943) who shows in an amusingly simple and idyllic style decorative images and the noble beauty of Latvian Tore. Then there areErasts S veics(born 1895), Janis Cielavs (born 1890) and Otto Skulme (born 1889) – all mature painters and artistic personages representing a lyrical and sensitive style in art. From the young generation quite a lot have become known as talented and very promising painters, amongst them, just to mention a few, the genrist Fridrich s Milts (born 1906), the landscape-painter Eduard Kalnins (born 1904), Janis Gailis (born 1903) and the scene-painter Peteris Rozlapa (born 1906). An unfolding of the graphic arts was evident particularly in the last decade of Latvia\'s independence when our graphic artists specialised in the different media and techniques of engraving, Sepulchral Monument, by T. Zalkalns etching, xylography, lino-cut, lithography, and pen and chalk-drawing, attaining notable results. Two outstanding figures amongst them are S i g i s – munds Vidbergs (born 1890) and Nik lavs Strunke (born 1893), the first — an accomplished aesthete and especially skilled in pen-andink drawing, the latter — known as an illustrator of books, and graphic composer excelling in images of the Latvian past and lore. Worth mentioning are also the graphic artists Francis Bange (born 1895), etcher, and Karlis Krauze and Janis Sternberg s. In the province of sculptural art Latvia boasts achievements in both monumental and decorative sculpture, which perpetuate the memory of the dead fighters for Latvia\'s independence (the Warriors\' Cemetery, Riga) and embody the idea of Freedom (the Monument of Liberty, Riga). Teodors Zalkalns (born 1876) and Burkards D z c n i s (born 1879) are the veterans amongst our sculptors. Zalkalns has developed along independent lines, working synthetically and simplifying form, and always respecting the intrinsic qualities of his medium. His silent, intiovertive Latvian mothers have been modelled in general, compact forms which are monumental, but do not lack a note of intimacy. Simplicity and a peculiar rhythm mark his statue of Rudolfs Blaumanis, and his sepulchral monument for the poet Rainis. Both Zalkalns and Dzenis have worked in Paris in Rodin\'s studio. Dying Horsemen, by K. Zale Dzenis is the author of many a portrait bust, realistic and expressive of an inner life, as well as of several memorial sculptures. The mOst notable representative of monumental sculptors is Karlis Zale (1888—1942). In collaboration with several architects he created the sculptural part of the Warriors\' Cemetry and the Monument of Liberty in Riga. His work is a hymn to and a glorification of heroism. He possessed a style of his own, grand and strongly rhythmical, but not devoid of a certain feeling of romance. His national symbols: the Ancient Horsemen, which silently guard the entrance gates to the Warriors\' Cemetery, the Dying Horseman, the statue of Mother Latvia — are full of an archaic solemnity. In his Monument of Liberty in Riga Zale perpetuated the symbolic figures of the Chain-Breakers, tearing asunder the fetters of serfdom, the Lacplesis (Bear-Killer), the ancient Player of the Cither (kokle), and the Statue of Liberty. Zalkalns and Zale have provided strong impulses for the development of Latvian sculptural art. Of the younger sculptors Emils Melderis (born 1889), Karlis Zemdega (born 1894) and Karlis Jansons (born 18%) in a rather short time succeeded in attaining, in their art, a level of European standards. Translated by Harolds Kundzins']
