Representative_Docs: ['On November 18, 1918 the Latvian nation declared its independence from Russia and established its own sovereign nat lonal republic. The Latvians and their Baltic neighbours have inhabited the territories of present day Latvia, Lithuania, and Estonia since time immemorial. Having suffered many foreign invasions during the past centuries and having been deprived of the political independence and property rights to the land they were tilling, they nevertheless preserved their national characteristics and language. During the period of 1721-1795 the Baltics were gradually absorbed into the Russian Empire, the consequence of aggressive Russia imperialism. However Russian domination, accompanied by a determined! policy of russification of con-; quered nations, did not succeed in breaking the national spirit of the Baltic peoples, even though they had been reduced to agrarian serfdom by them. In 1817-1819 Czar Alexander 1 restored personal freedom to the peasants of Latvia and Estonia. When after the disastrous Crimean War the Russian Empire was shaken by a wave of riots, in 1861 Czar Alexander II was linally compelled to liberate the peasants in all Russia. After the abolishment of agrarian serfdom, a swift economic and cultural emancipation began tp take shape in Latvia, gradually transforming the social life and national outlook of the people. Latvian farmers were now in the position to acquire landed property and to make themselves1 economically independent from the supervision and exploitation of the large estate owners. Having regained the right of migration, many Latvians flocked from the country into the towns where they took up handicrafts and trades. Young people eagerly sought instrumental in reviving nation letters and arts. Social progress ana a spiritual renaissance were in full suing in Latvia when a revolution broke out in Russia in | 1905, chaking the foundation of the Czars autocracy. Latvian \' nationalism manifested itself in 1 open demands for a national j j autonomy. Even though the j\' revolution was brutally sup- ļ pressed by force of arms, the |1 spirit of defiance against arfļ oppressive alien rule did not die ] in the Baltics, but developed j into claims for complete inde- ļ 1 pendpnce. World War II j brought about the overthrow of ( the Czar and the establishment j of a Bolshevik regime in Russia, i the defeat of Germany and the 1 disintegration of the multi- j national Austrian Empire. This i development created favorable ] conditions for the small nations i of Central and Eastern Europe, i to re-establish their sovereignty, i Their aspirations were aided by i general recognition of the prin- < cipal of self-determination of i nations, proclaimed by President \' Wilson, and also upheld by the j revolutionary Soviet regime of s Lenin. But while most of the 1 newly established European i states derived their indepen- i dence from the victory of the 1 Western Allies and the Versai- ; lies Peace Treaty, such was not i the case regarding the Baltic i countries. Their struggle for i independence was at first not < even favored by the Paris Peace ] Conference, on the assumption that the Communist revolution would inevitably collapse, lead- , ing to restoration of a "united , and indivisible" Russian empire, j On March 3, 1918, at Brest i Litovsk, the Soviet Government signed a peace treaty with Ger- < many. Russia renounced all its t claims to the Baltic territories | which at the time were occupied by German forces, and there i was no doubt of German intentions to incorporate them in the Reich. On November 11, 1918 Germany, however, surrended to the Western Allies and subsequently withdrew its armed forces from the East. The Sovfets immediately thereafter invaded the Baltic countries in spite of having recognized the right of all nations to determine their own fate, even by seceding from Russia. The Latv,an Provisional Government, headed by Kārlis Ulmanis, was not at nrs; 35th anniversary of Latvia\'s Declaration of Independence Raise, God, Thy gracious hand, Blessing our precious Land, Our Latvian fatherland. Bless it, O Lord. The soil, to which we cling M tol,,and 80n&s, we sing, May there our fortune spring. Bless Utvia! i I available manpower and resources and having obtained jsome foreign assistance, could •j Latvia\'s army of liberation 5 i finally take up the offensive. ; fiy the end of January 1920 all I of Latvia was recovered from , the Soviet invaders. On August • 11, 1920 the Soviet Government signed a peace treaty with LatI via, in which "Russia unreservedly recognizes the independence and sovereignity of the Latvian Republic and voluntar* jly and forever renounces all Sovereign rights over the Latvian people and territory which in the position to repel 1 Soviet aggression, and most of its territory was occupied and subjected to the terrors of the Red regime. Only after having mobilized all formerly belonged to Russia." ļ After having restored peace with Soviet Russia, the three Baltic Republics were recognized de jure by the Western Powers, and were soon admitted into the League of Nations. After the liberation of their country the Latvian people could at last devote themselves to the creation of solid foundatI ions for their political, economic and social life. A Constituent Assembly, elected by universal equal and secret ballot, adopted on February 15, 1922 a democratic Constitution, which still remains the legai basis of Latvia\'s sovereign rights. In the I social and economic field the i most fundamental and urgent j I task of the legislature was to I 1 change and modernize anti-! quated agrarian conditions and property rights inhereted from j ļ medieval feudalism. Latvia, ļ being primarily a nation of far- ļ I mers, was faced with the fact i that only 39 per cent, of the | ! arable land was owned by the! farmers themselves, and that j 75 per cent, of the rural pop-I ulation were completely deprived of landed property. On ļ j all land, including most of the( ! ly a thousand manorial estate j I owners of German descent, I while the rest was state property. Such unjust and indefensible distribution of wealth was bound to perpetuate intolerable social and economic conditions. It had become particularly dangerous in view of the fact that beyond the border lay Soviet Russia where an unprecedented social revolution was in progress. On September 16, 1920 the Latvian Constituent Assembly adopted a sweepihg agrarian reform, which provided for expropriation of all manorial estates, and allocation of farm plots to landless persons who were desirous and capable of tilling the soil. After the completion of the land reform the number of land owning farmers had nearly doubled. Agricultural production was steadily increasing in quantity and quality, yielding not only ample food supplies for the population of Latvia, but also a steadily increasing surplus for export. The reform once and for all did away with the main causes for social unrest in former years, having at the same time laid a solid foundation for a stable national economy. In the years before the last war Latvia enjoyed one of the highest standards of living in Europe. Communist subversion had lost much of its breeding ground, even among the Latvian workers. On the eve of World War II the Latvian Republic was nationally and internationally resting on firm foundations. Latvia had no quarrels with other nations. Her relations with the Soviet Union were well consolidated by a multitude of treaties, including a peace treaty, an agreement renouncing war as an instrument of national policy, a convention relating to concilliation precedure, a non-agression pact, and a convention devoted to definition of agression. In social and economic respects the majority of Latvian people were gainfully occupied and well satisfied, and they were proud of their political independence. All they wished for was to be left in peace and to preserve I their independent existence, determined to develop the re-I sources and creative gifts af their nation to new peaks in ļ the spirit of western Christian civilation. But of this opportunity they were soon to be deprived by the aggressive designs of their big neighbors. With Stalin\'s ascent to unlimited power in Russia, the Soviet regime, even though continuing to pay lip service to the rights of other nations, tried every device, ranging from open conspiracy or subversion to subtle trickery in the field of diplomacy, to bring the Baltic countries under Soviet domination. The Kremlin finally achieved its ends with the conclusion of the infamous Nazi-Soviet pact of August 23, 1939, which touched off World War II Subsequent events leading to the Soviet enslavement of the Baltics, followed in swift succession. In September and October of the same year the Baltic nations were forced to conclude under pressure the so called "mutual assistance" pacts with Soviet Russia, by which the latter gained military bases on their territories, pledging j herself in return not to interfere in Baltic internal affairs. Even though the Baltic states scrupulously adhered to treaty stipulations, it did not save them from being arbitrarily occupied on June 16-17, 1940 by overwhelming Red armed forces. As soon as the Soviet occupation regime and its political police had gained full control of the Baltic Republics, the socalled "people\'s governments" of Soviet puppets were forcibly installed. A few weeks later Soviet-style "elections", with only a single pro-communist slate of candidates being presented to the electorate, were imposed on the stunned population. Voting took place under the shadow of Soviet bayonets and tanks and was, to all practical purposes, compulsory, because persons who dared to stay away from the polls faced severest reprisals. The fraudulent election results -were interpreted by the Moscow wire pullers as a mandate for the "peopled representatives" to ask for "admission" into the Soviet Union. The Moscow Supreme Soviet promptly "admitted" Lithuania, Latvia and Estonia into the Union. Latvia was incorporated on August 5, 1940. Since there could not be the, slightest doubt that the Baltic countries had been occupied by brute force and were scheduled for annexation by fraudulent and illegal means, the United States Government came forth on July 23 with a declaration saying that the American people refuse to recognize such annexation. Other Western powers followed America\'s example, and today the legal independence of the Baltic Republics is generally acknowledged by the free civilized world, in spite of their continued subjection to Soviet occupation. In June 1941 Germany went to war with Soviet Russia, and for three years the Baltic countries were subject to German occupation. In 1944 the Reds returned. The horrors of Soviet rule in the Baltics defies description. Repeated mass deportations of large scale sections of the population became an established feature of Moscow\'s political practices. In Latvia the last and largest single wave of deportations occurred in March 1949 in connection with enforced collectivization of agriculture. However, arrests and deportation of smaller groups continue to the present day. Owing to the fact that little authentic news from behind the Baltic Iron Curtain reaches the free world, accurate figures on latest Bolshevik attrocities are not available. But conservative estimates put the total number of victims of Soviet deportations and extermination at several hundred thousand from a population which amounted to two million in 1939. For the Latvian people under Soviet rule it is no more the question of upholding civil rights and liberties, but of physical survival in i the face of Soviet practiced genocide.', 'In Commemoration of Latvia\'s Independence Day November 18, 1918 On November 18th, thirty-four years will have passed since the day when the Latvian people proclaimed their independence and established the Republic of Latvia. This event is solemnly commemorated by Latvian exiles in emigration and is remembered with silent devotion by the people in Sovietenslaved Latvia. The memory of Latvia\'s independence is a source of endurance and a ray of hope for the return of freedom, for which the entire Latvian nation is today united with the free world. The Latvian people, and their Lithuanian and Estonian neighbors, have inhabited their present territories on the eastern shores of the Baltic Sea from a time iunmemorial. Because of their georgraphical position exposing them east and west to the unruly energies of their expansive neighbors, they have suffered frequent invasions and periods of foreign domination. Conditions in Latvia and Estonia, which countries were from the 13th to the second half of the 16th century parts of the Livonian confederation, were in some respects comparable to the situation in Britain after the Norman invasion. Lithuania\'s destiny, linked since the 14th century with Poland because of a personal union, took a different course, until its loss of independence following Poland\'s partition. At the close of the 18th century the Baltic nations had been brought entirely under Russian rule. But they held on to their language and ancient customs and never merged with their alien overlords into a new nation. Russian domination of the Baltic countries brought the farmers under extreme servitude and strained to the breaking point the antagonism of the people against their alien rulers. Finally Russia\'s liberalizing Czar Alexandr I restored personal freedom to the farmers of Latvia and Estonia in 1817-1819. But Czar Alexandr II enacted in 1861 the liberation of the peasants in all Russia. Even though spiritually the Baltic nations had never been completely subjected by foreign domination, the abolishment of agrarian serfdom gave them powerful impetus for the advancement of their social, economic and national aspirations. The First World War, with German invasion, inflicted much suffering on Latvia and caused a great exodus of refugees and evacuees to Russia of whom i many never returned. In Febru- i ary 1917 revolution broke out in Russia, but on November 7 the Bolshevik Leninn regime ascended to power as a result of ■ a coup d\'etat against the demo- ļ cratic government headed by Kerensky. With Russia\'s military resistance unreparably collapsed, Lenin signed the Brest-Litovsk peace treaty with Germany, by which the Baltic countries were detached from Russia. The Baltic nations were faced with the alternatives of being absorbed in the whirlpool of i Soviet communism or German imperialism unless they could establish their own independent statehood. When Germany\'s attempts to incorporate them in the Reich were frustrated by its defeat in the West, the time was at hand for the Baltic countries to achieve complete independence. i On November 18, 1918, a Latvian National Assembly convened in Riga and solemnly proclaimed the independence of the Republic of Latvia. However, having renounced Brest-Litovsk following Germany\'s acceptance of the Armistice on November 11, the Soviets invaded the Baltic republics which had yet been unable to orgaize effective military resistance. Although most of Latvia had been occupied for half a year, the Red forceš regime signed a peace treaty with Latvia on August 11, 1920. Article II of this treaty contains the following provisions: By virtue of the principles proclaimed by the Federal Russian Socialist Republic of Soviets which establish the right of self-determination for all nations, even to total secession from the empire with which they have been incorporated, and in view of the desire expressed by the Latvian people to lead an independent national existence, Russia unreservedly recognizes the independence, self-government and sovereignty of the Latvian republic and voluntarily and forever renounces all sovereign rights over the Latvian people and territory which formerly belonged to Russia. Self-determination of nations, a principle advocated by President Wilson at the close of World War 1, was not only introduced in the covenant of the League of Nations, but it was also upheld by the Lenin regime. It is, as a matter of fact, incorporated in the Stalin Constitution of 1935, Article 17, of which states: "To every Union Republic is reserved the right to secede freely from the USSR." As early as 1913 Leninn had A TYPICAL WINTER SCENERY OF LATVIA branded Russia "a prison of nations." Since the dominant Great Russians constitute only 43 per cent, of the Empire\'s population, he reasoned that the Bolshevik Party must exploit the nationalistic sentiment of the non-Russian peoples to bring about a social revolution. This he recognized without limitations the right of all nations to self-determination — even to complete secession. The adoption of Lenin\'s theses secured for the Bolshevik Party the support of great numbers of non-Russian soldiers and workers and with their assistance it gained political control of Russia in November 1917. Unfortunately for the non-Russian peoples, Stalin was then Peoples Commissar for Nationality Affairs. When rather than the anticipated world revolution Russia was plunged into civil war, no less than eleven European and Asiatic nations declared their independenc from Russia. Stalin\'s reaction to this development was marked by contempt to the principles inherent in his office. At. the Convention of all-Russian Soviets held in January 1919, he succeeded in gaining acceptance of his thesis that questions of self-determination of nations are to be determined solely by the Soviets in the interests of proletarian class struggle. By this motion the right to self-determination of non-Russian peoples was subjected to the arbitrary interpretation of the Moscow dictatorship and thus completely deprived of its essence. Having overruled Lenin, Stalin emerged from the convention with vastly increased power, "Workers\' and peasants\'" governments of the seceding nations were formed in Moscow and Red troops were dispatched to reconquer them. Stalin took personal charge of the conquest oi his homeland, Georgia, where he acted with exceptional cruelty. However, he failed with Finland, the Baltic States and Poland. When in 1923 Lenin had passed away after long illness and infirmity, Stalin seized command of the Communist Party and Soviet Government. Having mummified and sanctified Lenin, and after having ousted his chief rival, Trotzky, he promoted himself to the position of Lenin\'s uncontested heir and the undis• puted "idol" of the Soviet people. In all this he effectively utilized the apparatus of the NKVD, which holds the key to his power. Stalin also succeeded in the creation of a powerful war\' machine which enabled him to win World War 11 with Western assistance and to enslave subsequently millions of previously free people. This raises the question whether the system which he created is going to prevail over so-called capitalism which, in spite of its shortcomings, leaves a wide scope for personal freedom, enterprise and human progress. In order to form an objective opinion, it is useful also to consult ideologies which deal with the subject of Stalin\'s own grounds. In a recently published article, Milovan Djilas, a leading theoretician on Marxism and a member of Yugoslavia\'s politbureau, calls Stalin "stupid" and ignorant in matters concerning Marxism. He draws the conclusion that the Soviet system has lost all its advantages over contemporary capitalism because it has deserted socialism for state capitalism and has devised the most cruel form of labor exploitation. To conceal its weakness, Soviet propaganda resorts to the obvious method of blackening Russia\'s capitalist rivals. Stalin and his associates ignore any change in the world of free enterprise for fear that any objective comparison would make it too obvious that Stalin\'s "socialism" is the most reactionary system to be found today. The first two years following (he declaration of Latvia\'s independence were marked by invasion and war, death and insecurity. Kārlis Ulmanis, Latj via\'s first Prime Minister who I guided the nation through this I stormy period, referred to it on occasion of the Republic\'s tenth anniversary as follows: Most of us still remember the deplorable state of affairs in our country at the end of 1918, caused by a great war, the revolution and German occupation. The people had neither bread nor security, and they were harrassed by the soldiery, the Bolsheviks, or common marauders. Under such circumstances they naturally looked for protection from the government of their own young republic. However, the government at first had neither funds nor real authority because the power still rested in the hands of a foreign occupation regime. All the government could depend upon was the justice of its cause and its impertaurbable faith in the people\'s will to become free and independent. At the time these words were written, the Latvian Republic j was nationally and internation-I ally already standing on jinn I foundations. The majority of the people were well-satisfied and proud of being politically inde-I oendent. Reconstruction of war devastation had been completed. A sweeping agrarian reform had eliminated the power of landlords, who formerly had owned 48 per cent, of the land, and it provided more than a hundred thousand new settlers with land for tillage. The reform blunted the edge of communist subversion and it stimulated agricul-I tural production to yield a growing surplus for export. Latvia\'s i independence was recognized by the entire civilized world, and the creative genius of the nation was boosted to unprecedented heights in all branches of industry, science, literature and the arts. The democratic Constitution adopted by the Constituent Assembly in 1922 may be called a model of liberalism. However, its electoral franchise, devised during a period of genuine repulsion against autocracy, tilted to the opposite extreme to promote dispersal of political representation in a polyarchic Saeima, thus undermining government authority. With more than twenty parties and shades of opinion represented in Latvia\'s house of representatives, several times a year one or two members came to hold the balance in the formation or overthrow of coalition cabinets. When the world wide economic crisis of the thirties, and the augmenting menace of tolitarian aggression from East and West clamped down on Latvia\'s economic life and political security, Prime Minister Llmanis dissolved the Saeima and established in May 1934 as an emergency measure his authoritarian regime. Nevertheless, the Constitution remained legally in force. Ulmanis, who later became President, was successful in restoring prosperity and a favorable foreign trade balance, and in promoting\' reasonable unity of purpose in national life. It cannot be denied that Ulmanis played a most priminent part in Latvia\'s political life throughout its independent existence. But it was beyond his power to prevent the catastrophy of Soviet aggression and occupation of Latvia in 1940. He himself was deported to Russia and nothing is known of his fate. When Stalin and Hitler concluded their treaty of friendship and non-aggression in August, 1939, which set off World War 11., the Baltic nations at first hoped that their neutrality would be respected by the contracting parties. They had then no knowledge of the secret clauses in the agreement which delivered the Baltic countries and Eastern Poland into the sphere of Soviet imperialism. After Poland had been divided between the two aggressors, Moscow imposed in September and October of 1939 treaties of "mutual assistance" on Estonia, Latvia and Lithuania by which the Soviets gained military bases on Baltic territory. The Kremlin, however, pledged itself not to interfere in Baltic interior affairs. Barely nine months later the Baltic states were suddenly and without the slightest provocation on their part occupied by overwhelming Red forces. As soon as the Soviet occupation regime and its political police had gained full control of Latvia, the now universally known Andrei Vihsinsky arrived from Moscow and took charge of preliminaries for its annexation. A "people\'s cabinet" of Soviet puppets was installed and it imposed on the stunned population a few weeks later Soviet-style "elections" with only a single pro-communist slate of candidates presented to the electorate. This fraudulent election farce was on August 5, 1941, • used by the Ail-Union Supreme Soviet assembled in Moscow as a pretext to "accept" Latvia into the USSR. Identical procedures were jused to annex Estonia and Lithuania. The United States Government imediately expressed its condemnation of the devious processes perpetrated by the Kremlin to absorb the Baltic countries. In his declaration of July 23, 1941, Under Secretary of State, Sumner Welles, having outlined his Government\'s principles and reasons for opposing Soviet military intervention and anticipated annexation of the Baltic Republics, concludes :— The United States will continue to stand by these principles, because of the conviction of the American people that unless the doctrine in which these principles are inherent once again governs the relations between nations, the rule of reason, of justice and of law— in other words, the basis of modern civilisation itself—cannot be preserved. Great Britain and the other western democracies adopted thereafter an essentially similar attitude, and today the continued legal independence of the Baltic Republics is generally recognised by the free world. Since, as is shown above, the principle of selfdetermination of nations has never been theoretically contested even by Stalin, it is clear that Soviet claims to sovereignity in the Baltics are based solely on brutal force and are, therefore, null and void in terms of international law. On this consideration, and the fact that the divided world cannot forever remain under the tension of the present cold war, hinges the hope of the Latvian people that freedom must and will be generally restored and consequently also to them — unless the entire globe falls under the sway of total communist tyranny, as has already happened to one third of humanity.', 'A. CEICHNERS The happy Age of Freedom of the Baltic Peoples * * * Cultural Autonomy of Minorities in Latvia THE HAPPY AGE OF FREEDOM OF THE BALTIC PEOPLES The collapse of Russia and Germany at the close of World War I gave new life to the many peoples of Europe whom they had exploited and surpressed. Among the foremost of these were the long-suffering natives of the three Baltic lands, whose grants of political independence were the costly, but prized culmination of centuries of struggle and strife. At last Latvians, Estonians and Lithuanians could shape their own destiny and solve economic, social and cultural problems from the viewpoint of their own interests. Political independence proved to be the motivating dynamic for twenty years of continuous progress. The economic and cultural forces of the Baltic nations, which had been so long suppressed by dominating foreign peoples, were suddenly released, and the abilities of these hardy people were proven in many different fields. The example of Latvia can show what the Baltic peoples had accomplished during the short period of their political independence. The Soviet troops were turned out of Latvia in the years 1919—1920, and the War of Independence against Germany and Soviet Russia came to an end. In August 1920, a peace treaty was concluded between Soviet Russia and the Republic of Latvia, allowing the new state of Latvia to form and develop its own economy. The economic life in Latvia was based on a modified free enterprise system of private property and private initiative. From the beginning, the state owned only the forests, the railroads, some industrial and commercial enterprises, and some few state banks. The land and all farming were in the possession of free independent farmers. Industrial, handicraft, and commercial enterprises belonged to private persons and Joint Stock companies while the houses in towns were also private property. The new Republic faced very great tasks. Having separated from Russia, Latvia had to base her economic life on other A. CEICHNERS foundations. The industry of Riga, which formerly was producing for Russia, was destroyed in 1915. At that time, all industrial enterprises were evacuated to Russia, at the threat of German troops. All machines, raw materials, as well as the workmen with their families were evacuated to Russia. There remained in Riga only empty factory buildings. The agriculture of Latvia was also devastated by the war : one fourth of the arable land lay untilled; 10% of all farm buildings were entirely destroyed; only about 80% of the pre-war live-stock were left, and that of inferior quality. Likewise the farming equipment was partly destroyed. The population of Latvia had also decreased: in 1920 Latvia had 1,6 million inhabitants, vhile before the war she had about 2,5 million. (In 1925 when the refugees had returned from Russia there were again 1,84 milion.) To restore a healthy, happy and productive Latvia became the challenging aim of every citizen. AGRICULTURE. In spite of the great devastations caused by the war, only some years of hard work by the free Latvian farmers were necessary to change the agricultural productivity. The task of the rural population was very great, because simultaneous with the reconstruction of farms and the raising of production, was enacted a radical agrarian reform. It was necessary to erect a great many new buildings on the old farms, as well as on the new ones which were established according to the Law of Agrarian reform. There were in the year 1920 about 700.000 various farm buildings in Latvia. In the years 1920 to 1937, 440,000 new buildingsdwelling houses, stables, barns, etc. were built; so extensive was the building activity in the country. 70,000 new established farms procured in dependent existence for 300,000 people—2s% of the whole rural population. The break-up of the large landed properties was of great political, social, and economic importance. The social peace was made more secure in Latvia, as the number of landed proprietors became larger, when there were no excessive differences as to the sizes of the real estates, and no more latifundia. Already some years after the beginning of the agrarian reform in Latvia the number of votes polled for the leftist, Marxist parties, diminished compared with the year 1920. when the Law of Agrarian reform had not yet been passed. In Parliament elections in the year 1931, the Communist vote was only but 7% of the total vote cast. As an agrarian country where the great majority of the rural population consisted of independent farm owners and their family members, Latvia had no occasion for developing extreme political trends. The agrarian reform had accentuated the intensive and highly qualitative work of the small farmer and his family members. Through their diligent and careful work, the soil was utilized to a hingher degree. The acreage of arable land in the year 1939 was 1,315,572 acres more, i. c. 30% more, than in 1913. The agricultural production was furthered by the state in different ways: the rural population was diligently trained; agricultural knowledge was propagated by instructive articles in magazines and newspapers, by courses and lectures, radio and books. The general educational level of the population was also raised. Agriculture received state credits, and export of agricultural produce was furthered. All these measures, coupled with the application of the Latvian farmers, who were proprietors of their soil and could freely develop their abilities for their own benefit, had led to a high increase in the agricultural output and raised the export of food and other agricultural products. The total corn crop amounted in the years 1909 to 1913 on the average to 896,500 short tons in a year, but in the years 1934 to 1938 to 1,369,500 short tons, i. c. it increased 50%. The entirely new culture of beetroots was introduced: a purely Post-World War I phenomenon. In the years prior to the second World War the production of beetroots increased to such a degree, that there was no need any more to import sugar from abroad because of the sufficiency of domestic sugar and grain. The sowing acreage was widened and the crops per acre increased. For instance, the average rye crop per acre in the years 1934 to 1938 was 47% more than before the First World War (1909 to 1913). The crop of potatoes increased 63%. Likewise the cattle-breeding was raised: the number of horses increased from 320,000 in the year 1913 to 400,000 in the year 1938, the number of horned cattle—from 912,000 to 1,224,000, etc. The average quality of the live-stock also improved. The result was that the milk production rose from 679,800 short tons in the year 1920/21 to 1,834,300 short tons in the year 1937/38; the meat production from 107,800 short tons in the year 1923/24 to 184,800 short tons in the year 1936/1937. The increase in the agricultural production is all the more remarkable, because at the same time the number of the rural population had remained nearly without any changes (about 1,2 million). In comparison with 1913, the population had even diminished. This indicates that the standard of life of the population in Latvia had risen, the supply of the farmers\' families had become richer and the farmers were able to sell more agricultural products to the urban population and for export. The export of butter rose from zero to 25,802 short tons in 1938. The cooperative dairies widened their activities more and more, so that in 1938 they converted 798,600 short tons of milk, chiefly into export butter. That totals twenty times more milk than in the year 1914, i. c. before the establishment of the Latvian state. Although the crops per acre and yields of milk per cow in Latvia were considerably lower than in Western Europe, they were relatively high in comparison with some East and South European countries, especially with the Soviet Union. For instance, the average corn crop (all sorts of cereals comprised) amounted per acre in the years 1933 to 1938 in the Soviet Union to 4 quarters a year, in Latvia to 5 quarters. So in Latvia 35°/o more corn was harvested per acre than in the Soviet Union. Likewise the crops of potatoes and beetroot in Latvia were 50% higher. The milk output per cow amounted in Latvia in the years 1937 to 1938 on the average to 8 quarters, on the collective farms of Soviet Russia in the year 1938 to 5 quarters, so that in Latvia the milk produce was nearly twice as great as in the Soviet Union. Not only in the quality and productivity of the cattle, but also in the number of the cattle there was great difference between these two countries. For instance, in the year 1938 the number of cattle per 100 inhabitants in Latvia was 61, in the Soviet Union—37. The number of pigs was 41 and 18. Latvia had relatively twice as many cattle as her huge neighbouring country. For comparing the agricultural produce of both the countries it is necessary to calculate the production per head. Shortly before the Second World War, in 1937, the corn crops and beetroot crops in both the countries were per inhabitant the same, the crop of potatoes in Latvia was twice as much. The cattle-breeding shows quite a different picture. In 1938, according to official statistics, the meat production in the Soviet Union was 46 lb, in Latvia 187 lb per head of the population. The milk production 374 lb and 1840 lb respectively. The meat production in Latvia was relatively four times, the milk production five times greater than in the Soviet Union. The chief cause of this tremendous difference was that there in the Soviet Union was no reasonable and productive foundation of the cattle-breeding and agriculture. In the same years when in the Soviet Union forcible collectivization of the farming and destruction of the independent peasantry were carried out, in the small neighbouring country, where the principles of state economy were quite different, the agricultural output rose to a high degree. The farmers of the Soviet Union whose soil and equipment had been expropriated and who were obliged to be only obedient slaves of the Communist party, had no pleasure and no interest in their work, especially in the careful breeding of the live-stock. Quite different were conditions in Latvia where the free-proprietors of their soil could work and produce with full energy and great care, having constant prospect of a good profit. The government of the Republic of Latvia was endeavoring to secure good prices to the farmers for their produce. INDUSTRIALIZATION OF LATVIA. After the evacuation of Riga by the Russian military authorities in 1915, only empty factory buildings remained there. It is estimated that at that time, approximately 30,000 boxcars with goods (machines, raw materials of the industry of Riga), worth 1300 million Lats ($ I.— = Lats 5,18) were carried away to Russia. The number of workmen and their family members, who were evacuated then to Russia, is estimated at about 80,000. Instead of 93,000 workmen employed in the big industry in Latvia in 1910, there were employed only 21,000 in 1920. The evacuated industry machines were never returned from Russia. The former extensive economic relations with Russia were broken off. Nevertheless, new foundations for the development of the Latvian industry were created and new markets for Latvian produce found. Protective duties and other restrictions of import insured the activities of the Latvian industry. The industrial enterprises gradually received large state credits; different foreign investments also helped to increase the industrial output. The most important basis of the Latvian industry was the inner market, chiefly the rural population. The purchasing power of the farmer was strengthened; the continued increase of agricultural production and the income of the farmers had a furthering influence. Also the export of industrial products was furthered by the state creating by it new employment and production possibilities for the industry and the workmen as well. New machines and various materials were imported from abroad. Latvia herself supplied her industry with important raw materials, such as wood materials, agricultural produce, grain, milk, meat, flax, leather, wool, potatoes, beetroot. Being supported by the said measures of economic policy, the number of workmen employed in the industry (in enterprises operated with machines or which had at least 5 workers) rose from 21,000 in the year 1920 to 63,000 in the year 1930, and to 100,000 in the year 1938. In the year just before the World War 11, there were employed in the industry five times more workmen than in 1920, approximately the same number as shortly before the World War I. Considering that the population of Latvia on the eve of the World War II was only 2 million, half a million less than it was before the World War I, it is clear that Latvia in 1938 was more industrialized than in 1915. This was effected despite the fact that commercial relations with Russia, formerly the most important basis of the Latvian industry, had become quite unimportant during the existence of the Republic of Latvia. Not only the number of workmen but also the technical equipment of the factories was considerably enlarged and modernized. For instance, in the year 1922 steam engines and steam turbines were employed in the industry with total 36.000 H.P.; in the year 1938 the number of H.P. was already 141,000. The number of electro-motors rose in the same time from 1246 to 14,733, resp. from total 13,000 H.P. to 107,000 H.P. The largest industry branches in Latvia were the metal industry, food industry, textile industry, and wood working industry. New industry branches came into being, for instance, the production of radio sets (in 1930 in Latvia only 1340 radio sets, in 1930/31—31,987 radio sets were produced), sugar industry etc. More and more cotton textiles, venler, bricks, cement, sugar, etc. were produced. Whereas the population in the years 1925 to 1937 had risen only 7%, the number of persons employed in the industry and trades had increased 65%. The conclusion is that in the Republic of Latvia the industry had very much developed. More and more industrial articles, formerly imported from abroad, were manufactured in the home country, as Latvia became more and more industrialized. BUILDING ACTIVITIES. Lively building activities flourished in Latvia for these 20 years of freedom adn independance. Vestiges of the war in the country were effaced, buildings erected on the devastated and on the new established farms. In the last years before the World War II the government built great power stations and erected Communal buildings. As it was mentioned before, in the years 1920 to 1937 440,000 new houses were built on the farms. In only 10 years (1920 to 1930) 10 million cubic meters of timber were assigned in state forestries to the new farms and to the farms destroyed during the war. This number illustrates the extent of the building activities. In the years 1926 to 1939 the increase of dwelling space in the towns amounted to 58,000 dwelling rooms. In the same period 8,000 buildings for other purposes were erected in the towns. Many new schools were built, as well as government offices, hospitals, sanatoria, railroad stations, postal and telegraph offices. Municipal buildings, bridges, railroads and highways were built. In the last years before the World War II the great power station Kegums was built at the river of Daugava. It was planned to build Kegums with 100,000 H.P. The construction of it was begun in 1936 and the costs of building shortly before the occupation of Latvia by the Soviet Union amounted to about 12 million dollars. The power station Kegums was to further the consumption of electric power in the country, industry, and towns, and to save a considerable quantity of imported coal, as the import of coal was to be reduced. The power of this new station was supposed to be twice as great as that of the power station of Riga, which up to that time was the largest power station in Latvia. In the last years before the World War II many monumental governmental buildings were erected in Riga, for instance the Palace of Justice, the building of the Ministry of Finance, etc. The extensive constructing activities in the last years of the independence of Latvia were characterized by a great increase in the production of construction materials. The production of cement rose from 77,000 short tons in the year 1930, and only 55,000 short tons in 1932 to 170,500 short tons in the year 1938. The consumption of cement was so large that a second cement plant had to be built in the years 1936 to 1938. The production of inland cement was not large enough, therefore, in the year 1938—26,400 short tons cement was imported (net import). The production of bricks rose from 47 million bricks in the year 1930 to 126 million in 1938. The Soviet Five Year plans and building activities in the Soviet Union have been praised by the Communist propaganda and glorified as an economic miracle which has never before occured. The small country of Latvia, however, can match the Soviet Union, and in the production of some building materials even surpass her. For instance, in the year 1938 there were produced per head of the population: cement—in the Soviet Union 75 lbs, in Latvia 162 lbs, bricks 51 and 63, respectively. The production of cement was in the year 1938, and also in many other years, about twice as much as in the Soviet Union. Also the use of timber was lower in the Soviet Union. That indicates that also in Latvia a brisk building activity has existed. FOREIGN TRADE. What have the Baltic states given to the world? What is their importance in the world economy? The rise of the Latvian agricultural and industrial production was connected with the rise of the foreign trade. The total export and import of Latvia in the year 1921 amounted only to 396,000 short tons, in the year 1937 to 3,536,500 short tons. During the existence of the Republic of Latvia a lively exchange of goods developed between Latvia and the rest of the world. Latvia exported agricultural products (about 50% of the total export), wood materials, and some industrial products. The export of butter rose from zero to 25,850 short tons in the year 1938, export of wood materials (beams, planks, etc.) amounted in the year 1937 to 1,760,000 short tons. Considerable quantities timber, paper and pasteboard were exported. Furthermore flax, meat and cattle were exported. Imports consisted of all kinds of raw materials, for the Latvian industry (cotton, wool, rubber, iron, steel, coal, tobacco, etc.), fuel, machines for agriculture and industry, automobiles and various consumers\' goods. Already before the great world economic crisis (1930 to 1933) the foreign trade in Latvia had reached a relatively large extent. The export of goods from Latvia amounted in the year 1930 to $ 25.— per head of the population. In comparison with some other countries that was a big export. The export of many other countries in the same year was much less: export of Poland amounted to $ 9.— per head of the population, of the Soviet Union only $ 3.—, Hungary 19, Spain 20, Portugal 7, Italy 16, Jugoslavia 9, Rumania 10, Bulgaria 8, Greece 12. The export of the other Baltic states was: Estonia $ 24, Lithuania $ 13 per head of the population. The importation of goods into Latvia and the other Baltic states also was larger than in the above mentioned other European states. The following comparison shows what an enormous difference in the foreign trade existed between the huge neighbouring country, the Soviet Union, and the Baltic states. In the year 1938 the Latvian export amounted to 45 million American dollars, the Lithuanian export—4o million dollars and Estonian export—29 million dollars, totaling 114 million dollars. The export of the Soviet Union in the same year was 253 million dollars (reckoning the nominal value of the rouble 0,19 dollar). The small Baltic states whose territory was only 64,478 square miles and total population only 5,6 million had approximately only half as small an export as the huge Soviet Union with an area of 8,360,000 square miles and 170 million inhabitants. The export per head of the population in the Baltic states was about 20 dollars, in the Soviet Union only 1,5 dollars. The same difference existed in the import figures. The importance of the small Baltic states for the world economy was only twice inferior to that of the Soviet Union. The surprising result is partly explicable by the great natural resources of the Soviet Union (many important raw materials need not to be imported and to be paid with the export), but chiefly by the very small productivity and great poverty of this country caused by its peculiar political and economical conditions. The production and export of butter can serve as a small example of the difference existing between the capability of production in the Baltic states and the Soviet Union. In the year 1937 the export of butter in Latvia amounted to 21,132 short tons, in Estonia 14,498 short tons, in Lithuania 16,564 short tons, in the Soviet Union 14,662 short tons. Latvia alone with her 2 million inhabitants had exported considerably more butter than the Soviet Union (in the year 1938 the Latvian export of butter amounted to 25,796 short tons). The total export of butter of the three Baltic states amounted to 52,193 short tons and was four times greater than the export of butter of the Soviet Union. Whereas the Baltic states delivered to foreign countries 19 lbs of butter per head of the population in a year, only 0,2 lbs of butter export per head fell to the population of the Soviet Union, i. c. a 100 times smaller quantity. The three Baltic states were delivering nearly 8 per cent of the world butter export, whereas the Soviet Union only 2.4 per cent. Not only the export but also the production of butter was uncomparably lower in the Soviet Union than in the Baltic states. For instance, the production of butter in Latvia (in the dairies) in the year 1938 amounted to 32,627 short tons, 33 lbs per head of the population. In the same year, in the Soviet Union 217,470 short tons of butter were produced, only a little more than 2 lbs per head of the population. TRANSPORTATION. Also here during the existence of the Republic of Latvia great progress was recorded. The railroad economy which had been destroyed in the war was very much enlarged and improved. 537 miles of new railroad lines were constructed, so that as of January I, 1938, the total length of the railroad lines amounted to 2082 miles. The government spent also large sums for construction of highways. In the year 1919 there were only 6 civil automobiles registered in Latvia, but in the year 1939—6,687. One out of every 298 inhabitants held an automobile. The number of automobiles was not large in comparison with West European countries or America, but at the same time the number of inhabitants to which 1 automobile fell in some other countries was much greater than in Latvia, for instance in Bulgaria— 1484, Yougoslavia—lo34, in Greece—496, in Poland—lo3o, in Rumania—777, in Hungaria—43B, in Estonia—226. In the year 1920 only 10 per cent of the Latvian prewar merchant fleet was left; but already in 1931 the merchant fleet was at 40 per cent larger than before the World War L In the year 1939 Latvia had 73 merchant vessels of total 199,058 gross tons. The merchant fleet of Latvia per head of the population, for example, was twice as large as in Belgium or Germany, much larger than in France, Portugal, Spain etc. The merchant fleet of Estonia was 176,376 gross tons, Lithuania 4,330 gross tons. The total merchant fleet of the three small Baltic states with its approximately 400,000 gross tons was only three times smaller than that of the Soviet Union (1,135,783 gross tons). Also the data pertaining to the development of the post, telephone, telegraph and radio prove the rapid successful economic and cultural progress of Latvia. During the independence of Latvia the number of post offices increased from 298 to 1428, the total length of the telephone lines from only 6214 miles in the year 1920 to 334,921 miles in the year 1939. In Latvia one post office fell to 1,335 inhabitants (in Sweden to 1,455, in Estonia to 1,551, in Finland to 1,054). In Latvia one telephone apparatus fell to 27 inhabitants, in the Soviet Union to 125, in Italy to 62, in Finland to 21, in Belgium to 20 inhabitants. The number of radio subscribers amounted in Latvia in the year 1939 to 140,000. To 14 inhabitants fell one radio set, in comparison with 11 inhabitants in Finland, 40 in Italy, 43 in the Soviet Union, 8 in France etc. FINANCE AND CURRENCY. The peculiarity of Latvia (as well as of the other Baltic states) was sound and well regulated finance. Latvia was one of the first countries which after the World War I introduced the gold standard or returned to a gold standard system. Already from the year 1922 the money unit was the "Lats" which contained 0.0102397 oz of fine gold and was made equal to the Swiss gold franc. The Currency Law of the year 1922 provided that the Bank of Latvia must change banknotes for gold at any time it was asked for by their holders. The rate of exchange of the lat was kept stabilized for many years, even after England had devaluated the pound sterling in September 1931 and many other countries had followed the example of England (in April 1933 the dollar of the USA was also devaluated). Only in September 1936 the lats was detached by a law from the gold and equalized to the Hu of the English pound sterling. The finances of the state in Latvia, upon which the development of the whole economic fife depends, were always in good order. In the budget the receipts and expenditures of the Government were always well balanced, so that the budget usually was closed with a surplus. In the last years about 15 per cent of the total government expenditures were used for the national education; about 13 per cent for the effecting of social legislation, the national defence required 23 per cent. Latvia, as well as the other Baltic states had small public debts. The total internal and foreign debt in the year 1938 amounte only to 15 dollars per head of the population. STANDARD OF LIFE OF THE POPULATION. Consumption of food, clothing, shoes and other goods was considerably raised during the existence of the Republic of Latvia. This is proved by statistics of the produce of crops and live-stock, and also by the amount of import. After restoration of the agriculture, which had been destroyed by the World War I, and the Law of Agrarian Reform having been carried out, the agricultural production rose considerably, especially in the years from 1931 to 1939, the result of which was the qualitative and quantitative increase of food consumption in Latvia. Food consumption (meat, butter etc.) in the last years before the World War II rose just in the needy families, in the families of town workmen, the small employees etc. The consumption of some food stuffs rose considerably also in the farmers\' families, as it is shown by data of the agricultural book-keeping. Regarding the consumption of milk and meat products Latvia took one of the first places in Europe and in the whole world. According to statistics of the League of Nations, the total meat consumption (except poultry and venison) in Latvia in the year 1935/36 amounted to the average of 187 lbs per head of the population, in Australia 200 lbs, in New Zealand 236 lbs, in Belgium 90 lbs, in Denmark 125 lbs, in France 75 lbs, in Great Britain 141 lbs, in the USA 136 lbs (the figures in the before mentioned countries refer to the average of the years 1930 to 1934). Consumption of milk products was at the same time: in Latvia 150 gallons of milk (all dairy products reckoned as milk) per head, in Switzerland 134 gallons, in Denmark 110 , in France 83, in Belgium 90, in Italy 27, in Great Britain 106, in Poland 59, in the USA 97. Particularly great was the difference between Latvia and the Soviet Union. According to official Soviet data the total consumption of milk in the Soviet Union in the year 1938 was 49 gallons of milk, in Latvia 150 gallons per head, meat consumption was 46 lbs and 187 lbs respectively. Although a considerable part of the milk production, in the form of butter, was exported, the consumption of milk and milk products per head in Latvia was three times, and the consumption of meat four times greater than in the Soviet Union. As a further sign of the rise of the standard of life of the population of Latvia the following must be considered: the increase in consumption of clothing and luxuries (tobacco, beer and other drinks), the consumption of electricity; the transportation of passengers by railroads and autobusses which increased considerably; the better housing conditions in towns and in the country. Although Latvia in this respect could not be compared with some Western European countries or America, Latvia surpassed the Soviet Union (and some Eeastern European countries). For instance, according to official data, the use of wool textiles per head in Latvia was about three times and use of silk textiles (chiefly artifical silk) four times larger than in the Soviet Union. As the number of live-stock in the Soviet Union was very small for many years after the collectivization of agriculture, the consumption of shoes there could also be very scarce. Likewise, in the year 1938 the population of Latvia had at its disposal, i. c. there were produced and sold for the inland market, nine times more radio sets and eleven times more bicycles than it was the case in the Soviet Union. One new radio set soled in Latvia fell to 100 inhabitants, and one bicycle to 40 inhabitants, in the Soviet Union—to 850 and 440 inhabitants respectively. According to official Soviet data an average living space of less than 5 sqare yards fell to one inhabitant in the towns of Soviet Union, but in the towns of Latvia 10 square yards. CULTURAL PROGRESS. The national education was very much raised in Latvia during her political independence. At the end of that period there were nearly no illiterate people in Latvia. An exception was the province of Latgale, at the border of the Soviet Union, where a lot of Russians lived, of whom a part were still illiterate. Shortly before the World War I, in the year 1911, during the Russian domination, the total number of pupils in all the schools of Latvia was 170,000, but in the year 1937—270,000 (as already mentioned, the population before the World War I was by half a million more than in the year 1939); the number of teachers was in the same years 7,577 and 12,935 respectively, so having increased at 70 per cent. The greatest progress was achieved by the Primary schools and the schools for higher education. Hundreds of new fine school buildings were constructed. The University of Latvia in Riga which was founded soon after the proclamation of independence of Latvia, comprised not only the departments of philology and philosophy, medicine, law etc., but also the technical schools: mechanics, chemistry, agriculture, etc. Whereas the sole school of higher education in Latvia during the Tsarist time, the Politechnical Institute of Riga, had only 2,000 students (in 1911), the number of students of the University of Latvia amounted to 7,000 —8,000. During the time of the Republic of Latvia about 7,200 persons were graduated from the University of Latvia, 1619 of them completed the course of the School of Medicine, 907 the School of Agriculture, 577 the School of Chemistry, and so forth. According to some estimates there were in Latvia in the year 1939 about 10,400 persons with university education. The way to schools in Latvia was not blocked to any gifted poor person. Shortly before the invasion of Soviet troops into Latvia, in the summer of 1940, 3800 students of the University of Latvia (the total of the students was 6500) received loans (fellowships) from a state bank, which enabled them to study. Even some students of the first semester received loans, if they had very successful completed the course of the Secondary School. In this connection it also can be mentioned that small Latvia in the last years of her existence accorded every year the "Fatherland\'s Prize" to 6 to 7 prominent writers, artists, scientists, composers, etc. This prize was founded by the last government of Latvia and amounted on the average to 8.000 lats. In the huge Soviet Union, with its population greater nearly 100 times, in the year 1941 a little more than 200 "Stalin\'s Prizes" were accorded for scientific, technical and cultural achievements. Each prize amounted to 25,000 to 100,000 roubles. If one considers that in the year 1939/40 the purchasing power of the lat in Latvia was equal to that of 10 roubles in the Soviet Union, it appears that the small Latvia accorded to her prominent men and women a state prize, which was equal to the first class "Stalin\'s Prize" (100,000 roubles). But in comparison with the population the number of prizes in Latvia was much greater than in the Soviet Union. In different public libraries of Latvia about 6 million books were available to readers. This number must be regarded great comparing it with the population of only two millions. During the existence of the Republic of Latvia approximately 26,000 books were published. In the year 1936 in Latvia 1601 various books and pamphlets were printed; each book was on the average of 144 pages. I the same year, for instance, in Sweden 2886, in Austria 1510, in Denmark 3505. in Great Britain 16,572 new books were published. In the year 1936 in Latvia 149 magazines and 54 newspapers were issued. There were comparatively many people in Latvia who mastered several languages. According to data of the census in the year 1930 there were in Latvia 1387 inhabitants who mastered six or more languages; 17,129 inhabitants mastered five, 66,636—four, 237,359—three languages. Consumption of paper in Latvia and in the Soviet Union shows to some extent the cultural level of the population in both these countries. According to official data in the year 1938 in Latvia 9 lbs of news-print, 4 lbs of printing paper and 3 lbs of writing paper per head of the population were used. In the Soviet Union in the year 1937 these figures were respectively: 2 lbs, 1 lb, and 2 lbs. Consequently in Latvia three times more newspapers, books and periodicals could be printed and more writing paper used. Also the sanitation improved in Latvia. The government and municipal authorities did not spare means for raising health of the people. This is proved by data about the number of physicians and about the extent of hospitals. The number of physicians amounted in Latvia in the year 1921 to 699, in the year 1938 to 1589. The number of dentists was 351 and 837 respectively. To 1 physician there fell in the year 1938: in Latvia 1,247, in Estonia 1,183, in Lithuania 2,727 inhabitants. The number of beds in the hospitals and sanatoria amounted in Latvia in the year 1922 to 4,767, in the year 1938 to 13,218. To 100 inhabitants there fell in the year 1938 the following number of beds: in Latvia 6.7, in Estonia 4.3, in Lithuania 1.4 beds. The number of hospitals and sanatoria in Latvia rose from 75 in the year 1922 to 141 in the year 1938. The increase of the medical personnel and the enlargement of hospitals and sanatoria in Latvia was not a sign of deterioration of the health conditions but, on the contrary, a sign of a greater attention bestowed to the medical care. SOCIAL CONDITIONS. Also in this respect the place of Latvia and the other Baltic states was not among the last ones in the world. During the period of the political independence of Latvia the social structure of the population underwent radical changes. Shortly before the World War II about two thirds of the population of Latvia consisted of owners of farms, trade and industry works, commercial enterprises, apartment houses, and of their family members. Only a third of the population were wageearners (workmen, employees, officials) and their families. But many employees and workmen often also owned a small house in town or had savings in the credit institutions. In the distribution of national wealth and national income there were no such great contrasts as there are in some other countries, where the land is chiefly owned by the great landlords and where the industry and commerce is in the hands of the big companies. Radical changes were made here by the agrarian reform. Before the World War I more than a half of the private estates belonged to about 1,300 big landlords, and 60 per cent of the country population were landless people, with their family members. After the agrarian reform 70,000 small farms were created from these latifundia and in the year 1930 nearly 80 per cent of the total population who lived on agriculture, were landowners, i. c. farmers\' families; only 20 per cent were landless people with their families. Also a great part of industrial and commercial enterprises were owned by small and middle enterprisers, tradesmen, petty industrialists and dealers. The state could greatly influence the economic life and social conditions by the many large and important state enterprises (factories, wholesale enterprises, banks, railways, state forestries), which were much enlarged, especially in the last years of the existence of the Republic of Latvia. So the social peace in Latvia could not be so much disturbed as in many other countries. But much care was taken also for the wage-earners, whose life conditions were improved and standard of life raised. Very important for the workmen was the fact that thanks to the energetic economic policy of the government in the years just before the World War II such good opportunities for finding job were created, that there was no unemployment in Latvia. Rather shortage of labour existed and up to 50,000 farm hands had to be brought in from the neighbouring countries of Poland and Lithuania. Therefore everybody could find job and the wages were on a comparatively high level having a rising trend. SOCIAL INSURANCE. Besides, the life of wage-earners and their family members was eased and secured by the sick-insurance, insurance against accidents and professional diseases ,the old age insurance, and a fund for combating against unemployment. Moreover, the social welfare of the municipalities cared for needy old people and small children. All the workmen and employees were compulsory insured with the sick-funds. Only the civil servants and the greatest part of the farm workers were not insured with the sick-funds (they had a special medical care). The means of the sick-funds came from the payments of the members of sick-funds (two per cent of the wages), payments of the employers (4 per cent of the paid wages) and contribution of the state (1 per cent); the sickfund received altogether 7 per cent of the sum total of wages of the workmen and employees. The medical treatment of the members of sick-funds and of their family members practically was free of charge. The members of sick-funds only paid 15 per cent and their family members 30 per cent of the price of the medicaments. The sick-funds also provided their members with special medical treatment in sanatoria, rest homes, and gave balneal treatment at health-resorts etc. Moreover, every summer the sick-funds sent children of their members aged from 6 to 12, to the country for recreation. Those members of sick-funds, who supported their families, received in case of incapacity for work, due to illness, for 26 to 52 weeks yearly (depending on the time of belonging to the sick-fund) the following subsidies: in case they were attended at horne—80 per cent, if attended at hospitals or sanatoria 60 per cent of their wages. Single persons received 80 per cent and 40 per cent. A female member of the sickfund received in the case of child birth a subsidy to the extent of her wages for 4 weeks before and 8 weeks after the delivery; thus total for 12 weeks. There were also special subsidies for feeding the new-born during the first 8 months. In the case of death of a member the sick-fund paid a subsidy for the funeral to the amount of 40 days wages of the deceased person. Almost only town inhabitants were insured with the sickfunds. The expenses for medical treatment of the country population were covered by the state and municipalities, according to a special law. The country population needed not to make payments in advance, but they had to pay a certain tariff for each treatment received. The needy country population did not pay anything to the physicians and hospitals, all those costs were paid by the state and rural communities. The well-to-do people (the law referred to all the country population: wage-earners, employees, farmers, tenants and their family members) paid the full costs of the medical treatment themselves. The farm workers during incapacity for work, due to disease, received from their employers food and lodging for 6 months. In case the sick farm worker was not able to visit himself the doctor, the employer had to convey him to the doctor and bring him back home, or had to take the doctor to the patient. All wage-earners and employees, and also all farmers, tradesmen, owners of small workshops, and fishers, and their family members as well, were insured against accidents and professional diseases. Nearly all means for this purpose consisted of payments of the employers and the state. The subsidy during a temporary incapacity for work was 70 per cent of the day\'s wages of the victims, and 50 per cent in case the patient was treated in a hospital and did not support a family. In case of 100 per cent of incapacity for work a pension was paid to the amount of 70 per cent of the wages. In particularly serious cases the victim received a pension to the amount of full wages. According to the Law of Pensions old-age pensions were granted to civil servants, officers, employees of the state enterprises, teachers of all schools, medical nurses, priests etc., also to scientists, writers and journalists, artists, permanently working artisans and workmen of the state enterprises etc. These persons were entitled to a pension, independently of their ability for work, in case they were discharged from employment, had served at least for 25 years and were not under 55 years of age. The pension fund consisted of deductions from wages and of payment made by the employers. After 25 years of service the pension amounted to 50 per cent of the salary; for each next year of service the pension rose at 2 per cent until it reached 70 per cent of the salary. Officials and employees of the municipal authorities were insured in special pension-funds. About 100,000 state and municipal officials and some other smaller groups were entitled to pension. Farmers, industrialists, merchants, professional men (physicians, lawyers, etc.) were usually sufficiently secure in old age by their property and sawings. For the time being the workmen had no old age benefits in that way; they and some other population groups that were incapable for work received subsidies from the municipal social welfare. The relief of the poor was for the most part open, in asylums there were living only about 10,000 adult persons. The last Latvian government had great plans about securing the old needy people and those unfit for work. In the year 1938 a law was passed concerning saving of funds for old age insurance. But this new plan was not carried out, because already in the next year the World War II began, and a year later the break-down of the Republic of Latvia came. For combating unemployment (for creation of various employment opportunities) a special fund existed at the Ministry of Social Welfare. The workmen and employees had to pay for that fund, usually 1 per cent of their wages; the employers had to pay the same amount. Besides the owners of real properties in towns and professional men had to give a certain contribution. But in the last years there was no unemployment in Latvia, but rather a shortage of manpower, especially in the agriculture. Moreover, some other means were used for clearing social contrasts in order to further social peace, for raising the standard of life and for family subsidies to the workmen. In the year 1937 the government adopted extra pay for families of the farm workers. The farm workers received additional pay from the state for all children who were up to 11 years of age: for the first two children 4 lats a month each, for the rest 5 lats a month. Farm workers who had one child received 4 lats, who had two children 8 lats, for 3 children 13 lats, for 4 children 18 lats, and so on. As, for instance, 1 lb of butter costed 1 lat and 1 lb of bread 0,10 lats, a farm worker\' family could buy for 4 lats 40 lbs of bread or 4 lbs of butter in a month. These additional payments were received also by the parents and grandparents of the yearly workers, if they were 65 years old and were maintained by the worker. In the year 1938 a further law eased the family life of farm workers by providing a fund for building farm workers\' homes. That fund appropriated more than 2 million lats annually. This sum was used for loans (at 1 per cent a year) to farmers for building farm workers\' houses. After completing the buildings, half of the loan was cancelled. During the Soviet occupation extra payments to workers\' families were not paid; also the steady improvement of housing conditions of the farm workers ceased in the Soviet time. FINAL REMARKS. Although the three Baltic states, as agricultural countries were not thickly populated, their entire territory (65,300 square miles) was comparatively large. It was, for instance, a little larger than Czechoslovakia (53,054 square miles) or a little larger than the half of Italy (119,691 square miles), or only a third smaller than Great Britain (86,486 square miles). Latvia alone is as large as Belgium (11,583 square miles) and Holland (13,327 square miles) put together. The Baltic states had no great natural resources, no coal, no iron ore, no petroleum etc. They had only their moderately fertile soil and forests. Nevertheless the national wealth in these countries was comparatively high. Considering that the whole world economy and mankind have profit in a small area of the globe being diligently and carefully tilled and utilized, one can say that the establishment and existence of the Baltic states has been an event of positively international importance. Comparing the economy and culture of Latvia with those of the Soviet Union we can judge from what a dreary fate the population of the Baltic states had escaped by separating themselves from Russia, fighting for their freedom in the years 1918 to 1920, and obtaining their political independence, be it only for a period of 20 years. The achievements of the Jiher Baltic states in this respect were to some measure equal to those of Latvia. During the Soviet occupation the very foundations of the economic life and productivity of the labour of the Baltic states were destroyed: the joy for work of the farmers, workmen and intellectuals was annihilated, the private initiative and industry of the enterprisers were entirely eliminated. The most diligent and prosperous farmers and enterprisers were regarded by the Communist regime as criminals and were dealt with as sueh—deported to Siberia for forced labour and executed. The forcible collectivization of the farms in the year 1919 and the deportation of the most diligent and prosperous peasants to Siberia has destroyed the most important basis of the economy and welfare of Latvia and other Baltic states. It will result in a total break-down of the agricultural production and cause hunger in the towns. The Baltic peoples can regain their happiness only when their political independence is restored. The Republics of Latvia, Estonia and Lithuania must be re-established again in order to save these peoples from the lowest misery, unhuman oppression, and gradual annihilation.']
