Representative_Docs: ['NATIONALISM—THE ENEMY More than a month before the 12th congress of the Latvian CP which was opened in Riga on Sept. 20, a wave of criticism and self-criticism concerning party-political and economic shortcomings began to sweep forth at the raion and oblast CP conferences as well as in the press and radio. The shortcomings mentioned prove once again that the spirit and patriotism of the Latvian people are still unbroken. It is not without reason when Professor Vladimir Mishke, head of the Maxist-Leninist chair at the Riga University, wrote in CINA (Aug. 16, 1952) that among the chief aims of the 11th CP congress was an unceasing struggle against the Latvian nationalists, the worst enemies of Communism; these nationalists are said to be in the employ of the Anglo-American imperialists and are carrying out orders from warmongers. To what an extent the party nas succeeded in attaining this aim since the 11th congress (on Dec. 27-29), 1951), is a question best answered by the CP functionaries themselves. At some time in the summer of 1952, P. Smilga (Russian of Latvian extraction) was degraded from his post of the director of the State Philharmonic Orchestra to that of its artistic leader (reported at the CP conference of the Kirov raion in Riga, according to SOVIETSKAIA LATVIA of Aug. 13, 1952). Nevertheless to date the activities of the orchestra are unsatisfactory from the point of view of Soviet ideals. Fricis Rokpelnis, the "minister without a portfolia," i.e., the director of the Art Board attached to the Council of Ministers, has also been discharged. Rokpelnis is a Latvian, formerly a leftist Social Democrat, a bad poet (probably a laureate of the Stahn premium), served in the Red Army during the war and was hitherto a member of the republican Peace Committee. He was replaced by the hitherto editor-in-chief of the magazine LITERATŪRA UN MAKSIA (Literature and Art), Voldemārs Kalpins (Latvian, in 1940-1941 secretary to the newspaper CINA, served in the Red Army during the war and became then substitute editor of CINA) who prior to his appointment declared publicly in CINA (Aug. 14) that the Latvian prosaists, critics, dramatic writers and particularly the poets show far too little interest in the present and future and are even, horrible to say, apolitical. At the abovementioned CP conference of the Kirov raion it was even stated that the Art Board and the CP committee have not paid sufficient attention to matters connected with cultural and educational establishments. Here the CP secretary in the raion, Arnolds Kandmanis, quotes two examples of "public outbreaks of bourgeois nationalism:" head of the medical section of the Microbiological Institute (the director of which is A. Kirchensteins!), Prof. N. Stoligno, has published a report as if the institute had made a biological discovery, something that was not yet discovered by the Russians, and, moreover, that this discovery was more important than the discoveries of Michurin or Lysenko. Evidently the political purpose of these statements is quite clear, Zandmanis said: it is to decrease the recognition due to all progressive Russian scientists and to deny the leading part played by the Soviet biology with its Marxist schooling. Zandmanis also denounced that at the Ist gymnasium of Riga there worked two teachers, Lazdins and Alsknis, who poisoned the minds of their pupils with bourgeois nationalism and praised the decaying culture of America and England. In the Griva raion, where Ist CP secretary Yefim Andriukhin was relegated from the party at the order of the CC owing LIFE IN OCCUPIED LATVIA to his insufficient political vigilance, there still exist teachers with anti-Bolshevik views and convictions. However, an exhaustive description of the present views of the Latvian people may be found in the above mentioned article by V. Mishke. He says that after the war 24,000 new Soviet specialists were trained from among "able workers and peasants" who have altered the character of the Soviet Latvian intelligentsia. Nevertheless the problem of the training of intelligentsia is still extremely acute owing to general and local circumstances. Now the probem is more acute than ever as the Anglo-American imperialists are feverishly preparing for a new war, it must not be forgotten that these nationalist bastards are fighting against the Soviet people and used as saboteurs and spies by the Western imperialists trying to drown the Latvian people in a sea of blood. Mishke then turns his attention to the "Law on Mutual Security Guarantees" signed by President Truman on Oct. 10, 1951, and states that the USA, in order to realize this criminal law, had brought a political corpse out of the refuse heap of history, namely these traitors to the Latvian people who once represented the Ulmanis government at the League of Nations and now represent Latvia\'s dead bourgeois government in the USA. Mishke exhorts all Soviet citizens to be on their guard against the mechinations of these evildoers. Speaking of local conditions, Mishke points out that the Latvian SSR is much younger than other Soviet republics and consequently there still exist remnants of the suppressed kulak class in Latvia and capitalist tendencies and facts may be observed proving that bourgeois nationalism is still to be found, [particularly in the ideological field. That is the reason why in the past years the CC of the Latvian CP had to apply stringent measures against bourgeois nationalists in institutes of the Academy of Sciences, State University, Teachers\' Institute, State Publishing House, schools and other educational and cultural establishments. Steps have been taken against the bourgeois nationalist views of primary and secondary school teachers. In course of their criminal activities the bourgeois nationalists try to spread rumours among the more easily impressed Soviet citizens, trying to germiate dis-1 trust towards the Soviet authorities. In science they try to promote various theories which are opposed to the Soviet science, detrimental and reactionary theories. The enemy tries to make use of the few mistakes in the work of the kolkfcazes, tries to instigate the instinct, of private ownership in the Kolkhozniks and to undermine the foundation of the kolkhoz — the public ownership. Another popular art of the bourgeois nationalists is to sow national differences between the inhabitants of the Latvian SSR. They try to create hostility between the Latvians and the Russians. And • lastly, they try to diverge the attention of the workers from the topical problems of Socialist constructions, playing with the abstract idea of a "native country," eliminating from this abstraction all Socialist-Soviet characteristics. Mishke\'s article ends with serious threats to the Latvian people: "It is our task to tear down the national costume from the Latvian bourgeois nationalism in order to unmask its roots in the hostile classes and to discover its real, abhorrent face; we must show that the Latvian bourgeois nationalists have always collaborated with the enemies of the Latvian people and are now faithfully serving the bloody imperialism of the Americans and the English."', 'OPEN LETTER TO THE OSCE ON LATVIA AND THE LATVIAN LANGUAGE Franeker, 14 June 2002 Dear Madam, Sir, On 20 March 2002, there was information in the media about Latvia and the status of language there. A top official of the Organisation on Safety and Cooperation in Europe (OSCE), the Swiss Gerard Stoudmann, criticised a provision in the election law of Latvia, which states that everybody who is a member of an elected administrative body has to speak Latvian on "the highest level". Mr Stoudmann also proposed giving Russian, together with Latvian, the status of state language. The Ministry for Foreign Affairs rejected these criticisms of the election law. The Ministry considers he proposal for Russian as state language as "an unsuccessful impromptu", all the more so considering the speed in which Mr. Stoudmann has had to correct former remarks. in order to understand this issue, some background knowledge is indispensable. In 1939, Hitler and Stalin signed the Molotov-Ribbentrop Pact, on whose basis the Russians occupied Latvia in 1940. In 1941, the Germans occupied Latvia and in 1944 the Russians occupied Latvia again, remaining there until 1991. During the first and second occupation the Russians deported, murdered or made disappear at least 243,000 persons. A further 115,000 persons fled in 1994 on account of the second invasion of the Russians. All this occured to a population of some 2 million people. The number of Latvians (Latvian-speakers) is even nowadays lower it was in 1935. On basis of an article of the Penal Code of the Soviet Union, Latvians (including infants) were deported on account of the following Grime: to be a Latvian. Furthermore, the Soviet authorities conducted an aggressive policy of Russificatlon. Everyone in the Soviet Union was indoctrinated by the State that the Russians were the Great Brother Nation which had brought culture and civilisation to the Small Brother Nations (such as the Latvians). This suggests a cosy family relationship, in reality, the Great Brother Nation and Small Brother Nations corresponded more to Oder-and Untermenschen. Regularly, Russians told Latvians that they must speak the language of human beings, and not thatoī pigs. Officially, the so-called Latvian Soviet Socialist Republic was made to let prosper and cultivate the Latvian language and culture. The reality was a severe Russification policy. Around 1990 only one Latvian Ministry (the Ministry of Forestry) used Latvian in stead of Russian. Latvians who declared openly for the use of Latvian on basis of the Soviet law were deported, even right into the 1980s. All countries of the world, with few exceptions, never recognised the de jure occupation of Latvia (and Estonia and Lithuania) by the Soviet Union; and so in international law the Baltic countries remained independent countries, which were de facto occupied by the Soviet Union. The High Commissioner on Minorities, Mr. Max van der Stoel, wrote on 6 April 1993 in his first letter to Mr. Georgs Andrejevs the Latvian Minister for Foreign Affairs: "I think, for instance, of the long years Latvia suffered under Soviet occupation (...)" Article 49 of the Geneva Convention, dating from 1949, states: The Occupying Power shall not deport or transfer its own civilian population into the territory it occupies". So ttie people, who migrated to Latvia during the Soviet Occupation, and their descendants are by international faw Illegal persons, who have to return to their own country. In 1991 Latvia regained independence as did The Netherlands in 1945. The Germans, who arrived in The Netherlands during occupation time, were interned in 1945 or throw out of country by The Netherlands. Even some of the Germans, who migrated legally to The Netherlands before the German Occupation suffered for the same treatment. France, Belgium, Denmark, Norway, Poland, Czechoslovakia did the same with the Germans in their countries, when regaining independence. Very generously Latvia gave ail persons, who migrated to Latvia during the occupation, and who not participated actively in the Russian occupation, residence permits and the possibility to let them become naturalised and obtain Latvian citizenship. in stead of receiving praise and appreciation from those people, from Russia, from human rights organisations and from the Western countries, this generous gesture of Latvia was used against Latvia. On account of this generous gesture, Latvia now had many stateless persons and according to the former occupier Russia, such a state of affairs was a violation of human rights, and so the Latvians were nationalists. Many people in the Western World uncritically adopt the Russian point of view. The fact that those people are stateless has been caused by Russia, which refused to automatically give them Russian citizenship. On joining the Council of Europe, Russia made a number of commitments. One of these was the abolition of the discriminatory rule for the former clti£JSl IS Ul U 1C OUViCI W! fiVi I tl 1 U !« UMUO WW To date, Russia has not kept its promise. For reasons of national security, Latvia wants to become a member of NATO and of the EU. In order for that to be achieved, Latvia has to be a democracy and obey the rule of law. Amongst other things, it is the task of the OSCE to judge this. In 1998, along other organisations, the OSCE pressed Latvia to give automatic Latvian citizenship to children bom in Latvia after 1991 and whose parents are stateless persons or foreigners. That was a minimal European norm and value, which every democratic country fulfilled. However the OSCE ommited any mention to Latvia, that at least three OSCE-countries (Germany, Italy and Switzerland) did not fulfil to this norm. Latvia had a referendum on this question in 1998, as Switzerland had had in 1994. There were some points of difference. The Latvian referendum received much a good deal of attention form the OSCE, and from Westernsponsored NGO\'s, and voting advice amounted to pressure fvote: yes"); whereas the Swiss referendum was neglected. in Switzerland, the issue involves children of foreigners, who obtained a permanent residence permit from the Swiss state and it did not involve people who came during an occupation. Furthermore, those children in Switzerland received a Swiss education in one of the Swiss languages from Swiss teachers. Those children in Latvia mainly received education in the language of the former occupier and from Russian teachers, people who are denying the Russian occupations of Latvia, people who think it is ridiculous that Latvia is an independent country and who consider the Latvian language inferior to their own. in Switzerland the majority voted "no". In Latvia the majority voted "yes"; but for many Latvians it was a vote of necessity for security and against democracy and justice. All countries are equal, but some are more equal then other ones? Outside Latvia stateless persons are treated as equivalent of Russians. This is wrong. On the one hand, one third of the Russians auto*v\\rtfrīrtr"bll%i v\'i-\\.-t^ii fpļrt i rtK/ī««  r\\ne*Wt r\\ V\\r* H icuiucuiy !owi»cu tut? i-cuvtcu i wui-ci 101 n\\j. uccause everybody who had this citizenship (I.e. Latvian citizenship) in June 1940, plus the descendants of Latvian citizens got the Latvian citizenship (back). Also therefore, 45,3% of the Russians in Latvia has, on Ist of July 2001 the Latvian citizenship. On the other hand, frie stateless persons are the people who migrated to Latvia during occupation and did not naturalise, becoming Latvian citizens. These are, for example, 52,0% of the Russians, 31,6% of the Poles in Latvia and even several thousands Latvians (as of Ist of July 2001). These people are descended from Latvians who migrated, or were deported, before 1914 to other parts of Czarist Russia After 1944 they remigrated to Latvia. These people do not fulfil the criterion that they were Latvian citizens in June 1940, or that they are descendants of one or more Latvian citizens. They also did not become naturalised (as about 20,000 other Latvians did). A large proportion of the stateless persons are denying the Russian occupation, are thinking it is humiliating that they have to learn Latvian, and are reacting negatively to Latvians who want to speak Latvian. They believe that it is a violation of human rights if Russians have to speak Latvian; to force on the other hand Latvians to speak the Russian language is, however, a matter of course. Yet those Latvians are regarded as nationalists, or worse. A part of the Russians in public service as well is behaving in the same way. Thai is why the Latvian government proposes – ten years after regaining independence – an amendment on the constitution, which states that every person has the right to communicate with local authorities in the Latvian language and to receive an answer in Latvian. The OSCE has always stated that for integration both the Latvian State as the Non-Latvians need to make a comparable amount of effort. Furthermore the OSCE states that Latvian is the state language of Latvia and its position should be strengthened. What is the OSCE actually doing? Time and time again, pressing recommendations and requests are comming to the Latvian State do not require demands on Latvian language or lower them to (groups of) Non-Latvians. Aga;n and again proposals to improve the precarious position of the Latvian language (after a lengthy Russian occupation) are rejected as contrary to the spirit of European values and norms or as an infringement of the human rights of Russians. The fact that still Latvians and other non-Russians in Latvia are forced to use the language of the former occupier, i.e. Russian, in all kind of situations (for example on their workingpiace, in elected administrative body and in shops) apparently (obviously) is, according to the OSCE, no violation of human rights or of European norms and values. On the hand, donating money to let Non-Latvians learn the Latvian language (there is no talk about usage) and on the other hand, again and again treating the position of the Latvian language as of little importance and playing down its status is no way of contributing to strengthening of the Latvian language. The OSCE also judges the integration on the basis of the naturalization of the stateless persons. This affects only a part of Non-Latvians. On this point Mr. Stoudmann of the OSCE criticised the fact that 23% of the population is stateless, and he urged Latvia to make sure that more people to be naturalised. The Latvian state has already foryears been encouraging stateless persons to naturalise and become Latvian citizens. Yet stateless persons and Russian citizens got Russian visa free of charge whilst Latvian citizens (e.g. ethnic Latvians, Russians Poles, Lithuanians) have to pay significant amounts of money fo their visas.Truck drivers who drive between Latvia and Russia, will think again and again before becoming Latvian citizens, as people who often visit members of their family in Russia Also stateies persons do not have to fulfil compulsory military service. Further, stateless persons can often study in Russia free of charge and Latvian citizens cannot. For visa reasons, those of study, of compulsory military service and because a large proportion of stateless Russians can live very well in Latvia without (or with little) knowledge of the Latvian language, the motivation to become a Latvian citizen is not very large in those groups. And, of course, they cannot be forced to become a Latvian citizen. That is their own choice. Yet the OSCE does blame Latvia for that choice for stateielesshood and do not blame the stateless persons and Russia on the same matter. The requested equivalent effort of the Non-Latvians Is not examined by the OSCE, let alone assessed. Moreover the OSCE (with a few exceptions) talks all the time about Soviet era, the Soviet rēpjme, the history of Latvia; and not about the Russian or Soviet occupation. The OSCE also uses using terms such as "the former Soviet Republic? and "Latvia, which broke away from the Soviet Unbn" This means that the Russian occupations are covered up, or denied by the OSCE. This is contrary to international law. OSCE-policy is recognised by all communities in Latvia as a continuation of Soviet-policy: officially Latvian is very important, but in practice Russian is much more important; and the Soviet occupations are denied, as mentioned above. OSCE-policy with regard to Latvia is very important for that country. Latvia is very eager, for reasons of national security, to become a member of NATO and of the EU, since any future occupation could spell the end of the Latvians nation, their language and culture. This access to international institutions also depends on the judgement of the OSCE on the record of human rights, minority rights, democracy and the state of law in Latvia This is why Latvia has to allow many interventions in domestic affairs by the OSCE. The Western world and the OSCE, among others, are saying they want to improve and strengthen the position of democracy and the rule of law (including human and minority rights) in Central- and Eastern Europe. So it is sensible fo regularly test wether OSCE policy on this point is successful. Not by asking the Western sponsored NGO\'s, but by examining the opinions and reactions, feelings and views of the population in question. The view of one young university graduate in one of the Baltic countries was: "thanks to the Western countries I know exactly what democracy and the rule of Law actually are. Democracy is counting atom bombs. And the rule of Law means that Russia can become a member of the Council of Europe when it is bombing thousands of innocent civilians in Chechnya whilst the Baltic countries cannot become members due to alleged discrimination of their former occupiers? His view is a bitter one, but the same feeiings exist among many Latvians, Estonians and Lithuanians. In view of afi that is mentioned above, it is understandable that the criticisms and remarks of the Swiss Gērard Stoudmann did not fall on such fertile soil in Latvia His cail to give Russian, together with Latvian, the status of official language, is no improvement of the position of the Latvian language. Furthermore Stoudmann states that you can not make old people in their seventies and eigthies learn a foreign language, and that it would be an act of generosity to make special rules of naturalisation for 70- and 80--year old people. However, Western countries and organisations such as the OSCE did not react positively with regard to former acts of generosity by Latvia. And what about Latvian as state language? Officially, the OSCE declares that Latvian is the State language of Latvia, and that its position has to be enforced, in practice, the OSCE makes opposite demands. Time upon time, Latvia has to make concessions. Before one concession has -been fully implemented, the next one follows. The Latvian language exam in the naturalization procedure was said to be too difficult, although it was already somewhat easier then the language exams of Finland and Sweden. For older people, a lighter exam ought to suffice. This too for invalids. Children bom in Latvia have be mentioned above. The language demands for truck drivers, counter clerks, doctors etc. were said to be too high. Therefore the reaction in Latvia is as follows. If Latvia drops the demand that everyone who is member of an elected administrative body has to speak Latvian at "the highest level"; what is then the next demand of Mr. Stoudmann and the OSCE? This could very well be that Latvia, in order to become a member of NATO and the EU, has to give Russians in elected administrative bodies translating facilities free of charge, because otherwise human rights are violated again. Latvia asked the OSCE to dismiss Mr. Stoudmann. Later on, Mr. Stoudmann issued a rectification. "ft was not his intentbn to suggest that the Russian language has to be the second state language. Furthermore he was fully aware of the sensitivities surrounding the issue in Latvia They are understandable in view of the country\'s history, and for the same reason I also understand, as I stated yesterday, the wish to strengthen the Latvian language as the state language and promote Its use by the whole population of Latvia." (Source: Press Statement, 21 March 2002; http:/\' /www.osce.org/news/generate.php3? newsjd=2364). In his rectification, Mr Stoudmann did not mention Russian or Soviet occupatton(s). Furthermore, he understands the wish to improve the position of the Latvian language. Thus there is no clear judgement that the OSCE and Mr Stoudmann himself completely support the improvement of the status of the Latvian language. It is possible that he can understand it, but does not agree with it. The Swiss citizen Gerard Stoudmann is director of the OSCE\'s Office for Democratic Institutions and Human Rights (ODIHR). That is the most important institute of the OSCE that promotes in the OSCE countries, the democracy and the rule of Law. However the Latvians -and not only theydo have a number of questions on the methods of the OSCE: 1) Is Switzerland, and the Swiss people, superior and Latvia and the Latvian people inferior, because Switzerland may refuse children born In Switzerland Swiss citizenship which, according to the OSCE, it is a serious violation of human rights if Latvia wish to do the same? And why? 2) Why are the violations of the human rights (for example on language) of Latvians not investigated and condemned? According to the OSCE the human rights of Russian have to be protected and the ones of the Latvians have not? 3) Why does the OSCE, with its noble ideals on democracy and the rule of Law, human rights and observance of international treaties, act so often in violation with these ideals by covering up and denying the Russian occupations of Latvia? 4) How often has the OSCE also condemned publicly in Latvia the Russian occupations of Latvia and how often has the OSCE pointed out publicly to stateless persons in Latvia that they, due to Article 49 of the Geneva Convention dating from 1949, must return to their own countries and that it is very generous of Latvia that they are allowed to stay? 5) Does the OSCE think that its methods have improved and deepened the position of democracy and the State of Law In the Latvian society; and have improved the integration of the minorities? A dear answer of these questions and as how the OSCE can implement them in practice, would be very illuminating; not only for Latvia but also for all citizens of the member states (countries) of the OSCE. The same is true for people outside Europe, those who are adherents of democracy and the rule of Law. Dr. R.W. Reuderink, Balticum-expert Former chairman of the Dutch-Baltic Society', 'LOCAL COMMUNISTS NOT LIKED IN LATVIA There is ample evidence on hand that another purge of highest Government officials in the occupied Latvia has been ordered by Moscow. This time even Communists of the "Old Guard" are not being spared: Cabinet Ministers, high party officials are replaced with Russian-born newcomers, some of which hare never lived in Latvia before. The signal for the purge was given by an article in CINA (Sept. 8, 1950), the organ of the CP., entitled "The Battle Task of the Republican Party Organization Is the Struggle Against the Bourgeois Nationalists," and appearing above the signature of Arvīds Pelshe, a secretary of the Latvian CP. Pelshe says that recently nationalist tendencies had manifested themselves in the most widely differing sections of public life. "American imperialist agencies have planted their spies and diversionists in the midst of Latvian, nationalists." After the victory over Germany part of the Latvian nationalists had escaped abroad in order to serve their Anglo-American masters, while others stayed in the country and are conspiring underground in accordance with Western directives. These underground enemies engage in banditism, spreading of rumours, attempts to sow hatred against the great Russian nation. The nationalists put all their hopes on the Anglo – American imperialists who are expected to restore \\ bourgeois Latvia after a war. The nationalists praise the former regime, exaggerate national characteristics. The Social Democrats or Mensheviks are as bad as the bourgeois nationalists. Pelshe blames "our cadres" for not recognizing the bourgeois nationalist danger, for not combating it properly and goes on to point out the greatest sinners: "Owing to the negligence of the minister for forestry Sakss (a Latvian from Russia. Ed.) and even with his direct support, the apparatus of his ministry proved filled with various people hostile to the Soviet regime." The academy of sciences and its institutes have been a bourgeois nationalist centre, especially the microbiological institute headed by Professor A. Kirchensteins (who is also chairman of Latvia\'s Supreme Soviet. Ed.). Actually the institute is said to be managed by the enemy of the people Oshins; * aa former deputy manager of the Public Health Board, Skaja, is working there as scientific secretary. Communists and Russian comrades are not admitted to work at the institute. Many facts unacceptable to the Soviet spirit have been discovered at other institutes, e.g., the institute for architecture (headed by Professor E. Stalbergs, formerly of Riga University), the institute for zootechnics and zoohygiene (headed by Professor Paul Lejinsh, chairman of the academy of sciences, formerly professor of the Riga University), etc. The institute for history and material culture (director K. Strazdinsh, minister of education at the time of writing. Ed.) has not fulfilled the charge of the Party—to prepare a Marxist textbook of Latvia\'s history; the institute for economics (director N. Kovalevski. Ed.) has not organized its rectives of the Party. Academician Endselins (a renowned authority on Baltic and Slavic philology. Ed.) and his school preach reactionary bourgeois conceptions in philology and do everything to emphasise the national differences and weaken the ties be-, tween the Russian and Latvian peoples. The periodical LATVIJAS PSR ZINĀTŅU AKADĒMIJAS VĒSTIS (Communications of the Latvian Academy of Sciences) has published articles introducing nationalist ideas on the sly, e.g., an article by P. Berzkalns on the setting up of household buildings Jin the centre of the kolkhoz "Nākotne," which doubts the application of the experiences of other republics in Latvia. Editor Kadeks (Professor M. Kadeks, vice-chairman of the academy of sciences, Latvian from Russia. Ed.) has overlooked the nationalist tendencies of this article. Kārlis Egle (a librarian and literary historian. Ed.) gets bamed for an article on the "persecution of progressive books," for confining himself to the persecution of "progressive" Latvian books under the Tsarist regime and neglecting to point out that also Russian books were persecuted in Latvia and that prosecution existed not only under the Tsars, but in "bourgeois" Latvia as well. Pelshe reveals that the leaders iof the academy of sciences, Kaļdeks, Peive (Professor Janis Peive, secretary of the academy, chairman of the society for the dissemination of political knowledge, a Latvian from Russia. Ed.) and Kirchensteins have been rebuked for their irresponsible Attitude to their work and lack of vigilance and that work in the academy of sciences is being reorganized. An institution which also comes in for a good deal of blame is the 10-years music school of the State Conservatory. The school (under Fricis Rokpeilis, a second rate author and Communist since 1940. Ed.) is said to have been a regular stronghold of bourgeois nationalist counter-revolutionaries, where an unbridled agitation was conducted against the Soviet regime. Pelshe winds up by saying: "We have not defeated nationalism yet and therefore it is necessary incessantly and in the genuine Bolshevik manner to conduct the struggle against all I the manifestations of this reactionary, counter-revolutionary, bourgeois ideology. Bolshevik maturity and political severity must not be reckoned and evaluated according to the fulfilment of every-day economic tasks, but according to the struggle against the excrescences of nationalism, against the activities of the Anglo-American agencies which act under "the national flag." Not only the nationalists must be unmasked, but also the liberals who offer them cover." The purge of government officials began at the very top: by a decree of the presidium of Latvia\'s Supreme Soviet, dated Sept. 12, 1950, the vice-premier of Latvia, Serghei Nikolaievich Gulanitski, has been relieved of his office "for failure to organize and effectively to control small river transport" (CINA, No. 217). As late as July last year Gulanitski was decorated with the highest order of the USSR, the Order of Lenin. No successor was appointed to Gulanitski. By a decree of October 4, the presidium of Latvia\'s Supreme Soviet relieved Foreign Minister Pēteris Valeskalns of his office and appointed in his stead Janis Ostrovs, hitherto deputy prime minister and chairman for several cultural committees, including the Song Festival/ Committee. P. Valeskalns came to Latvia in 1940, together with the Soviet occupational forces. After the liquidation of Minister for Educa-. tion Jūlijs Lacis he superceded him, retreated to Russia in 1941 when the Germans occupied Latvia and returned in 1944, to become Minister for Foreign Affairs. At the outbreak of the October Revolution he was a student at the Moscow University with natural science as his subject. He graduated with a degree in biology and Darwinism and also went through a 6-months\' political course for professors. Besides being Latvian minister for education in 1940, he was also professor at the Riga University. His Latvian colleagues described him as very ignorant in his subject. As a private person he had good manners and was not particularly nasty to anybody. It was rumoured that his Russian wife was a former aristocrat and that owing to the well-bred manners he had acquired from her, he was appointed Foreign Minister as fit to be shown to the world at large. By a decree of the presidium of the Latvian Supreme Soviet, dated Sept. 30, the hitherto minister for education, Karlis Strazdinsh has been relieved of his office. His successor is Vilis Samsons, a "candidate of philosophical sciences" (M.A.) and Hero of the Soviet Union. Strazdinsh, who was educated at the Teachers\' Seminary of Valmiera, was Commissar for Education of the Valka district during the Soviet occupation of 1917, went to Russia after the defeat of the Bolsheviks by the Latvian forces, took a Red professor\'s course in Moscow, was then active in education in the town of Molotov. In 1941 he came to Latvia and became deputy minister for education under Valeskalns. He was a quiet, hard-working and generally pleasant man. Vilis Samsons is designated as hero of the Soviet Union (one of the four or five in Latvia). He comes from Russia and nothing is known of his antecedents. However, he has published an article in the literary monthly KAROGS, describing the activities of pro-Soviet partisans in Latvia (actually almost non-existent. Ed.) in 1942-1944. Until his nomination he was professor at the Republican Party school of the CC. of the Latvian CP. About his partisan activities the Riga Radio said on February 5, 1945: "All the time Samsons\' brigade included 150 partisans who altogether killed 15,000 Germans. No Samson partisan Has fallen in action and there were no casualties, although several punitive expeditions were sent out against them. The brigade has derailed 118 enemy trains . . ."']

Name: 74_russian_chechnya_naval_submarine
Representative_Docs: ['CHICAGO LATVIAN NEWSLETTER In a statement released on December 15, Baltic-American organizations called on the Russian Federation and Chechnya to end fighting in and around the breakaway Russian republic, loacted in the Caucusus Mountains north of Georgia, and resume the non-violent conflict resolution which was abandoned prematurely on December 13. The text of the statement follows: As Baltic-Americans, we call on Russia to end it\'s siege of Chechnya, and we implore both groups to return to the negotiation table. The armed conflict between the Russian Federation and the self-declared independent state of Chechnya will not only result in mass human suffering, it will also pre-clude a lasting and just peace in the region. For over 150 years the Chechen people have been among those subjected to systematic genocide at the hands of Russia and the Soviet Union because their language, culture, and religion differ from that of Russia\'s. The use of large-scale force by the Russian Federation against the statistically overwhelmed Chechen people is unconscionable and should be internationally denounced. The only possibility for attaining a just and lasting peace in light of the historical realities of the Caucasus region is through bilateral negotiations, and if required, international mediation. We urge both Russia and Chechnya to resolve this conflict peacefully. The statement was issued by the Joint Baltic American National Committee on behalf of the American Latvian Association, Inc., the Estonian American National Council, Inc., and the Lithuanian American Council, Inc. * * * During a December 21 meeting between Baltic-American leaders and National Security Council Senior Director for Russian, Ukrainian and Eurasian Affairs R. Nicholas Burns, the U.S. policy towards the armed conflict underway in Balts Call For Peace In Chechnya; U.S. Declares Invasion "Internal Matter" January 1995 – Vol. XV, No. 1 Chechnya was described as differing from that pursued in reference to the Baltic countries. The U.S. considers Chechnya to be an integral part of the Russian Federation, and as a result considers Russian actions there to be a purely internal matter for Russia to deal with. As noted in the media, the U.S. has also called on Russia to avoid taking any action that could lead to substantial bloodshed and loss of life, and for Russian and Chechen officials to return to the negotiating table at the earliest possible time. * * * The Russian military is bombarding residential neighborhoods with fragmentation and cluster bombs designed to maim and kill, according to the latest news from Chechnya. This bombardment is taking place mainly under cover of darkness, according to some critics, in order to terrorize the population into turning on the President of the Chechen Republic, former Soviet Air Force General Dzhokar Dudayev, who was sympathetic to cause of Baltic independence while commanding an air force division in Estonia in the late 1980 s and early 1990 s. The failure of the Russian terror campaign was demonstrated on December 20 when, according to RFE, 10,000 Chechen responded to a call by Dudayev to join a human chain on the highway from Grozny, the Chechen capitol, to the neighboring republic of Dagestan. The demonstration was highly reminiscent of the "Baltic Way" human chain from Tallinn through Riga to Vilnius of August 23, 1989. Chechen efforts to draw parallels with the Baltic drive for independence may be one reason for the shrill accusations of Baltic meddling now being made by Russian officials. On December 20, Sergei Stepashin, director of the Russian Counterintelligence Service (formerly the KGB), announced that "Latvian snipers" were fighting against Russian troops in Chechnya, a report denied by the Latvian Defense Ministry on December 22. The denial of Latvian Defense Force involvement did not stop President Yeltsin from declaring, on December 26, that "Azerbaidjani and Baltic mercenaries" were fighting with Dudayev forces in Chechnya. All three Baltic legislatures have adopted resolutions condemning the bloodshed associated with the Russian invasion of Chechnya and have called on both parties to cease hostilities and resume negotiations. In addition, a Chechen Support Group has been formed by several Latvian parliamentarians. #', 'CHICAGO LATVIAN NEWSLETTER "Diplomacy is the Continuation of War by Other Means" On August 19, Latvians around the world looked back on that fateful day two years ago, when Russian armored personnel carriers stormed through the streets of Riga in an echo of the abortive putsch attempt taking place in the Soviet capital of Moscow. Since the failure of the putsch, the Baltic governments have taken huge steps towards reestablishing the sovereignty and independence that Soviet tanks robbed from them more than fifty years ago. The Soviet Union, which occupied the Baltics, is no more, having been replaced by 12 new nations, each moving at its own speed to establish their own independence. The days of repression, fear and intimidation seemed over—the former Soviet Union was much too busy trying to emerge from the rubble of a crumbled empire to bother with old dreams and desires. So it seemed. Today we know better. Russia, which has selectively declared itself the direct successor to the Soviet state, has made clear its wish to take up also the renewal of a greater Russian empire. In many parts of the former empire, it is doing so by stirring up armed conflict, backing one faction against another in bitter civil wars now raging in the Central Asian republics, the Transcaucasus republics and Moldova. In Ukraine, Russian lawmakers make demands for the return of Ukrainian territory while the governments play an upwardly spiraling game of "chicken over the former Soviet Black Sea fleet. However, armed intervention by the new bosses in Moscow is not taking place in Latvia, Estonia and Lithuania. Are these three countries any less threatened? Clearly, the Baltic states remain of interest to those planning to reconstitute the Soviet/ Russian empire, people like Russian Vice President Alexander Rutskoi and legislators Ruslan Hasbulatov and Sergei Stankevich. How else to explain the insidious, ongoing campaign concerning the alleged violation of the "human rights" of ethnic Russians in the Baltic states? September 1993 – Vol. XIII, No. 7 What other meaning can there be to the suspension of Russian troop withdrawal from Lithuania, where only 2,500 active duty soldiers remain? How else to interpret the hero\'s welcome in Moscow given to recently extradited former Riga "Black Beret" deputy commander Sergei Parfyonov, who was convicted by a Latvian court of involvement in the events of both January and August 1991, where a one-hour interview was broadcast on the Russian parliament\'s television channel? Russia seems bent on using diplomacy, veiled threats and public relations tactics to achieve in Latvia, Estonia and Lithuania what it is using tanks and guns to achieve elsewhere in the former Soviet Union-the renewal of Russian domination over the area its leaders continue to call the "near abroad". And, it seems, the American government, led by the American media, is more than willing to lend its collective credibility to these efforts. The Baltics continue to take a pounding on editorial pages over their citizenship policies and over other laws, which are collectively referred to as "human rights" issues. This, despite 15 multilateral investigations of the rights situation in the Baltic countries, which have found the Baltic governments blameless of violations of human rights. The fact that individual transgressions do take place against laws and procedures set by these governments is, to Russian eyes, a sufficient reason to tar them with the same brush used by the international community for nations like Myanmar (Burma) and Cuba. That such prominent columnists as Kremlinologist Dmitri Simes feel free to lend credence to Moscow\'s pleas of disappointment "that the Clinton team expressed concern over the slow pace of withdrawal of the few Russian troops remaining in Latvia and Estonia, but would not publicly criticize the disenfranchisement of the vast majority of Russian-speaking residents living there" (The Washington Post, 08/10/93) gives U.S. Government officials all the leverage they need to state that "ethnic Russians are not being treated fairly in Lithuania and the other Baltic states" (The Washington Post, 08/24/93). There is an old saying about men, or in this case governments, that live in glass housesthat they should be careful what stones they throw. About 200,000 ethnic Latvians live in Russia, 100,000 of them in the Siberian regions of Omsk and Tomsk. Are these people receiving the same minority rights the Latvian government has granted its minorities? No Latvianlanguage schools exist in these areas to educate this minority group. No Latvian books or newspapers are published in these areas, as opposed to the wide range of Russian-language materials available in Latvia. Ethnic Latvians wishing to reactivate their Latvian citizenship are being harassed and frustrated by Russian tax and property laws. Russia claims to have the interests of the Russian minorities in the Baltic countries in mind when it complains of discrimination and resists troop withdrawal. However, the Russian policy of preventing the completion of troop withdrawal from the Baltics is keeping many military officers and their families trapped on plundered military bases, with no heat, fuel or food. These people want nothing more to return to Russia and find a roof over their heads and food to eat. If this is how Russia treats its own defenders, how can the world take seriously its protestations of mistreatment? The only language the Russian government seems to understand is that of force—in this case, the force of a threatened cutoff of American foreign aid if the Russian and Baltic governments do not reach agreements on troop withdrawal, with definite dates of completion, by October 6, 1993. Both Russia and Lithuania seem to be moving back to the negotiating table. How successful, however, can these negotiations be if the Lithuanian side can no longer trust the Russian side to live up to its bargains? Troop withdrawal negotiations are also at a standstill with Latvia and Estonia. Latvia cannot get a response to its request that negotiations be resumed on August 29. The reason: the Russian side pleads "technical difficulties" involving delegation members out on vacation. It strains credibility to state that delegation members would be unavailable, especially at a time when desperately needed aid could be placed in jeopardy by American law and Russian inaction-or worse. What can we, as concerned Americans, do in this situation? I believe it is time to dust the cobwebs off of our press contact lists and renew old friendships with congressional offices. Although a major battle in the renewal of Baltic independence was fought, and won, in the late August days of 1991, the war against Baltic independence started by Nazi Germany and Soviet Russia on August 23, 1939 has yet to end. The legacy of the Molotov-Ribbentrop Pact continues to haunt Latvia, Estonia and Lithuania, in the form of Russian desires to reestablish its "sphere of influence" over the Baltic countries. It is time to once again start calling our Congressmen and Senators, the White House and the State Department, and the local newspaper editor and television reporter, to remind them about America\'s promises, and America\'s legal obligations, to the Baltic countries. I believe it was the German statesman yon Clausewitz that said "diplomacy is the continuation of war by other means". Today, we see the truth of this saying in Russia\'s attempts to win the upper hand in Latvia, Estonia and Lithuania. We, as people who care about the Baltic countries and their people, cannot and must not permit America to abandon the Baltics just when they need them most. □', 'According to the latest issue of "Fighting Ships," Russia is reported at present to have 37 0 submarines in active service, 120 under construction, while a new building programme which has been launched makes provision lor a further 1000 underwater craft. Britain has 53 submarines on the active list and another 18 on immediate reserve. A statement on the strength of the United States fleet shows 194 submarines, but it is estimated that by the end of 1952 only 100 submarines will be on the active list. In the foreword, it is pointed out by Jane\'s that the trend by the principal naval Powers during 1951 has continued towards the improvement of aircraft carriers and in the offensive against submarines. Larger aircraft carriers are being constructed so as to be capable of operating bigger and more effective aircraft, and smaller aircraft carriers for antisubmarine warfare. Large destroyers are also being adapted as submarine "killers" of a fleet of anti-submarine escorts, while smaller destroyers are being converted into antisubmarine frigates. Jane\'s describes the new Royal Navy anti-submarine frigates ReWorld\'s Submarine Fleet lentless and Rosket, as a new conception of submarine "killer" ships. Two other destroyers, Rapid and Roebuck, are being similarly converted. They have completenew armament. Great use has been made of aluminium. In discussing the fleets of the various Powers, Jane\'s says that it is extremely difficult to obtain information regarding the Russian navy, and emphasises that details of new construction in Russia are presented with the greatest reserve as no official confirmation can be obtained. The strength of the Russian navy is given as 520,000, excluding the navy flying corps. Royal Navy officers and ratings now number 143,500, not including naval air services, while the strength cf the United States navy is 705,000 officers and ratings. The marine corps is not included in the United States figures. The new programme of Russian naval construction is reported to include three battleships, 20 cruisers, 120 destroyers, and 1000 sea-going submarines. Construction of one to four battleships of a new powerful type in Russian dockyards is persistently reported. These ships, it is said, will have a displacement of between 42,000 and 45,000 tons — equal in size to the Royal Navy\'s biggest battleship, Vanguard, and the United States battleship Missouri. One of these new Russian ships named The Soviet Union, is re» ported to have carried out trials late this year. This class is said to be equipped with catapult towers for firing radio-controlled aerial torpedoes, and rocket-firing apparatus and radio-controlled guns. These vessels are also believed to carry very elaborate radar installations and anti-aircraft and anti-submarine protection devices. No Russian aircraft carriers are listed by Jane\'s, although Russia is known to have a strong naval air force. Russian submarines listed by Jane\'s vary from long-range 1500-tonners to small two-men craft. Britain\'s whole naval programme, says Jane\'s, is largely directed towards the underwater menace. The United States new naval construction programme includes a giant aircraft carrier, which will be the biggest in the world, two ocean escorts, seven submarines, 106 mine sweeping boats, and about 100 other vessels. The United States navy at present comprises about 5000 vessels, including 120 aircraft carriers, 15 battleships, 69 cruisers, 384 destroyers, 263 escort destroyers and frigates and 194 submarines. Less than a quarter of these are on the active list. . Jane\'s says that by the end of 1952, the United States active fleet will have three very large aircraft carriers, nine fleet aircraft carriers, four battleships, 19 cruisers, 340 destroyers and escort vessels, and 100 submarines. The United States navy will soon have in service three different cruiser types of novel design. They will be submarine, hunters and "killers." France and the Netherlands also are building powerful antisubmarine units, and converting old vessels into submarine chasers. Jane\'s  however, that many of the rearmament measures in the navies of the United States, Britain, France, the Netherlands, and Italy, which have been engendered by the inter-1 national tension, are still largely in the project stage.']

Name: 77_pact_1939_soviet_psr
Representative_Docs: ['THE TORONTO STAR Glasnost unleashes debate in Baltic states By Ron Vastokas Political ideas seldom work out the way they are supposed to. When Mikhail Gorbachev unveiled his program of glasnost (openness) three years ago. he intended to expose the problems of Soviet society to public scrutiny, debate and reform. Today, glasnost has started challenging the very legitimacy of the Soviet empire. Nowhere is this challenge more apparent than in the Baltic states where it centres around a sensitive chapter in Soviet history. On Aug. 23. 1939. foreign ministers Vyaeheslav Molotov and Joachim Yon Hibbentrop signed a Soviet-German non-aggression pact in Moscow. Attacned to the * pact was a secret protocol that placed Estonia. Latvia and Lithuania within the "sphere of influence" of the Soviet Union. The pact gave Hitler the green light to march into Poland and start World War II and the secret protocol left Stalin a free hand to seek a "solution of the problem of the Baltic countries." The "solution" turned out to be a military occupation of the Baltic states and their incorporation into the Soviet Union. The West, including Canada. *has never recognized the seizure of the Baltic states as legitimate. The Soviet Union, on the other hand, has remained silent about Stalin\'s accord with the Nazis and has kept a tight lid on the subject of Baltic independence. With the coming ol" glasnost. however, these issues have become difficult to avoid. At a writers\' seminar last year Vilnius, the capital of Lithuania, emigre authors were told they could have their articles published in a state-run weekly fco long as the incorporation of Lithuania into the U.S.S.R. is not questioned. There are no taboos (j about other themes." And the Soviet president An-W viromyko put the official line on the subject more bluntly. On a visit to Vilnius in early February, a few days before the anniversary marking the independence of Lithuania, he said that Soviet power.in Lithuania "is stronger than ever" although "possibly there ore some lunatics in some places abroad who fail to understand that." Despite official threats. Baltic activists put glasnost to a test last year and staged demonstrations on Aug. 23 in Vilnius, Riga and Tallinn. Speakers denounced the Hitler-Stalin pact, commemorated mass deportations of the earlyyears ol Soviet occupation and raised the question of self-determination. As today\'s 49th anniversary of the pact approached, there were renewed calls for demonstrations to repudiate it. A remarkably outspoken "appeal" signed by 25 Lithuanian activists described the pact as an instrument by which the Baltie States "became new colonies of Russian imperialism." These nationalist aspirations have received support from activists in Moscow. The shortlived Democratic Union that challenged the supremacy of the Communist party on May 8, also called for the withdrawal of Soviet torces from eastern Europe jnd the Baltic States. The most reeen! development is the publication and broadcast (Aug. 12) of the Stalin-Hitler pact un state-run media in Estonia. Activists who have been pressing for an independent Estonia say that this first official disclosure of the secret protocols portends a change in Kremlin policy. The full text of the pact was also made public in the Lithuanian News on Aug. 5. What is surprising about these developments is the aijeed with which they ore taking place, the blunt language that is being used and the expanding range of critiJ. Lancers Latvijas PSR Augstākās Padomes Prezidijs lecēlis J. Lanceru par Latvijas PSR Ministru Padomes priekšsēdētāja vietnieku un Latvijas PSR Valsts celtniecības komitejas priekšsēdētāju. * * * Jlnli Lancer* dzimis 1933. qadl. PSKP biedrs, latvietis, augstāka izglītība. J. Lancers strādā Latvijas PSR Celtniecības ministrijas sistēmā no 1958. gada. Viņi nogājis ce|u no meistara līdz ministra vietniekam. Kops 1986. gada viņi ir Latvijas PSR celtniecības ministra pirmais vietnieks. * * * Latvijas PSR Augstākās Padomes Prezidijs atbrīvojis Ņ. Deoisenko no Latvijas PSR Ministru Padomes priekšcism, which now includes the Communist party. Mounting public pressure has led to an unprecedented official permission to hold a rally in Vilnius today to commemorate the signing of the non-aggression pact between Germany and the Soviet Union. The pact is but one of a number of issues that deal with economic and cultural autonomy, the dangers posed to national survival by Russification and the worsening state of the environment. Up to now, the Kremlin\'s response has been somewhat ambiguous, giving no clear indication as to how Gorbachev intends to cope with the rising tide to national assertiveness. There appears to be an uneasy stand-off as Soviet authorities allow some national sentiments to be aired and then selectively crack down, in the heavy-handed manner of the past, on those who organize or steer them. At the same time. Gorbachev has said that "many of the problems the Soviet Union is facing now are a result of a too harsh and insensitive nationalities policy in the past." and has promised to deal "very substantially" with this "most fundamental and vital question." Whether Gorbachev is thinking of forcible seizure of sovereign territory when he speaks of a "harsh policy in the past" is not clear. But the Baits are no longer watting to find out. They are telling Gorbachev they know what happened 50 years ago and that they want amends. It is ironic that glasnost. meant to restore the legitimacy of the Soviet state and party, has now placed the initiative in the hands of the people. □ Ron Vastokas is Associate Professor of Anthropology at Trent University. On September 1, 1939, Lithuania declared her neutrality in the war that broke out between Germany and Poland, and on October 10, 1939, under the pressure of war circumstances, she concluded the Treaty of Mutual Assistance, with the USSR, the Preamble of which ouce again re-affirmed the principles of respect for national independence and- non-interference in internal affairs, and recalled the Treaty of Peace of 1920, and the Pact of Non-Aggression of 1926, with the provisions for the peaceful solution of conflicts. The infamous Molotov-Ribbentrop deal of August 23, 1939. amended by the secret Soviet-German Protocol of September 28, 1939, and the subsequent occupation of Lithuania, June 15, 1940, by the Soviet armed forces was an act of unprovoked aggression perpetrated for the political aims of the USSR, and a flagrant violation of all bilateral and international treatries and conventions binding both States. By this aggression the Soviet Government committed an international crime and, thus entire responsibility rests with the Soviet Union, obliging it to re-establish the violated right and repair the damage caused. The one-sided anu forcible incorporation of Lithuania into the soviet Union having taken place under illegal conditions and not having conferred any rights to the latter, the situation existing hefore the occupation and the socalled incorporation remains valid, and the legal status of Lithuania is the same as she enjoyed before the occupation. Lithuania remains a member of the interantional community and is subject to rights and obligations. All treaties concluded by Lithuania with other States remain in force and are binding on both parties. Lithuanian laws still remain in force and international tribunals in cases where one of both parties are Lithuanian citizens, apply— according to the rules of private interantional law — Lithuanian laws. The Lithuanian diplomatic and consular representatives accredited to foreign governments continue to function and assume the defense of the rights and interests of Lithuania and of its citizens. Only the exercise of the sovereign rights of Lithuania is temporarily suspended in Lithuania, by the fact of military occupation (occupatio bellica). Wherever ascendency by force ceases, the Lithuanian authorities legally exercise this power. All the States, except those which had adhered to the Axis and those within the Soviet sphere of influence, have refused to recognise the legality of the act of Soviet rule in Lithuania. Thus, the Soviet Union is an illegal occupant of the Lithuanian State and a fraudulent usurper of the inalienable rights of the Lithuanian people. As long as this situation will last, til: Gf,\'-ernment of the Soviet Union is bound by the law of nations which governs the rights and duties of the occupant State. Such acts as (a) forcible sovietisation of the country; (b) extension of the territory of the Lithuanian State; Soviet Union legislation to the (c) the Soviet electoral law and the compulsion exerted on the population of the occupied country to particpate in the elections of the constitutional organs of Soviet Russia; (d) the forcible recruitment of Lithuanian youth in the armed forces of the USSR- (c) the spoliation of the country and population for the benefit of the I Russian State; (f) the forcible russification of the younger generation; (g) physical annihilation of the enslaved nation by means of torture, murder and mass deportation into Siberia and the Arctic Regions of the Soviet Lmon—all that constitutes a continuous and flagrant contravention of this law. a By signing the Charter of the United Nations and later by adhering to the Universal Declaration of Human Rights—the Convention on Prevention and Pv> ishment of the Crime of Genocii lias equally been signed by the Soviet Government but not yet ratified — the government of the Soviet Union has assumed some explicit understandings in regards HUMAN FREEDOM IS BEING CRUSHED , to their citizens, namely: (a) an individual\'s right to life, freedom and personal security (Art. 3 of the Universal Declaration of Human Rights); (b) an individual\'s right to human treatment (Art. 5); (c) an individual\'s right to conditions enabling him, in the case of arrest and trial, to defend himself in a public process (Art. 11, I); (d) the principle according to which nobody can be condemned for actions which, at the moment they were committed, did not constitute an offense from the viewpoint of the national or international law (Art. 11, II); (c) an indiviual\'s right to freely choose his residence inside a country (Art. 13, I), and his right to return to his country (Art. 13, II); (f) the right to freely found one\'s family (Art. 16); (g) the right to manifestly confess one\'s religion (Art. 18.) A fortiori and implicitly the Government of the Soviet Union is bound to grant and to respect these rights and fundamental freedoms in regard to the citizens of the occupied Lithuanian State. 11. Realities in Occupied Lithuania. A. —Contravention Against The Provisions Of The UN Charter And The Law Of Nations In General. The pre-arranged aggression against Lithuania and the two other Baltic States has been carried on by the Government of the USSR at a time when the political and military equilibrium of Europe collapsed, France being defeated and Paris occupied by Hitler\'s armored divisions. These circumstances permitted the rulers of the Kremlin not only to invade the Baltic States but also to stage their incorporation into the Soviet Union. Sovietisation followed. Sovietisation or Soviet colonialism means, on one hand, the enslavement of men and their transporation into Soviet mass man by (a) spoliation and annihilation of all educated and comparatively well-to-do classes of the nation; (b) non-respect of all human rights and fundamental freedoms; (c) destruction of families; (d) breaking up national traditions; i ( (c) the communist indoctrination of the younger generation; territory of the Lithuanian state; (f) uprooting the people, and, on the other hand, the ruthless and reckless exploitation of the economic resources of the occupied country and its manpower for the benefit of the Russian State and its armament program. The Soviet rule in enslaved Lithuania Is but a mockery of all principles of the law of nations, of the provisions of the United Nations charter, and of the established principles of international morality. B. —Soviet Crimes Against Humanity — Crime Of Genocide. 1. Mass Murders. The arrests, deportations and liquidation of individual Lithuanians were started at the beginning of the Soviet occupation. It is but fitting that the following cases of mass murders of Lithuanians by the NKVD, NKGB, and MGB respectfully, must be recordedJune 22-27, 1941. A spontaneous and general uprising of the Lithuanian people against the communist oppressor took place in Lithuania on June 2 3, 1941, following the outbreak of hostilities between Germany and the Soviet Union. All larger cities were cleared of Russian garrisons, and the restoration of Lithuania\'s independence was proclaimed. At some places the Soviet administration was overwhelmed so suddenly that even the agents of NKVD and NKGB had to flee without taking or destroying their archives. Thus a number of top secret NKVD and NKGB files were seized by the Lithuanian insurrectionists in Kaunas. Siauliai, Alytus and other cities. These documents, some on the stationery of the Moscow headquarters of these nefarious institutions, revealed the inner operation of the NKVD-NKGB forces which maintain the Communist Party in power and extend Russian control over non-Russian areas and countries. Here are some of these documents pertaining to the mass deportations from Lithuania: (1) Order No. 0054 of November 28, 1940 of the nominal head of the NKVD for Lithuania, Guzevicius, directing the listing of all "people\'s enemies, including all non-Communists and aliens." Twice this document refers to the Order No. 00122 3 of the NKVD of the USSR, dated: 11th October, 1939. That proves that Moscow Headquarters of the NKVD had issued Its first order directing the preparations for mass liquidation of "the anti-Soviet and anti-social elements" of the Baltic States at a time when Lithuania, Latvia and Estonia were independent, but shortly after the signature of the second secret Molotov-Ribbentrop Proto. col which sealed the fate of Lithuania. Treaty ofNonaggression Between Germany and the Union of Soviet Socialist Republics The Government of the German Reich and the Government of the Union of Soviet Socialist Republics desirous of strengthening the cause of peace between Germany and the U.S.S.R., and proceeding from the fundamental provisions of the Neutrality agreement concluded in April 1926 between Germany and the U.S.S.R., have reached the following agreement: ARTICLE I Both High Contracting Parties obligate themselves to desist from any act of violence, any aggressive action, and any attack on each other, either individually or jointly with other powers. ARTICLE II Should one of the High Contracting Parties become the object of belligerent action by a third power, the other High Contracting Party shall in no manner lend its support to this third power. ARTICLE 111 The Governments of the two High Contracting Parties shall in the future maintain continual contact with one another for the purpose of consultation in order to exchange information on problems affecting their common interests. ARTICLE IV Neither of the two High Contracting Parties shall participate in any grouping of powers whatsoever that is directly or indirectly aimed at the other party. ARTICLE V Should disputes or conflicts arise betwen the High Contracting Parties over problems of one kind or another, both parties shall settle these disputes or conflicts exclusively through friendly exchange of opinion or. if necessary, through the establishment of arbitration commissions. ARTICLE VI The present treaty is concluded for a period of ten years, with the proviso that, in so far as one of the High Contracting Parties does not denounce it one year prior to the expiration of this period, the validity of this treaty shall automatically be extended for another five years. ARTICLE VII The present treaty shall be ratified within the shortest possible time. The ratifications shall be exchanged in Berlin. The agreement shall enter into force as soon as it is signed. Done in duplicate, in the German and Russian languages. Moscow, August 23, 1939. In the event of a territorial and political rearrangement in the areas belonging to the Baltic States (Finland. Estonia, latvia, Lithuania), and northern boundary of Lithuania shall represent the boundary of the spheres of influence of Germany and the U.S.S.R. In this connection the interest of Lithuania in the Vilna area is recognized by each party. 2. In the event of a territorial and political rearrangement of the areas belonging to the Polish state the spheres of influence of Germany and the U.S.S.R. shall be bounded approximately by the line of the rivers Narew, Vistula, and San. The question of whether the interests of both parties make desirable the maintenance of an independent Polish state and how such a state should be bounded can only be definitely determined in the course of further political developments. In any event both Governments will resolve this question by means of a friendly I agreement. v s STATE DEPARTMENT 3. ARTICLE V This treaty shall be ratified and the ratifications shall be exchanged in Berlin as soon as possible. The treaty becomes effective upon signature. Representative_Docs: ['AUSTRALIANS FIGHTING IN MALAYA Many Australian tin and rubber company employees in Malaya are now fighting an open war agiinst banditr and Communist guerrillas. Tin mining dredges and rubber plantations are equipped with armored cars, machine -gun posts and improvises pill-boxes. In Sydney this week, officials of tin and rubber companies Said Australia\'s stake in Malaya was worth more than £16,000,000. Chinese guerrillas were en – dangering one-third of the world\'s tin supply and nearly half the world\'s raw rubber production. A senior official of one of the 34 tin mining companies registered in Australia said:"Our staff is working in conditions of open warfare. "We dare not mention their names because of reprisals. "Companies are spending thousands of pounds arming their staffs and fortifying their homes. "Compounds are surrounded with barbed wire, lit by searchlights and guarded by constantly manned pill-boxes. "The British Government provides armed Malay police but nothing else. "We are trying to convince the Governments of both Britain and Australia that major action against the guerrillas is imperative now. "But they do not seem to be taking much interest." Since July, 1948, Malayan terrorists have lost 1126 killed and more than 1300 captured or wounded. Terrorists have killed 323 police, 154 soldiers and 803 civilians. There are 11,000 British troops in Malaya and 3500 Malayan Regiment volunteers. This Goverment force is to be strengthened by the addition of 2000 Gurkhas from Hongkong. In London last week, Colonial Secretary James Griffiths said the Government "had not overlooked the possibility of Commonwealth countries helping in the campaign against Malayan terrorists." Another mining company official said yesterday he thought there should be w ar time censorship and martial law throughtout Malaya. "It is scandalous that we cannot speak openly about our defensive precautions, even in Australia," he said. "But if we do, we know that whatever area is mentioned will be next on the guerrillas\' reprisal list. "Tin production has not suffered greatly since the war, but we have been unable to dc any prospecting. "Established tin mining fields will eventually be exhausted and that will be the end of tin production. "One of our branches started prospecting a new field recently. The men were warned to stop. "There was no sense In risking their lives In an ambush. They stopped prospecting." Sydney Stock Exchange research bureau said this week that Australian investors were interested in 34 tin mining firms and seven rubber concerns in Malaya. Total capital of these was £8,300,00 with a present market value of about £15,000,000. Rubber investments totalled £276,000 paid up capital with a share market value of £113,000. Malayan gold raining interests amounted to £LOO,OOO and other Australian interest in Singapore had a total capital of £950,000. Tin production in Malayf was 4890 metric tons for Oct ober last year. This was one third of world output. Raw rubber production was 50 to 60,000 metric tont a month last year – nearly half of the world\'s monthly output. Both these products make Malaya one of the chief dollarearning countries in the British Commonwealth.
