In his speech introducing the bill to outlaw Communism, Mr.Menzies said: "The Australian Communist Party engages in activities or operations designed to bring abour the overthow or dislocation of the established syotec of government of Australia and the attainment o_f economical industrial or political ends by force, violence, intomidation , or fraudulent practices "No person familiar with modern history would require much proof of these allegations, but if it is needed it is available in abundance. "Stalin, in his book "Foundations of Leninism", said: "The dictatorship of the proletariat dees not arise on the basis of the bourgeois order;it arises while this order is being town down, after the overthrow of the bourgeoisie, in the process of the expropriation of the landlords and capitalists, during the process of socialisation of the principal instruments ana means of production, in the process of violent proletarian revolution. "The dictatorship of the proletariat is a revolutionary power based on violence against the bourgeoisie." As the Communists use the term it descrribes the middle c classes, the capitalists – great or small – the shopkeepers, the farmers, all those who are notwage-eamers, who, having no means of production of their own, are reduced to selling their labour power in order to live. These lstter are \'the proletariat. Dictator* Dilenima iven of the arrest of rubbei planation manager W,A.C. McNamee. McHamee, who operated a 4000-acre planation In the Jabor Valley, was released after several days* arrest. Malayan Police would not disclose the reason for his arrest. The police told McNamee he could stay in the Kuantan area where his plantation is. McNamee\'s firm, Bukit Mertajam Rubber Company said they did not know if McNamee couMbe employed in any other area. Frith zlm\'TJie Herali Clearly, we in this Parliament belong to the •bourgeaosie" as do many hundreds of thousands of other Australians dwelling peacefully in their homes and among their children. In this book, Stalin also says: •The dictatorship of the proletariat is the domination of the proletariat over the bourgeoisie, untrammelled by law and based on violence and enjoying the sympathy and support of the toiling and exploited masses.*1 Such quotations might be multiplied. But they are more than sufficient to establish, not only that modem Communism is prepared ro adopt force and violence and frauds but that these diabolic doctrines enjoy the gal will and are written on the ban ncr of the Australian Communist Party. The Australian Communist Party is an integral part of the World Communist revolutionary movement, which in the King\'s Q> minions and elsewhere, engages in espionage and sabotage, and in activities and operations of a treasonable and subversive nature, Communism is a world movement. In its principal home it is the primal mover of the worlds prexent disorders and want of peace, and the prime cause of the fears which now distract millions of peace-loving homes the world over. We would not have tolerated ?„,ifth column in Australia frca 1940 to 1945. We certainly do not propose to tolerate one in J950, at a time when militant fcommunism, checked for the time I —a Western Europe, is moving east and south—east to carry out its plans of puting down democracy and ushering in the revolution* Until Russia was brought into the recent war by Hitler\'s invasion, the Australian Communist was opposed to the war; did his best to sabotage it, and denounced it as an imperialist, capitalist struggle for supremacy. I hope no Australian will forget that right through the vital stages of the Battle for Britain, and at a time when ovr own troops were performing im – mortal exploits of courage and endurance in the Middle East,ths Australian Communist was our enemy, and that it was only when Russia came in that he decided that patriotism was to be his new line. When the war was over he reverted to type. He may get a few dupes of both sexes to sit on his plat – form, to attend his bogus peace congresses and to put out stu – pir and pernicious propaganda to the effect that the Soviet Unia is the only friend of peace,and that her territorial aggressioai of the last six years ought to be regarded merely as friendly gestures. But the truth is that once more he is creating economic cha os, social disorder, and weakening of democratic institutions as a pre-requisite to revolutionary seizure of State power."', 'A Nation in the Making AUSTRALIA ACHIEVES NATIONHOOD The Anzac landing at Gallipoli on April 25, 1915, made Australia a nation in the eyes of the world. She went into the! first world war as a part of the 1 British Empire, not even making a separate declaration of war; by 1 the time the Treaty of Versailles was signed in 1919, she had become an independent, self-assertive nation, even among the great powers of the world. It is true that after the war, Australia tended to go into isolation again, but the second world war proved to her finally that she was only a section of struggling humanity. Aiinough in the years before 1914 Australia had frequently made it clear that she resented interference from the British government, and wished to control her own army and navy, no Australian thought it surprising that she automatically entered the war against the German Empire at the same time as the moiner country. The British government had wisely kept Australia fully informed about the diplomatic moves by which she sought to prevent hostilities in Europe while at the same time maintaining her treaty obligations. "The crimson thread of kinship" proved far stronger than political squabbles, either within Australia or within the Empire, and both the Prime Minister (Cook) and the leader of the Opposition (Fisher), supported Britain without qualification. The outbreak of the first world war found Australia in the midst of a political crisis. On the request of the Cook Government, in strife with a Labor-controlled Senate, the Governor-General, Sir Ronald Munro-Ferguson had granted a double dissolution on July 30, only five days before Britain declared war on Germany. A bitterly fought election campaign followed during the next six weeks, so that Australia entered the war under conditions which might have caused uncertainty in foreign policy. But no one contradicted the Prime Minister when he said tht day after the double dissolution "Whatever happens, Austral k is part ot the Empire and is ir the Empire to the full; when th< Empire is at war, Australia v at war." Indeed, the leader of the Op position was equally emphatic "Should the worse happen aftei every aung has oeen done tha honor will permit, we Austral ians will help and defend thi mother country to our last mai and our last shilling." The Great War was not th< first in which Australian troop had fought alongside the Brit ish. In 1885 a contingent iron New South Wales had been sen to the Sudan and in 1900 a smal naval force had gone to China More serious had been Austral ian participation in the Boe War (1899-1902) in which 518 o our troops had been killed. There had been many oppo nents of Australia\'s part in thes campaigns, especially in Soutl Africa. But even during the hea of the election controversies, n responsible politician suggeste> that Australia should not joi: in the struggle against Germany It was a unanimity that came t a surprise to many, including th German government, which ha been led to believe from i 1 agents that British dominion would act independently, an jealously refuse to be forced int war. The glorious records of the Australian volunteer army in Gallipoli, France, Palestine, andļ eiswhere earned the respect of i the whole world. By the end of the war, Australia had enlisted 417,000 troops, sent 330,000 of I them overseas, suffered 227,000 casualties, and had lost nearly 60,000 dead. This was an amazing record, especially when it! is remembered that the Austral- ļ ian people twice refused by referendum to endorse conscription. W. M. Hughes Prime Minister Fisher resigned and his office was taken by his brilliant and energetic Attorney- i General, William Morris Hughes.! Hughes was a member of the Imperial War Cabinet, in Lonao.i and his forthright opinions on a number of vital issues came as a shock to many British.  He certainly made i it impossible for anyone to forget mat Australia was playing an important part in the war, and that her views must always be considered. Although very provocative and sometimes rather too dramatic, Hughes gained many points against Mr. Lloyd George, the great British war leader, just \'as Mr. Curtin differed from Mr. Churchill during the second \' world war when he believed that the best interests of Australia ļ were concerned. Both Hughes and Curtin proved that loyalty I and difference of opinion can exist together. A delegation from Australia, j led by Hughes, took part in the discussions which led to the j Peace oi Versailles in 1919. Again the fiery little Welshļ man crossed swords with great s statesmen, and he gained for Australia, probably against Jap| an, the mandadtes for what had been German New Guinea and , inc. island.al Kauxu. Australia, like the other Dom| inions, signed the Treaty of Versailles as a separate nation, although she had not declared v. ur independently in 1914. -She j was aiso given a place in the League of aNtions and the Internation Labor Office. Thus the whole world gave i legal recognition to the nationhood of Australia which had oeen proved and earned during the war. This had been done without destroying the British Empire though its character had altered very much and has drastically changed since then. It is now understood that the Dominions could enter into separate treat-1 ies, appoint their own ambassadors, and generally follow an ! independent foreign policy from 1 Great Britain if they so desired It is interesting to note thai [ Australia has moved slowest ir • this direction, the Anzus Pact be-  ing the only important treatj ■ which she has negociated outsid* ■ the framework of the British j i Commonwealth relationships. M Always resentful of any kinc of imposition, Australia has nevertheless \'ertheless kept very close to tht ! mother country. The ties 1 blood have made understanding t and harmony the most remark ļ able features of relationshir I Britain ever since Federation. 1 It would not be right, how • ever to ignore another opinior J about the effect of the firs – ļ world war on the developmen • of Australian nationhood. It i; s expressed by Professor G. V • ļ Portus in his excellent pam phlet "Britain and Australia 3 (Longmans). He writes: "It is an exaggeration to sa> that Australia became a natioi at Gallipoli. Nations are no born in a day—not even in . day of battle. "There were many who sai< that the 1914-18 war brough Australia into world affairs an< taught her her place in th • comity of nations. But in trutl 3 the war had very little perman ent effect on the Australia: external outlook. The pre-wa belief that Britain and th British were more interested ii European politics than in th Empire tended to be confirmee "This encouraged the belie that Australians could go oi regarding themselves as a com munity that might fashion it own standards in isolation an let the world go by. "The war did not dispel thi illusion. It took the depressio: n to convince Australia that sh was bound in the bundle of ir 5. ternational life."', 'DOES AUSTRALIA NEED AN INDIAN OCEAN PACT? Following the Communists\' southward drive towards the borders of India and Pakistan two questions of vital importance are facing Australia: (1) will the Reds ever be able to cut the Empire\'s communications in the Indian Ocean; (2) does not Australia need a new system of security in her north-west, welding together the nations of Indian Ocean in some kind of military defence pact? Lieutenant-General H. Gordon Bennett discusses in the Sunday Sun the case for and against such fact and comes to the conclusion that there is no immediate danger of aggression to Australia from the north-west. (Lieut-General Bennett, a well known Australian Military Authority, commanded the 3rd Australian Infantry Brigade during 1917-1918 and the Bth Division in the second World War. His leadership in Malaya gained him world recognition). "A Soviet move south will not, in itself, create any immediate danger to Australia," says Lieut-General Bennett. The countries of Southern Asia which face the Indian Ocean can be invaded by land from the north. The control of the Indian Ocean is not necessary for such an operation. What would be the effect on Australia if ever the Indian Ocean passed into the hands of a foreign Power? The normal and natural sea approach to Australia from Europe is across the Indian Ocean— either via Suez or the Cape. The only other sea route is across the Atlantic and Pacific Oceans via Panama or round stormy Cape Horn. The distance to England\' via Suez is 9725 miles, via the Cape 10,670 miles, while via Panama it is 12,222 miles and via Cape Horn 13,320 miles. In the past the British Navy has had control of the seas. Under its motherly protection we have always felt secure from attack. It was only when the Japanese temporarily wrested the control of the Western Pacific from\' us that danger of invasion confronted us. Then the U.S. navy came to our aid and restored our security. This American help came because we were both at war with a common foe and it was essential for America to use Australia as a base from which to launch an attack on the Japanese. Australia could not expect similar help in the Indian Ocean. Should another war overwhelm the world, Britain\'s strength would be absorbed primarily in defending the homeland and in fighting a life and death battle in Western Europe. It is doubtful if Britain could spare forces large enough to defend her interests in the Middle East. Assuming that U.S.S.R. is the aggressor, a powerful drive will strike south through Iran to the Persian Gulf and the Arabian Sea, the objective being twofold: To capture the oil wells both for her own use and also to deny them to Great Britain and U.S.A. To establish herself on the warm weather ports on the Persian and Arabian coasts. With air and submarine bases there, she would dominate the Eastern exit from the Mediterranean and thus force all shipping to the long route round South Africa. This would not, in itself, create any immediate danger to Australia. Our great danger comes from the north. In the last war the Japanese succeeded in establishing themselves in the Dutch East Indies and were under 300 miles from our empty northern spaces. For a time they had control of the sea and the air; they could easily have occupied Wyndham, Derby and Broome—and possibly Darwin. The small handful of Australians in these distant outposts could not have prevented a landing and with the complete lack of roads and railways to this part of our continent, it would have been impossible to dislodge the invaders who could then have climbed down the West Coast at least as far as Geraldton. The Indonesians control the East Indies, and there is no danger of invasion from that direction. The Indonesians have no navy or air force with which to embark on any aggressive campaign. Just as the Japanese overwhelmed the Dutch and menaced Australia, so a Communist force equipped, trained, and led by Russians could oust the Indonesians. They would then become an even greater menace than the Japanese—provided, of course, they had a strong air force and navy and ample shipping, all of which are essential before landing operations could be undertaken. We have ample evidence of this southward move by the Communists and unless it is checked the danger will some day face us. No invasion of Australia, however, can occur unless an aggressor has a powerful air force and navy. These take years to develop.']

Name: 81_baltic_freedom_resolution_reagan
Representative_Docs: ['By the President of the United States of America A Proclamation During the past year, the long struggle of the Baltic peoples to recover their freedom has been marked by both triumph and tragedy: triumph in their bold calls for liberty and independence; tragedy in the bloody events of January 1991. The United States and. indeed, all freedom-loving nations have long denounced the infamous Molotov-Ribbentrop pact that led to the forcible incorporation of the independent Baltic States into the Soviet Union. The peoples of both the West and the Baltic States have continued to believe that the freedom of Lithuania, Latvia, and Estonia must and can be restored. Recent events suggest that our hopes have not been misplaced. In peaceful, democratic referendums, the peoples of Estonia, Latvia, and Lithuania have asserted overwhelmingly their desire for freedom. Toward that aim, they have sought to enter into meaningful negotiations with Moscow about their status. Despite the tragic events of January 1991. which resulted in the deaths of at least 21 Lithuanians and Latvians and many more injured, a dialogue between the Baltic governments and the Soviet Union has begun. We hope that it will bear fruit, and we urge the Soviet Union to move forward with the talks. However, the United States remains deeply concerned over the continued application of intimidation and force by Soviet authorities. Such actions are incompatible with the process of peaceful change through fair and constructive negotiations. The United States has never and will never recognize the forcible annexation of the Baltic States by the Soviet Union. I reiterated this policy during my recent meeting with Lithuanian President Landsbergis, Estonian Prime Minister Savisaar, and Latvian Prime Minister Godmanis. This was my sixth meeting with the Baltic leadership during the past 12 months. The Administration will remain in close contact with the Baltic leadership in the months ahead. As we commemorate "Baltic Freedom Day," we reaffirm our support for the right of the Baltic peoples to live in peace and freedom. The Congress, by Public Law 102-17, has designated June 14, 1991, and June 14. 1992, as "Baltic Freedom Day" and has authorized and requested the President to issue a proclamation in observance of this event. NOW, THEREFORE, I, GEORGE BUSH, President of the United States of America, do hereby proclaim the days of June 14, 1991, and June 14, 1992, as Baltic Freedom Day. I call upon the people of the United States to observe these days with appropriate ceremonies and activities to reaffirm their commitment to human rights and to freedom and democracy for all oppressed peoples. IN WITNESS WHEREOF, I have hereunto set my hand this 13th day of June, in the year of our Lord nineteen hundred and ninety-one. and of the Independence of the United States of America the two hundred and fifteenth. Baltic Freedom Day, 1991 and 1992', 'Baltic Freedom Day and Captive Nations Day observed by President Bush. Baltic-Americans...and people in Latvia. Once again this year, June 14 was proclaimed as Baltic Freedom Day by the United States Congress and President Bush. The Joint Congressional Resolution, co-sponsored by 58 Senators and 251 Congressmen, urged President Reagan to proclaim June 14 Baltic Freedom Day, and "call upon the Soviet Union, the Federal Republic of Germany, and the Democratic Republic of Germany to renounce the acquisition or absorption ofthe Baltic Republics by the Soviet Union as a result of the Molotov-Ribbentrop Pact." As another example of rampant \'glasnost\' in the Baltic States, the full text of the Congressional resolution was printed on the front page of the June 12, 1989 issue of \'ATMODA\', the weekly publication of the Popular Front of Latvia. Although it is not known whether the President has contacted the Soviet and German governments, he did issue a proclamation describing the "forcible annexation" of the Baltic States by the Soviet Union, and urging the Soviets to "listen to their calls for freedom and self-determination." The future looks brighter today than at any other time in the Baltic States\' post-war experience,"the President stated, "The undeniable voice of the of Baltic people is being heard..." After citing several improvements in human and religious rights in the Baltic States, the Presidential proclamation states, "These are important steps, but justice demands that more be taken...The people of Lithuania, Latvia and Estonia both demand and deserve lasting guarantees of their fundamental rights." The proclamation concludes, "The Government of the United States does not and will not recognize the unilateral incorporation by the force of arms of the Baltic States into the Soviet Union." The Joint Baltic American National Committee (JBANC) and the Baltic American Freedom League (BAFL) held a reception in Russell Senate Office Building in Washington, D.C. on June 14 to commemmorate Baltic Freedom Day. In attendence was Ilmārs Bergmanis, President of the United Latvian Associations of Chicago, and a BAFL representative. Mr. Bčrgmanis presented Chicago Congressman Frank Annunzio (D) with a special award on behalf of Chicago Latvians, in appreciation for his longtime support of the Baltic cause. Mr. Bergmanis also visited with Illinois Congressman Richard Durbin, who is of Lithuanian descent. Days of commemoration The Baltic States also featured prominently in President Bush\'s Captive Nations proclamation and speech on July 21. In the proclamation, the President again made mention of the 1939 Molotov-Ribbentrop Pact, and the continued U.S. refusal to accept the incorporation into the Soviet Union of the Baltic States. "But decades of oppression have not broken the spirit of the Baltic people..." This theme was repeated again during the President\'s address to Captive Nations groups in the White House Rose Garden, when he said, "and a spirit of renewal lights the Baltic States – Latvia, Lithuania and Estonia – striving to recapture their national history." This spirit of renewal had been dramatically demonstrated in Latvia five days earlier, when rallies were held in several Latvia cities on July 16 to praise the U.S. for its Captive Nations Week observances. At one rally, Visvaldis Brinkmanis, a representative of the National Independence Movement of Latvia (LNNK) said, "Considering the exceptional role the United States and yourself have in the world, we hope that you will find the opportunity to raise the question of illegal occupation of the Baltic States to an international forum as well as to your contacts with the leadership of the Soviet Union." Closer to home.in Chicago, a Captive Nations rally was held on July 16 at Navy Pier, followed by a three-day Captive Nation Week exhibit at the Illinois Center. The Third Annual Governer\'s Reception for Captive Nations Week took place in the Rotunda of the Illinois Center on July 19th.', 'Baltic Freedom Day 1986 Baltic Freedom Day Resolution introduced In US House and Senate – Baltic community bēgtu» campaign to win full Congressional support. On February 7 Senator Don Rlegle (D-MI) introduced Senate Joint Resolution 271 (S.J. Res. 271). calling for the designation of June 14. 1986 as "Baltic Freedom Day". The resolution was co sponsored by 30 other U.S. Senators, including Illinois Senators Paul Simon and Alan Dixon. In his remarks to the Senate. Sen. Rlegle stated: " With this resolution, the Congress focuses the attention of the world on the plight of the Baltic people and demonstrates solidarity with them in their continuing struggle for freedom. This year. June 14 will mark the 46th year of Soviet subjugation of the Baltic States. Despite steady erosion of the national identities of the people of Latvia, Lithuania and Estonia over the past four and a half decades, their determination to regain their lost freedoms has not been weakened...The past 46 years have shown that all the military strength of the Soviet Union can never succeed in rrushing the spirit and the drive of the Baltic people." Sen. Rlegle concluded his statement by drawing a comparison to South Africa. "Just as the South African policy of apartheid, which denies basic rights to a majority of its citizens. Is unacceptable, so too Is the continuing Soviet occupation of Latvia. Lithuania and Eston 1a... Express ion s of support for the aspirations of tl« citizens of the captive nations from the free world give hope to those still fighting to secure their fundamental human rights. Prompt Senate approval of this resolution will demonstrate the solidarity of the American people in their continuing struggle for freedom." At the same time, Cong. William Carney (R-NY), along with Congs. Brian Donnelly (D-MA) and Don Ritter (R-PA), Introduced a companion bill In the House. House Joint Resolution 500 (H.J. Res. 500) states in part, " The purpose of this resolution is to set aside a day of rememberance for the continuing plight of the people of Estonia. Latvia and Lithuania, who have endured 46 years of foreign domination and suppression by the Soviet Union." The Baltic Freedom Day Resolution Is of key importance to Baits on both sides of the Iron Curtain. It sends a clear signal to Soviet Union and to the world that the United States government still supports Baltic aspirations for freedom by publicly reinforcing the longstanding U.S. non recogn it ion policy toward the illegal Soviet occupation of the Baltic States. Perhaps even tore important, the passage of this resolution will be broadcast to the people of occupied Estonia. Latvia and Lithuania via Voice of America and Radio Free Europe, letting them know that they have not been forgotten. Thus, it la critical that Baltic Americans do everything in their power to ensure full Congressional support for this bill. While Senate support is already strong, in order to pass both the House and Senate versions of this resolution, a majority of U.S. legislators must sign on to these bills. While organizations such as the American Latvian Association (ALA), the Joint Baltic American National Committee (JBANC) and the Baltic American Freedom League (BAFL) will be working in Washington to win broadbased support for these resolutions, it is critical that Baits and others write directly to their Congressmen, urging them to co-sponsor H.J. Res 500. WHAT YOU CAN DO Write to your Congressman or Congresswoman and ask them to co sponsor H.J. Res. 500. indicating that the passage of this bill is extremely important to you. Ask your family members, relatives, neighbors and colleagues to send letters to their representatives as well – the more letters each legislator receives, the better our chances of winning his or her support. WRITING TO THE CONGRESS: Address your letter to: The Hon. (congressman\'s/woman\'s name) United States House of Representatives Washington O.C. 20515 If you don\'t know who your U.S. Representative is, call AlglB Silas of the Joint Baltic American National Committee at (301) 340- 1954. ALSO... JBANC and ALA have placed requests with the White House asking for a special Presidential signing ceremony for the Baltic Freedom Day Resolution. If this is approved. President Reagan will meet with Baltic-American leaders In the White House. In your letters to your Congressmen and Congresswomen. (especially the Republicans!) ask them to personally write the President, urging such a public reception. After you\'ve written a letter to your Congressman, you can also write to President Reagan, asking that he hold such a Baltic Freedom Day reception in the White House. Soviet leader Gorbachev is coming to Washington D.C. this year, and a public Baltic Freedom Day Ceremony would send hla a clear signal that the ongoing illegal Soviet occupation of the Baltic States has not been forgotten.']

Name: 82_visa_citizen_travel
Representative_Docs: ['Non-citizens of Latvia may travel to the EU without visas From 19 January 2007, residing noncitizens of Latvia will be exempt from visa requirements within the European Union, when travelling to Austria, Belgium, Bulgaria, the Czech Republic, Denmark, France, Greece, Estonia, Italy, Cyprus, Lithuania, Luxembourg, Malta, the Netherlands, Poland, Portugal, Romania, Slovenia, Slovakia, Spain, Finland, Hungary, Germany, and Sweden. Latvian resident non-citizens will also be able to travel without a visa to Iceland. The Regulation will not be applicable to the United Kingdom and Ireland. The Embassy of Norway in Riga has announced that Norway will not apply a visa-free regime to the residing non-citizens of Latvia until the relevant amendment to the domestic law is executed. The amendments envisage the exemption from a visa requirement also to those recognized refugees and stateless persons who are permanently resident in any EU member state and who have a valid travel document issued by that particular member state.', 'NEW VISA REQUIREMENTS FOR ESTONIA New visa requirements were implemented for people entering Estonia from June 1. The citizens of Andorra, Australia, Canada, Japan, Liechtenstein, Monaco, New Zealand, San Marino, United States, and The Holy See can now enter Estonia visa-free. Also from 1 June visas will no longer be issued at the border to citizens from the Commonwealth of Independent States and Georgia. However, visas will be issued at the border for citizens of European countries, South Africa and South Korea. Estonia has signed visafree travel accords with seven countries: Bulgaria, the Czech Republic, Denmark, Great Britain, Hungary, Latvia, Lithuania, and Poland.', "LATVIA'S EMBASSIES REPORT 70,000 VISAS ISSUED IN FIRST HALF OF 1996 At a press conference on 19 August, Minister of Foreign Affairs Valdis Birkavs spoke about Latvia's foreign policy activities in the first half of 1996 including consular work in Republic of Latvia embassies abroad. During this time period, the embassies have issued approx. 70,000 entrance visas, more than 21,G0G of which have been issued in Moscow, more than 8,000 in St. Petersburg, around 6,000 in Helsinki, and more than 5,000 in Minsk and Vitebsk. Mr. Birkavs told reporters that the introduction of a visa-free regime in co-operation with Nordic countries is one of Latvia's priorities. In this connection, Norway is ready to send its experts to assess the situation in Latvia. As of 1 May, a visa-free regime between Latvia and Ireland has come into force, and the Minister of Foreign Affairs foresaw that a visa-free regime with Bulgaria could be introduced by the spring of 1997, and noted that a draft agreement on visa-free travelling has also been submitted to Slovenia. The minister said that the Baltic states have jointly proposed to Luxembourg, as the presiding state of the Schengen agreement countries, to begin talks on visa-free travelling."]

Name: 83_rating_moody_credit
Representative_Docs: ["STANDARD & POOR'S UPGRADES LATVIA'S RATING The international credit rating agency Standard & Poor's upgraded the long-term foreign currency issuer credit rating of the Republic of Latvia lending from 888 to BBB+, according to the Latvian Finance Ministry. The short-term foreign currency issuer rating was raised from A-3 to A-2. Latvia was also given the long-term local currency issuer rating of A- and the shortterm local currency issuer rating of A-2, and the outlook was evaluated as Stable. Standard & Poor's said Latvia's ratings were upgraded in view of its economic development and resilience during the recent slowdown of world economic growth. The rating agency also noted that Latvia is a potential member of the European Monetary Union (EMU) and that after accession to this union the foreign and local currency ratings would no longer be different. Latvia's ratings were also upgraded owing to its good macroeccnomic indicators and the steps taken by the government to strengthen the market economy. Investment growth in Latvia is stable, structural reforms continue and the medium-term GDP growth is expected at 6%. The national debt is likely to be low at 17% of GDP in 2002. In addition, Latvia will probably get full EU membership in 2005 at the latest. According to Standard & Poor's, Latvia's economy has become increasingly flexible and competitive in recent years, and the EU preaccession process has contributed to the growth. Some negative aspects were pointed out as weii, such as the high current account deficit, although it is largely covered by foreign direct investments. Standard & Poor's expect Latvia's current account deficit at 9.3% of GDP in 2002. The agency said it expected prudent macroeconomic policy from the Latvian government, which, combined with EU membership and accession to EMU, would contribute to the development of a sustainable economy and further growth of ratings.", '"Standard & Poor\'s" affirms Latvia\'s previous credit ratings. The international credit rating agency "Standard & Poor\'s" Ratings Services announced on 16 July that it affirmed its \'A\' long-term and \'A-2\' short-term sovereign credit ratings for Latvia. The outlook is stable. The Agency justified its decision by the determination of the State to continue to promote implementation of reforms based on market economy and the fiscal indicators achieved to date. Similarly, the debt of the Latvian central Government in 2005 was kept at a significant low -12% of the GDP, which is the third lowest indicator among the member States of the EU. At present the Latvian credit rating for long term commitments in foreign currency given So Latvia by the rating agency Moody\'s, is A 2 with a positive future credit ra! ting, but the credit rating given by the rating agency F\'tch Ratings is A-level with a stable future credit rating assessment.', 'FITCH RATINGS UPGRADES LATVIA\'S CREDIT RATING The international credit rating agency Fitch Ratings has upgraded Latvia\'s rating for longterm loans in foreign currency from 888 to BBB+. For long-term loans in local currency, the rating has been raised to A, while for short-term loans in foreign currency the rating is now F3. The ratings agency also evaluates Latvia\'s economy as having "a stable outlook". In its report, Fitch Ratings points out that Latvia\'s rating has been increased in response to the country\'s stable economic development, structural reforms, strengthening of the banking sector and stable foreign investment. It says Latvia has one of the highest foreign investment ratios of European Union applicant countries. The international rating agency Standard & Poor\'s has already affirmed the Latvia\'s longterm foreign currency credit rating at BBB+, while Moody\'s has assigned a rating of A 2.']

Representative_Docs: ['LATVIAN PSYCHOLOGY (Cont. frcin Previous Issue% Nevertheless, this influencing takes place from both sides, for, [ on the one hand our mind influi ences and co-ordinates our desires, on the other hand, these desires : give our mind its peculiar character. Being to a great extent at the service of aims of life determind by desires, our mind is \' not absolutely free .and our thought as well as our imagination avoid excesses and an aimless roving that is characteristic [ of southerners, but proceed more * in a definite direction. "She Latvians are, therefore, fairly often ; inclined to be one-sided, and the structure of their mind urges them to be "party-men," towards i which they have a tendency also « for the reasons that we examined above. But exactly this coalition of the desires and the mind forms also the basis of Latvian idealism, which is nothing other than the ability to follow the conceptions of some aim—ideals, projected by the desires, but treated by the mind, generalised and raised above one\'s own personality. It is characteristic of the Latvians (and in this they are like the French) that they can be carried away by abstract ideas too, can become attached to aims and problems that are above their own personal interests. This is an interesting phenomenon: its foundation, without doubt, is the "anchoring" of life in innate natural desires, but thanks to the influence of the mind these desires deviate to a certain degree from their original egocentric orientation (this happens easier if the desires are not too violent), they become, so to say, sublimated and tend towards a very important change of the whole mental structure, which, it would seem, is capable of making a nation that possesses this a valuable factor of progress. Proof of this thesis is not wanting. That the Latvians were earned away by revolutionary ideas of their time (often only in order to stand up for justice and not allow unjustice to get the upper hand) is only one striking instance of idealism. This has revealed itself more strikingly perhaps in the recent past as readiness to sacrifice themselves for the liberating of their nation and the founding of their state. Without doubt, as there can be no positive phenomena without certain negative effects, so also the idealism of the Latvian struggle for independence had such; it often made the people onesided, narrowed their sphere of interests and at the same time the understanding for other people, which can result in an unjust and sometimes even a heartless behaviour towards them. Still dwelling on the positive sides of the structure of this idealistic spirit, we must here note that it is also the real basis of the Latvians\' highly developed sense of duty, and in general of precision, conscientiousness and perseverance in the performing of any kind of work. Duty is really nothing other than the submitting of all personal interests to some moral idea, but consistency in work—the ability not to lose sight of the idea of the aim. Let us point out once more that all these faculties could develop in a Latvian, because his desires are not too violent, and his mind is able to co-operate with and lead them. It is significant, therefore, that idealism is much more characteristic of Northerners than of Southerners, who are much oftener given to the alternation of moments of violent desire and moments of complete languor. The mind of a Northerner (on the one hand), and especially that of a Latvian, puts order and rank into the life of desires, co-ordinating them with his judgment of values, and in this way diminishing their direct and spontaneous influence; on the other hand, he can sometimes intensify his weak and smouldering desires and transform them into bright flames by adding the fuel of lofty ideas. We can also explain the Latvian will, strong and persistent as a rule, by this coalition of the desires and the mind. The Latvians showed this will of theirs strikingly by working their way up from the dependent class of agriculturists, deprived of the protection of the law, to wealth BY P. JUREVICS and the status of masters of their own country: therefore the Latvian will is now always looked upon as a model of strong will. Its basis, as was said above, is a certain stability and constancy of desires. But desires alone do not call forth will, for alone they are blind, their stability is relative, and they are governed by a rhythm of ebb and flow. They change to will only when the mind perceives the aims suggested by them, formulates them into the shape of a certain conception and consciously agrees with them; the aim of an action is then not desire itself (this remains a hidden source of power only), but the conception accepted by the mind. This governs action also when the real pressure of desires has abated or when there is even counter-pressure; but exactly this means will. The coalition of the mind and desires can be looked upon as a fortunate gift of our natures, a great gain for our life of desires which in this way is co-ordinated, stabilised, harmonised, made capable of action; in brief, it is a great gain that warrants practical success in action. However, the coalition of mind and desires reflects differently on the theoretical activity of the mind. On the one hand, there can be no doubt that theoretical thinking profits when a thought is government by desires: this gives him a certain direction in which to proceed and an axis round which it can revolve and finally crystallise. A thought governed by certain desires advances with greater tension and arrives at some positive result quicker than a thought that roams about without any other desire than that of finding truth, and so loses its way in the labyrinths of antinomies and the deserts of scepticism. However, on the other hand again, there can be no doubt that a thought governed byj desires has its faults: such a thought tends towards one-sided theories, it is schematic, and it has the tendency towards system; it does not always adapt itself to all the unexpected turns of reality, but is often constructed on hand and sharp lines as something that fs connected with concrete reality only in so far as this leads, in a seemingly logical way, to the result that was dictated by desires and that in some way can be of use to the action. Nor does a thought governed by desires lead, as would seem at first sight, to greater depths. In reality it is different, except in some special cases: a desire already dictating the required solution of a problem hurries towards its aim, and has neither time nor interest to linger over various "trifles," which in the end perhaps would have shown that the desired aim was out ot reach. Therefore, thinking governed by desires, however suited it might be for the attaining of certain concrete aims, is not an ideal instrument ol solving purely theoretical problems—if in general it ever attempts the solving of such problems—and only a strong, critical mind, which the Latvians fortunately generally possess, would be able to avoid too serious errors. It is interesting to note that the practical orientation of desires influences the life of emotions in a similar way. On the one hand, there can be no doubt that the Latvians as a people of desires are usually very sensitive, emotional. They are very thinskinned and inwardly their emotions react easily and sharply to the appearances of the outer world, especially to the actions of other people. A Latvian is, for instance, rather easily offended, and does not forget an offence soon Pretty often he even reacts to it, indifferent to the dangers to his own life: there are numerous instances of Latvians, who did not allow others to treat them roughly, being punished by courts martial of the former Russian army. On the other hand, a Latvian is easily carried away and delights in various new and pleasant things — also in ideas —-this seems explicable by a splitup emotional life; a Latvian is more capable of being many-sided and expansive than of being onesided and deep. Emotionally, i.e., reacting to many things and in many directions is, therefore, more characteristic of a Latvian than passion, which means con centrating in one direction: a Latvian as a man of desires is rather "multipolar" than "unipolar," if we may say so. Which of these structures is of greater value is difficult to say: "unipolarity," passion, one-sidedness makes us concentrate all our faculties in one direction only and so by intensifying the ability to act permits us to achieve great things; "multipolarity", which means also that the soul is open to the receiving of values, creates a greater atmosphere of humanity, furthers the expression of life, the richness of life, determines a conduct more just and in this sense it is socially of greater value. Be this as it may, we must distinguish this Latvian emotionality, this ability to yield quickly and acutely to experiences of emotion, from the ability to express these emotions quickly and spontaneously, especially if they are of a positive nature. In expressing positive emotions the Latvians are heavy and slow; they "interiorise" their feelings more than they "exteriorise" them; it seems that the main cause of this would be the tendency towards individualism, towards "shutting oneself up" — a certain unsociability, but often also simply shyness, the lack otā habit and practice; — but we 1 shall return to this later. \'Finally, we must distinguish aM third way of characterising the 1 life of emotions — a differentia- m tion of the emotions, i.e., refine- 1 ment. In this sense Latvian emo- $ tionality has not progressed very far perhaps. An active man of f desires who is always in a state I oi activity has not the time norjß the wish to plunge into emotional 1 experiences and to register all | their nuances and remoter ojM nearer relation to different stages 1 of our perception It is the same m with young people: they are very .1 sensitive, but the life of their tj emotions is not differentiated. It J would not be surprising, there- I fore, if the emotional life of ai Latvian who is actively orientat- 1 ed, like active members of other J nations, were not too refined and intellectual in spite of the Latvian j emotionality already mentioned, i I think there will be many who i will not agree with me, and will 1 point out that the lyric is the most I flourishing kind of our poetry*, 1 and that the number of our ly- J rical poets, not to mention the j anonj\'mous authors of the thou- § sands of folk-songs, is especially I large. Here we must take into | consideration that poets are Š people who have specialised in a I direction which is contrary to 1 that of a practical person: they J have, so to say, disactualised, turned from actions towards 1 themselves and their emtoions. J (Continued from Previous Issue) |', '(Continued from last week) Under these circumstances, and because of this innate tendency of ours, nobody seems content with the position he has attained: farmers are anxious to exchange country life for town life, tradespeople want to be clerks, officials to be merchants, and even teachers and professors have higher aspirations. There is reason tc think that this zeal of ours has ceased to be a means of attaining greater perfection in life, but has become degraded to an activity directed towards attaining easiei conditions of life, "easier life" in general. If this be so, then there is little reason to be proud of this tendency. Striving to advance is beginning to threaten the existence of our nation; for the sake of a life easier, more comfortable, more luxurious, and free from material cares, we often sacrifice even our natural tendency to have descendants. The retaining of this quality in circumstances to I which it is not adapted seems to prove the hypothesis set up before that endeavor is the specification of some more general quality; the process of specification took place in circumstances which existed for a long time, but have suddenly changed now. Indeed, endeavor may be looked upon as an expression of a certain exaggeration, or at least a very strong development of the life of desires that is at the root of our natures. In general, Latvians may be looked up as having very strongly developed the life of desires: they are always longing for something, craving for values, and Plato would perhaps have called them "erotic," for, as we know, Plato uses the word "Eros" in the sense of "desire"; desire for something we now would call "value." As there are values of a higher and of a lower degree, so there are also different kinds of "erotics," and they are also able to progress and rise in life, but also to regress. However, even Eros of a lower quality is full of promise; it is, so to say, capable of sublimination, for the same person who one day has devoted himself to saving money or objects of material value, may some other day develop into an admirer and collection of works of art, science or ethics. In order to explain certain contradictory qualities of the Latvian character, we shall have to take into consideration this tendency of the desires to metamorphosis. First, however, we shall consider some more general consequences which result from the whole of our spiritual life being centred in the sphere of desires. Every person swho is so centred seems to live beyond the boundaries of real life; he lives, to a certain degree, in an unreal world. This is the result of the structure of desire. "Desire" means striving for something that is not yet there, pursuing something that does not exist. It means the thinking, feeling, and wishing of things that are only yet a thought, a dream, a fancy. A man ef desires is, therefore, a person who seems to live outside himself, whose centre of gravity is outside his own person, and who seems to be constantly losing his balance. A person so constituted is always rußhing forward in order to keep his balance. The life of a man of desires is an incessant running after himself, after a proLATVIAN PSYCHOLOGY By P. JUREVICS jection of his own thoughts. This gives rise to the following question: As desires are so often satisfied, is there no standstill, nc stability, when the desired aim has been attained? Yes, there are types, pleasure-seekers, for instance, and some other types, perhaps, whose life is centred in the present, and desire is for them only a means of realising the future in the present. The life of the "desire" type, however, realises itself in the act of desiring, in the process of wishing and attaining something longed for, but less in enjoying the results oi this striving. The pleasure of having achieved is only a short moment of respite, and then begins anew the rushing after some object of desire that the thoughts have had time to project during their interval of rest. In this way every person, the action and fulfilment of whose life is "desiring" and not the achievement of what he desires, lives in the future and not in the present, or, to be more precise, in his thoughts of the future. It would be too much to say that a Latvian personifies the type of a radical and extreme man of desires. Altogether, it is even difficult to imagine the existence of a pure desire type; we can only look upon it as an ideal which is, however, approached by many concrete natures, and my own opinion is that the Latvians, too —at least at the present stage of their development — belong to those natures who orientate in that direction. Latvians can be looked upon as a "people of the future" in that sense of the expression that they live less in the present than in plans and problems of the future, and in the fulfilment of these problems, which are immediately exchanged for problems the moment the first have been solved, or set aside, or forgotten. Endeavor, with all its positive and negative qualities, was really only a peculiar expression of the disposition of this "man of desires," whose life has been transferred from the present to the future. As he is person whose life is centred in the future, a Latvian is a typical European. According to the opinions of some psychogists, the opinions of some psychologists shortly characterised in the following way: the yellow race—meaning the Chinese—live in the past, the black race in the present, but the thoughts and plans of the Europeans are directed towards the future. Because of this bent of their mentality, the Chinese, who attained a high level of culture in the past, no longer progress, for they cast their glances back at the past; representatives of the black race, unable to look ahead and to forsee the future are in general said to be little capable of culture; in that way the only real creators of modern culture are the Europeans who constantly look ahead, apprehensive of future dangers and preoccupied with thoughts of how to evade them and how again to profit by the favorable conditions they anticipate. Since the time of Count Gobineau, the long-limbed and dolichocephalic Nordic race, ricff in violent desires, enterprising and energetic, but inwardly unstable and often fantastic in its undertakings is looked upon by some as the real pioneer of European culture. \'ture. Pure representatives of thi race are not to be found amon the nations of our days (and prot ably also not among those of th past), but on the other hand ther can be doubt that the Latvians as well as some other nations, ar closely related to it; this is jn plied by our geographical positio: and by the significant numbei especially in the past, of dolichc cephalics, also by our more tha: average height, which makes v one of the tallest nations in Eui ope, as well as by the fact tha the majority of Latvians ar blonde and light-eyed. Without going into a discussioi as to the extent to which thi theory of race has a scientific basi we must admit that these conclu sions arrived at, correspond Witl what has been previously sai< about the Latvian mental struc ture; a certain exaltation of thi life of desires—which seemed t< us to be characteristic of the Lat vian nature—would seem to b< characteristic of the Nordic race This exaltation of the life o desires agrees, it appears to us with the attempt of modern char acterology to distinguish tw( groups of human beings; the on< comprising corpulent, the othei slender human beings. Character istic of the first group is a certaii inner calm and a concentration 01 life itself; whereas the members of the second group reveal a con stant dissatisfaction, impetuosity inner conflicts, and an incessant struggle with themselves and tht world. It has been further pointeč out that in cases of mental disease stout people are more liable tc suffer from cyclephrenia, i.e., the rhythmic change of abnormal\' exaltation alternating with dejection, whereas human beings of slender build are -often victims of schizephrenia—a splitting up of the \' individuality (a result of the hypertrophy of conflicting desires) and a mania of persecution. Types of the first kind, which in gener corresponds to the pynic structur are called, cyclethymic by the Ge man characterologist Kretschme types of the other kind schizoth mic. The Latvians who like th English are a nation of tall peop would accordingly be nearer tl sehizothymic than the cyclethym type. We shall again leave undi cussed the question of the validii of this somewhat too simp theory, but merely state that if w accept this point of view, the La vians would, in general be neare the sehizothymic than the cycl thymic type; experience seems t prove this. This would perhaps explai some negative features of Latvia life that are so often criticise by our moralists; i.e., disagreement, distrust, an attitude of op position towards everything and everybody (often found among us) the conviction of being the object of universal persecution and in general a tendency to strangeness and oddities of different kinds, these are all signs of a schizothy– mic charcter, which again is some-3 thing that pertains to the whole r of the North European race and – is connected with a certain hyper-5 trophy of desires and the develop; ment of a certain inner instability. , The Latvians, as sehizothymic ) northerners, as typical "people of – the future," possess a spirit of uni rest and from the standpoint of , enjoying happiness the prognosis – is not promising. On the other i hand, however, for just this reason j perhaps, they belong to that group – of people who, because of their t continual striving, could be the 5 real creators, or at least the cultivators and spreaders of cultural t values. The Latvians are, from i this point of view, akin to the i Germans who also easily lose their – inner balance in that they are i orientated towards the future, but I they are farther from the Russians ■ and Slavs in general, from peoples > who are more absorbed by the > present. The Latvians are also • farther from the harmonious i French who without rejecting the . past, are apprehensive of the fut: ure, but do not forget the present. , We can assume, therefore, that all that has been said above gives credence to the supposition that > on the whole, the Latvians belong to that group of human beings whose mental structure has its centre in the life of desires; it would therefore be reasonable to assume they also possess other qualities implied by this structure. It is clear, however, that these qualities would give us only a rather general idea of the Latvian character in so far as the Latvians belong to groups of people much larger than the Latvians, i.e., to all Europeans, or all northerners. How are we to find the qualities that are specifically Latvian and that distinguish the Latvian people from representatives of other nations spiritually related to\\ it? We have already endeavored to do this to a certain extent by trying to show that the general preponderance of the life of desires in the Latvian nature and the conditions of Latvian life has found its expression in the already mentioned fact of striving. But there can be no doubt that this striving is here not only an expression of life of desires, but a more complicated fact comprising many intellectual and emotional factors. Therefore, before turning our attention to the Latvian spiritual structure with all its complications, we shall try and characterise its simplest elements. (To be continued)', 'LATVIAN PSYCHOLOGY (Continued from last week) Be this as it may, our pleasures and the ood and bad habits nature by trying to immerse him-5 testify once more to our youthfuli ness, our nature of desires orientated towards the future and : action. As people of desires we { are in no sense pessimists, bul on the contrary, the effective keynote of our life is light and ļ optimistic, in spite of our harshness as fighters, our incessant • impetuosity and dissatisfaction, " life and its struggle, attract us \' and we are full of hope. This * optimism is an indispensable and active element of our mental structure. How could we possibly * be strivers and fighters if life • and its promises had no attractions for us? We can easily bring this into concordance with the psychology of desires in general. 1 Inherent to desire is, on the one hand, a dissatisfaction with what is given—and that this side of desires is sufficiently represented in us has been proved. But on the other hand, the desires contain a conception that can satisfy ļthe desires (an ideal), and it is I not possible to imagine this and still less to strive for it if we do not to a certain degree anticipate it, i.e., imagine it realised and feel it as such. But it is impossible to imagine and to feel it realised if we do not at the same time experience joy and satisfaction. This explains the sweetness of longing and dreams: thanks to them we experience beforehand the perfection and pleasure of an unrealised idea. Therefore it seems quite natural that the type of a dreamer is one of the main types found in Latvian literature. Though realisticallyinclined critics protest against the cultivation of this type, still, it is in no case only an invention of certain writers, but an essentially important variant of a man of desires, only in these types the accent has been shifted from a feeling of want to a feeling of fulfilment, often as a result of outward circumstances that are too difficult, that do not permit of any concrete approach to the ideal, and the soul, therefore, seemingly with the intention of self-defence concentrates on the plane of imagining the fulfilment of the desired. However, also in a normal life of desires the thing desired for in the future is always anticipated beforehand and experienced in the present, and this is the moment which permits a tone of optimism to resound in the psyche of desires, that is indispensible to it as a stimulant and a "supporter" of the tension of life. Joie de viv»e, briskness, ingenuity will naturally and indispensably sparkle in the language of Latvians and will find its expression in their works and thus level out certain heavier sides of their character, for instance, their relation towards other people: joy makes one like other people, makes one benevolent towards them and helps to break the hard crust of one\'s soul which other powers and their configurations try to develop. This tone of joy and optimism deprives Latvian society in a certain measure of its heaviness, makes it comparatively responsive, easier to stimulate and, therefore, causes it to be full of life, joy and tension. In drawing these last features of the Latvian character we have approached an understanding of a Latvian that seems to be antithetical to the one given above, but which was approved of and often treated by our best writers, such as Poruks, Fr. Barda, Saulietis, Skalbe, partly also by Rainis, Brigadere and others. As I have said before they represent a type of Latvian that they look upon to a certain degree as an ideal in two different ways; the first variation reveals him as a dreamer, a romanticist, endowed with a soul so sensitive and gentle that everything in the world of concrete things wounds him and he can find consolation only in ature by trying to immerse himself in it and fuse with it. The second variation, incidentally, is rather near to the first, reveals a sincere personality, turned to all the world and infinitely favorably inclined to all life, but especially to all human beings. This type represents a person of BY P. JUREVICS so little cunning, so reliable, that the world cannot call him anything other than "simpleton," the type of "Antins," so popular in Latvian folk-tales. One of our best poets, Rainis, has used the folk-tales about Antins to write one of his most beautiful dramatic works, "The Golden Horse." Antins is the youngest of three brothers: he is a fool, whereas his brothers are "clever," i.e., their sharp wits serve their own interests only. When their father dies, the elder brothers not only take possession of all his property, but even make Antins dress the dead in his own Sunday clothes; Antins does this with pleasure, for he has always been very fond of his father. Then the brothers drive Antins out of his father\'s house. It is winter, and very frosty, but that does not prevent Antins from giving his gloves, jacket, boots and cap to the first beggar who asks him for them. He does not care about his own life; he wishes only to watch some prince ascend the steep hill of ice and glass and wake the inexpressibly gently and beautiful little princess who lies there in a trance. Antins has seen the princess from afar in something like a dream and he has fallen in love with her fragility and delicacy. Later on it turns out that the beggar to whom Antins gave all he had was the "White Father"—God himself— who sends Antins, having now attained a stage of absolute selfdenial, wonderful horses, and Antins reaches the summit of the ice and glass hill on a golden horse and restores the princess back to life; he does not think himself worthy of her and so, though deeply in love with her, disappears again. But when the hfe of the princess is in danger (she feels the loss of a magic ring that she gave to Antins after he awakened her) he allows his fingers, into which the ring has grown, to be cut off, in order to save her. When that, too, does not help he at last ventures to go to her and to take her to wife. There can be no doubt that in this work of Rainis Antins symbolises that spiritual perfection and heroism that the man must possess who wants to free his nation, personified in the figure of the entranced princess. However, this type as well as the type of the unbalanced romantic mentioned above, are not the most usual of types; this is proved already by the writers who describe them, for they represent them as exception, as contrasts to the rest of the world. Still, perhaps, every Latvian soul possesses something of this romanticist, whose love of nature is somewhat exaggerated, of V Antins; innumerable folk-songs with their diminutives referring tc nature, with their warm and naive relation towards the world, and their great inner sincerity point to this. I think that these features of the Latvian soul do not contradict those that were mentioned before, but can be looked upon as consistent with them. I have pointed out already that the man of desires, ready for action and strife, was consistent with a dreamer. Every man of desires is really always a dreamer, but we do not notice this because all the potency of his soul changes to action. It is enough for this action to be impeded that the psychic forces should become independent and express themselves separately. Under these conditions the desires can lose egoism and turn towards some object above personal interests, especially nature, which is then looked upon as a deep being full of life, as the giver and creator of everything, as a mother who gives shelter tc those who come and look for it. Every active person of desires has moments that bring him near to the type of such a romanticist: continuous egoism is not possible, for it wearies, and therefore moments of rest, when a person turns from himself and rests from himself, are necessary. Therefore, nearly all Latvians without exception are great lovers of nature and travel. We can even say that in a certain sense nature is nearer to them than human beings. This can be explained partly by the fact that all these people of desires and emotions are in their hearts very sensitive and easily offended and therefore they do not like to reveal themselves. In society they generally wear armor, a shell consisting of willpower and insensibility which has often thistles of mockery and irony. Special circumstances are reeded for them to take off this armor, either a circle of trustworthy friends, or a lull in the activity of the mind, or a change oi direction: he must not think about possible harm and wounds, or he must not be capable of feeling these wounds — in a word, he must be a fool from the point oi view of the world. But after all the enthusiastic romanticist as well as the sincere Antins is the same Latvian man of desires, only here he has grown out of the egoism and one of these types has overcome his desires more completely; the other one less completely. In this way the concrete expression of a Latvian wavers between two poles. On the one hand we find the selfcontained and harsh man of action and strife with all his spiritual powers converted into activity; on the other, a dreamer and simpleton, intuitively turned to everything; the majority of Latvian has something also of the second type, and he has always some notion about the independent life of the mind and its values even though circumstances had carried him far away from this life. Thai this is so is shown by the enormous number of folksongs that are still often sung and also by the large number of lyric poets and the respect people feel for them, as, in general, for any creaors of spiritual values. Taking into consideration that we have, not only many poets, but also a large number of other artists — painters, sculptors, musicians — we understand why some observers and judges come to the conclusion that the Latvians have a special craving for beauty craving for beauty and that therefore the real call of the Latvians could be activity in. the realm of art However, for the time being we must ascertain the fact that the Latvians have turned their attention to the shaping of their concrete life and reveal in that activity the qualities indispensible to such builders. We can now look upon our attempt to sketch the psychology of , the Latvian people as finished. The result of our attempt we could perhaps summarise in the following way: the Latvians appear to us as a youthful nation which looks towards the future and is endowed with a spirit of endeavor that makes it strive constantly towards ever greater achievements. The basis of this spirit of endeavor appears to us to be the general desirous nature of the Latvian which shifts the centre of life from the present to the future and make it, if we may use this expression, "excentric." . We looked upon the possibility or "realising" one\'s personality as the central object of the Latvian\'s desires; moreover, characteristic of the Latvian is the coalition of his desires with the rationality of his mind which levels and harmonises the disequilibrium of his affective life. Various features of the Latvian character result from this; dissatisfaction, revolutionarism, the spirit of freedom, idealism, will-power, the sense of duty, the cult of justice, and — in less favorable cases — materialism, haughtiness, boastfulness, envy, unsociability, individualism. Because of its coalescing with desires, the thaugt of a Latvian is characterised by its proceeding towards a certain aim, by logicality, system, practicality, and at times a tendency towards doctrinairism and onesidedness. The predominance of desires and of the mind reflects itself inthTaffZHT life in such a way that the jl* * vian nature is free from tra*i * conflicts, the dynamics of the emr, i tions is restrained, which lean ■ on the one hand to their bein * , self-contained, to the narrowing of 4 t expansiveness and spontaneity t • the weakening of the ties of affection -tion with the surroundings and a" ; a result of this to a certain "ary I ness" and harshness; on the othPr : hand this leads to clarity of emo ; tions, to lyricism, romanticism and • even a certain panpsychism. pjn ; ally we pointed out that the vital : ity of the Latvian people expresses : itself naturally in joy and opti , mism, by which it is accompanied There can be no doubt that our . survey does not exhaust everything \'■ that could be said about the soul of our nation; only certain lines 1 of it have been traced here, and \' these too, can be questioned ; especially if we take into consideration the exceptionally large I number of individual varieties of ; peculiarities which, because of \' this, can never, so to say, be put into one boat. It is especially difficult to determine the mental creative powers of certain nations, for these are to a great extent dependent on certain specific conditions of life. We could perhaps regard the following conclusion as being a general rule; the more people devote themselves to action which is successful, the less they develop their "pure" mental life, and again, if something hinders the outer activity from developing then the inner spiritual activity is more intensive, so hat we can agree with the expression that the spirit or perception is really an impeded action. This circumstance must be taken into consideration when estimating the Latvian nation and its individual members; those moments when the nation has turned towards an outer activity are less productive in the sense of spiritual refinement, the same applies also to separate individuals. The same nation, however, at another period of time, and those of her members that live in peculiar circumstances will have other qualities that will seemingly not agree with those displayed in an other age and in different circumstances, but are still actually an expression of the same, forces. Therefore we should not look upon It as a reproach if someone were to point out those qualities mentioned in our sketch as contradictory. We must first of all remember that the character of a person, not to speak of that of a whole nation, is not a logical construction; it often contians contradictions bestowed by nature ib self, secondly, in the characterisation of some nation we must always take into consideration the variations of the main character— which depend on various secondary circumstances — that can express themselves antithetically when we try to explain ist. Only in this way can we explain that a Latvian can be at the same time an individualist, a materialist, a romanticist, a lyrist, a rigid and dry organiser, an everlasting dissatisfied striver and a friendly optimist full of joie de vivre. Let us not forget that the Latvian nation has not yet expressed itself, it is still full of various potentialities; to determine its character as unalterable and to forsee a course of development ordained by fate is quite impossible; the nation knows itself to be a young nation to whom all paths are open.']

Representative_Docs: ['ENGLISH LESSONS ARE FREE Free lessons in English are available to all people who have come to live in Australia and cannot speak the language. If you have relatives or friends who need help to learn English, you can assist them by telling them that they can have free lessons. Classes Classes are held in any place where there are six or more people to form a class. There are two classes at night, lasting for two hours each. A free textbook is supplied. Attendance at classes is the quickest way to learn the language. Correspondence Lessons If you live in a place where no class is held, and where there are not enough people to form one, you can have lessons sent to you by post. These are called correspondence lessons. The pupil sends his work to a teacher who corrects it and returns it to the pupil. This is not as quick a way of learning English as going to a class, and you should not ask for correspondence lessons unless you cannot attend a class.
