Name: 86_film_festival_feature
Representative_Docs: ['TWO NEW LATVIAN FEATURE FILMS The 22nd Montreal World Film Festival ended September 7, 1998, after a run of 10 days. As in previous years, an eager public came in droves to see the over 400 films presented from all over the world. Most of these films would be impossible to see in Montreal, due to a lack of theatres showing other than Hollywood movies. In tiie category of "Cinema of tomorrow – New trends" were two new Latvian feature films: \'The Shoe"(B3 min.) by Uila PAKALNIŅA (born 1962 in Liepāja, studied journalism, and then film, in Moscow). The story is set in the late 1950\'5, at the height of the Cold War, along the well-guarded Latvian coastline. Border patrols are omnipresent and each night tractors smooth the sand so that any foot prints would be shown. One morning a woman\'s shoe is found with footprints leading to nearby Liepāja, and a "dangerous Cinderella" search begins. The film is a German-Latvian co-production and with eleven short-films to her credit, this is her first feature ūhn. The second Latvian film is a Latvia-Canada co-production, filmed in Latvia with post-production work in Canada. It is a collaborative work by Mara RĀVIŅŠ (born in Toronto, studied ar the University of Toronto and Ontario College of Art, and worked in the film industry for the past decade as art director, director and producer) and Jānis KALĒJS (born in Riga, studied at the Theatre and Art Faculty of the liitvian State Conservatory\'; and immigrated to Canada 8 years ago, where he worked as assistant director, director and producer). The film "MORNING IN THE PINE FOREST" (110 min) looks in at the lives of six youths in their 20\'s in present day Latvia, searching for self-determination. The makers have not produced a docudrama nor journalism, nor is there a predictive formula to it. The central theme of young people\'s lives is treated in an "art-house" manner with the makers wanting to erase the border between documentary and fictional presentation. It is a difficult movie to categorize and not really made for the box office but there are elements which show that these people should make future films. The energy- of youth, their loneliness and particularly their music and emerging thinking can be engrossing on film. This film will liave its Latvian premiere at the Riga Film Festival this fall. In Latvia, during the Russian occupation Riga Film Studio produced about 10 feature films a year, with financing from the Russian propaganda machine. Now the production is about one feature film a year, with actors now mainly doing stage work. With co-production, however, these new films are showing the way. □ J. – J. Raudsepp The co-producers of "Morning in the Pine Forest", Jānis Kalējs and Māra Rāviņa at the 22nd Montreal Film Festival', 'Latvian films shown in more than ten countries this year Latvian films have been screened in more than ten countries this year. They have been screened at film festivals and expositions. In January, Latvian films were screened at eight events. For example, Laila Pakalnina\'s action film Hostage and short film Waferwere screened at the 18th Alpe Adria film festival in Italy. At a Children\'s Film Festival in the U.S.A., audiences were introduced to Dace Riduze\'s animation film Miega rA«Ai (literally Sleepy Gnomes) and Maris Brinkmanis animation film Faster, Munchausen, Faster. In early February, documentalist Here Franks\' film Ten Minutes O/c/erwas shown at an international documentary film festival in Spain. Ajointly produced Latvian-Estonian fulllength animation film Lotte from the Village of Inventors participated at the Berlin and Ottawa festivals, while participating in the festival film market were Director Viesturs Kairiss\' art film Dark Deer, and a joint Latvian, Austrian and UK production film: Midsummer\'s Night. Juris Freidenbergs, Office Administrator of the National Film Centre of Latvia, explained that the Latvian-Estonian production of Lotte from the Village of Inventors has also been screened in several film festivals which the Film Centre has not yet identified. The Estonians concern themselves with sending films to festivals," said Freidenbergs. During March, Latvian films were screened at 16 international events. The film Hostage has been screened at film festivals in Chicago (USA), Italy and in India. In a three-month period, Latvian films have also been seen in France, Sweden, the Czech Republic, Belgium, the UK, Hong Kong, Estonia and Greece.', 'The 25th annual Montreal "Festival des Films du Monde" took place August 23 to September 3, 2001. Over 12 days with more than 400,000 people in attendance, it showed itself, once again, to be immensely popular, and with over 200 screened films to choose from, from all corners of the world, it succesfully provided a fine introduction to and an overview of current world cinema (Each year, a detailed catalogue describing each of the films and the film makers is published and sold for $ 20.). The festival seemed ever better organized this year, and nice and mellow – 25 years of a fine Montreal tradition. One very welcome feature: all the daily press conferences with the film makers and actors were again open not only to the media but also to the general public – something other festivals do not all do. Baltic films were absent from the vie wings this year, but several film credits showed Estonian, Latvian, Lithuanian names signifying I guess that many of them are working outside the countries, gaining more and more experience (and foreign currency) for future efforts. This year, it was the turn of German cinema to be in the spotlight and they were here with quite a few films. At the "press previews", a week or so before the Festival, I saw more than a dozen very competent film workers, now they need the outstanding stories! And there were some good \'stories" too. At the Festival, two German films were particularly interesting. See them both, when possible! 1. Oliver Hirschspiegels (director). "The Experiment", a grandly educational, first rate experience from which we can learn something about human interaction – tigthly directed (for TV?) with an excellent engaging screenplay based on a book by Mario Giordano, about a controlled experiment in which 8 people acting as prison guards and 12 as prisoners interact – resulting in a fascinating dynamic development – a movie with enough tension there for three movies, from which you will emerge feeling having learned something; the viewer is really drawn into the drama. It was a box office first, well deserved,.in Germany and a film which can compete with the best from America, England or France, etc. \' From left to right: Oliver Hirschspiegel, director of the film "The Experiment", our intrepid reporter, J.-J. Raudsepp, and Norbert Preuss, producer of the film, at the German Film party (photo: Pierre Roussel). 2. Der Tunnel, directed by Roland Suso Richter. Definitely one of the most popular movies at the Festival. In two screenings, I didn\'t see a single seat empty, including all three levels of the \'Theatre Maisonneuve1 of the Place des Arts. A good story, well told and Martin Langer\'s photography was remarkable – It is always difficult to do tunnel pictures and it was remarked that the construction of the set itself was even more difficult then digging the real tunnel, on which the story is based. Try to see it! As always, films were seen, parties and receptions were attended, homages and tributes were given to• film makers and stars (Sophia Loren, Jackie Chan, Fernando Solanos, Francisco Rabal, Roberto Rosselini) and official juries, awarded various prices. The festival Grand Prix des I\'AMERIQUE was awarded jointly to two films: Ihe Hungarian film "Torzok" ("Abandoned") by Arpad Sopsits, director-screen writer, an excellent film, based on personal experiences; and the Iranian film "Baran" by Majid Majidi a beautifully shot film and simple story about Afghan réfugies m Iran – both excellent films and well deserving of the Grand award – you can not go wrong m seeing either or both of them. 25th MONTREAL WORLD FILM FESTIVAL – and many other films as well from this festival\'s menu! As the organizers put it, the real stars of the Festival are the films. Left to right: Tributes for career achievement: Argentinian director Fernando Solanas and Spanish film star, Francisco Rabal. And the poste for the Festival (Photo: Pierre Roussel). J.-J.Raudsepp']

Name: 87_baltic_state_soviet_union
Representative_Docs: ['Dr. Edgar Anderson on GREAT BRITAIN AND THE BALTIC PROBLEM AFTER WORLD WAR I In two recent articles for the Journal of Central European Affairs* professor of history Edgar Anderson has analyzed the role of Britain in the creation of the Baltic republics after World War I. At the collapse of the Tsarist Russian government in November, 1917, the Western Powers were faced with a thorny dilemma: the previously subject nations of the Russian empire showed aspirations to self-government, basing their claims on Allied declarations of people\'s rights to self-determination. These declarations, however, had not been formulated with these nations in mind. On the one hand, the Allies were afraid of creating a precedent for empire-breaking which affected a former and possibly a future ally and which might eventually affect themselves. On the other hand, they were glad about any anti-German uprisings, because after their peace treaty with the Bolsheviks, the Germans had to fight on only one front. Consequently, the Allies took no stand on the issue. In January, 1918, the British were contacted by the first representatives of Latvia and Estonia. They assured these representatives of the sympathy of the British government and of its unwillingness that these countries should fall under German rule. However, very soon the whole Baltic States region did fall under German occupation, legalized in subsequent treaties with the Bolsheviks. The necessary precedent for interference in Russian affairs came about with the Allied landings at Murmansk and Vladivostok to secure their stores against the Germans. The strength of anti-Bolshevik and anti-German attitudes among Baltic nationals influenced Britain to grant a temporary de facto recognition of Estonia as early as May 3, 1918. Britain granted such recognition to Latvia on November 11, 1918. The prickly question of de jure recognition of the Baltic republics was postponed until the Paris Peace Conference. Meanwhile, the British representatives asked assistance in the form of military supplies for the Baltic States from the Scandinavian countries, which were, however, afraid to interfere. Dr. Anderson writes: "In his memoirs the British diplomat Herbert A. Grant-Watson says that Great Britain was the first of the Western Allies to take a practical interest in the struggle for freedom of the three Baltic peoples and to realize that they had advanced sufficiently to form independent nations, capable of deciding their own political future." (I, p. 278) When the German empire suddenly collapsed and the Bolsheviks set up their own Baltic governments and invaded the area, the Allies formulated a clause in the German peace treaty whereby German forces would remain in the Baltic until no longer needed. In November, 1918, a British naval force under Rear Admiral Edwyn S. Alexander-Sinclair was dispatched to the Baltic to gain reliable information about conditions there and to aid the Baltic nationals in fighting the Bolsheviks. The force reached Liepāja in December, established there its main base, and procee*"The British Policy Toward the Baltic States 1918-1920," JCEA, XIX (1960), 276-289; "An Undeclared Naval War: The British-Soviet Naval Struggle in the Baltic, 1918-1920," JCEA, XXII (1962), 43-78. hi the abstract, page references to the first article will be preceded by I, to the second by 11. ded to Tallinn, arriving in time to prevent the collapse of the Estonian republic and to deliver badly needed arms to the Estonians. Thereupon the fleet turned back to Riga. An official communique issued at a conference between British officials and the Latvian government on December 17, 1918, stated that "a part of the British fleet will remain permanently at Riga. The British Government will also provide the Latvian Provisional Government with arms and other needed supplies for the organization of the Latvian State and its struggle against the Bolsheviks." (11, p. 47) The expected collapse of the Bolshevik government did not take place. Instead, the Bolsheviks, in the wake of the retreating German armies, proceeded with their occupation of the Baltic States. In view of the confusing situation there and the unwillingness on the part of the other Allies to intervene physically, Lloyd George ordered the British naval forces to leave Riga in January, 1919, and subsequently relieved Rear Admiral Alexander-Sinclair of his command. The Bolsheviks occupied most of the Baltic area, while a German general, yon der Goltz, established himself in the western corner of Latvia and Lithuania with designs of converting the Baltic into a German province and of eventually restoring the Imperial German government. In view of these developments, the British made an about-face and dispatched another naval squadron, now under the command of Rear Admiral Sir Walter H. Cowan, to the Baltic in order to check the Bolshevik occupation and to aid the anti-Bolshevik and anti-German forces. The British government had deliberately left Cowan without specific instructions,  he proceeded to reestablish the British base at Liepāja, much to the chagrin of the Germans and the pleasure of the Latvian government. A British military mission was established in Liepāja and through the efforts of a Major Keenan and Sir Stephan Tallents, who had seen conditions at first hand, the Allied Supreme Council organized several commissions in April, 1919, to deal with the political and economic situation in the Baltic. However, in a surprise move, the Germans overthrew the Latvian government and re-conquered much of the Bolshevik-occupied territory. In May, 1919, the Allied Commission on Baltic Affairs, because of the conflicting interests of various Allies, including, now, a White Russian representative, decided to regard the Baltic States as a buffer between Germany and Russia, but not to extend to them official recognition. During this time the chief of the British Military Mission in Finland and the Baltic States, General Sir Hubert Gough, in the absence of any official British policy of intervention, exerted all his influence for the good of the legitimate Baltic governments. The Baltic troops managed, with British assistance, to defeat the Germans, who signed an armistice treaty in July, 1919, and retreated from northern Latvia. A British officer was then appointed commander of the German forces in the Baltic, the so-called Landeswehr, and was instructed to use them against the Bolsheviks. Because of the impossibility of obtaining common agreement among the Allies, the very necessary\' economic assistance to these countries was meantime left to private organizations. When the efforts of General Gough to gain de jure recognition of the Baltic States went too far, Lloyd George relieved him of his duties. He was replaced by General Marsh who favored White Russian aspirations and attempted to create a common anti-Bolshevik front composed of the forces of the Baltic States, Poland, the White Russian Yudenich, and an army of German renegades (formerly under yon der Goltz and including some Russian prisoners of war) under Bermondt. While the Allied heads of state repeatedly failed to as;ree on a common and consistent Baltic policy, British naval activities in the Baltic Sea had almost succeeded in putting an end to the Bolshevik navy, and the Baltic forces had almost succeeded in expelling the Bolsheviks from their countries. The British government, however, while not holding out any promise of assistance or recognition of independence, instructed its military missions to discourage strongly any peace treaty between the Bolsheviks and the Baltic States which might not coincide with Allied wishes. Dr. Anderson gives the following illustration of the conflicting attitudes among British heads of state themselves in the fall of 1919: "On September 20, Churchill wrote to Lloyd George asking him to recognize the independence of the Baltic States at once... \'for in a little while it would be too late to secure terms for these small, free states which had given aid against Bolshevism.\' Lloyd George answered with unusual frankness on September 22: You want us to recognize their independence in return for their attacking the Bolsheviks. They would ask us to (1) guarantee it, (2) supply them with equipment and cash to fight with. And in the end, whoever won in Russia, the Government there would promptly recover the old Russian (sic) Baltic ports. Are you, Mr. Churchill, prepared to go to war with perhaps an anti-Bolshevik Government of Russia to prevent that? If not, it would be a disgraceful piece of deception o:i our part to give any guarantee to those new Baltic States that you are proposing to use to conquer Russia... You won\'t find another responsible person in the whole land who will take your view, why waste your energy and your usefulness on this vain fretting...?" (I, p. 286) Accordingly, the British government withdrew any stipulations on a Baltic-Bolshevik peace treaty. In October, 1919, Bermondt\'s German forces, which were supposedly part of the common anti-Bolshevik front, turned on the Latvian troops. The Allies immediately declared a blockade of Germany in the Baltic and it was enforced by Admiral Cowan and his naval force. When Bermondt ignored an ultimatum to withdraw from the fortress Daugavgriva, he was fired upon by combined British-French naval forces in the neighborhood of Riga. With the help of these naval detachments, the Latvians defeated Bermondt\'s forces at Riga and Liepāja and the remnants of these forces were evacuated under an Allied commission. In December, 1919, the British removed from the Baltic all warships except for a cruiser and several destroyers. The policy of the British government by this time had been settled in favor of an independent block of Baltic nations. The British thus had achieved a satisfactory disengagement in the Baltic. In view of the British attitude, the Baltic States signed peace treaties with Soviet Russia and renewed their efforts to secure de jure recognition and admission to the League of Nations. This recognition was extended by the Allied Supreme Council to Latvia and Estonia on January 26, 1921, but to Lithuania only in December, 1922. All of the Baltic countries were admitted to the League of Nations on September 21, 1921. Through the material and military assistance Britain rendered the Baltic States in the years 1918-1920, and through the watchful presence of a token naval force in the Baltic afterwards, she earned deep gratitude on the part of the Baltic peoples. In summing up, Dr. Anderson writes: "The zigzagging, meandering and wavering of the British policy toward the Baltic States could be partly explained as the result of the considerable disagreement among the Allies in regard to the future of those countries, and partly as a result of the disagreement between the British military men and diplomats and even among the diplomats themselves. Those who fought for the independence of the Baltic States finally won. Grant Watson proudly says in regard to the Baltic States: *We gave.. .active and practical assistance in the moment when their fate hung in the balance.\' The Baltic States needed no more than that." (I, p. 289) 0. Krātiņš', 'THE BALTIC QUESTION IN WORLD POLITICS The following is a summary of an article by Dr. Boris Meissner on the Baltic question in world politics, published in the German Baltic monthly magazine Baltische Briefe, July/August, 1966, No. 7/8. Dr. Meissner,. of Estonian descent, who is being considered in Germany as one of the outstanding authorities in East European affairs, covers in his article a wide field ranging from the emergence of the three independent Baltic Republics in 1918 to their absorption into the Soviet Union and their present colonial status. The same article was also published in the September issue of the German monthly Europaeische Begegnung. We shall dwell in this summary particularly on the part of the article which deals with the legal aspects of the Soviet annexation of Estonia, Latvia, and Lithuania. * The East European nations, having emerged as independent states after the First World War, were immediately concerned with safeguarding their freedom by forming a defensive alliance against future encroachment on them by their big neighbors east and west. Attempts to form a bloc of mutual assistance comprising the nations from the Baltic to the Black Seas failed due to the diversity of size and interests of the states involved. The greatest and final obstacle arose over the Polish-Lithuanian conflict concerning Wilno. Of all attempts of creating a common defense policy there remained only a Latvian-Estonian alliance which was ratified in November, 1923. The Baltic Republics signed nonaggression pacts with the Soviet Union in 1926 and 1932. Similar pacts were concluded with Nazi Germany in spring, 1939. But, as subsequent events show, they were to no avail. In 1936, Stalin\'s lieutenant, Zhdanov, announced in a speech the Red Army\'s readiness to widen Russia\'s window to the West in the direction of the Baltic States. With Hitler coming to power in Germany, the postwar period of reasonable security for the East Central European nations had come to an end. In 1939, Hitler selected Poland as his next target of aggression. This led to an attempt of Britain and France, who had obliged themselves to defend Poland\'s integrity, to seek a common front with the Soviet Union, being convinced that only the creation of a bloc of formidable resistance would discourage the aggressor before the shooting started. It turned out later, however, that the Kremlin was simultaneously and secretly negotiating with Hitlerite Germany. Soviet demands in both sets of negotiations centered on special arrangements, or "guarantees" regarding Poland and the Baltic States. But Poland was not prepared to grant the Red Army rights of transit through its territory in advance ol war. The Baltic States, on the other hand, were fearful of the Soviet conception of "indirect aggression which they rightfully considered to be a pretext put forward by Moscow to occupy them at will when the time is ripe. While Britain hesitated to sacrifice the freedom of the Baltic States, Hitler was less scrupulous, and thus he won the contest concluding on August 23, 1939, the infamous Nazi-Soviet pact of friendship, which was accompanied by secret protocols dealing with partition of Poland, and turning the Baltics over to the mercy of Stalin. Dr. Meissner dispels subsequent Soviet allegations of a British-German collusion at the expense of the USSR, by sayingIt is conclusively plain that the Baltic question, viewed particularly in connection with the Polish issue, did play a substantial part in the negotiations prior to the Second World War. The research, here presented, serves unquestionable evidence that, next to Hitler, it was Stalin who took an active part in unleashing the holocaust of World War 11. In the fall of 1939, after Germany and Russia had invaded Poland, the Kremlin compelled Estonia, Latvia, and Lithuania to sign so-called pacts of mutual assistance with the Soviet Union, granting the latter military bases on their territories in exchange of Soviet pledges not to interfere in their internal affairs and sovereignty. But the following year in June, the Baltic States were served with unprovoked ultimata which led, first, to unlimited Soviet military occupation, and soon thereafter to annexation and sovietization. Says Dr. Meissner: The methods of communist seizure of power and of subsequent sovietization are nowhere more plainly visible than they were in the case of the Baltic States. The author also observes that today the "federal" Baltic Republics of the USSR enjoy less autonomy than they did under the tsarist regime, when they were mere provinces of the Russian Empire. * The last part of Dr. Meissner\'s paper is devoted to the legal aspects of the Soviet annexation of the Baltics, examined in terms of international law. The claim of the Kremlin that the Baltic nations have voluntarily joined the USSR are groundless. They were arbitrarily occupied by Red armed forces. So-catied elections on June 15 and 16, 1940, with only one pro-communist slate of candidates permitted, were a violation of democratic procedures, Soviet-Baltic treaties, and of the constitutions of the independent Baltic States. The pressure of the Soviet armed forces and secret police on the voters and the candidates alike precluded any exercise of free choice and preservation of national sovereignty. The United States reacted swiftly to the "devious processes" of the Soviet invaders in the Baltics. On July 23, 1940, acting Secretary of State, Sumner Welles, issued a strong declaration refusing to recognize the Soviet absorption of Estonia, Latvia, and Lithuania. This initial attitude of justice prevails to the present day. Each year hence, the Government of the U. S. has come forth with renewed condemnation of the Soviet rape of Baltic independence. Soviet apologies, says Dr. Meissner, that the occupation of the Baltic Republics in 1940 was justified by Russia\'s concern of selfpreservation, is contradicted by the fact that her armed forces were already stationed on the Baltic territories in accordance with the treaties of October, 1939. Besides, Hitler\'s intention of attacking the Soviet Union was far from evident at that time. Even granting validity to later Soviet fabrications as to why it was necessary to occupy the Baltic countries while Russia was at peace with Germany, they fail to provide an explanation why Estonia, Latvia, and Lithuania are still being held in colonial captivity long after the menace of German aggression has been eliminated. Moscow, surely, had no legal justification, other than the law of the jungle, to impose on the hapless Baltic nations unwanted revolutionary social changes shaped on the Soviet pattern. The Western community of free nations, having never accepted the Kremlin\'s claim that the Baltic peoples joined the Soviet Union voluntarily, is still principally adhering to the Sumner Welles declaration of nonrecognition of the Soviet absorption of Estonia, Latvia, and Lithuania. During the most crucial period of the war there was, indeed, a tendency in the United States and Britain to seek an accommodation regarding the future of the Baltics on Stalin\'s terms. But it came to nought, because of the unsaturable appetite of the Kremlin for much bigger gains in East Central Europe and the Far East. At the present time the United States is still in the lead of upholding the legal prerogatives of the formerly independent Baltic nations, while Britain and France have compromised by recognizing the "de facto" inclusion of the Baltic Republics in the Soviet Union, but not their annexation "de jure, " Western Germany\'s stand in relation to Baltic sovereignty is interesting, insofar as the Hitler-Stalin agreement still looms in the background. According to Dr. Meissner, A German-Soviet Russian covenant of January 10, 1941 on border regulations was never ratified owing to the outbreak of the German-Russian war some months thereafter. Thus Germany has never recognized the Soviet annexation of the Baltic States "de jure"; and the present Bonn Government adheres to this fact in relation to Baltic nationals. Dr. Meissner\'s conclusions regarding the future of the Baltic nations are worth while quoting: Restoration of national independence of the Baltic States may seem to be irrelevant today, because the existing balance of power and the unbroken will of the Soviet Union for expansion seemingly excludes such possibility. However, power constellations are transitory and the intensity of extreme political doctrines tends to erode in time. It is entirely possible to envisage a situation where the interests of Russia, concerned with renovation of its image, may welcome a solution of the Baltic question according to the principles of international law, or at least grant the Baltic Republics the right of genuine self-determination.', "ALL WE ASK IS FREEDOM American Latvian Association Latvian Federation of Australia and New Zealand European Committee for Latvian Freedom Latvian National Federation In Canada Latin American Latvian Association The following is the text of the opening remarks given by Dr. Oļģerts R. Pavlovskis, president of the World Federation of Free Latvians, during the Baltic Tribunal in Copenhagen, July 25th and 26th. For almost 4000 years the Baltic people have lived on the shores of the Baltic Sea. For 4000 years Baits have tilled the land and prospered. For 4000 years Baits have been able to repulse attempts at foreign domination, in order to further the'development of their culture, 4000 years of heritage. Until the two greatest tyrannies of modern times — the Soviet Union and Nazi Germany — joind forces. 46 years ago, in the fall of 1939, the Soviet Union and the German Reich signed a secret protocol. Within a few months, between June 15—17, 1940, while the Western world was stunned by Hitler's actions in the West, in Eastern Europe Soviet armored divisions quietly crossed the borders of the Baltic countries and occupied Estonia, Latvia, and Lithuania. Today the Baltic countries — once free and independent republics — are under Soviet military occupation. The Soviet government has eliminated almost all human, national and religious rights of the Latvian people. To the mass arrests and deportations in the 1940*5, 1950's and early sixties new tactics and methods have been added in order to carry out the Soviet government's destructive Russification and genocide policies against the Baltic peoples. Incarceration in psychiatric institutions and mind control by drugs are only two of the latest and most insidious developments in the Soviet prison system. Today, after 4000 years of existence, only 53 percent of the population in occupied Latvia is Latvian — a direct result of Soviet government's policies. Baits have been made second class citizens in their own countries. Their culture, their land and their industry are exploited and subjugated to the goals of the Soviet empire. Baltic youths are forced to fight and die in Afghanistan for Soviet interests. The Baltic Sea, once peaceful and prosperous, has been turned into a huge Soviet nuclear naval base for its growing armada, where boarding of foreign vessels and intrusions in foreign waters by the Soviet navy has become almost commonplace. We, the Baltic people, do not ask for territorial gains. We do not ask for power. But we DO DEMAND for Estonians, Latvians and Lithuanians, the right to live as free and peaceful people in our lands and to exercise our God given rights. All we ask is FREEDOM. WHEREAS the armed forces of the Union of Soviet Socialist Republics (hereinafter referred to as the USSR or the Soviet Union) have occupied Estonia, Latvia, and Lithuania, and WHEREAS the USSR has established a totalitarian dictatorship in Estonia, Latvia, and Lithuania and has deprived the peoples of the Baltic States of the possibility of expressing their will and managing their own affairs in political, economic, cultural, and religious fields, this indictment is submitted on behalf of the Estonian, Latvian, and Lithuanian nations by the Baltic World Conference (hereinafter referred to as the BWC). The BWC is a federation of the three Supreme Executive bodies of each of the Baltic nationalities and their representative organizations in the Free World, namdy, the Estonian World Council, the World Federation of Free Latvians, and the Supreme Committee for the Liberation of Lithuania. Each of these three central organizations defends, respektively, as negotiorum gestor the interests of the Estonian nation, the Latvian nation, and the Lithuanian nation, in concurrence with the diplomatic representatives of the independent republics of Estonia, Latvia, and Lithuania, who are accredited in the United States of America and other countries, and seeks, as the ultimate goal, an end to the illegal Soviet occupation with the subsequent re-establishment of the independent and democratic states of Estonia. Latvia, and Lithuania. ALL WE ASK IS FREEDOM... INDICTMENT The BWC accuses the Soviet Union, under international law, of the following crimes against humanity and against the States and Peoples of Estonia, Latvia, and Lithuania. A. Conspiracy, Aggression, and the Illegal Annexation Baltic States 1. On August 23, 1939, the Soviet Union, in collusion with Nazi Germany, signed a mutual nonaggression treaty, known as the Molotov-Ribbentrop Pact with secret protocols attached and supplemented on September 28, dividing Eastern Europe into spheres of influence. According to the terms of the pact, Finland, Estonia, Latvia, Lithuania, Bessarabia, and Northern Bukovina came under Soviet Russian domination. By this act of conspiracy, the Soviet Union violated Article 10 of the Covenant of the League of Nations and Article 1 of the Kellogg-Briand Pact, and it shares the responsibility with the German Reich in causing World War 11. 2. By implementing the Molotov-Ribbentrop Pact, the Soviet Union, by use of military force, coerced the signing of the Mutual Assistance Treaties with Estonia on September 24, 1939; with Latvia on October 5, 1939; and with Lithuania on October 10, 1939. The Mutual Assistance Treaties provided for the garrisoning of Soviet troops in the respective neutral and sovereign Baltic republics and were instrumental in carrying out the military occupation of Lithuania on June 15, 1940; Estonia on June 16, 1940; and Latvia on June 17, 1940 (occupatio bellica). These military acts were in direct violation of a number of international treaties which the Soviet Union had signed with Estonia, Latvia, and Lithuania in 1920, 1926, 1929, 1932, 1934, and 1939. They were also a willful violation of the Convention for the Definition of Aggression of July 5, 1933. 3. The Soviet Union established the Soviet system of government in the Baltic States, annexing Lithuania on August 3, 1940, Latvia on August 5, 1940, and Estonia on August 6, 1940, thereby incorporating the three countries into the U.S.S.R. B. Exploitation of the Baltic States 1. The Soviet Union has deprived the Baltic States of their status as countries committed to the ideals of law and the promotion of peace in Europe. Through the installation of rocket and submarine bases which far exceed any normal national needs, the U.S.S.R. has turned the Baltic States into a place d'armes. The implementation of such measures forces the Baltic States into serving Soviet offensive designs against other peaceful European countries. 2. In violation of international law, the Soviet Union is drafting Estonian, Latvian, and Lithuanian youths into its armed forces. They are compelled to serve outside of the Baltic States and are forced to take part in military actions against other countries, i.e., Hungary in 1956, Czechoslovakia in 1968, Afghanistan since December 27, 1979. 3. The Soviet Union is economically exploiting Estonia, Latvia, and Lithuania. Accelerated industrialization is used to gain greater political control over the Baltic States, but not to improve their economies or to make them more efficient. 4. The Soviet Union, in exercising its colonial-master's powers, discriminates against the ethnic Baits in employment, housing, education, communications, the arts, and other endeavors and services. 5. Soviet colonial rule in Estonia, Latvia, and Lithuania, based on alien Russian domination and exploitation, constitutes a violation of the United Nations Organization's Charter and is especially a violation of the Declaration on the Granting of Independence to Colonial Countries and Peoples of December 14, 1960. C. Deportation and Systematic Russification of Estonians, Latvians and Lithuanians with the Purpose of Eliminating Their National Identities, Cultures and Languages. 1. The Soviet Union has deprived the Estonians, Latvians, and Lithuanians of their national self-determination, destroyed their democratic way of life, and subjected them to a totalitarian, imperialistic regime. 2. The Soviet Union is steadily decreasing the proportions of the native populations in the Baltic States. a. During the 45-year Soviet occupation of Estonia, Latvia, and Lithuania, approximately one million Baltic nationals have either been deported to Soviet forced labor camps or resettled to distant areas within the Soviet Union. b. The Soviet Government is systematically resettling Soviet Russians and other non-Baltic nationalities into Estonia, Latvia, and Lithuania, while coercing the Baltic people by various means to relocate to distant lands within the Soviet Union. 3. The Soviet Union is destroying the phusical and national-political existence of the Estonian, Latvian, and Lithuanian people. It is deliberately imposing conditions of life which are designed to rob the people of their potential of resuming their status as sovereign nations, to which they are entitled under the right of self-determination. 4. The Soviet Union is systematically pursuing a policy of Russification of the Estonian, Latvian, and Lithuanian languages and the cultures of the Baltic nations. D. Violations of Human Rights 1. The Soviet Union has deprived the peoples of Estonia, Latvia, and Lithuania of basic freedoms and fundamental human rights, such as the freedom of thought, conscience, religion or belief, freedom of opinion and expression, freedom of peaceful assembly and association, and the right to a fair and public hearing and independent tribunal. 2. Threats of imprisonment or placement in psychiatric institutions are used to quell all forms of dissent. INTRODUCTORY SUMMARY The Baltic World Conference will present detailed evidence, eyewitness testimony and documentation, to support the abovementioned charges. Our purpose is to demonstrate to this Tribunal, and thus to the world, that the actions and policies undertaken by the Soviet Union and its leaders, have been designed with one primary goal in mind: to inflict on Estonia, Latvia, and Lithuania conditions of life calculated to bring about their destruction as nations. Furthermore, we will demonstrate, that these actions are an assault, not only on the well-being of the Baltic peoples, but all freedom-loving peoples throughout the world. We will show that Soviet policies in the Baltic States are contrary to international law, and thus all universally recognized codes of human decency and justice."]

Name: 88_energy_prime_minister_forum
Representative_Docs: ['Baltie States\' Ministers discuss increasing independence in energy supply On 26 January, Latvian Minister for Economics Krišjānis Karins was in Lithuania on a working visit, where he took part in a seminar "Development of Electricity Markets and Security of Supply in the Baltic Sea Region". The seminar was organised by the Lithuanian Ministry of Economics in collaboration with Lithuanian Energy supplier Lietuvos Enerģija AB. The aim of the seminar was to discuss overall cooperation between the Baltic States in matters of energy supply such as ensuring adequate supply of electricity to the Baltic market, widening of the market, and future operation of the Ignalina nuclear power station. During the Seminar, Minister Karins emphasised Latvia\'s interests: increasing independence and safety of energy supply, to be ensured through estabishing of a joint Baltic-European energy system, increasing the variety of primary energy resource suppliers and increasing energy selfsufficiency. During the seminar Minister Karins briefed on Latvia\'s Energy Development Guidelines for 2006-2016. Seminar participants passed a joint declaration on the energy situation in the Baltics and future development, emphasising, at Latvia\'s initiative, the need to create and implement a joint EU energy policy. A basic objective of the policy is to create a balanced long-term energy resource structure, consisting of various sources of energy. During 2006 an overall Baltic States\' energy strategy is to be developed. Declaration of the Workshop "Development of the electricity markets and security of supply in the Baltic Sea region". Taking part in the seminar were ministers for energy matters from Estonia, Finland, Latvia, Lithuania, Poland and Sweden, together with representatives from ministries, public services regulators, electricity producers and consumer associations. As part of his visit to Lithuania, Minister Karins met with Lithuanian Minister for Economics Kestutis Dauksys. The Ministers discussed bilateral economic cooperation between Latvia and Lithuania, with particular emphasis on future cooperation in the energy sector, as well as topical EU issues and external trade matters.', "11th Baltic Economic Forum to Focus on Raising Regional Competitiveness On September 23 – 24, Rīga will host the yearly Baltic Economic Forum. On the agenda of this year's forum are discussions among business people, politicians, and experts on the experience gained by the Baltic countries when dealing with the global economic and financial crisis, on the possibilities and challenges for raising the competiveness of the region according to the European Union's Strategy for the Baltic Sea Region. On September 24, invited to participate in a special roundtable discussion are the Prime Ministers of the Baltic states – Prime Minister of Latvia. Valdis Dombrovskis, Prime Minister of Estonia, Andrus Ansip, and Prime Minister of Lithuania, Andrius Kubilius. The Commissioner for Regional Policy of the European Commission Johannes Hahn is scheduled to open the discussion, and the Commissioner for Development of the European Commission Andris Piebalgs will also participate. On September 23, the Minister of Economics Artis Kampars and the Governor of the Bank of Latvia Ilmārs Rimšēvičs will give presentations. The Minister of Economy and Communication of Estonia Juhan Parts is also expected tc participate. Business leaders from the Baltic countries will share their experiences on obstacles that hinder a full use of the opportunities provided by the common EU market, and will discuss what should be done to improve conditions for business in the region. During the Forum a debate will be held on the impact of the global crisis on the banking sector and future scenarios, organised by the Association of Latvian Commercial Banks and hosting bank directors and financial experts from the region. The role of corporate governance in state enterprises will be also be discussed from different aspects, for instance, the options to privatize a company or to run it more efficiently. Discussions on several co-operation priorities under the EU Strategy for the Baltic Sea Region are also planned. In a separate session, Ministers of Transport from Latvia and Lithuania will engage in a dialogue with business representatives and experts regarding the issue of how to facilitate the Baltic Sea Region's availability in the sector of transport. The closing session of the Forum will be on the availability of the financial resources and the role of the public and private funding. Registration is open until September 15 and the number of participants is restricted. For further information and registration, please, visit: www. balticeconomicforum.com", "11th Baltic Economic Forum to Focus on Raising Regional Competitiveness On September 23 – 24, Rīga will host the yearly Baltic Economic Forum. Name: 89_privatisation_lasco_ebrd_company
Representative_Docs: ['NEW LATVIAN GOVERNMENT DECLARATION FORESEES COMPLETING PRIVATISATION WITHIN ONE YEAR The new Government declaration, issued on 14 July, foresees completing the privatisation process in Latvia within one year, i.e., the Government and Privatisation Agency are to adopt privatisation terms of large state-owned enterprises and start their privatisation. Latvenergo and Lattelekom privatisation should take place taking into account the interests of consumers so that not to allow a formation of uncontrollable private monopolies, the declaration says.', "EBRD LAUNCHES NEW STRATEGY FOR LATVIA RIGA, 21 Dec, BNS – The European Bank for Reconstruction and Development (EBRD) has developed a new strategy for operations in Latvia. The EBRD will do more power, rail and municipal infrastructure deals in Latvia, moreover, the bank will in particular seek to do more infrastructure deals that do not require a guarantee from the central government, said the EBRD director in the Baltic states George Krivicky. The bank will also focus on supporting the privatisation of remaining large-scale businesses in the shipping and oil transhipment areas, subject to suitable conditions being met, and other large-scale projects to help attract foreign direct investment. The EBRD has offered Latvian officials that it would join privatisation, for example, of the Latvian Shipping Company (LASCO). Krivicky said the aim of the bank's offer is to create a situation in which there is higher investor confidence in LASCO privatisation and conviction that it is safe and will proceed well. He noted though the bank has not yet discussed with government officials the new situation created by approval of the new privatisation model envisaging to sell 51% of LASCO shares on bourse forcash. Krivicky said the bank also has ideas about privatisation of Ventspils NaftaoW terminal but the EBRD would not make them public for tine time being. The EBRD is keen to provide selective support to banks to assist in consolidation of the financial sector. The bank also aims to support the development of non-bank financial institutions, particularly in areas such as leasing, mortgage finance, insurance and private pension funds. The EBRD will give priority to the development of small and medium-sized enterprises throughout Latvia. Presently the EBRD's credit facility for small and medium-sized businesses in Latvia is served by Latvijas Unibanka. Krivicky said the co-operation with Unibanka is successful which is indicated by the fact that the EBRD has extended to Unibanka he second credit facility of 10 million euros. He noted though that in the area of lending to small and medium-sized businesses the EBRD on principle wants to co-operate with more than one institution in Latvia. Assessing Latvia's development in general Krivicky said that on the one hand the country shows big progress while on the other hand many things still should be done. He especially noted Latvia's external vulnerability which remains high given Latvia's current account deficit. The genera! vulnerability of developing markets towards external shocks has grown lately in the worid. The EBRD cited improvement of the investment environment, completion of privatisation, introduction of the new commercial law, elimination of corruption, small and medium enterprise development, promotion of local authorities' work and improvement of regulatory bodies work as important factors for Latvia's development. The EBRD to date has joined 26 projects in Latvia worth 300 million euros and helped to mobilise an additional 740 million euros in investment from sponsors and co-financiers for a total project value of over 1 billion euros.", "LATVIAN GOVERNMENT APPROVES LASCO PRIVATISATION PROCEDURES The government on 17 August approved procedures for the long-delayed privatisation of the Latvian Shipping Company (LASCO), one of the world's largest shipping companies. According to these procedures, the authorities plan tc sell 44% of the company to a strategic investor, with part of the proceeds being used to satisfy claims of employees at bankrupt state enterprises. The government will offer an additional 15% of LASCO to holders of privatisation certificates, while reserving 6% for LASCO employees. Economics Minister Vladimirs Makarovs said LASCO's privatisation should be completed by 1 September 2000. Privatisation Agency head Janis Naglis predicted that the first-round auction could be completed by 1 December 1999."]

Name: 90_bait_prussian_century
Representative_Docs: ['THE KINGS OF THE BALTIC PEOPLE — PART II By N. Bojārs, Ph. D. The Division of the Baits In the preceding paper\' the prehistoric origin of the Baits was briefly discussed and the maximum expansion of the Baits around the Ist century A.D. was shown in Map I. It was based largely upon the researches by the Lithuanian scientist K. Būga2; the territority of the Baits3 then covered approximately 430,000 km sq. or 166,000 square miles. The Baltic lands then were larger than East and West Germany put together, in our times, or larger than the state of California. The history of the Kurs was summarized in Table I. The traditional division of the Baits is given in Table 11, and it is described in more detail in books by Stalšans,2 Jurgēla,4 and in other sources. The division of the ancient Prussians is taken from a chronicle of Prussia;5 as it is evident from the names, a minor part of the Galinds and Sudavs (Yatvings) inhabited ancient Prussia, while their major settlements ranged far east and south, in the territories occupied in our times by Poland and Russia. TABLE 11. THE NATIONS AND TRIBES OF THE BALTS BEFORE 1200 A. D. Nation of the Baits Area occupied, in modern Tribes geographic terms GALINDS (Galindae, Russia to Lake Ilmen, East Galinds (center around Golyad) Yaroslavl on the Volga, Smolensk) ; South Galinds near Moscow, Tula, Kursk (to Pannonia in the 3rd (until 6th cent. A.D.) cent. A.D.) ; West Galinds (in Prussia). YATVINGS (Jatweg, North Poland to the Vis- East Yatvings (around the Soudinoi, Sudavians) tula, Bug, and Pripet; Pripet and Dniepr) and White Russia and the West Yatvings (Sudavians, Ukraine to Upper Dniepr south of Lithuania). Ancient PRUSSIANS East Prussia and North Colms, Pomesans, Pogesans, (Borussi, Prusi, Prutheni, Poland from Danzig to the Warms, Natangs, Sambs, Sembi) Vistula and Bug Nadravs, Scalovs, Sudavs, (Sudavians), Galinds, Barths Ancient LITHUANIANS Central part of the Baltic Aukshtaitians (High or East (Lettowe, Litvini, Littauer) lands mcl. modern Lithua- Lithuanians) and Zhemaitinia and partly White Rus- ans (Lowlands or West Lisia (Grodno-Gardinas, thuanians, Samogithians or Minsk, Vitebsk, Polotsk) Samagites, northwest of Nemunas and Nevezhis). Ancient LATVIANS Modern Latvia and some Kurs (Curones, in Kurze(Lethi, Letti, Lotva) borderlands to the south me), Zemgals (Semigalli, in and east central Zemgale and south), Seles (in east Zemgale), and Letgals (north of Daugava) The boundaries occupied by each nation and tribe of the Baits, as indicated in Table 11, are necessarily vague. One reason is that these boundaries shifted during the prehistoric (2000 8.C.—0 A.D.) and early historic (c. 1 A.D.—1200 A.D.) periods of the existence of the Baits.38 The second reason is the similar customs, culture, ornaments, weapons, and tools, and, above all, the common Baltic (or Aistian) language of all these tribes and nations; therefore any determination of their boundaries, in particular in the centuries before Christ, is highly arbitrary. However, the division of the Baits into their major nations was completed in the first two centuries of the Christian era; thus, Ptolemy in the 2nd century A.D. mentions the Galinds (galindoi), Yatvings or Sudavians (soudinoi), and Prussians.6 The Aistians (Aestii) of Tacitus in the Ist century A.D. were probably "the people of the estuaries of the rivers" (Aestyorum gentes) who collected amber; the Kur-Prussian word for amber (glese)7 is mentioned by Tacitus, and their rivers were evidently the Vistula, Pregel, and Nemunas. Archeological evidence indicates8 that by the beginning of the Christian era, the original Bait peasant culture had divided into a western (ancient Prussian) and an The Galinds It appears logical that the name Galindoi is derived from the ancient Baltic word galindis for an inhabitant of the Baltic borderlands (the end or the limit is in Lith. galas and in Latv. gals) ; in modern Lithuanian this word would contract to galinis.4 This is perfectly plausible, since "d" and some other consonants have disappeared in many words after other consonants such as m, n, z, etc.,4 by the end of the 16th century (e.g., veizdi has contracted to veizi in Lith., and the German und has contracted to un in Latv.). Several other names of nations and localities of the Baits show this root gal-; thus the name of Zemgale (Zemigola—Russ.; 6V migallia—Latin chronicles; Žiemgala— Lith.) is probably derived from ziema (winter—Latv.) and gals and signifies "the north end country." Similarly, Latgale (Lietigola—Nestor\'s chronicle; Lethigallia—Henry the Lett\'s chronicle) means "the end country of the Letts." In Baltistan (i.e. "the country of the Baits" in northern Kashmir, between the Indus river and Karakorum), there is a town called Baltite (also called Hunza) ; a mountain pass Dardugali ("end of thunder"—Latv.) over the Dardu mountains (dārdēt—to make a thunderous noise, in Latv.) ; in North Pakistan, Saldu (salds—sweet in Latv.; Saldus, a town in Latvia) is the principal town of the district of Swat (svāts—holy in Latgalian dialect; svēts in Latv.). Many other typically Baltic names can be found in detailed maps of North Pakistan and northern India. Irrespective of the disputable fact of the location of the original Indo-european homeland1 either near the Black Sea or north of India and east of the Caspian Sea, eastern (later Lithuanian and Latvian) branch. The differentiation of the language spoken by the Zemgals and Letgals from that of the Lithuanians10 became well established _only in the 9th to 11th centuries A.D., while the Kurs were historically and linguistically closer to the western branch, the Prussians. it appears quite probable that one branch of the Baits migrated to Baltistan and neighboring regions in the Indian subcontinent, while the others moved gradually from the southeast toward the Baltic Sea. The first region of the prehistoric and early historic homeland of the Baits1 to be settled was that occupied by the Galinds, who were the oldest branch of the Baits, and perhaps originally the largest. Since the 6th century A.D., the Galinds became gradually separated from the other Baits and encircled by a "sea" of Slavs.17 Of all the five principal Baltic nations, the Galinds were the first to be absorbed by the Slavs, since they lived deep in the territory occupied in our times by the Russians. Of all the Baltic nations at the beginning of the Christian era, the Galinds probably occupied the largest territory. The Letts or Letgals (according to Henry the Lett\'s chronicle,9 Lethi gui proprie dicuntur Lethigalli), a tribe of the eastern Baits, broke out of the Slavic encirclement in the region of the Upper Dnieprlo and settled in northern Latvia in the 6th century A.D.; before that, this region north of the Daugava had been settled successively by the Zemgals (who later moved south) and, even earlier by pre-Balts.lo It is reasonable to assume that the Letts constituted only a minor part of the eastern Baits, while the major part became lost by assimilation and warfare during the almost explosive expansion of the Slavs starting with the 6th century A.D. For this reason, the territory of the Baltic nations around 1200 A.D. (Map II) is much smaller than the Baltic lands around 100 A.D. (Map I, see Part I of this paperl). In the 3rd century A.D., the Galinds were a powerful and warlike tribe. Allied with the Goths (a Germanic tribe), they menaced the frontiers of the Roman Empire in Pannonia (Hungary). 6 Regretably, history has not preserved the names of the Galind kings; however, the Roman emperor Volusianus assumed the honorary name Galindicus (as it appears on some Roman coins 6) since he was able to repel the invasion of the Galinds. The friendship of the Galinds and Goths continued for several centuries; the lands of the Galinds belonged (either directly or as allies) to the huge kingdom of the East Goth ruler Hermanarich, who, after many years of happy rule, was defeated around 375 A.D. (at the extreme age of 110 years) by the Huns. This event signified the beginning of the great migration of nations (V olkerwanderung). Subsequently the Goths became trusted allies of the Huns; the name of the most famous king of the Huns, Attila (d. 453), is of Gothic origin (in Gothic ata means father; atila —little father, German Vaterchen). Such a gentle name seems incongruous for the man who personified terror for the civilized lands of Western Europe, since it was said that "even the grass will not grow where a Hun war horse has put its hoof." Some of these causes have been suggested, more or less explicitly, by other authors; nevertheless, it seems useful to discuss the reasons why the Baits, who were much stronger than the Slavs at the beginning of the Christian era, found themselves practically encircled by the Slavs a thousand years later. 1.) The birth rate of the Slavs was higher. This is true even to our days. This fact does not seem to have any physical (biological) but rather a cultural reason. An important Baltic (Latvian or Lithuanian) value was great consideration for the future welfare of the children : normally a Bait did not have a family unless he could support it adequately. The rapid increase of the Slavs is a warning of possible extinction of the Baits. While their lands covered 166,000 sq. miles in the Ist cent., they had shrunk to less than 100,000 sq. miles by 1200 A.D., and to 56,000 sq. miles in our time (and even in this territory, in occupied Latvia and Lithuania, the slavonization continues). While the population of many Slavic and Germanic lands has increased 50 to 100 times in the last 1000 years, the population of the Baits has increased perhaps 3 to 5 times in the same millennium. While the kings of the Baltic people could successfully repel many Germanic and Slavic invasions, while Lithuania probably was the strongest military power in Europe from 1410 to 1430, in our times the dozen divisions which perhaps could have been mustered (in a short period of time for mobilization, in case of a blitzkrieg) in 1040 by Latvia and Lithuania could not have stemmed the Slavic tide for more than a few days. This was the major reason of the capitulation of these states to the Soviet Union. Of course, the question whether religious, moral, and financial scruples should be relaxed in favor of larger families, is beyond the scope of this discussion. It is connected with the question whether it is important for a nation to exist, or whether the comfort of the individual is the higher good. It does not matter really whether we regard the growth of a nation as a Darwinistic competition for survival, the expansion in an ecological niche, similar to the growth of a weed to the fullest extent of all available land, or as the fulfilment of God\'s first command "to grow and multiply and fill the earth." One thing is certain: if this law is not fulfilled, the nation sooner or later will die. The chief factor in the "survival of the fittest" is the birth rate. As Oswald Spengler once remarked in his book Der Untergang dcs Abendlandes, "a man\'s victory is on a battlefield, and a woman\'s victory is in childbed." And it seems that a woman\'s victory is more permanent. Although the the heroic legions of the Grand Duke of Vytautas, defeated all enemies, although his uncle Algirdas threw his spear into the gates of the Kremlin, they did not have enough Lithuanians to settle the huge conquered territories. Turned toward the East, the Grand Dukes forgot the dream of Mindaugas\' youth—to unify the Baits, to liberate the ancient Prussians and Latvians, which then could have been possible. While Lithuanians were crowned kings of the unified country of Poland and Lithuania and achieved victories of a man, the victory of a woman belonged to the Polish and the Russians. Significantly, the very populous nations—Russia, China, India—have been defeated and conquered many time, chiefly because of their lack of organization and education connected with overpopulation. And yet, they emerge again and again, every time more powerful than before, because their very numbers absorb their enemies and conquerors. The Latvian writer Rainis said in his dramatic work Indulis un Ārija: "We are a small nation, but we shall be as great as our will; through centuries will fly the Latvian falcon, through murky nights and swamps of darkness, to reach with the sun the shore of the Amber Sea." These words he ascribed to the ancient Latvian (Curonian) Prince Indulis. Will the Latvian will be great enough to grow? For that, Latvians need not only achievements as individuals, which the Baltic people do not lack, but also the vital force for the growth of our numbers. If not. the Latvians as a nation would share the fate of the Galinds. 2. ) In contrast to most of the nations in the preceding millennium that did not tolerate strangers in their territory, the Galinds were pacific, poorly organized peasant people. The huge plain of today\'s Russia was then sparsely populated; the Galinds did not mind when Slavic settlers came and made themselves at home as initially peaceful neighbors. The populations intermingled freely, since the Galinds had not yet acquired a national "feeling" of unity, of identity to be preserved. The Slavs were not much better organized until the conquest by the Varyagsl in the 9th century A.D.; according to Nestor\'s chronicle, the Slav elders said to the Scandinavians: "Our land is huge and bountiful, but it has no order; come and order and rule over us." At any rate, there was no organized resistance by Russia as a whole. However, while the Slavs were able to absorb the Galinds (mostly peacefully), the Varyag conquerors were not very numerous, and their traces vanished in Russia just like so many other conquerors over the centuries. 3. ) The Galinds had no unified leadership, since a part of them moved east to the Oka, others stayed around Smolensk, still others moved south and to Prussia. Thus, even those who wanted to preserve their nationality and stay together, became dispersed. In contrast, the Letgals, although less numerous, must have had able and energetic leaders to lead them out of the Slavic encirclement in the 6th century. Those Baits who settled near the Baltic Sea had a shorter borderland with the Slavs and thus were able to organize an effective resistance. The Yatvings The Yatvings, a brave people and excellent horsemen, defended their lands with more vigor and unity than the Galinds well into the time of medieval history against superior numbers of Russians, Poles and Germans. They had a national feeling and a readiness to the greatest sacrifices for their liberty; but they lacked great leaders of the scope and vision of the Lithuanian king Mindaugas who might have seen the only way to preserve their nationality as Baits: to join Lithuania and eventually the other Baltic nations in a single state. In other words, while the clannish feeling of tribal loyalty was strongly developed, there was nooverriding dream of unity of all Baits. The Slavs developed earlier the dream of Pan-Slavism as supranational spirit of unity; this was the goal of the Tsars of Muscovy for centuries: to unify and "get back" all Slavic lands, and even to enforce their nationality upon various non-Slavic conqured peoples. Another factor which contributed to the defeat and assimilation of the Yatvings by their enemies, except for those who ultimately joined Lithuania, was their unfortunate geographical position. They had no protection by a seacoast, and they often had to bear the brunt of predatory and concerted17 attacks by the Kiev Russians, Poles, Galicians, and the Teutonic Order. Within two years (see Table IV; a remarkable feat of cooperation defying the slow and difficult communications of those times!) the Teutonic Order conquered the West Yatvings (the Sudavs near Prussia)4 and the Polish Prince of Krakow, Lieszko the Black, destroyed the South Yatvingslo; their eastern lands were left to be ravaged and subjugated by the Russians and Tartars. Polish historians like Halecki16 do not mention this common undertaking of conquest.17 On the other hand, Halecki deplores, and with reason, the partitions of Poland (1772-1795) by Russia, Prussia, and Austria, the three "predatory powers" as "crimes against international morality."l 6 Halecki exposes the brutal attack of Nazi Germany and Soviet Russia upon Poland (1939), and we agree with his views. However, with respect to the Polish military seizure and occupation of Vilnius, the ancient capital of Lithuania (October 9, 1920), two days after the pact of Suwalki where Poland left the city to Lithuania,4 Halecki seems to criticize mildly the military method but thoroughly approves the result.16 An impartial discussion of history should be more objective. Predatory and coercive methods should be condemned whether they are exercised by first-rate powers or by a middle-sized country. However, for the common good of eastern Europe, let us hope that the future relations of Poland and the Baltic states will develop on the basis of international justice and genuine friendship. After 1282, the Yatvings (Sudavians) who had survived and remained free in the borderlands of Lithuania joined the unified state and shared in the development of the Grand Duchy of Lithuania, in the victories over the Teutonic, Slavic, and Tartar aggressors in the 14th and 15th centuries. The restored free republic of Lithuania after World War I did not include the former Yatving lands in its territory, however. A minor part of the former territories of Aukshtaitians (High Lithuanians) around and including Vilnius has been returned by Poland to the Lithuanian S.S.R. after World War 11. The former territories of the ancient Prussians and Yatvings have been partitioned equally between Poland and the Soviet Union. Poland has obtained the southern part of East Prussia and the Sudavian lands north of the Bug and west of Grodno (Gardinas. the capital of Lithuania in the time of its greatest ruler Vytautas, in the early 15th century). Russia has the rest. Although many descendants of Yatvings live in those regions, with the loss of their language and the decline of the state of Greater Lithuania, these people were slavicized and were lost from the Baltic family of nations.
