BOOK REVIEW The French Defense Ministry publishes an academical quarterly called Historique de I\'Armee. In the August 1963 issue, an article appeared written by the noted French military historian and former military attache to Latvia — Colonel Jean Chabanier. In his article, "The Rattle of Kurzeme from October 1944 until May 1945", the author mentions that there are very few outside military historians who know of the battles in Kurzeme, and the fact that Soviet armies captured Kurzeme only after the armistice. In view of the fact that the Soviet forces tried so desperately to overrun Kurzeme and organized numerous offensives to accomplish it, the author assigns importance to these battles as examples of defensive combat, and stresses their value to military theoreticians and historians. Col. Chabanier at first takes a short look at the historical highlights of Kurzeme, noting that Kurzeme was captured by the German Teutonic Knights at the beginning of the 13th century. He mentions the good relations between Ludwig 14th and the Duke of Kurzeme. On October 13th, 1944, the Red Army captured Riga — capital city of Latvia — and soon afterwards the front line stabilized in Kurzeme, from Jūrmalciems at the Baltic Sea on the south, to Plieni at the Baltic Sea on the north, thus the fighting forces being completely isolated from the rest of the Eastern front. At this time there were 23 German divisions, 2 Latvian divisions. 1 Dutch division, 3 armored divisions, and some air support. The Soviet forces on the same sector used 120 divisions. The author indicates that as late as in March 1945. it was possible to evacuate the encircled forces by sea to Germany, which was discussed in high level meetings and strongly encouraged by the military high command. Hitler, however, refused to consider evacuation and the ground was set for one of the fiercest and most heroic battles of World War 11. Col. Chabanier describes in detail each of the 6 Soviet offensives which took place on Oct. 16th and 27th, Dec. 21st, Jan. 24th, the end of Feb., and on March 18th. During these battles, the losses to Soviet forces were 90,000 killed, 300,000 wounded, 4,000 prisoners, 2,651 vehicles, 1,389 heavy weapons, 1,091 artillery pieces, and 722 aircraft. The author especially emphasizes the valor of the Latvian 19th division, which on Dec. 31st, alone, withstood 27 separate major attacks without loosing any ground. He praises the morale and esprit de corps of this division, and states that it was exemplary in view of the fact that the attacks became fiercer and there were no replacements. The defending units surrendered only after the Armistice order on May Bth, 1945. Part of the German forces were evacuated to Germany, the rest were taken prisoners in Kurzeme. Only a handful from the 19th division survived the following years of hunger, disease and forced labor near the Arctic Circle. The lucky ones were released only in 1953. Their last commander, General Hilpert, died in slavery only one year after the surrender. Col. Chabanier points out that the destiny for the members of this Latvian division was much more severe than the German units, and, in conclusion, regrets that these heroic and gallant defenders of Kurzeme had to face this tragic fate. Translated by Ainis Meņģelis Capt. — USAR', 'THE KINGS OF THE BALTIC PEOPLE By N. Bojārs, Ph.D. The German Conquest of Prussia. Livonia, and Estonia While the Yatvings, in the numerous wars of liberation from the Slavs and Tartars, became associated with the Lithuani ans and formed with them a single nation, the fate of the Prussians was to be conquered by the Germans. Since the ancient Prussians and their heroic battles for liberty have been described in numerous chronicles and documents, the knowledge of their early history is more complete than that of the Yatvings and Galinds. In fact, it would be a difficult task to condense to a brief summary a description of the life and customs of the ancient Prussians, their religion and politics, the depressing story of their conquest by the Teutonic Order in the 13th century, their desperate revolts and escape to Lithuania. However, our task is facilitated by a lucky coincidence : in an earlier issue of this magazine several articles deal with questions related to the ancient Prussians, and we do not have to repeat the information here. E. Brastiņš\'9 discusses the Prussian religion and the early attempts to convert them to Christianity; L. Lieven20 describes the history of Prussia in the general framework of the medieval history of East Europe and gives interesting samples of the ancient Prussian language; while H. Biezais2\' reviews the description of the German conquest of Prussia and Livonia originally given in H. Schreiber\'s new book.22 While all these articles appear to be interesting and largely objective, dis- (Continued from Zintis Nr. 11/12) closing a sympathy toward the ancient Prussians and their tragic fate, and while these papers mostly complement and confirm each other, there is a note of discrepancy and perhaps even contradiction concerning the methods of conquest in Prussia, the treatment of natives by the German knights of the Teutonic Order, and the fate of the descendants of the Prussians. Quoting Dr. Schreiber\'s book, Dr. Biezais talks about the "annihilation" of the ancient Prussians; he writes that "after countless destructive battles, the Prussian nation succumbed, but it never accepted the new faith." He further quotes from p. 82 of the book:22 "The few remaining living warriors did not embrace Christianity .. . The men who after this long war had }ret remained alive wandered over to Lithuania .. . And despite this, there are historians who dare to maintain that the Prussians were not annihilated but only Christianized and Germanized." L. Lieven defends precisely such a view; he writes20 that at the end of the Great Uprising in Prussia (1260-1282) "probably one third of the Prussian people had perished or had sought freedom by leaving for Lithuania and thence for Russia, but two thirds of the Prussians remained to form the bulk of the population." In his article, he attempts to refute "that it was the policy of the Teutonic Order ... to exterminate the Prussian people in order to settle an empty land with German peasants."2o While it appears somewhat futile to argue about the number or the percentage of Prussians who remained in their land after the German victory, since no impartial census was then taken, the methods of conquest of the Baltic lands by the Teutonic Order are well known. We do not need the testimony of non-Germanic historians who might be said to be prejudiced against the Germans. It is sufficient to quote German chronicles written by people filled with religous zeal, who on the whole approved the conquest and subjugation of the Baltic nations. Such a person was Henricus de Lettis (either a German or a Germanized Latvian, a hostage grown up and educated in Germany), since he was happy about "the awakening of the idolatrous Livs from their idol-worship and sin through the fire of love."21 22 In what manner was this "fire of love" expressed by the friars of the Sword Bearers and Teutons? Was Christianity preached in the peaceful manner of Christ, or with blood and fire and sword? L. Lieven apparently does not believe that the conquests took place according to the "sword, blood and fire" doctrine, since he attempts to refute this "19th century German nationalist doctrine."20 Let us turn for the answer to this question to Henricus de Lettis. He describes 23 the arrival of Bishop Berthold and his German army in Riga in 1198. The Livonians made a camp beyond the Riga hill. The chronist says further: "Nevertheless, they (the Livs) sent a messenger to the Bishop, asking why he had brought an army against them. The bishop replied that as dogs return to their vomit, so they had returned too often from the faith to paganism. The Livonians then said: \'We shall do away with this reason. If you send back your army, you may return with your people to your bishopric in peace, and you may compel those who have received the faith to keep it. You may lead others to accept it, but with words, not with blows.\' " That was not Berthold\'s way to preach Christianity: he did not accept the peaceful proposal of the Livs,. but elected to wage war and lost his life, since "those who raise the sword will perish by the sword." He was killed in battle by Ymaut on July 24, 1198. Under the name of Imanta this Livonian was popularized as a national hero by Latvian writers in the 19th century. The behavior of the German knights of the Order of Sword Bearers in Estonia is described by Henricus de Lettis24 as follows: "When we arrived there, we spread troops over all roads, villages and districts of the country; we set on fire and destroyed everything; we killed all that was of masculine sex, captured women and children, and took away much cattle and horses .. . and returned to Livonia with great rejoicing ... We divided among u< the whole booty ... and returned joyful] y ... as the victors exult when they divide booty." In this manner, the German militant friars conquered the relatively small territory of the Livs by 1212 and Estonia by 1227. Both Livs (Livonians) and Estonians belong to the Finno-Ugric family of languages. Although geographically they live in the Baltic region, linguistically they do not belong to the Baltic people or to the Indoeuropean race. Later (13th-16th centures) the whole territory of Latvia and Estonia was named Livonia by the German conquerors, since the first German strongholds in the Baltic were built in the land of the Livs, although originally the Livs had only about four castles and had played no important part in the early history of Latvia7 (p. 54). Bishop Christian\'s and Prince Konrad\'s Polish crusaders were the first to attack the Prussians,4 but without much success. Konrad invited the Teutonic Order to Prussia in 1230, hoping to profit in the deal. Thus, a Polish ruler is to blame for creating this menace to the Baltic lands and Poland.25 In a way, though, Konrad\'s design has been realized now: with Prussians destroyed in the 13th century and the Germans exiled in 1945, Poland has ultimately profited, since it has now occupied most of the old Prussian lands. The Teutonic Order started their first full-scale "crusade" in Prussia in 1231. The chronicle of Oliva describes the torture and horrible death of Pipin, a ruler of a Prussian castle who was captured by the Teutons.26 Innumerable times, balked by the valor of the Prussians, the Germans resorted to scorched-earth tactics and imposed impossible taxes upon the survivors. The elders of Varmians and Natangians in Prussia sought some relief from the grain taxes levied by the Teutons, designed to crush the Prussian spirit by starvation:27 "You know that our poor brothers worn out by the many working days on your estates, have no time left to till their own fields, and their fields remain unfilled. Their children are suffering from hunger, and now you want to take their last piece of bread from their mouths." Friar Wolradt "Mirabilis," the voigt of  invited fifty Prussian elders to a feast to hear his answer. The guests were treated in a most hospitable manner. Suddenly, he left his guests, all exits were locked and barred, the building was set on fire, and the elders burned alive.27 This atrocity (and not so much the remote battle of Durbe, as indicated by Lieven20) ignited the Great Prussian Insurrection, in which the Teutonic Order was very nearly expelled from Prussia. The greatest leader of the Prussians in this insurrection was Prince Monte. The German chroniclers never cited a single deed of chivalry of their own knights but extolled the chivalry of Monte and of Zemgallian princes in 1260, and, a century later, the chivalry shown by King Kestutis of Lithuania.4 Other Prussian princes who directed the Great Insurrection were Glappas, Auktums, and Divane. The Prussians defeated the Teutons in many pitched battles and destroyed all their fortresses except Kdnigsberg, Balge, Elbing, and Kulm.4 The siege technique of strongly fortified stone castles had not been sufficiently developed; there was no artillery as yet, and catapults and ballistas were not effective against huge walls; and Mindaugas, the Lithuanian king, hesitated too long, because of his baptism by Germans, and denied his help to his Prussian kinsmen at a crucial time. This omission was later called treason by the revolting princes who killed the king in 1263. Instead, Mindaugas unnecessarily sent an army against Bryansk, deep in Russia. Prince Monte had been kidnapped by the crusaders as a young boy and had been reared by the Teutons in Brandenburg. His Catholic name was Hinrik (Heinrich). An idealist and a true knight, he once offered his life for that of a captured enemy knight whom he had known in Germany. A constant stream of misinformed crusaders arrived in Prussia from Western Europe, and as years went by the Prussian struggle for liberty became exceedingly difficult. Prince Monte was ambushed by Hermann yon Schonenberg, the commandant of Christburg. He had Monte tied to a tree and then pierced his brave heart.4 The Great Insurrection was largely suppressed by 1274, but sporadic battles continued until 1282. In 1281, while storming a German castle in Prussia, King Nameitis of Zemgale was killed; he had previously defeated the Teutons in many battles in Latvia and then had hastend to help his unfortunate kinsmen in the south.2B Prussia was so completely devastated in this desperate struggle for freedom that it took the Germans the next fifty years to rebuild their chain of fortresses and settlements.4 The Teutonic Order, now securely entrenched in its castles in Prussia, continued its policy of extermination in the 14th century, principally against the Samogithians (Zhemaitians) of Lithuania. More than a dozen chronicles written by Germans and enumerated by Jurgēla29 are replete with terrible stories of Teuton atrocities. For example, a Teuton raid surprised a peaceful Samogithian village at the time of a wedding in 1337. The village was burnt, a bloody "wedding dance" was staged, and the people were hunted down and killed by the Teutons, "just like foxes and rabbits are hunted."30 In another raid, "by the Marshal\'s orders, all pagans of both sexes were killed, the old and the young, and the district (of Streva) was laid waste by fire."3l As a German historian scornfully writes,32 "the Order changed in the shortest time to a colony of convicts, where vagabonds and criminals from all Germany took refuge." Numerous Papal warnings did not have any effect on the Teutons.4 6 7 On April 4, 1325 the Archibishop of Riga excommunicated the Teutonic Order. The Teutons did not pay any attention. Even bishops were held imprisoned in the high towers and underground cellars of their castles. The bishop of Oesel was murdered in 1381.35 Bishop Theodore of Samland was sentenced to death by starvation by the Grand Master of the Teutonic Order in 1477.36 When killing someone, the Teutonic knights derided the victim with the words: "Papa te iubavit!—The Pope will help thee !"33 More complete data on the behavior of the Teutons in the Baltic TABLE V. THE KINGS OF THE BALTIC PEOPLE — PART 111 The Division of the Eastern Baits and the Western Baits In the preceding two papers of this series {Zintis, No. 10 and 11/12), the origin of the White race and of its northern branch, the Baits, was discussed. It was shown that in the late Iron Age the Baits were divided into five nations: 1.) Galinds, 2.) Yatvings, 3.) (Old) Prussians, 4.) Lithuanians, and 5.) Latvians, which were each in turn subdivided into several tribes. In particular, the coalescence of the ancient ~ Latvians (i.e., the Kurs or Couronians; the Zemqals or Semigallians; the Letgals or Lettigallians, also called Letts by the chronicles; and the Seles or Selonians, which were actually the southern branch of the Letgals) into a single nation was as yet by no means accomplished. The location of these nations and tribes were shown on Map 111 (around A.D. 1200). The Prussians, Zhemaitians (West Lithuanians), Couronians, and (according to Professor A. Schwabe) Semigallians were largely descendants of the earlier and larger unit: the Western Baits. The Lettigallians (including the Selonians), Aukshtaitians (or Eastern Lithuanians), as well as the Galinds and Yatvings, were predominantly descendants of the Eastern Baits. Of course, since the minor tribe of the West Galinds moved to Masuria and the West Yatvings (Sudavians) migrated to the region between Lithuania and Prussia (see Map 111 in the preceding paper), which probably happened in the early Iron Age, these western branches of the Eastern Baits became largely similar in their culture to the Western Baits. According to Professor F. Balodis, the Semigallians were not entirely Western Baits, but they rather were formed in one or two centuries before and after the birth of Christ by a gradual coalescence of a fraction of Lettigallians and a branch of Couronians. To summarize this discussion, we can say that the division between Eastern and Western Baits is discernable already at the beginning of the early Bronze Age (1600 8.C.) and even before; that these two large groups had formed several distinctive cultures by the beginning of the early Iron Age (800 8.C.) ; that the division of Eastern and Western Baits into (nearly 20) tribes took place (according to Professors J. Endzelīns and J. Puzinas) starting with the Ist century after Christ; and that by 1200 A.D. these tribes were about to coalesce culturally and politically into the five above named Baltic nations. For example, the burial customs, the weapons and the ornaments of the Couronians had begun to differentiate from those of the Old Prussians and to approach those of the Lettigallians in the first few centuries after Christ.38 The Lettigallians already had one major kingdom (of Jersika), and all four tribes of ancient Latvians were close to political unification at the time of the German attack in the 13th century.7 The "Lost Tribes" of the Baits It is said that Israel had its "lost tribes"; what about the Baltic people? were there some ancient tribes which disappeared in the grey mist of oblivion without even leaving their true name? It turns out to be that such is indeed the case, and that archaeologists have unearthed evidence of the existence of such ancient tribes, unknown or at least unrecognized by historians. Most of this evidence is quite recent, and it has been admirably summarized by Professor Marija Gimbutas in her recent book "The Baits".39 Is is by no means vague or inconclusive evidence: numerous and tangible, beautiful artifacts, tools and weapons and jewelry testify about the high cultural accomplishments of these "lost tribes" of the Baits in the Bronze Age. This review, however, is directed more toward the description of the political and military organization of the Baits, of their kings and famous warriors, and therefore no detailed description of the cultural accomplishments of the Baits will be included ; the reader is rather referred to the outstanding books of Latvian38 and Lithuanian39 archaeologists on this subject. It is of some interest for our topic of discussion, however, to summarize the geographical location and the time of existence of these "lost tribes." As indicated before, the prevalent theory is that the White race came from Central Asia (perhaps somewhere in Sinkiang and Turkestan or Kazachstan) and moved westward along the northern shores of the Black Sea, reaching the lands of the Lower Danube not later than 2300-2200 B.C. In their movement, they left in the Ukrainian steppes their peculiar burial mounds or barrows (in Russian, kur gan), which had been noticed by the local inhabitants of our age long ago. However, the scientific exploration and, in particular, the determination of the age (for example, by the content of the radioactive carbon 14 in carboniferous materials, such as bones or coal) of these graves is beginning only now. Enough evidence has been assembled to show that these burial mounds (or most of them) belong to the epoch of the Indoeuropean expansion, and the whole cultural period (ca. 2300-2000) of the White race has been designated as the Kurgan or Pit-Grave culture.39 Around 2000 8.C., the primitive nuclei of various people of the White race (Hittites, Baits, Celts, Achaian or Mycenean Hellenes, perhaps somewhat later Germanic, Protoslavic, and Italic tribes) were formed, when the primitive people (Indoeuropeans) became mixed with the original sparse population of the darker races of the ancient past. Continuing the Kurgan culture, the Proto-Balts (the northern branch of the Indoeuropeans) formed their Corded Pottery or Battle-Axe cultures, so named after the most distinctive archaeological deposits. At the end of the Neolithic Age (2000-1800) and during the Chalcolithic Age (1800-1600 8.C.), the differentiation between the Eastern Baits and their Fatjanovo culture (so named after the discovery of the first known deposit of this culture, the Faijanovo cemetery near Jaroslavl on the Upper Volga) and the Western Baits and their culture of ceramics adorned by impressions of cords (i.e., the Corded culture) became more pronounced, although both had developed from a single source, the Kurgan liv-oeuropeans and their culture. During the early Bronze Age (1600-1250 8.C.), the Baits and their cultures reached the maximum geographical extent. The extreme Eastern Baits, the "Fatjanovians" (we, of course, don\'t know how they called themselves), settled throughout the heartland of the Eurasian land mass, the wide East European plain, from the Lakes of Ladoga and Onega to the region north of the Upper Volga, and southward to the region of Kiev on the Dnieper. The eastern "Fatjanovians" formed their distinctive {Abashevo) culture and penetrated from the basin of the Oka and the Volga along the rivers of Kama and Belaya to the southern part of the Ural mountains39 This easternmost branch of the Baits became particularly rich with bronze artifacts, since the rich ores of copper and tin in the Urals became available, and travelling smiths evidently brought weapons and jewelry of beautiful workmanship throughout the region of the Eastern Baits. Then a disaster struck. The Proto-Scythians formed their Timber-Grave culture and advanced from Asia toward the Urals and the lower Volga. According to later Greek historians (Herodotus), the Scythians spoke a language related to that of the Persians, therefore they must have originated from the Indoiranian branch of the Indoeuropean (White) race. However, according to their culture, customs, and appearance, the Scythians and their ancestors (the Timber-Grave people in the Urals) were Asiatic nomads, excellent horsemen and wild warriors of the character of the (later) Huns, who (both the Scythians and the Huns) acted like "divine punishment" (flagellum Dei) by devastating many cultural settlements in Central Europe and elsewhere. In the so-called "classical" Bronze Age of the Baits (1250-1100 8.C.), while the Western Baits enjoyed a high culture and traded their amber for the bronze weapons and tools of the Central Europeans, the lands of the extreme Eastern Baits, the "Fatjanovians" and their descendants or relatives, the "Abashevians", were occupied by the Proto-Scythians (the Timber-Grave people). In this manner, a large portion of the Eastern Baits became the first "lost tribe", and the extreme eastern frontier of the Baits was established in the region of the Oka, in today\'s Central Russia. It is not known exactly how many of the people of the "lost tribe" were killed in the war with the Proto-Scythians, how many fled or were absorbed. Yet, taking into account the poor organization and communications at that time, it must be assumed that the greater part of the "Fatjanovians" and "Abashevians" failed to escape the onslaught. Soon after that, perhaps in the beginning of the late Bronze Age (1100-800 8.C.), the remaining "Fatjanovians" (north of the Upper Volga and the upper Daugava) and the northern branch of the Western Baits (the Corded culture settlers in parts of North Latvia, throughout Estonia, and in TABLE VI. One of them is Prof. Dr. Arnold Spek-Ige\'s The Ancient Amber Routes and the Discovery of the Eastern Baltic, publ. by Zelta Ābele, Stockholm, Sweden; it deals with the economic and political life of the ancient Baits and with their amber trade with the Roman empire.
