Name: 94_referendum_eu_vote_accession
Representative_Docs: ['CHICAGO LATVIAN NEWSLETTER April 1991 Vol. XI No. 4 "Do you support the democratic and independent statehood of the Republic of Latvia?" That was the question that 1,666,128 residents of Latvia responded to on March 3, when the Republic of Latvia held an advisory vote on independence. The respondents represented 87.57% of all those eligible to participate in the advisory vote. It was open to all people, age 18 or over, who were permanently registered in their internal passports as residents of Latvia. Soviet army personnel, who do not possess such passports while on active duty, were not eligible to vote. The results in Latvia exceeded the most optimistic predictions. Ethnic Latvians represent, at the most, 53% of Latvia\'s population. Latvian government officials had hoped that enough non-Latvians would support independence to achieve a \'yes\' vote between 60%-70%. Support for indpendence exceeded 50% in ever Latvian city and rural district. Even in the heavily Russified city of Daugavpils, where Latvians represent only 12% of the population, the \'yes\' vote was 51.29%. In Riga, where Latvians make up approximately 36% of the population, pro-independence votes reached 60%. In rural regions, were ethnic Latvians predominate, the cumulative vote total was 85% \'yes. YES – 73.68% NO – 24.69% Unexpected support, predictable reactions. Anatolijs Gorbunovs, President of the Supreme Council of the Republic of Latvia said that the vote was a strong demonstration of support for the parliament and its pro-independence program. Jorgen Vinter, chairman of the Danish parliament and an observer of the vote in Latvia said, "I am very happy about the results." Anatolijs Aleksejevs, leader of the antiindependence Interfront group claimed the vote did not reflect public opinion and said that it could only be viewed as a "preliminary phase" to a Soviet referendum. Mikhail Gorbachev said nothing. Last year Gorbachev had insisted on referenda in the Baltic States, claiming that the pro-independence governments were operating without a demonstration of public support. When Lithuania planned its February vote, Gorbachev changed his mind and declared all Baltic votes \'illegal. A public relations poll? Gorbachev did have a point in claiming the Baltic votes (Estonians voted 77% for independence, Lithuanians 90%) had no legal status. In the eyes of the Baltic governments, these votes had nothing whatsoever to do with referenda called for in Soviet secession laws. Since the Baltic States are not a legal part of the Soviet Union, they do not have to follow Soviet procedures to secede. Nor, as occupied countries, do they need referenda to establish their legal right to independence, no more so than should Kuwaitis have held a referendum on whether they wished to remain under Iraqi occupation. The votes were held, largely because many Western governments, including the United States, had encouraged the Baits to hold them. It was felt that a public show of support would strengthen the Baltic cause and take another argument away from Gorbachev. Until now, Gorbachev had claimed that a majority of Baltic residents were against independence. It was on this pretext that he had threatened presidential rule, i.e. to defend the rights of the silent majority. But a majority in the Baltic States has been far from silent, and used the votes to register their views. Independence for ALL the people. In Lithuania, where over 80% of the population is ethnic Lithuanian, a large pro-independence vote was anticipated. In Latvia, no one was really sure what to expect. The results suggest that between 1/3 and 1/2 of all non-Latvians in Latvia are firmly pro-independence. Another sizable segment did not vote, suggesting indifference or neutrality on the issue. What of the 24% who voted against independence? One group that no doubt espoused this view was Latvia\'s large collection of retired Soviet army officers. It is estimated that at least 60,000 Soviet veterans have retired to Latvia, bringing with them another 120,000 family members. Many of them reside in Riga and in nearby Jūrmala, and because they are retired, were elgible to vote. (Nevertheless, 68% of Jurmala\'s voters endorsed independence.) They make up the bulk of the anti-independence Interfront movement. Soviet government bureaucrats and family members, and many factory workers who have emigrated to Latvia only recently, probably made up the remainder of the \'no\' votes. Despite this core of hardline Soviets, the vote tally indicates that there is no broadbased anti-independence sentiment in Latvia, not even among Russians or other non-Latvians. Non-Latvian supporters of independence have either lived there for generations and feel loyal to their adopted homeland, or simply feel they will have a better life in an indpendent Latvia. The non-voters may be waiting to see who wins in the tug-of-war between Riga and Moscow, and probably are willing to back the winner, regardless of who it turns out to be. Get me to the polls on time. According to the Associated Press, one 102-year-old woman voted on Feb. 24, when balloting opened for people who could not make it to the polls on March 3. She was voting early, she said, because she was afraid she might die before Sunday. German parliamentarians saw two women, one 92 and the other 90, walking to the polling stations to vote. Anatolijs Gorbunovs, President of the Latvian parliament, voted at the polling stations located in the the Zubar Candy Factory in Riga. While waiting for him to arrive, election officials passed out sweets to reporters and photographers on the scene. Gorbachev\'s referendum Mikhail Gorbachev\'s March 17 referendum on preserving the Soviet Union brought out the anticipated majority, but barely. The Baltic States, Moldāva, Armenia and Georgia all refused to participate in the Soviet referendum. The Latvian parliament ordered all government agencies and branches to not participate in the organization or implementation of the vote. It stated, however, that it would not prevent Soviet citizens in Latvia from voting in the referendum if they chose to do so. According to one Latvian reporter, those who did participate had ample opportunity to vote as many times as they wished; to test the system, the journalist voted 6 six times. The Soviets reported that 500,000 voted in Latvia, 90% of them supporting preservation of the Union. Since several hundred thousand Soviet miltary are stationed in Latvia, and were elgible to vote in Gorbachev\'s referendum, the vote total sounds plausible. It is not known how many times each soldier voted.', "PUBLIC SUPPORT FOR LATVIA'S ACCESSION TO EU STABLE A public opinion poll carried out in September by the European Integration Bureau, in co-operation with the State Chancellery and ihe market and public opinion survey company Latvijas Fakti, reveals that support for Latvia's accession to the European Union (EU) is stable. if a referendum on Latvia joining the EU had taken place in September 2002,46.2% of Latvia's residents would have voted for EU membership (46.6% in July) and 35.8% against (35.3% in July); 17.9% of residents were undecided (as compared to 18.1% in July). The number of EU supporters has therefore remained stable over the past months. According to the poli, people aged 18-39, ethnic Latvians and people with higher incomes and higher education are generally more positively inclined towards EU membership. Among Latvian citizens, support for accession was at 48.4% in September, the highest result so far in 2002. Among non-Latvians, Latvian citizens were more positive about joining the EU than non-citizens. Residents of the Vidzeme region of Latvia are most strongly in favour of EU accession, while in the Latgale region the percentage of euro-optimists is lower than in other parts of the country.", 'INCREASING SUPPORT FOR LATVIA\'S MEMBERSHIP IN THE EU AND NATO A public opinion poll carried out in July by the European Integration Bureau in co-operation with the State Chancellery and the market and public opinion survey company Latvijas Fakti shows a gradual increase in the number of EU supporters. If a referendum on Latvia\'s joining the European Union had taken place in July 2002, 46.6% of Latvia\'s residents would have voted for accession (45.5% in June) and 35.3% against it (38.5% in June); 18.1% of the residents were undecided (16.0% in June). Thus, the number of EU supporters has increased, while the number of sceptics has gone down; on the other hand, the number of undecided residents has grown. Young people aged 18-39 tend to favour Latvia\'s joining the EU, whereas residents over the age of 64 tend to have a negative view. According to the poll, people with high Income, higher education, citizens of the Republic of Latvia, urban residents and ethnic Latvians are generally more positively Inclined toward the EU. Unlike the public opinion poll results in May and June, the results in July show that mors female respondents (48.1%) support Latvia\'s EU accession than male respondents (44.8%). Euro-optimists are in the majority in the city of Riga and the regions of Zemgale, Latgale and Vidzeme. Only in the region of Kurzeme are there more euro-sceptics than euro-optimists. An opinion poll carried out by Latvijas Fakti in July 2002 found that support for Latvia\'s membership in NATO had risen since the previous poii conducted in March: 66.1% of respondents were in favour or did not oppose Latvia\'s membership in NATO, compared with 63.9% in March (14.8% replied that they did not oppose Latvia\'s membership in NATO, but wished to receive more information on this issue, compared with 16.7% in March). The most frequently cited reason for supporting membership was better security for the country and its people (30.7%, up from 21% in March). The proportion of "undecideds" fell from 11.5% in March to 7.3% in July. Meanwhile, 26.6% of respondents believed that Latvia\'s membership in NATO is not necessary (compared with 24.5% in March). The only sociodemographic groups where more than a third of respondents did not support NATO membership were non-Latvians (43.8%), non-citizens (40%) and residents of the region of Latgale (41.1%).']

Name: 95_production_output_industrial_product
Representative_Docs: ["RIGA, 11 September, BNS – This July saw industrial produce in Latvia amount to 136.9 million lats ($U5219.4 mln), which is 10.6% up year-on-year in comparative prices, reported the statistics office. This included mining production up 18.6%, processing industry up 11.3%, electricity, gas and water supply up 4.9%. The food and drinks sector increased 12.5%, the largest rise being in fish and fish processing – up 52.8%, with drinks production up 21.6%, animal food production up 16%, dairy product production up 7.8%, while meat and meat processing was up 7%. Chemicals, chemical products and chemical fibre production was up 44.2%, agricultural and forestry machinery and other equipment and electric appliances up 38.9%, non-metallic mineral production – ceramic, cement, concrete etc. – up 38.6%, while furniture production has grown 30.5%. Rubber and plastics production gained 21.9%, while pulp, paper and paper goods were up 20.9%. This July saw a fall in production since last July in the textiles sector, down 12.7%, ship and train engine repairs down 9.7%, clothing, furs and dyeing down 9.5%, while other electrical appliance production was also down 3.9%. Within the food and drinks sector, fruit and vegetables fell 8.3%, while grain milling, flour and starch production was down 4.2%. Industrial output in July was down 3.8% compared to June, including mining down by 27.9%, processing down 4.2% and electricity, gas and water supply down 0.9%. LATVIA'S INDUSTRIAL PRODUCTION IN JULY UP 10.6%", 'INDUSTRIAL OUTPUT IN LATVIA GROWS 7% Y-O-Y IN AUGUST RIGA, 10 Oct, BNS – This August the industrial output in Latvia grew 7% year-on-year, reported the Latvian National Statistics Office. In August 2001 industrial output in Latvia reached 145 million lats ($U5233.87 mln) in actual prices. The statistics office said that this August most industries were able to boast growth yearon-year. Production grew by 51.2% in the extracting industry and mining, by 7.4% in the processing industry and by 0.5% in electric power, gas and water supply. In the food industry the production increased by 7.9% this year. Likewise, a 29.8% growth was registered in production of miscellaneous machinery and equipment not falling into any other classification (agriculture and forestry machinery, machine tools, electric tools). Furniture production was up by 29.1% year-on-year, production of non-metal minerals items (ceramics, cement, etc.) grew by 24.4%, chemical industry output rose 23.7%, production of rubber and plastic items jumped 13.1%, output of timber and wooden articles was up by 7.2%, production of pulp and paper increased by 6.1%, etc. At the same time, this August production fell year-on-year in the following industries: textile output was down 7.6%, the publishing, printing and reproduction industry suffered a drop by 4.2% and production of leather items shrank 3%. In August 2001 industrial output in Latvia grew 6% over July. Month-on-month, the production fell by 8.4% in the extracting industry and mining while there was a 6.8% growth in the processing industry and a 1.7% rise in the electric power, gas and water supply sector.', 'LATVIAN INDUSTRIAL OUTPUT UP 10.5% IN APRIL RIGA, 11 June, BNS – This April industrial output in Latvia grew 10.5% year-orvyear in constant prices, the Latvian National Statistics Agency reported. In April 2001 the production increased by 13.5% in the extracting industry and mining, 11.5% in the processing industry and 6.2% in electric power, gas and water supply. This April industrial output in Latvia totalled 136.8 million lats (SUS 213.75 mln) in actual prices. On a year-to-year basis, almost all industries posted a growth in April 2001. Output grew most (by 40.5%) for nonmineral products (ceramics, cement, etc.). Production of paper increased by 27.5%, and production of furniture by 26.8%. The food industry saw a 4.3% growth year-onyear with the greatest increase registered in production of ready-made animal feed (by 43.8%) and fish processing (by 29.4%). At the same time, production fell by 38.8% for leather items and by 9.1% for other means of transport (ships, boats, locomotives, railway carriages and running gear). In the food industry, a 14.7% reduction hit the production of beverages. Month-on-month industrial output in Latvia fell by 12.7% this April, including a 6.4% reduction in the extracting industry, a decrease of 8.2% in the processing industry and a 27.2% decline in the electric power, gas and water supply sector. (SUS 1 = LVL 0.640)']

Name: 96_museum_exhibition_occupation_project
Representative_Docs: ['Record number of visitors in the Museum of the Occupation of Latvia In July and August, the Museum of the Occupation of Latvia was visited by 23,020 people, exceeding the respective attendance in 2003 by 26%. The greatest number of visitors was observed on 14 August, when 634 people came to see the Museum. Most of the visitors, especially in the summer season, are guests from abroad, who want to learn of the recent history of Latvia, to find out what happened behind the "iron curtain", as well as acquire an understanding of the current situation in Latvia. Visit to the Museum of the Occupation is included in the plan of events organised by the State Protocol ? by the middle of September, 40 official delegations to Latvia have visited the museum. Aim of the Museum is to remind of what happened to Latvia and its people in the period of occupation between 1940 and 1991, as well as of the harm done by the invading powers to the land and people of Latvia, to commemorate the victims and survivors of the occupation, as well as the fugitives from the invaded country. The Museum is managed and funded by the Foundation of the Museum of the 50year Occupation of Latvia.', "Minister of Foreign Affairs highly values co-operation with the Museum of Occupation On 31 July, the Minister of Foreign Affairs of Latvia, Indulis Berzins, met Chairman of the Board of the Foundation of the Occupation Museum, Paulis Lazda, Deputy Chairman of the Board Ofars Celiis and Director of the Latvian Institute Ojars Kainins. The Minister expressed appreciation of the contribution of the Museum to a deep and truthful understanding of Latvia's history in the societies in Latvia and abroad. He noted that good cooperation had developed between the Ministry of Foreign Affairs and the Museum of Occupation and pledged to continue these efforts. Issues such as the research into history, dissemination of knowledge on the history of Latvia and the occupation and the related events were discussed during the meeting. The Minister favourably evaluated the plans of the Museum related to new exhibitions that would focus on the Holocaust and the destiny of Russian prisoners of war, and commented favourably on the travelling exhibition Latvia Returns to Europe organised by the Museum. The exhibition has already visited many countries and has been appreciated by audiences there.", 'Exhibition "Changes of occupation regimes in Latvia In 1941" in the Occupation Museum On 2 Juiy 2004, exhibition "Changes of occupation regimes in Latvia in 1941" was opened in the Latvian Occupation Museum. in 17 June 1940, the Army of the Soviet Union occupied the Republic of Latvia. In a year\'s time, the war between the Nazi Germany and the Soviet Union began and Latvia was occupied by German forces. Now, the guests of the Museum are welcome to see and experience memories of eyewitnesses, documents and Items that tell of events in the time of change of the two totalitarian occupation regimes in Latvia. The exhibition was prepared by two historians of the Occupation Museum, Ojars Stepens and Kārlis Briedis. The exhibition is open till October 2004.']

Name: 97_coin_currency_bank_lat
Representative_Docs: ["New coin marks Latvia's 90th anniversary Andris Straumanis – October 09, 2008 A new commemorative coin featuring a stylized drawing of two children holding a national flag has been released by the Bank of Latvia to mark the 90th anniversary of the country's declaration of independence. The 1-lat silver proof coin on its front shows Latvia's first coat of arms, which was designed by Burkards Dzenis (1879-1966), the bank announced in an Oct. 9 press release. On the coin, the red-white-red Latvian flag is presented in color. The coin's reverse has a drawing of a boy and girl—symbolizing the country's future—holding a Latvian flag, which also is in color. The coin was designed by Aigars Bikše. The coin, which is legal tender but will appeal most likely to collectors, is minted by the Finnish company Rahapaja Oy. Circulation will not exceed 5,000. Cost of the coin if purchased at the Bank of Latvia is LVL 22.50. It should also be available in souvenir and jewelry shops in Latvia.", "BANK OF LATVIA RELEASES NEW ONE-LAT COIN FEATURING ANT To mark the tenth anniversary since the restoration of Latvia's national currency, the lat, the Bank of Latvia has released a new one-lat coin featuring an ant on the reverse side. This means that there will now be three one-lat coins in circulation, the others depicting a salmon and a stork respectively. As with the stork 1 -lat piece, this coin is a single-issue special release. Overall 250,000 such one-lat coins have been struck. Since ancient times, Latvians have seen ants as a symbol of diligence and industri ousness. Some 6 000 species of ants have been found on the planet, and Latvia is home to 42 of them. The redwood ant (Formica rufa) is the most common ant in Latvia. This is the ant featured on the new coin.", 'Latvian coin featuring beer mug \'amounts to hidden alcohol advertising\' By Andrew Osborn, – 22 Jun 2011 A new Latvian coin featuring a beer mug on one side has sparked a spat between the country\'s health ministry and central bank amid accusations it amounts to hidden alcohol advertising. One million coins with a frothy stein of beer on one side have been produced The silver coloured one Lat coin (which is worth about £1.30) was released earlier this month to mark the Baltic country\'s midsummer solstice festival where beer drinking looms large. But the former Soviet republic\'s health ministry took exception to the new coin, writing an angry letter to the Bank of Latvia alleging that releasing one million coins with a frothy stein of beer on one side amounted to illegal alcohol advertising. "The coin contradicts our efforts to cut down on the consumption of alcohol in society," the health ministry complained. It seems that the Bank of Latvia\'s press release accompanying the issue of the new coin caught the health ministry\'s eye too. It waxed lyrical about beer\'s positive qualities, noting that it was a well known cure against insomnia and had been used in the past for semi-medical purposes. "After water and tea, beer is the third most popular beverage in the world," the Bank of Latvia enthused. "The builders of the Egyptian pyramids were given three vessels of beer a day." A spokesman for the bank dismissed the health ministry\'s criticism as "not serious" however. "We might as well ban national traditions and the midsummer solstice itself (according to their logic)," he said. Strongly denying that it amounted to hidden alcohol advertising, he said the health ministry\'s criticism had only increased interest in the new coin and that Latvians were queuing up outside the bank\'s branches to get hold of one. In the throes of an anti-alcohol campaign, the health ministry also wrote to a school which tested final year pupils on the history of Scotch whisky as part of an oral exam this year, complaining that the topic was "unacceptable."']

Name: 99_article_s_magazine_cemetery
Representative_Docs: ['To the Editor LIFE Magazine Time-Life Building • , Rockefeller Center New York, New York 10020-1393 I am aghast at the article "Soon They Will Come For Us" by Edward Barnes in the December, 1992, issue of LIFE magazine, accusing the Latvian people of everything inhuman and uncivilized . I am equally troubled by the editorial board that this seven page article appeared in the December issue when this month "men of good will" will seek peace with each other and not inflame the passions of their forefathers, be they Latvian or Jew. The Republic of Latvia is barely a year old since it has regained independence after 50 years of Nazi and Soviet occupation; yet the author, in an inflammatory manner, condemns the entire Latvian nation for the complicity of the few who coopted with the Nazi and communist governments. No Latvian was charged at the Nurenberg war crimes trial, and no international tribunal has yet convened to judge the Soviet crimes against Latvians or Jews. To reply to every paragraph would entail restating historical facts that would only underscore the author\'s lack of knowledge of who the victims were during this brutal occupation period. The world has acknowledged the illegality of the Molotov-Ribbentrop Pact, yet the Soviets, now the Russians, find it very difficult to relinquish their once privileged position as occupiers. Unfortunately, the world press continues to convey the Russian allegations of human rights violations in Latvia, ignoring the findings of the U.N. commiseicn that such charges are unfounded. In search of ân answer to this perplexing question of how irresponsible, reporting finds its way to the pages of respectable publications, some insight can be gained from a number of prominent editors of the U.S. media. As a member of the Consular Press Organization in Los Angeles, I note that we frequently query the principals of leading publications on the subject of biased reporting. Not so surprising is the consensus regarding news stories from eastern Europe. Unquestionably all publishers economize to compete. Not all publishers have the resources to staff full time journalists around the world. People sell stories and journalists buy stories; people pay to have their story told, especially to large readership audiences. The disinformation or propaganda machine in Moscow has not stopped. Any stringer arriving in Moscow is not only assisted but facilitated to cover issues that are of interest to the government. Journalists who do not speak the local language, or aspiring freelancers, have copy material and photographs supplied to them. Less critical journalists even write human interest stories without leaving the comforts of their hotel rooms. Staged photographs are not different from quotes out of context. Journalists can get paid twice, by the sponsor and by those who sell the printed medium. The media resources will continue to play a crucial role in world politics through the molding of world public opinion. The present reality of one military super-power has not diminished the interdependency on the economic scene in the maneuvering for economic advantage. If U.S. aid to Russia is being hampered by the legal preconditions of removing Russian occupational troops from the Baltics, then the forces in play work hard to build a false case against a sovereign nation, to which the aforementioned legal criteria does not apply. Whether the above mechanisms have impacted this feature article will remain an open question, but for the more serious readers of Latvian-Jewish relations I recommend journalist-writer Frank Efrayim Gordon and his recent publication, "Latvians and Jews between Russia and Germany" in which the author follows the association of Latvians and Jews dating back to the 1500\'s when Jews started to settle in the present territory of Latvia. Through his research and analysis, Mr. Gordon concludes that, "First, Latvia never was an xanti-Jewish\' state. Second, I myself, being an Israeli, an ethnic Jew, and a Social Democrat, openly declare that I am anti-communist. It is not a shame. The paths of Latvians and Jews have often crossed, but only recently did the good relationship between both peoples deteriorate. In the dramatic year 1940, tragic misunderstandings occurred, whose consequences are still felt today. These consequences are deepest not in Russian-occupied Latvia or in the Jewish state of Israel, but in the Latvian diaspora, which in part coincides with centers of the Jewish diaspora." I believe the editors of LIFE magazine do owe a humble apology to the Latvian people because the article did evade the scrutiny of responsible journalism. i Sincerely, A December 4, 19 9 2 Aivars Jerumanis Former/Consul of Latvia', 'Britu laikraksta The Times š.g. 23. jūnija numurā parādījās korespondentu Deivida Čārtera (Dāvid Charter) un Rodžera Bojesa (Roger Boyes) raksti, kuros sniegta melīga informācija par Latviju. Publicējam pazīstamā sabiedriskā darbinieka Andreja Ozoliņa atbildes raksta oriģinālu un komentāru. The Fditor, The Times 1 Pennington Street London E9B ITA Sir, Re.: The Times article Toties attacked over links to party that honours Wajfen SS by Dāvid Charter (Tuesdav, June 23) and Nazi lovers? Not us, we just helped to fight Russians, say Tortes\' new friends by Roger Boves. Both artides carry a picture of the Latvian flag to vvhich Russian propagandists nāve added Hitlers photograph and the SS svmbols. In Dāvid Charter\'s article, the comments about I.atvia read as if they have come from the Russian Foreign Ministry disinformation department. Roger Boves m his article, has made an attempt to get the facts right but he struggles to understand the information he gained by travelling to Latvia. Boyes incorporates information from Efraim Zurof, vvithout knovving that ZurorTs comments about Latvia and Latvians neēd checking rrom other sources. I can provide evidence of an article m a British nevvspaper m vvhich a Latvian, Jānis Škujevics (dcc.) was slandered asa Nazi. Later, vvhen ZurofT was questioned by a BBC Local Radio Hereford/Worcester correspondent, he vvithdrevv the allegation printed. ZurofT states that veterāns march rrom the Freedom Monument to theCathedral In fact, after the comAndrejs Ozoliņš atspēko melus par Latviju memoration service, the people vvalk from the Cathedral to the Monument The vvord march suggests a closed formation, vvalking m step. Obviouslv, ZurofT has never been present, as the people vvalk vvithout any kind of organisation. ZurofT also says: "In the past they have vvom their old uniforms" In 1999 I met Nikolajs Romanovskis (vvho died some years ago), initiator of the commemorative service. Romanovskis assured mc that at no time had there been any SS military uniforms or insignia. Nevvspapers have printed false allegations about uniforms being vvorn. At least one nevvspaper has even vvritten that black SS uniforms had been vvorn by the \'marchers! The vvriter obviously did not knovv about the three types of SS formations. Black uniforms had been vvorn by Hitler bodv guard units. Members of the VVafren SS had to be Germans vvhose famify tree shovved that they had been Germans for several generations. Non Germans could not serve m the VvafTen SS. They vvere under SS control (an der SS). ZurofT, m his statement about the Latvian state prosecutors, says that, so far, they are failing to investigate the 13 suspects vvhose names he had submitted. He fails to mention that Latvia vvas Soviet occupied until 1991. KGB records vvere moved from Latvia to Moscovv, before the restoration of independence, vvhence, the information he vvants could be m Moscovv. t reocaipāfioh bf lAitvia, 174/4/4 d, mal es rrom lo to 65 vvere imprisoned m so called \'filtration camps! Those vvho had served m any German occupation administration service vvere sent to Siberia. Those vvho had served m any German controlled military unit, regardless of vvhether they were mobilised or not by the Germans, vvere punished severelv, receiving sentences up to 25 years m Siberia. Thousands did not survive the harsh conditions. Knovving about the Soviet treatment of legionnaires, I find it difficult to believe that a Latvian, former legion ofßcer, kept his uniform mothballed during all the years of the Soviet occupation. I should be very interested to knovv hovv Boyes found this man. In his last paragraph Boyes betravs his lack of knovvledge about the Russians vvho settled m Latvia as part of Moscovvs Russification programmē. He should, also, note that during the first Soviet occupation there vvas a population loss of more than 35,000 people, killed or deported. Mass deportations took place on June 14m , 1941 vvhen more than 15,000 vvere deported to Siberia m cattle trucks. Even larger deportations tcx>k place on March 25m, 1949, vvhen some 43,000 vvere deported to the Soviet Far East. Arrests and \'disappearances conh\'riued ūnnTtfie lāte 1980ties.-lf 1s verv llkely that Mr Tbrnels farnilv had suffered under Russian rule. VVhence the chill m his voice. I sincerely hope that the many comments I have made vvill be noted, but m addition I vvish to mention that The Times authors, and anyone else vvriting about Latvia, vvould benefit by reading thebook THE CASE FOR LATVIA Disinformation Campaigns against a Small Nation vvritten by the Finnish journalist and author Jukka Rislakki. vvhich I am sending separaterv. Yours sincerely, Andrejs V. Ozoliņš Redakcija lūdza Andreja Ozoliņa komentāru par to, kāpēc tapusi šī vēstule. A. O. – Ja es izlasu vai dzirdu kaut ko, kas nomelno Latviju un latviešus, man nav miera, pirms neesmu kaut ko darījis, lai atspēkotu melus un nepareizības. Ļaunie raksti parasti nākuši no Maskavā bāzētiem korespondentiem vai krievu dezinformācijas/ melu avotiem par Latviju un latviešiem. Šādā katēgorijā liekami The Times korrespondenta D. Čārtera 23. jūnija raksta informācijas avoti. Raksts saistās ar Britu konservātīvo Eiropas Parlamenta grupējuma maiņu, kurā būs arī TBI LNNK, kas tiek raksturota kā nacistu atbalstītāja. 27. jūnija raksta autors R. Bojess ir bijis Latvijā un mēģina labot 23. iūniia raksta nepareizības, bet ar visu jauniegūto infbVrnaciju īsti netiek galā. Vēstulē izsaku savu attieksmi pret Bojesa teikto, ka viņš ticies ar kādu leģiona virsnieku, kas glabājis SS uniformu visu okupācijas laiku, un vēlos zināt, kā Bojess saticis šo cilvēku. Pieminu pēckara filtrācijas nometnes, bargos sodus leģionāriem un deportācijas. Bojess atsaucas uz Efraima Zurofa teikto, kur nepareizības sākas ar aplamo gājiena virzienu, runa ir par maršēšanu un uniformu lietošanu. Pirms desmit gadiem kāda Toronto avīze pat rakstīja, ka leģionāri valkājuši melnas (Hitlera miesassargu) uniformas. Paskaidroju, ka nevācieši pat nevarēja būt Waffen SS, bet visi nevācieši bija anderSS. To, ka ir atsaukts Zurofa apmelojums par Jāni Skujevicu, zinu tāpēc ka man bija intervija ar BBC Hereford&Worcester radio korespondentu tajā pašā reizē, kad Zurofam. Panācu apmelojuma atspēko šanas raksta ievietošanu avīzē (Western Daily News). Avīzes redaktors tālruņa sarunā atjēdzās, ka viņam piespēlēts melu raksts, jo pateicu, ka The Jewish Chronide bija ievietojis līdzīgu melu rakstu par Paulu Reinhardu. Tajā bija rakstīts, ka vācu okupācijas laikā Reinhards deportējis 100 000 žīdu jauniešu spaidu darbos uz Vāciju. Prasīju redaktoram, kādam žīdu skaitam Latvijā vajadzēja būt, lai starp tiem būtu tāds skaits jauniešu. Atbilde – vismaz pusmiljonam. Paskaidroju, ka Latvijā pirms Otrā pasaules kara visu žīdu kopskaits nepārsniedza minēto skaitu un Renhards neko tādu nevarēja izdarīt, jo visa vara bija vāciešu rokās. Šā gada jūnijā aizrakstīju vēl divas vēstules un aizsūtīju Jukas Rislakki grāmatas. • Tautieši Lielbritānijā bija satraukti pdtBBC TV2 3. jūnija raidījuma vadītāja Endru Neila (Andrew Neil) teikto, ka LNNK ir nacistiski noskaņota partija. Aizrakstīju vēstuli ari viņam. Pašā jūnija sākumā rakstīju Amnesty International par viņu ļoti tendenciozo ziņojumu par Latvija', 'Britu laikraksta The Times š.g. 23. jūnija numurā parādījās korespondentu Deivida Cārtera (David Charter) un Rodžera Bojesa (Roger Boyes) raksti, kufos stiiegta melīga informācija par Latviju. Publicējam pazīstamā sabiedriskā darbinieka Andreja Ozoliņa atbildes raksta oriģinālu un komentāru. The Editor, The Times 1 Pennington Street London E9B ITA Sir, Re.: The Times article Tories attacked over links to party thai honours Waffen SS by David Charter (Tuesday, June 23) and Nazi lovers? Not us, we just helped to fight Russians, say Tories\' new friends by Roger Boyes. Both articles carry a picture of the Latvian flag to which Russian propagandists nave added Hitlers photograph and the SS symbols. In David Charters article, the comments about Latvia read as if they have come from the Russian Foreign Ministry disinformation department. Roger Boyes in his article, has made an attempt to get the tacts right but he struggles to understand the information he gained by travelling to Latvia. Boyes incorporates information from Efraim Zurof, without knowing that ZurofTs comments about Latvia and Latvians need checking from other sources. I can provide evidence of an article in a British newspaper in which a Latvian, lanis Skujevics (dec.) was slandered as a Nazi. Later, when Zuroff was questioned by a BBC Local Radio Hereford/Worcester correspondent, he withdrew the allegation printed. Zuroff states that veterans march from the Freedom Monument to the CathedraL In fact after the comAndrejs Ozoliņš atspēko melus par Latviju memoration service, the people walk from the Cathedral to the Monument The word march suggests a closed formation, walking in step. Obviously, Zuroff has never been present as the people walk without any kind of organisation. Zuroff also says: "In the past they have worn their old uniforms" In 1999 I met Nikolajs Romanovskis (who died some years ago), initiator of the commemorative service. Romanovskis assured me that at no time had there been any SS military uniforms or insignia. Newspapers have printed false allegations about uniforms being worn. At least one newspaper has even written that black SS uniforms had been worn by the marchers! The writer obviously did not know about the three types of SS formations. Black uniforms had been worn by Hitler body guard units. Members of the Waffen SS had to be Germans whose family tree showed that they had been Germans for several generations. Non Germans could not serve in the Waffen SS. They were under SS control (an der SS). Zuroff, in his statement about the Latvian state prosecutors, says that, so far, they are failing to investigate the 13 suspects whose names he had submitted He fails to mention that Latvia was Soviet occupied until 1991. KGB records were moved from Latvia to Moscow, before the restoration of independence, whence, the information he wants could be in Moscow. After the Soviet reoccupation of Latvia, 1944/45, males from 16 to 65 were imprisoned in so called filtration camps! Those who had served in any German occupation administration service were sent to Siberia. Those who had served in any German controlled military unit regardless of whether they were mobilised or not by the Germans, were punished severely, receiving sentences up to 25 years in Siberia Thousands did not survive the harsh conditions. Knowing about the Soviet treatment of legionnaires, I find it difficult to believe that a Latvian, former legion officer, kept his uniform mothballed during all the years of the Soviet occupation. I should be very interested to know how Boyes found this man. In his last paragraph Boyes betrays his lack of knowledge about the Russians who settled in Latvia as part of Moscow\'s Russification programme. He should, also, note that during the first Soviet occupation there was a population loss of more than 35,000 people, killed or deported. Mass deportations took place on June 14m , 1941 when more than 15,000 were deported to Siberia in cattle trucks. Even larger deportations took place on March 25th, 1949, when 50me43,000 were deported to the Soviet Far East. Arrests and \'disappearances\' continued until the late 1980 ties. It is very likely that Mr Tomels family had suffered under Russian rule. Whence the chill in his voice. I sincerely hope that the many comments I have made will be noted, but in addition I wish to mention that The Times authors, and anyone else writing about Latvia, would benefit by reading the bxx)k THE CASE FOR LATVIA Disinformation Campaigns against a Small Nation written by the Finnish journalist and author Jukka Rislakki. which I am sending separately. Name: 101_satire_curtain_iron_replied
Representative_Docs: ['Iron Curtain Satire EXPERTS IN NUMBERS Jokes satirising the muchvaunted Soviet "aid" to Hungary have recently become popular there. One of them is quoted below: "A master in an elementary school asked his pupils, \'Which of you can introduce politics into an arithmetical problem?\' "One of the children got to his feet and said, \'Two and two are four, teacher.\' " \'Of course that\'s right, but there are no politics in it,\' replied the teacher. "Two and two are six," volunteered another. " \'That is not right and, besides; there are no politics in it.\' said the teacher. "Finally little Maurice raised his hand. "•Well,\' said the teacher, \'let\'s see what Maurice has to say.\' " Two and two make three, and  four*TM* " \'Very good, Maurice,\' the teacher said." ONLY JOKING After his execution, Rudolph Slansky was greeted at the gates of Heaven and taken on a tour by Saint Peter. Slansky found the quiet, pious life there unbearable and asked to be transferred elsewhere. Saint Peter then led him to the gates of Hell and turned him over to Lucifer. Slansky was 1 taken to a noisy bar where there was dancing, singing, and much revelry. \'This is for me!" declared Slansky, but Lucifer\'s reply was to lead him into the kitchen where he was dropped into a kettle of boiling oil. Slansky protested and demanded to be returned to the bar. "Brother," replied Lucifer, "that was just propaganda . . .! copied from your own system." \' DRILLING COFFEE SPOONS Drilling holes in coffee spoons is th<J latest step taken to halt the petty thefts which are on the rise as consumer goods become increasingly scarce in Czechoslovakia. Thefts of spoons in the cafeteria of the Main (formerly Wilson) Railway Station in Prague, reached such proportions that the management was forced to devalue its coffee spoons by drilling one-inch diameter holes in them. The spoons can be used only to stir, not to taste, the coffee. At the famous Karlovy Vary spa, now a workers\' resort, soup spoons were similarly treated. REFRIGERATOR CARS The Prague newspaper Prace, January 20, published an article under the headline "Refrigerator on Wheels, With Every Comfort." Describing the hardships of travelling over long distances in unheated railway cars, the paper declared: "These luxurious new coaches now in use on the main inland lines have only one shortcoming: they* are heated by electricity. As there is not yet one single electrified railway line in Czachoslovakia and the coaches have no steam heating, the travellers arrive at their destination frozen stiff, cursing the railways and the designers of the new coaches, wishing them nothing worse than a trip around the world—on the northern route —in this beautiful icebox on wheels." 1 HIGHER LITERACY 1,075,000 copies of the works o: Stalin have been published in Bui jaria, according to official statistic; quoted in the Sofia newspapei Scmedelsko Zname, December 20 Hi is means one copy for every sixth Bulgarian, including babies !literates, and old people.', 'HUMOUR HAS GONE UNDERGROUND Things are pretty grim these I days for humorists behind the Iron Curtain. The Soviet government newspaper, Izvestia, complains that clowns, particularly funny clowns, have almost vanished from Russian circuses. i "Things are going badly for clowning," says the August 14 issue of Izvestia, "not only because there are few good clowns, but because the Soviet Circus Administration rarely produces good jokes for the circus artists." In Hungary a playwright has written the Budapest newspaper, Szabad Nep: "Muct has been said about the lack of humor on our stage. Since 1945 there have been no true comedies. All this is the more to be regretted as our public is simply thirsting for laughter. As it is, th epeople stay away from our plays in droves. "We have theatre conferences at which is laid down a strict formula for making people laugh. We are told to make jokes about capitalism, bourgeoism, absenteeism, Trotskyism and other political subjects. But the public refuses to be told what it shall take for humor." Unde rthe communist formula playwrights and comedians may not make jokes about the regime, its leaders, or its laws. Nor may they be funny about political commissars, secret police, fiveyear plans, political meetings, factory managers, or unrest among the people. Russia\'s great humorist, Mikhail Zoshchenko, found it impossible to write unde rthese rules. Soviet shortcomings had been the subject of his gay and gentle satire. His books outsold all other writers hut Gorki and TVmyan Bedny. He made jokes about ignorance, cruelty, green, lack of culture, the housing shortage, povety, the gap between the high sounding pretensions of propaganda and the painful Soviet reality. He knew the weakness in high places and low, the tragedy of human condition that makes men laugh when they might otherwise cry. In 1946 Moscow put an end to Zoshchenko\'s career—his writings were termed subversive. Bim and Bom, famous Russian clowns, also made the mistake of failing to follow the communist By Paul L. Ford laugh formula. Instead they made the people laugh too heartily at themselves, their troubles and at their rulers. They have been barred from public appearances. Humor has gone underground in the Soviet orbit. The communists would do well to listen to what the people themselves are saying. The people make their own jokes in private. The following story, for example, is being quietly circulated in Russia: Swedish custom officers were examining the luggage of a Russian traveller: First they found a bottle of medicine. "That\'s just a remedy against sea sickness," explained the Russian. Then they discovered a box of white pills. "That\'s for air sickness," he said. Finally they pulled out an enormous picture of Malenkov. \'That\'s a sure cure for home sickness," said the Russian. Then there is the story about the businessman who went to the Leipzig People\'s Bank in East Germany with 1,000 marks to open a new bank account. But first he asked the manager about the bank\'s solvency. "Well," the manager said, "our institution is backed by the government." This did not seem to satisfy the businessman. "Just between you and me," he said, "It might happen that the government ..." The manager interrupted him. \'Now listen." he said; "surely that would be worth a measly thousand marks to you . . . !" Iron Curtain Satiro A Budapest factory guard repeatedly challanged a worker leaving the plant with a wheelbarrow of sawdust. Each time he found nothing but sawdust. Finally the guard exclaimed: "Just between us, it is known that everyone steals — the engineers, the accountants, the director. I myself steal. You want me to believe you are only carrying sawdust?" "Between us," explained the worker, "it is not the sawdust! I\'m stealing the wheelbarrow!"', 'Iron curtain satire Some time ago, the story goes, a footloose young Greek set out on a hunting trip, tie lost his way one foggy night, and when the fog cleared he discovered he had blundered across the frontier and was In Bulgaria. Seeing an opportunity to learn Bulgarian he signed up in the Communist regime\'s army. After several bewildering months he was iriven leave to visit the capital, Sofia. Thinking this an excellent chance to practise his newly acquired tongue, he attempted to en-I gagp several civilians in conversa/ tion. But the Bulgarians could not understand him, they simply stared. No more could he understand tliem—for the language he had been taught was Russian. PERMANENT headachf: A drunk in a Prague streetcar jostled a Communist, who flashed his Party badge. "Watch what you\'re doing!" stormed the Communist. "A line thing it is to get drunk during the Five Year Plan! Look at me; I\'m never drunk!" The dsmk smiled good-naturedly, touched the Party badge, and whispered, "But I can sleep it off!" FINE POINT The following anecdote is reported circulating in Central Europe: "In a small border town, an American ran into a friend, an old Czechoslovak tailor he had known during the war. " \'You are still alive\' exclaimed the surprised American. \'How do you manage to survive?\' " \'Very simple,\' replied the old man. \'When the Americans came, I told them it was wonderful that they were here. When the Russians came, I also told them how glad I was that they had come.\' " \'In other words,\' said the American, \'you make no distinction between us and the Communists?\' . ."The old tailor smiled and said, 1 \'Oh, yes, there is a difference. You ■ see, I would never tell the Communists that 1 said the same thing • to them as I said to the AmeriI A Ql ESTION OF IDENTIFICATION Two strangers were admiring a handsome new car on a street in Prague. One said to the other, , "Isn\'t that beautiful? Another superb achievement of the unexcelled Soviet industry!" The other man stared at him and said, "But that\'s an American car. Don\'t you know one when you see one?" The other replied, "Certainly I do. But I don\'t know you!" RUNAWAY REDS The poor quality of consumer goods is an everyday target of criticism in today\'s Hungary. A I recent escapee gives the follow-\' ing example of the situation: "At Budapest swimming pools \' it frequently happent that when . people emerge from the water their skin is streaked with color \', running down from their bathing suits. This is particularly the case with bright red suits. Outraged ! customers protest to the State stores, but the fact is that onceused bathing suits cannot be returned. The sales people are able to offer only one piece of advice: don\'t go into the water." TOTAL BREAKDOWN In a letter to the Lithuanian Communist daily TIESA (Vilnius) November 27, a would-be student of motor cycling described his ex| periences in a State training school: "It was April, tļMR, when the Council of the Sports Association of Vilnius announced tha a training class for motorcyclists was being organised. "Thirty boys appeared for the classes on May <i. Comrade I\'elec* kis was the instructor. The studies went very well. We finished the course in theory in two months. Onl- the practice remained to be completed. But . . . practice was the main stumbling block, because the organizers of the school had neglected to provide even one motorcycle to use for practice. We gathered at the stadium many times, each time only to hear Comrade IVleckis s;i>, \'Sorry, there is ! still no motorcycle. We will have to "\'■ home and reconvene in two days . . .\' "Much precious time was wasted until finally the motorcycle was received. We paid !.") rubles apiece to the Auto Ins|>ection for examination and started to practice. But i disappointment came very soon. The motorcycle was so old that it fell to pieces after two lessons. "However, Comrade Peleckls found a way out of this trouble. He collected 23 rubles from each student for repair of the motorcycle, and has not been seen by us since that day ..." DEAD LANGUAGES According to a Munich correspondent the last private language school in Budapest, the Beritz School of Languages, was forced to close on October 1(5. Continued ■Mtests on the part .of the French "fjmbassy, which had unofficially taken the school under its wing, l proved useless this time although the Embassy\'s intervention had saved the school on previous occasions. LATE COMER Workers in the Budapest electric light bulb plant were astoņi- i shed one morning recently to see I the name of President-elect Eisenhower on the list posted of employ- i ees who have arrived late to work. ] Fifteen minutes after the name i was noticed, members o" the AVII (Security Police) arrived to inyes- a titrate, but according to a refugee j report, were unable to determine ļ who was responsible.']

Name: 102_research_historian_commission_holocaust
Representative_Docs: ['Latvian President stresses need for EU to cooperate with Russia on archival research On 18 December, Latvian President Vaira Vike-Freiberga congratulated the Commission of Historians on the publication of Volume 18 of their compiled research papers, saying that the Commission ot Historians should be pleased at their achievements over the past eight years. Urging co-operation among the most diverse levels, not only of politicians, but researchers and teachers, the President said she was pleased that not only researchers from Latvia were working in the Commission of Historians, but also researchers from other countries, as, in her opinion, in conducting of research in Latvia it is important to take into account the results of research undertaken in other countries. ļn her address, the President also indicated that it would be beneficial if the EU in its discussions with Russia included the issue of revealing the material in its archives that was taken out of the occupied countries, including Latvia, as these countries regained their independence, so as to make these unique materials accessible to researchers. The Commission of Historians has now completed and published Volume 18 of its research. The Volume, entitled The Holocaust in Latvia, compiles the latest research findings on this topic. This is now the fourth Volume dedicated to the mass annihilation of the Jewish people in Latvia during Wold War 11.', 'PRESIDENT OF LATVIA OPENS INTERNATIONAL CONFERENCE "LATVIA UNDER THE OCCUPATION OF NAZI GERMANY, 1941-1945" On 12 June 2003, a two-day-long international academic conference on "Latvia under the Occupation of Nazi Germany, 1941 -1945\' opened in the Great Hail of the University of Latvia with an address by President of Latvia Vaira Vike-Freiberga. The first day of the conference featured three plenary sessions on a variety of themes, including research on the Holocaust in Latvia; sources and historiography of Nazi Germany\'s policy of occupation in Latvia; and issues and objectives of research into the Nazi occupation policy in Latvia. At this session, research papers were presented by guests from the United States, Russia, Finland, Ukraine, Germany and Sweden. The theme of the plenary on 13 June was "Latvia during the Second World Wan Collaboration, Resistance, Public Opinion and External Factors". The papers presented at the conference are scheduled to form part of the next volume of research to be published by the Historians Commission. The organisers of the conference were the Historians Commission of Latvia, the Latvian History Institute and the Faculty of History and Philosophy of the University of Latvia.', "LATVIA'S HISTORY COMMISSION SUMS UP ACTIVITIES IN 2000 On 4 December, Latvia's History Commission evaluated the results of the research work carried out in 2000. Four task forces of historians were working on 26 different history themes. The first research group, comprising Latvian State Archives' specialists, is investigating crimes against humanity committed in the territory of Latvia in 1940-1941. The task force worked on preparation of the collection of documents The Deported, which would contain the most important documents – pieces of evidence of the deportations of the Latvian population carried out by the Soviet regime in 1941. In addition four research projects on the repressions against Latvian diplomats, ethnic minorities of Latvia, army officers, and civilians as performed by the Soviet authorities during the first weeks of the Second World War have been completed. The work in the Holocaust research task force has yielded most substantial results. Marģers Vestermanis, Director of the Museum and Documentation Centre Jews in Latvia, is presently working on an extensive monograph about resistance against the Holocaust in Latvia. Materials on those people in Latvia who rescued Jews during the Second World War are yet to be collected. The mass execution of Jews in Latvian towns and villages, the punishment of the members of the Arajs' commando during the period of the Soviet regime, the fate of Jewish refugees in Latvia in the period of 1933-1940, and the red terror against Jews in 1940-1953 are the themes under the scrutiny of a number of historians. Investigation of the crimes committed in Latvia during Nazi occupation in 1941-1945 is the third research theme being studied. Work on this project will also be continued in 2001. Research on the operation of the Latvian selfgovernment during the Nazi occupation period, the national resistance movement, and the elimination of Latvian intellectual potential has been accomplished. The fourth theme being studied is entitled The crimes against humanity committed in Latvia during the second Soviet occupation in 1941-1956. A research on the formation and operation of the state security institutions of the Latvian SSR has already been completed. Part of the research is dedicated to the activities of the authorities of the Latvian SSR state security institutions and their role in genocide against the Latvian nation. It is hoped to publish these findings in 2001."]

Name: 103_chernobyl_plant_nuclear_radiation
Representative_Docs:
['BALTIC UNREST ABOUT MOSCOW'S NUCLEAR POLICY

ANXIETY OVER NUCLEAR POWER PLANTS AND TOXIC WASTE DUMPS "Extreme concern" in the Baltie Sta tes about the course of Moscovv’s nuelear energy policy is deseribed by Ingo Urbān in the August 9 issue of Hamburg’s daily Die Welt. He vvrites that Western tourists returning from the Baltie area have obser ved a "rising anxiety" of the Lithuanians about the nuelear povver plant near Igna lina, in northeastern Lithuania. This plant is of the same type as the one in Cher nobyl. According to Urbān, the anxiety about the mammoth project at Ignalina has spillcd over into neighbouring Latvia. The chief source of concern there is the

radioactively contaminated water used to cool the reactor. This water reaches the Latvian river Daugava that flows into the Baltie Sea at Riga.

NEW POWER PLANT PLANNED IN LATVIA An additional nuelear povver plant is planned for Pāvilostā, a Latvian harbour betvveen Liepājā (pop. 112,000) and Ventspils (pop. 51,000). That area has been already dangerously polluted by a recently built crude oil refinery complex.

CANCER RĀTE INCREASE IN LATVIA The insecurity of the Latvian people, Urbān vvrites, has been intensified by the inerease in the cancer rāte in the vicinity of the research reactor (Type IRT-3), in Salaspils near Riga, which is run by the Latvian Academy of Sciences.

DANGEROUS PLANT NEAR RIGA ĶEPT SECRET The existence of a nuelear conversion plant in the area of Riga, Capital of Lat via, vvas accidentally brought to light in 1984 when the freighter “Mount Louis” stranded near Ostende, Belgium. Its cargo vvas uranhexaf]uoride, the starting Chemi cal for fuel used in nuelear povver plants and atomic bombs. The freighter vvas on its way from France to Riga.

LATVIA AS A TOXIC DUMP SITE In spite of this dangerous situation, the Soviet authorities do not seem to be changing any of their plāns, the German correspondent vvrites. A map published in the March 1985 issue of the Soviet Lat vian periodical Zinātne un Tehnika (Sci ence and Technology) indicates dump sites “for storing particularly dangerous

and toxic materiāls’’ that cover more than 25% of Latvia’s territory, or more than 15,000 square kilometers. In 1979, the same nevvspaper had designated these areas as future dumps for atomic vvaste and has reported about the “experiments to divert particularly toxic and radioac tive polluted vvaters in the depth of 1200 meters.” ELTA, VVashington, October, 1 986', 'LITHUANIAN NUCLEAR PLANT SHUT DOWN

Russia’s har-Tass news agency said a Lithuanian nuclear power plant was shut down Thursday after radiation was detected in a confined area of the plant. The Ignalina nuclear povverstation —the same design as the Chernobyl plant in Ukraine, site of the world’s vvorst nuclear power plant accident in 1986 was under going repairs when a rise in radiation levels was detected in a building housing the oper ating unit. No radiation was reported escaping the facility located in eastern Lithuania near the Belarus border, which suffered extensive contamination follovving the Chernobyl disaster. Lithuanian nuclear inspection chief Povilas Vaisnis said the cause of the radia tion was unknown and the plant was shut down until the reactor could cool and allow operators to trace the source of the problem. He described as isolated the building where radiation was detected and said “Ra diaiion did not spew out into the atmos phere”. The world has been nervous about nu clear plants across the former Soviet Union especially Chernobyl-type reactors ever since the Chernobyl accident released a cloud of radiation across Europe. This is the second problem to arise at the Ignalina nuclear station in the last three months.
