Name: 109_crime_number_registered
Representative_Docs: ["REGISTERED CRIME RATE GOES DOWN IN LATVIA The number of registered crimes in Latvia has decreased by 11.6% in the first six months of 1995, compared to the same time period in 1994; the Ministry of Internal Affairs' Press Centre reports that 18,333 crimes were registered in Latvia in the first six months of 1995. Compared to the first six months of 1994, the number of murders has gone down by 16.9% and the number of robberies by 27.6%. In turn, the number of fraud cases has increased by 33.8% and cases of pickpocketing have increased by 54.9%. The number of crimes committed by persons with a criminal background has increased by 15.7% and number of crimes committed by teenager is up by 20.1%. Criminal police have solved approximately 27.3% of the registered Grimes. Edited by Vidvuds Koskins.", 'CRIME IN BALTIC STATES In the first eight months of 1995, the number of registered crimes was 24,542 for Latvia, 40,103 for Lithuania, and 25,878 for Estonia, BNS reported recently. The crime rates per 10,000 residents were 96.3,107.9, and 173,4, respectively. Compared with the same period in 1994, the number of crimes declined by 9% in Latvia but grew by 12.8% in Lithuania and 11.1% in Estonia. The number of economic crimes decreased by 3.2% in Estonia but increased by 18.7% in Latvia and 8.1% in Lithuania The number of registered smuggling cases increased by 142.4% in Lithuania, 120% in Latvia, and 63.6% in Estonia.', 'THE CHICAGO LATVIAN NEWSLETTER * In December four Latvian high school students took part in an International computer science competition in Capetown, South Africa. Three of them returned home with medals; one gold and two silver. This is the first time when high school students from Latvia take such high places in international contests. * According to the "Business Eastern Europe" rent for office space in Riga is the highest in the Baltics and it is the same as in Helsinki or Stockholm. On the average it is 25 USD per square meter. * According to the official statistics of Ministry of Agriculture half of the meat in the meat processing industry in Latvia has been brought into the country illegally. The representatives of the meat processing industry classify this information as an accusation that is not based on facts. It is possible that the numbers don\'t add up due to the shortcomings in book-keeping. * According to the number of prisoners per capita Latvia takes a lead among European countries. There are 400 prisoners for every 100,000 people. In the countries of Northern Europe this number is 8 times smaller. * The former prime minister Andris Šķēle announced that he will establish his own political party. The first congress of this party may take place around Easter. So far official announcements about who will be in this party have not been made, but many popular names are being linked to it. * The Board of directors of the European Bank of Reconstruction and Development has made a recommendation to its authorized agents to hold the next meeting of European Bank of Reconstruction and Development in Riga. The Minister of Finances of Latvia says that usually the recommendations of the board of directors are the ones which are accepted. It means that some 3000 NEWS FROM LATVIA February 1998 reported by – 4000 leading financiers from around the world may come to Rīga for this meeting. * The Growth Domestic Product (GDP) in the third quarter of 1997 has grown in comparison to 1996 and has reached 7,6%. This is the highest growth of GDP after the regaining of Independence. Among the three Baltic states Latvia takes the middle position. The Minister of Finances Roberts Zīle has announced that the growth of GDP is one of the most important facts that have happened in Latvia in 1997. * The level of unemployment rate in Latvia has constantly been decreasing. On December 1,1997 the rate was 6,7%. The highest level of unemployment is in the Eastern part of the country – in Rēzekne 28,5% Krāslava 22%, in Preiļi 21,8% and Balvi 20,7%. In May the unemployment rate in the country peaked at 7,7%. * Although at the end of December the working hours for the Citizenship and Migration Service (Pilsonības un migrācijas pārvalde) were lengthened to help all Latvian citizens to change the old USSR passports to the passports of the Republic of Latvia, 20 000 citizens still haven\'t done it. Starting January 1998 the old USSR passports are no longer valid. Most of the citizens who haven\'t changed the passport have decided to do it during the last days of the year. A fee of 25 Lats is going to be charged to persons who haven\'t made the change required by the law. * The National Women\'s League of Latvia (Latvijas Sieviešu nacionālā līga) has started to develop a program of equal rights for all genders. The goal for this program is to concentrate on four different themes: legislation; education and employment; health and violence; women in social life and politics. This program will be finalized in Spring 1998. A similar program from Spain has been used as a base for the project. The program is financially supported by the PHARE program of EU. Šarlote Līduma in Rīga * The number of children born In the city of Riga in 1997 was 5505. This number is lower than in 1996 – 5715. The number of deceased in Riga in 1997 is 10 580, which also is lower than in 1996 (10,753). Also, last year 3898 marriages have been (3674 in 1996) registered in the city. 938 (912 in 1996) of these have been registered in churches. * The President of the Bank of Latvia Einārs Repše considers that 1997 has been a year of quiet but consistent development and that these traits will continue into 1998. Mr. Repše predicts many mergers of Latvian banks. He also predicts that the GDP (Growth Domestic Product) this year will be 6% and the inflation will be around 8% Last year the inflation was 7% instead of the predicted 8%. * A complete tourist guide for Latvia will be available in the stores starting April. It will be available in Latvian, English, German and Russian languages. The volume of 200 hundred pages will contain information about the possibilities of vacations in Latvia. So far it will be the only tourist guide which will look separately at Latvian regions. The book will contain descriptions of the most characteristic features of the regions. The information also will be placed on Internet. * According to statistics in 1997, 2994 persons have been naturalized. In the previous two years 1995 and 1996 together that number has been 3999. * In 1997 the number of registered enterprises has been the smallest after regaining of independence. It is considered that most of the people living in Latvia who have wanted to have their own enterprise, have it by now. Right now there are 51 private enterprises for every 1000 people living in Latvia. * According to the chairman of the Latvian Police Aldis Lieljuksis, growth of violent crimes has been observed lately. Altogether in 1997, 36,865 registered criminal acts have been committed. One of the most negative traits is the growth of violent crimes – 259 homicides and 400 crimes which resulted in the victim\'s death. Also, the crimes connected to drugs and alcohol are still on the rise. * 56,6% of people living in Latvia consider that corruption is very common in the country. 40,3% claim to have come into contact with the problem of corruption. 71,1% claim that officials are corrupt, 48,5% that policemen, 47,7% that the judges and 47,6% that the deputies are corrupt. Only 0,9% think that the corruption in Latvia is not common. * 27% of all investments in Latvia are from Denmark, 14% from Russia, 11% from the USA, 7% from UK, 5% from Germany, 5% from Sweden, 4% from Switzerland, 3% from Finland, 3% from Liberia, 2% from Austria, 19% from various other countries. The largest investor in terms of the number of investing companies and persons is Russia. The biggest investors come from USA with 177 million USD investment into Lattelekom. The second place with 40 million USD takes Norwegian company "Statoil" and Norwegian "Varner Gruppen" and "Hakon Gruppen" with the same amount of investment. Until the end of 1997 foreign investors had invested 520 million Lats in the Latvian economy (in 1993 – 50,3 million Lats). * After seven attempts to introduce changes of the legislation of the Republic of Latvia, parliament has lengthened the presidential and parliamental terms from three to four years. New changes will also affect the voters – instead of two days of voting now there will be only one day – the first Saturday of October. These changes will not affect the current parliament and president because they will become effective only after the next elections. * At the EU Summit in Luxembourg a proposal to enroll all 11 countries aspiring to enter the EU was accepted. In April bilateral dialogues are anticipated with the first 6 candidating countries, with the rest which includes Latvia, the talks about accelerating the entering into EU will be held. The remaining 5 countries can join the first 6 candidates as soon as they fulfill the necessary criteria towards joining the EU. In September 1998 new reports will be issued on the progress that the candidating countries have made. * In December a closed meeting was held by the government of Latvia to review the proposed agreement about the border with Russia. There were no objections raised against the newly revised version of this agreement. The talks about the border between Latvia and Russia have lasted for about a year and a half. The progress in these talks was reached when Latvia renounced it\'s demand to include a reference to the Peace treaty of 1920 in the agreement. The Ministry of Foreign Affairs in Russia has not yet agreed to name a concrete date for the signing of it. * According to initiative of the President of the Republic of Latvia a special government meeting was held regarding the country\'s defense. The Minister of Defense Tālavs Jundzis is planning to decrease the army by some 20 – 25% by April 1998. This action was initiated by comparatively small defense budget for the year 1998. It is smaller than in 1997; only 0,67% from Growth Domestic Product (GDP). In comparison Estonia and Lithuania have raised their defense budgets to 1,5% from their GDP. * * International credit rating agency MOODY has granted Latvia a credit rating Baa, which is equivalent to "Standard & Poor\'s" rating 888 and was granted to Latvia last year. Estonia and the Czech Republic have been granted one rating higher, but Lithuania – two lower. * The historical center of Rīga was included in UNESCO\'s World\'s inheritance centers committee\'s list where currently more than 400 different objects from all around the world, are included. So far the historical center of Rīga, the archeological complex of Grobiņa, the Livs village of Kosrags, the valley of Abava, the fortress of Daugavpils and wooden cottages in Jūrmala have been included only in the temporary list. Now the work towards joining all other objects to the list and to international acknowledgment will continue. * Beginning his visit to the United States of America President of the Republic of Latvia Guntis Ulmanis has addressed so far two audiences. First speech was held at a Institute for East – West Studies where President talked about possibilities of business and investments in Latvia The second speech was during meeting with the bank "Credit Suisse first Boston" which was interested in hearing about different projects of investments in the Baltic\'s. * Protesting against the death penalty the Dean of the Police Academy Zenons Indrikovs has refused to lead a group that was assigned to design a law of criminal penalty. According to Z. Indrikovs EU doesn\'t need this kind of barbaric leftover from the Middle Ages. Along with his refusal he has sent. 85 letters from "Amnesty International" which all express a protest towards the death penalty in Latvia. In 1996 President of the Republic of Latvia Guntis Ulmanis announced a moratorium on the execution of the death penalty. By entering European Council in 1995 Latvia agreed to undertake within a year the protocol Nr. 6 from Europe\'s Human Rights convention, which anticipates to abolish the death-penalty during the time of peace. However, in 1996 two people have been sentenced to death and executed.']

Name: 111_building_risbergs_1951_builder
Representative_Docs: ['CONSTRUCTION: That Crazy Latvian Bouncing out from behind his kingsize desk, the president of General Builders Corp. popped a flashlight into his mouth, corona-style, stooped before the office doorframe, and began pounding away with a make-believe hammer to demonstrate how he used to work all night as a carpenter before he became a building tycoon. "It\'s not such a damn big deal," Janis Risbergs noted in richly accented English at the end of the little show in his Babylon, N.Y., headquarters. "Only thing is yon have to take the flashlight out every fifteen minutes to spit." A flamboyant and bluntly goodhumored man, the onetime refugee from Latvia was busy last week nailing down the final details on General Builders\' two latest projects. These are a pair of high-rise apartment buildings in Fort Lauderdale, Fla., which will add about $8 million to the $30 million worth of housing now being put up by Ceneral Builders—a company that was rushing into bankruptcy four years ago when Risbergs took over. His\' success formula: hire hard-working, hard-bitten Latvians like himself. Hammer and Saw: Risbergs\' company of Latvians began forming in 1949, two yean after the laughing Lett arrived in the U.S. as a displaced person. He refused to return from a wartime stint of forced labor in Germany to a homeland overrun by Russians. Risbergs, who is 45 and looks even bigger than the 6 feet 2 inches, 210 pounds he admits to, studied chemical engineering at the University of Riga and economics at Heidelberg. But he went to work in the U.S. as a carpenter, a trade he learned on the Bay of Riga farm where he grew up. As Risbergs explains the decision: "Everybody thinks Norwegian, Finn, and Lett make good carpenter because homeland has lots of woods. So I work as carpenter." He picked his spot as well as his trade, eventually heading for Long Island where William Levitt had begun to build the first of his mass-produced Levittowns. Working on a piecework basis, Risbergs was soon the best-known and highest-paid carpenter on the project. There was good reason. Jānis Rīsbergs With his trusty flashlight, Risbergs often worked day and night, sometimes putting in twenty-hour shifts and sleeping on the job so he could get an early morning start. Where two men normally completed the molding in the interior of a house in a day and a half, Risbergs alone often finished the job in a single day. Risbergs was banking more than $200 a week, but it takes more than hard work to insure the kind of .success he wanted. "You have to advertise," he recalls. "You have to be a little cuckoo. So I carry tools around in a little wagon like you see in supermarkets. I wear short pants and long shirt. Sometimes I go barefoot. All the time, I sing. Soon everybody calls me that crazy Russian." Proposition* Levitt himself finally noticed Risbergs, and Risbergs was ready for him with a proposition. There were lots of skilled Latvian refugees around New York, eager to work—with flashlights in their mouths, if necessary. Risbergs became a subcontractor to Levitt, sometimes hiring up to 500 of his fellow countrymen and training them in the ways of mass-production builders. Within two years, he and his Latvians were in the home-building business for themselves. By the time the carpenters\' union organized his men, Risbergs was one of the biggest builders on Long Island. A good number of the men he gave jobs to had been professional people in the old country, offering, as Risbergs explains it, "a dollar\'s worth of talent for a dime. It just had to be organized." Among the Letts "organized" by Risbergs were Paul Irbe, now General Builders\' No. 2 man and the city of Riga\'s former manager of real estate and building; Nikolajs Silins, commercial sales manager and a former assistant district attorney of Riga; and Hugo Yitols, residential sales manager and a former law professor and newspaper publisher in Latvia\'s capital city. LellM at Large: Risbergs\' operations have more than doubled since he bought the controlling interest in General Builders in 1959, thus diluting the all-Latvian flavor of his work crews. But of the hundred top men in the company, all except a handful of specialists remain Letts. They run the middleincome home-building and apartment jobs General Builders has going for it on Long Island, in New York City and up the Hudson River in Newburgh, and in Atlanta, Fort Lauderdale, and St. Petersburg. Success, however, has done little to shorten Risbergs\' hours. His wife, Gaida, and the five boys, age 11 to 5, live on the expansive second floor of the sprawling, cinder-block building that serves as General Builders\' headquarters. That way, Risbergs explains, he doesn\'t waste time going to and from work. Reprinted with permission Newsweek, March 9,1964', '"Soviet Achievements" In Estonia "On the sites of the Tartu building trust the use of the new technique has not been learnt yet. Some of the machines are standing or working at an inexcusably low capacity. The plastering machine is idle because of the lack of an unessential detail, the work is done by hand." (June 5, 1951). "This spring the repair of the Tammistu high school building near Tartu as well as the building of a boarding house was started. At the beginning of the scholastic year the work was not finished yet. Therefore some pupils have to walk 15 kilometres daily. The windows are without glasses, the floors unpainted, the doors not set in* and the passages full of building shambles." (Oct. 12, 1950). "The boarding house of the Abja high school is in a bad state. The end of the repairs of the boarding house is not to be foreseen as yet. The pupils have to walk to the school from a distance of 35 kilometres daily. That is very hard, especially with regard to the fact that lessons at the high school take place in the afternoon and end at 8.30 p.m. (Oct. 19, 1950). "The building trust No. 3 is building the Jogeva school house since last year. The building has to be ready -by the third quarter of the year, but not even the rofing is finished as yet. The scholhouse is to be provided with water and drains, but the work on them has not been started yet. The building work is lagging chiefly because the building has not been supplied with building materials in time." (June 9, 1951). "The building office of the combine "Eesti Polevkivi" is building the house of the Mining Institute in Tallinn, 60-62 Parnu Street. The building was started already in 1949, but hitherto only the walls have been raised and even then not in their entirety, the roof is leaking, the ceilings between the stories are not built yet . . . Similar faults are occurring with regard to other schoolhouses which seriously endangers the possibility of their being ready in time." (June 9, 1951). "When a newcomer is inquiring for the raion culture house in the town of Jogeva, he is told to seek out the* most dirty house in V. Kingissepa Street, as that is the culture house of the Jogeva raion . . . The exterior of the culture house has become rather dilapidated in the course of years. Teh windows are broken. Around the culture house mud puddles are bounding and dirt is lying. The interior of the house is in no better state. The windows and panels are covered with the dust of years and it is very difficult to determine the original color of the floor under the thick coating of dirt." (June 8, 1951). Such on the admission of the Bolsheviks themselves are the housing conditions of the workers in the foremost country of Socialism. —News Letter.', 'Achievements of Communism — In Theory and Practice "The constant improvement of the living conditions of the workers is the foremost characteristics of Socialism. Care for the increased welfare of the people is dazzlingly demonstrated by the great extent of building of residential houses." —Leading article of RAHVA HAAL, June 12, 1951. Thus far the Bolshevik theory. We should like to illustrate these dazzling results of building activities with some examples culled from RAHVA HAAL, the organ of the Estonian Communist Party "The fulfilment of the build ing plan for housing has grea faults to contend with. Dur ing the Five Year Plan ther< have been built and rehabili tated (i.e., repaired building damaged by warfare. Ed.) resi dential buildings with a tota area, of 1,135,000 sq. metres ii towns and industrial settle ments" (i.e., about half a sq metre per inhabitant and year Ed.). (April 18, 1951, repor by Ist CP secretary I. Kabin) "Fifty-three per cent, of th< living space of Tallinn hac been damaged or destroyed bj warfare. In the postwar year: over 10,000 sq. metres of living space have besn built and rehabilitated in Tallinn" (i.e. the inhabitants of Tallinn whc lost their flats through warfare have obtained less than one sq metre of living space per inhabitant during seven years. Ed.). (April 22, 1951). "Only during a few years the living space turned over to the Tallinn workers has a deficit of more than 34,000 sq. metres (i.e., as compared to the plan. Ed.). The building of residential houses has not improved during the present year. During four months only 10 per cent. of the living space provided for by the plan have been turned over to the Housinge Fund." (June 3, 1951). "In the postwar years altogether .64 residential houses have been built and rehabilitated in Tartu." (During the war more than 2000 residential houses were desroyed in Tartu. Ed.). (June 10, 1951). "During five years Kohtla-Jarve has got almost 100,000 sq. metres of new living space." (i.e., every new town-dweller has received 4 sq. metres of living space. Ed.), (March 28, 1951). "During the Five Year Plan 2800 residential houses have been built and rehabilitated in the countryside." (After World War I, during Estonia\'s independence, about 40,000 residential houses were built in the countryside during five years. Ed.). (April 18, 1951). "Of the republican plan for 1950 in the feld of house building only 50.9 per cent, are fulfilled." (March 29, 1951, the budget speech of the chairman of the council of ministers, A. Muurisepp). "Most serious is the. backwardness of building of residential houses." (Feb. 9, 1951). "The housing shortage in our towns is also most severe." (Jan. 1, 1951, the new year speech of the chairman of the council of ministers, A. Veimer). "Everybody knows that the quality of work at many of our buildings is far from satisfactory." (Jan. 9, 1951, leading article). "The bad quality of the building work is causing frequent alterations." (June 12, 1951, leading article). "The Tartu Building Trust is one of the few building organisations in the republic which have fulfilled the building plan But "the factory of building details is still often producing crooked steps, ceiling plates are inexact . . . The quality of building works receives little attention. E.g., the house on 4. June 21st Street, was turned over into exploitation two years ago, but the roof is already leaking, windows and doors have centimeter-wide cracks, the ranges have cracks through sinking . . . On the building of the schoolhouse at Paiestee ■ Street, crookedly laid walls : foundations with holes, etc may be observed." (June £ i 1951). As regards the Tallinn Build ing Trust No. 1, "on mos building sites one may observ many rejects, alterations ar frequent . . . E.g., the briga idier of the housepainters, Mer iste when plastering house No 1, at 106, Parnu Street, discov ered that the plaster on th< walls was not even, full o cracks and partly lacking. Ir stead of ordering the respon sible workers to repair th( faults discovered, the mange ment of the trust decided tha the discoverer of the faults should remedy them." (Jan. 9 1951). "The communal house for young building workers of the building trust of the Tallinr . executive commitee (built duri ing the second Soviet occupation. Ed.) is in extremely bad repair. The roofs are leaking." (May 31, v 1951). "The communal boarding houses of the preparatory school i for the leading cadres of kolkhozes (at Kehtna. Ed.) were I turned over into exploitation last year, but this year capital repairs are necessary already. ... In houses which got ready a few months ago capital repairs are under way. The roofs were not rainproof, as a consequence the ceilings were spoilt and the chimneys got cracked, becoming a danger. By a common effort of the pupils canals for the fresh water pipes were dug, but the water was available for a few days only in the communal boarding houses, because the pipes broke." (August 24, 1950). "Many building organisations, especially the Tallinn and Narva building trusts have systematically not fulfilled and are not fulfilling their plan for capital buildings . . . The Tallinn building trust is unable systematically to supply the building sites with materials and the builders with necessary tools." (June 12, 1951, leading article). As regards the Tallinn building trust "there often are long pauses in the work, work is lagging, the workers are earning little . . . Often such works are quickly finished which quickly fulfil the plan. Other jobs frequently remain undone." (June 12, 1951). "Some economic managers have permitted people to move into houses not yet completely ready. It often happens that in the houses where people have moved in work remains unfinished for years, thus causing various inconveniences for the inhabitants as well as dilapidation of the houses." (Oct. 2, (1950, leading article). "The building and assembling trust of the ministry for sovkhozes was to have built a silo barn for the Kostivere sovkhoz by June 15, but on June 10 there was not even a project in existence for this building. Furthermore, on May 1 the trust had to deliver to the sovkhoz one cattle byre and one residential house and on June 1G two residential houses, but the trust could not manage this. The reason is that work is stopping constantly because of lack of building materials. The necessary equipment is also lacking." (June 10, 1950). "At the Party meeting of Kohtla-Jarve there were complaints because of planless building as the town lacks a general plan to date." (Kohtla-Jarve is a place where there has been more building than anywhere else. Ed.) (Jan. 16, 1951). "Already in November of last year the building of a new two-storied house was started in Parnu-Jaagupi. The house was to be ready in February, but is unfinished to date as all stoves were sinking crookedly and had to be rebuilt." (June 22, 1951). "The building work is rendered difficult by the bad work of the Supply Office of Agriculture as regards the supply with necessary materials. The projects and calculations are not organised any better. They arrive late and often contain grave faults."  23, 1951) "The "Sade" kolkhoz in Marjamaa raion has not been able to start the building of a cattle barn because of lack of cement and bricks. The kolkhoz "Punane Koit" has not been able to start the building of a pigsty for lack of cement." (June 23, 1951). "The foundation of the byre at the kolkhoz "Punane Taht" in Tartu raion is unfinished because of lack of lime. The work has stopped. The ground is dug for a pigsty in the kolkhoz "Uhme Soprus," but the work cannot be continued because lime is lacking. In the kolkhoz called for Zhdanov the building of a cattle barn has been discontinued for lack of lime and cement." (Estonia is rich in both. Ed.), (June 26, 1951). (To be continued)']

Name: 112_operation_co_border_agreement
Representative_Docs: ["CROSS-BORDER CO-OPERATION DISCUSSED AT LATVIA-RUSSIA MEETING On 8 April a delegation of the Ministry of Foreign Affairs, led by the Under-Secretary of State Mr Martins Virsis visited Pskov where they met Governor of Pskov region Mr Evgeny Mikhailov, representative of tie Ministry of Foreign Affairs of Russia in Pskov Mr Sergey Yefimenko, Mayor of Pskov Mr Mikhail Khorenen and President of the Chamber of Trade and industry of the region Mr Vladimir Zubov. In fie course of the meeting tie parties discussed tie following aspects of Latvia-Russia co-operation; cross-border co-operation, issues pertaining to the improvement of border crossing procedures and border infrastructure and the opportunities to stimulate business contacts between the two countries. The officials noted ttat the frontier regions of Latvia and Pskov region had common intereste in raising the level of welfare in the regions and the field of economy in general, which provided a good foundation for constructive co-operation. On 9 and 10 April the delegation visited St. Petersburg where they met Plenipotentiary Representative of tie Russian Federation President in the North-Western region Mr Victor Cherkesov, representative of the Ministry of Foreign Affairs Mr Victor Lopatnikov, Governor of St Petersburg Mr Vladimir Yakovtev, vice-governor of the Leningrad obtast Mr Vladimir Kfritov and Deputy Chairman of Russia's North-western Customs Authority Mr Pavel Korchazhkin. During tie meetings the parties discussed issues pertaining to the development of the legal basis for Latvia-Russia relations, stimulation of business contacts, improvement of border-crossing procedures and border infrastructure, cross-border co-operation, co-operation in the Baltic Sea region, the Northern Dimension and other topics, The parties were in agreement that Latvia's accession to the EU would stimulate bilateral economic co-operation and serve the intereste of the people of both countries. The Russian side acknowledged the good co-operation and active relations that had developed between that particular region of Russia and Latvia. On 11 and 12 April 2002 delegation visited Moscow where they met Deputy Minister of Foreign Affairs of Russia Mr Yevgeny Gusarov, Director of the 2nd Department of European Affairs Mr Alexander Udaftsov, Member of the Russian State Duma (Russian Regions) Mr Gleg Morozov, Vice-president of the Union of Industrialists and Entrepreneurs of Russia Mr Igor Jurgens and Chairman of the Foreign and Defence Policy Council Mr Sergey Karaganov. The Russian side pledged to stimulate the signing of bilateral agreements that are important for both sides. Officials of tie Ministry of Foreign Affaire of Russia expressed readiness to stimulate cross-border co-operation and business contacts. The sides agreed to continue co-operation between the Ministries of Foreign Affairs of both states. The discussions took place in the atmosphere of mutual interest", 'Foreign Ministry highlights the importance of two-way dialogue in the promotion of constructive Latvia – Russia relations On 5 March, political consultations took place between the foreign ministries of Latvia and Russia. The leader of the Latvian delegation, State Secretary of the Foreign Ministry Andris Teikmanis and the leader of the Russian side, Deputy Foreign Minister of Russia Vladimir Titov discussed the development of mutual political dialogue, both past and future, as well as the key issues relating to practical co-operation between the two countries. Both sides welcomed the exchange of visits by foreign ministers, as well as the close practical co-operation which has evolved between state institutions of both countries. It was agreed that this kind of co-operation should be continued, and that this year there could be a further meeting between foreign ministers where an intergovernmental agreement could also be signed, for example, an agreement on the simplification of trans-border travel for inhabitants of border areas. Work must also be continued on draft economic agreements, as well as draft projects on co-operation in recovery from a state of emergency and on co-operation in combating terrorism and organised crime. The positive dynamic of trade was also evaluated, and both sides acknowledged that the Latvia and Russia Intergovernmental Commission has a significant role in the resolution of practical issues. It is anticipated that the next meeting of the said commission will take place in July, in Riga. The Latvian State Secretary and the Russian Deputy Foreign Minister were in agreement that the work of the of the Border Demarcation Commission and the Combined Commission as provided for in the Agreement on Burial Places should begin as soon as possible. For a more effective operation of the latter, the Latvian side drew attention to the importance compiling of a list of burial places. Mr Teikmanis emphasised the importance of the involvement of Russian experts in the investigation of displaced Latvian cultural historic heritage now located in Russia. Both sides agreed that a meeting between Latvian and Russian archive representatives should be held, in order to discuss existing issues and to reach an agreement on further mutual co-operation. During the consultations both sides exchanged opinions on co-operation between the EU and Russia, as well as on NATO and Russia, and in addition reviewed current international events.', "Foreign Ministers of Latvia and Russia note historic significance of visit On 18 December, as part of his working visit to Latvia, Sergey Lavrov, Foreign Minister of the Russian Federation, met with Maris Riekstiņš, Foreign Minister of Latvia. During the meeting, the Ministers discussed topical issues of bilateral co-operation as well as co-operation between the EU and Russia, and the most important international issues: the situations in Kosovo and Iran. During the meeting, the Ministers exchanged the ratification documents for the State Border Treaty between the Republic of Latvia and the Russian Federation, and signed an Agreement between the Governments of Latvia and the Russian Federation on the status of Latvia's burial sites in the territory of Russia and Russia's burial sites in the territory of Latvia, and an Agreement between Latvia and Russia on cooperation on social security issues. The Foreign Ministers noted the historical significance of this visit, and agreed that the first bilateral visit to Latvia by Russia's Foreign Minister, as well as the coming into force of the Treaty on the Latvia-Russia State Border Treaty, should be regarded as a positive step within the context of bilateral relations. The Ministers agreed that work on the enhancement of the border infrastructure should be continued. It is also necessary to establish a Latvia-Russia state border demarcation commission in order to begin work on determination of the border. Minister Riekstiņš emphasized that it is important to sign an agreement between Latvia and Russia on border crossing points. The Ministers also acknowledged that the practical advantages of the Latvia-Russia Intergovernmental Commission (IGC) instrument should be actively utilised to resolve issues of practical co-operation in the future. They agreed that the IGC Task Force on Humanitarian Co-operation would convene in February 2008 in Riga, followed by a meeting in Riga of the Task Force on Economic Co-operation in April 2008. The third IGC working group, the Task Force on Co-operation in the Transportation Sector, will meet in Riga on 20 December 2007. Minister Riekstiņš emphasized that solutions should be sought for the normalisation of the situation on the EU-Russia border, and for the increase of the throughput capacity of border crossings. The Ministers concurred that the experts should reach an agreement in the near future on the opening of the Opuli-Mogili border control point. The Ministers confirmed the need for expert consultations to continue in order to achieve definite results in the issue of displaced items of cultural and historical value. The Ministers also agreed on the priority to finalise such agreements as On the Avoidance of Double Taxation and the Prevention of Fiscal Evasion with Respect to Taxes on Income and Capital and On the Protection and Promotion of Foreign Investment. Likewise, it is important to finalise negotiations on the draft agreement On the Procedures of the Issue of Visas and The Crossing of State Borders by Frontier Zone Inhabitants in the near future. Work should continue towards the signing of the agreement On Co-Operation in the Prevention of Emergency Situation Threats and the Resolution of their Consequences and the agreement On Co-Operation Regarding Environmental Protection. Minister Lavrov informed his Latvian counterpart of the invitation by Russia for Latvian State President Valdis Zatlers to visit Russia. Latvia noted that it has forwarded an invitation to the leader of Russia's government to take part in the summit of the Council of the Baltic Sea States (CBSS), to take place in Riga in June 2008. The Ministers agreed that co-operation between historians in the research of various historical issues should be encouraged. Mr Riekstiņš accentuated the need to provide the same level of access to archives as is granted to Russian researchers in Latvia. Responding to the Russian Foreign Minister, Minister Riekstiņš provided a briefing on the integration processes Edited by Pēteris Kļaviņš within Latvia's community. Mr Riekstiņš went on to familiarise Mr Lavrov with the priorities of the Latvian Presidency in the CBSS and on progress in their implementation. The Ministers also discussed potential areas of cooperation between the EU and Russia, including talks on the new Partnership and Co-operation agreement. Latvia expressed support for Russia's accession process to the World Trade Organisation in the near future: highlighting the'need to resolveseveral existing issues, including the imposition of equal tariffs for exports and imports by rail freight within the territory of Russia. With regard to the Treaty on Conventional Armed Forces in Europe (CFE), Minister Riekstiņš noted that Russia's unilateral decision to suspend its participation in the Treaty would not promote mutual trust. However, he commended Russia's intention, as confirmed by Foreign Minister Lavrov, not to increase the concentration of Russian armed forces near Russia's borders. Mr Lavrov noted that Russia is prepared to continue negotiations with the NATO alliance on this issue. During the press conference Minister Riekstiņš expressed Latvia's view that the U.S. negotiations with Poland and the Czech Republic regarding the deployment of an anti-missile defence shield should not have a negative impact on the balance of power in Europe. Mr Riekstiņš also indicated that it is important to ensure the transparency of this negotiation process. As part of his visit, the Russian Foreign Minister also met with President Valdis Zatlers, and Prime Minister Aigars Kalvitis."]
