Name: 114_lileikis_trial_gimzauskas_aleksandras
Representative_Docs: ["VILNIUS TO FORMALLY CHARGE LILEIKIS WITH WAR CRIMES Lithuanian investigators on 4 February announced they are bringing formal charges against alleged war criminal Aleksandras Lileikis. Kazimieras Kovarskas, the head of the Special Investigations Department at the Prosecutor-General's Office, said the case could be in court within the next few days. He added that the trial itself could begin within four to six weeks and last about a week. Lileikis was head of the Vilnius security police during World War Two and is alleged to have ordered the deaths of scores of Jews. The Simon Wiesenthal Center has repeatedly accused Vilnius of dragging Its feet over putting Lileikis and other alleged war criminals on trial.", 'LILEIKIS TRIAL TO BEGIN IN SEPTEMBER A Vilnius judge has set 1 September as the start of the trial of suspected war criminal Aleksandras Lileikis, 91. Lileikis, who is currently bedridden owing to ill health, is accused of having sent scores of Jews to death camps when he was head of the Vilnius security police during World War 11. He was charged earlier this year, but his trial was postponed to allow testimony to be gathered from two more witnesses, one of whom says Lileikis saved her life. The Simon Wiesenthal Centre has strongly criticised the delay in starting the trial, saying it is "outrageous... [and] does not reflect well on the will of Lithuania to prosecute its countrymen who collaborated with the Nazis".', 'LITHUANIAN DOCTORS SAY ALLEGED WAR CRIMINAL FIT TO STAND TRIAL A panel of Lithuanian doctors has found that alleged Nazi war criminal Aleksandras Lileikis is fit to stand trial. "The current state of Lileikis\'s health does allow him to take part and testify in the trial, but any stress could lead to a health condition hazardous to his We," the panel concluded in its report. The 91-year-old Lileikis is accused of handing over Jews to Nazi death squads while head of the Vilnius security police during the German occupation of Lithuania. His trial had to be postponed in September after his legal team said he was too ill to appear in court. Under Lithuanian law, trial in absentia for medical reasons is prohibited.']

Name: 115_nato_membership_support_respondent
Representative_Docs: ["Support to Latvia's NATO membership continues to grow Support within Latvian society for Latvia's membership of NATO continues to grow and has currently reached its highest level ever. The most recent survey by the market and public opinion research company Latvijas fakti shows that Latvia's membership in NATO is supported by 78.8% of the population. In comparison to survey results from June 2005, public support for membership of Latvia in NATO has increased by 1.1 % (in June 2005 the figure was 77.7%). Latvia's residents most often (30.7%) justify their support for NATO with the arguments that membership of Latvia in NATO increases safety of the state and of its residents; that a common defence is cheaper than an individual one (15.5%); and that the membership improves Latvia's international image and influence (13.3%). Latvia's residents consider that the key task of NATO is participation in the peace-keeping processes around the world (41.1%).", 'VISAS NO LONGER REQUIRED As of 1 January 2003, citizens of Australia, New Zealand and Canada, in possession of their country\'s official citizen travel document are able to enter the territory of the Republic of Latvia without a visa and remain there for up to 90 days within a six-month period. The unilateral establishment of a visa-free regime for the citizens of the said countries is based on Latvia\'s efforts to promote economic and cultural contacts with Australia, New Zealand and Canada, and contribute to efforts to achieve the easing of entry requirements for citizens of Latvia wishing to travel to these countries. TWO THIRDS FAVOUR NATO MEMBERSHIP The most recent survey by the market and public opinion research company Latvijas Fakti, conducted in December 2002, shows that two thirds of Latvia\'s population support NATO membership for Latvia. In comparison to survey results from July 2002, the number of respondents supporting or not disagreeing with Latvia\'s membership of NATO has risen from 66.1% to 68.5%. Of those not disagreeing, 14.1% expressed the wish to receive more information on the subject (in March the figure was 16.7%, in July 14.8%). Results of the survey also reveal that with Latvia having been invited to become a NATO member state, the number of people who have not formed an opinion on Latvia\'s NATO membership has fallen (from 11.5% in March to 7.3% in July and 5.5% in December). Meanwhile 26.0% of respondents (26.6% in July) consider that Latvia\'s membership of NATO is unnecessary. Of those with a negative point of view, in response to the question "How is it possible to guarantee Latvia\'s security without NATO membership?" 26.4% answered that Latvia should preserve its', "PUBLIC SUPPORT FOR NATO MEMBERSHIP HIGH IN LATVIA The most recent public opinion poll conducted by market and public opinion firm Latvijas Fakti in December 2003 shows that the majority of people in Latvia support their country's membership of NATO and that their support is increasing. The poll reveals that over two thirds of Latvia's residents or 68.7% are for Latvia's membership of NATO whereas in June 2003 the figure was 67.4%. The most frequently given reason for endorsing NATO membership (25.4% of all supporters) was higher security for the country and its people. This was also one of the most popular arguments in the previous public opinion polls. In the December 2003 survey, another increasingly popular argument for joining NATO was that membership would raise Latvia's prestige and increase its influence internationally. As for opposition to Latvia's membership of NATO, there has been a small decrease year-on-year: in December 2003 23.1 % of respondents were against Latvia joining NATO, whereas in December 2! 002 that figure was 26% and in June 2003 – 24.2%."]

Name: 116_eurovision_contest_final_naumova
Representative_Docs: ['GOVERNMENT APPROVES EUROVISION FINALS IN LATVIA The Cabinet of Ministers at the meeting on 4 June 2002 approved organising the finals of the 48th Eurovision song contest In Latvia, Minister of Environmental Protection and Regional Development, acting Minister of Culture Mr Vladimirs Makarovs told reporters. The Ministry of Culture, in conjunction with the State Chancellery, will sign Latvia\'s letter of confirmation that will state Latvia\'s determination to organise the event, and guarantee that the Latvian State Television (LTV) is allotted no less than CHF 5 million to organise the contest. A task fGrce will be set up by the LTV, National Radio and Television Council, several ministries\' state secretaries and their deputies. The task force will work on organising the Eurovision song contest and report to the government on estimated expenses, the most suitable location and venue and other matters. By the Midsummer Holiday {23 June), LTV could prepare a rough estimate, the composition of the task force and guidelines for implementation of the project, Mr Makarovs said. The contest\'s organisational matters will be reviewed by LTV, while the task force will be a "political guarantee that the event takes place". Latvia earned the opportunity to organise the contest\'s finals in 2003 after pop singer Marija Naumova won the 47th Eurovision song contest in Tallinn this year. Edited by Ēriks Zvirbulis', 'LATVIAN SINGER WINS EUROVISION SONG CONTEST 2002 Latvian singer Marija Naumova, who participated in the Eurovision Song Contest 2002 under the stage name of Marie N„, won the Eurovision finals in the Estonian capita! Tallinn on 25 May 2002, As the contest was won by the Latvian representative. Latvia will get the right to host next year\'s Eurovision finals. When a German reporter asked Marija Naumova what\'s really going on with Eurovision so that small countries like Estonia and Latvia keep winning it, she replied: "Forget what you\'re doing, forget that you\'re Germans, forget the Ist place, just give a little wamrth in your performance" and earned a rapturous round of applause from the audience. The song / Wanna, performed by Naumova, scored 176 points in the Eurovision finals, and the runner-up was Maltese singer fra Losco with 164 points she earned for the song 7th Wonder. Naumova herself wrote music to the winning song / Wanna and created lyrics together with Marats Samauskis.', 'BOOKINGS FOR EUROVISION 2003 IN LATVIA TO OPEN IN DECEMBER The booking of tickets to the Eurovision Song Contest finals, to be held in Riga next spring, will be available as early as this December, said the public broadcaster Latvian Television (LTV), responsible for organisation of the event. Ticket prices have not yet been set, but are likely to be a iittie iower than the admission fee to the Eurovision 2002 finals in Tallinn, that varied from 120 to 190 lats. There will be a total of three concerts – two dress rehearsals on the night of 23 May and on the morning of 24 May, with the gala concert held late on 24 May, said LTV spokeswoman Ingrīda Šmite. alt is not certain yet how many seats there will be for the audience because this will depend on the arrangement of cameras in the Skonto Hall and we are working on it now," said the spokeswoman. The Skonto Hall in tile Latvian capital Riga, picked as the venue of the Eurovision 2003 finals, can seat up to 5,000 people. On 29 November, Eurovision 2003 contenders will come to Riga to draw lots to determine the sequence in which singers will perform at the finals in Rīga next spring. Then Latvia will also announce hosts for the Eurovision ceremony. Latvia gained the right to host the Eurovision 2003 finals after Latvia\'s representative Marija Naumova won the Eurovision 2002 finals in Estonia under the stage name of Marie N.']

Name: 117_bank_deposit_rāte
Representative_Docs: ["BANK OF LATVIA LEAVES INTEREST RATES UNCHANGED On 13 November 2003, the Board of Governors of the Bank of Latvia, despite earlier concerns that the interest rates could be raised to counter the growth of Latvia's current account deficit, decided not to change current annual interest rates on refinancing, bank deposits at the central bank and lombard loans. The Bank of Latvia said the refinancing rate would remain at 3%. For lombard loans of up to ten days the interest rate is 5%, from 11th to 20th day the rate of 6% will apply and the rate of 7% will be used, starting from 21st day. The interest rate on seven-day bank deposits made with the central bank in lats is 2%, and for 14-day deposits the rate is 2.25%. The central bank decided to leave the interest rates unchanged based on evaluation of Latvia's economic growth. The President of the Bank of Latvia, llmars Rimsevics, said at a news conference that Latvia's economic growth had been rapid enough in recent months but there was no reason to fear that the economy was overheating. Therefore the central bank will stick to its current GDP growth forecast for 2003 at 7%. The last time the Bank of Latvia changed its rates was on 16 September 2002, when it lowered them by 0.5% to bring down interest rates in the country and close the gap between rates on loans in the national and foreign currencies.", "LATVIA'S BANKS POST 23.5 MILLION LATS PROFIT-UP BY 16.9% YEAR-ON-YEAR The aggregate profit of Latvia's banks in the first six months of 2002 was 23.5 million lats, up by 16.9% year-on-year, according to Bankof Latvia information. Assets of Latvia's banks amounted to 3.832 billion lats at the end of June, up by 20% year-on-year. The residue of loans made to domestic companies and private individuals were 1,514 billion lats at the end of June, up by 41.2% yearon-year. The residue of deposits by domestic companies and private individuals at he end of June amounted to 1.229 billion lats, up by 26.7% year-on-year. The capital and reserves of the banks were 345.3 million lats at the end of June, up by 30.7% year-on-year. At the end of June Latvia had 23 commercial banks operating, including one branch of a foreign bank.", 'LATVIAN AVERAGE DEPOSIT RATES 3,3% HIGHER IN OCTOBER Latvian credit institutions increased their average annual deposit rates in October by 3.3%, according to the State Statistics Committee. The average rate of long-term deposits in October was 20.5% as compared to 17.2% in September. Rates were 24.9% lower than during the same period a year earlier, when they averaged 45.4%. The figure was 87.7% in October 1993. The average annual rate on short-term deposits fell to 12.3% as compared to 14.7% in September. The rate was 21.3% a year earlier and 29.9% in October 1993. The average annual long-term credit rate was 32.1 % in October, 2.5% higher than in September. The long-term credit rate a year earlier was 44.2%, while in 1993 the figure was 43.1 %. The short-term annual credit rate was 32.5% in October as compared to 35% in September. The short-term credit rate was 30.7% last year and 81.5% in October 1993.']

Name: 118_valodas_camper_period_camp
Representative_Docs: ['ļVIEPOKLIS Valodas periods pašlaik > ii|or k.is universitātes (New York University) "Study Abroad" programmas ietvaros Ganā, Āfrikā I am half-Latvian by birth, and grew up speaking Latvian, going to Latvian school on Saturdays (not always willingly) Māra Dajevskis, and the Latvian sessions of New York Latvian Church Camp in Elka Park (more than willingly.) 1 began going to Nometne, as the camp is universally known among those who find their home for the summers there, every year since l>efore I can remember. I grew up with Nometne as a part of me, and still maintain that it is the most beautiful place on earth – if not for mountains and the trees, then for the sacred memories it holds for me. Camp was a constant for me, and a cornerstone of my childhood. The friends I met there are connected to me profoundly, deeply and permanendy, in a way tiiat is incomparable to any other group of friends or acquaintances I have ever had. Through the ups and downs and inevitable struggles of growing up, I believe that having this network of people who had known me since l>efore I knew myself, who accepted me unconditionally and who have became my extended family, was a foundation of my being. In retrospect, the knowledge that I would be returning to this haven every summer, to a place I a mid call my own, that belonged to me and my extended family is one of the things that held me together through the tumult of adolescence. During high school I transferred my attentions temporarily to Garezers Latvian Summer High School in Three Rivers, Michigan. However, as soon as I graduated I returned to Nometne, my home, as a counselor and discovered a side of Nometne Fd never seen: Valodas Periods, the English-speaking session. As a camper, I\'d had minimal contact with Valodas Periods. There was a silent divide between participants in the Latvian speaking and non-Latvian speaking sessions. As We, the Latvian speakers, packed up our trunks and suitcases at the end of the second session to make way for Them, the Valodas lads, they seemed alien. They were strangers to me, and seeing all the unfamiliar laces in my home was disconcerting. What were They doing here? As a Valodas counselor the last two years, I found out, They were doing the same things we always did at camp: singing, laughing, folk-dancing, swimming, playing volleyball, going to bonfires, roasting marsnmallows, breathing fresh air, running through the woods. Sharing secrets, starting friendships, growing up. My experiences at Valodas brought about some small epiphanies. One was related to the truly wonderful people I met on the staff. My colleagues at Valodas professed the same passionate and undying love that I did for Nometne and all that it entails: the legacy and traditions, the sense of wonderment and mystery of what lies up the mountain that never quite disappears with age, its solidity ana solidarity. Trie campers and counselors at Valodas had the same feeling of at-homeness there that I did. I lus place l>elonged to them as much as it did to me. The other epiphany came last summer when I had the opportunity to teach the Latvian language class to the campers. In doing so, I discovered something amazing about the kids – they were excited to learn Latvian! For me, growing up speaking Latvian had always been a matter of pride ana guilt. It was ingrained in me that holding on to the Latvian language was the only thing that would hold our Latvian community together. Although knowing Latvian is intensely precious to me now, I seem to recall that more often than not, my shame at speaking imperfectly and my struggles with grammar and the nominative and genitive cases tended to overshadow the pride that had been instilled in me for the Mother Tongue. To my surprise and gratification, the campers did not view learning Latvian as a chore or a mandatory imposition on freedom and leisure rime, or some profound but vaguely archaic responsibility to times past. For them it was like a special privilege and most of all, it was fun. It was so exciting to me to see Valodas periods the campers\' enthusiasm – even some of those who had already "graduated" from camp and moved on to the next echelon in camp hierarchy, the dishwashers (affectionately called Kitchen Slime) came to class, and it was heartening to observe how much the older groups knew and had learned over their years at Valodas compared to the little ones. As it becomes more and more difficult, in some cases impossible, to remain fluent in Latvian as the Latvian community assimilates into American society, Valodas Periods is growing, while the numbers of campers and counselors that can commit to the full Latvian language sessi< his seem to be dwindling. I see this not as some kind of loss or failure, but as a great opportunity to embrace our potential to form a new identity, to recognize what makes the Latvian American community valuable, and in my eyes indispensable: not the ability to name every river in Latvia or conjugate all the irregular verbs without error, but the human connections our Latvian identity allows us to have with those who share a common past. Latvia is a very different world than it was M) years ago when my father attended Nometne. It is changing and expanding along with the rest of the world, and neither will the Latvian American community remain frozen in time. We are a new generation. We are no longer Latvians in exile, but Latvian Americans, and I believe that Valodas Periods is the future of the Latvian American community. What has become important now are not so much our ties to the Fatherland, but our ties with each other, and the lifelong friendships that have come from our collective identity as Latvian Americans.', 'Love is in The Air at Valodas Periods By Alexandra Winzeler From the instant of arrival at the Valodas Periods, the Language Session, of the Latvian Lutheran Church camp, the members of the 2008 staff were vibrant. This was not merely from their glowing personalities, but because the new workers shirts were hot pink, fitting for this year\'s theme of milestiba, love. Whether named or not, love has always been a feeling experienced by those involved with Valodas Periods. That is the reason why so many campers continue to return and why so many new campers are inspired to join. The 2008 session experienced its biggest enrollment to date: eighty-tour campers. Many of these new additions to the Language Session, were younger campers, discovering afresh why so many people love their two weeks at Latvian camp. It is hard not to put your heart into your time at camp when having so much fun with great activities like taking a hike to Spruce Top, or going on a mountain biking field trip. Not to mention the favorite standards of dances, campfires, sports, swimming, language class, dance class and art class. Even these activities get new life every year with new projects, games and challenges. The art department had the campers hammering ancient shield designs on copper and aluminum to make cool, modern medallions. The kids had to invent commercials for camp to act out as their campfire skits, producing great results such as a War in the Woods training video or an ad for a company where you can buy all your favorite counselors. Sports were especially full of excitement this year. For the first time, campers had the opportunity for additional soccer practices and games coached by Guntars Tannis. Sports got even more exciting with the staging of a camp-wide Olympic games, complete with the lighting of an Olympic torch. Everyone\'s love was even more widespread than the boundaries of camp on the day of the games, all the athletes showing a stronger caring and pride for their heritage, for Latvia, as they eagerly asked for any news of tne score in the real Olympics where the Latvian volleyball team was playing. Along with everyones favorite traditional activities, a group of the older campers continued a relatively new tradition. Under the guidance of Macitajs Laris Salins, the students of the third confirmation class met during camp to have discussions and further their religious studies in preparation to be confirmed at camp next year. There was also a fresh embracing of Latvian culture in the closing ceremonies of the session. Tne new generation of counselors, who had been campers at Valodas Periods for years, wore elements of folk costumes especially for their dance performances. In keeping with the overall theme of milestiba, for the midsession costume ball the attendees were to dress as someone or something they love. An opportunity for people to truly wear their hearts on their sleeves, everyone\'s genuine care was apparent at the dance; friends and siblings dressed up as each other, campers dressed up as counselors and counselors dressed up as campers. One of these camper-clad counselors commented on his costume saying, his years as a camper here were "the best ten years of his life." This is undoubtedly a sentiment on the minds of some of the older kids, nearing the bridge when they eventually get too old to be campers. But there\'s no need to cry: this doesn\'t mean the end of their camp experience. Take a look at the new camp director, Elissa Millers. She has experienced every role at Valodas Periods from attending as a camper, to working in the kitchen, to being a counselor, teaching tautas dejas, to organizing the games as activities director, and now being in charge as camp director. Elissa has kept coming back for the love of camp. So who knows? Maybe the graduating campers will keep coming back too. The camp might need them as staff or counselors in order manage the constantly growing group of campers who will be finding out for themselves, all the reasons to love Valodas Periods.', 'This years iesvētāmie are leva and Roberts Eason, Kersti Francis, Eriks Muir, Christopher La-Fonte, Nikolette Robertella, Anda Simmons and Laura Van Valkenburg. "The iesvētāmie come from many areas, Kersti Francis, as far away as West VA., who travels every year to Catskills are bonded by their summers at nometne. Most of them have known each other since īkšķīši and most recently by an overnight confirmation retreat held on April 14, 2007 in the Catskills where they all stayed in the Ezen Maja. As individuals, they bring their own interests and insights to the group and this confirmation experience. Erik Muir never misses the opportunity to educate his friends about Latvia. He feels being Latvian has afforded him many different experiences, including this confirmation instruction, which has given him many new perspectives. Erik\'s best memory of īkšķīši is the Olympic circuit that was set up for them every year and standing on the podium to receive their ribbons. He always looks forward to War in the Woods, Capture the Flag and any activity associated with the "sporta komandas." For the last 2 summers these children have been studying with Māc. Laris Saliņš. During Nometne and at home by e-mail. Māc Saliņš said: "Many of you really were able to sum up what the study has meant to you. The camp experience is both the most demanding and the most rewarding thing I do every year." "The Children also requested a special mention of loved ones that have past away but we still want to remember on this special day. Thank you for all your work and help to make the lesvētības a beautiful and meaningful experience." Nikolette Robertella, Anda Simmons, Roberts Easton, Ieva Easton Iesvētāmie in the Catskills Psalm 24: Who shall ascend the hill of the LORD? And who shall stand in his holy place? (Kas drīkst kāpt tā Kunga kalnā, un kas stāvēs Viņa svētajā vietā?) Heb. 11: They were strangers and pilgrims on the earth. But now they desire a better country, that is, a heavenly one. (Tie bija svešnieki un svētceļnieki virs zemes. Bet tie nu ilgojas pēc labākas, debešķīgākas piederības.) Last year this camp, the camp of the Latvian Lutheran Church of New York, celebrated a memorable anniversary – what Latvians call "apaļi gadi" – "round years" – the big "5 – 0". That was a good chunk of history to look back on. But let\'s also not forget that "Valodas periods" – the part of our camp program targeted to children who don\'t have fluent Latvian – has been around now for at least half that time – depends on how you count the beginning of Valodas periods. The program began in 1979 when it ran besides other camp programs. In 1982 Valodas periods became a separate period with its own 2-week time slot. If we count 1982 as the beginning of Valodas periods, then this is our 25th anniversary summer. We can thank Ingrida Miemis for her initiative in beginning this period. She started it for her brother-in-law\'s daughter Joanne, but – her personal need resonated with a much broader need. And it is no accident that Ingrida\'s children and grandchildren are with us here now. This extended of family spirit is what has made our camp work all along. At first this Valodas periods faced substantial obstacles. When the post-WWII refugees first conceived the mission of the Latvian church and the church\'s camp, the model was "fortress Latvia". That meant that only Latvian was spoken. Even in the early 1980s any retreat from this consistent stance was seen by many as blasphemy. It is maybe for this reason that this program is somewhat misnamed as the "Language period". If the goal was to turn the campers into Latvian speakers, most everybody could buy into that. Fortunately this goal was quickly seen as largely unrealistic. The new and more appropriate emphasis was and has remained on the Latvian heritage taught in English but supplemented with Latvian folk songs and dances, and Latvian Lutheran church hymns and liturgy. Language classes offer those who have that calling more in depth opportunities to learn the Latvian language. Especially now, when we can thank God that Latvia is secure in its regained freedom, we no longer need a "fortress mentality" and can reconceive our misson to be a welcoming community for everyone who has a connection to the Latvian Lutheran family. Looking back on the time that I have been involved with the Valodas periods, I like to think that we have come a long way. And I believe that this worship service with the confirmation of 8 of our young people is a beautiful expression of how we\'ve been able to develop meaningful traditions that we can pass on to the coming generations. As a point of contrast I want to share what amounts to a horror story from the early years of the Valodas periods. I\'ve been a pastor of the Latvian Lutheran Church of New York since 1984. In 1986 I was the director of the summer camp. Like this year, there was a Latvian Song Festival over the 4th of July weekend. It did not seem feasible to begin the Latvian language camp while the Song Festival was going on. The camp committee, therefore, decided to rearrange the order of the periods. That summer camp began with the Valodas periods. The camp commit tee rightly figured that the Song Festival would not much affect the Valodas periods. But there were other significant factors that the committee had seriously misjudged. Even though the campers were here for the beginning of camp, most of the counselors were still at the Song Festival in Toronto. As I recall, only Baiba Kļaviņa, who was the program director, and less than a handful of counselors were there for the first evening. We attempted to begin camp in what was the traditional way – with a candlelight service in this sanctuary. If memory serves me, there were about 60 kids in camp. Before beginning the winding journey up the hillside with the pastor in the lead, we instructed the campers that they were to walk quietly and in single file. As this was being told to the campers they were already talking among themselves. 1 think the gist of what they were saying was: "Yah, right! No way, Jose!" That night we didn\'t have a silent, single file procession but a loud and unruly mob. Instead of leading the climb, I found myself Laris Saliņš in the role of frazzled sheep dog running up and down the hillside trying to direct and "shush" the campers. My attempts to lead the campers through a liturgy for the service were a shambles. On that evening it seemed that I would have had just as much lucking trying to get the campers to sing in Swahili as in Latvian. Eventually, as the counselors trickled in from the Song Festival – and slept off their hangovers – the Valodas Periods program got in gear and the outcome was a good experience, but the beginning was terribly shaky. This year when leva Alversons asked me to begin this period with a candlelight service, we knew that we would not have to sell the campers on "why the silent walk" or "why sing in Latvian". Even though a good number of the campers were here for the first time, we knew that we had a solid core of campers who have been here from year to year. They know the drill and they know that the whole experience is better, more beautiful and more meaningful when we respect our traditions. I wrote the text to the hymn that we just sang for the camp\'s 50th anniversary in order to evoke our shared experience of worshiping God in this sanctuary. If this period\'s characteristic walk to our hillside sanctuary were still like mob experience 20 years ago, the hymn would not be appropriate for this group. I felt challenged to translate the hymn into English, because this period has so fully grown into our camp\'s traditions, that we all have a snared experience of the power of worship in this sanctuary in the heart of nature\'s beauty. I was confident that we could sing – without irony – about "calming silence (that) guides our course". Today is not the first Valodas periods Confirmation. 3 years ago Brita and Astrida Vallens were confirmed here. But that was not yet the beginning of a Confirmation program. It was just one family asking for a service and me doing what I could to provide it. But once they took that step, a whole group of other families realized that they would like something similar. We began preparing for this day over a year ago and we met at camp every day last year and this year during Klusā stunda, the Quiet Hour in the early afternoon. We studied the Creed, the 10 commandments, and the high points of the biblical story. We did what Confirmation classes usually do. The main difference being that we lived at substantial distances form one another – some of us as far away as Ohio and Virginia. But we live in the era of the Internet and I was able to give and receive assignments via email. And we all even made our way back here in April for a confirmation retreat. Maybe some of you saw our picture in the church newsletter. We were hit by a late season storm on Sunday morning and almost got snowed in. The whole Confirmation process culminates in this service. By reciting the Apostle\'s Creed, the confirmands affirmed the faith that had been affirmed in their stead – by their parents and godparents – when they were baptized. And soon they will assemble before this altar with a cross of birch to receive Christ\'s body and blood above, below and through the blessed bread and wine. That\'s why we sang that the "God of nature shares His nature." In the letter to the Hebrews we hear about "strangers and pilgrims on earth (who) desire a better country, a heavenly one." This period the whole camp is focusing on the themes of thanksgiving and pilgrimage. Today we thank God that we were able to make a small pilgrimage to this special place of worship, where we can more deeply experience the faith that has sustained the Latvian family thro ugh good days and bad – "dienās baltās, nebaltās" as part of Christ\'s all-embracing family. This is the Good news that Christ confesses – "the Cross has been crowned with Eternal Life, Life sublime." When we open our hearts and minds to Christ\'s words, we receive "Seeds of light" that with God\'s blessing "will blossom in their season". We thank God that God has blessed our work in this Valodas periods for 25 years and will bless it for many years to com. The ultimate harvest will be reaped when God\'s Kingdom comes and His will is done – kā debesīs, tā arī virs zemes – on Earth, as it is in Heaven. Āmen.']

Name: 119_eisenhower_united_nation_peace
Representative_Docs: ['Common need of Nations President Eisenhower declared that all nations defending freedom "are one in their common need and their common cause—and none can sanely seek security alone." The The President made this statement in an address to the National Junior Chamber of Commerce, an organisation of younger businessmen from all parts of the United States. "It is no wonder that our national security is so vast a matter," he said, "for the struggle in which freedom tod ay is engaged is quite literally a total and universal struggle, tl engages every aspect of our lives. It is waged in every arena in which a challenged civilisation must fight to live." President Eisenhower said that it is also an economic struggle, a political struggle, a scientific and an intellectual struggle. "It is a spiritual struggle," he continued, "for one of communism\'s basic assumptions about the nature of men is that they rae incapable of ruling themselves, incapable of attaining the spiritual standards and strength to solve national problems when these require voluntary personal sacrifice for the common good. "This is the communist\'s justification for regimentation. All this we deny. And we must seek in our churches, our schools, our homes and our daily lives the clearness of mind and strongness of heart to guard the chance to live in freedom. "For this whole struggle, in the deepest sense, is waged neither for land nor lor good nor for power— but for the soul of man himself." He referred to the so-called "fortress" theory of defence, whose advocates ask why the United States cannot stand alone and make its own decisions, regardless of other nations. The President gave a few answers to this question. "A total struggle — let us never forget — calls for a total defence," he said. "As there is no weapon too small — no arena too remote — to be ignored, so there is no free nation too humble to be forgotten. "All of us have elarned — first fro mthe onslaught of Nazi aggression, then from communist aggression — that all free nations must stand together, or they shall fall separately. . . "There is no such thing as partial unity. That is a contradiction of terms. "We cannot select those areas of the globe in which our policies or wishes may differ from our allies — build political fences around these areas — and say to our allies: VVc shall do what we want here — and where you do what we want, there and only there shall we favor unity. That is not unity. It is dictation. And it is not the way free men associate." The President took note of the "unhappy murmurings" about the United Nations, and the dismay caused by its "seemingly vain and tedious" political discussions. He pointed out that to the communist world the U.N. has seemed to be exploited in spreading disunity and confusion. To the Free World, he said, the U.N. has seemed it should be a constructive forum for free discussion of the world\'s problems, an effective agency for helping to solve those problems peacefully. "Perhaps," he said, "the greatest worth of the U.N. is previcely this: It holds up a mirror in which world can see its true self. Auti what shall we want to see in such a mirror but the whole truth — at such a time of total struggle?" toots two tunes at once. To its captive audience behind the Iron Curtain it plays a constant dirge of hate tor the Free World. To the Free World it often plays a siren-like tune that drips ot peace and goodwill. To understand such contradiction one must realise the critical internal problems in the Soviet Union. There are indications of a grim struggle for power among its new leaders—Premier Georgi Malenkov; Lavrenti Beria, chief of the secret police; Nikolai Bulganin, head of the Red army; Nikita Khushev, communist party secretary; and Viacheslav Molotov, minister of foreign affairs. All are striving for the support of a restive and oppressed people. "Pravda" says the Soviet Empire is being ruled by an "efficient collective party leadership," but history proves that anything but eventual one-man rule is incomprehensible in a dictatorship. It is evident that these men in the Kremlin have a purpose in maintaining the status quo—of following the Statin line of preaching hate at home and peace abroad. They do not want anyone rocking the boat — either from inside or out — until the scuffle in the wheelhouse is over. That was Lenin\'s line, too. He once said: "If it is possible to win time, to get even a short respite for organisational work, we must obtain it." Apparently the new Soviet corulers feel if they can stir up enough hate against the free nations they will be able to maintain unity at home while they iron out their leadership problems. Analysis of recent Red radio stations showss that Malenkov and company are playing the dissonant tunes of hate and peace as loudly as Stalin ever did. On May 29, Radio Budapest said the United Nations forces in Korea "again used grenades filled with poison gas. The grenades were shot from 155 m.m. cannons along the Sing-Sung sector." The false charge of using poison gas has been refuted many times, but here the communists .refute it themselves. tl is impossible to fire grenades from a cannon and there is no Sing-Sung sector in Korea. On June 1, the communist broadcasters for the East German Home Service launched an attack on Cchancellor Konrad Adenauer. The Free German leader, they siad, reminded them of President Syngman Rhee of the Fepublic of Korea. "What Rhee did in Korea," they said — "create an aggressive army and then launch an attackis what Adenauer hopes to achieve." The communists ginore, of course, two basic facts: that the Republic of Korea was invaded on June 25, 1950, by communist armies and that Adenauer hopes to build a denfence that will prevent a similar communist invasion of his country. On April 25, "Pravda" printed, for the outside world to read, a long and, for "Pravda," relatively gentle comment on President Eisenhower\'s April 16 speech, in which Mt. Eisenhower made a plea for "a true and total peace." Four days after the "Pravda" comment, the Russian clandestine radio station in Azerbajan province of northern Iran referred to "the demagogic speech of Eisenhower . . . who wants to cover up American imperialism and war activities." The Azerbajan station is a propaganda outlet for which the So, viet regime does not officially assume responsibility in order to be able ot use invective more freely.', 'Truman attlee disagree ON COMMUNIST CHINA! After five days of conferences in Washington, the following communique was issued by President Truman and Prime Minister Attlee, stressing the need of building up the defence forces of their respective countries. The troubled circumstances of the world, says the communique, leaves America and Britain no other choice but to devote themselves with all vigor to this immediate task. Mr. Truman and Mr. Attle< said: "We believe that Communis leaders of the Soviet Union an< China could if they chose modif; their conduct in such a way a to make these defence prepara tions unnecessary. "We shall do everything tha we can through whatever chan nels are open to us to impres: this view upon them and to seel a peaceful solution of existinf issues. "The objectives of Britain anc the United States in foreigr policy are the same—to maintair world peace and respect for th( rights and interests of all peoples to promote strength and confi dence among the freedom-loving countries of the world, to eliminate causes of fear, want anc discontent and to advance thf democratic way of life. "Unity of objectives of our twc countries underlay all discussions There is no difference between us as to the nature of the threat which our countries face, or the basic policies which must be pursued to overcome it. "We recognise that many of the problems which we have discussed :an be decided only through the procedures of the United Nations or the North Atlantic Treaty organisation. The peoples of the United States and Britain will act together with resolution and unity to meet the challenge to peace which recent weeks have madt ?lear to all." After stating that the situatior ;n Korea was one of "great gravity and far-reaching consequences" brought about by the intervention of large numbers ol Chinese Communist troops, the communique continued: "The United Nations forces have the advantage of superior air power and naval support, but on the ground they are confronted by heavy numerical superiority. "The United Nations forces were sent into Korea on the authority and at the recommendation of the United Nations. "The United Nations has not changed the mission which it has entrusted to them. The forces of our two countries will continue to discharge their responsibilities. "We were in complete agreement that • there can be no thought of appeasement or of rewarding aggression whether in the Far East or elsewhere. "Lasting peace and the future of the United Nations as an instrument for world peace depend upon strong support for resistance against aggression. "For our part we are ready, as we have always been, to seek an end to hostilities by means of negotiation. "The same principles of international conduct should be applied to this situation as are applied in accordance with our obligations under the Charter of the United Nations to any threat to world peace. "Every effort must be made to achieve the purposes of the United Nations in Korea by peaceful means and to find a solution of the Korean problem on the basis of a free and independent Korea. "We are confident that the great majority of the United Nations takes the same view. "If the Chinese on their side display any evidence of similar attitude we are hopeful that the cause of peace can be upheld. "If they do not, then it will be for the peoples of the world acting through the United Nations to decide how the principles of the charter can best be maintained. "For our part we declare in advance our firm resolve to uphold them. "We considered two questions regarding China which are already before the United Nations. "On the question of the Chinese seat in the United Nations the two Governments differ. Britain has recognised the Central People\'s Government and considers its representatives should occupy China\'s seat in the United Nations. "The United States has opposed and continues to oppose the seating of the Chinese Communist representatives in the United Nations. "We have discussed our difference of view on this point, and are determined to prevent it from interfering with our united effort in support of our common objectives. "On the question of Formosa we have noted that both Chinese claimants have insisted upon the validity of the Cairo declaration and have expressed reluctance to have the matter considered by the United Nations. "We agreed that the issues should be settled by peaceful means and in such a way as to safeguard the interests of the people of Formosa and the maintenance of peace and security in the Pacific and that consideration of this question by the United Nations will contribute to these ends. "The free nations of Asia have given strong support to the United Nations and have worked for world peace. "Communist aggression in Korea increases the danger to the security and independence of these nations. "We reaffirm our intention to continue to help them. "Pressure of Communist expansion existed in Europe and elsewhere long before the aggression against Korea and measures were taken to meet it. forces of collective security has already been recognised. Action for this purpose is under way Clearly decisions regarding the Far East have their repercussions and effects elsewhere. "In considering the necessities of the Far Eastern situation we have kept in mind the urgency of building up the strength of the whole free world. "We are in complete agreement on the need for immediate action by all North Atlantic treaty countries to intensify their efforts to build up their defences and to strengthen the Atlantic community. "In this critical period, it is a source of satisfaction to us that the views of our Governments on basic problems are so similar. "We believe that this identity of aims will enable our Governments to carry out their determination to work together tc strengthen the unity which has already been achieved among tht free nations, and to defend thoļjjļi vaules which are of fundamenta! importance to the people w< represent."', 'Eisenhower\'s Inauguration Speech Here in full is the Inaugural speech made to the people of America and the world by President Eisenhower on January 21, 1953. "My fellow citizens, the world and we have passed the midway point of a century of continuous challenge. We sense with all our faculties that forces of good and evil are massed and armed and opposed .as rarely before in history. This fact defines the meaning of this day. We are summoned by this honored and historic ceremony to witness more than the act of one citizen swearing his oath of service in the presence of his God. We are called asa people to give testimony in the sight of the world to our faith that the future shall belong to the free. Since the century\'s beginning a time of tempest has seemed to come upon the continents of the earth. Masses of Asia have wakened to strike off shackles of the past. Great nations of Europe have waged their bloodiest wars. Thrones have toppled and their vast empires have disappeared. New nations have been born. For our own country it has been a time of recurring trial. We have grown in power and in responsibility. We have passed through the Anxieties of depression and of war to a summit unmatched in man\'s history. Seeking to secure peace in the world we have had to fight through the forests of the Argonne to the shores of Iwo Jima and to the mountain peaks of Korea. In the swift rush of great events we find ourselves groping to nkow the full sense and meaning of the times in which we live. In our quest of understanding we beseech God\'s guidance. As we summon all our knowledge of the past and we scan all signs of the future, we bring all out wit and will to meet the question how far have we gone in man\'s long pilgrimage from darkness toward light Ar* we nearing the light, a day of freedom and of peace for all mankind or are the shadows of another night closing in upon us. Great as are the preoccupations absorbing us at home, concerned as we are with matters that deeply affect our livelihood today and our vision of the future, each ol these domestic problems is developed by and often created by this question that Involves all human kind. This trial comes at a moment when man\'s power to achieve good or to influence evil surpasses the brightest hopes and the sharpest fears of all ages. We can turn rivers in their courses, level mountains to the plains. Ocean and land and sky are avenues for our colossal commerce. Disease diminishes and life lengthens. Yet the promise of this life is imperilled by the very genius that has made it possible Nations amass wealth. Labor sweats to create and turns out devices to level not only Mo>intains but also cities. Science seems ready to comer upon us as its final gift the power to ♦rase human life from the earth. At such a time in history we, who are free, must proclaim anew our faith. This faith is the abiding creed of your fathers. It is our faith »n the deathless dignity of man governed by eternal, morail and natural laws. This faith defines our full view of life. It establishes beyond debate those gifts of the Creator that are man\'s inalienable rights and that make aU men equal in his sight. In the light of this equality we know that the virtues most cherished by free people — love i of truth, pride of work, devotion I to country — all are treasures equally precious in the lives of the most humble and of the most exalted. The men who mine coal, and fire furnaces, and balance ledgers, and turn lathes, and pick cotton, and heal the sick, and plant corn all serve as proudly and as profitably for America as the statesmen who draft treaties, or the legislators who enact laws. This faith rules our whole way of life. It decrees that we, the people, elect leaders not to rule but to serve. It asserts that we have the right to choice of our own work and to the reward of our own toil. It inspires the initiative that makes our productivity the wonder of the world, and it warns that any man who seeks to deny equality in all his brothers betrays the spirit of the free and invites the mockery of the tyrant. The enemies of this faith know no God but force, no devotion but its use. They tutor men in treason. They feed upon the hunger of others. Whatever defies them they torture, especially the truth. Here, then, is joined no pallid argument between slightly differphilosophies. This conflict strikes directly at the faith of our fathers and the lives of our sons. No principle or treasure that we hold, from the spiritual knowledge of our free schools and churches- to the creative magic of free labor and capital, nothing lies safely beyond the reach of the struggle. Freedom is pitted against slavery — light against dark, the faith we hold belongs not to us alone but to the free of all the world. This common bond binds the grower of rice in Burma and the planter of wheat in lowa, the shepherd in southern Italy and the mountaineer in the Andes. It confers a common dignity upon the French soldier who dies in Indochina, the British soldier killed in Malaya, the American life given in Korea, We know, beyond this, that we are linked to all free peoples not merely by a noble idea but by a simple need. No free people can for long cling to any privilege or enjoy any safety in economic solitude. For all our own material might, even we need markets in the world for the surpluses of our farms and of our factories. Equally, we need for these same farms and factories vital materials and products of distant lands. This basic law of interdependence, so manifect in the commerce of peace, applies with thousand-fold intensity in the event of war. So are we persuaded by necessity and by belief that the strength of all free peoples lies in unity, their danger in discord, to produce this unity, to meet the challenge of our time, destiny has laid upon our country the responsibility of the Free World\'s leadership. So it is proper that we assure our friends once again that, in the discharge of this responsibility, we Americans know and observe the difference between world leadership and imperialism — between firmness and truculence — between a thoughtfully calculated goal and spasmodic reaction to the stimulus of emergencies. We wish our friends the world over to know this above all: We feel this moral strength because we know that we are not helpless prisoners of history- We are freemen. We shall remain free, never to be proven guilty of the one capital offense against freedom, a lack of staunch faith. In pleading our just cause before the bar of history and in pressing our labor for world peace, we shall be guided by certain fixed principles. m These principles are: Abhorring war as a chosen way to balk the purposes of those who threaten us, we hold it to be the first task of statesmanship to develop the strength that will deter the forces of aggression and promote the conditions of peace. For, as it must be the supreme purpose of all free man, so it must be the dedication of their leaders, to save humanity from preying upon itself. In the light of this principle, we stand ready to engage with any and all others in joint effort to remove the causes of mutual fear and distrust among nations, and so to make possible drastic reduction of armaments. The sole requisites for undertaking such effort * are that, in their purpose, they be aimed logically and honestly toward secure peace for all and that, in their result, they provide methods by which every participating nation will prove good faith in carrying out its pledge. Realizing that common sense and common decency alike dictate the futility of appeasement, we shall never try to placate an aggressor by the false and wicked bargain of trading honor for security. For in the final choice a soldier\'s pack is not so heavy i burden as a prisoner\'s chains. Knowing that only a United States that is strong and immensely productive can help defend freedom in our world, we view our nation\'s strength and security as a trust upon which rests the hope of free men crywhere. It is the firm duty of each of our free citizens and of every free citizen everywhere to place the cause of his country before the comfort of himself. Honoring the identity and heritage of each nation of the world, we shall never use our strength to try to impress upon another people our own cherished political and economic institutions. Assessing realistically the needs and capacities of proven friends of freedom, we shall strive to help them to achieve their own security and well-being. Likewise, we shall count upon them to assume, within the limits of their resources, their full and just burdens in the common defense of freedom. Recognizing economic health as an indispensable basis of military strength and the Free World\'s peace, we shall strive to foster everywhere, and to practice policies that encourage productivity and profitable trade. For the impoverishment of any single people in the world means danger to Ihe well-being of all other peoples. Appreciating that economic need, military security and political wisdom combine to suggest \' regional groupings of peoples, we I hope, within the framework of the United Nations to help strengthen such special bonds the world over. The nature of these ties must vary with the different problems of different areas in the Western hemisphere. We join with all our neighbors in the work of perfecting a community of fraternal trust and common purpose in Europe. We ask that enlightened and inspired leaders of the Western nations strive with renewed vigor to make the unity of their peoples a reality. Only when a free Europe unitedly marshals its strength can it effectively safeguard, even with our help, its spiritual and cultural treasures. Conceiving the defense of freedom, like freedom itself, to be one and indivisible we hold all continents and peoples in equal regard and honor. We reject any insinuation that one race or another, one people or another is in any sense inferior or expendable. Respecting the United Nations as the living sign of all peoples\' hope for peace, we shall strive to make it not merely an eloquent symbol but an effective force. And in our quest of honorable peace we shall neither compromise, nor tire, nor ever cease. By these rules of conduct we hope to be known to all peoples. By their observations, an earth of peace may become not a vision but a fact. This hope—this supreme aspiration—must rule the way we live, jWe must be ready to dare all for ; our country, for history does not long entrust the care of fjreedom to the weak or the timid. We must acquire proficiency in defense and display stamina in purpose. We must be willing, individually and as a nation, to accept whatever sacrifices may be required of us. A people that values its privileges above its principles soon loses both. These basic precepts are not lofty abstractions far removed from matters of daily living. They are laws of spiritual strength that generate and find our material strength. Patriotism means equipped forces and a prepared citizenry. Moral stamina means more energy and more productivity on the farm and in the fifJlor*. Love of liberty means the guarding of every resource that makes freedom possible — from the unity of our families and the wealth of our soil to the genius of our scientists. So each citizen plays an indispensable role. The productivity ot our heads, our hands and our hearts is the source of all the strength we can command for both the enrichment of our lives and the winning of peace. No person, no home, no community can be beyond the reach of this call. We are summoned to act in wisdom and in conscience, to work with industry, to teach with persuasion, to preach with conviction, to weigh our every deed with care and with compassion. For this truth must be clear before us. Whatever America hopes to bring to pass in the world must first come to pass in the heart of America. The peace we seek— this is nothing less than the practice and the fulfillment of our whole faith, among ourselves and in our dealings with others. It signifies more than stilling the guns, erasing the sorrow of war, more than an escape from death. "It is a way of life. More than a  for the weary, it is a hope lor the brave. This is the hope that beckons us onward in this century of trial. This is the work that awaits us all. To be done with bravery, with charity—and with prayer to Almighty God.']

That same nighL latvia vvas being shut dovvn by Germanv They vvere sorely missed. Anyone m Canada or the United States vvho follovvs hockey novv knovvs about the Lavian hockey team. In difierent areas, the same fans vvould have sailed on marauding kurši Viking ships, battled against Gennan crusaders, marched m Czarist armies, fought for Latvian independence or battled on the Eastern front Today it\'s hockey. I one for one say vve skip the next Song Festival and instead head over to the VVorld Hockey Cahmpionships and go crazy. Let(t)\'s go! This opinion was available on Latvians Online Opinions on 2/28/02 Viesturs Zariņš vvas born m England and lives m Canada vvith his family near Toronto. He is a former president of the Latvian National Federation m Canada. He also is chair of the Toronto Latvian School "Valodiņa". Professionally he is a director of information technology m a large finacial insritution. Zariņš has been a hockey fan since grovving up m the east end of Montreal and has follovved Latvian hockey since 1996.', 'With just 2 million citizens and just over 1000 hockey players, Latvia is thrilled every time one of its native sons cracks the big time And defenseman Karlis Skrastins of the Colorado Avalanche is one of the best contemporary examples. Of course, there are others. Helmut Balderis no longer had the speed that earned him the nickname of "Electric Train" by the time he played 26 games with the Minnesota North Stars in 1989-90, but the iconoclastic right wing from Riga proved he was still a proud competitor. More recently, goaltender Arturs Irbe and forward Sergei Zholtok prospered versus NHL competition, although Irbe now earns his living in the Austrian League and Zholtok tragically died while playing for Riga 2000 in November 2004. But Skrastins still soldiers on in the NHL today. The hard-hitting 6-1, 196-pound veteran is Skrastins embodies Latvian hockey pride currently the league\'s reigning ironman, with more than 400 consecutive games played. He also captained Latvia\'s 2005 HHP World Championship entry. The former Nashville Predators draft pick realizes that while his professional involvement is a source of pride to his fellow countrymen, what truly electrifies Latvia is the prospect of success at the top level of international competition in the 2006 Winter Games. "We are proud to be in the Olympics," said Skrastins. "It\'s a big, big deal for a small country like Latvia. It\'s going to be quite an experience. We\'re going to have a lot of new guys, and we\'ll be playing against big teams like the USA and Russia, with all NHL guys. It\'s a good thing for our players and fans. Everyone\'s looking forward to seeing how we do against those big guys." Hockey fans around the world remember the shocking upset Belarus achieved versus Sweden in the 2002 Olympic quarterfinals. But not everyone is aware that Latvia pulled off a similar feat in a must-win situation on home ice in Riga last year. On February 13,2005, Skrastins and his Latvian teammates rallied from a 4-2 deficit in Olympic qualification play to triumph 5-4 over (you guessed it) Belarus. The result secured Latvia\'s place in Torino 2006. "It was like a miracle," Skrastins said with a smile. "There was about four minutes left until the end of the game, and we almost all had our heads down. Then we scored a goal. It was a good thing we were playing at home, because the fans started getting louder and louder. Then we scored another goal and another, and everything had changed. Of course, we were very lucky at the time. But we tried to do our best, and we were happy to get into the Olympics. Latvia defeated the mighty Russians at the 2000 and 2003 11HF World Championships. Could similar upsets be in the oiling for the small Baltic nation at the 2006 Olympics? "It would be nice if we would beat some big teams," Skrastins admitted. "It\'s not our goal, but we\'re going to take it one game at a time. It\'s hockey. You never know what is going to happen. Maybe we can beat some big guys and get into the quarterfinals." Regardless of the final Olympic results, 2006 will be remembered as a banner year for Latvian hockey. The country\'s U-20 team participated in its first-ever lIHF World Junior Championship last month in the Canadian province of British Columbia, and despite finishing ninth out of 10 teams, players like goalie Ugis Avotins and captain Martin Karsums became fan favorites. In May, Riga will proudly host the 2006 lIHF World Championship, showcasing a new downtown arena with a capacity of 12,500. "I think people are going to like it," said Skrastins. "We have a very nice city in Riga. The Old Town in Riga is beautiful, full of culture. Everybody\'s welcome. We\'ve got crazy fans, but in a good way. Latvia will do the best it can do for our visitors." Lucas Aykroyd IIHF.com Olympic writer', 'With just 2 million citizens and just over 1000 hockey players, Latvia is thrilled every time one of its native sons cracks the bie time. Name: 121_zrp_zatlers_party_coalition
Representative_Docs:
["Harmony Center Not Offended by Decision to Form Coalition with National Alliance

Latvia’s leftist pro-Russia Harmony Center does not feel offended about the decision ofthe centrist Zatlers Reform Party (ZRP) to invite the National Alliance to the parliament, not the Harmony Center who won the snap parliament elections, said Harmony Center representative Andrejs Klementjevs, the Baltie News Service reported.

“VVe do not feel offended, but we stili believe that it vvould have been best to form coalition of ZRP, the center-right Unity bloc, and the Harmony Center,” Klementjevs was quoted as saying, commenting on the agreement reached by ZRP, Unity, and the National Alliance on Monday on the formation of a reform and rule-of-law coalition.

According to BNS, Klementjevs said that the Harmony Center will continue vvorking in the opposition and will follow the government's work, fighting against adoption of controversial issues.

Klementjevs said that the new govemment will have a strong opposition with 44 votes on the 100-seat parliament because the Greens and Farmers Union (ZZS) has also been left in the opposition. According to BNS, ZRP and the Unity on Monday finally made a decision on the vvould-be government coalition with the National Alliance after three-week long discussions follovving the snap parliament elections held in Latvia on September 17, and the coalition would have 56 votes in the 100-seat parliament BNS also reported that on October 1, ZRP made a decision to invite the Harmony Centerto the govemment coalition, but Unity insisted that the coalition should be formed with the National Alliance.", 'Zatlers makes his political mark, but not without controversy Uldis Ozoliņš – August 12, 2011 On July 23, by a 94 percent majority, Latvian voters as expected supported the referendum instigated by ex-President Valdis Zatlers to dismiss the Saeima. They will return to the polls on Sept. 17 to elect a new parliament. This ended a truly unprecedented series of political events, and signalled the way for at least one new political force to take the stage in Latvian politics—Zatlers himself and his now many supporters. On the same day as the referendum, Zatlers officially founded his party. Curiously, by many commentators' estimation, he put his own persona in the party title: the Zatlers Reform Party (Zatlera Reformu partija, or ZRP). Such a person-driven approach seeks to capitalise on his popularity in dismissing the Saeima, which has rocketed Zatlers' personal approval to 79 percent—an unusual rating for any Latvian politician. As the referendum dust settles and Zatlers' political course becomes clearer, two radically different interpretations are put on his motivation to dismiss the Saeima. The first takes Zatlers at his word that he wanted basically to rid the Saeima of the influence of oligarchs who control several political parties and who had instigated a series of votes against the national interest, or who had in other ways resisted necessary policy implementation. The trigger for Zatlers' move was the Saeima's decision not to allow the Corruption Prevention and Combating Bureau (Korupcijas novēršanas un apkarošanas birojs) to search the home of "bulldozer" MP Ainārs Šlesers. The concern of Zatlers had been to restore a measure of democracy to the working of the Saeima. The leading party, Unity (Vienotība), sharing such sentiments, in fact then invited Zatlers to join it, but he declined in favour of founding his own. The second interpretation is far less favourable. When he triggered the referendum on May 28 by recommending the Saeima dismissal, Zatlers was faced with the likelihood that he would not be elected by the parliament to a second term. As it turned out, the Saeima appointed the financier Andris Bērziņš to the presidency. The dismissal of the Saeima thus is viewed more as an act of revenge. And Zatlers further declined to join Unity as it had stood staunchly against any idea of a coalition with the second-largest party in the Saeima, the Moscow-leaning Harmony Centre (Saskaņas centrs). Zatlers was looking for his own political advantage, being willing to go into coalition with anyone to secure his own power. Debate over Zatlers' motives will continue, but meanwhile his popularity with the electorate has not diminished. In the first party ratings since its founding, ZRP ranked first with Harmony Centre, both favoured by 17.5 perent of the electorate. It seems that Zatlers had taken voters away from all parties, even marginally from Harmony Centre. Unity—the largest party in the dismissed Saeima—slumped to 8.7 percent, while the pivotal Union of Greens and Farmers (Zaļo un zemnieku savienību) declined to 8.1 percent. The Union of Greens and Farmers is very much associated with oligarch and Ventspils Mayor Aivars Lembergs. Also in decline, though less sharply, was the National Alliance (Nacionālā apvienība), claiming 6.3 percent support. No other party came near to the 5 percent barrier required to sit in the Saeima. The great loser appears to be the oligarch Šlesers. His First Party of Latvia/Latvian Way (Latvijas Pirmā partija/ Latvijas Ceļš) had managed to get into the Saeima on previous occasions after blitz advertising campaigns, but support for the party has dropped to just 1.7 percent. On these figures, any kind of coalition is a possibility, either including or excluding Harmony Centre. Yet with more than a month to the elections, we may expect volatility as much as stability in predictions, as the heat is turned on Zatlers and his relative lack of political experience, and the very mixed bag of supporters who have flocked to his flag. An uncomfortable feeling comes precisely from the populist nature of his campaign so far, and the relative lack of substance in policy terms he has announced. While we are used to party platforms in Latvia often being very similar—so voters' decisions revolve around personalities—the search for power seems to override careful policy considerations, making ZRP perhaps just one more party devised for an individual leader, rather than being a genuine party representing stable interests. However, a further sense of discomfort comes from Zatlers' own background. Four years ago, he was an apolitical surgeon who was installed as president by the same oligarchs he now berates. Also in the background is his self-admitted taking of "envelopes" (bribes) as a surgeon, as is the common practice in most post-Soviet countries. This makes his claims to be against oligarchy or corruption ring somewhat hollow. Further, Zatlers' battle cry to get rid of oligarchs has taken on a somewhat bizarre life of its own, with a number of popular events including a "festival for buried oligarchs" (Oligarhu kapu svētki) arousing hilarity. Yet the real problems facing Latvia are not restricted to the activities of oligarchs, however odious. The painful road back to economic health in Latvia is hurting many. The country is experiencing depopulation as workers head abroad, and many small and medium enterprises are in trouble. The desire to repay loans to the International Monetary Fund and other lenders has led the government to slash wages and increase taxes. Latvia now enjoys a two-track economy. One part, largely concerned with transit and finance, is experiencing a boom, but the rest of the economy depressed. Yet the parties struggle to devise any policies to address these issues. Unity's insistence on repaying the debts, maintaining the value of the lat and tightening belts is being increasingly criticised as undermining any growth potential. And corruption is still widespread in administration, while the shadow (non-tax paying) economy remains strong. Not to be outdone, Harmony Centre's potential prime minister candidate, Nils Usakovs, has proposed a radical suggestion: Political parties should put aside all issues to do with nationality and ethnicity—citizenship, official state language, language in schools, etc.—for a period of three years so that they can concentrate on solving the nation's serious economic issues. Directed clearly at nationalist attempts to mobilise around issues such as having Latvian be the only language of instruction in schools, Ušakovs' approach may yet strike a chord among voters, though it is unlikely to be accepted by other parties, whose own economic credentials are often weak and for which national issues may sometimes be a useful pretext to mobilise support. Voters will be faced with more choice in these elections, but the substance of this choice will still be an open question as this intriguing battle intensifies.', "President invites Dombrovskis to form Latvia’s next government

Andris Straumanis

-Oct. 19, 2011

Latvian President Andris Bērziņš has nominated current Prime Minister Valdis Dombrovskis to form the country’s next government, but he also suggested that ali five poNtical parties elected to the Saeima be included the coalition.

Bērziņš made the invitation on Oct. 19 after meeting with representatives of ali parties, according to an announcementfrom the president’s press Office. The president’s invitation follovvs weeks of unsuccessful negotiations between the parties. The Zatlers Reform Party (Zatlera Reformu partija, orZRP), Unity (Vienotība) and the National Alliance (Nacionālā apvienība “Visu Latvijai!” - “Tēvzemei un Brīvībai/LNNK”) announced Oct. 14 that they had come to terms on a three-party coalition that would be backed by 56 of the parliament’s 100 members. But two days later six ZRP MPs broke away from the party, throvving the solidity of the coalition in doubt.

Efforts to include the other two parties have run into objections. When the reformist ZRP at first tried to create a government w 'th just the center-left Harmony Centre (Saskaņas Centrs), many voters protested that Harmony Centre’s pro-Russian štance cast doubt on its loyalty to Latvia. Efforts at broadening the coalition to include the centrist Unity and the right-wing National Alliance ran into trouble, too. The National Alliance has said it would not work with

Harmony Centre. Meanvvhile, ZRP leader and ex-president Valdis Zatlers has said his party would not work with the conservative Union of Greens and Farmers (Zaļo un Zemnieku savienība), which is seen as controlled by Latvia’s oligarchs. Even a last-minute effort to strengthen the three-party coalition by inviting Jānis Duklavs of the Greens and Farmers to take the minister of agriculture’s portfolio in the new government—vvhich would have earned the backing of his party’s 13 seats in the Saeima—was dropped after ZRP objected.

While Klāvs Olšteins and the other five MPs who split from Zatlers’ party have said they would backthe Oct. 14 coalition modei, the president has expressed his doubts.

Nontheless, Bērziņš said in his Oct. 19 announcement, the three-party coalition appears to be the only one that realistically could form the new government. At the same time, the president’s invitation to Dombrovskis does not prevent the parties from vvorking on an improved— and broader—modei, according to the press Office announcement. “The first serious tēst of the would-be coalition’s ability to take action vvill be the vote on the State budget for 2012,” the president said, “vvhich cannot become an experiment with the State and threaten the state’s intemational agreements.”

Once a coalition is assembled, Dombrovskis vvill have to present it to the Saeima for confirmation.

The new Saeima, elected Sept. 17, began work Oct. 17. Zatlers, who campaigned on ridding the parliament of the influence of oligarchs and on reforming govemment and politics, suffered his first defeat, losing in his bid to become speaker ofthe Saeima. Unity’s Solvita Āboltiņa, who served as speaker in the previous Saeima, was elected instead."]

Name: 122_election_candidate_supreme_voting
Representative_Docs:
['ELECTIONS TO NEW SOVIET CONGRESS

Easter Day saw the first contested elections in the Soviet Union in 70 years.

The Soviet voting age is 18, and about 190 million of the 280 million citizens were eligible to vote.

Voting was not mandatory and ballots were secret.

It is estimated that in Latvia the voting tumout was approximately 87%.

The elections were for the new Soviet Congress of People’s Deputies. It is to be noted that this body does not replace the Supreme Soviets. The new body will meet a number of times during the year, instead of the one time a year get-to gether of the Supreme Soviets.

The new Congress consists of 2250 seats, 750 of which are reserved for Soviet Union wide official “social organi sations”. The various Popular Fronts, which exist in only one of the occupied Republics, were not eligible to nominate candidates in this group.

The organisations which were able to nominate candidates include the Com munist Party (100 seats), the trade un ions (100 seats), Komsomol, the Com-

munist youth league (75 seats), the So viet Women’s Committee (75 seats), the consumer co-operative societies (40 seats), war and labour veterāns (75 seats), the various cultural unions writers, film-makers, artists etc. (75 seats), the Academy of Sciences (20 seats) and so on ... right down to bodies like the All-Union Conference of Rshing Farms (2 seats).

Latvian branches of these organisa tions were able to nominate candidates proportional to their numbers in these organisations. Elections to this group were held prior to Easter Sunday. How ever, it is not known at this stage who was elected.

The remaining 1500 deputies were directly elected from the same number of constituencies. These consist of 750 “territorial” constituencies, much like in Australia, with an average of 200,000 to 250,000 voters in each, and 750 “na tional territorial” seats, aimed at guaran teeing adequate representation of indi vidual republics and autonomous re ģions.

In the first category, occupied Latvia

was allocated 8 constituencies. In the second category, each of the 15 union republics has 32 constituencies. Irre spective of its size each republic has the same number of seats. Thus a “national territorial” constituency in the Russian Federation (population 145 million) isfar bigger than one in, say, Lithuania (popu lation 3.8 million). That is why Mr Boris Yeltsin’s “national territorial seat No 1” in the Russian Federation, covering ali of Moscow, is so big. The system also means that every Citizen casts two votes: one for a “territorial” and oe for a “na tional territorial” seat. To be elected, candidates bad to obtain 51% of the EUGIBLE vote.

The number of candidates in each of the forty Latvian constituencies is as follovvs:

8 constituencies had 1 candidate 23 constituencies had 2 candidates 5 constituencies had 3 candidates 1 constituency had 4 candidates 1 constituency had 5 candidates 1 constituency had 6 candidates 1 constituency had 11 candidates

Only 29 deputies were elected in the first instance, because in the other 11 constituencies no candidate attained the required 51% of the EUGIBLE vote. In 5 consitutencies, where there were more than 2 candidates, voting will take place to elect one of the top two candidates of the first ballot. However, in the 6 con-

stituencies where there were only two candidates, new elections will take place.

We in the West have political profilēs on only a small number of the deputies. Therefore it is not possible to make a value judgement as to their collective štance. The view of Eduards Berklavs, a one time high communist Latvian offi cial, who was banished in 1959 and who now is the de facto leader of the Latvian National Independence Movement, is that 24 of the elected representatives represent “democratic forces”. The Lat vian Popular Front claims to have sup ported 25 of the elected.

The Latvian electorate has not tumed against the top Communist Party offi cials as has happened elsewhere in the Soviet Union. For instance, Jānis Vagris, the First Secretary of the Latvian Com munist Party, was elected in preference to Juris Dobelis, the leader of the 8000 member Latvian National Independence Movement. The electorate did, however, reject a number of local Communist Party chairmen in the provinces in favour of more practical people like the chair men of collective farms.

These elections created great interest in the West as well as in the Soviet Union.
