Thus, de facto: the communist party al sponsors the Onlyl for affect, they are "chosen" by tne workers at some work place where the acceptance of the prescribed nomination list is fully assured, where no "mischance" can occur, and when, nevertheless, an "accident" did occur, as for instance in Latvia in 1941 (in the provincial capital of Jekabpils) where the workers elected their own candidate, after a little flurry in the circles of the Supreme Council, and after all local party functionaries had vanished without a trace, the work place in question was notified that the candidate elected was suspected of a crime and that the candidature is therefore annulled. The next candidate is then merely entered in the i.r. without referring once again to the workers. Then setting up the Central Election Commission,as well as the local election commissions, the rural districts receive a circural stating that the representatives ofthe local communist party are to be summoned, or "the members of commission shall comprise representatives of the "poor farmers" and the workers who are loyal to the Soviet regime". When in this "assured freedom'*the nomination list is ready and the Election Commission is established, there dollows the most important institution of the Soviet "election": the Voters' Register. In the free state of Latvia no such register existed. The people voted on the basis of their personal legitimation or passports, i.e. they had only to identify their person and the registration of their vote was stamped on this legitimation to show that the person in question had already voted). The Voters/Register was drawn up by the local councils, i.e. from 100% communist agencies, and this Register is the magic wand with the help of which the famous 9878% electoral participation is attained because in the column of the register following the name of the voter the fateful "Yes" must be entered, that is to say the citizen has voted (for the only nomination list). But it can happen that the voters destroy the voting papers, or merely throw bits of paper into the ballot boxes. In the booths each voter is carefully watched and only few risk loitering in order "to do something" with the voting papers. But there are remedies even for such eventualities. What happens on the election day in the Central Election Bureau? It is a strict ruling that all local election commissions shall report to the Central Election Bureau every Threehours, day and night, how many votes have been filed and what, percentage it represents of the total Voters' Register. This is rigidly insisted upon. If any local agencies neglect to do so they are immediately requested by telephone to submit the report. On the last election day, Sunday, when it was already 3 p.m. and only six hours remained before the election closed it transpired that the electoral participation was very low, in fact only 53%. Strict orders were given that automobiles, wagons, volunteers, were to be mobilized in order to bring the missing voters to the polls. Telephones rang incessantly. But even this campaign only resulted in a slight increase., At the telephone sat the president of the Central Election Commission himself, and rang to all the local commissions, swearing at them in Russian, with the coarsest and most vulgar oaths,in particular at the heads of the commissions,whom h( censured and threatened. This swearing took such proportions that the women clerks sat blushing and frightened. Again the percentage rose, but still insufficient-the count rose to 73 – 78%. Naturally the election authorities were extremely "liberal" in approving spoilt voting erasures, added remarks, etc. and they paternally excuses these blemishes and passed the voting papers as valid, Even if something very biting was written the election authorities only commented that the voter wanted to give expression to his enthusiam (!). Not bad! and approved the papers. Still not 98.8%! Then followed the decisive decree (13.1.1941 No.lll/4): "Referring to the telephonic ruling of the Presidenty of the Supreme Council I herewith order that the imme" diately be"checked and the removal therefrom of all invalids and absentees,as also all those who have deliberately refrained from voting (!?). After which the correct number of voters shall be determined according to the Register". After the enactment of this decree naturally only those who had voted remained on the register. (These removals had such consequences that, for instance, in the rural district of 201 voters were removed from 599.1n the district Al§vanga 426 from 2175, etc. That is a very high percentage). What would have been the proportion of electoral participation without these removals? So in the election of 1941 the participation percentage was forced up to 94 – 95. Thus closed the election. But it had to be 98.8% since the Party Secretary Kalnberzins demanded it in his address at the Ninth Congress of the Latvian Communist Party (because some other ''republic" had already reached over 98!). What now? After the conclusion of the election a quick sitting of the Central Election Commission took place,fol..owing which two members of the central committee of the communist visited the technical election statisticians and stated plainly and clearly: "Now take out your calculating machines and calculate what the number of voters in *Ehe register must be so that the percentage of participation equals 98.5". The statisticians calculated quickly. And the election result was recorded with 98.49% participation. Precisely so was the election result in July 1940 calculated. This election too was carried out at "Stachanov speed" because the world was to be confronted with "the voice of the people" who unanimously ballotted for the – communist candidates. When it was found that the best statisticians couldnot physically manage to have everything ready at this speed the communist head of the election proceedings thumped his fist on the table and shouted in Russian "This is sabotage! You must work!" and the leader of the Riga Statistical Bureau had a member of the Checka at his heels for several days because he was suspected of sabotage. An old-established official of the bureau, a nlump gentleman, during the whole election work (for the super-democratic election) wiped cold sweat from his brow and sighed "Oh, Ch, What will happen now! What will happen now!" Because these were statistics in the "new style" that he had not yet accepted. When the election was dc facto closed the expert statisticians were no longer allowed to participate in the counting and recounting of the final number of votes which was recorded and published. The men from the communist party managed alone. Such was the procedure of the "freest election", which was really nothing more than an enforced plebiscite for the candidates introduced by the invaders." There was no possibility of checking or controlling this "election". Even the election posters could only be obtained from the Party, and were by no means selected at will since it was decreed that they must only be issued in numeral order, and with no right to alter them. Naturally, the day after the election the communist press jubilated: "98.49% of voters had ballotted for the Stalin bloc of communists and impartial candidates. Thisis a gigantic victory for the Soviet power and the communist party..." (Central Newspaper of the Communist Party "Cina", 18. 1. 1941). Yes, the whole masquerade was performed merely in order to circulate this contention over the whole world, whereby the most elementary rights of mankind were violated. Representative_Docs: ['LETErS FROM BEHIND THE IRON CURTAIN (Continued from previous issue) The Soviet regime has also an evil and depressing influence on the younger generation, especially on school children who were brought up in a national spirit and under the influence of the Western culture. Now the maxim preached in Soviet schools is "ex orientē lux"! All culture allegedly comes from the Soviet Union. An attempt is made to convince the youth that the educational achievements hitherto made are nil, that the Baltic States have remained far behind in cultural achievements, that the wars of liberty have been wars of crime, never of patriotism. Such instruction publicly turning lies into truth gives rise to indescribable sufferings for the youth of these countries. It is hard for the teachers to speak against their own consciences. For example, a quotation from a letter received in May 1948, written toa friend by a secondary school girl: "... Next year I shall attend school no more. We are finding things very hard, for daddy is ill and I must help mother in the house. My heart aches for it, but at school it was not easy either. We were so far behind that it was difficult for me to follow all that was taught there. How ignorant have we been! We even had not known haw many illiterates there were among us. Who is responsible for it? Our former teachers, of oourse. Some of them are aware of their faults and regret it publicly at school. But others are obstinate and refuse to recognize their error. You surely remember the joung B. who taught us history and played violin so beautifully. He spoke indecently in the classroom and after that went to the forest and committed suicide. What else can you expect from such a man? These lines disclose sarcasm as well as disappointment, and reflect the ruined souls and sufferings of the youth. *** BALTIC EMPLOYMENT SERVICE Pty.Ltd., 20, Burton St., City, (near Oxford St.), Sydney, atvērta no 10-16, sestdienls no 9-12. Pastāvīgi brīvas vie as ar labu atalgojumu fabrikās, daž. specialitātēs un vienk. darbos; gadījuma darbi sestdienās un svētdienās; vietas mājsaimniecībā siev. ar bērniem un vieniniecēm. Labiem angļu valodas pra – tējiem kantoristu, pārdevēju v. c. vietas. Informācija un starpniecība namu būvē un pirkšanā – arī ārpus N.S.W. dzīvojošiem. Apgādā istabas un dzīvokļus. Being conscious of their misery, those remaining in the native country attempt to save those who have escaped the grip of the Soviet regime. Therefore in almost every letter you can read between the lines hints like: "Do not come," "Do not return!", "Remain outside the Iron Curtain and endure!" It is a clear warning. And,therefore, in spite of all the promises of the repatriation officers and the reports of official propaganda the number of those returning has been exceedingly small among the Baltic peoples. As a good and expressive example I want to quote fully a letter of a young Latvian to his father, a former teacher, who, with his family – wife, four daughters and brother – in law Imants lives in a D.P. camp in Germany. The letter was written in autumn 1947. The author was conscripted during the time of German occupation. He was wounded and taken prisoner by the Russians in August 1944. We read in the letter: "My dear father, my dear mother! I take advantage of my leave to visit my motherland and now I want to tell you how I am getting on and what you should do. Now I am doing compulsory military service and I am getting on well. I have recovered though the recovery lasted a long time. After we parted I lived one year on mother\'s own farm where I had to work hard. Also for you, on your return, there will be the best place, for there is plenty to work for all. Particularly, Imants, whom we are eager to see, will be able to work "by the sweat of his brow". I am glad you are all together. Stay together in future. I think that uncle James also is longing for you. You will have a good life with him." Every reader of the letter will appreciate the contents of it knowing that the mother of the author of the letter is daughter of an Orthodox priest massacred by the communists. She was born in Siberia and spent her youth there. Imants is a former Latvian soldier who fought against the Soviet Union. Uncle James is brother of the author\'s father. He lives in U.S.A. This is the tale told by letters arriving from the native country. It ie evident the reader understands the real meaning only if he is informed about the life and home conditions both of the sender and the recipient of the letter.And these letters are an everlast – ing accusation against the terror and brutality of the bolshevik regime exercised in the Baltic States. Tens and hundreds of such letters can be found in any D.P. camp where there are Baltic DP\'s. They all warn those who still enjoy freedom against returning home. The dying warn the living – remain in foreign parts, do not let yourself be betrayed and confounded. Those living in camps seldom receive letters from the few compatriots who in spite of the situation have responded to the invitation of repatriation officers and have returned allegedly to their native countries. The news received is seldom encouraging. It tells about disappointment and poverty, and other hardships that the repat- i By Rev. A. Lūsis riants have to face. Their property is generally not returned, they are not accepted for the work they used to do, and often they are forced to change their domicile. After a few months\' stay in their native land many of them apparently are banished for forced labour to the polar regions of the Soviet Union. There are many who after lea ving the D.P. camp go this way directly without seeing their destination. This refers particularly to the former soldiers, the police officials and the municipal functionaries etc. of the period of the German occupation. Generally, these people are kept back at the repatriation assembly center in Brandenburg from where they are sent to Siberia or other regions in need of slave labour. In fact the Soviet repatriation officers have a keen interest in persons of this group. All possible means are used to get these unhappy people in the grip of the Soviet regime. To illustrate the case 1 shall give an example. A. Latvian D.P.S. with his wife (Russian by birth) and two smal children lived in a D.P. camp in Germany. During the time of the independance of Latvia S. had been a police officer. He was a quiet and industrious man and enjoyed the full esteem of other inmates of the camp. His family life, however, left much to be desired, for his wife was quick-tempered and rather rude besides. In autumn 1947 she had visited the repatriation officer expressing the wish to repatriate. S.,however, resisted his wife\'s intention to 3?#tum? He made up his mind to remain and to let his wife go alone. The children, much loved by their father, caused a conflict in the D.P. camp. Twice the transport truck waited in vain for the repatriants. Evidently the repatriation officer had gi yen instructions to S.s wife to persuade her husband to re?atriate, and made her feel hat it was not advisable to come without her family. S., however, refused and remained in the camp. But in spring 1948 S. yielded, persuaded by his wife and not wishing to be separated from his ohildren. After some months a letter was received from his wife stating that she and her children had arrived in Latvia, but her husband had gone directly to the East. obviously deported to a slave laborers camp in inner Russia. Life in D.P. camps in Germany was not as easy or as rosy as described by some journalists. Yet the inmates of D.P.i camps felt happier under the protection of the U.S.A. and the British than their compatriots in their native countries. The inmates of these camps enjoyed a liberty, which has been denied by the Soviet regime. And that is why these three Baltic nations call for help. Human conscience, made dull by the last war, often does not hear this call and does not see the sufferings of the Estonians, the Latvians, and the Lithuanians. But we DP\'s, believe that God\'s mill grinds slowly, but surely. We believe that God will not suffer the rule of injustice in the world, but His right hand will change all that has been done by His left hand. And in this faith we look towards those nations and countries who in the name of liberty and justice are awake to the need for a better future for mankind.', 'Exploitation of Youth In U.S.S.R. "The State Labor Reserves pre pare qualified workers for a wid variety of specialities. The shar of Labor Reserve graduate among the workers in enterprise and construction sites is continu ally growing. . . The Soviet Gov eminent provides a great deal o assistance to State Labor Reserv Schools."—"Pravda," October 4 1950. For ten years these State Labo Reserve Schools have been use by the Soviets as a screen for th exploitation of adolescent labo Evidence mounts that not only d the youths receive little educa tional training, but that the prac tlcal skills a graduate may ac quire are not used, and youn graduates are treated as inden tured servants. The establishment of the Labo Reserve system in 1940 put an end to the quasi-liberal system o depending on the natural drift o labor from the countryside to th towns. Henceforth, labor for in dustry was to be provided by planned and compulsory recruit ment of youth. Although design ed primarily as a war measure the system of regimenting vout into industry has been continue and even expanded since the war as the Soviets have found it a ; easy and  method of flllļi • their factories with low-cost man power. According to the Sovie Encyclopaedia, 2,250,000 trainee were turned out from 1940 t 1945. The 1946-1950 plan calle for 4,500,000 Labor Reserv School graduates, although pre sent indications are that the pla will fall short by some 800,00 students. Under this system there ar three types of schools: (1) Th Trade Schools, giving a two-yea training course, with the aim o turning out qualified "metal work ers, chemical workers, minln workers, oil workers, and worker in other complex professions, anc also qualified workers for marin transport, river transport, and th communications enterprises"; the Railway Schools, giving two-year training course for qual fied workers in railway transport and (3) the more elementary Fac tory and Works Training Schoo (FZO), which give a six months training course with the object o producing "workers for the mas professions, primarily for the coa industry, the ore mining Indus try, the metal industry, the oil in dustry, and for construction work ers." It is the FZO, or on-the-jo training schools, that are the larg est reservoir for the cheap man power of Soviet industry. The "educational" training giv en the youths In the FZO school consists of at least five hours o work on the production line in th plant, and two hours of actlvitie devoted to "ideological and politi cal development." For worklnj In the factory the pupils recelv( a tiny salary, but the State take one-third of this back as par compensation for the "free school ing" it is giving, the factory take! another third as a kickback foi having the youths In its plant with the remaining third left fo: the student. me xautuiy uaimus Btuwia b have been unpopular with youth 3 from the very start. High-press sure recruiting campaigns have 3 never fulfilled the quotas, and out– right drafting of youths has had . to be adopted. After several f years of operation, on February b 14, 1947, "Moskovski Bolshevik" indicated that a voluntary enrolment of even one-third was exceptional. Probably one reason for I the great unpopularity of the 1 schools with youth is that they not 5 only find themselves drafted into • the system, but after their train-3 ing, as in the case of all upper school graduates, are regarded as mobilised, and obliged to work for at least four years at the State \' enterprise to which they are ar" bitrarily assigned. Any attempt to step out of the system is punr ishable by a prison term, i The reliance of Soviet industry f on this 16 to 18-year-old labor f force is shown by "Pravda\'s" res port of February 15, 1848, that – during the previous year \\ "trainees" had accounted for the – production of 2,000,000 tons of – coal, 270,000 tons of ore, the , overhaul and repair of 2000 locoi motives and 17,000 railroad cars, 1 the construction of 2400 metallur, glcal machines, 60,000,000 roul bles\' worth of spare parts for ? agricultural machinery, and many – other items. t "Molodoi Bolshevik," No. 6, 3 June, 1948, reported that in some > industrial enterprises youths com-1, prised 40 to 50 per cent, of the 3 j total number of workers. "Pray-! da," on October 4, 1950, stated i that "in the largest enterprises ) trainees constitute more than half of all the workers, and in the a Urals, for example, some shops are g entirely made up of labor reserr vists." f Those in charge of building up . the labor reserve system seem to , have become primarily interested ģ In volume, with little thought giv-1 en to actual needs. "Izvestia," on » June 17, 1950, admitted that the s Ministry of Labor Reserves and I the Gosplan "sometimes permit in-  accuracies in their planning for _ the training of cardes. Some of ; the schools prepare workers with-1 out adequate attention to the 1 needs of the enterprises." \' After being dragooned into the f system for "training," the youths i themselves are beginning seril ously to complain about the lack – of plan and co-ordination in the ■ programme. "Moskovski Kom– somolets" of July 25, 1950, report» ed that students of the labor re• serve training programme were not ■ being allowed to work in the specialities in many plants. The fol. lowing was given as a typical ; "industrial career," of fourth-class – steam fitter Boykov: — ļ "July 7, 1948: Went to work i in Shop No. 5. "November 21, IP48: Became ! helper in Shop No. 1. "January 11, 1949: Went to work in garage as freight handler. "September 22, 1949: Emerywheel operator in Shop No. 4. "November 2, 1949: Transferred to Shop No. 9 as carpenter. "December 24, 1949: Became furnace charger in Shop No. 1. "February 13, 1950: Ladle pusher in Shop No. 3." A group of graduate carpenters of FZO School No. 130 wrote to "Moskovskaia Pravda" on August 30, 1950:— "We finished school over two years ago with the speciality of carpenter. However, from the time we left school to this day we have been shifted from one place to another. . . The State teaches us a good speciality, but we are deprived of the opportunity to emI ploy it, although there is a big demand for carpenters, and there j are not enough of them. When are they going to change their atj titude toward young workers?" (To be Continued)', 'CHILDREN IN THE U.S.S.R. The Soviets have in various ways been trying to express their appreciation of and love for children and youth. At present this glorification of youth is a standing feature of Soviet propaganda and the happy life of Soviet children is tirelessly compared to the children\'s misery in capitalist countries on every occasion that offers. On June 1 of this year, the Communist "International Day in Defence of Children," PRAVDA, IZVESTIA, TRUD and thousands of provincial papers declared that "in U.S.A. ... the children ravenously seek rotten vegetables in the garbage cans at the sidewalks, devouring this refuse together with mould . . ." that "six million children in U.S.A. cannot go to school whence they are kept away by poverty. Over three and a half million American children must work more than 12 hours daily at farms and industrial enterprises, earning only the meagrest salary. .. . The slave labor of children has become a permanent feature of American life . . ." that the American authorities insist that American youth become "bold and fearless murderers . . ." that "hundreds of millions of children are doomed to starvation and death in the capitalist countries," etc., etc. A glorious contrast to these horrors is "the country of victorious Socialism," where the government and Stalin personally have created a "shining and happy world" for the children. "In the USSR," exclaim the Soviet papers, "we are building schools, pioneer palaces, obstetrical clinics, day nurseries, children\'s homes, in the USSR children are taught free of charge, in the USSR we have motherhood medals and the order "Glory of Motherhood," etc, etc. We have no intention to deny that day nurseries, kindergartens, orphanages, etc., are built in the USSR like in any other country for even a "Socialist state" cannot manage without them. But no propagandai can hide the sufferings that children have gone, and are still going, through in the Soviet Union, no "sun of the Stalinist constitution" dry the tears they have wept during Bolshevik rule. Who does not remember the million-headed armies of the wild, dirty, ragged homeless children of the Civil War and the NEP period? Bridges, ruins, graveyard vaults, abandoned cellars were the only home they knew and while other children were caressed by their mothers, they had to run for their lives from the militia. Who can forget the horrors of the "kolkhoz famine" in 1932 and 1933 when millions of children in all villages of Ukraine, the Black Earth provinces and Northern Caucasus were swollen from starvation and for months ate only grass, the bark of trees, carrion, chewed bits of old dry leather and died like flies of exposure and dysentry. Their despairing and starving parents, the new kolkhozniks, daily brought their dying children by the dozens and hundreds to the railway stations of Moscow, Leningrad, Kiev, Kharkov, Rostov and left them to the mercy of the Soviet police. At that time the Communist leaders did not care a rap for the sufferings of the children. They were consolidating their power, introducing collectivization, building palaces of culture, adorning the halls of the Moscow underground with beautiful stones, creating a war industry and robbing for this not only the kolkhoz peasants but also their children. With the great purges of the thirties millions of children lost their fathers and mothers through deportations and mass arrests. When at last "Socialism was victorious," the Soviets built a considerable number of "Socialist" camps for "juvenile delinquents" and homeless children. The educators were NKVD men who trained the unhappy orphans into faithful servants of the regime. At present the orphanages in the USSR are a peculiar kind of "forge of Socialist cadres." The enormous number of children who lost their parents in World War 11. are there brought up in the spirit of "unconditional loyalty to the cause of Lenin-Stalin." Among the inmates are also the children of the arrested, executed or deported "enemies of the people" and the forcibly repatriated Soviet citizens. The images of father and mother in the consciousness of these children are replaced with the images of Stalin, Molotov, Beiia. and other "loving" leaders who have deprived the little ones of their home and children. According to the Soviet press the regime has built for the children over 1000 pioneer palaces, 430 stations for young technicians and scientists, 140 toy towns, a multitude of libraries and reading rooms, is publishing millions of children\'s books. This is quite true. But it is also true that every pioneer palace, every school, every children\'s book has the same object: to eradicate from the chUdren\'s souls everything proper to children, to poison them with atheist materialism, to inculcate into them the adoration of Stalin\'s person, to turn them into Communist fanatics, dumb and uncritical servants of the regime ready to inform on their own brothers, sisters, fathers and mothers. The Soviet press proudly declares that tuition in the USSR is free of charge and that the road to education and choice of profession is open to all Soviet children and youths. It does not mention with a single word the law of October 3, 1940, introducing payment for tuition in the Bth, 9th and 10th classes of the middle school, all technical colleges and institutes of higher education. This fee is high enough to put secondary and higher education out of reach of the children of workers, kolkhoz peasants and small employees. As to the choice of profession, this is determined by plans of how many particular specialists are to be turned out each year and, furthermore, after graduation every specialist must work at least five years at a job assigned to him by the government in any part of the vast empire. The Soviet government has shown its solicitude for the children in other ways: e.g., with the law "On Labor Reserves," dated October 2, 1940. Under this law every year up to a million Soviet children from the age of 14 and upwards are forcibly drafted without their own or their parents\' consent and sent to barrack-like factory and railway schools at a distance of hundreds and thousands of kilometres from their home. In order to get so-called "industrial practice," the 15-10 year old pupils of these schools work in mines, oilfields, metallurgical plants, or . building sites in winter, taking part in the compulsory Socialist competitions and often enough being encouraged to fulfil up to two norms of a grown-up worker. For escape from these schools and breaches of discipline the children are punished with confinement in labour camps for up to one year under the very "humane" law of December 28, 1940. The loving treatment meted out to children in the USSR is further exemplified by the law of April 7, 1935, on the responsibility of children for criminal acts as from their twelfth year with the application to them of all the punishments provided in the various cases, including the death penalty. A regulation of December 10, 1940, introduced prosecution of 12-year-olds for . . . the unscrewing of railroad. nuts "with the application of all prescribed penalties." What other state in the world can boast; of such "love" for children? Much is written in the USSR about assistance to single mothers; and mothers of many children. Indeed, in the USSR, "highly productive" mothers are hung with medals like pedigree cattle in other countries. A woman having given birth to eleven children is called "mother-heroine," she gets the order otj "Glory of Motherhood," a bonus of 2,500 rubles and, moreover, a monthly benefit of 150 rubles. This sum is not enough to pay for a pair of shoes. In the ordinary course of things, the benefit per child is 20 rubles month-.;, ly, for which the mother can bus 10 pints of milk. Such is the "solicitude" of thfl Soviet government for the chiļfl dren at home. In the rest of tbfl world, however, the Soviets podjfl as the defenders of childhood ajffl youth, glorify youth at Berlin an shed crocodile tears over the sufl ferings of the Korean childnM with which they charge the UM forces. Among the many hypoJ crisies of the Soviets their hypj crisy with regard to children_J the most abominable.—(NETļH LETTER).']

Name: 125_captive_nation_week_july
Representative_Docs: ['Senator Brooke\'s Essay: On Human Rights And Self-Determination Since 1959, the U.S. Congress, by joint resolution (73 Stat. 212) has authorized and requested the President to designate the third week in July as Captive Nations Week. In order to illustrate the true sentiment and full meaning behind Captive Nations Week, we present an essay by Senator Edward W. Brooke (R., Mass.) which the Senator has dedicated to this Bulletin: – One of the principle virtues of the United States in international affairs has been its willingness to give support and comfort to those nations and individuals denied the freedoms that we cherish. It is that virtue that has led, in recent years, to increased emphasis on human rights considerations in American foreign policy. Such considerations are and should be a major factor in determining our relationships with other countries. They are also a measure of our own pursuit of moral goals in the very immoral world of international politics. Human rights violations come in many forms. We are all very sensitive to the abuse of individuals by dictatorial governments. Auto-genocide in Cambodia, the butchery that is common in Uganda, and the perpetuation of the Gulag Archipelago in the Soviet Union all stem from a basic disregard of the sanctity of the individual. Equally immoral are human rights violations which seek to destroy the cultural heritage of any group of individuals. Far too often this type of human rights violation is ignored. In this short essay I intend to call attention to it by focusing on the plight of the Baltic nations as they suffer continued attempts at "colonization" by the Soviet Union. Specific mention will be made of conditions in Latvia to illustrate the general nature of Soviet policy toward these Captive Nations. In singling out the Latvia situation for specific attention I do not in any way mean to ignore the plight of others suffering a similar fate. lam well aware of the travails of the other nations subjugated by the Soviet Union. Their experience under totalitarian rule is mirrored in the Latvian tragedy. The struggle of the Latvian people, as that of the Lithuanians and other subjugated peoples, to maintain their cultural and national identity has been a heroic one. Situated as they are between the two behemoths of Europe – Russia and Germany – Latvia and its sister Baltic states have suffered many tragic setbacks but, through all the dark hours, the desire for liberty and the hope for eventual self-determination has not waned. It is that desire and that hope, evident in those of Baltic heritage, that kindles and sustains the dream that, one day, when justice reigns supreme, independent free Baltic states will be numbered once again in the family of nations. The Soviet Union, even after exercising dictatorial rule for many years, has been frustrated in its efforts to destroy the justified national aspirations of the Baltic peoples. Yet, it continues its concerted effort to extinguish the wiU of these peoples to maintain their cultural and national identities. It has done so by forced internal deportation to various areas of the Soviet Union; by external exile of dissidents; by suppression of ethnic literature and newspapers; and by persecution of those in the Baltic countries who dare to speak out against Moscow\'s continuous violations of basic individual and collective human rights. Let me briefly recount certain aspects of the Soviet effort to destroy what is uniquely Latvian. Moscow, after occupying that country by force, set about to "integrate" the Latvian economy into that of the Soviet Union. Russian became the language of business in the economic and political life of Latvia. The highly productive agricultural strength of Latvia was sacrificed to artificially imposed "industrialization. " This resulted, in turn, in mass migration to Latvia of Russian workers, thus lessening the employment and career opportunities for Latvians. In turn, these individuals, faced with meager prospects for a decent life in their own country, were either forced by economic circumstances or by Soviet government pressure to relocate outside of Latvia. It is impossible to escape the conclusion that this "Russification" of Latvia through imposed industrialization was part of a conscious plan to destroy the Latvian culture by dissolving the homogeneity of the country. Such a conclusion is further substantiated by Moscow\'s subsidization of Russian "colonists" willing to move to Latvia. This "colonization" of Latvia continues to this day and truly can be characterized as "cultural genocide." The United States has a moral obligation to protest against this Soviet disregard of the right of individuals and the right of the Baltic nations as a whole to preserve their cultural and national heritages. That obligation has special meaning because many Americans of Baltic heritage have made major contributions to the well-being of this country. Yet, all too few of us know of their contributions. Let me mention a few examples from the pages of Latvian-American history. One of the first Union soldiers killed in the Civil War was a Latvian – Martins Bucins of Ldbau (Liepaja – Ed.). Among the leading Latvian-Americans of the 20th century was Karlis Ulmanis, who, during his life, returned to Latvia to become, first, its Prime Minister and later its President. Many in the New York and New England area were and are familiar with the work of the Latvian composer and organ virtuoso Alfred Kalnins, who came to this country in 1927 and served as the organist for Christ\'s Lutheran Church in New York City. Kalnins is best remembered as the composer of several operas, including Banuta and The Islanders. He returned to Latvia in 1932. One of the great heroes of the United States Navy during World War II was the Latvian Thomas Blau, who, as commander of 33 convoys, never lost a single ship through enemy action. For his incredible skill and valor he was awarded the Navy Cross. I mention these specific individuals to emphasize the great debt this country owes to those among us of Latvian heritage. Similar individuals of Lithuanian or Estonian heritage could also be identified. Our debt to them is great for they have proven themselves to be a bulwark of our democratic republic for the best of all reasons; they cherish the freedom that is ours in the United States because they have known – either first-hand or from family accounts – the suffering and oppression that has so long afflicted their ancestral homelands in the Baltic and they desire and work for the preservation of the freedoms they have found in this, their second homeland. They know full well that "eternal vigilance is the price that must be paid for liberty.tf It is the continued efforts of all those in this country who believe fervently in the ideals of personal freedom and self-determination that sustain those in Latvia, Lithuania and elsewhere who will not acquiesce willingly to Soviet tyranny. Sustained protest against Soviet oppression is our minimum obligation to those who are risking so much to keep alive the spirit of freedom in the Captive Nations. We know that our protests can have a positive effect as evidenced by the release of Viktors. Kalnins. But, we must not be lulled into complacency through such gestures that are largely \'token" in nature. It is only too obvious that success in celebrated cases, such as Kalnins1, offers little recompense to those who continue to suffer Russian persecution. Nor does it in any way justify a lessening of opposition to Soviet attempts at "cultural genocide" that I have referred to in this essay. The West must not be bought off by token gestures. It must continue to protest and publicize both individual cases of Soviet repression and the evil of the concerted Soviet attempt to destroy the unique cutural heritages of the Baltic nations. One of the greatest testimonies to the unending quest of the human spirit for freedom has been the failure of the Soviet Union to destroy the desire of the peoples of the Baltic countries for freedom and self-determination. That desire lives not only in the courageous souls in Latvia, Lithuania and Estonia who have joined with others in the Soviet empire in speaking out in defense of human rights, but also in the hearts of those in this country who have not forgotten their cultural and historical ties to the Baltic. They have refused to succumb to the temptation to "give up" in face of overwhelming odds against them. So long as we, as they, keep in remembrance the sanctity of the quest for liberty through our continued efforts to promote it, the past and present sacrifices of the peoples of the Baltic countries will not be in vain. * * *', 'The week of July 17-23, 1966 marked the eighth observance of Captive Nations Week as proclaimed by President Johnson on July 8. The full text of the President\'s proclamation was published in the July issue of this bulletin. Vice President Humphrey added a statement which reads in part: By Joint Resolution of the Congress, approved July 17, 1959, the President is authorized to issue a Proclamation each year designating the third week in July as "Captive Nations Week." President Johnson has asked all of us "to give renewed devotion to the just aspirations of all people for national independence and human liberty. "... Regretfully, these precious values are still not enjoyed b, many peoples throughout the globe. Our hearts go out to these peoples. We are mindful of their plight. We know how they yearn to be free. Each people\'s right to work out its own destiny--in liberty--is as deeply meaningful to us--as it was to our Founding Fathers. . . (The Ukrainian Bulletin, August 1-15.) Captive Nations Week observances were held in the United States and other countries of the free world on a greater scale than ever before. During this week, 14 Senators and a hundred Representatives of the House came forth with appropriate statements on the floor of the Congress, or at rallies in their home states, expressing support of the Communist subjected nations\' struggle for freedom and independence. Captive Nations Week observance in the House of Representatives was initiated by Daniel J. Flood (D. , Pa.). He said: We observe Captive Nations Week because it is legally provided for in Public Law 86-90. Moreover, the week is a tremendous moral symbol signifying that we Americans will never forget the captive nations or accommodate ourselves to their permanent captivity. (C. R, July 20. ) Rep. Flood once again advocated the creation of a Special House Committee of Captive Nations, saying that it is "more necessary now than ever before. " CAPTIVE NATIONS WEEK, 1966 The Speaker, John W. McCorrnack (D, , Mass, ) said in reference to the Baltic nations: As the consequence of the Soviet Union\'s militant aggressive policy in Europe there are today a number of Eastern European nations which are captives of the Kremlin, . , The peoples of the three Baltic countries, the Estonians, Latvians, and Lithuanians, have endured the oppressive Communist rule longer than other captive nations. These people suffer under an additional stigma in that their homelands have been annexed by the Soviet Union, and, as far as the Soviet Union is aware, there are no such independent and sovereign entities today as Estonia, Latvia, and Lithuania. (C. R. July 21. ) Senator Thomas H, Kuchel (R. , Gal. ) also laid stress on the plight of the Baltic peoples, saying: America must make its position clear that it respects the rights of all the people under Communist rule. Last year I cosponsored in the Senate, Senate concurrent resolution 6, calling for national independence and self-determination for the brave Baltic peoples of Estonia, Latvia, and Lithuania. I urge the Senate Foreign Relations Committee to deal favorably and expeditiously with the bill. (C. R. July 20.) A similar resolution (H. Con. Res. 416) was unanimously adopted by the House on June 21, 1965. Senator Milward L. Simpson (R, , Wyo.) reviewed on October 5 the gratifying results of this year\'s observances, and announced that the many speeches and reports published in the Congressional Record pertaining to Captive Nations Week will be reprinted in a separate edition. He evaluated the observances as follows: Mr. President, this past July citizens throughout the country observed the eighth Captive Nations Week. From all accounts, this observance turned out to be the best yet. In terms of citizen activity, newspaper coverage, TV and radio programing, and public discussion forums, the 1966 Captive Nations Week surpassed all others. This sustained and growing interest in the plight of all the captive nations augurs well for the future of our country in the months and years ahead. For the past 2 months, the RECORD has been replete with reports of this highly successful week, (C. R. Oct. 5.) * Captive Nations Week was also observed in other free countries. In Australia Mr. V. Meckman, Minister of Finance, and other prominent politicians addressed a large gathering in Melbourne. Descendants of the European captive nations attended in national costume, carrying their national flags. The event was covered by the major Australian daily papers. In Argentina, President J. Onganin proclaimed September 18-24 Captive Nations Week, urging government institutions and the people to mark the week by appropriate observances. Major J. Schettini of Buenos Aires issued a similar proclamation. The various subsequent events were widely reported by tne press, radio, and TV. Cardinal Dr. A. Caggiano devoted a special solemn Mass to the captive nations in the Cathedral of Buenos Aires. The Argentine delegation of the Assembly of European Captive Nations, headed by Latvian V. Gusts, took a very active part in organizing the observances.', 'Come, help the helpless! by Ojars Kalnins Has a friend ever asked you to donate a Saturday afternoon to help him move? Or maybe called you for help when his car broke down? Like all of us, I\'m sure, you were glad to help. After all, that\'s what friends are for. Well, a few million friends are asking for a very special favor on July 18. It won\'t cost you much. And it won\'t take more than a few hours of your time. All you have to do is bring your friends and family to Daley Plaza and join thousands of other concerned citizens as we raise our voices in support of the Captive Nations of the world. Perhaps you don\'t know what the Captive Nations are? Or – why this rally on July 18? Here\'s a brief history. On July 17, 1959, President Eisenhower signed a proclamation declaring the third week of July as Captive Nations Week. That week was set aside each year to draw attention to the plight of over 1.1 billion people living under Soviet gun. This proclamation, known as Public Law 86-90, stressesstresses the sympathy and understanding the American people feel for that better than one fourth of the world\'s population that is enslaved by Communism. It also points out that the vast and growing Soviet Empire – the largest in human history – "poses a dire threat to the security of the United States and of all the free peoples of the world." When this proclamation was first written, Poland, Hungary, Lithuania, Ukraine, Czechoslovakia, Latvia, Estonia, White Ruthenia, Rumania, East Germany, Bulgary, mainland China, Armenia, Azerbaijan, Georgia, North Korea, Albania, Idel-Ural, Tibet, Cossackia, Turkestan, North Viennam, and others, were considered Captive Nations. Today those "others" include Cuba, Afghanistan, Laos, Cambodia, South Yemen, Angola, Ethiopia, Grenada, all of Vietnam ... The reasons why this proclamation was written 22 years ago still hold true today. Only more so. The Soviet empire continues to grow as Soviet aggression spreads to all continents of the globe. Soviet supported terrorists are destroying lives as far away as Afghanistan and as near as El Salvador. Marxist revolutionaries are threatening American interests in the Phillipines and undermining peace throughout Central America and the Caribbean. Talk about the Soviet "octopus" spreading its tentacles to embrace the whole world may have saounded exaggerated 22 years ago. But today it is sad reality. Perhaps there isn\'t much we can do as individuals to stem the tide of Soviet military expansion and terrorism. But then, again, as free men in a free country we can speak out. Captive Nations Day is that opportunity. On July 18 Americans from around the country will send a signal to the Kremlin that the world is watching and will long remember. We are wathing Poland. We\'re watching Afghanistan. We will keep remembering Soviet dissidents who ose their freedom and their lives in Siberian death camps. But there is more. This year Captiva Nations Day will also be known as POW-MIA Recognition Day. This is doubly significant for American POW\'s and MlA\'s met their fate In Communist controlled countries, the very same lands which the US government has recognized as Captive Nations. So – please join us as we mourn the terrible fate of all the "captives" of the world. You may even have friends or relatives who are POW\'s or MlA\'s ... or political prisoners in Soviet Union. It is important to remeber that all the suffering which the Captive Nations / POW-MIA Recognition Day seeks to publicize comes from one and the same source. Without the bloodlust of the Kremlin and without Marxist propaganda which is used dupe the innocent, none of this would have occurred. Remember .... if you can\'t make it this July 18, noon, to Daley Plaza, keep in mind that next year, too, there will be a Captive Nations Day,and, unless a miracle happens, the year after next, and the year after that ... As long as there are Captive Nations, it is our enlightened self-interest to fight for their freedom. For as long as there are captives, nobody\'s freedom is safe!']

Name: 126_ribbon_pact_hitler_black
Representative_Docs: ["WE DEMAND PRESIDENT GORBACHEV TO HONOUR HIS PLEDGE Today we are marking, appropriately displaying black ribbons, the fiftieth anniversary of a day which can logically be viewed as one of the darkest in modern history. The Secret Protocols of the Hitler-Stalin nonaggression treaty ( generally referred to, after the signatories, as the Molotov-Ribbentrop pact ) signed on August 23rd, 1939, enabled Nazi Germany, by securing its eastern flank, to plunge the world into the cataclysm of World War 11, which left tens of millions dead and caused hundreds of millions to lose their freedom and many nations the right to be masters of their own lands. This day marks an event which will live in deepest infamy in history. While we somberly recall this tragic event and mourn its countless victims, our intention on this day is not to look back in anger and cry for vengeance – which never solves any problemsbut to demand that the lingering wrongs from this black pact be rectified – that the peoples doomed to conquest and foreign rule or domination by the Hitler-Stalin deal that are still suffering this fate be released from their bonds and granted the right to guide their own destinies. Among those still in political bondage, probably none has suffered a harsher fate than the three Baltic nations of Estonia, Latvia, and Lithuania. The freedom they were brutally deprived of in 1940 as the direct result of the Hitler-Stalin deal of August 23, 1939, has yet to be returned to them – the freedom and independence they regained as their natural right in 1918, bravely defended in 1918-20 and which the Soviet Union recognized without reservation and for all time in the peace treaties of 1920, subsequently reaffirming and cementing these assurances with nonaggression treaties. All these treaties turned out to be not worth the paper they were written on when Hitler and Stalin banded together 50 years ago and agreed to carve up Eastern Europe between them. Poland, the largest and strongest of those earmarked for conquest, met its fate first; attacked with ovJwhelming force from the west on September 1 and sixteen days later stabbed in the back from the east, it was crushed despite heroic resistance and divided between the two plotters. Within about a month – in late September and early October, after Poland's fate was scaled – it was the turn of the Baltic States, Lithuania having on September 28 joined Estonia and Latvia on Stalin's hit-list in the Secret Protocols after declining to by Nazi-German protection by joining the attack on Poland. Thrcatened with immediate invasion in ultimatums with deadlines so short as to prevent effective mobilization of defence forces and thus virtually defenceless, the three governments saw no other choice than to yield to the Soviet Union the military bases it demanded; the Soviets signed of course treaties full of all sorts of assurances and pledges of friendly relations, nonaggression, and nonintervention. Soviet forces then poured into the bases, greatly exceeding the numbers agreed on, and the stage was set for the last act of the drama scripted on August 23, which was duly played out through the military occupation by massed Soviet forces in mid-June 1940. The Baltic part of the Hitler-Stalin plot – which deal had also led to the Soviet attack on Finland in November 1939 and would soon, in late June 1940, lead to the forced cession of part of Romania to the Soviets – had thus been efficiently carried out. There is no need to describe here the meaninglees, farcical process of the ensuing forced annexation or comment on its utter illegality in international, let alone constitutional law. We all know it; those of us old enough witnessed it ourselves; all informed historians and political observers of the world know the stark facts, as do the governments of the Free World, which accordingly refuse to recognize the annexation as legal. And the lapse of decades has not rendered the continuing Soviet occupation of and rule in the Baltic States any more legitimate – the people having never in any democratic way approved it or legitimized the functionaries of the so-called Baltic Soviet Republics. Nor is it necessary to dwell here on the slaughter and terrible suffering inflicted on our peoples through the events unleashed by the pact we are recalling today. Those of us here old enough remember the horrors only too well ; there can hardly be anybody here who did not lose relatives or friends; and these events have been endlessly chronicled and have sunk into the deepest recesses of the consciousness of our much -tried nations. Estonians, Latvians, and Lithuanians want nothing more than that the foreign occupation end and they be left alone to be masters of their own lands, democratically to work out their own destiny. That this it the national will ie elementary knowledge among Balta everywhere, in the homelands and abroad. Soviets acquainted with them know this well and so do all informed Western observers, as we have so often heard in the media during the past year. This unwavering national will and purpose can any time be gauged and confirmed by duly verified democratic process. The Soviet Government has just, at long last, publicly admitted what the world has known for decades – the existence of the Secret Protocols. But while the admission of any truth is of course as a matter of principle to be welcomed, what is the profit or merit in it in this instance unless it is linked to admission of the occupation and forcible annexation of the Baltic States? What is the point in admitting the cause while denying the effect? It's like admitting the hen and denying the egg. The sincerity of glasnost is very much on trial here; admitting or not admitting such a well-known fact as the forcible seizure and annexation of the Baltic States is indeed a touchstone, a test of glasnost. Will the falsification of historical facts now end? And will this crime against international law and morality be remedied as far as this is possible at this date, i. e., by allowing full self-determination for the Baltic nations? This ie what we here and Baits everywhere else demand. President Gorbachev only recently declared that the Soviet Union does not wish to impose its will on other peoples. Let it now honor this pledge and let Estonia, Latvia, and Lithuania again become masters of their own destiny. Then these countries will be happy to do what they have always wanted to do – live in peace and good neighborliness with the country east of their borders. M. Puhvel.", '/ THE \\ / TORONTO \\ wo), Conceived and initially promoted by Canadians of Baltic origin, the idea of an annual reminder of the 1939 Nazi-Soviet Pact has taken hold around the world since Aug 23 was declared International Black Ribbon Day at a few commemorative meetings in North America four years ago. *«This year. Black Ribbon Day Commit tees are putting finishing touches to special programs of countless rallies that will recall the soth anniversary of the infamous deal Ribbentrop and Molotov Hitler s and Stalin\'s foreign ministers* struck in 1939. The significance of that deal, which in conjunction with the preceding cave-in of the western democracies to Hitler\'s Munich Diktat unleashed World War 11. is sufficiently known to need detailed recount ing And some of its enduring tragic consequences that are still with us, such as the continued Soviet-enforced division of Europe and enslavement of many nations by murderous communist regimes (the latest reminder of that meonven iently harsh fact was the Beijing massacre i. are too self-evident to be doubted What may need a reminder, though, are die secret clauses of the Hitler-Stalin Pact, until recently resolutely denied by The Baltic day of infamy Moscow, about carving up Poland and eradication of the Baltie Republics – clauses that set the stage for eventual Soviet expansion into Central Europe and the still far from resolved protracted East-West conflict of the second half of this century About a year ago glasnost cracked the spurious official Soviet version and "justification* of the Hitler-Stalin Pact as a brilliant Soviet tactical move to gain time for strengthening inadequate defences. As former Soviet diplomat and eminent publicist Semyon Rostovsky courageously put it, the pact was a "criminal error" which "almost led to the destruction of the Soviet Union." But even with that admission the secret clauses still remained taboo. Since then several attempts have been made by the partly unmuzzled Soviet media to explain nationalistic and political ferment in the Baltic republics and Poland But without reference to the Secret Protocol of the 1939 Stalin Hitler Pact the background essential for under standing is missing, and the reasons given are perversions or distortions of historical facts For example, the Soviet Military Heview a rote earlier it.is year about Soviet magnanimity in concluding protec tive treaties of mutual assistance with the three Baltic states in late September and early October 1939. The •benefits" allegedly included strict rules of friendly behavior of Soviet troops Stalin dispatched to "protect" the Baits \'ostensibly from Nazi invasion). In fact the fate of Estonia. Latvia and Lithuania was settled in the Secret Protocol of the Soviet-Nazi Pact which, apart from dividing Poland between the two totalitarian predators, put the Baltic nations and Finland in the Soviet "sphere of influence." From then on the eventual formal liquidation of the Baltic republics as independent sovereign states was merely a matter of Moscow\'s timing and method of the grab. The "mutual assistance treaties" the Baits couldn\'t refuse signing as they watched in helpless horror the carving of Poland, were just cynical camouflage of gradual Soviet takeover which was completed with the charade ol "free democratic elections" in July 1940. With that particular Soviet grab decided in principle in the Secret Protocol of the Stalin-Hitler Pact 50 years ago. all that remained to be settled was the line dividing the \' spheres of influence LUBOR J. ZINK factually control- of the two totalitarian empires That was settled at another Molotov-Kibbentrop meeting on Sept. 28. IfQfl — the very same day the Soviet-Estonian Treaty of Mutual Assistance was signed — which produced the little known, but for understanding the history-shaping events of that tragic period important. Soviet Nazi Boundary and Friendship Treaty \' The formality of "official" incorporation of the three Baltic states into the USSR came, as I have already mentioned, aftfl I the Moscow-ordered and staged "parlia mentary elections" held simultaneously ir. the "Soviet-protected" (i.e., occupied: Estonia, Latvia and Lithuania on July 14 and 15, 1940 With Soviet tanks and secret police everywhere, the communists won the elections handily. A week later the new "Baltic parliaments" asked Moscow to let them join the common home oi heroic builders of socialist paradise. Black Ribbon Day should make us itsten for subtle echoes of these 50-year-old deceptions. For a start I recommend Mik hail Gorbachev s seemingly visionary and apparently seductive rhetoric of "a common European home " Ask the Baits what the Kremlin\'s notion of a "common home" means', 'AlO THE WALL STREET JOURNAL THURSDAY, JUNE 22, 19h9 Secret Nazi-Stalin Pact Haunts Gorbachev By PeterGlmbel Staff Reporter of Thv.W/*i.lSthf.kt Journal BONN-Locked away in a gray steel Closet in the West German Foreign Ministry here is a skeleton of Soviet history that is haunting Mikhail Gorbachev and his policy of glasnost. It is a colored map of Eastern Europe, divided into 28 sections and stuck on a grayish-green backing. On the right-hand side of the map, just below the town of 6rest. Joseph Stalin has written his name ih blue ink. His huge signature ends with an energetic downward flourish that continues for more than 22 inches. Next to it, Joachim yon Ribbentrop, Hitler\'s foreign minister, has signed more compactly in red, scrawling the date 28/IX/39 (Sept. 28. 1939) just under his name. " Together with a handful of other original papers in the closet, the map provides hard evidence that Nazi Germany and the Soviet Union concluded secret agreements in 1939 to carve up Eastern Europe between them. A squiggly black line on the map next to Stalin\'s signature marks the agreed division of Poland. That Hitler\'s Germany and Stalin\'s Russia signed a nonaggression pact in August 1939 is undisputed. But for five decades, Moscow has denied Western assertions about a secret protocol attached to the pact that split the countries between them into German and Soviet "spheres of interest." Indeed, the Soviets have been so infuriated by such suggestions that they once published a polemical book on the issue entitled "The Falsifiers of History." The pact was a pivotal point in history. On Sept. 1. 1939, a week after it was signed, Nazi Germany invaded Poland, setting off World War 11. The Soviet Union invaded eastern Poland on Sept. 17. But the Germans smashed the accord on June 22, 1941, by invading Russia. Forgery Alleged Moscow says it "can\'t find" the secret protocol in its archives, and alleges a German Foreign Ministry microfilm of it is a forgery. Its attitude has long been dismissed as untenable by the West. Now, thanks to glasnost, the new spirit of openness, its arguments are coming under growing fire in the Soviet Union itself. Bowing to growing calls for the truth by the three Baltic republics and some Moscow historians, Mr. Gorbachev reluctantly set up a parliamentary panel this month to investigate the Hitler-Stalin accords. New Evidence Tests Limits of Glasnost, Stirs Baltic States INTERNATIONAL "We understand that it is not so easy lo escape the past," Mr. Gorbachev said in a speech during his recent visit to West Germany. He disclosed that lie even asked Chancellor Helmut Kohl about the .">O-yearold documents during the West German\'s visit to Moscow in October. The issue goes far beyond the question of historical truth. It has become a test of the credibility of official glasnost-and a potentially explosive domestic political problem in the Soviet Baltic btates. Under the secret protocol, signed by Ribbentrop and the Soviet foreign minister, Vyacheslav Molotov, on Aug. 23, 1939. Germany agreed to leave the fate of Latvia and Es tonia to the Soviet Union. A month later, in an amendment to the protocol, Lithuania was also declared part of the Soviet sphere of interest. The Red Army forcibly an nexed the three republics in 1940. But Moscow continues to insist that the republics joined the Soviet Union of their own free will. If it now acknowledges there was indeed a secret deal with Hitler, it will be conceding that its push into the Balticstates was tantamount to an occupation – an admission likely to fuel growing calls for Baltic independence from Moscow. Sticking to the old version could be even more dangerous, however, as activists In the three republics prepare to mark the pact\'s 50th anniversary this summer. \'Baltic Seething\' "The Baltic region is seething as these issues are being debated," Mr. Gorbachev told the Soviet parliament this month. "Indeed, doubt is being cast on whether the will of the people was involved at all in the incorporation I of the Baltic states) into the Soviet Union. That is scarcely the truth." Most Baits disagree. Demonstrations were held last August to protest the pact and even bigger ones are expected this year. Two Estonian newspapers tested the limits of glasnost by publishing the text of the secret protocol, and an Estonian deputy read it out to the parliament. Big cracks have also opened in the official Soviet version. A commission of Soviet and Polish historians set up to smooth over disagreements between the two nations recently reported they couldn\'t find the original version of the secret protocol. But according to Pravda, the historians concluded that "the subsequent events and diplomatic correspondence provide grounds for concluding.. .that in some form or another, agreement on the division of the sphere of interests was indeed readied in August 1939.\'* One Soviet historian, Lev Bezymensky. came ciose to a similar conclusion in a series of articles he recently published in the Soviet magazine New Times. Mr. Bezymensky is so far the only Soviet who has bothered to visit the Foreign Ministiy archives In Bonn to look for himself. Despite hints that he accepts some of the evidence there, he timidly concludes that, "It is too early now to pass the final political and legal verdict." The controversy has aroused so much interest that Theodore Gehling, deputy chief archivist at the Bonn Foreign Ministry, has moved the documents out of the cavernous basement where they are usually kept to a ground-floor office near his own. "They\'re taking up a lot of my time," he says. Many Documents Lost The secret protocol to the Aug. 23 agreement isn\'t there in the original, but exists on film. Many key German documents were filmed starting In 1943. when the Allies made major bombing raids on Berlin. The archives were eventually moved out of Berlin and scattered. Many documents were lost. At the end of the war, 20 rolls of film containing about 10.000 documents were salvaged and hidden by Carl yon Loesch, a German official who had worked in Ribbentrop\'s office. Mr. Loesch was supposed to destroy them. Instead, he turned them over to a British army officer in the town of Muehlhausen. The films and other documents were given to Bonn in 1956. Even if the original copy of the secret protocol is missing, there are plenty of other original documents in the German archives that point to its authenticity. Apart from the map showing the demarcation between the German and Soviet "spheres of interest" in Poland, there is the ratification of the Aug. 23 nonaggression treaty, enclosed in a leather folder with a large red and gold official Soviet seal on the cover, and a detailed descripton of where the border should run. And if any more evidence was needed, there are the original internal files from the German Embassy in Moscow, typed or handwritten, that record how the secret, protocol was negotiated. At one point during the talks on Aug. 23. Ribbentrop cabled Hitler for advice because Stalin wanted to include two more Baltic ports in the secret protocol. Berlin\'s response was rapid and terse: "Yes, agreed."']
