Representative_Docs: ["Conscripts from the Baltic States of Estonia, Latvia, and Lithuania that are drafted into the Red Army and sent away from their homelands to distant places within the Soviet Union,are mercilessly brutalized at the hands of their superior officers and other conscripts. This persecution is a form of retribution in response to the Baltic peoples' demands for independence, which was lost when the Soviet Union illegally occupied their countries in 1940. . Baltic conscripts are tormented, beaten, physically tortured, raped, and murdered. . Many Baltic conscripts have disappeared under suspicious circumstances and their whereabouts are  unknown. . Many Baltic conscripts have committed suicide as a result of their unceasing persecution, whereas others have required psychiatric hospitalization. . Baltic conscripts, unable to withstand the persecution, desert the Red Army. When caught, they are tried by the War Tribunal and characteristically receive lengthy prison sentences. . Baltic conscripts of like ethnicity serving in the same unit are forbidden to converse in their native tongue. . Baltic conscripts cannot send letters home to friends and family in their native language. The following Baltic conscripts are known to have suffered at the hands of the Red Army: BEATEN TO DEATH – Raitis Marauskis, Juris KLavinieks, Juris Kancans. DIED UNDER SUSPICIOUS CIRCUMSTANCES – Ainārs Reinis, Igors Guberskis, Agris Vīksna, Ints Ozols, Martins Svilāns. DISAPPEARED; WHEREABOUTS UNKNOWN – Egils Pukitis, Ilmārs Salenieks, Klimovics. BEATEN AND RAPED – Viesturs Kaneps, Miervaldis Staharovskis. Mothers of Baltic conscripts who have been persecuted in the Red Army are asking that Baltic men be allowed to serve within their homeland. Other Baltic conscripts are planning to burn their draft papers as a protest to the drafting of Baltic men into the army that occupies their country. YOU CAN HELP Baltic conscripts by voicing your concern with Prime Minister Brian Mulroney who will be meeting with Soviet President Gorbachev in Moscow this November. Write – Rt. Hon. Brian Mulroney, Prime Ministers Office, House of Commons, Ottawa, Ontario, KLA OA6, ph. 1-613-992-4211. For further information please contact: CANADIAN COMMITTEE FOR HUMAN RIGHTS IN LATVIA P. 0. Box 1534, Guelph, Ontario, NIH 6N9 ph. -1416-569-1379 Contributions towards the publishing costs of thi3 section and the publication will be appreciated. Mail them to Montreal Latvian Society, 291 Lorraine Dr., Baie d'Urfé, Que., Canada H9X 2R2, ph. 514-457-9832 RED ARMY BRUTALIZES BALTIC CONSCRIPTS.", 'It was inevitable that events in Poland should produce repercussions in the Baltic Republics incorporated into the U.S.S.R. This is especially true of Estonia, where high school students have staged demonstrations against the Kremlin\'s steady push for "Russification," a process made easier by the low birthrates of both the Estonians and their southern neighbors, the Latvians. A group of Estonian intellectuals have even dared sign a resolution expressing their solidarity with the rioting students and repeating their grievances against Moscow. Late last year there were also strikes in some Estonian factories, another index of the political tension in this ethnic enclave of slightly over one million. Even though more than a quarter of the people in Estonia are now Russians, the Estonians are particularly susceptible to freeworld influence because they are within reach of Finnish television broadcasts. (Estonians and Finns are closely related, and an intelligent Estonian can understand Finnish without having studied it.) Foreigners often fail to appreciate that the only thing common to the three Baltic nations is the historical fact that they have gradually fallen under the sway of Russia. In 1919 they Ihrew off the Russian yoke, but in 1940, thanks to a secret agreement between Germany and the U.S.S.R., they were again incorporated. Although Estonians and Latvians are not ethnically related, prior to being conquered by Russia both countries lived under the sway of the Teutonic Order of the Sword. Their culture is Germanic, and the vast majority of Estonians and a considerable majority of Latvians are Lutherans (the eastern Latvians, having once been under Polish rule, are Catholics). There is nothing "Eastern about their culture and civilization: their cities and towns resemble those of northern Germany or Scandinavia. This continues to be true today, despite the considerable Russian immigration. Riga, the capital and main port of Latvia, has a Russian majority. Lithuania, which has an ancient history and whose land once extended almost to the Black Sea, is the most populous of the Baltic states and offers the most rigorous resistance to the U.S.S.R. A Catholic country united for centuries to Poland, Lithuania seems to belong to Central rather than Northern Europe. Perhaps even more so than their Latvian relatives, the Lithuanians are a tough people, conscious of their glorious history and not easily subdued. Though not overly friendly toward the Poles (the country still has a Polish minority), they share with them a common period of history covering four hundred years and keep a keen watch on events in Poland. In the past, they were the most Restless Baltic Republics relentless antagonists of Christianity, and the conversion of this pagan island in an already Christian sea came only at the end of the fourteenth and beginning of the fifteenth century. Today their resistance is at once national and religious, with the accent on the latter. There have been arrests and new deportations in recent years, but a clandestine Catholic underground press is in operation, and an iron will to resist, manifest through the ages, still exists. A very high birthrate — the most effective weapon against Russification — has led to a large emigration, especially to the United States, where well-organized Lithuanian immigrants continue to work for the liberation of their homeland. The United States does not acknowledge Soviet rule over the Baltic States. Such recognition has never been on the program of even the most "progressive" presidential candidate. Yet, the prospects for liberation of these hapless nations are no better — and no worse — than those of the other Eastern European satellites. Two not utterly unlikely possibilities for liberation are 1) a third world war that the Soviet Union would lose, and 2) the collapse of the U.S.S.R. through internal upheavals. There have been upheavals in the Soviet Union that were brutally quashed by the KGB. To the best of my knowledge the gravest of these "incidents" occurred in 1969 in Novocherkask, after the regular army had refused to intervene. (It took seven months for the news to reach the West, which is a good indication of how opaque the U.S.S.R. still is.) Some day the "fire brigade" may arrive too late and the conflagration spread. The proportion of convinced Communists in the U.S.S.R. is extremely small. In October 1980 a New York Times correspondent who has spent the past three years in the U.S.S.R. hazarded that only 2 percent of the Soviet population actually believe in Communisi r My own guess is that the number might be about twice that size. The figure of 17 million Party members, in a total population of 250 million, gives a misleading impression. Even the Party harbors dissidents (there being no better camouflage than outward loyalty). Therefore, though not likely in view of the organizational aspect of Communism, a collapse should not be ruled out even though the people have no alternative ideology, no alternative Utopia. Such an alternative vision could be achieved only through concrete, methodical, ideological propaganda from the West or through the people\'s freedom to speak and to publish. Such an evolution (or revolution) is therefore a Russian, not a "Baltic" problem. Once liberated and perhaps integrated into a federated Europe, the Baltic nations would face an immense job of reconstruction. They have suffered inordinately from all the executions, deportations, incarcerations, and deprivations of higher education and civil freedom. Even when their wounds have healed, these three martyred nations will bear their scars: victimized by an infamous Nazi-Soviet pact and by Western paralysis. -By E. van Kuehnelt-Leddihn, National Review, March 20,1981', 'THREE TIMES INVADED WITHIN 30 YEARS... From the Editor: Every year as the month of June approaches the three Baltic nations – the Estonians, Latvians and Lithuanians – with their hearts filled with sorrow – remember the unspeakable losses their countries have suffered through the ruthless occupation by a state which is a member of the United Nations and which under its adored leader is still talking about "international peace movements". The Latvian National foundation in Scandinavia has this year decided to commemorate the fatal day – 17th June – a Letter of Protest. On this day ten years ago the Red Army pushed into the Baltic States. This protest against the extortion of the Baltic wealth is written on behcJf of allßaltic people and sent to leading persons and institutions round the world. We herewith publish a review showing how the rulers of Kreml carry out their tyranny in the Baltic countries. The author is Oswald Freivald, formerly active in the Latvian Red Cross for which he organized the collection of factual data regarding the second Bolshevik occupation 1940/1941. With world peace and liberation of workers all over the world on their lips the masters or Moscow have three times occupied the Baltic countries. The fiist time was in 1919, just after the Russian Bolshevik government had declared the right to decide their own matters. This first occupation which – thank God – only lasted 6 months, cost the Latvian people 3772 murdered, 20.000 prisoners in concentration camps and deported to Russia and 8.950 dead from starvation. (About the same figures were registered for Estonia and Lithuania). The second occupation followed the invasion of the Red Army in the Baltic States – 15/17 th June 1940 – and lasted until the Ist July 1941, when they had to retreat under the pressure of the German Army. The Baltic people call this the year of horror. One year was sufficient to realize that the final goal was thespiritual, material and physical annihilation of the Baltic nations. This will no doubt be confirmed by the third occupation that begun in 1944 and still continues. In the material that the Checka left behind in the town Walk in Latvia an instruction has been found recommending the annihilation of certain groups of thepopulation which actually embraces the whole people. Other documents have also been traced as for instance an instruction that was found in Lithuania (See "INFORMATION" Issue No. 2). The first instruction for annihilation was issued by the NKVD the second day after the Baltic States had signed a treaty with Moscow for mutual help. This treaty was signed by Molotov. The instruction No, 001223 is dated 11th October 1939, and calls for registration of counter-revolutionaries who were suuposed to be against the Soviet government(without proving such activities). This annihilation of anti-bolshevik elements began already on the 14th June 1940, when the Bolsheviks at the frontier town Abrene assaulted the quarters of the frontier guard. On this occasion two soldiers, a woman and her child were murdered and H of the frontier guards and 28 civilians were deported to Russia. . i . And just a year later, in one single maht, the гШГЖпз 1941. ib.OOO tieopiewere done away_  to instructions later round the  were to take place Згф July anR this time 62.0UU Latvians were to—be deported. Only the arrival groups j.n the Baltic States stopped these However,deportations are taking place with double intensity during the third occupation from 1944 onwards. troups Immediately after the arrival in 1941 the Latvian Red Cross started a of the deported fellow countrymen that gave the following results: Murdered by NKVD М*l"lУ Fallen in partisan fights against the Kille^^ Deported from the prisons to Russia Deported during the night Arrested in the streets and atwrkmg places and deported after.the outbreak of war between Russia and Germany \'ЩИ\'3s6^B consisting of: Men 24.135, Women 7.497 and Children 4.196. Among these 1.086 officers, 490 professors and teachers at high schools, 366 students, 175 authors, artists and journalists, 105 doctors, 99 engineers and architects,64 lawyers, 57 judges, 44 agriculturists and 38 clergymen. Thus Latvia has in one year lost 18 out of every 1000 of its inhabitants. Such a loss could be compared to the following: for USA 2.4 millions, for Germany 1.2 millions, England 856.000, France 756.000, Sweden 111.000 people.The workers and farmers suffered most: 287% of the above total. Then came the Army and the Police – 15,8%, middle class 13,6, students 10.2% etc. Mass arrests. After the bolshevik invasion of the Baltic States new prisons were continually built and even the factories that had earlier been built close to the prisons so that the prisoners could carry on constructive work wore now turned into prisons. People had to spend their nights in constant agony: "Now they are coming! Are they coming to us?" No house,no family spent a peaceful night.... Murder of the arrested. As the Bolsheviks were forced to retreat owing to the German superiority in 1941, they were not able to remove all traces of their horror regime, and left mass graves, Checka dungeons etc. behind. There were rumours about murders and death sentences passed without trial. After the first of July 1941 the prisons were opened and a lot of mass graves were immediately found: in the court of the central prison in Riga, in the forests at Dreilini, Baltezers (Weissensee), Babitsee, Litene, in the garden of the Christus Church, etc. Only then did the dreadful fate of the prisoners become clear. 979 murdered people were found and in most cases identified. Certainly not all mass graves were found.The murdered people were just thrown together on top of each other, many with their hands bound behind their backs,broken skulls and with their lips cut off (perhaps they had rrotested or utttered words of contempt before the execution). It is now reported from the Baltic States that the ground above these graves has been levelled and visitors are arrested and deported to Siberia, even mothers and wives of the victims. For what "crimes" had the people been arrested and murdered? In nearly all cases the prisoners were accused of such things as: "hostile attitude\'towards the USSR","membership of the home guards (aizsargi)", "ex-policeman", "sung Latvian patriotic songs", "served in the White Army" (20 years ago!). Without investigation and without a chance to defend themselves these people were sentenced to penal servitude or death. Often only one Checka official sentenced them, as appears from the death sentence signed by the Checka leader in Latvia, Schustin, on the 26th June 1941, which sentence concerned 77 people: 70 men and 7 women. Deportation of the prisoners. Mass deportations started in the beginning of March 1941. The first big transport to Siberia is dated April 1941 and comprised 900 prisoners. The 23/24 th June all prisoners were evacuated to Russia. According to statements by the Red Cross 6192 persons havebeen deported to Russia: 5815 men, 351 women and 16 children. (A report in this matter was sent by the Red Cross in Latvia to . the International Red Cress in the autumn of 1941). The priscneis were deported to slave camps – Petschorlag, Kotlas, Vorkutskystroj, Karaganda. In all more than 1000 railway trains have been destined for Siberia according to statistics of*therailway administration. During the night of 13th June 1941 15.081 people were arrested and deported: 6.447 men, 5.302 women and 3.332 children below 16 years (among these 296 babies) and 315 older than 70, 4 even over 90 yearsf This clearly shows that the arrests were not for political reasons, but the beginning of an annihilation oT the whole people. 24.6% of the deported people were farmers and 12.8% industrial t workers. The number of arrested and deported people was probably the same in Estonia and Lithuania. Ten years have already passed since the beginning of"the year of horror 1940/1941". The 36.000 martyrs from this year have now been followed by hundreds of thousands other victims who are now bravely their tragic fate,as we know from returning German and other prisoners of war. Not until the last hour of this tyranny has passed will the world find out what sufferings people have gone through in the Bolshevik occupied countries. These destines will witness to the sacrifices that whole nations had to make in an era that was blind and deaf to the sufferings of millions. "We suffer from thirst!" one of the deported in 1941 wrote , on a piece of paper, which he threw out through the air hole of the waggon. Similarly millions of tortured Europeans thirst for freedom and humane treatment. And we, who are united to these millions Tolhedepths of our souls, can only hope that these Europeans will not die from their thirst, but that freedom will come before this gruesome death overtakes them.']

Name: 134_food_dough_mcdonalds_product
Representative_Docs: ['GREEN FAIR TO BE HELD DURING RIGA FOOD 2002 EXHIBITION A Green Fair will also be held during the international exhibition Riga Food2oo2\\o offer visitors biologically pure and certified products. 10 to 15 farms will offer their summer and autumn products at the fair. During the exhibition, farmers hope not only to popularise healthy food, but also to find potential co-operation partners who would market their products. The Green Fair originated during the Riga Food exhibition several years ago when a "farmstead" was opened within the framework of the exhibition that offered biologically pure products. It became unexpectedly popular with visitors, and became a tradition. The 7th international food, beverage, food processing, technology, packaging, public catering, store equipment and service exhibition Riga Food2oo2m\\\\ be held from 11 to 14 September at the Kipsala International Exhibition Centre. Already 500 companies from 23 countries have signed up to participate in the exhibition. Riga Food is the largest food industry exhibition in the Baltic countries.', 'To Eat Like A Latvian... Latvian homes are known for their hospitality, and for the simple, yet hearty and always tasty food offered to family and guests. Latvian cuisine reflects its natural and cultural environment. Latvian families generally kept to a meat-and-potato diet, with dark rye bread. As Latvia is on the Baltic Sea, fish is also a mainstay. Recipes show German and Scandinavian influences, while the substantial use of butter and cream mark Latvia as a country rich in dairy products. Spices, generally not prominent in Latvian cooking, do appear in specialty baked goods like pīrāgi and kriņģelis. Cardamom, a costly spice, is used in both recipes. Saffron, even more expensive, is needed in the kriņģelis. The following recipes were submitted by Dr. Ruta Kļaviņš of Toledo, Ohio, and were first published in the Toledo Blade. □ Pīrāgi Filling: 4 to 5 slices bacon, diced fine 4 to 5 small ham slices, diced fine 1 small onion, chopped fine Pepper to taste The filling can be prepared in advance and refrigerated. Saute onion in pan until translucent. In another pan, quickly fry bacon and ham; turn heat down and continue to saute. Drain off fat. Combine with onion. Cool. Pastry: 34 stick butter 1 cup milk 3 T sugar 2 envelopes dry yeast V* t salt V 4 t cardamom 3 cups all-purpose flour 1 lightly beaten egg Scald milk. Dissolve butter and sugar in milk. Cool to lukewarm. Put in large bowl. Sprinkle yeast over. Let set 5 to 10 minutes. Add salt and cardamom. Gradually incorporate flour until a light dough forms. Cover and let rise an hour or longer. Knead on lightly floured board. Remove walnut-sized pieces of dough. Flatten them to 2V£-inch circles on palm of hand. Place about 1 t of filling in the center of each circle, fold over each side, and pinch dough to seal securely. Place seam side down on lightly greased cookie sheet and shape into a half moon, or crescent shape. Let rise again in a warm place. Brush with lightly beaten egg. Bake in 375-degree oven 10-11 minutes until golden brown. Remove to a rack to cool. They freeze well and can be reheated in a low-degree oven for 5 minutes. Serve warm. Kriņģelis 6V4 to 7 cups all-purpose unbleached flour 2 cups lukewarm cream or milk 2 sticks butter or margarine, at room temp. 1 cup sugar 2 egg yolks 3 packages dry yeast (Rapid Rise preferred) Crated rind of one lemon Crated rind of one orange 1 t salt Vi t saffron Vi t ground cardamom IV4 cups golden raisins Vi cup slivered almonds 1 egg, lightly beaten with a little cold water Powdered sugar Crumble saffron in V* cup boiling water. Set aside. Combine 1 t sugar and V 4 cup lukewarm water. Sprinkle years over top and let stand 10 minutes. In a large bowl, mix warm cream, yeast mixture, and 3 cups flour. Mix thoroughly. Cover and let rise in a warm place, about Vi hour. In the meantime, cream butter and sugar; add egg yolks. Stir into risen dough. Add lemon and orange rinds, salt, saffron, and cardamom. Reserve Vi cup of the remaining flour and set aside. Gradually add remaining flour and raisins to dough. Mix until dough is smooth. Cover and let rise until doubled. Sprinkle reserved Vi cup flour on mixing board. Knead dough until it is smooth and elastic. Lightly grease a large cookie sheet. Form dough into a long rope and then form it into a pretzel shape. Cover and let rise. Brush with beaten egg and sprinkle with almonds. Bake 30 minutes in 375-degree oven until golden brown. Cool on pan before dusting with powdered sugar. This amount of dough can be divided into 3 parts and formed into loaves to make yellow bread.', 'UNPRECEDENTED NUMBER OF FOREIGN COMPANIES TO PARTICIPATE IN RIGA FOOD 2002 EXHIBITION For the first time, one third of companies participating in the trade fair Riga Food 2002 at the Kipsala international Exhibition Centre from 11 to 14 September 2002 will be from abroad. Already more than 400 companies from 21 countries have applied for participation in the 7th international fair focusing on food, beverages, food processing, technology, packaging, public catering, store equipment and service. For the first time seminars for producers of food and production facilities will be held by the Latvian Technology Centre. The seminars will address European Union policy for development of small and medium-sized enterprises; options for export of Latvian food products to the EU; latest trends in food production, packaging methods and materials; and food quality control system at public catering enterprises. As in the previous fairs, Riga Food 2002 will feature taste-testing of food products. Contests among bakeries and fish canneries wili also be held as well as the 3rd business meeting of representatives of food sectors from the Baltic and CIS countries. The 3rd international hotel equipment, household appliances, detergent, design and accessories fair Riga Gastro 2Oo2m\\\\ be held simultaneously with Riga Food 2002 at the Kipsala centre.']

Name: 135_ferry_rostock_ventspils_saaremaa
Representative_Docs: ['New ferry link between Latvia and Sweden A new ferry line {VVLine) between Ventspils in Latvia and Vestervik, Sweden, was opened on 31 July. The ferry will depart from Vestervik on Mondays, Wednesadys and Fridays, butfrom Ventspils on Tuesdays, Thursdays and Sundays. The trip will last 12 hours. The length of the ferry is 137 m and there are 69 beds in it. The maximum toad is 13,730 tons. The name of the ferry is Finn Maid. The ferry was built in 1972 and repaired in 1996. The ferry will transport both freight and passengers.', 'Edited by Eriks Zvirbulis Ferry line Ventspils-Saaremaa commenced On 17 May, a new ferry line Ventspils-Saaremaa (Estonia) was commenced. The destination of the first trip was the Roomassaarest port in Saaremaa, but from now on the ferry will go to the Montu port, which is not far from Kuressaare, the largest city of Saaremaa. To ensure more comfort to tourists a new bus route linking Montu and Kuressaare has also been commenced. The duration of the ferry trip is 4 hours. As pointed out by the Director of Sea Administration, Ansis Zeltins, the ferry has been modernised and equipped with new generation navigation equipment and complies with all international navigation requirements. Dn the first trip of the ferry, the Estonian Minister of Economy and Transport, Edgar Savisaar, arrived to Latvia. The Minister underlined that the new ferry line stood not only for a bridge between the two neighbouring countries, but also allowed the tourists from Western Europe to shorten their way to Finland, Sweden and other Scandinavian countries. ~ Ministry of Transport <http:// www.sam.gov.lv> (17.05.2005)', 'Ferry line Ventspils-Saaremaa commenced On 17 May, a new ferry line Ventspils-Saaremaa (Estonia) was commenced. The destination of the first trip was the Roomassaarest port in Saaremaa, but from now on the ferry will go to the Montu port, which is not far from Kuressaare, the largest city of Saaremaa. To ensure more comfort to tourists a new bus route linking Montu and Kuressaare has also been commenced. The duration of the ferry trip is 4 hours. As pointed out by the Director of Sea Administration, Ansis Zeltins, the ferry has been modernised and equipped with new generation navigation equipment and complies with all international navigation requirements. On the first trip of the ferry, the Estonian Minister of Economy and Transport, Edgar Savisaar, arrived to Latvia. The Minister underlined that the new ferry line stood not only for a bridge between the two neighbouring countries, but also allowed the tourists from Western Europe to shorten their way to Finland, Sweden and other Scandinavian countries.']
